Labourers' Cottages (Loans) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In rising to ask the House
to give the Bill a Second Reading I may
perhaps inform the House that this Bill
is more or less in the nature of a Money
Bill and, therefore, I do not think I need
detain the House at any great length.
The Bill makes reference to Subsection (2) of Section 1 of the
Labourers Act of 1923, under which
annual loan charges of rural district
councils in respect of Labourers Acts
schemes have hitherto been subsidised by
the Government to the extent of 36 per
cent., and it proposes to repeal that Subsection as regards future schemes from
1st April of this year up to 31st March,
1930, and to provide instead a subsidy
of 40 per cent of the annual loan charges
of rural district councils.
As hon. Members are probably aware,
the financial scheme under which the
Labourers Acts are administered was
formulated over twenty years ago when
advances could be obtained much more
cheaply than at the present day, and so
owing to the rise in the value of money
the fixed percentage grant no longer
truly represents the real assistance
which is being given by the Government
to the construction of these houses.
In future, therefore, advances for the
erection of labourers' cottages will be
made from the Government Loans Fund,
thereby eliminating the anomalies which
arose as the result of issuing stock at the
low price necessitated by the low rate of
interest payable. This entails a readjustment of the fixed percentage grant which
has hitherto been paid, and it has been
decided that a Government grant of 40
per cent. of the loan charges will have
the result of fairly distributing the
financial responsibility as between the
local authorities and the Government.
I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: I have previously assured
the House of the anxiety of local
authorities to provide cottages for agricultural labourers, and I have also dealt
with their difficulties. They are mainly
to provide a suitable cottage for a
labourer at a rent which that labourer
can pay on the small wage he receives.
Labourers' Cottages Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
Local authorities have been in a difficulty.
because they found in the past that, for
the reasons stated by the noble Viscount
who moved the Second Reading of this
Bill, they could not provide a cottage at
what was considered an economic rent for
agricultural labourers. They were reluctant to subsidise these agricultural
labourers out of the rates, and, if I am
correct in my view, on a cursory reading
of this Bill, that the object and the
result of the Measure will be to enable
local authorities to bridge that difficulty,
and facilitate the building of labourers'
cottages, I shall certainly welcome the
Bill. My view, after a cursory reading
of the Bill, is that it will be a step in that
direction, and will contribute to that
result. Therefore, I support the Second
Reading.
I
Mr. DORMAN: I also wish to welcome
the introduction of this Measure, or any
Measure which is calculated to improve
the conditions of the working classes.
especially welcome this Bill because I
know that for many years disparaging
comparisons have been made as to the
moral status of the people living in this
particular part of Ireland as compared
with the people living in other areas.
These statistics have had a more or less
world-wide dissemination, and have, no
doubt, lead to a misconception as to the
real moral status of the people in this
particular part of the country.
I believe that that has been mainly due
to the want of proper housing facilities
being brought within reach of the
agricultural labourers in the Province.
of Ulster as compared with those in
Munster and Leinster. The labourers in
the other parts of Ireland, from the time
that the Labourers Act was placed upon
the Statute Book, have forced their representatives to make every provision for
the housing of the coming generation,
the young men and women who are
entering into the bonds of holy
matrimony and setting up homes. Unfortunately that progress has not been
correspondingly made in the North of
Ireland, with the result that the disparaging comparisons I have already
referred to have been made, and
justifiably made, from the Government
statistics.
There is another aspect of this particular Measure I would like to refer to,
and it is this. Making a comparison of
the incidental expenses charged in connection with the erection of labourers'
cottages, I believe, speaking from
memory, that the legal and other expenses in regard to the erection of labourers
cottages in this part of the country have
been abnormally high in comparison with
the expenses in Munster and Leinster. If
my memory serves me right, I think that
in one district of Cork the average legal
expenses in connection with the erection
of each labourer's cottage are somewhat
over £7, and in some parts of County
Down they have amounted to £32. That
is an aspect of the erection of labourers'
dwellings that ought to be given due consideration by public representatives on
local bodies to whom these facilities will
be extended. I have great pleasure in
welcoming the Bill, believing that it will
do good to those whom the Government
is anxious to assist.
we
are to
Mr. HILL DICKSON: This being a
Money Bill I take it that we can have
very little to say to it. I understand,
however, from the Labourers Acts that
rural district councils in Northern Ireland were receiving a grant of 36 per
cent., but under this Bill
receive 40 per cent. Therefore, in connection with those schemes that are at
present under consideration but have not
been completed, it would be in the
interest of rural district councils to hold
them up until after 1st April, when
they will get 40 per cent. instead of 36
per cent. In the district I represent,
Newtownards, we have a big scheme coming on and we have done our level best
to force the Government's hands to get it
ready, but if this Bill becomes law I
should be inclined to tell the rural
council to hold back until 1st April and
get 4 per cent. additional grant.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope I need scarcely
say that on a Bill of this kind I can only
rise to support it. All of us who have
been concerned in any capacity during
the past few years in securing or promoting the erection of labourers'
cottages know what the difficulties have
been and what the necessities have been.
We must all agree with the hon. Member,
the Chairman of the Londonderry
County Council (Mr. Long) that the real
problem is to provide a cottage which
can be rented at such cost as the agricultural labourer can pay in the way of
rent. That is always our problem and
the only way we can meet it is, I think,
73 Labourers' Cottages Bill
31 MARCH 1927
Dogs Bill

by cheapening the various costs of
erection.
was
As regards those costs, Senator Dorman mentioned the heavy percentage of
the cost which is taken up by the legal
expenses of transfer. Those expenses in
the past have been very heavy. Indeed,
for reasons that do not matter I have
more than once come into contact with
them, but I think it is fair to remind the
House this afternoon that owing to the
transfer, which is now almost complete,
of the agricultural land in Northern Ireland from the landlord to the tenant
occupier those expenses will necessarily
tend to diminish. The expense
almost invariably caused by the mechanical difficulty of proving title, and it
frequently happened that where the
property had been in the hands of one
family for two or three centuries to
prove the family charges, not to mention any other charges on such property
none of them, perhaps, then held
at the day on which those charges were
first imposed, led to a cost of transfer
which was absolutely exorbitant.
of the advantages of land transfer is that
that cost will to a great extent disappear,
and to that extent we shall be assisted
in our beneficent endeavour to improve
the housing of the agricultural labourer.
as
One
There is one other point that I should
like to mention, because I think it is
worth while that it should be mentioned,
and that is that I take it that this is a
Bill which entails as the noble Viscount
has pointed out, the financing of these
Measures is from the local funds in the
ordinary way--which entails the final disappearance or, rather, marks perhaps the
disappearance which has already taken
place of what used to be called the "Land
Act terms' in connection with the erection of some of these cottages. One hears
that expression "Land Act terms" frequently in the country districts. It has
been inappropriate for some years past,
and the objection to it of course is that
if people think you can get money for
labourers' cottages on the terms on which
land could be bought under the Act of
1903 they are making a grievous mistake.
It is sometimes thrown up both against the
local authority and against the Government that, although they could obtain
money for labourers' cottages on Wyndham Land Act terms they are not doing
it. Of course, the charges are entirely
unfounded, and, as I say, the passage of
the Bill may perhaps serve to remind the
public of the fact which is known to the
instructed and ought to be more widely
appreciated. I have great pleasure in
supporting the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Royal Assent.
I am
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
authorised by the Prime Minister, on his
behalf, to inform the Senate that His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland,
in the name of, and on behalf of, His
Majesty the King, has been pleased to
give his assent to the following Bills:
Agricultural Research Station.
Consolidated Fund.
Dogs Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
:-
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move the Second Reading of this very
short Bill, which seeks to enact that it
shall be a valid defence to an action for
damages for the shooting of a dog to prove
that the dog was shot when chasing or
worrying cattle. The Bill is very simple,
but it is hoped by the Government that,
if it receives the approval of Parliament
and is placed upon the Statute Book, it
will go some way towards mitigating an
evil from which almost every sheep-keeper
in the country districts has suffered.
The last legislation on this difficult
matter is contained in the Dogs Act of
1906, which provides that the owner of a
dog shall be liable in damages for injury
done to any cattle by that dog. It is
common knowledge, however, that the Act
has by no means proved as efficacious as
was hoped. First of all, the dog and its
owner must be identified. Hon. Members
know as well as I do that dogs that worry
sheep are generally dogs that come from
a distance. The reason for that is beyond the capacity of the Leader of the
House to give, but it is a fact, I believe.
Dogs Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
In the second place, when the owner of
the dog has actually been identified, and
an action brought against him by the
farmer whose sheep have been worried, it
frequently happens damages cannot be
obtained owing to the fact that the dog
is not of sufficient financial
Owner
standing.
My right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, received a deputation a
short time ago from the Ulster Farmers'
Union, who supplied details of fully
authenticated
cases of sheep-worrying
dogs. During 1926 there were 115 sheep
and lambs killed by dogs, 95 worried and
7 flocks chased; while, since the beginning of the present year, there have been
75 killed, 2 worried and 4 flocks chased.
Now, there is no doubt whatever that
these figures are not final. Indeed, I
could add to them myself from my own
personal experience, and I have no doubt
many other hon. Members of this House
could do so also.
I
There has recently been in Northern
Ireland an increase in the number of
sheep kept by our small farmers, who are
beginning to find it a profitable occupation, and this Bill is designed to help
them, so far as can be done without acting unfairly towards the dog owner.
do not think that I need say any more; I
do not anticipate that any Member of
this House will oppose the Bill, or try
to show that it is designed unfairly
against the dog owner. A very important safeguard towards that end is inserted in the Bill, by which it is required.
that the person who shot the dog shall
notify the police within 48 hours. I beg
to move.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: This Bill will be
heartily welcomed by the farming community in every part of the Province,
and I think the statistics, which the
noble Leader of the House has given,
show the absolute necessity for it. The
present remedies in the case of sheep
worried or killed by dogs are quite ineffective, and people hesitate to let themselves in for a law suit with very doubtful results. The law being ineffective it
became necessary for some steps to be
taken. I think the Government is to be
congratulated on bringing forward a
Measure of this kind which, though
perhaps not remedying the evil to the
fullest extent, will nevertheless go far
to mitigate it. The difficulties of tracing
dogs that have worried or killed sheep are
very great, and the art of eluding the Act
by owners has been brought to a very
fine point.
last
I know myself where within the
few months eight ewes have been killed
and the rest of the sheep so driven and
tormented that the lambs they had to
produce have been abortions. Unless
some Measure of this kind was introduced
the keeping of sheep would not only
diminish but would probably be terminated in the Province. Sheep are most
valuable animals. First of all, as we all
know, we have lambs and we have wool,
and the sheep themselves are beneficial to
the land, often bringing back land to a
state of productiveness when it has been
exhausted. They also have a very good
taste for noxious weeds, which is another
advantage. Therefore, it is essential
that the keeping of sheep should be
encouraged as much as possible, and,
consequently, I very cordially support
the Measure proposed.
Mr. LESLIE: Like the hon. Senator
for Armagh (Mr. Armstrong) whose
voice, if I may digress for a moment, we
are very pleased to hear once again in
our counsels, I welcome this Bill, and I
desire to thank the Government for having brought it in, principally, of course,
from the public standpoint, and partly,
as I must admit, from the private
standpoint, as one who endeavours
to mitigate the losses that agricultural property entails by rearing
sheep and lambs. The difficulty is one
which has already been alluded to. The
noble Viscount, who moved the Second
Reading, pointed out that if a farmer
who had been injured was able to identify
the dog and the owner, and to bring an
action, he stood to lose the fruits of his
successful action owing to the fact that
the owner of the dog was a man of straw.
If you
and
There is another very serious difficulty.
The heaviest loss is really not the worry.
ing of a few incidental sheep, although
sheep may be killed, but the hunting of
ewes that are in lamb. These ewes do, as
has been said, cast their lambs.
can identify the dog and the owner,
if the owner is a man of substance still
you cannot recover damages for the loss
of those lambs because it is to some extent
hypothetical and the law cannot go SO
far. The lambs may have been lost for
some other reason, and you have to stand
Dogs Bill-
Second Reading
31 MARCH 1927
your loss.
This Measure will assist in
preventing losses of that kind.
As regards the Bill itself, the thing that
I think ought to be noted particularly is
that it is an additional Measure, that is,
it affords additional protection. It does
not in any way take away from the
common law right of a man to protect his
property, but it does tend to protect him
from vexatious actions when he has acted
in defence of that property. It does that
provided he has taken the precaution of
notifying the police authorities.
I am
bound to say if this Bill had originated in
this House I should have felt obliged at a
later stage to draw the attention of the
House to the fact that notice had to be
given within 48 hours, because it seems to
me, perhaps, open to question whether in
the case of a shepherd on one of our
sheep mountains a period of 48 hours is
sufficient within which to give notice.
Shepherds' houses, as we know, are rarely
provided with a telephone, the pillar
box is invariably at the bottom of the
mountain, and the police barracks are
sometimes several miles distant.
are
It might be felt in isolated instances
that a shepherd on a mountain might find
some difficulty in giving notice. On the
other hand I think, as I believe is the
general impression amongst those who
have endeavoured to study the Bill and
work out its possible consequences, that
this giving of notice and so on will operate
probably not so much on the mountains
as on the smaller farms to which the
noble Viscount alluded and which
more or less contiguous to towns of some
size or places where there are police barracks or, at all events, post offices.
the whole I do not believe that the 48
hours provision will be found to be of any
serious detriment. It will certainly
operate on the minds of those who describe
themselves as dog lovers and yet allow the
objects of their affection to wander at
will at unsuitable times in very unsuitable
places and it will to some extent satisfy
such people that the interests of the dog
have not been entirely neglected.
On
neces-
The only other point it seems
sary to make is to suggest to the Government, if indeed a suggestion is necessary,
that some steps will have to be taken to
ensure that the police authorities keep an
adequate record of any notice that may be
given to them. I mean that if the notice
were given, as it sometimes will be, by
word of mouth, a note of the notice will
be taken in what I believe is called the
day book at the station and a record
maintained in that. If a letter be sent by
post I presume that any man sending a
letter himself, or permitting his servants
to send it on his behalf, would have the
commonsense to request the police authorities to whom the letter was addressed to
send him an acknowledgment of it. I
think that perhaps that would be a desirable precaution in order that if it should
be necessary to plead the defence provided
by the Bill in a possible action, evidence
that the necessary steps had been taken
might be forthcoming.
I do not think that in practice any
difficulty will arise. On the other hand
the
I do think that the mere fact that the
Bill has been passed through this Parliament will have a great effect amongst
those who are indirectly responsibleonly indirectly responsible-for
ravages of dogs, and that a farmer who
has suffered by loss to his stock will not
in the future be exposed as often as he
has been in the past to the further suffering of an unnecessary and possibly unsuccessful legal action.
Mr. LONG: I should like to join with
the two previous speakers in welcoming
this Bill. The matter is one of some importance to those engaged in agriculture.
I understand that the Londonderry Committee of Agriculture were the first to
bring this matter under the notice of the
Government. I should like to quote
some statistics as to the damage reported
in the County of Londonderry during the
years 1926 and 1927.
In one night in
1926 two flocks were chased, five sheep
were worried, 26 sheep were chased into
a river, two were drowned, two killed,
and one died the next day. In another
instance 14 sheep were worried, five
flocks being involved. During 1927, so
far two ewes and two lambs have been
killed in one case; in another case four
sheep and three lambs were killed and
two ewes were worried, the estimated
loss being £28. In another case seven
sheep were killed and the remainder of
the flock were so damaged as to be unsaleable as mutton, the estimated loss in
this case being £30. In a further case
eight sheep were killed, the estimated loss
being £20. Three years ago a farmer
lost 30 sheep killed in one week
The importance of this matter will be
apparent to Members of the Senate, and
Second Reading
Dogs Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
I am very pleased with the reception the
Bill has got up to the present. The
County Londonderry Committee of Agriculture went a step
further than that
contemplated in this Bill, and I regret
that the Government have not adopted
It was that the protheir suggestion.
duce of dog licences should be utilised
to form a fund from which sheep owners
who suffered in this way could be compensated. The administration of that
proposal might cause the Government
some difficulty, but I feel that the suggestion is one worthy of consideration,
and I very much regret that the Government have not adopted it.
As the Bill stands the sheep-owner will
not receive any compensation, but will
merely have the questionable satisfaction of knowing that the animal that
did the damage to his sheep will probably
be destroyed. No compensation, however, can be given. If the suggestion of
the County Londonderry Committee of
Agriculture had been accepted I believe
it would have been practicable, that it
would not have been unduly complicated,
and that it would have tended to redress
a grievance from which all sheep-owners
feel they suffer at the moment.
I support the Bill as far as it goes.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
rise to support this short Bill and I am
glad that it does not contain a Clause
providing for compensation. I think
the preventive measures contained in the
Bill should first be given a chance to
prove whether they are effective and if
they are not an amending Bill can, if
necessary, be introduced later on. I
knew
little about sheep-worrying
very
until the matter was mentioned in the
King's Speech. Since then I have heard a
great deal about it, and since lunch time
to-day I have received a report that there
have been three sheep worried in the
country. I shall be very glad to report to
those interested the favourable reception
this Bill has received in this House and,
like the previous speakers, I shall rejoice
to see the Measure placed on the Statute
Book.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to support the
Bill and to express the hope that it will
accomplish the object the Government has
in view. That is to reduce the amount
of the loss sustained by sheep-owners
through ravages by dogs. At the same
time I do not think this Measure will
own
ultimately prove a success. If there was
some method introduced whereby dogs
could be controlled after the hours of
dark it would do more to diminish the
loss caused in this direction than any
other step that the Government could
take. Hon. Members should know, from
a proper understanding of their
nature, that the savage ancestry of the
animal creation is more likely to assert
itself during the hours of darkness than it
is during daylight. That is as true of all
animals as it is of man, and I believe
that much of the injury that has been
perpetrated by these dogs is due to the
urge of that vestige of savage ancestry
which asserts itself at night.
Many years ago I was struck with this
particular feature of the animal creation
by a cat I had. I was living in the neighbourhood of a nobleman's estate on which
there were a large number of rabbits, and
a large income was derived from the sale
of these rabbits. I happened to have a
cat and every morning that cat got over a
wall eight or ten feet high with a young
rabbit. I ultimately had to destroy the
cat because practically every morning,
after the cat had been out all night, we
found a rabbit in the yard. That was
the urge of the savage ancestry of that
young cat which influenced the animal to
go back to the habits of its savage
ancestors. I have no doubt that if I had
kept the cat, which was a great pet, it
would have succeeded in destroying a
large number of rabbits.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
am afraid the hon. Member himself benefited by the urge in his cat. (Laughter.)
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I desire to
support the Second Reading of this Bill.
my only objection to it being that it does
not go far enough. With regard to the
question of compensation, I cannot agree
with the suggestion put forward by my
hon. Friend (Mr. Long) at least until
this Bill has been given a trial. As to the
hon. Senator opposite and his cat, I think
that if the cat had been fed in the daytime it would not have run after rabbits
at night. (Laughter). I think that the
suggestion to confine dogs at night is useless. A dog, to be of any use, must be
trained to be loose at night to look after
his owner's poultry, etc. I think the
power given in this Bill to shoot dogs
that are caught chasing sheep will be a
very useful provision in the interests of
agriculture.

CLAUSE 2 (Prohibition of employment of women and young persons in
painting buildings with lead paint).
On and after the nineteenth day of
November, nineteen hundred and
twenty-seven, it shall not be lawful to
employ any woman or young person in
painting any part of a building with
lead paint:
With
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
regard to the Amendment put down by
Senator Leslie for consideration on
Clause 7 of the Bill in Committee, I
understand the object he had in mind
was to secure that there shall be no possible doubt that the special protection
afforded by Clause 2 shall apply to a
person under 14 years of age, who is
covered by the expression child, as defined in the factory code, of which this
Bill is to form part. Therefore, in order
to meet the hon. Senator's views, I
desire myself to move the following
Amendment :--
•
""
Clause 2, page 2, line 23, omit woman
or young person," and insert" any person
or child.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps, as having been
originally responsible for bringing this
matter before the House, I may be permitted to say that
the Amendment
which the noble Viscount has moved
completely meets my views. The matter
was one on which there was no difference
of principle at any time amongst any of
us, but which did present a most extraordinary technical difficulty. Wherever
one looked one appeared to be tripped
up by definitions which were not uniform.
We had two definitions which were
absolutely contradictory, and we had to
consider whether the definition which the
noble Viscount had quoted, the Act of
1901, was not necessarily the over-riding
definition.
The matter has been looked into very
carefully during the past few days, and
it will be remembered that the Leader of
the House was so good as to postpone the
Third Reading of the Bill. The matter
has been most carefully examined from
every point of view. I think I may say
that everybody is quite satisfied that it
is more convenient to give effect to our
purpose by inserting the words just read
out in the particular Clause of the Bill
to which they particularly relate rather
than to follow what is, perhaps, the more
normal practice, which I endeavoured to
follow in the Amendment on Clause 7;
the more ordinary practice of putting
our definition in the definition Clause.
If the House accepts the words now
offered it will be utterly impossible for
anybody, however perverted in mind or
however stupid, to misunderstand the
plain intention of the Act that in this
process a child under 14 shall not be
employed. I have, of course, the
greatest possible pleasure in supporting
the Amendment, and I have to thank the
Government for the trouble they have
taken in introducing this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.
--(Viscount Charlemont)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons

Mr. LESLIE: May I ask if it is proposed to take the Committee Stage of the

I hope I myself may not have any

VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
in the hands of the House and will take

VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I have received the
following Message from His Grace the
Governor :
:-
I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the
speech with which I have opened the
present Session of Parliament.
LOCAL BILLS.
Joint Unopposed Committee.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move: That the
Questions to Minister.
HOLYHEAD-KINGSTOWN MAIL
SERVICE.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before calling upon
the right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Samuel
Cunningham) to ask the question on the
Paper I would like to say that I had some
doubt in my mind whether these questions
should be allowed or not, as they referred
to a reserved service. However, there
seems to be no doubt that the Ulster
Government first proposed this change in
the mail route and, therefore, morally if
not technically, they are responsible for
it.
I think it would be absurd, therefore, to debar any question or any debate
on the subject, but I hope hon. Members
will keep as closely as possible within the
scope of the questions.
Mr. McDOWELL: Might I respectfully submit that the Postmaster-General
across the water distinctly stated that it
was with the concurrence of the Northern
Ireland Government that such a proposal
was adopted?
Mr. SPEAKER: With the concurrence
of the Northern Ireland Government?
Mr. McDOWELL: Yes.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think that is what
I have already said. Does the hon.
Member mean that the fact that the
Postmaster-General used the expression
((
concurrence of the Northern Ireland
Government," shows that the Government of Northern Ireland had been consulted? Is that what the hon. Member
means? I think the position is as I
have stated that though the Imperial
Government are technically responsible,
the Ulster Government are morally responsible.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state what is the advantage of changing the mail route to Kingstown if the letters are not to be sorted
on the train between Dublin and Belfast,
and if with the almost perfect system of
transhipping at the ports and the powerful steamers in use, there is no prospect
of any appreciable acceleration of the
service, while with the present service via
Larne the delivery is due to be made at
the same time.
Mr.
SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
have to thank you, Mr. Speaker, for the
trouble you have taken and the liberty
you have given in connection with this
question. Under the circumstances I
will say nothing beyond asking the question which conveys all I wish to say.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
advantages possessed by the Holyhead-
Kingstown route over the Stranraer-
Larne route as a mail service are twofold. The first is the inclusion of the
Northern Irish letters in the first London
delivery instead of in the second, or an
even later delivery if the Stranraer-Larne
route is used. The second advantage is
that, taken over a considerable period of
time, the arrival of the morning Dublin-
Belfast mail train has been shown to be
more punctual and less affected by
seasonal traffic than is the case with the
Stranraer-Larne service.
In regard to the second part of the
question, I can only say that, while the
Ministry of Commerce will lose no opportunity which may present itself of obtaining acceleration, it is difficult to indicate any particular respect in which the
handling and transit of the mails at and
between Holyhead and Kingstown could
be expedited.
LARNE-STRANRAER MAIL ROUTE.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham:—
To ask the Leader of the House if he
can state whether, when negotiating with
the Railway Company about the change
of route, the Government took into consideration the fact that if the time of the
departure of the Stranraer route to
London were changed to 5-30 p.m. there
would probably be no serious difficulty
in including the mails in the first London
delivery.
It
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: In
asking the second question standing in my
name, I would like the Government, if
possible, to give the House some information as to the suggested accelerated
service on the Larne-Stranraer route.
is a matter of general knowledge that
there was some acceleration arranged by
a deputation that went to the Post Office
in January last, and I should be very
glad if the Leader of the House could
inform us what those accelerated times
were.
On
My own idea is that the Holyhead-
Kingstown service is so near perfect that
there is very little likelihood of getting any
further advantage by that system.
the other hand, the Larne-Stranraer
route has been neglected, and I am afraid
of late it has been intentionally retarded,
and I believe that if that route were properly organized and speeded up it would
be of even greater advantage to Northern
Ireland than the present route. I think
the House would welcome information
from the Government as to the time it
would be possible for Northern Ireland
mails to be delivered in London if the
Larne-Stranraer route were accelerated as
suggested.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I cannot at the present moment
give any information as to the times asked
for by the right hon. Gentleman, as it
was not requested on the question on the
Paper. Perhaps the right hon. Gentleman would put down a supplementary
question at a later date, by which time I
might be able to give the information
asked for.
With regard to the general question,
the practicability of arranging an earlier
departure of the train from Belfast so
as to secure inclusion in the first London
delivery of Northern Irish letters conveyed via Larne and Stranraer has been
closely investigated by the London Midland & Scottish Railway Company, at the
instance of the Northern Government, on
more than one occasion. The last occasion
89 Labourers' Cottages Bill.
Dogs Bill
5 APRIL 1927

was immediately prior to the Northern
Government's decision in favour of reversion to the Holyhead-Kingstown route.
As a result of the inquiries made on that
occasion the company stated that, in order
to procure a sufficiently early arrival in
London to permit of connection with the
first delivery, the mails would require to
leave Belfast before 5-30 p.m., so as to
leave Carlisle at 11-30 p.m., and that such
a change would necessitate additional
train mileage from Carlisle to London at
a cost of approximately £12,000 per
annum. As such additional mileage was
not required by the company, nor could
be utilised for the company's own traffic
purposes, the London Midland & Scottish
authorities were not prepared to entertain
such a proposal.
I think that these facts will convince
the right hon. Gentleman that the suggestion underlying his question does not
lend itself to practical application at
present.

CLAUSE 1 (Chasing and worrying of
cattle, horses and sheep by dogs).
Mr. LESLIE: A question of some
importance arises on Clause 1 of this Bill,
and I think I can perhaps best express
it by saying the point is the extentlooking at extent from the point of view
of time of the validity of the defence
that it is proposed to provide. All
the circumstances that are likely to
surround an Occurrence of the kind
against which this Bill is directed are
so fully in the minds of all of us that
it is not necessary, or perhaps not very
desirable, to take up time by alluding to them this afternoon. I think
Dogs Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that if we look at the question we are
rather driven to the conclusion that any
attempt at meticulous definition would
serve to complicate the question and,
perhaps, to defeat our object. I think,
then, I might limit what I have to say
this afternoon to simply asking the noble
Viscount in charge of the Bill whether,
having regard to the advice which has no
doubt been tendered to the Government
and the amount of consideration they
have bestowed on the Bill, we may rely
on the fact that the expression "when
chasing or worrying cattle" will not be
unduly narrowly construed by the Courts.
I have put no Amendment on the Paper
as a vehicle of discussion because the
point seems to me to be better dealt with
on the question that the Clause stand part
of the Bill.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
perfectly certain that a great deal of the
time taken in drafting this Bill was occupied by discussions as to the exact meaning
of the word " when, and I think the
point may safely be left to the discretion
of the Court which may try one of these
cases. I think myself it would be wiser
to trust to the discretion of the Court
rather than to meticulously define words
in the Clause.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and title agreed to.
:

to move, That this House do now adjourn.

93 Horticultural Produce Bill-

6 APRIL 1927
Second Reading
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 6th April, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Petitions against Local Bill.
The following Petitions, praying "That
the Petitioners may be heard by their
Counsel, Agents and Witnesses against
the Bill after mentioned," were presented, read, and ordered to lie upon the
Table, viz:
Name of Bill
Antrim Electricity Supply
Bill.
Petitioners
Belfast and North-East Ireland
Electricity and Power Company.
The Right Hon. Anthony Earl
of Shaftesbury and the
Toome Eel Fishery, Ltd.
Larne Electric Light and Power
Company.
Board of Conservators of Fisheries for the Coleraine District
Antrim County Council.

Trustees.
Londonderry County Council.
Lagan Navigation Company.
Honourable the Irish Society.
Antrim Electricity Supply Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.
Horticultural Produce (Sales on
Commission) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I beg to move the Second
Reading of this Bill, the object of which
is to regulate the sale on commission of
horticultural produce, that is, vegetables,
fruits, flowers and plants. The Bill has
received the whole-hearted support of the
fruit-growing interests, and the Ministry
of Agriculture are confident that it will
prove of great assistance in improving the
grading and packing of fruit. I may say
that an identical Bill was introduced into
the Imperial Parliament last year, and
became law for Great Britain in last
December.
By whom Deposited
Robert Wallace
French and French
James O'Brien
Tillie, McDermott and Munn
William Currie
Crookshank, Leech and Davies
W. A. Lane
White, McMillan and Wheeler
W. A. Lane
The Bill provides that the salesmen
of horticultural produce must keep
a record of the actual price paid
by every purchaser for the consignments which have been entrusted
to him for sale on commission and
must forward that information to the
owner, together with particulars of the
commission which he has charged, and
details of any amounts paid by him on
behalf of the owner in connection with
the sale.
The whole object of the Bill will be
quite clear to hon. Members of this House,
and I am sure that it will receive their
hearty support. It is designed to secure
that the grower who has taken pains in
grading and packing his produce will be
paid the extra price to which he is entitled, and that he will not be obliged
to content himself with a sum which has
been reached by averaging all the sales
on the same day, thereby giving the unprogressive grower exactly the same price
for his produce as his more enterprising
neighbour. I beg to move.
Horticultural Produce Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we shall all
sympathize with the object of the
Measure, the Second Reading of which
has been proposed by the noble Viscount.
I take it that the particular object of the
Bill is the prevention of fraud, because
something in the nature of fraud would
appear to have taken place in former
times. If a man does not really get the
fair selling value of his produce he is, I
think, defrauded, and we should all
naturally desire to protect him from
injury of that description, and we should.
all be equally anxious to assist the ultimate consumer to obtain fruit and vegetables that have been properly graded
and packed and delivered to the salesman.
The Bill, as I understand it, is ultimately based on a Report of the Linlithgow Committee, which sat, I believe,
about 1921, and it follows word for word
the English Bill as it was last year, and
the English Act as it is now, to which
the noble Viscount has alluded. I think
there is scarcely a word of difference
between the two Measures, but I am
afraid that if we are going to introduce
a new criminal offence it behoves us to
be sure that we are doing it in words.
which are absolutely clear.
((
is
Looking at almost the principal Clause
in the Bill, we find owner or consigner
referred to. I must admit that I am
in great doubt as to whether, in relation
to the owner, the word "consigner
intended as an alias or alternative.
Throughout the Bill the owner or consigner is to receive this or that information, but we do not appear anywhere to
be told how the selection is to be made
between the two if, as may be, they
happen to be different persons. I think
it is not unreasonable to suppose that in
the varied transactions of Northern Ireland the owner of produce and the man
who consigns it will not be the same
person.
I should imagine the person to whom
the information is intended to be sent
is the person who sends the advice note
to the salesman, but we do not appear
to be told. Indeed, taking the Bill as a
whole, it seems to me-I am afraid I
must say, although it is based word for
word on the English Act; it is not the
production of Northern Ireland, because
it was not drawn here--to be a very disjointed production which will require a
certain amount of attention at a later
stage.
We find expressions which we are not
accustomed to, and one or two references
which make me doubt whether the Bill,
admirable as it may be for English process, is quite adapted for producers in
this country, great or small. Apparently
the Bill draws no distinction between
relatively large transactions in, let us
say, apples from the County of Armagh,
and small transactions in market gardening produce in any country town. I am
not quite clear that I really know what
salesman means in this connection, particularly if one has regard to the fact
that the salesmanship may not be a oneman business.
a
It seems to me that there are obvious
difficulties. Fruit or what-not may be
consigned to somebody acting as
broker He may pass it on to someone
to sell at auction. Who is the salesman,
the auctioneer who actually sells the produce or the broker who employs the
auctioneer to sell? We have to be clear
as to what person it is on whom we cast
the liability to render the account. There
is no difficulty about the terms of the
account. The account required is exactly
the same as that to which anybody who
deals in the Belfast or other market is
accustomed. We are not putting anything unreasonable on anybody, but I do
think we have left it a little doubtful
on whom we have placed the responsibility and towards whom it is to be
exercised.
I think that before we pass from this
Bill it will be necessary for us to consider
whether certain alterations are really
necessary. There are many alterations
that might be made, but one would not
wish to suggest anything unless it proves
on inquiry to be really necessary. I
understand it is not the intention of the
noble Viscount to ask us to take
any later
stage of the Bill before Easter, and it
might be possible before the Recess to
have some little conversation amongst
those interested in the Bill to see if any
alterations are really required to render
the English Measure adaptable to Northern Ireland, and, if so, the necessary
course to be taken. As regards the main
principle of the Bill one would naturally
support it if only for the reason that I
gave that it is calculated to prevent
further frauds.
Horticultural Produce Bill
Adjournment
6 APRIL 1927
hon.
Mr. DORMAN: Unlike the
Senator who has just sat down, I am
unable to criticise the structure of the
Bill which I have only just got possession
of, and have only been able to glance over
the first page.
If this Measure is a first
step towards the creation of legislation to
enable the Government to intervene with
a view of preventing the exorbitant profiteering that goes on in the necessary
food stuffs of the community in their
transit from the original producer to the
consumer it is well worthy of the support
of every Member of this House, and it
will commend itself to the community as
a whole.
There is no machinery at present whereby the Government could inform the
Senate in the interests of the community
of the extent of the profiteering going on
in this area or in any area of the great
Commonwealth of Nations of which we
form a part. I hope that ultimately
legislation will be introduced not merely
to control foodstuffs in their transit from
the producer to the consumer, but to see
that there is no wanton destruction of
foodstuffs on the part of producers for the
specific purpose of inflating prices.
mention this because I have recently been
in communication with a working man
who left this country and went to live in
a fruit-growing part of the Empire.
a letter which he wrote me from there he
stated:
I
In
As I write this letter at the window
there is within my view a dump of
from 400 to 600 tons of the finest fruit
ever grown destroyed and kept off the
market so as to inflate the price.
I really believe the legislature will
ultimately have to intervene and interest
itself in this matter, and I welcome a
Measure such as this simply because in my
opinion it will be a step towards creating
the machinery necessary to prevent this
undue profiteering against the most helpless part of the community, namely, the
working classes and the poor.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I thank
hon. Members who have spoken for
their support of the general principles of
the Bill, though for the purpose of my
reply I had better adhere strictly to the
Bill itself. As regards the points raised
by Senator Leslie, I hope the Committee
stage will clear up any difficulties or confusion that may result from the terms in
which the Bill is drafted. I do not propose to take the Committee stage until
after the Easter Recess.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Workmen's Compensation
(Miscellaneous Amendments) Bill [S.]
A Bill to amend the provisions of the
Workmen's Compensation Acts (Northern
Ireland), 1906 to 1923; and to give effect
in Northern Ireland to certain enactments
of the Workmen's Compensation Act,
1925, was presented by Viscount Charlemont, read a First time, and ordered to
be printed, and to be read a Second time
to-morrow. (Bill No. 2.)
Oral Answers to Questions.
SECONDARY TEACHERS'
PENSIONS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Glendinning:-
To ask the Leader of the House if,
in computing the retiring allowance of
Secondary teachers in Northern Ireland,
thirty years' service is reckoned as ten
years, and if a teacher retiring now after
forty years' service would be regarded as
having served only seventeen years; if
it is a fact that a teacher retiring in
England after forty years' service would
receive a pension equal to half his salary,
together with a lump sum of 40/30 of
his pensionable salary; whether the
101 Secondary Teachers' Pensions
Secondary Teachers' Pensions 102
26 APRIL 1927
Northern Government when introducing
the Pension Scheme offered a 21 per cent.
contribution from the State; and whether
they have since amalgamated the Superannuation Fund with other Teachers'
Superannuation Funds, and whether they
are no longer paying a 2 per cent. contribution into this Fund, and whether he
will state whether the "Secondary
Teachers' Superannuation Fund" still
exists as a separate Account.
Mr. GLENDINNING: In rising to ask
the question which is on the Order Paper
in my name I desire the House to understand that I am not making any attack
on the Government. At the same time
my sympathies are strongly with the
teachers, and I am acutely conscious of
the fact that my advocacy will fall very
much short of the merits of their case.
I have been brought a good deal in contact with Secondary School teachers,
particularly the older men, and I have
often heard their grievances fully and
ably discussed. They complain that considering their services in the past they
have been very badly treated, and they
maintain that as Irish schools have all
along been State-aided, they have even a
stronger claim to recognition than have
the English Secondary School teachers.
They, therefore, strongly protest against
the de-valuation system under which such
a pittance is given to them in the way
of pension.
Many of these gentlemen came over
here from England long ago. They have
always, as far as my experience goes,
been in sympathy with Unionist opinion
in this country and, to my own knowledge,
as far as their little resources went, they
materially supported that cause.
They
are now to a man hostile, and in some
cases, I very much regret to say, bitterly
hostile to the Government. On the other
hand I am fully alive to the necessity
for economy properly applied. I cannot
say anything as to the working of the
Ministry of Education or as to its financial relations, but I know that Lord
Londonderry is a fair-minded man, and
I am convinced that no one would be
more sorry than he if any provision of
the important Measure for which he was
so largely personally responsible should
leave grounds for great resentment and
discontent. If I may take the liberty of
being a little personal, the opinion I have
formed of the noble Lord, the Leader
of this House, and the Minister of
Education, leads me to hope that he will
find some grounds for sympathy with my
appeal.
As regards the series of questions I
have asked, which more or less explain
themselves, I do not intend to go into
any discussion of figures. I shall merely
consider the matter in general terms, and
very shortly, because the circumstances
of the case are known to everyone. There
are two aspects of the case. Secondary
School teachers are a very small but very
important section of the community.
They have got high mental attainments;
most of them, indeed, possess distinguished scholastic degrees. They perform
a duty to the State of the greatest consequence, but they are miserably paid
compared with other equally highlytrained people. As a business man I
know what commerce has suffered during
late years.
We also hear a great deal
about the hardships which agriculturists
have suffered and are suffering, and I
certainly should be very far from saying
that they are not deserving of the
greatest possible sympathy. But business
men and farmers can marry and bring up
a family, and many of them can send
their sons and daughters to universities,
and when they die they leave property
behind them.
A Secondary School teacher, if he is
not the headmaster of a good school, cannot afford to marry, or, if he does marry,
it is at the sacrifice of practically every
other interest and enjoyment of life. The
salary which he earns during his working
years is barely adequate to enable him to
make a decent appearance in ordinary
society, so that the most frugal of them
can make very little provision for the
future. If a teacher becomes incapacitated through infirmity or advancing
years the allowance he receives under the
present Act would not pay for his board
and lodgings. Women teachers
Secondary Schools are even worse off. I
know a case of a lady who retired a short
time ago and she received a pension of
£60 a year after thirty-five years'
devoted service. Compare this with what
an English Secondary School teacher gets.
She gets a substantial sum down and a
pension two and a half times greater
than the pension a teacher receives here.
in
It may be argued that these teachers
had no hope of a pension when they
voluntarily adopted the profession, but it
is a poor argument to say that because
Secondary Teachers' Pensions 104
Secondary Teachers' Pensions
THE SENATE
at
[Mr. Glendinning]
they were badly treated in the past they
should be equally badly treated now. As
a matter of fact, in the old days Secondary School teaching in Ireland could
not have been carried on at all except
the miserable pittance at which
teachers could be got in those days.
When the war came along it was unavoidable that a lot of these teachers should
run into debt, and they think these
things should be taken into account and
that some concession should be made to
them. I submit that the occasion calls
for some concession. If the Government
met these teachers in a fair spirit, a
liberal spirit, and even treated them
generously, it would not put an unduly
heavy burden on the Exchequer. After
all, there are not very many of them, and
retirements will occur very gradually, and
even if the Government did meet them
in a liberal spirit I do not think any
grave strain would be put upon the
finances of Northern Ireland. These are
the reasons which impelled me to raise
this matter when seconding the Address
in Reply to the Speech from the Throne,
and I am very glad the Leader of the
House has given me this further opportunity of expressing more fully my views
about the matter.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should
like to confine myself at the beginning
to merely answering the question on the
Paper. All service given by Secondary
teachers prior to 1st August, 1922, is
reckoned for pension purposes at onethird of its actual duration. Service
given subsequent to that date counts in
full. Accordingly, the hon. Senator is
correct in stating that a teacher retiring
now after forty years' service would be
regarded as having served seventeen
years for pension purposes.
As regards
the position of a teacher retiring in
England, the hon.
the hon. Senator states it
correctly.
With reference to the Government's
contribution to the Pension Scheme, it
was originally contemplated that, in
addition to meeting the whole cost of the
pension payable in respect of the period
prior to 1st August, 1922, the Government would make an annual contribution
equivalent to 2 per cent. of each
teacher's salary. Subsequently, by the
Teachers' Superannuation Act (Northern
Ireland), which was passed by this House
last
year, the system of financing
teachers' pensions, both Elementary,
Secondary, and Technical, was altered.
By this recent Act three separate funds
were amalgamated, although the Ministry
of Finance is required to keep subsidiary
accounts in respect of each class of
teacher. All contributions are now payable into, and benefits paid out of, a
single Teachers' Superannuation Fund,
and an annual Government contribution
is voted of a sufficient amount to meet
any deficit that is likely to arise on the
Fund by reason of the charges on it in
the year in question. From the point of
view of the teachers this should be a more
satisfactory arrangement, as it constitutes
an absolute guarantee that moneys will
be available to meet their pensions as
they emerge.
On the general question raised by the
hon. Senator, I do not think anybody
can refrain from having a great deal of
sympathy with Secondary School teachers,
to whom I would like to pay a tribute.
I think they are a most efficient body of
men and women, and in all my dealings
with them and I have had a good many
-I have had no reason to complain of
any bitterness with which they have put
their own
case forward. There have
been a good many letters in the Press
on the subject, and it is a fact that when
people air their grievances in the Press,
as they do, they very often put their
views with considerably more bitterness
than they do when they are met in a
more informal and friendly spirit. I
have endeavoured to my utmost to meet
the teachers in that spirit.
At the same time the economic aspects
of the case cannot be gainsaid. At the
time the Act was brought in the Government was in a very difficult position with
regard to ways and means, and it was
not possible to give the teachers a more
generous salary or pension. It must be
remembered that in England the salaries
of Secondary teachers are to a large extent rate-aided, whereas in this country
Secondary teachers' salaries are practically paid by the State. I should like to
say, however, that my right hon. Friend
the Minister of Finance is going into
actuarial computations on the whole
question. It will be understood that
these calculations take a considerable
time, but when the investigation is completed the result will be laid before both
Houses, and then I think hon. Members
both in this House and in the Lower
House will be in a better position to
appreciate the exact position.
Horticultural Produce Bill 106
Industrial Disputes
26 APRIL 1927
LAW RELATING TO INDUSTRIAL
DISPUTES.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay:
To ask the Leader of the House if it
is the intention of the Government to
introduce legislation to amend the law
relating to industrial disputes, and to
protect Northern Ireland against illegal
strikes.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to ask the noble
Leader of the House the Question which
stands in my name on the Order Paper.
I may say that my reason for putting
this question is that I understand that at
the present time a Bill dealing with this
matter is before the Imperial Parliament,
and as the head offices of the different
trade organizations in Northern Ireland
are in England it will be necessary that
we should have legislation on the same
lines as that in the Imperial Parliament.
The Bill before the Imperial House
does not extend to Northern Ireland, but
I understand that the Parliament of
Northern Ireland has power to legislate
on all questions of labour. Therefore, I
would like to know if it is the intention
of the Northern Government to follow
the Imperial Government in this important matter.
A few days ago the Prime Minister
of Great Britain said the objects of the
Bill introduced in the Imperial House
were to protect the nation as a whole
from industrial coercion-that is, from
another General Strike the restoration
to the individual Trade Unionist of freedom from victimisation, intimidation, and
unjustifiable pressure to contribute to a
political fund the object of which he may
be out of sympathy with. If it is necessary to pass legislation in Great Britain
to protect the people there from industrial coercion I say it is necessary that
we should have the same protection in
Northern Ireland. If the object of the
Bill is, as the Prime Minister stated, to
free the individual Trade Unionist from
intimidation and unjustifiable pressure to
contribute to a political fund, the object
of which he may be out of sympathy
with, it is equally necessary and important that the individual Trade Unionist
in Northern Ireland should receive
similar protection. Therefore, I hope the
noble Leader of the House will give a
favourable answer to my question and
will state that it is the intention of the
Government to bring in legislation on the
lines of the Bill in the Imperial Parliament.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
going to say even less on the subject
than the hon. Senator who has just
spoken. The answer is in the affirmative.
Horticultural Produce (Sales on
Commission) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move,
"That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that perhaps
this is a convenient opportunity to ask
the noble Viscount, the Leader of the
House, whether it is correct to assume
from the reference he made in speaking
on the Second Reading of the Bill, that he
intended to include in the expression
"fruit growing interests" the wholesaler
or other salesmen who are engaged in
handling the produce with which the Bill
deals. I think it will be obvious to us all
that if this Bill comes to us as an agreed
Measure between the people who grow
fruit and the people who are engaged in
actually selling it, we need not concern
ourselves particularly with the apparent
ambiguities which seem to occur in the
Measure before us, nor perhaps need we
lay too much stress on the inartistic expressions in the Bill.
On the occasion of the Second Reading
I was careful not to suggest any alteration
in the wording of the Bill because there
is a distinct reason why on this occasion
we should not interfere with the wording
if we can avoid it. It is partly because
the wording follows the English Act
exactly, and there is a possibility that
English produce might be coming over to
Northern Ireland for sale here, in which
case it would be better that the Acts
governing such matters should be the
same. Finally, and above all, if I read
correctly the speech made by the Minister
of Agriculture in another place, it is the
desire of the Government to have this
Bill actually on the Statute Book by 2nd
May, next Monday, when, I understand,
the Armagh apple sales are likely to
begin. If then, the noble Viscount can
Horticultural Produce Bill
THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
assure us that this is really in every sense
of the word an agreed Measure with these
parties it would perhaps facilitate the
of the Bill in a few minutes
progress
time.
The
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
hon. Senator is perfectly correct in what
he assumes. This is an agreed Measure
between the Government and the growers
and wholesale dealers, and for that reason
I think it would be inadvisable to alter
the Bill in any form.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1. (Duty of salesmen to
record and deliver particulars of sales.)
Mr. LESLIE: The only thing I desire
to say on Clause 1 is to draw attention for
a moment to the wording of the proviso
in Sub-clause 3, beginning on line 18.
The wording of that Sub-Clause is such
as to strike out on a new line. It is
wording to which we are not accustomed,
and I venture to express the hope that
the wording of this Clause will not be
used in future. It is not our own Clause.
We have not drafted it. The particular
point that ought to be noticed is the expression at the end of the proviso that a
defendant "shall be discharged from the
prosecution." That is an expression
which is not used in our Petty Sessions
Act, and this Act, if it is necessary for
the magistrates to take a hand in it-and
I very much doubt if it will be-will have
to be dealt with under our Petty Sessions
Act, and that Act does not contain that
expression. It means, of course, that the
man shall not be convicted, but the importance of using the more accustomed
phraseology seems to lie in this; that the
Petty Sessions Act gives certain powers
as regards costs in certain cases, and it is
always open to question, if a different
form of words is used, whether the ordinary practice is or is not to be followed.
I should have laid rather more stress
on this matter but for the fact that the
English Summary Jurisdiction Act, which
preceded our own by only two or three
years, contains very much the same
wording as our own as regards costs, and
it occurred to me that if this particular
proviso is considered unlikely to occasion
any difficulty in England, it can be presumed that it will not create any difficulty
in this country. Therefore, I do not
suggest any alteration in the Clause, but
will content myself with drawing attention to it.
The
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
Government are legally advised that the
expression, if not customary, is sound in
law. At the same time I agree it would
be undesirable that it should become a
precedent for any future legislation.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Inspection of books,
etc.), 3 (Interpretation), and 4 (Short
title) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
:
Ordered That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved: That this Report be now
received. (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill ordered to be read the Third time
to-morrow.

direct.

mind as to whether it will not be necessary to take the Third Reading of the
Com-
Agricultural Produce (Sales on
mission) Bill, before 2nd May.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think
the Motion to adjourn until such time
as Mr. Speaker shall direct will cover
that date.
Mr. SPEAKER: Might I suggest to

Omnibus Time-Tables
Omnibus Time-Tables
THE SENATE
Question to Minister.
OMNIBUS TIME-TABLES AND
SPEED LIMITS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he is aware that certain timetables, notifying times for departure and
arrival, to which it is not practical to
conform unless the speed limits fixed for
Public Service Vehicles by Regulation
under the Act of 1927 are disregarded,
have been published in Northern Ireland;
and, if so, whether in order to secure
compliance with the law and to protect
the drivers of such vehicles from that
suspension of licence to drive a motor-car
which must necessarily follow a third or
subsequent conviction for exceeding a
speed limit fixed by Regulation under the
Act of 1927, it is intended to take proceedings against those responsible for the
preparation and issue of such timetables.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid the question
standing in my name is of some length,
and, therefore, I will endeavour to atone
for that length by the brevity of my
remarks on the subject. It will be remembered that last Session Parliament
gave, or rather renewed, powers to the
Minister of Home Affairs to regulate the
speed of a certain class of motor vehicle,
for this purpose, omnibuses. These powers,
as might be expected, and as was, no
doubt, intended, have been exercised.
And I am afraid, perhaps, as was also
to be expected, it is found that drivers
of omnibuses do not conform to the speed
limits which were laid down in the Regulations made by the right hon. Gentleman. Consequently, those drivers, when
the police catch them, are summoned in
the ordinary way, convicted, and fined.
I do not propose to pillory any particular firm employing these motor omnibuses and the men driving them, but I
must point out that time-tables have been
issued and published in the newspapers
which, if they are conformed to, show
that two hours, let us say, is allowed for
a journey of a distance exceeding forty
miles. As the speed limit usually applicable to this class of vehicle is one of
twenty miles an hour it follows, even if
there are no stops at all for the service
of passengers, that the distance exceeding
forty miles has somehow to be covered
in two hours, and to do that it is obvious
that the speed limit of twenty miles an
hour must be exceeded. The drivers exceeding such speed limit are fined. It is
to be presumed that the time-tables are
issued to the drivers, and that they are
instructed to conform to them. At all
events the public expect that when they
see the time of arrival and departure
given in the time-table it will be kept,
and they take their passages accordingly.
But the unfortunate driver who exceeds
the speed limit is brought up and fined,
and it is provided that if a driver is convicted three times of exceeding the speed
limit fixed by the Regulation under the
Act, on his third conviction that man
must necessarily be sentenced, in accordance with the words of the Act, not only
to the minimum fine but also to have his
driving licence suspended for a period of
three months, and he is also liable to a
term of imprisonment with or without the
fine. That is, if a man on three occasions
is caught endeavouring to conform to
the time-table he must lose his licence
for three months. Probably the man is
an ex-Serviceman, with a wife and family
dependent upon him. I understand that
the majority of these drivers are
Servicemen, but in any case it is revolting
to our ordinary sense of justice that a man
should risk the loss of his licence because
he has conformed to orders which, after
all, are illegal.
ex-
It has happened to me quite recently
to be sitting on Benches in country districts, and on one occasion the same driver
was fined twice for two offences on the
same day, and on another occasion a man
was fined for going over a measured
quarter of a mile at the rate of 23 miles
and a few yards per hour. It is obvious
that if that man comes up again he must
lose his licence, and I think it is not
altogether equitable that the
the entire
penalty for a breach of the law-I am not
defending the breach of the law; the
speed limit is made in Parliament; I
am not arguing whether it is too low or
too high; it is a Parliamentary limit
and has to be conformed to-but it is
not fair that the penalty for the breach
of the law should fall entirely on the
employee when he is, in fact, as I suggest, induced to break the law by his
employer, who issues the time-table.
I do not know whether any steps have
been taken against the people responsible
10 MAY 1927
Omnibus Time-Tables

Omnibus Time-Tables
KC
case is brought to see that justice is done
as between servant and employer.
for issuing these time-tables. I should
imagine the law was strong enough. The
offence is neither a felony nor a misdemeanour, but the offence of not conforming to the Regulations is one for which
there is power to deal under the Petty
Sessions Act. I do not know whether
the Government will take any steps of
that sort, but it is in the interests not
only of the preservation of the law but
of the driver to prevent the issuing of
time-tables that cannot be complied with
without a breach of the law and without
the penalty falling on the innocent driver.
Mr. DORMAN: Many of these drivers.
feel that they are placed in an extraordinarily peculiar position owing to the
conditions that prevail in endeavouring
to keep to the time-tables laid down by
their employers. Many of the drivers
feel that in order to continue their right
to earn their living they have necessarily
to comply with the orders of their employers and reach the terminus in the
interests of their passengers at the times
set out in the time-tables. They have
many stops along the route, and they
necessarily cannot comply with the conditions laid down by the Act of Parliament if they are to meet the requirements
of their employers and the travelling
public. I do hope the Ministry of Home
Affairs will make an examination of these
time-tables, and if they do it will reveal
the fact that it is utterly impossible for
the drivers under the existing conditions
to comply with the law, and necessity
very often compels them against their
wish to run the risk of breaking the law
in preference to being victimized in
their employment in the manner which
Senator Leslie has so very ably drawn
attention to.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): The attention of the
Minister of Home Affairs has been drawn
to the time-tables which have been
mentioned by the hon. Members, and it
would appear from an examination of
these time-tables that it is impossible for
the buses to keep within the time-tables
and within the law. At the same time,
hon. Members must realize that it is a
very difficult thing for the Government
to legislate at the present moment in a
case where an owner makes his servant
offend against the Regulations. After
all, that might be done under any law
at present in existence, and it is really
the duty of the Bench before whom the
At the moment we cannot take any
proceedings under the present law against
owners of buses, but Part II of the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulations) Act,
Northern Ireland, 1926, when it comes
into operation on the appointed day,
which I think will be 1st July, will enable
proceedings to be taken against the owners
of buses who offend against the law, and
when framing the Regulations to be made
under this Act the Minister of Home
Affairs will consider whether it is possible
to include in the Regulations a provision
dealing with the matter referred to by
the hon. Members who have spoken. In
the meantime, if either of them know
of any special case where injustice has
been done in the manner suggested, and
they will bring it to my notice I shall
be very glad to forward it to the proper
authorities.

Omnibus Time-Tables
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
the Second Reading of the Bills read a

(Miscellaneous Amendments) Bill (S.)
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to refer
for a moment or two to the answer the
Leader of the House gave to my question.
I should like to draw attention to the fact
that if the new Regulations under Part
II of the Act, to which he referred, do
not come into operation until 1st July,
there is every possibility that some of the
drivers may have lost their licences for
three months. It is to that I would
direct attention, and I would suggest that
instructions might at once be issued to the
police that if they prosecute the driver
they should prosecute the employer also.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I cannot, of course, give a promise binding
another Ministry, but I will to-day bring
the matter before the attention of the
Ministry concerned.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes before Four o'clock until
Thursday next.
