Ed
to
a simple code suitable to the requirements
of the industry and readily available for
the information of employers and workers
alike.
The principal Amendments incorporated
in the Bill are as follows:-
(a) The definition of "quarries" to
which the code is made applicable is
altered so as to include all quarries;
(b) A definition of the term "occupier" is introduced so as to attach responsibility to persons working the
quarries who hitherto could not
definitely be held accountable;
(c) Provisions are introduced for
further safeguarding the use of steam.
boilers and machinery;
(d) Rules of general application are
introduced approximating closely to the
Special Rules which heretofore were
applied only in individual cases.
The
justification for this course is that the
ordinary precautions and rule of conduct necessary to ensure the safety of
the workers thus imposed will be placed
on record and brought to the notice of
all concerned in the operation of a
quarry.
The Bill does not impinge upon the
Workmen's Compensation Act (Northern
Ireland), 1923, except in the subject
matter embodied in Sections 25 and 26,
the general provisions whereof have been
re-enacted in Clause 6 in a form simpler
and more in consonance with quarry
conditions, or upon the Check Weighing
in Various Industries Act, 1919, or upon
the Employment of Women, Young Persons and Children Act, 1920.
It is not anticipated that the additional
work of inspection and administration
proposed by this Bill will call for the appointment of any additional staff. I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite sure the
administrative details of the Bill before us
are not likely to occasion this House any
trouble. The only point that occurs to me
as likely to be one which will require careful consideration is the point to which the
noble Viscount alluded at the very commencement of his speech, that is the
alteration in the definition of the term
"quarry." The word "quarry" as distinguished from mine is always taken to
mean a place where minerals are got at
from the surface rather than a place where
they are got at by a process of underground tunnelling, and if one thinks for
a moment of a common stone quarry and
compares it with a coal mine the difference is at once apparent.
But the Bill proposes to definitely alter
the limitation of a quarry which was put
in the Act of 1894 as regards depth of
feet, and the point this House will have
to watch with great care is that we are not
introducing what I might call a "farm"
quarry, the little stone quarry which is to
be found on many farms throughout the
Province, or the little gravel pit--that we
are not introducing there a code of Rules
and Regulations which would be in no
way applicable. There is a very wide difference between the twenty feet in the Act
of 1894 and these small quarries or pits.
I do not know how it will be thought well
to deal with that, whether by inserting as
a qualification a quarry in which not more
than four to five feet is worked below the
surface.
At any rate we shall have to exercise
great care, because in the ordinary farmyard occupation of ploughing you not only
come upon small stones but you often disclose the presence of boulders of some size.
In that case do the workers on that farm
resort to that place for the purpose of
getting stone, which the owner of the farm
would probably afterwards use to mend
roads? They do resort to that place and
get stone and we must see that we do not
inadvertently turn that field into a quarry
or the yard where the stone breaking is
subsequently done into a place adjacent to
a quarry. No doubt, the Government will
look into that matter and tell us later how
it is proposed to guard against these possibilities.
There is just one other point I should
like to mention, because the noble Viscount alluded to it. If we look at the
Second Schedule we find a reference to
Sections 25 and 26 of the Workmen's Compensation Act (Northern Ireland), 1926.
A consolidating Bill dealing with Workmen's Compensation is about to engage
our attention, and I thought it might save
time if I pointed out that these two particular sections referred to in the Schedule
of this Bill are exempt from the general
Quarries Bill
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
repeal which will be found in the Schedule
of the other Bill. Taking the Bill as a
whole, there is nothing to occasion objection, but I hope the points I have raised
will not be lost sight of.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Senator Leslie has asked part of the question I was going to put to the Leader of
the House. It refers to the meaning of
the word "quarry." I thought the Bill
might be prejudicial to County Councils,
and especially those that employ direct
labour and are the renters either by right
of law or by agreement with the tenant of
certain stone quarries. The County of
Down, which many people say ought to
be called the County of Up and Down,
has many of these stone quarries but,
except in the case of one or two of the best
known quarries, most of the stone we
raise is not got from below the surface at
all. 75 per cent. of the stone we get is
obtained from eruptions of rock or stone
above the ground, and I should like to
know if it is the intention that this kind
of quarry should
come within the provisions of this Bill. I do not think they
should be interfered with, and if they are
it will require a large number of inspectors
to supervise them. We have no really
deep quarries in County Down.
The only other point I should like to
mention is this. This is the age of officialdom, and I hope the noble Leader of the
House will tell us how many inspectors it
is proposed to appoint under this Bill, or
that he will at any rate give the House
an assurance that the number to be appointed will be limited.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to welcome this
Bill because it is bringing under the protection of the law a number of workmen
who hitherto have not been protected by
Act of Parliament. I happen to be a
representative of a party that is continuously accused of preaching what is
commonly termed "class war," and there is
always held up to us the importance of
individual initiative and incentive for
gain and competition, a trinity of ideals
we are accused of being out to destroy.
Since I have taken my seat in this House
it has struck me that of the legislation
which the Government, as the representatives of the public and the safeguarders
of their health and well-being, have
found it absolutely necessary to bring before us, nine-tenths has been found
necessary because of this particular trinity
that is always being held up for our admiration and as being essential for the
welfare of society.
This Measure is nothing more or less
than an attempt on the part of the legislature to mitigate the suffering of a class
that has hitherto not been protected and
that is now going to have extended to it
the protection of the law. What for? To
protect it from greed and desire and a deliberate attempt to obtain gold and other
money at the risk of the life, limb, and
health of those who from day to day are
engaged in attending the necessary machinery to carry on the business.
I am surprised that a number of the
Members of the Government of Northern
Ireland and their supporters are from
platforms continuously endeavouring to
induce the people of Northern Ireland to
believe that this small party, the
Socialist party, is out to disseminate the
doctrine of class war. We are not out for
anything of the kind, but we are out for
the specific purpose of seeing to it that the
unfortunate working class are not overlooked and neglected. Not only the
legislature of Northern Ireland but the
legislatures of every civilized country in
the world are from day to day engaged
in introducing legislation to mitigate the
sufferings that necessarily accrue from
that particular warfare that we are out to
abolish if possible. I welcome this Bili
simply because its purpose is to protect
the most helpless army engaged in that
class war from accidents due to the negligence of those who have charge of this
machinery.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
would like to ask the noble Leader of the
House if the provisions of this Bill will
apply to well-sinking. In order to sink
wells we often have to cut down through
stone, and I hope the Bill will not interfere with farmers who sink wells to get
spring water.
CHARLEMONT:
I
VISCOUNT
would prefer to postpone until a later
stage of the Bill the answers to some of
the points that have been raised during
the course of the debate. It would be extremely difficult for me to define the dif
ference between a quarry from which
stones bigger than one could lift are obtained and a quarry from which stones
used by County Councils are procured. I
will consider that, as well as the question
Motor Vehicles Act-
Endorsement of Licences
27 OCTOBER 1927
of well-sinking, when we reach the Committee stage of the Bill. I think I should
prefer not to enter into the question of
class war in relation to a Quarries Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
You did not reply to my query regarding
the number of inspectors to be appointed.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
furnish the answer to that question on the
Committee stage.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation)
Act.
ENDORSEMENT OF LICENCES.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Hill
Dickson:
To ask the Leader of the House if he
will state whether any convictions took
place as a result of the misleading circular
which was issued by the Ministry of Home
Affairs in respect of the Motor Vehicles
(Traffic and Regulation) Act, 1926; and,
if so, whether any steps are being taken
to remedy any injustice so done
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In putting the
question which appears in my name on the
Paper I desire to say that the word "conviction" should read "endorsement." It
was not the question of convictions I was
so much interested in. I do not mind a
man being convicted for committing an
offence, but when a circular is issued to
magistrates by the Ministry of Home
Affairs which makes it appear to magistrates that when they have decided to convict they must endorse the person's licence,
and when the terms of that circular are
afterwards upset by the King's Bench
Division on appeal and the circular is
subsequently withdrawn by the Ministry
of Home Affairs, I think it is very unfair
to the persons who may have had their
licences endorsed in consequence of that
circular. If the magistrates had not received this circular and had not been
under the impression that they were bound
to endorse an offender's licence it might
not have been endorsed. When people
who have had their licences so endorsed
go to seek employment elsewhere the first
thing the prospective employer asks is to
see their licence, and when he sees a
licence has an endorsement on it, it militates against the man's chance of employ
ment.
SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Colonel
And quite right, too.
The
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
hon. Member having made it clear that
the word "conviction" was wrongly used
in the question, and that he refers to endorsements, I will omit the first part of
the answer to the question, which was
based on the assumption that "conviction" was the word intended to be used.
From returns furnished to the Ministry it appears that between the dates on
which the two circulars were issued, 208
licences were endorsed. It is impossible
to say how many of these were endorsed
by the magistrates, by reason of the view
expressed by the Ministry that endorsement was compulsory. It may be admitted that in all probability a considerable proportion were so endorsed.
The question appears to suggest that
an injustice has been done, but this suggestion would seem to result from a misapprehension as to the effect of an endorsement of a licence under the Act.
Many people, no doubt, think that an
endorsement under this Act is analogous
to an endorsement under the Intoxicating
Liquor Acts, but this is quite fallacious.
Under the Intoxication Liquor Act it is
only convictions which are actually endorsed on the licence that entail consequences if the holder is subsequently
convicted. In other words if a
licence-holder upon whose licence
two convictions have been endorsed is convicted a third time, his licence is forfeited. An endorsement under the
Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulations)
Act has no such effect.
The increased penalties for second or
subsequent offences depend on previous
convictions whether they are endorsed or
whether they are not. If the previous
conviction is not endorsed it is the duty
of the police to inform the court thereof
at the proper time, and to offer proof of
it. Endorsement on the licence is merely
a convenient form of record, which enables the previous conviction to be easily
proved, but it does not in any way preclude proof of the previous conviction
which has not been endorsed.
223 Motor Vehicles Act-
Endorsement of Licences 224
THE SENATE
Viscount Charlemont]
The convictions in these 208 cases did
not in any way depend upon the Ministry's
circular, and whether they were endorsed
or were not endorsed, the police, in the
case of subsequent conviction would be
bound to bring them to the notice of the
magistrates and to prove them in the
ordinary way.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of what has
just been said, I may perhaps remark
that I am very glad to find that a considerable interest is taken in this House
in the matter to which the question of
my hon. Friend refers. It has just been
admitted that in no less than 208 cases
something may have been done which
would not have been done but for a mistake made in the Ministry of Home
Affairs. We were told the day before
yesterday that the circular was issued on
the advice of the Ministry's legal advisers,
and I can only hope that those legal advisers, whoever they may be, will feel
something in the nature of repentance
when they learn of the consequences of
their mistaken action.
To suggest that there is a popular idea,
a popular mistake, of the effect of an
endorsement under the Motor Acts, and
that the situation is in any way comparable with that under the Intoxicating
Liquor Acts seems to me to be somewhat
absurd. The confusion, if any, does not
seem to be in the mind of the general
public. I am afraid I must say that I
think some confusion
as regards this
matter has been in the minds of the Parliament of the United Kingdom and the
Parliament of Northern Ireland. I venture to say, when speaking on the Second
Reading of the Bill of 1926, that I had
never been able to clearly appreciate with
what object licences under the 1903 Act
were endorsed, because however many
endorsements a man had on his licence
under that Act, it did not in the least
prevent his getting another driving
licence. County councils had no power
to refuse to issue another licence merely
because his previous licence was plastered
with endorsements.
But there is this effect. If a man is
seeking employment and his licence is
endorsed he is far less likely to get that
employment; in fact the endorsement of
a licence is, as I think the Lord Chief
Justice said, a penal provision. If
magistrates do become aware of the endorsement of a licence, a fact of which they
should be and are kept in the most complete ignorance until the question of conviction or acquittal is decided, they are
likely to consider, and I venture to say
I think they should consider, whether the
previous offence has been of so serious a
charcter that the bench who tried it felt
it right to endorse the licence, and we
should consider whether that does not lead
them to consider the imposition of a more
severe penalty than the minimum penalty
which happens to be provided in the Act
Certainly I, myself, would give effect to
that consideration.
The whole question of the production
of these licences in court seems to me to
be one of great difficulty. It is universally admitted that until a conviction has
been decided upon a licence cannot be
produced. It is equally universally held
that if a licence is produced to the court
it is the best possible evidence for satisfying the requirements of the Prevention of
Crimes Act, as being an abstract of the
proceedings of another court, and it
usually settles the question of identity.
But our 1926 Act does not provide any
satisfactory method of getting the licence
before the court. There is a provision
that the holder of a licence must produce the licence in court if required, and
the requirement can only be by the court
itself. I think, reading our Act of 1926
with its predecessor of 1903, we are driven
to the conclusion that the only purpose
for which a court can possibly call for the
production of the licence is so that it may
be endorsed, in which case the licence in
most cases would never get to the bench
at all, but would simply be handed to the
clerk, who would make the necessary endorsement.
If a court were to require a man whom
they had just convicted to produce his
licence, to see whether or not it was endorsed, it seems to me they would be requiring the man to give evidence against
himself. As an old writer said—and I
am sure this will be familiar to the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Sharman Crawford)--
'Magistrates must be very careful as
regards the common law lest it break
in upon them and level their proceedings.
I think that is one of the classics with
which magistrates ought to invest themselves. On the other hand it is obviously
Workmen's Compensation Bill. 226
Motor Vehicles Act
27 OCTOBER 1927
most convenient that the licence should
be produced and if, as I have heard, a
police officer asks a man in court for his
licence, although I do not think the man
is bound to give it to him, if he does in
fact give it to him, the court cannot interfere to prevent the police officer from using it.
Therefore I hope those points
will attract the attention of the House or
the legislature, but in the meantime I
think we are satisfied that due care will
be taken in future to render, as far as
possible, absolutely impossible the recurrence of any mistake of this sort, which
has such far reaching consequences for so
many comparatively innocent people.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
had not intended to intervene in this debate, but Senator Leslie always seems to
have the faculty of provoking me to get
up to say something.
I cannot agree
with his views on this question at all.
The number of endorsements on licences
that has been quoted by the leader of the
House cannot be wholly ascribed to the
circular issued by the Ministry of Home
Affairs Speaking for the bench on
which I sit, I can say that 90 per cent.
of the endorsements put on licences are
put on solely by the desire of the bench,
and not in consequence of the circular
issued by the Ministry. I think it is
most desirable that in every case of a first
offence the licence should be endorsed.
In the end it is the quickest and easiest
way, because otherwise the police might
have to spend some considerable time
searching the records to ascertain whether
a man had been before the court before
or not.
I also think it is in the interests of the
motor driver himself, because if his
licence is not endorsed for a first offence
he might go and commit similar offences
in other parts of the country, where they
would be regarded as first offences.
hope all benches of magistrates will follow the example of my bench, and endorse
licences for all first offences.
I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think
I can give the assurance Senator Leslie
asks for that as far as the Home Office
can preclude the possibility of anything
of the same nature happening again they
will do so.

accordingly,

(c) a member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary Force; or

Mr. LESLIE: Before I move the first
of the Amendments which stand in my
name, as the one who had the honour to
act as Chairman of the Joint Committee,
there are two things I should like to say.
First I should like to remind the Committee that when speaking on the Second
Reading of this Bill I drew attention to
two matters, the cases of adopted children
and a special class of illegitimate children,
particularly the illegitimate girl between
the ages of 15 and 16. Those matters, I
ought to tell the House, were carefully
considered by the Joint Committee, and
it was decided that the Bill before us
made no alteration in the existing law,
and that those two questions were in no
way affected by the Bill before us. That
really appears in the second paragraph
of the Report laid on the Table yesterday,
with special reference to the cases at issue.
The other matter on which I should like
to speak on behalf of the Committee is to
express our great obligation to Sir Arthur
Queckett, partly for the careful marginal
notes with which he furnished us and
which shortened our task very much, and
partly for the evidence he gave before us
and the memorandum he submitted.
Without that assistance our task, as indeed I think you, Mr. Deputy-Chairman,
will agree as you were a member of the
Committee, would have been very much
more onerous, and I am not sure that we
H
227 Workmen's Compensation Bill-
THE SENATE

Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
would have discharged it so well as we
hope we have done.
Passing to the first Amendment, I
move that in Clause 3, page 4, line 10,
to leave out the word "Force" (Royal
Ulster Constabulary Force). It is merely
because the expression Royal Ulster Constabulary is the proper expression, being
the expression used in the Statute establishing the Force. I beg to move.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I should like
to take this opportunity of very heartily
concurring with the remarks of Senator
Leslie as regards the great assistance Sir
Arthur Queckett gave the Joint Committee. The fact of the matter is that
the matters which came before the Committee were largely of a legal character,
and the time of the Committee was largely
saved owing to the fact that we had in
the chair a lawyer of the ability of
Senator Leslie, and another able legal
representative in the person of Mr. J. C.
Davison, K.C., M.P. Both these Members had taken the trouble to go carefully
through all the previous Bills dealing with
this subject, and they had compared them
very thoroughly with the Bill now before
the House. That action saved the Committee a git deal of time. The
memorandum and notes which Sir Arthur
Queckett placed before us were also of the
greatest assistance, and I readily concur
with Senator Leslie in paying that tribute to Sir Arthur Queckett.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Dependants entitled to
compensation), 5 (Meaning of "employer'), 6 Liability in case of Workmen.
employed by contractors), 7 (Provision
as to cases of bankruptcy of employer),
8 (Amount in fatal cases), 9 (Amount in
cases of total or partial incapacity), 10
(Rules for determining earnings), 11 (Reviews of weekly payments), 12 (Limitation of power of employer to end or
diminish weekly payments) and 13 (Redemption of weekly payments), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 14 (Requirements as to
notice of accident and claim for compensation).
Provided that-
(a) the want of or any defect or inaccuracy in such notice shall not be
a bar to the maintenance of such
proceedings if the employer is proved
to have had knowledge of the accident from any other source at or
about the time of the accident, or if
it is found in the proceedings for
settling the claim that the employer
is not, or would not, if a notice or
an amended notice were then given
and the hearing postponed, be prejudiced in his defence by the want,
defect or inaccuracy or that such want,
defect or inaccuracy was occasioned
by mistake, absence from the United
Kingdom, or other reasonable cause;
and
(b) the failure to make a claim within
the period above specified shall not
be a bar to the maintenance of such
proceedings if it is found that the
failure was occasioned by mistake,
absence from the United Kingdom,
or other reasonable cause.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move the following Amendments standing in my

Page 53, leave out "Second Schedule"
and insert "Part II." Leave out "Provisions as to Payments into Court and
Investment" and insert "Paragraph 3 of
the Second Schedule to the Workmen's
Compensation Act, 1925."
Now we come to what is really our
chief trouble. As the Bill is before us
we see in large type "Second Schedule."
If you look further down you will see
"Sixth Schedule." It was found by
every member of the Joint Committee that
to leave in the expression "Second Schedule" was most misleading, because it is
not made immediately apparent that this
Second Schedule was formerly referred to
as the Fifth Schedule. Accordingly we
propose to leave out "Second Schedule"
and insert" Part II," and then also to
leave out the sub-heading and to insert
the words we took out in the early part of
the Schedule. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Schedule, as amended, agreed to.
PREAMBLE.
Whereas it is expedient to consolidate, as respects Northern Ireland, the
law relating to compensation to workmen for injuries suffered in the course
of their employment:
And whereas the enactments relating
to such compensation as aforesaid, so
far as they were enacted by the Parliament of the United Kingdom, contain,
in relation to Northern Ireland, certain
provisions as to matters in respect of
which the Parliament of Northern Ireland have not power to make laws (in
this Act referred to as "reserved provisions"):
And whereas it is expedient, for the
purpose of consolidating the law as
aforesaid, that the reserved provisions
should be set out in the Fourth Schedule to this Act in the manner in which
they are applicable to matters arising
under the provisions of this Act:
Be it therefore enacted by the King's
most Excellent Majesty, and the Senate
and the House of Commons of Northern
Ireland in this present Parliament
assembled, and by the authority of the
same, as follows:-
*
*
*
*
*

words after the word "Whereas" to the
end of the paragraph and to insert instead
thereof the words
"certain provisions (in this Act referred to as 'the reserved provisions')
contained in the enactments relating to

This is merely a verbal alteration. It
was thought that the words on the Paper
rang better than the words in the Bill,

Mr. LESLIE: On page 2 of the Freamble I beg to move, to leave out the first
five lines and to insert instead thereof--
"and whereas it is expedient for convenience of reference that certain of the
reserved provisions, namely those which
relate to appeals to the Court of Appeal
and House of Lords, and to the investment of certain sums in the Post Office
Savings Bank, should be set out in the
Fourth Schedule to this Act in the
manner in which they are applicable to
matters arising under the provisions of
this Act, and that such of the reserved
provisions as are contained in the Workmen's Compensation Act, 1925, passed
by the Parliament of the United Kingdom, should be set out in the Fifth
Schedule to this Act."
That is really the third paragraph of
the Preamble. The Committee felt it was
better that we should bring in a distinct
reference to appeals to the Court of Appeal
and the House of Lords, and to investments
in the Savings Bank, which we have dealt
with in the Fourth Schedule, and also that
we should say what the Fifth Schedule
was to be about. Accordingly, we propose
the alteration as shown on the Paper, and
which we think will make for clearness.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Preamble, as amended, agreed to.
Title agreed to.

Moved: That this House do
adjourn.-(Viscount Charlemont.)
now
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Can the noble Leader of the House inform
us on what days the House will sit next
week, and the nature of the business to
come before us.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps before the noble
Viscount replies to that question I may
be permitted to make something in the
nature of a personal explanation. Hon.
Members who were here yesterday will be
aware that when speaking on the Second
Reading of the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill, I made a reference to the
Local Government Emergency Powers
Act, 1921. Senator Dorman made references which I think were not only to
that Statute but also
to the Civil
The
Authorities (Special Powers) Act.
two Statutes are entirely distinct in every
way and my remarks were limited to the
Local Government (Emergency Powers)
Act, but most unfortunately the morning
papers published in Belfast so reported
both of us as to make it appear that the
remarks of both Senators referred to the
same matter.
I wish to make it absolutely clear that
I was referring solely to the Local Government (Emergency Powers) Act, as indeed
would appear from the reference I made
to the Local Government Act of 1898.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
sorry the hon. Senator was mis-reported.
Mr. LESLIE: But not misunderstood.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: With
regard to the business for next week, on
Tuesday the House will go into Committee
on the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill,
and on Wednesday, the Committee Stage
of the Sale of Milk Bill will be taken.
On Thursday the Report stages of those
Bills will be proceeded with.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes after
Four o'clock until
Tuesday next.
239 Expenses of Educ. Authorities
Expenses of Educ. Authorities 240
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 1st November, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. JOSEPH CUNNING-
HAM (Deputy Speaker) in the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
The Northern Ireland Winter Assize
Order, 1927.
Presented (pursuant to Act), and
ordered to lie on the Table.
Ministry of Home Affairs (Provisional
Orders Confirmation) Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on the Bill:-"That
they had considered the said Bill, and
examined the allegations thereof, which
were found to be true, and the Committee
had gone through the Bill, and directed
the same to be reported without Amendment to the House."
Expenses of Education Authorities-
Amending Regulations.
MOTION FOR ADDRESS.
The following Motion stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Viscount
Bangor :-
That an Humble Address be presented
to his Grace the Governor of Northern
Ireland, praying that the Regulations,
dated 26th September, 1927, made by the
Ministry of Education, under Sections 73,
76 and 77 of the Education Act (Northern
Ireland), 1923, with the approval of the
Ministry of Finance, and entitled Parliamentary Grant in aid of Expenses of
Education Authorities for the year
1927-28 Amending Regulation No. 1"
may be annulled.
""
VISCOUNT BANGOR: With regard
to the Motion on the Paper which stands
in my name, I should like, if I may be
allowed, to give this House a slight explanation of the events which led to my
giving notice of this Motion. The whole
matter has arisen owing to the placing on
the Table of both Houses of Parliament
of the Regulation to amend Article 3 of
the Parliamentary Grant to Education
Authorities Regulations, 1927-1928. The
Amendment or Proviso reads as follows:
Payments made in respect of capital
expenditure incurred on works or equipment, whether such payments are made
by way of interest on, or repayment of,
loans obtained to meet such expenditure
or otherwise, shall not rank as part of
the approved expenditure of an education authority unless the expenditure
on such works or equipment was incurred under the lowest tender received
therefor by the education authority or
unless the Ministry, in any case, authorizes such capital expenditure to be incurred otherwise than as aforesaid.
This proviso, however, shall not apply
to payments made in respect of capital
expenditure incurred under contract
entered into by education authorities
prior to 1st December, 1927.
The Regional Education Committee of
County Down, over which I have the
honour to preside, were very dissatisfied
with this procedure, and it was decided
that a representative deputation should
approach the Minister of Education to explain their views. This deputation was
as representative as possible, and was very
kindly and courteously received by the
noble Viscount, the Leader of the House,
on Wednesday, 19th October, and they
expressed to his lordship that they were
pressing the claims of those who were
empowered to speak for the whole county.
We represented that we had a concrete
case when the committee disagreed with
the Ministry of Education as regarded the
acceptance of a tender. I refer to the
tender for the enlargement of a certain
technical school.
In this case the lowest tender was not
taken by the Regional Committee, and for
very good reasons which I shall be quite
prepared to give if necessary. The difference between the lowest and next lowest
tender on a contract of between £3,000
and £4,000 was under £80, which was
subsequently reduced by an amendment
241 Expenses of Educ. Authorities

1 NOVEMBER 1927
Expenses of Educ. Authorities
of the lowest tender to a difference of
about £50. The matter was referred to
the Ministry of Education, who passed
it on to the Ministry of Finance, and the
latter said that it considered that the
lowest tender in this case should be
accepted. The Ministry of Education
concurred, but the Regional Committee
was not definitely ordered to accept the
lowest, or any tender at all. The next
lowest was accordingly accepted, and when
the item came before the Local Government Auditor he approved fully of the
reasons given by the Committee for the
acceptance of the next lowest tender, and
there was no question of any surcharge.
I merely quote this case because, on the
occasion of the deputation to the Ministry of Education, we were informed that
it was this case which gave rise to the
amendment of Article 3 of the Parliamentary grant. The deputation explained
to the noble Viscount, the Minister of
Education, that in their opinion the imposition of such a Regulation indicated
that the Ministry had little confidence in
the judgment of either Local Education
Authorities, or Local Government Auditors, that the members of the Education
Committees gave their energies, time, and
abilities, entirely gratuituously, and that
they used their best endeavours to safeguard the ratepayers in every way. They
further expressed the opinion that Regulations such as these, instead of fostering
local interest .in matters educational,
would most successfully stifle it. They
also said the lowest tender is not, ipso
facto, the best, nor is it always the cheapest or most economical in the long run.
We
The deputation finally asked that the
Regulation might be withdrawn.
were most courteously and kindly received,
but I can only say that our efforts were not
successful. I do not wish to weary the
House with a long narrative, but I want
to give a perfectly straightforward account
of these events, and the reason for my
present action in the matter. Our
Director of Education was then instructed
to communicate with the Committee's
Solicitor, who advised that the matter
Accorshould be raised in this House.
dingly, a sub-Committee, mainly composed of members of the deputation, was
formed, and this Committee was granted
an interview with the Prime Minister,
with the object of securing an amendment
to the Regulations which would be acceptable to the Regional Committee and to
the Ministers of Finance and Education,
and, in the event of such amendments
being favourable, the withdrawal of the
Motion now before the House.
The sub-Committee put their case before the Prime Minister, and were very
favourably and sympathetically received,
and they left under the impression that
the Regulations would be amended so as
to give the Local Education Authority
discretionary powers in respect of tenders.
to cover cases of new constructions without limit as to amount, but with a
limit on the discretion of the Committee,
either in the shape of a fixed sum, or of
a sliding scale of percentages, according
to the amount of the contract.
I was in hopes that we should have
received Amendments which would have
enabled me to-day to have asked the leave
of the House to withdraw this Motion,
but I can only tell you that the text of
the amendments, when they reached us,
merely gave the Committee discretionary
power over contracts not exceeding £500,
which would only include ordinary maintenance or repair work. I am sorry to
say these amendments were wholly unacceptable, and therefore, I felt that my
only course was to submit the Motion
which stands in my name.
Accordingly,
I beg to move.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I rise to support the Motion of the noble Lord who has
just sat down. In the days before the
passing of the Education Act the Commissioners of National Education had charge
of the education in the country, and at
that time the repairs and other work in
connection with schools was usually done
by the managers in charge, except in the
case of those schools which the Commissioners themselves had charge of. In the
case of those schools the Commissioners
sent down an architect from the Board
of Works if a slate came off a roof. A
specification was prepared and tenders
were invited, and perhaps in about six
months' time the slate was put on again
at about three or six times the cost at
which the work could have been done
locally.
The whole spirit and intention of the
Education Act passed by this Parliament
was to place this work in the hands of the
local Committees, and I do not think it
was ever the spirit or intention of the Act
that Regulations should be framed and
Expenses of Educ. Authorities THE SENATE
Expenses of Educ. Authorities
[Mr. Dickson]
passed through Parliament at the instance
of either the Ministry of Education, or the
Ministry of Finance, Regulations similar
to the circular we discussed in this House
last week, which was issued by the Ministry of Home Affairs, directing magistrates to endorse licences under the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Act,
and which was afterwards proved to be
altogether illegal. If the Regulations
which have been issued by the Ministry
of Education are enforced, it will give
the Ministry power to insist that the
lowest tender shall be accepted, irrespective of the knowledge which the local
people may possess.
Why should a Regional Committee be
treated in a different manner to a Board
of Guardians, a Rural District Council,
or any other local authority? They all
have power to use their own discretion in
these matters, and I do not think officials
have any right to treat people who are
giving their time to the work of education in this red tape manner.
Of course,
it may be said that the Government give
£66 out of every £100 that is spent, but
that comes from the taxpayer, and the
other £33 comes from the ratepayer. It
makes very little difference, because all the
money comes from the people of Northern Ireland. I think that by their
superior knowledge of local conditions the
members of Regional Committees have
a right to be allowed to use their own
discretion in matters of this kind without
being harassed by Regulations such as
those which this Motion seeks to annul.
I have great pleasure in supporting the
Motion.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): The noble Lord has given
an account of this matter from his point
of view, and I should like to give the
House a little precis from the point of
view of the Ministry of Education. The
County Down Regional Committee had
obtained the authority of the Ministry for
raising a loan for making these alterations to Banbridge Technical School, and
having considered the tenders submitted,
they decided to accept the second lowest
tender, and they requested the authority
of the Ministry for proceeding with the
work. The Ministry, after consultation,
did not see any reason why other than the
lowest tender should be accepted, and
they gave this opinion to the Committee
who, nevertheless, proceeded with the
work.
The Local Government Auditor, when
auditing the Committee accounts, was con
fronted with the fact that the Committee
had acted in opposition to the advice of
the Ministry, and he had an interview
with the Ministry to find out what their
views in the matter were. He pointed
out that in ordinary circumstances he had
no power to disallow expenditure incurred
under contracts, or approved, for
other than fraudulent or improper
conduct. That was rather news to the
Ministry, because they thought he had
far greater powers than that. It is
obvious that fraudulent reasons for
accepting a contract are at the present
time non-existent, and we hope they will
be in the future. The Ministry were
under the impression that the Auditor
had power to disallow expenditure on the
ground of indiscretion or extravagance in
accepting contracts, and their discovery
that this was not so was the reason for the
issue of this amending Regulation.
The
The object of this Regulation is that
expenditure incurred by a local authority
on buildings or equipment shall not rank
as approved expenditure unless the lowest
tender is accepted, or the Ministry is
satisfied that there is very good reason
to depart from the lowest tender. A
draft of these Regulations was sent to
the Association of Education Authorities
more than two months ago. This Association comprises in its membership representatives of the Regional Committees in
Ulster, but although this draft of the
Regulations was sent, I think, on 25th
August, we have received no communication from them on the subject.
Chairman of this Association, Senator
Armstrong, will, no doubt, be able to explain what happened when the matter was
discussed by that body, but I have received no official communication from it.
I should like to add also that this
amended Regulation has passed through
another place without any objection being
taken to it. No doubt the noble Viscount
felt that as he was in negotiation with the
Prime Minister and the Ministry of Education his hands were tied in the matter,
but that did not apply to the representatives of other Regional Committees who
are represented in another place.
As the Members of this House know,
the Ministry pays out of public funds.
two-thirds of all approved expenditure
of education authorities up to the produce of a shilling rate. If a contract
245 Expenses of Educ. Authorities 1 NOVEMBER 1927
Expenses of Educ. Authorities 246
costs £9,000 the Ministry pays £6,000,
and I think it is encumbent on the Ministry to see that the best value is obtained
for its money, otherwise the Public
Accounts Committee might very possibly
arraign the Ministry of Education for
maladministration of public funds.
It is true the Auditor of the Ministry
of Home Affairs goes through the accounts
of education authorities, but his function
is confined, as I have explained already,
to ascertaining whether these tenders are
accepted bona fide, and if he detects a
case where, in his opinion, negligence or
extravagance only has been exhibited, he
cannot surcharge the offending body, but
can only draw attention to the matter.
But the Comptroller and Auditor-General
will want to know why the Ministry paid
over public moneys to education authorities without satisfying itself that these
moneys were expended to the best advantage.
There is nothing at all in this Regulation directed against the County Down
Regional Committee. On the contrary,
I think that at the present moment the
Ministry of Education look upon that
Committee with feelings of the greatest
gratitude, because they have drawn the
Ministry's attention to what, I think, is
rather a serious defect in the Act as it
stands at the moment. Of course, we are
not suggesting anything against other
Regional Committees elsewhere. This
Regulation is a safeguard for the future.
Circumstances might arise where
Regional Committee might be guilty of
extravagance or negligence, and in such
a case I think it is only right that public
moneys should be protected. I might
draw the attention of the House to the
fact that there are many other examples
in public life where safeguards of this
nature are imposed without anyone feeling a great resentment against them.
a
Some time ago Senator Hill Dickson
and myself had the pleasure of sitting on
an Industrial Commission which sat for
two or three years, and whenever we put
in a charge for our railway tickets it had
to be countersigned by the Secretary of
the Commission, who gave his word that
we were at a certain place on a certain
date when we said we were. I do not
know that I felt very much offended by
this rule I put in my claim and the Secretary signed it and I got my return ticket,
and I hope Senator Hill Dickson did also,
We did not feel that any unfair imposition had been placed upon us, nor were we
in any way offended by this rule, and I do
not think it is unreasonable that a similar
Regulation should be applied to public
bodies.
I would like to add that the Ministry
of Finance is not mentioned in this
amending Regulation, but it is possible
the members of the Down Regional Committee are concerned with the question
of delay and the conflict of opinion between
themselves and the Ministry of Education.
I have already given to the noble Viscount
(Viscount Bangor) my personal assurance,
which I should like to repeat in this House,
that I am willing to give a guarantee that
I myself will investigate personally any
such case that may arise in the future,
and I undertake to do so with the greatest
possible despatch. I regret that for the
reasons I have given I must oppose this
Motion.
Mr. LONG: I have the honour to be
the Chairman of the Regional Education
Committee in the County of Londonderry,
a position I have held for the past
three years, and I am familiar with the
procedure adopted in inviting tenders. My
sympathies are entirely with the noble
Viscount who has placed this Motion on
the Paper, because I regard the amending
Regulation issued by the Ministry as striking against local authorities. It is, no
doubt, quite true, as the noble Leader of
the House said, that this Regulation is
not aimed at the Down Regional Committee only. If that were so I would not
be greatly perturbed, but it is even worse
than that, because in my opinion it is
aimed at all Regional Committees in the
Six Counties. It strikes at a principle
which I have found to be very valuable,
namely, the local knowledge of the persons
constituting these Regional Committees.
I could recite to this House numerous
instances where it was true economy for
the Regional Committee to refuse to accept
the lowest tender. From their local knowledge of the person sending in the lowest
tender they were convinced that the work
would not be carried out satisfactorily,
and that it would not be in the interests
of true economy to accept it. I, therefore, very heartily support the Motion.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: The noble Leader
of the House has referred to the Associa-
247 Expenses of Educ. Authorities
Expenses of Educ. Authorities
THE SENATE

[Mr. Armstrong]
tion of Education Committees, which was
formed last summer. The Executive
Council of that body met in the course of
last month and, as I think has already
been stated, the Regulations that are referred to were laid before them. They
were discussed and no objection was taken
to them except by the representatives of
the County of Down. A resolution was
passed to circulate the Regulations to the
eight counties--because the County
Boroughs are included-and, so far as I
am aware, no objection has been received
from any of them. The representatives
of the Down Regional Committee did resist the resolution to refer the Regulations
to the Committees and voted against it,
but it was carried. Some members of
different bodies did express approval of
this Regulation, which was not necessary,
because it was for criticizm and not for
adoption.
I think the question very much narrows
itself down as to whether the Auditor or
the County Council or the Ministry of
Education is to raise the matter when the
lowest tender is not accepted. I do not
think it makes very much difference which
authority raises the question although,
perhaps, it would be more convenient that
the Ministry should do it because
we
would get their decision sooner. The
Auditor only comes every half year and
sometimes he is not able to be very punctual. In that case the matter might be
delayed or surcharges involved, whereas
if we got a decision from the Ministry the
work could go on at once. I am sure
that any Regional Committee which does.
not accept the lowest tender is able to
give very good reasons for it. There are
many reasons why the lowest tender
should not always be accepted, but I am
sure that, with the assurance the Leader
of the House has given, there is no reason
why the work of the Regional Committees
should not proceed quite smoothly even if
this Regulation is enforced.
Mr. LESLIE: Speaking generally, I
find myself in agreement with the theory
propounded by the noble Viscount, the
Chairman of the County Down Regional
Education Committee, whose voice, if I
may venture to say so, is not heard as
often in our councils as we could wish.
Almost the first thing that occurs to me
in endeavouring to consider the matter
before us is the recognition of the fact
that the calling for tenders is almost universally insisted upon and adopted when
public money is in question, because it is
always thought that competition has the
effect of lowering prices. But competition, although an admirable servant, is, I
to think, an extremely bad
venture
master.
Almost every body who has had practical experience of tenders is aware that
unvarying adherence to the principle of
accepting the lowest tender is a policy
which is very often penny wise and pound
foolish, and I would venture to suggest
that if there is one form of contract more
than another to which that remark applies
it is contracts in relation to building. It
is, perhaps, in connection with contracts
for building that the hon. Senator, the
Chairman of the Londonderry County
Council (Senator Long), has just said
that local knowledge is of more importance
than anything. It is not, of course, possible to confine the acceptance of tenders
to tenders in the immediate locality, but
when it is possible to accept a local tender
properly possible, the result is, I think,
as a rule most satisfactory.
You will remember, as we all remember, that when the noble Marquess, the
first Leader of this House, laid before us
the Education Bill, which has since passed
into an Act, he laid great stress on the
intention of the Government to decentralize education and all matters connected therewith. I think that is a fairly
accurate summary of that part of his
Second Reading speech-decentralization
and popular control of what is, after all,
a popular matter. Then there arises the
obvious question of finance. At the present time the Government makes from
public funds a very liberal contribution,
up to two-thirds of the expenditure of a
Regional Committee. That contribution
is subject to review, I think, at any
moment. The balance of the money
comes from the rates in the area. It is,
as a matter of fact, laid down in a judgment of Sir Peter O'Brien's, which is
quoted in Sir Gilbert Branston's wellknown book on local government, that
a rural district council is under no obligation to accept the lowest tender.
If not a rural district council, why an
education authority, which is usually for
practical purposes a county council?
The answer, perhaps, is to be found to
some extent by going back to the question
of finance. Rural district councils were
249 Expenses of Educ. Authorities
Expenses of Educ. Authorities 250
NOVEMBER 1927
spending only rate money, and an education authority is spending money which
comes both from rates and taxes. All
the same it is the deliberate policy of Parliament to commit the administration of
the details of this matter to that local
body, and I do not think it is desirable
that the Ministry should interfere in the
manner in which this Statutory Rule insists. After all, I think the proper time
for the Ministry to say anything they
may have to say about the acceptance or
rejection of the lowest tender is while
that tender is before the Committe which
is considering it. That is, the Ministry
might not improperly seek to obtain the
admission of a representative of theirs
to the meeting of the Committee which
is considering the tender.
It is obvious that those on whom the
duty is cast of considering the matter must
come to a comparatively immediate decision. I do not think it is in practice
possible to refer the question of accepting
or rejecting the lowest tender to the
Ministry. Tenders do not remain in force
indefinitely, and I seem to remember in
a not very distant Report of our own
Public Accounts Committee some criticizm of the method of dealing with tenders
in one of the Government Departments,
by which a certain amount of loss was
incurred by the public. A tender ought
to be accepted or rejected as speedily as
possible, and should not, I think, be referred to a Ministry before a decision is
arrived at.
Now, it seems incumbent upon us to
When
consider what ought to be done.
the noble Viscount, the Chairman of the
Down Regional Education Committee, put
his Motion on the Paper, he had not had
the advantage of hearing the statement
which the noble Viscount, the Leader of
the House, has just made, when he gave
an undertaking personally to consider
these matters at as early a date as circumstances might admit. The Leader of
the House has given us that assurance
to-day, and I am inclined to wonder if it
would not be possible for him to go a
little further and give an assurance that
the wording of this Regulation, if I may
use that phrase, would be re-cast in such
a manner as would be more acceptable to
the local bodies concerned.
We have heard this afternoon, not only
from County Down but from other counties, an expression of opinion that the
I
Regulation, as it stands, does not quite
meet their wishes, and I think it must be
admitted, after more mature consideration, that this attempt to follow money,
so to speak, after it has been for all intents and purposes earmarked for local
purposes, is contrary to the principles
which the Education Act embodies.
would, therefore, appeal to the noble
Leader of the House to see if he could not
meet the proposals of Senator Viscount
Bangor and re-cast this Regulation in
some way that will be more acceptable to
Regional Education Committees than the
Regulation of 26th September last is.
On the whole, I must support the proposal
which is now before the House.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I think all
the Members who have spoken to this
Motion are either chairmen or members
of Regional Education Committees. I am
very glad to say that I can speak as one
who is neither a chairman nor member of
such a committee. I should very much
have liked to have supported Senator
Viscount Bangor's Motion, but I think it
would create a very dangerous precedent
if we decided that where the Government contributes two-thirds of expenditure out of public money it should not
have the power to see whether that money
was being properly spent or not. lf I
had the choice of living under one of two
evils, Regional Committee. or the
Government, I should prefer the Government. I am sorry I shall have to vote
against Senator Viscount Bangor's
Motion, and in favour of the Government.
Mr. DORMAN: In reading over this
Regulation it strikes me that it is calculated to unsettle the minds of members of
Regional Education Committees, and
especially the poorer members, when estimates are brought before them from time
to time. It states definitely that contracts other than the lowest contract shall
not rank as being entitled to two-thirds of
the Government grant.
I can visualize
these
large contracts coming before
Regional Committees when it would be
imperative in the interests of the community itself that the committee should
not accept the lowest tender, and they
should be enabled to accept any other
tender that they considered would best
suit local needs. For instance, it is quite
possible that some external contractor,
seeing the revolution that has taken place
in the educational machinery of Northern
Expenses of Educ. Authorities
Expiring Laws Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
Ireland, and the large number of schools
that must necessarily be erected, might
so specialize as to be able to reduce the
price of the materials to be used in the
construction of those schools, and he
might send in the lowest tender without
complying with the Fair Wages Clause
that is supposed to have been adopted
by all local bodies, and which I take it is
placed in all contracts given by Regional
Education Committees.
We have really revolutionized the educational system of Northern Ireland, and
we are merely building up on the Education Act certain Regulations which will
control those who administer the Act in
the future. In view of what Senator
Leslie has stated the Marquess of Londonderry said when moving the Second Reading of the Education Bill, I think it is
our duty to safeguard the interests of the
Regional Education Committees, and to
preserve their right to accept other than
the lowest tender.
My mind goes back to another great
change that took place in this country,
when for four or five years local governing
bodies continuously contested with the
Local Government Board this question of
their right to accept other than the lowest
tender. Numerous local bodies insisted
upon accepting not the lowest but the
highest tender submitted, on the ground.
that the contractor who had the highest
tender was calculated to do the greatest
amount of good in the area over which
that particular local body had control.
At that period numerous contracts were
submitted to local governing bodies, although our own industries were languishing and our people were being sent to the
ends of the earth in order to obtain a
living. Circumstances similar to those
exist to-day. We are training our children to go out to the Colonies, and thousands of young men are leaving the
country because they cannot obtain employment here.
If this Regulation is enforced it will,
in my opinion, act as an intimation to
many members of Regional Committees
that they may be surcharged if they accept
any tender other than the lowest. For
these reasons I feel it my duty to vote for
the Motion.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: A really very interesting
debate has taken place on the Motion of
the noble Lord (Viscount Bangor). We
must remember that the Ministry of Education has to adminster public funds, and
that the Ministry can be held accountable
for the manner in which those funds are
expended. That is an important point
that must not be overlooked. If the
noble Viscount (Viscount Bangor) is prepared to withdraw the Motion which
stands in his name, I am authorized by
my noble Friend, the Leader of the
House and Minister of Education, to say
that he will take up this question at once
with the Association of Education Committees to see if some Regulation can be
drafted which will better meet the views
of Senator Viscount Bangor, and at the
same time safeguard the Ministry of Education. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. LONG: May I inquire from the
noble Leader of the House whether this
Regulation will be kept in abeyance in the
meantime?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: In any
case this Regulation does not come into
force until 1st December, and I think
that in the meantime we may be able to
come to a satisfactory agreement.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: If an agreement is not reached, it will not debar us
from pressing this Motion to a division
later on?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Certainly not. The matter can be brought
up again.
VISCOUNT BANGOR: On those conditions I am prepared to withdraw this
Motion.
Motion, by leave, withdrawn.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM
(Deputy Chairman) in the Chair.]
253 Expiring Laws Bill-

1 NOVEMBER 1927
Committee
CLAUSES 1 (Continuance of Acts in
Schedule), and 2 (Short title and Construction), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
SCHEDULE, PART II (Enactments
of the Parliament of Northern Ireland
continued in their duration).
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to move to omit
from Part II of the Schedule the following:-
(2)
12 & 13 Geo.
5, c 5.
The Civil
Authorities
(Special
(Northern
Powers) Act
Ireland), 1922.
The whole
Act.
This Act was placed on the Statute
Book at a time of emergency when the
threat of what was practically Civil War
existed in Northern Ireland, and when
it was impossible for those who had no
means of living except by the sale of their
labour from day to day to exercize that
right of freely offering themselves in the
labour market of Northern Ireland, because of the influence of savagery and
barbarism and sectarian ill-will on the
part of
a certain section of the community. This section was, to a certain
extent, the dominant party, and they deprived thousands of their fellow creatures
of the right to sell their labour because of
an accident of birth, they having been
born of parents of a different religion to
that which the other section professed.
Few hon. Members, except perhaps those
who sit behind me on this side of the
House, are aware of the true circumstances,
and those who are aware of the circumstances would be prepared to-morrow to
support the Government in bringing in a
similar Measure should the same circumstances arise again.
It is all very well for us to blame this
unfortunate event on one part of the community, but I happen to have gone
through it, and I know what it meant.
At the time these conditions existed I
was engaged in working as a labouring
man in the shipyard, and I incidentally
happened to be taking my food from day
to day with another man whose religious
beliefs I never inquired into. On the
day the violence broke out, which ultimately resulted in the Government finding
it necessary to place this legislation on
the Statute Book, I was called over and
commanded to put my hat and coat on
and go home. I asked why, and the
answer I got was that men were coming
from other parts of the works to hit me
on the head with a spanner.
The only
reason I could find for such a threat was
that I happened to have been having my
meals with a man whose religion I did
not know, but at whose feet I was happy
to sit for half an hour or so every day to
gain a little knowledge.
I
Those conditions have all disappeared
and I hope they will never be renewed.
would remind the Government that since
this emergency existed a new party has
arisen in the country whose policy it is to
bring the warring elements together, and
I would be almost bold enough to prophesy
that never again will circumstances arise in
this area as a result of which the working
classes will perpetrate on each other the
crimes that were committed during the
period I have referred to. The party to
which I belong, a constitutional party,
attempted to hold a public meeting in the
principal hall in Belfast. This constitutional party, on whose leader his Gracious
Majesty thought fit to confer the highest
office in the great British Empire, was deliberately deprived of the opportunity of introducing its opinions
to the public in order to obtain
its support at the then forthcoming election, and now the Members of this Government, who approved of the conduct of
breaking up public meetings and suppressing the civil liberties of their fellowcitizens, are asking us to continue to
confer upon them these extra legal powers.
I wish at least to express my disapproval
of the practice of carrying on year after
year powers such as this Act confers.
When King William and Mary ascended
the Throne they had to take an oath that
they would cease to suspend or dispense
with laws as a legal right, and the great
revolution we are actually commemorating
from 12th July to 12th July is based upon
the removal from the Statute Book of the
legal right to suspend all laws unless an
emergency had arisen. As one who has
some respect for the memory of that revolution I intend to divide the House on this
question.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not intend to take
any part in the debate on this Amendment, but I think it is only right to point
out that the principles laid down in the
Bill of Rights are shared, I think, by
Expiring Laws Bill
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
conceivable kind of
every Member of
every
constitutional party in Northern Ireland.
After all, the dispensation of laws which
are complained of, and to which their late
Majesties, King William III and Queen
Mary II were bound to avoid, was a dispensation of laws on the part of the
Crown. The Civil Authorities (Special
Powers) Act is not connected with the
Crown at all. It is an Act of Parliament
consisting of the Senate and House of
Commons of Northern Ireland and the
Crown. There is all the difference in the
the
world between interference with
ordinary rights of the subject arbitrarily
by the Crown and its being done deliberately by Parliament. I think that if the
Bill of Rights is to be brought into this
discussion that fact ought not to be lost
sight of.
Mr. BARCLAY: I hope the Government will not accept the Amendment
Senator Dorman has put before the House
It would not be a very
this afternoon.
wise thing for the Government to do so,
that was
because I believe the law
good for bringing about peace is also good
There is an old sayfor keeping peace.
ing that if you want peace be always
We want to have someready for war.
thing in hand so that if the occasion
should arise we would not lose weeks and
perhaps more than weeks making pro-
I am sorry
vision to overcome the evil.
that the hon. Senator has mentioned any-
Unthing that took place six years ago.
fortunately, things were not then as they
are to-day, and I do not think it is very
wise to rake up these old sores when everything is beginning to pass away out of the
minds of the people.
as
as
As far as the shipyards are concerned
I happened to be in the shipyard at the
time myself, and the section of the shipyard workers which the hon. Senator tries
to put the whole blame upon was not to
far I could
blame
but
see,
taunts and other
they acted upon
out by another
suggestions thrown
section of the workers, and of course the
day came when there was a burst.
do not want any more of that at all.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) The only
way we can keep that away is simply by
letting the thing die out and never mentioning it again. I think this is a very
useful Measure. If we had had something like this in force in 1918 and 1919
perhaps we would not have had the
We
trouble we had in part of 1920 and 1921.
The authorities would have been in a
position to know where those nests were
assembled I mean to say where those
people organized this thing and those
disloyal organizations against the Government and against the country-they
would have been in a position to search
those places, as they are now doing, and
it is quite probable the thing would not
have gone so far. Another thing about these
regulations is that it is a good thing to
have them. They prevent a certain section
of the community from making these inflammatory speeches which are not good
for the keeping of law and order. Therefore, I think it would not be advisable for
the Government to accept the Amendment
the hon. Senator has put before the House
this afternoon.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The hon. Member who
desires that this Act should be expunged
from the Schedule was, if I may say so,
commendably brief in his speech in striking contrast with the speech the hon.
Member made on the same subject a year
ago. In listening to the speech of the hon.
Member to-day and comparing it with the
speech he made about twelve months ago
I find nothing new in his argument to convince me of the necessity of removing this
Act from the Statute Book. After all,
it cannot be said that any loyal or lawabiding citizen in Northern Ireland has
anything to fear from this Act. Certainly
not. It is true that we have peace at the
moment and I hope we may have it for
many years to come, but is it not also true
that there is a possibility-it may not be
a very near one I admit- but there is a
possibility there might be, if the opportunity arose, a recrudescence of those
troubles we went through six years ago
when people were being murdered and
private dwelling-houses and business
premises were being burned and millions
of pounds worth of property were destroyed? It was the passing of this Act
and the enforcement of this Act that put
a stop to those practices.
The Government after all are the guardians of the law and of the liberty of the
subjects of this Province and they cannot
afford to take any risks, and, therefore,
they cannot afford at the present time to
throw away a weapon which has been of
use to them in the past disturbed times.
Assuming that this Act was allowed to
lapse and disturbances such as we had in
those particular times were to occur again
Expiring Laws Bill

the Government would be compelled to go
through the long and very toilsome task
of passing this Act again through both
Houses of Parliament, and meanwhile un
calculable damage might be done which
might be avoided by the retention of this
