Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 2nd November, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. JOSEPH CUNNING-
HAM (Deputy Speaker) in the Chair.
Lemon's Divorce Bill.
Resolved: That the Amendment made
by the Joint Committee to the Bill be
agreed to
Sale of Milk Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill.
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to
In Committee accordingly,
[Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM
(Deputy Chairman) in the Chairl.
CLAUSE 1 (Licences for sale of milk
under special designations).
Mr. LONG: A considerable number of
people in Northern Ireland have already
qualified to obtain a licence under this
Bill. The Committee will understand that
the milk specified in this Bill is put into
bottles and these people have already provided themselves with bottles at a minimum cost of £40 for each person, and in
some cases the cost was considerably more.
A number of these people have asked me
to endeavour to obtain an assurance from
the Government that the bottles they
have provided will not have to be scrapped
and that they will not be required to obtain bottles stamped with a Government
stamp, as they have somehow been led to
understand they will. These bottles contain slightly more than the standard
quantity for each bottle. That is to say,
a quart bottle contains slightly more than
a quart. It would be a very gracious act
if the Government would allow these
people to continue to use these bottles
while they last.
The
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
Government is prepared to allow the continued use of these bottles.
Mr. LESLIE: Rather on a point of
order, I was going to ask you to put each
sub-clause of Clause 1 separately. You
will observe that the Bill really only consists of two Clauses and I think there are
eight possible sub-clauses to Clause 1.
and it would be convenient if you put
them separately.
(1) A person shall not, either by himself or by any servant or agent, except
under and in accordance with a licence
granted by the Ministry of Home
Affairs-
(a) sell or offer or expose for sale
any milk as "certified" "Grade A,"
"tuberculin tested" or "T.T.." or
under such other designation as may
be from time to time prescribed
by regulations made under this Act:
or
,,
as
(b) on or in connection with any
sale or offer for sale or proposed sale
of any milk or in any advertisement,
circular or notice relating to any milk,
describe or refer to the same
"certified," "Grade A., "tuberculin tested" or "T.T.,' or by any
other designation prescribed as aforesaid, or use any description or designation including or resembling any
such description or designation.
""
(2) A licence may be granted for the
purposes of this section for such period,
and subject to such terms and conditions
(including conditions as to the payment
of fees), as may be prescribed by regulations made under this Act; and a
licence so granted may be at any time
suspended or revoked by the Ministry
of Home Affairs by reason of the noncompliance of the holder thereof with
any conditions prescribed as aforesaid,
or for any other reason which the said
Ministry may deem to be sufficient.
(3) If any person is guilty of a contravention of the foregoing provisions of
this section he shall be liable, on sum
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1927
mary conviction, for the first offence,
to a fine of two pounds or any greater
sum not exceeding ten pounds, and for
the second or any subsequent offence to
a fine of five pounds or any greater sum
not exceeding twenty pounds, and, if
the offence is a continuing offence, to a
further fine not exceeding two pounds
for each day during which the offence
continues.
(4) Any authorized officer shall have
power to take and submit for expert
examination samples of any milk offered
or exposed for sale, by or on behalf of
a person holding a licence granted by
the Minister of Home Affairs, under
any designation or description authorized by such licence, and for that purpose to enter any premises upon which
such milk is so offered or exposed for
sale; and any person who obstructs or
impedes an authorized officer acting in
the execution of his powers under this
<ubsection shall be liable on summary
conviction to a fine not exceeding
twenty pounds.
For the purposes of this sub-section,
the expression "authorized officer' means
any police officer or constable having
authority to act as an inspector for the
purposes of the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts, 1875 to 1907, and any
person authorized by the Ministry of
Home Affairs in writing.
(5) A prosecution for any contravention of or offence under the provisions of this Act shall not be instituted except by, or with the consent of,
the Ministry of Home Affairs.
(6) All fines recoverable under this
Act shall, notwithstanding anything in
any enactment, be paid to the Ministry
of Home Affairs, and the amount so
recovered and all sums received by way
of fees under this Act shall be paid to
the Exchequer of Northern Ireland,
except so far as they may be appropriated in aid of the moneys provided
by Parliament for defraying the expenses of carrying this Act into effect.
(7) The Ministry of Home Affairs
may make regulations (subject in respect of fees to the approval of the
Ministry of Finance) for prescribing
anything which under this Act is to be
prescribed; and all such regulations
shall forthwith after they are made be
published in the Belfast Gazette, and
shall be laid before both Houses of
Parliament as soon as may be.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 2, line 2, to leave out from
the second "or" to the end of line 3 (resembling any such description or designation), and to insert instead thereof the
words, "so closely resembling any such description or designation as to be calculated to deceive." It occurred to me that
the words used in the text before us, and
which follow the English Measure, are
perhaps not quite as helpful as they might
be. It seems to me that in the event of
any question arising before a Magistrates'
Court it would be very desirable to direct
the attention of that Court to the point
which was really at issue; that is, the
intent of using the expression.
ordinary expression is "with intent to
deceive." The point you want to direct
attention to is the fraud, and therefore I
suggest to the Committee, although it is
not a matter of first-class importance, that
it would be better to follow the earlier
English Statute and use the expression,
The
so closely resembling any such description or designation as to be calculated to
deceive." I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: (Leader
of the House): I am sorry to say I am
unable to accept this Amendment. The
words in the Bill have been examined
with the greatest care, and it is not considered that any additional or alternative
words are required to strengthen their
meaning.
Mr. LESLIE: I shall not press the
question.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Clause 1, page 2, leave out lines 10 to
13 inclusive (by reason of the non-compliance of the holder thereof with any
conditions prescribed as aforesaid, or for
any other reason which the said Ministry
may deem to be sufficient), and to insert
instead thereof:
If the holder thereof has been convicted of a contravention of the foregoing provisions of this section, or has,
since the granting to him of a licence
under this Act, been convicted of any
offence against the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts, 1875 to 1907.
THE SENATE
Sale of Milk Bill-

Committee
much lessened if we adopted the suggestion
of limiting the duration of the Bill to
some comparatively short period-I suggest three years later on-in order that we
might see how the Bill goes, and whether
it does in fact assist us in inducing people
to supply milk of a super-excellent quality
for the benefit of those who desire it. It
must always be remembered that if we
pass any Measure here which will prevent
the supply of something for which there is
really a public demand, in this case for
honest T.T. milk, we are doing something
which I think Parliament ought not to do,
because I do not think that is the purpose
for which Parliament exists. It is our
business, I think, to assist people to meet
any legitimate public demand, and if we
insert in a Bill here a provision which will
prevent people from coming forward and
seeking to get a licence to carry on a
trade we are not performing our proper
function.
Mr. LESLIE: This is a matter of
greater importance and if I may say so,
a matter of some considerable difficulty.
We have just heard incidentally that some
of the people in Northern Ireland who
supply T.T. milk-and I understand.
there are five such people-have incurred
an expenditure on bottles to the amount of
£40 each. £40 is a sum of some substance.
It would appear that if any of these
people or any other people who may subsequently seek to obtain and actually obtain.
a licence at any time afterwards fall under
the displeasure of the Ministry of Home
Affairs for some cause or other it would
be open to the Ministry to suspend or revoke that licence for any reason the
Ministry might deem to be sufficient. If
this sub-section goes in the Bill as it
stands those licences would be subjected to
any change of opinion at the Ministry
during the regime, we will say, of successive officials, and the licensees would have
no power of appeal at all.
It is not in accordance with my own
views of the constitutional liberty of the
subject that a man's rights should be
committed to a Government department to
be dealt with as that department might
see fit, unless the man be given some sort
of appeal. The point at once arises as to
how one should deal with a matter of that
kind, and it occurred to me to suggest the
words on the Paper which limit the right
to suspend or revoke a licence to a case
where the holder of a licence has been
actually convicted of a contravention of
the provisions of his licence, or has been
convicted of any offence against the
ordinary Sale of Food and Drugs Acts.
That seems to me a possible solution of the
difficulty, but I feel bound to point out
that if the Committee did pass that
Amendment as it stood there might be a
difficulty as regards the temporary suspension of a licence due to some cause which
although it would prejudice the supply of
T.T. milk, might be only of a temporary
character.
Things might happen which would
render it necessary and proper in the
interests of the public that a licence should
be temporarily suspended. On the other
hand, even such a temporary provision
might inflict a serious hardship on a man's
trade. I think I said on the Second Reading of the Bill that it did occur to me that
the possibility of harm being done by using
the words of the Bill as distinct from the
words of the Amendment would be very
On the other hand, there are admittedly
other difficulties if you are to commit
the administration of a Measure of this
sort to a department. In this case the
intention is to bring two departments into
it, although only one is mentioned in the
Bill-the intention is apparently that the
inspection of all byres and herds and so on
should be done by the Ministry of Agriculture, who presumably would report any
serious case to the Ministry of Home
Affairs, and then that Ministry could take
action either by instituting a prosecution,
or, if they preferred-and there is no
reason to doubt they would--by the sus
pension and subsequent revocation of the
licence.
But if you pass the Bill as it stands
there will be no right of appeal whatever,
and I think it is open to question whether
you would be doing any good. If it were
thought the difficulty could be met by
limiting the operation of the Bill in point
of time, I am not too sure that it would
not be the more satisfactory method of
dealing with the question, and that it
would not be better than accepting the
Amendment which for the moment I beg
to move.
I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
should like to deal with the question of
limiting the duration of the Bill to three
years on an Amendment to be moved
later. The effect of this Amendment
would be to deprive the Ministry of the
power which the Bill in its present form
gives to suspend or revoke a licence for
265 Sale of Milk Bill-

2 NOVEMBER 1927
Committee
breach of a prescribed condition, and for
any other reason which the Ministry may
deem sufficient. If a licence holder does
not keep his cowsheds, etc., in a clean
and proper condition it would appear to
be desirable that the Ministry should
under such circumstances be able to
suspend or revoke his licence, or at least
have power to threaten suspension or
revocation.
It will be realized that under such circumstances it might be difficult to obtain
a conviction, and accordingly the Amendment would defeat the whole aim and
principle of the Bill. This Amendment,
and possibly some of the other Amendments, seem to be based on the assumption that the Bill will be administered in
a harsh and hostile spirit by the Ministry
of Home Affairs and the Ministry of
Agriculture. The whole object of the
Bill is to encourage the production of
milk free from tuberculosis, and I think
the power of suspension and revocation
would only be used when absolutely
essential, and the power of prosecution
would only be used as a last resort.
Mr. LESLIE: On that answer I think
it seems only fair to point out that after
all the duty of Parliament is to see that
a Government Department has no opportunity of acting harshly towards the
public. I am not myself disposed-I do
not know whether the Members of the
Committee generally are disposed-to accept the infallibility of the Department as
a matter which is to be taken for granted.
I should rather have submitted to the
Committee that it was for us to see that
no possibility of it occurred. However,
I should be the last person to desire anything which would militate against the
objects of the Bill, which the noble
Viscount has told us are to encourage
the production of super-excellent milk.
The point I suggest the Government
should consider is whether the provision
in the Bill would operate as an encouragement to people to engage in the
supply of super-excellent milk. Howeyer, the matter has been laid before the
Committee and discussed, and although it
has been discussed between the Second
Reading and this stage, I do not observe
that any hon. Member has risen to continue the discussion. I, therefore, ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Clause 1, line 13, at end of Sub-section
(2), insert the following new Sub-section.
"(3) The fees prescribed by regulations made under this Act shall in no
case exceed the respective maximum
amounts set out in the Schedule to this
Act."
Mr. LESLIE : We now approach
another matter dealing with a point which
attracted some attention during the
Second Reading, the question of fees to be
charged those people who are accepting the
encouragement held out by the Ministry
of Home Affairs and the Ministry of Agriculture to engage in the supply of superexcellent milk. It was pointed out on the
Second Reading that before a man applied
for a licence he was not unlikely to want
to know what fee he was going to have to
pay for his licence. I think it was thought
to be not unreasonable that a man wishing
to get a licence under an Act of Parliament would want information as to the fee
he was to pay and that he would refer to
the Act of Parliament for information on
the subject.
If there is a man outside, shall I say the
circle of the favoured five, who wishes to
supply this milk under these conditions
and he wants to know what fee he has to
pay, how is he to find out? It is the
ordinary rule when anything in the nature
of taxation is proposed to put the rate of
taxation definitely into the Measure in
order that Parliament may get the control
which it ought to have, and the same
practice is followed usually as regards fees.
It is usual to insert the limitation as to
fees. In the case before us there can be
no difficulty in setting out in the schedule
of the Bill the fees which are going to be
charged.
Hon. Members who examine the Bill
will see that as it stands at the moment
no date is named for the commencement of
the Bill. It therefore follows that the
moment it receives the Royal Assent anybody supplying T.T. milk without a licence
-and he will not have a licence-will be
committing an offence. The only protection that man will have will be the protection in Sub-clause 5, which states that a
prosecution shall not be instituted except
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
with the consent of the Ministry of Home
Affairs. But the fact that the Bill comes
before us in that condition seems to me to
indicate fairly plainly that the department
must know to-day what fee they propose to
charge and for what services they propose
to charge it. Therefore I cannot see any
objection to inserting in the Bill a
schedule showing what the fees are and
what for, in order that Parliament may
have an opportunity of examining those
fees and saying whether they are too much
or too little.
The point was made by Senators other
than myself on the Second Reading, and
no doubt we shall have the advantage of
hearing their present views on the subject.
For the moment I can see no suggestion
that could be usefully made other than
the proposal to enumerate the fees in the
schedule and to attach them to the Bill.
The suggestion I have made is that the
fees should be inserted in maximum, in
order, of course, that the Ministry should
not be deprived of the possibility of revising their fees on an upward or downward
scale. Provision as regards the assessment
of these fees is, as you will see from
Sub-section 7, left to the Ministry of
Home Affairs, subject to the approval of
the Ministry of Finance.
Although I believe the Ministry of
Home Affairs, probably with the great
assistance of the Ministry of Agriculture,
will do everything in their power to keep
these fees down to the irreducible limit, as
the Ministry of Finance is to be brought
into it and is entitled to a voice in the
settlement, it has occurred to me whether
that Ministry will not be disposed to see
that special steps are taken to ensure that
the business under this Bill is self-supporting, and it may very well be that the
Ministry of Finance may take a far more
severe view in the matter of fees than
either the Ministry of Home Affairs or the
Ministry of Agriculture. I cannot help
thinking that it is for Parliament to interpose and exercise its authority and to
say definitely how far those fees shall
go and at what point they are to stop.
Accordingly I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I cannot say anything more about
this question than point to the fact that
it was fully debated in another place,
when a specific pledge was given by the
Prime Minister that in prescribing the
fees the utmost care would be taken to
ensure that they would be calculated cn
a basis having relation only to the cost of
the service of inspection, and that under
no circumstances would any profit be
made out of the fees. The Members in
another place accepted the Government's
assurance on that point. Perhaps in my
auswer to this Amendment I may be able
to tack on a little about the infallibility
of Government Departments, and also
about the Act being administered harshly
in connection with this and the preceding
Amendment. If the Bill was administered harshly or the cost of the fees was
too high it would defeat the very object
of the Measure.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount
having appealed to those who have
authority, and those who wield that power
having incurred such responsibilities as
there may be in passing the proposal, it
would not be for us to continue the discussion.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
""
fine
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
1, to leave out from the word "fine,"
in line 16, on page 2, to the word
not in line 17 (of two pounds or
any greater sum). This is another problem-the problem of the minimum fine.
The problem of the minimum
has engaged the attention of this House,
either sitting as a House or as a Committee, on more than one occasion of late.
Since the Parliament of Northern Ireland
was established it has engaged the attention of both Chambers on more than one
occasion. Strong remarks are to be found
in the Reports of the proceedings in another place emanating from the Government Bench, and pointing out the impropriety of interfering with the discretion of magistrates by requiring minimum
fines to be inflicted. A statement made
by the noble Leader in this House is on
record that the Government are not
wedded to the principle of the minimum
fine, yet here we find in a Measure dealing with a perfectly new subject, a
matter which magistrates have had no
opportunity of dealing with, a proposal
for a minimum fine.
In speaking on this subject on a former
occasion I pointed out that one of the
difficulties of dealing with the matter was
that whenever a proposal of a minimum
fine was made you
had to examine
it not alone on its
Own
particular
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1927
merits. There is also the general
question of interfering with the discretion of magistrates, an interference
which anybody who moves about
the country or reads the newspapers
cannot fail to notice is greatly
resented by magistrates themselves. Expressions are to be found reported again
and again that magistrates feel they are
required to sit there, as I think one gentleman said, as the dummies of the Ministry
of Home Affairs. If that feeling grows
what will the effect be? It will be that
the class of man we most desire to have
as a magistrate will decline the honour,
or, if he accepts it, he will not go on the
Bench. If once you interfere with the
exercise of the discretion of a man in any
office you will find that the best men will
not take the office. I do not say you will
not have plenty of applicants for what is
very erroneously called "Appointment
to Justice of the Peace," but I do say
you are gravely risking the chance of
getting the best men.
Another thing I said when speaking
on this subject was that there were cases
to be found in the Statute Book where
minimum fines were justified, even in my
opinion, and it so happens that with care
and good management the case to which
I am now referring might have come
within that category. The standing case
is the case of what they call the Fishery
Act. I think it is under a different Act,
but everybody knows what I
mean.
Certain offences in connection with the
taking of fish are subject to a minimum
fine of £4. That Act has been on the
Statute Book for some time and can be
defended, and I have always myself been
prepared to defend it on the ground that
that offence against the law, besides being
an offence against the Crown in the
ordinary way, was to some extent an
offence against private rights. That is,
there is a certain wrong done by the use
of illegal instruments in fishing to those
who have the legal right to take fish in a
legal way.
Similarly in this Measure if a man who
has not got a licence to sell T.T. milk
does purport to sell it he is not only offending against the Statute, but he is doing
something in the nature of wrong to the
man who is lawfully supplying T.T. milk.
If the minimum fine had been limited to
cases of that sort I should not have had
anything to say against it. Indeed, being
anxious to encourage the supply of this
super-excellent milk in every way that
would induce men to make a success of it
I think I would support it. Unfortunately
the provision does not relate only to that.
It will catch the person who contravenes
in any way the conditions of the licence.
That sort of contravention is purely a
matter for the discretion of the Court
which hears the evidence and tries the
case.
I am sorry that we have not in this
House a House which we endeavour to
model on the principles and practice of
another Second Chamber across the
water; I hope on the principles of that
Chamber as it was last century rather
than to-day-we have not copied that
Chamber by the introduction of what I
understand is called a Protest Book. If
there was such a book I would long ago
have recorded my protest against the insertion of anything which impaired the
discretion of a legally constituted court.
Again and again I have suggested that
if you cannot trust your magistrates to
administer the laws faithfully and properly, or "indifferently" as in an ancient
expression which I think I noted the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Sharman Crawford) using, if you cannot
trust them change them and make some
other arrangement, but as long as you
have a court to administer the King's
law do not impair its discretion, but leave
it to do the work for which it was appointed. Therefore, I am obliged to
oppose this proposal, although had it
been limited in the manner I have indicated I should certainly not have troubled
the Committee with this Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I think that if ever
there was a Bill brought before the Committee that justified the Government in
establishing a minimum fine for transgressions of that Bill when it became law
it is this Bill. I should have expected
that Senator Leslie would have been disposed to propose an Amendment to increase the minimum fine in this particular case rather than to eliminate it
from the Bill. I have no desire to cast
any reflection upon magistrates, but we
all know that magistrates of Petty
Sessions in every part of Ireland, in adjudicating in cases of offences against
such Acts as this, often come to a decision by a majority vote. I am perfectly
satisfied that occasionally cases do occur
of a majority being artificially manu-
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
factured in favour of some individual who
may be brought before a bench to answer
for an offence in an area where he may be
able to exercise some influence. I do appeal
to the Government not to agree to the
abolition of the minimum fine from this
Measure, because I think that instead of
being taken out of the Bill it should be
increased to £5.
Mr. LONG: I have a certain amount
of sympathy with the Amendment proposed by Senator Leslie, but I regret to
say I cannot go all the way with him.
It seems to me there are two classes of
offences possible under this Bill, namely,
fraudulent contravention of the conditions of a licence and violation of the provisions of the Statute which might be due
to pure carelessness or negligence. It
seems to me that some differentiation
should be made in those two classes of
offences, and therefore I suggest that if
the hon. Senator altered his Amendment
to confine the operation of the minimum
fine to fraudulent contraventions of the
Act, and eliminate it as regards other
offences it might meet with the approval
of the Government.
I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
would prefer not to enter into an argument with Senator Leslie on the question
of the minimum fine itself. As regards
the introduction of a Protest Book, I do
not think that is at all necessary because
immediately a minimum fine is mentioned
Senator Leslie jumps up, so I do not
think it is necessary for him to inscribe
his protest with pen and ink.
As regards this particular Amendment,
I am prepared to meet both Senator
Leslie and Senator Long in differentiating between the two different classes of
offenders, the actually fraudulent person
and the merely forgetful one. I would
therefore suggest that Senator Leslie's
Amendment should stand, and at the end
of Sub-section (3) on page 2 of the
Clause there should be added:
-
Provided that where a person not
having a licence granted by the Ministry
of Home Affairs under this Act is con.
victed of selling milk, or exposing or
offering milk for sale in contravention
of the foregoing provisions of this
Section, he shall be liable for the first
offence to a fine of not less than two
pounds, and for a second or subsequent
offence to a fine of not less than five
pounds.
That would differentiate between the
fraudulent and the negligent man, and if
Senator Leslie will agree to that I will
accept his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: If it were physically
possible I would almost leap the Table
to receive the suggestion of the noble
Viscount. It seems to me to be most admirably adapted to meet the case, and it
corresponds exactly with the differentiation I myself desired. Therefore, I will
certainly accept it. I take it the position
now is that the noble Viscount will accept
on behalf of the Government my Amendment on the Paper and that which follows,
and that when we reach line 22 he will
move his own Amendment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT
cated assent.
Amendment agreed to.
indi-
Mr. LESLIE: I formally move the
consequential Amendment, in line 18,
page 2, to leave out from the word "fine"
to the word "not" in line 19 (of five
pounds or any greater sum).
Amendment agreed to.
at
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, in Clause 1, page 2, line 22,
the end of Sub-section (3) to add :
-
Provided that where a person not
having a licence granted by the Ministry
of Home Affairs under this Act is convicted of selling milk, or exposing or
offering milk for sale in contravention
of the foregoing provisions of this
Section, he shall be liable for the first
offence to a fine of not less than two
pounds, and for a second or subsequent offence to a fine of not less than
five pounds.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Line 23, leave out from the beginning
of the line to "such" in line 29,
and
insert: "For the purposes of this Act
it shall be lawful for any authorized
officer to take and submit for analysis
or for bacteriological or other examination samples of any milk offered or exposed for sale, and for such purposes
to enter any premises upon which he has
reason to believe that
Sale of Milk Bil-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1927
Mr. LESLIE: Since I put this Amendment on the Paper I have had the advantage of a consultation with those
officers of the Ministry of Home Affairs
and another Ministry who are likely to be
concerned in the administration of this
Measure, and I learn from them that the
purpose for which I put down my original
Amendment as it stands on the Paper
would not really be of any great assistance. That is to say, they do not contemplate exercising the power which I
proposed to give them. Accordingly all
that it is now necessary for us to do is to
leave out of line 24 on page 2 the word
'expert" (for expert examination), and
to insert "analysis or for bacteriological
or other." That means that a sample
may be taken and treated in exactly the
same way as is provided for in the case of
samples across the water. I venture to
think that that will be rather better than
using the expression "expert examination" which is not defined and is perhaps
open to difficulty. I beg to move accordingly.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: Would the hon. Senator
read out the lines as they will then
stand?
Mr. LESLIE
Certainly.
Any authorized officer shall have
power to take and submit for analysis
or for bacteriological or other examination samples of any milk offered or
exposed for sale
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
prepared to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:-
Clause 1, at end of Sub-section (4) insert the following new Sub-section :-
"(4) (a) The result of an analysis or
bacteriological or other examination of
a sample of milk taken under this Act
shall not be admissible as evidence in
proceedings under this Act, or in proceedings under the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts, 1875 to 1907, unless the
provisions of the last-mentioned Acts
which relate to the division of samples
into parts are complied with, but if
those provisions have been complied
with, the result of the analysis shall be
available for proceedings under the
said Acts (as if it had been procured
in accordance with those Acts), as well
as for proceedings under this Act."
I
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it will
be necessary to trouble the Committee at
any length with this Amendment.
will formally move it for the sake of keeping myself in order, but if the noble
Leader of the House can assure us that
this T.T. milk will only be sold in sealed
bottles, and transported in sealed bottles,
I think that would dispose of the difficulty
one feels as regards the abandonment of
the ordinary provisions of the Sale of
Food and Drugs Acts. In that case I
should ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Milk
will only be sold and transported in sealed.
bottles.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: Now I pass to the
following Amendment:---
In Clause 1, at the end of line 39, after
Sub-section (4) insert the following new
Sub-section :-
"At the hearing of the information
in any proceedings under this Act the
production of a certificate purporting
to be signed by the person to whom any
sample of milk taken under this Act
has been submitted for analysis or
bacteriological or other examination,
shall be sufficient evidence of the facts
stated therein, unless the defendant
shall require that such person shall be
called as a witness."
I think this proposal, which is, of
course, to bring in a practice which is
common under the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts is one which should go into
this Bill. It will save expense. If a
man is summoned for a contravention of
the provisions of the Act, and if it is
necessary to produce a certificate, I think
we should provide that the production of
a certificate purporting to be signed by
the person who made the examination,
shall be accepted as evidence, unless the
defendant requires that the person who
made the examination shall appear as a
witness. I think it is desirable that this
should be inserted in the Bill, and I move
accordingly.
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
After Sub-section (4) of Clause 1, insert
the following new Sub-section :
:-
"A warranty or invoice shall be
available as a defence in a prosecution
for an offence against this section in any
case where a warranty or invoice would
be so available if the prosecution had
been taken under the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts in respect of the sale of an
article of food, and section twenty-five
of the Sale of Food and Drugs Act,
1875, and section twenty of the Sale of
Food and Drugs Act, 1899, shall apply
accordingly."
That is a simple protection for the
honest retailer, and I think it ought to go
into the Bill. The provisions of the two
sections it is proposed to apply are well
known. The principle is that the seller
who proposes to rely upon the defence of
selling stuff in the same condition in which
he received it must give notice to the prosecution. It is a provision that has been
found of use on the sade of Food and
Drugs Acts, and it is required in the interests of the retailer. It must be remembered that under this Bill not only
the man who is responsible for the production of the milk, the man who has
got the byre and the cows, but the retailer in Belfast or elsewhere to whom he
sells the milk in sealed bottles will also
need a licence.
It is readily comprehensible that that
man might be held responsible for an
offence he did not commit unless he had
this protection. Therefore, I suggest
we should insert this Sub-clause, which is
not in its wording original. I cannot
claim credit for its invention, but I suggest we should insert it in the Bill.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Line 40, leave out from "prosecution"
to "under" in line 41.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title).
This Act may be cited as the Sale of
Milk Act (Northern Ireland), 1927.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 2, to insert the following
new Sub-section :-
(2) This Act shall come into operation on the second day of January,
Nineteen hundred and Twenty-eight.'
In this Amendment I propose that this
2nd
Act shall come into operation on
January, 1928. I think it is necessary
that we should insert a date for the commencement of the Act, and I need scarcely
remind the Committee that the fact that
it does not commence until the date suggested will not deter the Ministry and
others from taking the necessary preliminary steps to bring the Act into
operation. I imagine that the Department are prepared to accept 2nd January,
1928, as giving sufficient time, and therefore I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
Government will accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I now beg to move to
insert at the end of Clause 2 the words,
"and shall continue in force for three
years and no longer, unless Parliament
otherwise determines."
We have already spoken more than once
about this proposal, and I am hoping we
shall be assisted with an expression of
the views of hon. Senators upon it. The
idea is, of course, that we should give
the Bill a limited duration in order that
we may see whether it is a success or not;
whether it is possible to satisfy the public
demand for T.T. and other milk, or
whether it is found in fact that when this
Measure has become an Act the public demand is still unsatisfied. We can then
consider whether perhaps that is due to
the more or less stringent provisions we
have inserted in the Bill.
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1927
The other point of view, which I expect
some other hon. Member will refer to, is
that it might be said that if we limit the
operation of the Bill to three years we
should be doing something that might
discourage a man from taking out a
licence. He might say he would not build
his byre and buy his cows if the protection is only to last for three years.
I am
extremely doubtful which way the chief
difficulty lies, but I still do not like a
certain provision which I mentioned before. On the other hand, the objection
to that may, as often happens, disappear
with practical experience, and for the
moment I will put before the Committee
the desirability of inserting the words,
which will have the effect of limiting the
operation of the Measure to three years,
unless, of course, Parliament otherwise
determines.
It is very likely that before the three
years are out Parliament may be dealing
not only with the question of T.T. milk,
but the whole question of the milk supply,
and if that did happen it is not unreasonable to suppose that this Bill would
be repealed and incorporated in the new
Measure. As a matter of fact, the Committee is probably aware that the corresponding English enactment to this Bill
is part of the Milk and Dairies Amendment Act, and it may very well be that
in the future we shall be following on the
same lines. I, therefore, beg to move
this Amendment, and I do not think its
existence in the Bill would create
great many difficulties.
a
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I shall have to resist this Amendment. If the Bill proves to be successful
the operations under it will develop. If
on the other hand producers find it impossible for them to comply with the prescribed conditions, they will simply cease
to apply for licences, and the Department
will find that their Bill has failed in its
object. If it became generally known
that the Bill was to have a life of only
three years very few people would go to
the trouble of building up herds which
were immune from tuberculosis. For
those reasons I must oppose the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount has
taken the other view, and I do not
desire to press the matter against the
advice he has given.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put, That Clause 2 as amended
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to draw
attention to the Amendment which
stands in my name at the end of the
Paper. I should like to take the opportunity of explaining that both that
Amendment and an earlier one which I
did not move were put down on the Paper
originally as matters of precaution in case
the acceptance of other alterations in the
Bill might have rendered it necessary to
make those Amendments. They were
not put down on the presumption that
they were originally left out of the Bill.
It was a possibly necessary precaution.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: If I am in
order I would like to give the Committee
some facts as to what has already been
done in County Down in connection with
the supply of tubercular-free milk. This
Bill will, undoubtedly, make a great improvement in the milk supply, but I
would not like it to go out that nothing
has already been done to secure cows free
from tuberculosis. The following are the
amounts paid for the year ending 30th
September last for animals slaughtered in
County Down:--
£ S. d.
166 animals slaughtered 1,213 2 6
Veterinary Surgeons'
Fees paid
384 17 0
Total £1,597 19 6
That is for cows and other animals that
have shown signs of tuberculosis in
County Down alone. I take it that other
counties have expended sums in respect of
the matter. My only reason for rising
was to show that we have been doing something in the past in this matter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill as amended be
reported to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported as amended.

Quarries Bill-
Committee
8 NOVEMBER 1927
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 8th November, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Roval Ulster Constabulary Pay
(Amending) Order, 1927.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and
ordered to lie on the Table.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS
DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (General provisions as to
safety).
For the prevention of dangerous
accidents, and for ensuring the safety
of persons employed, in every quarry,
the following provisions shall have
effect: :-
(a) The working of the sides and of the
overburden or tops of the quarry
shall be carried on so as to prevent
dangerous falls of material;
(b) The top of the quarry, if dangerous, and the top of every shaft
shall be kept securely fenced;
(c) If quarrying work is carried on
after dark, the quarry shall be
under the supervision of a competent person appointed by the
owner, occupier or agent for the
purpose;
Lemon's Divorce Bill.
The Bill was read the Third time and
passed.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the Amendment made by
the Joint Committee, without Amendment.
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Orders Confirmation Bill.
The Bill was read the Third time and
passed.
And a Message was sent to the Commons to acquaint them that the Senate.
agree to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Quarries Bill [S.]
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.

*
in
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
Clause 1, page 1, line 11, to leave out the
words" or tops." The point is that the
expression or tops" is perfectly well
understood in the Rules made under the
Metalliferous Mines Act, but I think they
are a little inappropriate here. They are
apparently used as an alternative to the
word" overburden," which is defined for
us in the ordinary dictionary manner in
Clause 30 of the Bill, and does not relate
only to anything that overhangs
quarry after the manner of a snow cornice in an Alpine mountain The word
"overburden" is perfectly clear by itself,
and I think the introduction of the two
words, "or tops
"would be rather superfluous. I beg to move.
a
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
prepared to accept that Amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
may be out of order, but I understand
that all the Amendments on the Order
Paper are accepted by the Government,
and if the hon. Senator (Senator Leslie)
would confine himself to formally moving
the Amendments it would mean a great
saving of time.
Quarries Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
Mr. SPEAKER: The Amendments
will have to go before the Committee.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
But they might be proposed without
speeches.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 1, page 1, line 13, leave out
from "quarry" to "be" in line 14, and
insert, and any opening made for the
purpose of quarrying therein shall, if
dangerous."
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid that, at the
risk of coming under the censure of the
right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Sharman Crawford)--
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
And of the Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not understand
that the right hon. and gallant Gentle
man spoke for anybody but himself-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
And the Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I must give a word or
two of explanation, because I have to ask
the Committee to allow me to move the
second part of the Amendment in
slightly different terms to those on the
Paper, and I ought perhaps to explain
why I want the alteration made. The
Amendment runs:
Line 13, leave out from "quarry
"" be" in line 14.
""
to
That is the first part of the Amendment,
and that stands. Then we come to the
alternative words to be inserted I want
them to read:-
And any opening or shaft made for
the purposes of quarrying shall, if
dangerous.
Those are the words I want inserted. The
difficulty about the wording in the text before us is that if you use the word shaft
without any qualification you at once
imply a mine, and a quarry is not a mine.
Also it is desirable to get in the two
words, "if dangerous." I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 1, line 19, to add at the end of
paragraph (c) the words, "of such work,"
the point being to clarify the paragraph.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
accept that Amendment.

CLAUSE 4 (Power of Court to make
orders as to dangerous conditions and
practices).
*
(2) Where a complaint has been
made under this section, the court, or
a resident magistrate appointed under
the Constabulary (Ireland) Act, 1836,
may, on application ex parte by the inspector to the effect that the use of any
such part of the machinery or plant
involves imminent danger to life, make
an interim order prohibiting, either
absolutely or subject to conditions, the
use thereof until the earliest opportunity for hearing and determining the
complaint.

Quarries Bill—
Committee
8 NOVEMBER 1927
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Clause 4, page 5, line 14, leave out from
"a" to "may" in line 15 (resident magistrate appointed under the Constabulary
(Ireland) Act, 1836), and insert instead
thereof the word "justice."
Mr. LESLIE: This is a point of somewhat greater importance-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
But it is accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: Even so, perhaps I
ought to explain why I have put down
the Amendment. I am sorry if I shall
not be able to interest the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman
Crawford).
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Nor the Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: But I cannot help feeling that some explanation ought to be
offered to the Committee of these Amendments.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member is quite justified in giving an
explanation of his Amendments; otherwise how can the Committee know the
reason for the Amendments.
Mr. LESLIE: The point here is that
this Sub-section, as we shall see in reading it, is only to come into operation if
something is going on in a quarry which
in the opinion of the inspector involves
imminent danger to life. Anything of
that sort must be dealt with immediately,
and it is therefore necessary, I think, that
the appropriate remedy should be found
at the earliest possible moment. Quarries,
as a rule, are situated in the country,
very often quite a long way from a town,
and Courts of Summary Jurisdiction in
the country usually sit only once a month.
It is therefore not very much use waiting
to apply to a Court of Summary Jurisdiction when you are dealing with a case
of imminent danger to life.
Resident Magistrates appointed under
the Act of 1836 are not usually to be
found in a spot immediately adjacent to
a quarry. Therefore, I substitute for
"Resident Magistrate " the word
"Justice" in order that any Justice of
the Peace having jurisdiction in the
a
county concerned may, if necessary, make
this Order. He cannot do much harm.
The most he can do is to give an Interim
Order suspending operations until
Court of Summary Jurisdiction does sit,
and he has this advantage over a Resident
Magistrate, who may be residing in
Belfast or some other large town, that he
can himself go and see the quarry before
he makes the Order. I am advised that
the conferring of this power on ordinary
magistrates is more in accordance with the
Factories and Workshops Act than limiting it to Resident Magistrates. I think
the point in suggesting Resident Magistrates was to prevent any possibility of
local influence, but I do not think that
ought to weigh against the urgency of
imminent danger to life.
Mr. McDOWELL: I do not think the
argument of the hon. Senator is in conformity with the speech he delivered the
other day. We were then led to believe
that it very frequently occurred that
benches of magistrates were packed for a
specific purpose. If that is so I do not
see why the hon. Senator wants to do
away with Resident Magistrates. For
this reason I shall vote against the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot charge my
memory with the speech the hon. Senator
refers to, but I do not think I made any
suggestion against magistrates. My remarks have always been rather on the
other side, I ought perhaps to explain
that this is not a case for a bench of magistrates at all. This application will be
made ex parte to the selected magistrate
very much in the same way as an application for a writ of Habeas Corpus may
be made to a judge. It may be made
to a magistrate in his bedroom or diningroom. The only point is that it shall be
made as speedily as possible.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: Has not the
hon Member (Senator Leslie) made a mistake in drafting the Amendment to leave
out from " a to may. Should it
not be to leave out from "or" to "a."
""
""
Mr. LESLIE: The effect of the alteration will be that the Sub-clause will run
like this, if the Committee accepts the
Amendment:
Where a complaint has been made
under this Section, the Court, or a
justice may, on application ex parte.
and so on.
Quarries Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I see.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
prepared to accept this Amendment.

CLAUSES 5 (Employment of children
and young persons), 6 (First-aid arrangements), 7 (Provisions as to wages), 8
(Observance of directions), 9 (Penalties
for non-compliance with provisions of

I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:

I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
should have said that at the present time

The following Amendment stood on the

.---
"(4) Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, nothing done by
virtue of any power under section one
hundred and sixty-two of the Grand
Jury (Ireland) Act, 1836 (as adopted
by the Local Government Adaptation
of Irish Enactments Order, 1899), as

power.")
Mr. LESLIE: I am suggesting here as
a new Sub-clause the words on the Paper.
The point is, that, as most hon. Members
are aware, there are compulsory powers
now under the Local Government Act,
1898, inherited, so to speak, from the old
Grand Jury Act, under which a person
may enter upon the lands of another
without his consent, chiefly in connection
with the mending of roads. It seems
only fair that a man who has no choice in
the matter should be protected by such
words as I suggest against such a possibility as the place entered being deemed
to be a quarry for the purposes of this
Act and the man himself becoming liable
to the provisions of the Act, which might
perhaps involve expense. It is a perfectly ordinary protective Clause which I
think the Committee might desire to
insert.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
The hon. Senator has not given us the
full information he generally gives us on
most Amendments.
Mr. LESLIE: Why? (Laughter.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
I do not know what the effect of the
Amendment will be. County councils
experience considerable difficulty from
persons who object to the officials going
on to their lands for stones for road mending, and I should like to know whether
this Amendment will take
away from
county councils the power they have to
go into quarries on the side of the road
for the purpose of procuring stones.
Bill will be introduced in another place
later on to give county councils additional powers in this respect, and I do
not know whether this Bill will in any
way clash with the provisions of the Bill
to be introduced at a later date. I hope
Senator Leslie will be able to assure me

If it has any effect at all as regard county
councils it will be to render the exercise
of their powers more easy by removing

of the owner.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: There is one thing I
want to say on the Third Reading of this
Bill. I oberve in the report of the
speeches on the Second Reading that I
drew attention to the possibility of our
having to deal during the Committee
stage with the question of appeal from the
decision of a Court of Summary Jurisdiction. I did not put any Amendment on
the Paper on that subject, because I ascertained in the meantime that it was
considered that no special provision was
necessary. I only mention the matter because it is not unlikely that a similar
point may occur to us in dealing with
future Bills, and that will explain why
there was, so to speak, a ragged end left.

Mr. DORMAN: I hope that in the
