Housing Bill,
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In rising to move the
Second Reading of this Bill, I should like,
first of all, to inform the House that the
object of the Bill is firstly, to alter and
in some
cases increase the Government
grants and otherwise continue the operation of the Housing Acts for another year,
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
and secondly, to extend the powers of
local authorities in connection with the
sale of houses.
Hon. Members are aware that, as the
law now stands, the existing Housing Acts
come to an end in March of next year.
Under these Acts grants are made in respect of houses coming within a minimum
of 500 superficial feet and a maximum of
950, and a free grant of £80 is given by
the Government where the house
erected within six months from the date
of commencement, and £60 where a longer
period is taken. Further, local authorities are empowered either to give a loan of
£100 free of interest for twenty-five years
and to remit the rates up to a period of
ten years, or to give a free grant of £40
and to remit the rates for a like period.
The Government now feel that the need
for the larger type of house has been met
to a great extent, and that subsidies in
respect of this type shall cease. A great
deal, however, still remains to be done to
provide houses of the type which is
within the means of the working man and,
accordingly, it is proposed in this Measure
to subsidize houses of not more than 650
superficial feet. In addition, the subsidy
will be increased from £80 to £100 in the
case of those houses completed within six
months from the date of commencement,
and from £60 to £80 where the house
takes longer to build. This is contained
in Clause 1 of the Bill.
It will be noted that the grants will be
payable in respect of houses commenced
on or after 1st August, 1927, and completed before 1st April, 1929, and that
local authorities will retain the power to
make supplemental grants or loans free
of interest, and to exempt houses from
payment of rates for a period not exceeding ten years. The Bill also expressly
provides that the arrangements made
therein are not to affect in any way the
grants to the Sailors' and Soldiers' Land
Trust for the erection of ex-Servicemen's
houses.
I come now to the second Section of
the Bill, which is rather technical and
somewhat difficult to follow. Hon.
Members will, I trust, bear with me if
I appear somewhat tedious in endeavouring to explain the purpose of its provisions in simple language. As I have said
before, local authorities may subsidize
the building of a house in alternative
ways; that is, they may give a loan of
£100 free of interest and repayable in
25 years, or they may give a free grant
of £40. The relationship which these
figures bear to each other will be apparent
when I explain that it costs the local
authority the same amount of money to
give the loan free of interest as it does
to give the free grant. It will also be
helpful if hon. Members will bear in
mind that the proportion of 100 to 40 is
as 5 is to 2.
Under Section 1 of the Housing Act of
1924 it was provided that local authorities
might sell houses erected by them at
the price which they actually cost to
build, less the amount of the Government
grant and the local authority grant, if
the house happened to be in an area
where the local authority had agreed to
give a subsidy. For example, if the cost
of a house was £370, the Government
subsidy being £100 and the local
authority subsidy £40, the purchaser
would pay £370, less £140; that is £230.
The usual method by which this £230
was provided, the purchasers generally
being people without much capital, was
for the local authority to require the
payment in cash of a small sum, say £10,
the balance being advanced to the
purchaser, to be repaid by instalments
with interest over a period of 35 years.
very
Under this system, as hon. Members
will appreciate, the repayments made in
the early years of the period consisted
chiefly of the interest due, and the capital
sum outstanding was reduced
slowly. Thus a considerable number of
years had to elapse before the purchaser
could feel that he had paid off a substantial portion of his debt, and that his
payments for the rest of the period of
35 years were rapidly reducing his capital
liability.
The object of the Clause in the present
Bill is to enable local authorities, if they
so desire, to finance these purchasers by a
somewhat different method which, without
placing any additional burden on the
rates, will be more advantageous to the
purchaser and will have the further merits
of clearing off the mortgage in a shorter
period and of enabling the annual payments by the purchaser to be put in reduction of the capital outstanding, rather
than in repayment of interest.
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
10 NOVEMBER 1927
The
I mentioned earlier in my speech that
£40 in cash was equivalent to a free loan
of £100. The proportion of 5-2 being
observed, it follows that £140 in cash is
equivalent to a free loan of £350.
local authority, with its Government
grant of £100 and its own subsidy of £40,
will be allowed under this Clause to give
the purchaser of a subsidy house a free
loan of £350, instead of, as in the past,
paying over a sum of £140 towards the
reduction of the gross price of the house.
I will, if I may, take a concrete example to show the effect of the new
proposal, as compared with the former
state of affairs. If the gross cost of a
house was £370, under the Housing Act
of 1924 the authority would deduct the
grant of £140 and would sell the house at
£230. The purchaser would pay £10 in
cash and would borrow the balance,
£220, under the Small Dwellings Acquisition Act, making himself liable for the
repayment of £15 3s. 7d. per year
for a period of 35 years. Under
the new scheme, the £140 grant is
converted into an interest free loan of
£350. The price of the house, as before,
is £370 and the purchaser pays £10 cash,
being still liable for £360. But he is
given an interest free loan of £350, which
he pays back in twenty-five years at the
rate of £14 per year. He has also to
pay back the remaining £10, with interest, which involves him in a payment
of 15s 8d per year, making his total
annual repayments £14 15s 8d. Thus he
discharges his liability at a less cost, and
in twenty-five years instead of thirty-five
as formerly, and furthermore, he is able,
at any date during the twenty-five years,
to estimate his position with regard to repayments, instead of finding himself inextricably entangled in the difficulties of
Sinking Fund finance, as was the case
under the 1924 Act. I trust I have said
sufficient to indicate the general purport
of the Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I want, in the first
place, to draw attention to the fact that
the Bill, which we are now considering,
only came into the possession of some
hon. Senators late last night, and I want
once again to protest against being called
here for the purpose of considering a Bill
that I have not had sufficient time to
read and digest. Those of us who have
to follow our daily work and return
home after a hard day's work the night
before this House sits are not in a position to give proper and careful consideration to even short Measures, such as this.
In the first place, we are not provided
with the Acts of Parliament that may
be referred to in the new Measures
brought before us, and we are debarred
of the right of taking advantage of the
facilities afforded us in the Parliamentary
Library. Therefore, I again desire to
appeal that more consideration be given
to those of us who have not had the advantage of a legal training, and, therefore, require longer time to study the
Bills that are brought before us.
With regard to this Bill, I welcome it,
and I welcome the fact that the Government have realised the necessity of increasing the subsidy and encouraging the
building of houses that are within the
reach of the working classes. I noticed that
the Parliamentary Secretary of the Ministry of Home Affairs, referring to the model
house placed in the smoke-room for the
inspection of Members, made the statement that the model represented a house
of 500 superficial feet. I find that the
first Clause of the Bill is to enable
authorities in rural districts to erect
houses of 500 superficial feet, and houses
erected in other districts are required to
have an area of 550 superficial feet.
would like the noble Leader of the House
to tell us whether that model house will
be adopted in rural areas in Northern
Ireland in contravention of the first
Clause of this Bill. In the Bill it is distinctly laid down that houses erected in
Belfast must have an area of at least
550 superficial feet.
I
There is one matter to which no reference is made in the Bill, and about
which I would like to ask the noble Viscount a question. That is what will be
the average ground rent of these houses.
After all, I do not think it is possible for
the Government of Northern Ireland, or
any other Government to devise satisfactory housing schemes for the workingclasses to comply with the justifiable demand that the house rent ought to be in
proportion to a man's earnings if the
ground rent is not reasonable.
If my
memory serves me right, before the War
the Belfast Corporation was about to
launch out on a housing scheme by means
of direct labour. I think they called
together the auctioneers and estate agents
and a special committee was set up and an
investigation made as to what was the
minimum cost at which they could secure
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. Dorman]
was
land for the erection of working class
It
the
houses.
discovered that
land inside the city boundary was £5,800
per acre.
that
After the War the Coalition Government set up a special Commission for the
purpose of considering and reporting
to Parliament what was the aggregate
number of working-class houses
should be erected
The
acre.
per
this was to
the
lessen
object of
possibility of a recurrence of what happened during the War, when the medical
branch of the Army returned one-third of
the men who offered to enlist as unfit for
service. In other words they were Class
C3 men.
This Commission came to the
conclusion that if the nation was to raise
healthy and physically perfect men in the
future, the erection of houses should be
restricted to a maximum of twelve houses
per acre. Assuming that the Belfast Corporation doubled the maximum recommended by that Commission and erected
twenty houses per acre at a cost of £5,000
per acre for the land, the ground rent
alone of each working man's house would
amount to 5s 6d per week.
In other words, the ground rent itself
would constitute the total amount that an
unskilled working-man ought to be asked
to pay for a house, because no workingman ought to be asked to pay more than
20 per cent. of his aggregate earnings for
house accommodation. If he is compelled to pay a larger proportion than
that for house accommodation, it is
utterly impossible for any working man
to get sufficient of the necessaries of life
to maintain the standard of comfort that
prevails in his class of life. I hope the
noble Viscount will be able to give the
House an answer to my question, because
the figures he has given us are very
attractive. I can see that the scheme
can be taken advantage of by unskilled
workers, because there are numerous
agencies in the community that will place
facilities at the disposal of the working
classes to enable them to take advantage
of these increased subsidies and other
benefits that local authorities will be
entitled to give to prospective purchasers
of houses under this Bill, but much will
depend upon the ground rent per house.
I regret that the space allotted to
houses to be erected under this Bill is not
greater. I tried last night to examine
the figures given by the Parliamentary
Secretary to the
the Ministry of Home
I
Affairs, and discovered that the
smallest room in the model house that was
exhibited in the smokeroom should never
be occupied by more than one person at
a time. It contains the minimum air
space that Dr. Newman, who is an
authority on public health, states is
essential for sleeping accommodation.
That minimum should be at least 300
cubic feet, and according to Dr. Newman's work on public health the air in
the smallest room in that model house
would require to be renewed no less than
eight times an hour, or sixty-four times
during the night.
I can speak with authority on this particular subject, because I have had
peculiar opportunities of observing the
effect of insufficient housing accommodation upon the health of the working
classes. I know there are a large number
of people who think it is of
very little
importance what cubic space there is in
the houses of the working classes. As
I have said before in this House,
I happen to have been engaged for a
period of over forty years in an occupation
which gave me wide opportunities of observing the effects of overcrowding, and
insufficient space, upon the health of the
working classes.
I have known of a working man living
in a small house with a large family, and
I have watched the progress of that family
for 15, 20, 25, and sometimes over 30
years, and I state here without the possibility of any Member justifiably denying it, that the evil consequence of overcrowding and sufficient air space is not
manifested during the early years of the
life of a working class family. It generally manifests itself after the children of
that family have finished their time in a
skilled occupation, and when they are exposed to the effects of the stress and strain
of their work. They more easily fall victims to any diseases that are prevalent if
they have been reared in circumstances
where they have had insufficient accommodation. Consequently, much unneccessary suffering is inflicted upon the working classes.
Notwithstanding the fact that I regret
the insufficiency of the space allotted to
these houses, I do say that this Bill is
a step in the right direction. I hope local
Housing Bill-
Second Reading.
10 NOVEMBER 1927
authorities in Northern Ireland will take
advantage of the Bill and although I
know this will not meet with the approval
of many Members of this House will
launch schemes for the construction of
working class houses by means of direct
labour, because, in every instance where
local authorities in Great Britain have
erected working class dwellings by direct
labour, they have built those dwellings at a cheaper rate than they could
have been erected by contract, and competent authorities have stated that the
material and workmanship were far
superior to those in houses erected by private enterprise. While I support the
Bill I regret the cubic space in the houses
to be erected is not what it ought to be
to enable the working classes to rear
healthy and strong men and women,
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I regret that the hon. Member did not get his copy of the Bill until
late last night. If the hon. Member, at
the close of our sitting on Tuesday, had
gone to the library he would have obtained a copy of the Bill without any
trouble.
Mr. DORMAN: I took the trouble to
come here yesterday to try to get a copy
of this and the other Bill that is to come
before us to-day and I could not obtain
a copy.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I got a copy from the library
two days ago and I took it there were
other copies available for hon. Members. The hon. Member referred to the
model house as containing 500 superficial
feet. I understand that that house is somewhat over 500 superficial feet, and I think
that if the hon. Senator inspects it he will
find that what I say is correct. As regards
the question of ground rents, that is a
matter that is not referred to in this Bill
and therefore we cannot deal with it here.
This Bill is to enable houses to be built
and it has nothing to do with the question
of rents. I would like to say one word as
regards direct labour. My experience
when I had the privilege of being a nember of the London County Council a good
many years ago was directly contrary to
that of Senator Dorman.
Mr. LESLIE: I was anxious-
Mr. SPEAKER:
It would be much
more
convenient if the hon. Member
would speak before the Government replies to the debate, because in order to
obtain a reply from the Government
another speech will have to be made by
the noble Viscount.
Mr. LESLIE: I was about to explain
that I did not rise before the noble Viscount because what I was going to say
would not require a reply.
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not think that
makes any difference. I think all the
speeches should be made before the
Government replies.
Mr. LESLIE: What I was about to
say was that I hoped that this advance,
or continued advance, along the lines of
the housing policy of the Government
would be noted outside the two Chambers
of Parliament as an indication of the fact
that this Parliament was engaged in
really big work in the interests of the
Province. I was somewhat afraid, as I
have no doubt other hon. Senators are
perhaps sometimes afraid, lest criticisms
which are directed in the newspapers,
and even for that matter in both Houses
of Parliament, against the Government
on more or less points of detail, details
sometimes in the Measures presented to
us, and sometimes in the administration
of those Measures, should attract an undue amount of attention, and really big
matters, such as the housing policy of
the Government, should be lost sight of.
Persons have mentioned this Bill to
me, basing their remarks on what they
had seen in the newspapers during its
progress in another place, and I have
heard the opinion expressed uniformly
that the Bill might be of use in the rural
areas as providing for the possibility of
the further erection of houses of the type
that are most required. I have not
heard any criticizm of the superficial
area of the houses. On the contrary, I
have heard it said that the smaller the
house a man can do with the better, because there is more chance of the rent
being within his means. For that reason,
I welcome the Bill.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill
be now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Rent Restrictions Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Rent and Mortgage Interest Restrictions
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move the Second Reading of this Bill,
which is designed to
to cope with two
abuses which have arisen under the Rent
Restriction Acts. The Bill need not detain us very long, I think, and I shall
confine myself to a brief indication of the
remedies which it proposes to effect.
As this House is aware, a furnished
house is exempted in some respects from
the provisions of the Rent Restrictions
Act, a fact which has led to some landlords adopting a practice of putting in a
nominal amount of furniture and letting
their houses as furnished houses. This
practice is clearly against the spirit of the
Acts and, accordingly, it is proposed, in
Clause 1 of the Bill now before you, that
a dwelling-house, the rateable value of
which is greater than £8 and which is subject to control, shall not be deemed to be
bona-fide let as a furnished house unless
it contains all such furniture as is reasonably required, having regard to the
character of the house. The same Clause
also provides that in future no house of
a valuation of £8 or under can,
in any
circumstances, be let as a furnished house,
the reason being that tenants of these
small houses seldom need a furnished
house, and the landlord's action in letting
them as furnished is simply a pretext for
evading the restrictions of the Acts.
I come now to Clause 2. Hon. Members are aware that it is possible for a
landlord under the former Rent Restriction Acts, to obtain possession of a house
when it is required for residence by himself or by certain specified relatives.
It
is provided in the Act of 1920, as amended
by the Act of 1925, that where a landlord
has so obtained possession of a house and
it transpires subsequently that the order
was made on misrepresentation or concealment of facts, the court may order the
landlord to pay the tenant adequate compensation for loss or damage incurred by
him as the result of the order.
Now this remedy only applies to cases
where the tenant has compelled the landlord to take him to court, and no provision
is made for the tenant who has received a
notice to quit, and who has come to the
conclusion that it is useless for him to
fight the matter, but who may, in fact,
have been induced to give up the house
on fraudulent representations. It is not
right that the tenant who has acted thus
should have no remedy, while the tenant
who forced his landlord into court should
be protected, and, accordingly, it is proposed in Clause 2 that the tenant shall
have his remedy, whether there have been
proceedings for possession or not.
It is also provided that the landlord,
when giving notice in writing to the tenant, shall state that he requires possession
of the house for himself or some specified
relation. It is hoped by this provision to
make it easier to obtain proof of misrepresentation on the part of the landlord,
whenever such has occurred.
I may say that the drafting of Subclause 2 of the second Clause does not
appear at present to be perfect, and I
hope, when the Committee stage is
reached, to move a slight amendment
which will make it clear that its provisions are not retrospective. I beg to
move.
. Mr. DORMAN: I rise to welcome this
Measure, because I think the conditions
that have been prevailing in Belfast
with regard to houses of the class dealt
with by this Bill have not been realised
as they should be. I, myself, know of
houses that in pre-war days were let at
3s 9d and 4s a week. The landlord has
placed in those houses furniture of the
value of 15s or £1 or 30s, and has then
charged the incoming tenant a rent of
from 15s to £1 a week on the pretence
that he was letting the house furnished.
The housing conditions in Belfast at
present are really very bad. I think
Belfast is probably the only large city
in Great Britain and Northern Ireland in
which one-room dwellers have increased
to the extent they have. For every oneroom dweller resident in Belfast before
the war there are to-day seven. That is
a shocking state of affairs to exist in any
progressive community, and it points to
the necessity of the Government and local
authorities concentrating their attention
on this problem.
Anyone who knows the congested
working-class areas cannot but be convinced that if the laws at present on the
Rent Restrictions Bill- 10 NOVEMBER 1927

Second Reading.
conditions under which these small tenements are offered. This is a case of the
Government having to protect some of the
poorer members of the working class from
some of their own class who are imposing
upon them, and I would like to know if
this protection will extend to those persons
who occupy apartments that have been let
to them by persons who own large buildings which they have divided and sub-let
as furnished apartments.
Statute Book were scientifically and
rigidly administered the conditions could
be improved. If an inspection of the
conditions of working-class dwellings in
Belfast was undertaken and a report was
published, it would shock the more fortunate section of the community to know
that so many of their fellow creatures
were living under such conditions. This
Measure is calculated to mitigate, in some
degree, those conditions inasmuch as it
will curtail the propensity of certain
landlords to take advantage of the housing shortage to profiteer at the expense
of those who have no alternative but to
pay the exorbitant rents demanded for socalled furnished houses.

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
desire to say how pleased I am that the
Government have introduced this Bill.
It is a Bill intended for the protection
of the working classes and, what is most
regrettable, it is to protect them against
unscrupulous members of their own class.
I noticed that Senator Dorman was not
able to refer to the bloated Capitalists in
this connection.
Mr. DORMAN: I never used the term.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I said
he was not able in this connection to refer to the bloated capitalists. It is quite
true that in regard to the abuses which
this Bill seeks to remove a state of affairs
has existed in Belfast that is deplorable.
Some members of the working class have
imposed on those in much worse circumstances than themselves and they have
compelled them to pay large rents for
small houses in which they have placed
some furniture. There is one point I
would like to bring before the notice of
the Government. I understand there are
a good many instances in the city where
persons have taken large buildings and
sub-let them into smaller tenancies. They
have provided a certain amount of furniture for the different tenants and they
have charged them exorbitant rents.
would like to know if the provisions of
this Bill will apply to such cases to protect these poor people who are suffering
at, in many cases, the hands of workingclass landlords.
I
It is quite apparent that it is
the shortage of houses that is responsible
for all this crowding into houses and other
buildings. If there were sufficient houses
people would not be driven to accept the
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to ask
how this Bill is going to affect those people
who have taken these furnished houses in
the past.
Hon. Senators have referred
to the fact that houses with a few articles
of furniture in them have been let as
furnished houses, and I would like to
know whether the people who took those
houses and occupy them at present will
have to continue to pay the rent they are
now paying, or whether the Bill will enable them to obtain a reduction in their
rent. Take the case of a house which
was occupied by a tenant from before the
War up to last year. The house then
became vacant and the landlord put into
it a sofa, a set of curtains, a table, and
perhaps a few pots and pans. Previous
to the War the rent of that house was
something like 5s a week, and with the
rise in the rates and the increase authorized by law, that rent was increased to
7s 6d or 8s a week. When the house
becomes vacant and the landlord puts
those few articles of furniture into it he
lets it at 25s a week. I would like to see
tenants who have taken houses under
those circumstances protected as well as
those tenants who may take such houses
in the future.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask the
noble Viscount if, when he is considering
the amendment of Sub-clause 2 of Clause
2, he will at the same time consider
whether we ought not to do something in
the
way of defining the word "subsequently" used in the first Sub-clause of
Clause 2. As it stands it seems to me
that the suggestion of misrepresentation
and so on can be made by an outgoing
tenant at any time, however long, after
he had given up possession of the house.
That is scarcely fair to the man from
whom he took the house. If a charge of
misrepresentation and so on and concealment of material facts is to be made, it
not unusual to require
it
require that
should be made within some specific
time, and not of great duration.

not know whether there is anything in

will consider the desirability of adopting

and 16 Geo. 5, c. 12, as to houses let with

VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: When
this Bill was last before the House two
questions were asked by Senator Joseph
Cunningham and Senator Barclay respectively. Senator Cunningham asked what
would be the position of persons who have
taken large buildings and let them into
smaller tenancies, and if they would come
within the provisions of this Bill. The
answer to that is in the affirmative.
Senator Barclay asked if existing tenancies would come under the operations of
the Bill. The answer to that is also in
the affirmative in the case of those tenancies which, if the letting had been unfurnished, would have been under control.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Further provisions as to
obtaining possession of houses by misrepresentation).
315 Rent Restrictions Bill

THE SENATE
The Price of Pork
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move the following Amendment:
Clause 2, page 3, line 10, at the end of
paragraph (b) (iii) add:
Provided that in the application of
sub-section (1) of section five of the
principal Act to a case where the landlord has given to the tenant a notice to
quit before the passing of this Act such
landlord shall give to the tenant within
fourteen days after the passing of this
Act the notice in writing required by
paragraph (d) and sub-division (iii) of
paragraph (e) of the said sub-section.
I should like to point out that there is
a misprint in the Amendment as it appears
on the Order Paper. In the last line but
one, after paragraph (d) the word "of"
should read "and". It is a printer's
mistake. As the Members of the Senate
will recollect, I stated in moving the
Second reading of this Bill that the drafting of Sub-clause 2 of Clause 2 was not
entirely free from objection. The object.
of that clause is to compel a landlord to
give to a tenant a written statement showing the purpose for which he requires the
house, and the Clause provided that this
notice shall be given at the time that the
notice to quit is being served. I think it
will be agreed that under ordinary cir
cumstances, that is the proper and fairest
time for giving the notice. It was, however, pointed out at a later stage of the
proceedings in another place, that the
Clause so drafted would render all notices
to quit served before the passing of this
Act and now running invalid, in that it
would be impossible for the landlord to
satisfy the requirements of the Clause,
and to show that he had served the notice
at the same time as the notice to quit.
The
The Government are of the opinion
that the best course is to give an interval
after the passing of the Act, within which
the necessary notice may be served in
cases where the notice to quit was served
before the passing of the Act.
Amendment accordingly provides that in
such cases notice shall be served within
fourteen days of the passing of the Act.
It may possibly occur to hon. Members
that this period is somewhat short, but in
the first place I would point out that by
this time the provisions of the Bill are
widely known, and all persons concerned
in the administration of property will be
aware of its requirements. In the second
place the first Quarter Sessions after the
passing of this Bill will be held in January
next, one month after the Bill is likely
to become law, and it is desirable that the
landlord should have as long as possible
within which to serve his notice, and that
the tenant should have a reasonable
opportunity of considering his position
and determining whether he will resist
the proceedings in the light of the in-
Accordformation given in the notice.
ingly, it seems fair to divide the available interval equally between the two
parties. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to welcome the
introduction of this Amendment, because
at the present time there are a large number of landlords who are exercising the
right of serving notices for the purpose
of obtaining part of the arrears of rent
due under the tenancy. This particular
Amendment will make it compulsory upon
landlords who have been resorting to that
method of getting part of the arrears of
rent to serve, at the time they serve the
notice to quit, a notice specifying the
purpose for which they require the house.
I welcome the Amendment, as it will protect many of those persons who are receiving these notices from day to day.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, order to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Short title), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
:

House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendment.
The Price of Pork.
QUESTION TO MINISTER.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay:
:-
To ask the Leader of the House if he
can state the reason for the great dis-
The Price of Pork
The Price of Pork
15 NOVEMBER 1927
crepancy in the price of pork as sold
by the producer and bought by the
consumer; and whether the Government will take steps to stop profiteering
in this commodity.
Mr. BARCLAY: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House the question which
stands in my name on the Order Paper.
I do not intend to say very much, because
it is a subject that offers an opportunity
to every Member of the House to say
something if they so wish. It is a question that severely touches the pockets of
two sections of the community. The producer does not get sufficient for the
trouble and labour he expends to put the
article on the market, and the consumer
has to pay far too much for the article
when he comes to buy it. The reason
that prompted me to ask this question
was that I saw in the newspaper a few
nights ago a report of a farmers' meeting
that was held in the County of Fermanagh I think it was in the town of
Enniskillen at which a number of farmers gave expression to their feelings as
to the injustice inflicted upon them,
owing to those engaged in the pork trade
not receiving the price they were entitled
to.
One farmer said that pork could be
bought in the market at Enniskillen at
from 48s to 52s a cwt. It is brought to
Belfast and sold there at a price 100 per
cent. higher. A lady from whom I made
inquiry to-day told me that of late she
had not bought so much ham or bacon as
she did some time ago, and the reason
she gave was that it was too dear and she
could not afford to pay the high price
that was charged for it. The result was
that she and her family had to deny themselves this very important article of food.
She informed me that a few weeks she
ago
was paying 2s 3d per lb. for ham. Then
there was a reduction to Is 10d per lb.,
and last week she paid 1s 8d a lb, for
ham. Therefore, you have the fact that
pork can be bought in the town of Enniskillen for 48s a cwt., and in the city of
Belfast it is retailed over the counter at
186s 8d a cwt.
I would like to know where the
difference between £2 8s Od and £9 6s 8d
goes to. I think the Government should
interest itself in this question, because in
the past the Government has done a great
deal and has spent a considerable amount
of money to encourage the agricultural
industry in Northern Ireland. The
Minister of Agriculture nas not spared
himself. He has spent both time and
energy in visiting the different parts of
Ulster encouraging the farmers to experiment in different ways. Some of those
experiments have not proved very profitable to the farmer, who has been put to
a loss in connection with them. I think
it was and is the intention of the Government that the farmers should have the
benefit of the money that was provided
for this purpose, but we find that instead
of the producer and the consumer benefiting, both are losing. The farmer is
not getting a fair price for his produce,
and the consumer has to pay too much for
the necessaries of life the farmer produces. Therefore I think the Government should inquire whether some step
cannot be taken to remedy this evil, so
that justice may be done to both producer
and consumer.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I would like
to support the remarks of Senator Barclay on this subject. I am sure all the
Members of this House are aware that
this question has been the subject of considerable agitation in the country for
some time, and it is a very vital question with the people in the country, and
particularly the poorer people. There is
an old saying that a pig pays a tenant's
rent, and that is one of the reasons why
the Government should do something to
stabilise the price of pork. Two or three
counties have sent deputations either to
the Government or the Ministry of Agriculture on this subject. If pork can at
one period be sold in the market at 106s
or 120s a cwt, as it was eight or ten
months ago, and then drop down to what
Senator Barclay states is the present
price, namely, 48s a cwt, there must be
something radically wrong.
Senator Barclay mentioned as the price
of ham in the shops in Belfast, a sum
which I thought was rather high, but I
dare say he is correct. We do not find
the price of pork fluctuating in the shops
in the same way that it does in the country
markets. If the Government could help
the country in any way in this matter
it would be one of the most beneficial acts
they could perform. When the poor
farmer has to pay, as he is now paying,
20s a bag for yellow meal, and has to buy
his young sucker pigs for £2 or £3 and
The Price of Pork
The Price of Pork
THE SENATE
[Mr. Porter Porter]
keep them for six months, and then sell
them at 48s a cwt.-and no pork is being
bought over 2 cwt. in weight-he will not
make much money.
Mr. DORMAN: This is another instance of the folly of producing things for
profit instead of for use. Farmers are not
producing pork for the purpose of feeding
the people of Northern Ireland. They
are producing pork for the purpose of
making a profit, and the larger the profit
the farmers get, the better they are satisfied with the result of their labour. The
man who buys from the farmer does not
buy to provide for the needs of the people,
but that he also may make a profit. The
man who cures the pork when it comes
to his factory does not cure it, and will
not cure it unless he knows there is a
certainty of his making a profit, and the
higher the profit he makes the more satisfied he is. I am very pleased to see the
hon. Senators behind me coming over to
the philosophy of the
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is
rather wandering from the question before
the House. He appears to be giving an
essay on a subject that may be very interesting, but it has nothing to do with the
question.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to express my
approval of the question which appears
on the Order Paper. It is calculated to
draw the attention of the public to the
wrongs that are inflicted upon consumers
owing to the vast profits that are made
between the time food leaves the producer
and reaches the consumer.
This
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
question of the discrepancy between the
price of pork received by the producer
and that paid by the consumer, is not, of
course, purely a local question. It was
considered inter alia in its wider range by
the Departmental Committee established
under the Chairmanship of the Marquess
of Linlithgow in 1923, I think, by the
Ministry of Agriculture and Fisheries, to
inquire into the distribution and prices
of agricultural produce. If the hon.
Member will refer to the report of that
committee he will find that the position
regarding the spread between consumers'
and producers' prices has been examined
at some length. While it cannot be
satisfactorily dealt with in a reply to a
Parliamentary question, I would draw
attention to the fact that the Linlithgow
Committee, after stating that “a marked
difference beween the position of primary
producers and that of distributors is a
common feature of every industrial depression in this and other countries," go
on to say that " apart from the special
circumstances arising out of the economic
conditions created by the war, a stage of
national development has been reached,
of such complexity and interdependence,
that the fundamental problem of the
cheap and efficient distribution of foodstuffs is one of the most pressing and important with which modern society is
faced. Primary producers, whose produce
is sold in the wholesale markets in competition with supplies from abroad, are
restricted in their returns by the prices
realized for the imported article. The
prices they receive are largely fixed by
forces outside their individual or even
collective control."
So far as the local aspect of this matter
is concerned, the immediate question
turns, perhaps, on the stabilisation
of prices. It is admitted that fluctuations in the price of pork have been
acute from time to time, and occasionally
unexpected. At conferences summoned
by the Ministry of Agriculture the whole
question of stabilisation has been gone
into by representatives of the Ulster
Farmers' Union, the Ulster Agricultural
Organization Society and the bacon curing interest. While the Ministry would
welcome an arrangement whereby an advance indication of probable prices would
be available for the farming community,
it is clear that there would be serious
difficulties in putting such an arrangement into force as a voluntary measure.
Official machinery for the fixing of prices
is clearly out of the question.
I understand that in several districts
in Northern Ireland there has been a tendency to sell fat pigs by live weight, and
that in some quarters serious consideration has been given to the question of
catering for the British pork market in
Great Britain. These two factors will,
no doubt, tend towards the stabilisation
of prices.
I propose to circulate, in connection
with this matter, a lengthy tabular
statement showing the prices of Northern
Ireland pork and Irish bacon and hams
from the beginning of July to the end of
October of this year.
15 NOVEMBER 1927
The Price of Pork

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 16th November, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In the absence of my noble
Friend, the Leader of the House, I beg
to move, That Standing Order No. XXV
be suspended for this day's Sitting in respect of the Rent and Mortgage Interest
Restrictions (Amendment) Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Housing Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill without any
Amendment.
Rent and Mortgage Interest Restrictions
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for Receiving Report of Amendment read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move, That this
Report be now received.
On the Second Reading of the Bill,
Senator Leslie suggested that consideration should be given to the word "Subsequently" in line 25 of Clause 2, Subclause (1). I think the idea the hon.
Member had in his mind was that the
word "subsequently" was too wide, as it
would enable the tenant to bring an
action many years after his cause of
action had arisen. It may be pointed
out, however, that this Sub-clause merely
extends to persons who have not resisted
the landlord in Court the remedy of
false representation which tenants who
had resisted legal proceedings already
have got. The wording of the Section is
precisely the same in this respect as is the
original Section of the principal Act of
1920, which is still in operation in respect
of what might be termed Court cases. No
hardship seems to have resulted from the
use of the word in the original Section,
although this Section has been in force
for a number of years, and actual
experience does not show any necessity
for altering this form of words.
the
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to thank
the noble Viscount for looking into the
question, and I am glad to have elicited
the information he has just given to the
House. I quite agree that in
circumstances it would be a great mistake
to alter a phrase which has been
sanctioned by custom and found efficacious
in the past.
I think there is no doubt
that the resources will be sufficient to deal
with the possible difficulty I anticipated
if it should unfortunately ever arise.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
•
The said Amendment was accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill, with an
Amendment, to which they desire the
concurrence of the Commons.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 29th November, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
The Registrar-General's 5th Annual
Report containing general abstracts of
Marriages, Births and Deaths registered
in Northern Ireland during the year 1926.
Report of the Ministry of Finance for
Northern Ireland on the Administration
of Charities in Northern Ireland for the
year ended 31st December, 1926.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Dangerous Drugs (Approved institutions) (Northern Ireland) Order (1927).

ordered to lie on the Table.

on the Transit, Prices, and Marketing of
Agricultural Produce."
Presented (by Command), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Moved: That Standing Order No.
XXV. be suspended for this day's sitting,
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Intoxicating Liquor and
Licensing Bill.-(Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
PARLIAMENT HOUSE AT
STORMONT.
Mr. LONG: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether he can state what
progress is being made with regard to the
building of the new Parliament House at
Stormont, and whether he is in a position to say when the building is likely to
be completed; and whether it is a fact
that the expense of renting the present
temporary Parliament House is borne by
the Imperial Exchequer.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I have to inform the hon.
Gentleman that the basement work in
connection with the new Parliament
Buildings at Stormont has now practically been completed, and that, as explained in answer to a similar question
in another place on the 22nd instant, a
contract for the superstructure of the
building has now been advertised. The
Imperial Office of Works, who are the responsible authorities, are prepared to
prosecute, with all possible expedition,
the work yet to be completed, but in an
undertaking of this magnitude it would
be unwise to indulge in any forecast of
definite or even approximate dates. The
Government has lost no opportunity of
stressing the importance of the new Parliament House being available at the
earliest possible moment. I suggest to
the hon. Member that he should avail himself of the kind invitation of the contractors to see, to-morrow morning, the progress that is being made with the work.
The reply to the latter part of the
question is in the affirmative.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Arising out of the answer of the noble
Viscount, I should like to ask him
whether, in any further contracts that
are given in connection with the building
of the Parliament House, the Government
will take steps to safeguard the County
Council of Down against liability
they might incur on account of the cutting up of the road leading to Stormont.
During the progress of the work that has
already been done there, the road which.
was used for the cartage of stones was
very badly cut up. The Down County
Council was involved in a law case over
the matter and owing to something being
omitted from the contract the County
Council lost the case and had to bear the
cost of keeping the road in repair.
hope that in any contract that may be
I
329 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

29 NOVEMBER 1927
Second Reading.
placed for the building of the superstructure, the County Council of Down
will be safeguarded in this respect.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I cannot say whether any
steps have so far been taken, but I will
certainly bring the matter before the
notice of those responsible
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time.-Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Its
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In the regrettable absence,
through illness, of the noble Viscount,
the Leader of the House, I beg to move
the Second Reading of this Bill.
object, as hon. Members all know, is to
carry more fully into effect the general
intention of the Intoxicating Liquor Act
of 1923, and to remedy certain technical
defects of that Act which have been disclosed by actual practice and experience..
This House will recall that on the
Second Reading of the Intoxicating
Liquor Bill in 1923 we were favoured
with a visit from the Prime Minister,
who spoke eloquently in its support and
expressed high hopes that it would do
much to promote the cause of true temperance throughout Northern Ireland.
That those hopes have been realized will
be admitted by all here to-day, and there
must be very few people, I think, who
will deny that the Act of 1923 has justi
fied its place on the Statute Book.
The Bill before us is an Amending
Bill and it will, therefore, be necessary,
in explaining its general effect, to keep
before us the main provisions of the
parent Act, which is being amended. The
title of that Act reads as follows:
"An Act to make provision for the
closing on Sunday and Christmas Day
of certain premises licensed for the sale
of intoxicating liquor, the abolition of
certain off-licences, and the settlement
of claims for compensation arising
thereout; to impose restrictions on the
sale of methylated spirits and to amend
the law relating to the sale and supply of
intoxicating liquor by retail, and for
purposes connected therewith."
Clause 1 of the present Bill must
be read in conjunction with Section 2 of
the 1923 Act. It will be remembered
that that Section states that it shall not
be lawful for any person to carry on, in
any part of any premises licensed for the
sale of intoxicating liquor by retail for
consumption off the premises, any trade
other than that trade or the sale of table
waters. One of the effects of that Section has been that the legality of selling
liquor by wholesale, in off-licensed premises, has been rendered doubtful, and
as such a prohibition was never intended,
it is made clear in Clause 1, Sub-clause
(1), that henceforth it shall be lawful for
the holder of a retail off-licence to carry
on in the same premises the sale of liquor
by wholesale.
Sub-clause (2) of the first Clause, besides providing a more satisfactory wording for Section 6 (1) of the 1923 Act, has.
also the following effect:
(1) It permits the inclusion in hotels,
of legitimate subsidiary businesses as
hairdressers' shops, stock rooms, etc.;
(2) It definitely permits publicans to
carry on the sale of intoxicating liquor
by wholesale in the same premises as
are used for their on-licence business;
(3) It prohibits the sale of nonintoxicants in a public house during
closing hours. The necessity for such
a provision will, of course, be quite
clear;
(4) It permits publicans to provide
temporary stabling accommodation, not
only during opening but also during
closing hours. This concession, which
was not allowed by the 1923 Act, has
been made to convenience farmers who
arrive in country towns at the early
hours of the morning, especially on
Fair days;
331 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
(5) It prescribes a specific penalty
for mixed trading. This is necessary,
as it was doubtful whether any penalty
could be imposed under the 1923 Act,
for a breach of the statutory prohibition regarding mixed business.
Now I come to Clause 2. Clause 2 relates to the hours during which liquor
may be consumed in hotels. Sub-clause
(1) provides that the hours during which
it is legal to sell or consume drink in a
hotel on Sunday or Christmas Day are
ordinary week-day hours, that is, 10 a.m.
to 9 p.m. or 10 p.m., as the case may be,
and it will no longer be necessary for a
bona-fide resident in the hotel to have a
substantial meal in order to obtain a
drink. Sub-clause (2) makes it unlawful
for the proprietor of an hotel to allow the
consumption of liquor after closing hours,
unless a dinner or other entertainment is
being held and he has secured a permit.
from the police authorities authorising
such consumption.
Clause 3 is a lengthy Clause, and will
require some detailed explanation. As
regards the first Sub-clause, under Section 9 of the 1923 Act, new licences were
allowed to be issued for premises which
were licensed between 1920 and 1923, and
there was nothing in the law to prevent a
man from obtaining compensation and
then applying for a new licence in respect
of the same premises. It is now proposed
definitely to put an end to the possibility
of a compensated licensee obtaining a new
licence for the same premises on the
ground that he was licensed in 1920.
Sub-clause (2) provides that where a
new licence is substituted for a licence
previously in force, the new licence shall
be of the same quality as the old; that
is, if the old licence was an off-licence,
the new one shall also be an off-licence,
and so on. Sub-clause (3) provides that no
licence which was an early-closing licence
at the date of the passing of the 1923
Act can be extended to a full licence
under Section 9 (a) of the 1923 Act, and
every licence so extended shall revert to
an early-closing licence.
I will pass over Sub-clauses (4) and (5),
which are not very important, and I will
come to Sub-clause (6) Under Section
9 (c) of the Act of 1923, the issue of a
new licence is permitted in a ward or
electoral division where the population
has increased by 25 per cent. since the
last census and the applicant must surrender two licences of the same quality
in substitution for the new one.
Under
the Sub-clause before us a new onlicence is permitted for the surrender of
one on-licence and one off-licence, but the
surrender of these two licences must involve the cessation of the sale of intoxicating liquor in two different sets of premises. It is also made clear that the
phrase "the last census" means the census
of 1911, that is, the last census before the
passing of the 1923 Act.
Clause 4 deals with the provisions as
to licences for the sale of wine and sweets,
and I may, perhaps, here remark that
the word 'sweets" means, in this connection, fermented liquor made from
fruit and sugar. Under Sub-clause ()
the future issue of a wine retailer's onlicence or a sweets retailer's on-licence is
absolutely prohibited, and the future
issue of sweets retailer's off-licences is restricted to premises where spirits or beer
off-licences are in force. These licences
have hitherto escaped the provisions of
the 1923 Act, and in view of the, what I
might call pernicious, concoctions which
are at present being sold, it has been
thought advisable to restrict their issue
in future.
The chief effect of Sub-clause (2) is to
provide that no future wine off-licences
shall be issued, except in respect of premises continuously licensed since before
the passing of the 1923 Act. Sub-clause
(3) provides that no person shall be entitled to take out or hold a wine retailer's
off-licence for any premises in respect of
which compensation was awarded under
the former Act, while Sub-clause (4) confers upon wine off-licensees the same
benefit as is conferred upon publicans and
beer or spirits off-licensees by Clause 3,
Sub-clause (4), of the present Bill. The
last Sub-clause of Clause 4 exempts from
all the foregoing provisions of the Clause
those wine licences which are issued to
chemists and which authorize the supply
of wine only on presentation of a doctor's
prescription.
Clause 5 allows the issue of a spirit
dealer's licence to wholesalers, which will
authorize the sale of quantities of not less
than 2 gallons consisting of different
denominations, such as whisky, brandy,
rum, etc., to holders of retailer's on- or
off-licences. Perhaps I might explain to
333 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

29 NOVEMBER 1927
Second Reading
or
two
the House that under the former Act a
man could sell two gallons of whisky
gallons of
not
brandy,
less. Under this Clause, so long as the
total amount he sells is two gallons or
over, he can distribute the two gallons
among the different denominations of
spirits. Hitherto the wholesaler has
been prohibited from selling in these
smaller quantities and inconvenience has
been caused to certain retailers which it
seemed unfair to impose upon them.
It
will be observed that a somewhat similar
concession is also allowed to wholesale
cider dealers.
Clause 6 is administrative and will ensure that the description of the licensed
premises in the Excise licence and in the
magistrates' renewal certificate is always
the same, and does not vary from year
to year, except in accordance with a definite order of the court.
Clause 7 is fairly simple and calls
for little explanation, except, perhaps,
with regard to sub-Clause (4). Hon.
Members are aware, of course, that under
the 1923 Act if a publican gets three endorsements on his licence that licence
automatically becomes null and void, but
where two or more persons consecutively
possess licensed premises, four, and not
three, endorsements are necessary to cancel the licence and they must all occur
within five years. It will be appreciated
that these provisions gave scope for
evasion and the present sub-Clause is designed to put an end to it. It provides
that in all cases three endorsements shall
annul the licence with, however, the further provision that where there has been
a period of five years with, so to speak, a
clean sheet, the licence shall be considered
to be free of all endorsements.
Clause 8 provides a definition for
the term "licence" as used in the 1923
Act, the chief practical result being that
by bringing wine licences within the provisions of Sections 2 and 3 of the former.
Act the payment of compensation to
holders of extinguished wine licences is
legalised. It also brings wine licences
under Section 8 of the 1923 Act and renders them endorsable.

I come now to Clause 9, which deals
with methylated spirits and methylated
ether. It has been found necessary to
tighten up the restrictions imposed by the
1923 Act upon the sale of these products
and sub-Clause (1) will, it is hoped, go
far towards affecting that end. It gives
the Ministry of Home Affairs power to
make regulations prescribing the kinds of
accounts and records that are to be kept
by the retailers, and it will enable the
Ministry to provide for a record of all
purchases being kept, thus devising a
check on the retailer. It also provides that these regulations shall apply
to all retail sales and there will no longer
be a hiatus between the sales up to one
gallon and the sales over four gallons.
Hitherto, as the House is probably aware,
no record of sale was required for
quantities between these two figures and
the result, as one might expect, has been
abuse. The remaining sub-Clauses are
simple and do not require any explanation from me.
"
Clause 10 also is simple and is concerned with restrictions to be placed
upon the sale of alcohol other than ethyl
alcohol, which is, of course, the alcohol
of which wine, beer, whisky, etc., are
composed. The Clause has been inserted
in view of the increasing sale by
publicans of iso-propyl alcohol to fortify
cheap wines. This type of alcohol is
practically duty free and can be purchased very cheaply. It is a by-product
of petroleum and in its effect is much more
toxic than whisky.
Clause 11 amends the law with regard
to the registration of clubs. With regard
to the first Sub-clause, I may explain
that at present there are certain specified
grounds of objection to the grant or renewal of a club certificate, and if no objection is lodged, the certificate is granted.
automatically. The Sub-clause now before us is designed to make it very difficult for any club, other than a bona-fide
club, to obtain a certificate, by placing
on the clubs themselves the onus
proving affirmatively certain specified
matters which, as hon. Members will see,
are set forth under the heading (a) of
the first Sub-clause. I
mention
may
that the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister for Home Affairs, speaking on this
Clause in another place, recited some
figures with regard to clubs which were
certainly very striking and which will,
perhaps, bear repetition.
of
He said that registered clubs in Northern Ireland had increased, since the 1923
Act, by seventeen, their membership had
335 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
increased by less than 4,000, but their
expenditure on the purchase of liquor had
mounted from £38,426 to £76,202-that
is, by considerably more than £37,000.
That is sufficiently clear evidence, I
think, that certain of these clubs are not
genuine, and the Government are justified
in asking for additional powers which will
do much towards weeding out the mala.
fide clubs. Incidentally, I may point
out that Sub-clause (3) gives power to a
district-inspector to enter a registered
club at any time, and it will no longer be
necessary for him to obtain a warrant in
open court.
as
Clause 12 seeks to clarify the law relating to the issue of liquor licences to
theatres and music halls. It provides
that no building shall be used a
theatre or music hall without a licence
for that purpose having first been obtained
from the licensing court, and in this connection the word "licence" refers to a
licence for the theatre as a theatre, and
has nothing to do with a liquor licence.
The conditions governing its issue are
contained in the Second Schedule to the
Bill, and the police and the sanitary
authority are given power to object if
the proprietor refuses to comply with the
sanitary authority's regulations regarding proper accommodation, fire escapes,
etc. When this "theatre" or "entertainment" licence has been obtained,
application may then be made for a liquor
licence, and objections may be urged by
the police to such an application just as
if it were an application for a publican's
licence.
I must apologize to the House for
taking up a good deal of time in an exposition of the various aspects of the Bill.
I trust, however, that I have made myself sufficiently clear, and I shall be happy
at a later stage to go more fully into
any points that may require elucidation.
I beg to move.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I do not intend
to say very much at the present time with
regard to this Bill. My attitude towards
strong drink in general is very neatly expressed by a modern writer who said he
regarded alcohol in the same way that
he regarded a dictionary. He did not
often have recourse to it, but it was very
useful to have when it was required. I
sympathize very much with the Government in their effort to steer a fair and
even course between the two extremes of
opinion in this country, and I certainly
welcome this Bill as very valuable legislation. There is very little in it that I
take serious objection to. The new
Clause with regard to the sale of methylated spirits and ether, and the provision
to prevent the doping of wine and so on
should prove very useful. I followed
closely the passage of the Bill in another
place. I listened to some of the speeches
and I read a good deal of others, and
some of the arguments put forward by
the Opposition had a good deal of my
sympathy from a common sense point of
view.
I only intend, at this stage, to refer to
two points in the Bill. I do think the
Government should have agreed to admit
an amendment to remove all restrictions
on persons resident in hotels. There has
been a lot said about the tourist traffic
but I do not want to refer to tourists in
general. It has always caused me a
certain amount of detestation to think
that a person coming here from England
or
Wales on business, or for any other
purpose, should find himself up against
something which makes him think that he
is in a different country. We should try to
preserve the same social conditions as
exist in England, and it is not pleasant
to think that a person coming over here
and staying in an hotel has reason to
believe that he is in a less pleasant and
sociable country than the one he is accustomed to live in. A person resident
in an hotel, if he knows he cannot get
drink at a certain hour of the day, will
have that drink sent to his bedroom, and
I think that is a practice that would be
objected to by those who have very strong
temperance ideas. It leads to
pernicious habit of secret drinking. I
think that a person living in an hotel
should have all the comforts he would
have if he were residing in his own house.
In the course of their business commercial
travellers and other people are, unfortunately, obliged to live in hotels and I do
not see why they should be under any
restrictions whatever.
the
The only other part of the Bill I want
to refer to is Sub-clause 3 of Clause 3.
I understand that this affects about ten
traders, all in the County of Fermanagh,
and that no other traders in the entire
Six Counties are affected by it. It seems
that this is special legislation dealing
with these particular traders. They had
early-closing licences prior to the passing
337 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

29 NOVEMBER 1927
Second Reading
of the 1923 Act, and on the passing of
that Act they applied to the County
Court Judge for a full-time licence, and
it was granted. Since then they have
been carrying on their business as fulltime licensees, and no objection has been
taken to that position of affairs. Now
this special legislation will make them revert to their former position as earlyclosing licensees, and will deprive them of
one hour's trading, that between 8 and 9
o'clock at night, which is the most important hour of their day's business.
I do not think it is fair that the
Government should pass special legislation relating solely to these very few
traders, and the Government would be
doing a very fair, reasonable, and kind
act to these particular traders by leaving
them alone and letting them enjoy the
class of trade they have had since the
passing of the 1923 Act. With regard
to the remainder of the Bill, as I have
said, I do not take any objection to it. I
think it is a very good Measure, and I am
prepared to support the Second Reading.
Mr. DORMAN: I do not profess to
have any knowledge of the licensed trade.
I settled this question for myself more
than fifty years ago, having for that
period been a total abstainer. It appears
to
me there are certain provisions
in this Bill which deprive the
citizens of common rights that even those
who are total abstainers and may be designated Pussyfoots regard as being unfair,
because they recognize that the rights
and privileges they claim for themselves
should be accorded to others.
I especially want to draw attention to the Clause
which has been referred to by Senator
Glendinning, the Clause which deprives
the man resident, under ordinary circumstances, in an hotel of the right of
extending to his guests in licensed premises an opportunity of participating in
social intercourse and having liquor
during that social intercourse. There
are a very large number of individuals in
Ireland, and in Northern Ireland also,
who make licensed hotels their ordinary
home, and it seems to me that this Clause
will deprive those men of a right they
could exercise in a temperance hotel in
the same locality.
Assuming that I was living in licensed
premises and had some friends calling on
me on a visit, I would be deprived of the
liberty of giving them hospitality in the
form of intoxicating liquor, whereas if
I was resident in a temperance hotel, in
the same town or even in the same street,
I might bring in whatever intoxicating
refreshments I might choose for the purpose of offering hospitality to friends in
my apartments.
Sub-clause (1) of Clause 2 states:-
Where such liquor is sold to or
consumed by a guest or traveller who
has lodged for the preceding night or
has in writing engaged a room for the
succeeding night in such hotel at or during the hours specified for the purpose
of the sale of intoxicating liquor in
article 1 of Part I of the Second Schedule to that Act.
Sub-clause (2) goes on to say:
Where any dinner or other entertainment is held in an hotel or restaurant which is licensed for the sale of
intoxicating liquor, it shall not be lawful for the proprietor or manager
thereof to allow the consumption of intoxicating liquor, by persons attending
such dinner or entertainment, at hours
other than those specified for the purpose of the sale of intoxicating liquor
by the Act of 1923 or any other enactment:
I want to know if all such residents in
hotels will be deprived of the right of
giving this hospitality to their friends
after licensed hours. There is another
question I would like to refer to, and it
also has been touched upon by Senator
Glendinning. That is in regard to Subclause (3) of Clause 3. It seems to me to
be foolish for the Government to extend
a right, such as has been extended to a
small number of licence holders in the
County of Fermanagh, and then shortly
afterwards to deprive them of that very
same right. I understand that the hour
that was added to their licensed hours
was the most important hour of the day
from the point of view of their business.
It is certainly the hour of the day when
those who have been engaged in a hard
day's toil, during the summer season,
would be inclined to resort to licensed
premises for the purpose of obtaining a
stimulant.
I understand that on the faith of the
Government continuing this right to this
339 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Dorman]
small number of licence holders, they have
expended fairly large sums of money in
the extension and improvement of their
premises, looking forward to recovering
the cost from the proceeds of the extra
hour during which they would be allowed
to keep open. We all profess to be lovers
of liberty, and there is no part of the
world where we hear so much about
liberty as we do in this Province. Therefore, we ought to be prepared to see that
these individuals are not deprived of this
particular right. I thoroughly appreciate the efforts of the Government to
restrict the sale of deleterious spirits,
and I hope that on the Committee stage
steps will be taken to preserve for these
Fermanagh licence holders the right
which the Government itself and the
court has given to them.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: When
the Act of 1923 was passed I considered
there was a very unfair restriction imposed on grown-up people throughout the
Six Counties, and I am sorry to say the
Government has not been guilty of any
rash action in any Amendments they have
put forward to remove that restriction.
Their Amendments are of a very minor
character and of very little
consequence.
a
have evidently attempted
course.
Ι
to
They
take
middle
know
that the views of the Government
on this particular question are very
strong, and perhaps that would account
for the very weak action they have taken.
It is unfortunate that when a Bill dealing
with intoxicating liquor is introduced we
have wiseacres on both sides telling us,
on one side, of the benefits to be derived
from the consumption of alcohol, how a
little of it makes the tired workman
vigorous and energetic, and that if the
consumption is reduced the Chancellor of
the Exchequer will be stranded financially.
On the other hand we have people
telling us that it is the ruination of the
homes of the people and of their health,
and we are actually told it is the cause of
the present unemployment. It is very
difficult for one to decide which of the
two sides to take notice of.
From my
own experience I have come to the conclusion that it is a matter of agreeing
that it is only the excess consumption of
alcohol which makes it injurious. I have
mixed with the workers very closely on a
Friday night when they are paid, and on
a Saturday night, and I must admit that
if alcohol is taken in moderation there is
certainly some benefit to be derived from
it. I do know that if one feels mentally
tired and unable to sleep, and one is
lucky enough to have spirits in the house
they act as a great soother and put one
to sleep. I have experienced it, and I
know it is also very good for indigestion.
I consider that in this House we have
a calm atmosphere, with reasonable common-sense minds, that should be able to
view this Bill from an impartial standpoint. In my opinion, further Amendments in this Bill are necessary.
I remember very well that on the first dry
Sunday after the 1923 Act came into
operation even horses, cows, and donkeys
that happened to be stabled on licensed
premises had to go "dry," because the
police in some districts would not allow
the men to attend them. I see that in
this Bill the Government has made some
little improvement in that respect, and
they have now given permission for temporary stabling and for accommodation
to be given for vehicles and animals on
licensed premises. I look upon that concession as a great boon, particularly to
those in the country districts. There
are men there who set out early in the
morning, probably on a stormy and wet
morning, when it may also be snowing,
to go to a fair or market, and, unfortunately, when they come to the first inn
it is closed. They cannot even put their
horses up for a rest or to give them a feed.
This Bill has removed that injustice
to some extent, and now the poor woman
trudging along the road with a donkey
will be able to have the donkey stabled.
But the Government has not given any
consideration to the woman in charge of
the donkey, or to the man in charge of
the horse or motor vehicle, and I think
the Government should still further
amend the Bill by enabling accommodation to be provided for the persons in
charge of these horses and carts and other
vehicles. Surely the Government cannot expect that a person in charge of an
animal is going to rest in the stable until
such time as the fair or market may be
opened. A short time ago I read a book
-I think it was "The Cloister and the
Hearth"-in which travellers were advised to lie close to the cow, where they
would get some little heat. Probably it
is intended that these poor people should
lie close to the animal to keep warm instead of being allowed in a room of the
inn to warm themselves at the fire.
341 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

29 NOVEMBER 1927
Second Reading
Although there has been considerable
clamour against clubs, the Government
have not interfered with the hours of
clubs, but they have decided to require
the district-inspector to exercise supervision over club premises. I quite agree
that that is necessary, because since Sunday closing was established a considerable
number of clubs have sprung up for purposes for which I do not think clubs
were originally intended. I was responsible for the formation of one club, probably the largest in existence in Northern
Ireland. A club is intended to prevent
excessive drinking. It is intended to provide recreation and other amusements for
the members of the club, so that they
will not be inclined to drink to excess,
and from my experience I must admit
that clubs have had that result. I have
known men who were excessive drinkers,
and when they have become good billiard
players they were quite willing to spend
their time playing games, and they forgot
about drink. From my own observations
I know that to be a fact.
Therefore, I say that clubs were not
intended for the purpose for which some
of those that have recently been established are being used at the present time,
and I am very pleased that the Government, while they have not interfered with
the hours, have decided to exercise supervision over clubs and to see that they are
used for the purpose for which they were
intended. I believe that will result in
the abolition of the bogus clubs that have
been set up since the passing of the Act
of 1923.
With regard to hotels, I think the
Government have done wrong in not
giving a full license to hotels and entire
freedom to the person who makes an hotel
his home. Surely if the Government
were satisfied to grant unlimited hours
for drink in the clubs, in workmen's
clubs as well as in the better class clubs,
they are, in fairness, bound to give the
same liberty to those residents and other
travellers who are continuously making
use of hotels. I remember the great fight
there was in England before workmen's
clubs had the same liberty as they
enjoy to-day. At that time the argument was used that all men are equal in
the sight of the law, and that workmen
were just as much entitled to a club and
its privileges as employers and other
better-class persons. The people who use
an hotel as their home or as a temporary
residence are just as much entitled to this
freedom as are the working-men members
of a club in either the city or the country,
and I am sorry the Government have not,
so far, seen their way to amend the 1923
Act in that direction.
It must be injurious to the North of
Ireland when people come from England
and other places where they have been
accustomed to the liberties and privileges
enjoyed by clubs and licensed houses, to
find that they are thus restricted in the
North of Ireland. Personally, I cannot
see how the Government are going to
carry out this restriction. If a person
cannot obtain drink during ordinary
hours, I do not see how they are to be
prevented having drink in their bedroom
in the hotel. It is merely leaving the
gate open for persons to violate the law.
If an Amendment is put down on the
Committee stage to delete Sub-section 1
of Clause 2 I shall vote in favour of it.
If the latter portion of that Sub-clause
was deleted it would leave hotels with the
full liberty that, in my opinion, they are
entitled to. On the whole, I have pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of
the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
With regard to the restrictions on hotels,
I made inquiries in official quarters, for
the benefit of tourists and strangers and
people who are bona-fide occupants of
hotels, and I was assured-and I think a
study of the Bill will confirm it that the
serious restrictions which were formerly
imposed on people living in hotels have
been taken away by this Amending Bill.
Under the Act of 1923 people could not
obtain liquid refreshment in an hotel on
Sunday unless they had a meal, but under
this Bill such persons will be able to get
liquor in an hotel at any time of the day,
whether it is a week-day or Sunday.
That takes the sting out of the special
point that has been made with reference
to the restrictions on hotels. I was, myself, going to move an Amendment on
the Committee stage dealing with that
matter, but I am so satisfied that that
restriction has been removed under this
Bill that I do not now intend to do so.
I would like to point out what is almost
a comical thing in connection with liquid
refreshment that is served in an hotel.
I might ask Senator Porter Porter to
join me at dinner in an hotel, and
343 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Colonel Crawford]
although I might have a bottle of champagne on the table and be able to drink
it, I would not be allowed to give Senator
Porter Porter a drink. I think that is
an anomaly that should be removed. I
am told that one of the difficulties in the
way of allowing persons to entertain
guests in hotels, especially on a Sunday,
is that there are people who, knowing
the restriction, would send one of their
friends to take a room in an hotel on the
Saturday night, and if that restriction
was not in the Bill a man might entertain a party of forty or fifty people who
would all pay for the drink consumed,
although one man would be regarded as
the host. With the exception of such
small points as that, I think the Bill
meets with the approval of both sides the
Government were anxious to meet, and I
heartily support the Second Reading.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
There is one matter to which I would like
to draw the attention of the Government,
