THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
ance to demands which, if submitted
to, will be extended to the whole working class.
The mineowners and the Government
are now merely waiting until the sufferings of our people become unendurable and they are forced by sheer starvation to submit.
We are convinced that the organised
Labour movement will not allow us
to go under, and is fully prepared to
give us the necessary support.
Now Mr. Speaker, you will see the connection.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid I do not.
Mr. DORMAN: The statement proceeds:
Such support will naturally take two
forms, financial aid and sympathetic
action.
We are asking every trade union to
assist us, both centrally and through
its local organization and membership,
with financial aid.
This Bill is calculated to prevent sympathetic action being taken, because if
men in one trade come out in sympathy
with men in any other trade or industry
they are liable to be fined or imprisoned.
Hon. MEMBERS: No.
Mr. DORMAN: Very well. The appeal concludes:
We also ask all organizations concerned with transport to take definite
sympathetic action by authorising their
members to refuse to handle coal while
the lock-out continues.
We submit these requests for your
earnest consideration, and shall be glad
to have your decision at the earliest
possible moment.
Yours fraternally,
On behalf of the Miners' Federation
of Great Britain.
(Signed),
Herbert Smith, President.
Thomas Richards, Vice-president.
W. P. Richardson, Treasurer.
A. J. Cook, Secretary.
The result of that lock-out and that
appeal was that a large number of the
organized trades unionists of Great
Britain, in sympathy with their fellow
workers in the coal industry, withdrew
their labour from the market in which
they usually sold it, in order to manifest
their feeling against what they conceived to be the oppression imposed on
their fellow workers in another branch
of industry. There has always been a
state of fear and dread in the minds of
a certain class with regard to the actions
of the working classes when an industrial
conflict has taken place. I was struck
in reading in this connection from an
authority that I do not think any Member
of this House will object to have quoted.
Mr. Benjamin Disraeli wrote a book
called "Cybil, or The Two Nations,"
which I would commend to the Members
of this House. He was speaking of a
time when the country was passing
through a crisis something like the crisis
of last year when the general strike took
place. The governing class, and certainly
the upper classes whose opinions the
writer imaginatively tries to describe
were in a state of panic, and he directs
these words to them at that particular
time. We who are members of the great
Labour Party, the coming party in Great
Britain, and I hope in Northern Ireland,
were accused of creating class hatred and
ill-will, and of stirring up strife in the
community of which we are a part, but
under almost similar circumstances Benjamin Disraeli wrote the following words:
"Two nations between whom there is
no intercourse, and no sympathy, who
are as ignorant of each other's habits,
thoughts and feelings as if they were
dwellers in different zones, or inhabitants of different planets, who are
formed by a different breeding, are fed
by different food, are ordered by different manners, and are not governed
by the same laws, the Rich and the
Poor."
Then he goes on to say in another part
of his book and this is almost prophetic
of the conditions that prevail in Great
Britain, if not in Northern Ireland, today:-
"Predominant opinions are generally
the opinions of the generation that is
vanishing. Let an accident, which
speculation could not foresee, the
balanced state at this moment of Par-
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
1 DECEMBER 1927
liamentary parties, cease, and in a few
years, more or less, cease it must, and
you will witness a development of the
mind of England, which will make up
by its rapid progress for its retarded
action. I live among these men; I
know their inmost souls; I watch their
instincts and their impulses; I know
the principles which they have imbibed,
and I know, however hindered by circumstances for the moment, those
principles must bear their fruit.
will be a produce hostile to the oligarchical system. The future principles of
English politics will not be a levelling
principle; not a principle adverse to
privileges, but favourable to their extinction. It will seek to ensure equality,
not by levelling the few, but by elevating the many.
All that the people want is justice;
that labour should be as much respected
by law and society as property."
That is what I believe this Bill is calculated to retard. This Bill is distinctly
calculated to retard the combination of
Labour, not in the industrial field, but in
the political field. So long as the workers
were willing, as I have known they
were willing during the last quarter of
last century, through the Parliamentary
Committee of the Trade Union Congress,
to set a certain number of their members
apart and pay them for remaining in the
vicinity of the Parliament of Great
Britain and Ireland in London, for the
purpose of lobbying the Members of the
two great political parties in the State,
the Tories and the Liberals, no objection
was raised. There would be no objection
at all to their continuing to spend money
in that way, but there is objection to their
spending money for the purpose of getting
direct representation in Parliament, and
in my opinion one of the objects for which
this Bill is going to be placed upon the
Statute Book is to prevent the workers
obtaining that direct representation to
which they are entitled.
If we look back at the constitution of
Parliament in Great Britain and Ireland
as it existed 100 years ago, we find that
267 individuals had the right of nominating 487 Members of the Imperial Parliament out of a membership of 658.
Some people each had the right of nominating half a dozen Members. I hope I
will not be out of order when I state that
a nobleman of County Down had the right
of nominating three Members of the Irish
Parliament before it was abolished, and
he received considerable fees for relinquishing his privileged position of nominating those Members. I have gone into
the figures, and I have calculated that if
his ancestor had invested that money at
5 per cent., it would to-day amount to a
sum of £23,000,000. It was stated then
that 280 persons had the right of nominating 487 Members of Parliament, and
that there was an electorate of only
500,000 persons in Great Britain and
Ireland.
In other words, the Parliament of
100 years ago was elected by 100,000
odd less electors than we have on the Parliamentary franchise of this little State
of Northern Ireland to-day. The fran
chise has been widened, and widened on
foundations that constitute the aggregate
of sovereign power, and instead of one out
of twenty-eight having the right to exercise the Parliamentary franchise, one in
two has the right of exercising the Parliamentary franchise to-day. It is only
to be expected that when the vast majority
of people have the right of exercising the
Parliamentary franchise, they will seek
to obtain direct representation in Parliament.
Mr. SPEAKER: I find it very difficult
to connect what the hon. Member is saying with the Bill before the House. I
wish he would find it possible to stick to
the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: It is perhaps my
ignorance of Parliamentary procedure
that causes
me to get off the beaten
track when speaking on an important Bill
such as this. I believe this Bill is going
to create new crimes. It is going to
bring chaos into many branches of municipal undertakings in Great Britain and
Northern Ireland, which thirty-five or
forty years ago were in a state of chaos
through men leaving their work because
of the injustice of long hours and so on.
I believe that if the heads of the most
efficient departments of the municipalities of Great Britain and Northern Ireland were consulted to-day they would
place it on record that the introduction of
organizations containing 100 per cent. of
the workers of the various municipal
branches over which they have control
has brought peace, efficiency, and economy into those municipal undertakings.
I believe that if our gas manager in Bel-
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
fast, one of the most eminent gas managers to be found in any part of the world,
was consulted on this particular question
he would say it was his desire that 100
per cent. of the men under his control
should always be in one organization, because that would enable him to satisfactorily deal with the men through the
officials of that organization.
What will working men have to decide
under this Bill? The ramifications of
trades unions extend over a large number
of industries and trades and if I am requested by the Committee of my trade
union to go and interview a member of
a trade union, who has come out on strike
it may be that immediately I have interviewed that individual that strike may be
declared to be illegal. There are various
trades whose ramifications extend to all
industries. The engineering trade, for
instance, is probably represented in every
large undertaking in Belfast. If the
engineers in any of the shipyards strike
against what they conceive to be an injustice so far as their remuneration is
concerned, would the engineers, say, in
some of the big linen mills in Belfast,
who are members of the same trade union
as the men in the shipyard, be justified
in coming out on strike also, and if they
did come out on strike would they be
liable to the penalties imposed under this
Bill? How is an engineer to decide
whether or not he is participating in a
strike confined to a particular industry
under circumstances such as these?
is impossible for him to decide that
question.
It
He will have to ask himself what is the
object of the strike, and whether the
object is to intimidate the Government.
Eight years ago eight and a half million
workers in Great Britain and Northern
Ireland were members of trades unions.
Assuming that each member represented
a family of four, it meant that the part of
the community which constituted trades
unions was composed of thirty-two
million people out of a total of forty-five
million people. The man who is interviewing a person who is on strike has also
to consider whether the individual he is
interviewing has a reasonable apprehension of suffering an injury. It is wellknown to everybody that traitors are
cowards and that it is part and parcel
of their cowardly nature that they entertain unreasonable apprehensions. That
is the case whether the traitor is engaged
on the battlefield or in the navy, and I
know something about one branch of the
service, having served in it for many
years.
A coward is always pre-eminetly distinguished by entertaining unreasonable
anticipations, and his cowardice is
mainly composed of the entertainment of
these unreasonable anticipations. Why
do not the Government interfere with
other organizations that are inflicting
injury on the community? There is a
large trade union of newsagents and
book-sellers in Belfast. A friend of mine
recently invested a large portion of his
savings in starting a bookshop and he
made application for membership of this
organization. But he is debarred from
Membership because he has not established his business outside a quarter mile
radius from the business of any other
member of that organization. That is
tyrannical, and why should the Government not legislate to prevent such a thing
as that?
Mr.
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: And
that is a trade union.
Mr. DORMAN: You' are a member of
it.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
not.
This is
Mr. DORMAN: I am sorry.
the first reactionary principle introduced
in this country for 125 years, since the
first Apprentices Act was placed upon the
Statute Book. Hon. Members who will
take the trouble to see how the workers
were treated under the old Combination
laws will realize that they were not treated
as badly as they will be under this Bill
when its provisions come to be applied by
the courts when conflicts take place between capital and labour. Under the
old Combination laws fourteen men were
engaged in the winter in a certain business undertaking, and their employer
proceeded to deduct 10a. from their
wages for light during the dark hours of
those winter days. When the fourteen
men withdrew their labour as a protest
against the employer's action they were
brought before a court of summary jurisdiction and sent to prison for three
months. A celebrated economist who is
called the father of British political
economy, Adam Smith, points out in the
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
1 DECEMBER 1927
Wealth of Nations that under these
old Combination laws the employers
combined to reduce wages, prolonged
hours, and worsen conditions, and that
they frequently sat on the Bench and administered the law against their own
employees who had gone on strike.
This Bill has produced a lot of talk
about a general strike. There never has
been a general strike under what we call
free labour. There have been three great
strikes but no general strike under free
labour in any country. There was the
great strike of the Israelites in Egypt
when they went out of Egypt. Probably
had they remained behind they would
have found that the law was too powerful
for them, but they had to get out to get
free. There was the great strike of the
slaves in one of the states of Greece, and
there was also the great strike of slaves
in one of the West Indian Islands. There
have been other strikes in Western China
during recent years when all those engaged in that particular area ceased work.
What is a general strike? May I
presume to give a definition? I believe
a general strike may be said to have taken
place when those in any town, county,
province or country who have no means
of livelihood but the sale of their labour
power from day to day withdraw that
labour power
from the control of those
to whom they are accustomed to sell it
in order to obtain their living. As
Mr. Ramsay Macdonald has said, there
never has been a general strike under free
labour. He stated that it was the last
resort of a lunatic, and I think my colleague, Mr. William McMullen, M.P.,
improved upon that when he said it
would be the first resort of a lunatic and
the last resort of sensible men. A general
strike would mean the withdrawal of the
labour of the cook, the butler, the housemaid, the nurse, the keepers in our
asylums, the warders in our prisons, and
by all those who have no means of living
except by the sale of their labour.
A general strike of that kind would
bring us back to a state of barbarism and
savagery. Under a civilization such as
ours there cannot be a general strike.
People talk vaguely of a general strike
without taking time to visualise what it
would mean to a civilized community as
a whole. A general strike would not be a
revolution. It would be a revelation;
it would reveal to those who control industry and command the credit
of the country that, after all, we are all
inter-dependent, and that the many are
not under a greater obligation to them
than they are to the many by reason of
the service the many give to them from
day to day.
As I have quoted an eminent Tory
statesman may I be permitted to also
quote an eminent Liberal statesman.
The words are taken from an essay by the
eminent Duke of Argyle on the subject of
Law and Politics. He said:
"What combination does for the
protection of labour, it does better,
and with better consequences, than
positive institutions. Men are driven
to excessive labour, because if they don't
work excessively, others will. But it
is the effect of combination that others
won't.
Under positive institutions they are not allowed. Under
combination they are determined not."
.
.
He goes on to say:
"It tends to subordinate the present
to the future, and the temporary interests of self to the permanent welfare of the brotherhood of men. And
this it tends to do in classes otherwise
prone to follow only the impulses of
the moment.
I believe the Governments of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland are largely
following the impulses of the moment in
trying to place legislation such as this on
the Statute Books of both countries.
"and to consider only the apparent interests of the individual. These considerations should disabuse our minds
of the unjust and unreasonable prejudice against the principle of combination which still betrays itself so
strongly in the language of many
politicians. When the working classes
combine for the protection of their
labour against the effects of unrestricted
competition, they are simply taking
that course which is recommended alike
by reason and by experience. It is the
course which Parliament has indicated
as the right course both by what it has
itself done, and by what it has declined
to do."
Parliament had indicated the course
because other interests had combined to
obtain representation in Parliament to
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
protect themselves, and why should
Labour not do likewise? There is not one
working man out of probably 500,000 who
could aspire in his individual capacity to
be returned to Parliament. How many
working men are in a position to write a
cheque for the nomination fee of £150
to entitle them to become a candidate for
a seat either in the Imperial Parliament
or Northern Parliament? Even if they
had sufficient money to put down the
necessary nomination fee how many
members of the working class could face
the expense of £1,000 or £2,000 that
would be entailed in fighting a Parliamentary election They could not do so,
and therefore they have found it necessary to do what was stated by the Duke
of Argyle in his book-to combine
together for a specific purpose and under
more just and reasonable conditions.
I was reading in the newspapers yesterday about two of the great armament
firms, one of which has written down its
capital by eleven million pounds and the
other by twelve millions. That had been
done as a result of a majority vote of the
shareholders under the Limited Liability
Act. Have the minority of those shareholders the right to vote themselves out
of that scheme? No. I myself have a
few shares in a company the capital of
which has been reduced to one-eighth of
its original value.
JOSEPH
Mr.
Capitalism.
CUNNINGHAM:
Mr. DORMAN: Does the law give me
the right as a minority to vote myself out
of that Limited Liability Company and
secure my savings? It has meant a reduction of all the savings I have on earth
to one-eighth of their value a year ago.
In my opinion this Bill has been conceived in fear, it has been born in a panic,
and it is calculated in a very short time
to come to an untimely end. The only
hope for the working classes of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland is that the
promoters of this Bill, those who have
fathered this reactionary Measure, may
come down from the seats of the powerful
before that time arrives. We are not
asking anything unreasonable.
We are
asking that a fair opportunity should be
given to the workers to combine in the
industrial field and in the Parliamentary
field, just as the Limited Liability Companies and Legal Associations and other
such organizations have a right to decide
what they will do by means of a majority
vote of their members. We claim that
the Government should continue to allow
us to use that majority vote in the old
way, extending the right to everyone of
our members, if they wish to exercise it,
of contracting out of the obligation to pay
this Parliamentary levy.
a more
Where is the justification for this Bill?
Where are the petitions from the trade
unions? Where are the court records
that any particular member of a trade
union in Great Britain or Northern
Ireland has been persecuted or penalized
or has been assaulted or injured because
he has exercised his right to contract out
when the majority of the members of his
trade union have decided to contract in
and pay a reasonable contribution to the
upkeep of the party that is out to try to
establish more just, more reasonable, and
merciful relationship between
citizens themselves and between employers
and employees. I feel that in the further
stages of this Bill it would be utterly futile
to exercise my right to waste the time of
the Senate by moving amendments, but I
propose to exercise my right to divide the
House on every Clause of the Bill in order
to at least hand down to future generations a record of the fact that there was
one Member of the Senate who saw the
possible evil consequences of legislation
of this particular kind which, as I have
already said, was conceived in fear and
born in panic.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
am sure we are all somewhat puzzled to
know whether Senator Dorman was
speaking in favour of or against the
Second Reading of this Bill. Some of the
arguments he put were satisfactory from
my point of view, particularly his reference to the Newsagents' Association,
which was formed some time ago, a body
which is so tyrannical that its members
object to another newsagent starting a
business within a certain radius of the
businesses of the members of that association. It is another instance of the
tyranny that can be exercised by a body
of persons.
Senator Dorman almost led
the House to believe that this Bill was
the result of the general strike brought
about in a hurried manner owing to the
coal strike, but surely he forgets that the
trade unions and the Labour Socialist
party threatened the country for years
and years that they were going to have

a general strike. Therefore, it did not

And let me tell Senator Dorman more,
that they had actually set up a body and
had asked the trade unions throughout
the country to vote for or against giving

Therefore, the general strike was not an

our
I am very pleased to see this Bill introduced, and I am glad that
Government have followed the policy of
Great Britain step by step. Never was
there a greater necessity for a Bill of this
character than there is at the present
time. Trade unionists such as myself
and others in Northern Ireland
are very closely associated with trade
unions in England. Our headquarters
are nearly all situated in London and
some are in Manchester and Newcastle,
so that to have legislation controlling the
affairs of trade unions for England alone
without the Northern Paliament adopting the same legislation would be injurious to trade unionists as a whole.
It has been said that those who are likely
to support the Second Reading of this
Bill are inspired by motives of hostility
to trade unions. I do not think there is
any person who would dare to say that I
am prompted by a feeling of class or
partisan hostility to trade unions. No
man, I do not care who he is, has a greater
or higher respect for trade unions than
I have.
There was no necessity for Senator
Dorman to put forward arguments in
favour of trade unions, because I am
satisfied that they are a great benefit to
the country. They prevent a great deal
of friction and trouble between employers
and workmen, and I am satisfied that in
Belfast and the North of Ireland we have
very sensible and moderate-minded trade
union officials; who, I believe, help considerably to settle differences that may
crop up between employers and their
workers. For the past twenty years I
have given the whole of my spare time
to the trade union of which I am a member, that is the Engineers' Society, and I
feel very proud when I think how the
members of that trade union appreciate
my
efforts as shown by the fact that they
have elected me to some of the highest
positions they could place me in in that
society. It is something for one to look
back upon with pride, and I can honestly
and openly say that I have given of my
best to the interests of the members of
that society, and I believe with very good
results.
During the time I occupied those
positions improved conditions were obtained for that trade without any strike
or any trouble. At the time I am speaking of the trade unions were conducted in
a fair and proper manner. We had men
of all shades of political and religious
views associated in one trade union for industrial purposes, and the members were
most particular that no offence was given
to those who were in the minority. Had
a man suggested anything of a political
or religious nature he was severely
penalized. Those were the days when
trade unions had no politics. They knew
no politics, and they had to pay no
political levy. I welcome this Bill because I believe it will improve the trade
unions and will restore to them the
freedom and liberty that has been
filched from them. During the past few
years a small body of extremists has
gradually obtained control of the
machinery of trade unions. The great
majority of trade unions have become
disgusted with these extremists, and they
have left the administration of those
trade unions to this small minority to
carry on as they wish.
Mr. DORMAN: Shame.
It
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
is a shame. The ordinary member is
absent from the meetings of his trade
union while these extremists monopolize
the official positions and carry on the
affairs of the union as they think fit. One
thing every person must admit, and that
is that this Bill has been one of the
biggest surprises that any Opposition.
particularly the Labour-Socialist party,
could have had. They hoped to engineer
great opposition to this Measure, and they
expected the workers to rise in arms and
create a revolution because the British
Government in the first instance and the
Northern Government afterwards introduced this Measure. But the fact of the
matter is that the workers throughout
the country were glad that this Bill was
introduced. There is no doubt about
that. It has saved them from the
N
388:
Trade Disputes Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
tyranny of those people who are trying
to dominate the positions and use the
honest trade union members for political
reasons. I believe that all workers
throughout the country with the exception of a small number of extremists are
very pleased to find this Bill introduced
for their safety and welfare.
It must be admitted that trade unions
were formed and set up by decent, honest
workmen, who bound themselves together
for industrial purposes, to improve their
conditions and to protect themselves from
unscrupulous employers. While that
policy was carried on it was very satisfactory. Then we find that a number
of years ago the Labour-Socialist party
introduced the political levy and persuaded the workers to submit to this levy.
They said, "You cannot be represented
in Parliament by bloated capitalists or
employers. It is necessary for workmen to
go to Parliament themselves; it is necessary for one who knows the exact conditions under which the workers live and
work to be able to go to Parliament to
properly express the feelings of the
workers." They persuaded the workers
to submit to this levy, but what do we
find to-day? We do not find the Labour-
Socialist party saying that it is only the
worker who is entitled to represent the
workers. Only recently we find Mr.
Mosley, a nobleman who, I believe, had
Royalty attending his marriage, standing
as a Labour candidate, and when he was
seeking election, instead of referring to
his wife in the manner in which she was
entitled to be referred to, he called her
"Mrs." to make her more popular
amongst the mill workers and miners.
Such piffle and bluff! One is surprised
that men could be gulled by such a proceeding. It is not only the young men
who are gulled, because you even see greyhaired men like myself taken in by such
piffle and nonsense. Not only have they
wealthy men in the Labour party, but
they have men of title, and what in the
name of heavens they know about the
conditions of the workers, or how they
live or work, is more than I can understand. As for lawyers, at one time the
Labour-Socialist party
looked upon
lawyers as the greatest enemies they
could have, and now there are any number
of K.C.'s in the party looking for posts.
It is ridiculous to think of the way the
poor workers have been gulled by these
extremists. They have been successful in
exploiting the workers and obtaining this
political levy from them, and they have
got such an amount of money that the
late Mr. Keir Hardie was able to have a
trip to Egypt and India, and Mr. Ramsay
MacDonald has been able to have a similar
trip at the expense of workers like myself.
They have been successful in getting
that money, and they have attempted to
control the trade union movement, but I
am afraid they have made a terrible
blunder, and there is no doubt they have
acted in a brutal way so far as the controlling of the trade union movement is
concerned. In the first place, they engineered the general strike undoubtedly
for political purposes. They thought
they would be able to drive the British
Government out of office, and step into
office themselves. The Government were
well aware that the workers wanted some
change in the law relating to trade
unions. They were continually appealing
for such a change because trade
unions were being carried on for purposes
for which they were never intended. They
came along with the general strike which
they forced on the people, but, unfortunately for them, they had not the workers
behind them.
Mr. DORMAN: The Labour party
had nothing to do with that. It was the
Trade Union Council.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
Trade Union Council may have been
directly responsible, but the Labour party
has never repudiated the conduct of the
Trade Union Council. They have never
had the courage to say their action was
wrong. Mr. Clynes and Mr. Thomas
what
have pointed out
a mistake
their action was, but the Labour party
have not had the courage to condemn the
conduct of the Trade Union Council.
The Government were forced to take
action which I think was necessary,
if the general strike did nothing else
except to force the British Government
to protect the trade unionists of the
country from these tyrannical individuals
who had obtained control of the unions it
has done
great deal. It has
very many humiliating positions. The question is
asked repeatedly why this Bill has been
introduced here. I
as
realise,

necessary it was that legislation should
be carried in Northern Ireland similar.to
that carried in Great Britain.
There is
one thing sure, and that is this. I believe
there is no place throughout the United
Kingdom where you will find workmen
more inclined to be free and independent
than in the North of Ireland. I know
they were much disgusted and dissatisfied
with the conditions as they were with regard to trade unions, and there was considerable agitation and friction to have
some change made. There was no one did
more than myself to draw the attention
of the Government to the injury which
the decent trade unionist was suffering.

general strike the people who are going
to suffer most are really the workers who
are striking. The food supply is cut off.
Trains cease running, and there is no
means of getting proper food to the
proper localities. The result is that the
greatest sufferers are the wives and children of the strikers. It is a case of the
Government protecting the workers from
themselves.
I remember speaking to someone who
had been very busy during the general
strike on the North-East Coast. They had
been sending fish and flesh from a big
centre to the smaller districts.
It seems
that the people, the miners particularly,
in these smaller districts are the most
difficult to please. They insisted on
having fresh fish when it was impossible
to get it, and fresh meat when it was
difficult to send it. That is the difficulty
they found themselves in. Actually, the
strike that did take place was simply an
endeavour to coerce the Government, but
at the same time it was causing tremendous grievance and hardship to the
workers themselves and their families
during the coal strike.
an
I do not think
Since the introduction of this Bill they
have howled at the street corners and at
other meetings about the injustice inflicted on trade unionists, but the most
remarkable thing is that these people,
who are howling most about trade
unionism, are men with no record of
trade union work, and who simply know
nothing about trade unions. For instance, we find insurance agents championing the cause of trade unionsinsurance agents who have been
infliction on trade unions, as I know.
They are the men who lapse policies on
their poor unfortunate victims, and when
death comes the poor people get no
insurance benefit. Some of these are the
people who are championing the cause of
decent trade unionists in the country,
telling the Government it is an injustice
they are inflicting on trade unionism by
passing this Bill. It can be said at the
same time that there is no injustice inflicted on trade unionists. Trade unions
have still the right to strike, just the
same as they ever had. It is altogether
ridiculous to say that the trade
unionist is hampered in any of his legiti
mate rights. If any disagreement or
trouble occurs between employer and employee and the society representing the
employees, it is quite within their rights.
to withdraw their labour at any time.
The only restriction placed by this Bill
on the workers is that they shall not strike
against the Government or against the
community. And, good gracious, anyone
will admit that you are only protecting
the worker against himself
Col. SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Hear,
hear.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:———by
insisting on such restrictions. In
a
there is anyone in a decently conducted
trade union movement who would support a general strike. Then we have
picketing. Certainly some restrictions
have been introduced with reference to
picketing. I did not hear even Senator
Dorman say one word against the Bill as
far as the restriction on picketing is concerned. I have always been very willing
to urge my fellow workmen to become
trade unionists, and to stand shoulder to
shoulder with one another, but I never
did see that it was proper or humane to
inflict injury or to force those who were
not so willing as others at the time to
strike. The cases we read of during the time
of the miners' strike were deplorable.
There is not a decent trade unionist would
defend such conduct. I say it is well the
Government have introduced legislation to
prevent such coercion during these periods
As far as the levy is concerned, which
Senator Dorman made reference to, I
think this is the Clause which gives the
greatest satisfaction to the greatest, num-...
ber of trade unionists throughout Northern
Ireland to-day. In the North of Ireland,
unfortunately, we had to suffer a double
dose of this political levy. Not only were
the workers required to pay a levy for the
Imperial elections, but they were also
called upon to pay a levy for the Northern
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
If any
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
penalty imposed on the workers.
one would ask the reason why this Bill
should be applied to Northern Ireland I
think that is complete justification more
than anything else. There is more reason
why Northern Ireland should have this
Bill than why the British people should
have it, because they are only penalized
to the extent of one shilling a quarter
against Northern Ireland having to pay
a double levy.
Another thing must be admitted to be
very unfair, and I am sure any decent
honourable trade unionist will admit it.
It was very unfair to force a young man
to join a trade union for industrial purposes-it was looked upon as something
necessary for a boy finishing his apprenticeship to become immediately a member
of a trade union which was kept in existence for the protection of the interests
of the trade, and then for him to find, although he may have been a Nationalist,
a Liberal, or a Conservative, he was forced
to pay subscriptions towards a party which
he probably detested and looked upon
with disgust. That he should be asked
to pay a levy towards the upkeep of these
people was more than anyone could accept
or tolerate, especially in a place like Northern Ireland where the people had been
so willing for years and years even to fight
to maintain that civil and religious liberty
with which they had been so much impressed. I think it was almost impossible
to expect that they would allow the levy
to go on for any length of time without
trying to force the Government to take
some proceedings.
As far as the Civil Servant is concerned
I must admit that I think it is only proper
and right that a Civil Servant should be
responsible only to his employer and to
the Government. I think it is very unfair that they could belong to a trade
union, or be associated with those revolutionary people who may be inclined to
bring them out on strike and create
trouble and disturbance in Government
circles. As far as the Civil Servants are
concerned I very much approve of the
Bill. If it was not for the Labour Socialist Party's deceptive methods
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:-by
which they have taken control of the trade
unions one might not have had reason to
complain of trade union law at the present
time. When the trade unions were being
conducted by the old trade unionists
they gave their services liberally and
freely. It was at that time a labour of
love. You very often heard of a trade
unionist who gave his services for two or
four hours in the evening without any
return or any pay. That is all changed.
The administration of the different
societies now since the Socialist party got
control has increased tremendously. Instead of a labour of love these Socialists
in office insist on getting their full pound
of flesh. They insist on being paid for
everything they do. I think they have
adopted the Yorkshireman's advice, "If
thou dost ought for nought do it for
thysel." They do nothing for any other
person. If the Socialists had acted towards the trade unions as the old trade
unionists acted there would have been no
need for this restrictive legislation. I
am very pleased indeed to give my support to
the Second Reading of the
Measure.
Mr. LESLIE: A Measure corresponding
in its terms to the Bill which is before us
this afternoon has, during the present
year, been discussed ad infinitum
across the Channel, and the actual
Bill which We have here to-day has
been discussed in another place perhaps
one might say ad nauseam. I think that
what emerges from all the discussions is
a recognition on the part of all people
who are reasonably sane that the Bill contains nothing but certain prophylactic
provisions which are necessary as a safeguard to the public as a whole. When I
say the public as a whole I mean deliberately to include the employee just as
much as the employer. The Bill, in fact,
is really nothing but the common law as
defined in a recent judgment commonly
known as the Astbury judgment-nothing
but the common law supported by such
additional provisions as modern developments have shown to be necessary.
protection given in the Bill is really under
four heads. The public as a whole is to
be protected from coercion. The wouldbe worker is to be protected from intimidation, and the extension given to the
definition of intimidation is really nothing
more than we have been accustomed to
for some years past when it has been necessary to deal with that matter.
worker is also to be protected from having
his money spent against his will on subjects of which he does not approve, and
Ireland elections. It was rather a double
The
The
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
1 DECEMBER 1927
finally, the purity of the Civil Service is
to be maintained 'That in brief is the
object of the Bill.
And now after the able exposition of
the aims of true labour and real labour
which have been given, not for the first
time, by the hon. Senator on my left, I
do not think there is any necessity to
detain the House longer on the subject,
to the general principles of which I have
already alluded, but I want to say a few
words now as regards the methods of
administration which may perhaps unfortunately become necessary. We
all hope that this Bill when it becomes
an Act will remain quiescent. We should
like it to be a strictly dormant statute,
but it may be necessary to enforce the
provisions of this Bill, and, if so, the
enforcement will have to be the work of
the courts. One of the main principles
after all of the Bill is, that no association
whatever is above the law, and the law
is above any association. If we have to
go to the courts to enforce this Measure
it is possible that one of three courts may
be resorted to. First of all, the court of
summary jurisdiction, and as to that court
there is apparently a somewhat complicated tangle which I am afraid it will
be necessary to ask the House in Committee to unravel.
was
The Bill before us, as hon. Senators
will see, is to be construed and cited as
one with the Trade Union Acts, 1871 onwards. Now, the Trade Union Act of
1871, the first of the series, most clearly
provides for the hearing of cases by a
court of summary jurisdiction, which,
in Ireland, outside Dublin, was to consist of one resident magistrate. The exthe rather loose
pression used
expression "resident magistrate" and of
It further prothat I am rather glad.
vided for appeal to the ordinary court of
quarter sessions. It, therefore, would
seem to follow that any summary jurisdiction proceedings under this Bill, when
it becomes an Act, will be taken before
a court consisting of a resident magistrate.
There is another Act referred to many
times in this Bill and closely connected
with it, and when this Bill is passed the
two Acts will be inextricably interwoven.
I am referring to the Conspiracy and Protection of Property Act, 1875, to one of
the sections of which we are adding a
provision. Now, under the Conspiracy and
Protection of Property Act, provision is
made for the constitution of a court of
summary jurisdiction in Ireland, and it
is provided that that court of summary
jurisdiction shall consist of two magistrates sitting in petty sessions, and again
appeal is given to the ordinary court of
quarter sessions. It seems, therefore, that
if we pass this Bill as it is under one set
of contigencies which might conceivably
arise-I think it is in Clause 2-that
proceedings would have to be taken before one court and in another set of contingencies they would have to be taken.
before another court.
What would happen as regards section 5
of the Conspiracy Act I really do not
know. It would appear that the one
part would be tried by the ordinary
magistrates and the other part by
another court. Of course, it is I think
obvious, unless there is some explanation
that that state of affairs cannot be
allowed. Therefore, I am proposing
to ask the House when in Committee to
accept an amendment definitely confining
the jurisdiction under the Trades Disputes
Act, 1871, the Conspiracy and Protection
of Property Act, 1875, and this Act, to
the resident magistrates, with appeal to
the county court judge sitting alone. I am
not proposing to include the new Emergency Powers Act which we passed last
vear and which is also referred to in this
Bill, because, if I understand the matter
correctly, the powers conferred by that
Act on His Grace to make regulations
are sufficently wide to enable His Grace
to provide for that court.
The
a
Leaving the court of summary jurisdiction we come to the county court.
county court may, perhaps, be called into
operation under an early Clause of the
Bill, and, as a matter of fact, I think I
am correct in saying the county court in
England has already been put in operation in Liverpool in connection with
case arising there. There is nothing to
be said as to the county court for the
county court is under our own control.
There is also the possibility of the intervention of the high court being sought,
and the possibility of the high court coming into the matter appears, for the first
time, in the same Clause as the county
court. What we are doing there seems
to me to be nothing but providing what
I should describe as an alternative atonement to be made by the wrong-doer, but
the position seems to me to become very
different when we get to Clause 7. That
is one of the most essential Clauses of
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
•
[Mr. Leslie]
the Bill-I think the noble Viscount drew
special attention to it-the Clause which
enables the Attorney-General to take certain action. In that Clause we have
followed, word for word, the English Act,
and, so far as England is concerned, I see
no objection to the wording of that
Clause. But there is a point, I think,
we must consider here, and although I
shall put an Amendment on the Paper
it is quite possible that discussion may
show there is no reason for alteration. The
difficulty is, we say an injunction may be
granted. The power of granting an injunction is inherent in the high court. It was
specially preserved for our own high court.
in 1920. It is one of the foundations of
the equitable jurisdiction. The power to
grant an injunction is one which this Parliament did not give, and I do not think
this Parliament can give. It certainly
cannot take it away, and I think we ought
not to attempt to define it. It seems to me
that it will be necessary to make some
slight alteration, so to speak shedding the
shadow and supporting and securing the
substance. May be by some words we should
definitely give to our Attorney-General the
power which at present we imply, but we
should refrain from telling the high court
by what means they are to do their work.
That, as I say, is a matter that really calls
for most careful consideration on the part
of those competent to express an opinion.
I am sure it would be most repugnant to
this House to do anything in any way
trenching on a reserved subject, such as
the prerogative of the high court.
It
There is another officer to whom I want
to refer, that is, the officer appointed to
exercise in Northern Ireland the functions
of the registrar of friendly societies.
will be seen that that officer has very
important functions.
I am,
therefore,
going to ask the noble Viscount to be so
kind as to tell us at a later stage whether
he is quite certain, to put it bluntly, that
the provisions we have made respecting
that officer are watertight. The difficulty,
if there is one, seems to arise in this way.
The work of the friendly societies was
committed to the Government of Northern Ireland in 1920. This Parliament, if
I remember correctly, put the matter
under the supervision of the Ministry of
Commerce. The Minister of Commerce
appointed an officer to discharge those
duties and that officer, as I think we all
know, is the permanent secretary of the
Ministry of Commerce. But the question
that does occasion some little anxietythough I am quite prepared to believe it
is all right-is whether by a chain of
adaptation orders or otherwise, we have
sufficiently thrown into the hands of our
registrar all the work of societies registered under the Trade Union Act of 1913.
Hitherto, all the work in Northern Ireland under the Trade Union Acts, which
required the registrar, was done by the
assistant registrar. I am, therefore,
asking the noble Viscount to satisfy us
at a later stage that everything in that
way is as it should be.
now.
I have two questions to ask the noble
Viscount which he can reply to at a later
stage. I do not wish to press for a reply
I want to draw attention for a
moment to Sub-clause 3 of Clause 2. I
want to ask whether I am correct in
assuming that that Sub-clause is introduced only for the sake of conformity
with the English Measure, and, so to
speak, for the prevention of accidents?
Am I correct in assuming it has no special
meaning so far as the North of Ireland
is concerned? The second question is in
relation to the minimum fine which the
noble Viscount spoke of, and which is
carefully provided for in Sub-clause 4 of
Clause 1. I think I am correct in saying
that that minimum fine, which is the
small one of £1, is reducible to 5s under
a provision of the Conspiracy of Protec
tion of Property Act. I would like the
noble Viscount to confirm that, lest it
be thought to be a big minimum fine. I
have really nothing more to weary the
House with except perhaps that I should
say I support the principles of the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am only intervening for a few minutes in
the debate. I am sorry that the hon.
Senator (Mr. Dorman) has left, as the few
remarks I wish to make were in reply to
what he put forward. Time has been
pretty well taken up by previous speakers
on matters which could largely have been
dealt with on the Committee stage.
Senator Dorman referred to the injury
sustained by the coal industry through
the loss of foreign markets. I am afraid
that it was the fault of the leaders of
the trade union movement who recommended the general strike that the foreign
coal markets were lost. Owing to various
reasons it is now very difficult to get them
back. We have heard different opinions
expressed here to-day by Labour Members
of the House as to the necessity for the
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
1 DECEMBER 1927
introduction of this Bill. Having heard
those different expressions of opinion how
is the ordinary man with no knowledge of
trade unions to judge of the matter? I
am glad that the hon. Senator (Mr.
Dorman) has come back again. I wish to
impress on him that everyone in this
House knows how much good trade unions
have done. What are the reasons for the
introduction of this Bill? It is not the
fault of the trade unions. It is the fault
of the men whom they appointed as their
leaders, or we might call them agitators,
who make a livelihood out of the business.
Trade unionists as a whole are in favour
of this Bill. It is really the trade union
leaders who are to blame for the
and
necessity
of this
introduction
Bill. I would, in conclusion, make
a personal appeal to Senator Dorman.
There is
Member whose
no
views
and ideas though we do not agree
with his views politically-are more
respected in this House. I was astonished
when at the end of his speech he said that
simply for the purpose of obstruction, not
because the Bill was right or wrong, he
was going to oppose all the Clauses of the
Measure. Now that he has made his
strong protest I hope that when he thinks
more seriously over the matter he will
see his way not to take up time in obstructing the passage of this useful Bill
at a later stage.
Mr. McDOWELL: As a trade unionist of 35 years' standing and as one who
has occupied all the important positions
in connection with the local trade union
movement I think I have a right to speak
with some knowledge on the Bill before
the House. I am strongly inclined to
think that the old principle of trade
unionism as we used to understand it has
ceased to function. We are in favour of
the Bill before the House, but the
Socialist element are trying to capture
and govern our trade unions. As I have
said, I have occupied the principal offices
in my trade union for practically thirtyfive years, and I never had a dispute
except one. Yet I was able to bring that
trade union into a position second to
none, both as regards wages and conditions of labour, in the North of Ireland,
nay, perhaps in Great Britain. The trade.
union rules at that particular time provided that neither religion nor politics
circumshould be allowed under
any
stances. Leaders of the trade unions of
to-day, because I hold a certain political
view, say that I am opposed to the
interests of the working men. They cannot dispute the fact, however, that it was
men like myself who brought trade unionism to the perfection it was in a few years
ago.
The men then combined only to
improve conditions so far as wages and
labour are concerned.
What do we find? We find that the
trade unions are really becoming the
great political organizations, and that is
why they have ceased to function as
trade unions. There is scarcely a trade
union which fulfils the promises of years
gone by, but we have tried to work in
conformity with the rules of our trade
unions. No matter what a man's political
views might be, and no matter what his
creed might be he had the same right to
occupy the highest position as any other
man had. Now these men say because I
believe in Conservative or Unionist
principles I am an enemy of the working
man. Let me point out that 75 per
cent. of the social reforms brought about
for the welfare of the working man and
working woman have been passed by
Unionist or Conservative Governments,
and yet the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman)
would try to make us believe, as he always
does, on every conceivable occasion, that
as a class we are opposed to one another.
I do not think he is right. In connection
with the general strike, Senator Dorman
says that the majority has the right to
rule.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. McDOWELL: We dispute this.
We say we are in a majority when a
general strike takes place. Take one
shop. The men leave off work at halfpast five o'clock in the evening and go
out. The next morning there is a picket
standing at the gate and they prohibit
the men from going into work. They
had no knowledge the previous night that
anything was wrong. They are told the
next morning that they cannot start work.
I wonder where the majority comes in
there. In the old days there had to be
a two-thirds majority before a strike,
let alone a general strike, was called.
The Labour leaders have tried to
manipulate the position with a conference of five or so and they are able to
declare a general strike, and we, the poor
humble tools, must fall in with their
wishes whether we desire it or not. We
are in favour of this Bill because it is
against the principle of a general strike.
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr.
McDowell].
Let me now touch on the sympathetic
strike. I remember at one time there was
a trades council here. I was a member of
that council, and we carried on business
successfully as far as the interests of the
working men were concerned. Then it
commenced to take a political turn, and
a great many of the unions fell out of it.
After that a federation was started; it
was called the Shipbuilding and Federated
Trades Council. What did that stand
for? At that particular time one trade
was infringing on the work of the
other. Shipwrights were against the
joiners and joiners were against the
shipwrights; plumbers were against
engineers and engineers were against
plumbers, and this association was formed
for the purpose of settling the differences
between the different trades.
What is the upshot of it now? This
body has become a political organization.
It is composed of quite a number of
trades, the members of which are skilled,
semi-skilled and otherwise. If a man
does not belong to the same trade as I do
and he has no conception of what work I
have to perform, how can he be in
sympathy with me when he is receiving
10/- a week less than I am receiving?
He cannot be. It is unnatural to expect
him to do so when the question of a
sympathetic strike comes in. So far as
this Bill is concerned it is essential that
it should be passed without Amendment.
In connection with the political levy, I
think it is ridiculous that a young man
joining a trade union should be practically
compelled to pay to this political levy.
Mr. DORMAN . No, no.
Mr. McDOWELL: The hon. Senator
says "No, no." I quite admit so far as
the law is concerned that is right, but so
many obstacles are placed in the that
way
trade unionists cannot possibly comply
with the law. Consequently they have to
pay or fall into arrear, and if they do not
pay they are looked on as something
beneath the others. We do not wish to
do them an injury. Under the Bill if
they want to pay to the political levy they
are quite at liberty to do so, but we who
object will not pay any more.
This Bill
gives us the right not to be intimidated
as far as this political levy is concerned.
I welcome the Bill, and I think it ought
to be accepted and passed without Amendment.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support
the Second Reading of this Bill. I do not
intend to say very much, as a good deal
has been said this afternoon in this
chamber, but I would like to say I believe
it is necessary that a Bill such as this
should be brought in. I am sorry that
the Governments of to-day have found it
necessary to bring in such a Measure.
One would naturally think, seeing we are
living in an age of education, enlightenment and advancement, that we would
have left behind us those barbarous tactics
which Senator Dorman made reference to
a short time ago. One would think that
we would have left them behind, and that
we would have had a more sane way of
settling these disputes. Strikes and lockouts to my mind are inhuman. They
interfere with the livelihood and the
comfort and the happiness of the people,
especially those who are weak. What I
mean by the weak are the aged and the
children of the men and women who are
brought out on strike or who, perhaps,
find the door of the workshop or factory
in which they earn their living closed
against them. To my mind strikes and
lock-outs are inhuman and ought to be
done away with altogether.
We have been told on several occasions
there was not the slightest necessity for
either the Imperial Government or the
Government of Northern Ireland bringing
in such a Measure. If we let our minds
go back over the past few years since the
war-since 1918--what do we find? We
find up to the present year nothing but
industrial wars and rumours of industrial
wars. I was going to say, vapour, and
fire and smoke, but I am very glad to be
in a position to say it is not necessary to
say that. We find wars and rumours of
wars, and we also find threats of revolutions of different kinds, and we find an
upset of our social conditions in these
islands. I would like to draw the attention of the House to some of the strikes
which took place during that period. I
would mention first the general strike,
which has been emphasized here so
much this afternoon. This general
strike cost the country something like
£30,000,000-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: More.
Mr. BARCLAY--and, remember, it
was of short duration. It also cost the
ratepayers over £3,500,000 in extra
.
Second Reading.
Trade Disputes Bill--

1 DECEMBER 1927
relief. It added over 500,000 to the
number of unemployed, and it cost the
workers of the country £20,000,000 in
wages lost.
That was the general strike.
I would also refer to the coal strike of
1926. It cost the country between
£250,000,000 and £300,000,000, and
every day the miners stopped out of the
mines added £2,000,000. It cost the
workers, apart from the miners, at that
time something like £40,000,000 in wages
lost. It cost the trade unions £5,000,000
of the savings they had set aside for superannuation and other purposes. It added
600,000 to the unemployment list, not
counting the miners. I would also draw
the attention of the House to the railway
strike of September, 1919. This strike
cost the country directly something like
£10,000,000, and it put out of work
about 375,000 men. Then the coal strike
of October and November, 1920, cost
the country directly something like
£26,500,000. The miners lost in wages
£15,000,000, and the other workers who
were thrown out of work numbered about
350,000. There was also the coal strike
of 1921.
Mr. DORMAN: The lock-out.
The
Mr. BARCLAY: Was it a lock-out?
This cost the country something like
£33,000,000. It increased the unemployment payments to £13,000,000 and
it lost markets abroad which some hon.
Member made reference to this afternoon. From April to July the country
lost something like £50,000,000.
House will see from those figures that it
was necessary for the Government to take
some drastic steps to save the industrial
and social welfare of the country. That
is one of the reasons why they have
brought in this Bill. Then, again, we
have been told that there was no demand
for this Bill, that the country had given
no mandate to the Government to bring
it in. I do not think it is necessary for
the Government to go to the country for a
mandate to bring in such a Bill as this.
A democratic Government is elected by
the votes of the people for the purpose of
catering for and looking after the welfare
of the people. When a small section of
the community rises up in a tyrannical
way against the great majority it is the
duty and the business of any democratic
Government to save the majority from
the power of the minority. Therefore,
we find the Government has brought in
this Bill for that particular purpose.
When the Imperial Government introduced this Bill into the Imperial House
of Commons there was a terrible lot of
talk and there was a terrible lot of threats.
Some hon. Senator has mentioned here
to-day some of the threats. There were
going to be great demonstrations in the
city of London. There were going to be
great processions in the streets, so great
that the traffic would be held up, and the
parks and other places in and about
London were to be crowded with trade
unionists protesting against this Bill.
What happened? I never heard tell
of a procession. I never heard tell of any
of the big demonstrations in the parks.
The only protest that was made was made
at the Trade Union Congress, and as
Senator McDowell has said, the men there
were elected by a certain section of the
community who were favourable towards
them, and they only spoke for one
section of trade unionists. I would like
to draw the attention of hon. Members to
the Trades Congress in Edinburgh, and if
this is not a mandate from the electorate
I do not know what can be a mandate.
At the Trades Congress in Edinburgh
Mr. J. H. Thomas, who was Colonial
Secretary during the Labour party's term
of office, speaking on Tuesday, September
6, 1927, said
there were three by-elections at which
the Trade Disputes Bill was made the
Labour issue. Their own people constituted 90 per cent. of the electorate,
but their candidates were turned down
by the votes of their own people.
If that is not a mandate I do not know
what you would call one. Here are three
by-elections fought during the time this
Bill was before the Imperial House of
Commons. The Labour party at those
three elections made the Trade Disputes
Bill their main issue, or, as we would say
in Belfast, the principal plank in their
platform. We must not forget that Mr.
Thomas said that 90 per cent. of the
electors of those three constituencies were
working class people, trade unionists.
He says "their own people," and, therefore,
we find that 90 per cent of "their own
people" turned down three candidates
and returned to the Imperial House of
Commons three men in favour of this
Bill.
I said I was not going to say very much,
but I am afraid I am going further than
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
I intended. (Hon. Members: No, no.)
Looking at the Bill I naturally come to
the conclusion that it is a very harmless
document. I do not see anything that
any working man may be afraid of.
In
the first place, it does not interfere in
any way with the rights of trade unionists
to organize themselves in trade unions
for the purpose of collective bargaining
or for the purpose of improving their
working conditions. All the Bill does is
simply to safeguard trade unionists from.
any political party. It sets a man practically free. I would not like the Unionist
party to have any kind of a grip upon me.
I have an independent spirit. Although
I might not be independent financially,
still I have something within me that is
independent. I would not like any
Unionist, even you, Mr. Speaker, to
come to me and tell me to do a certain
thing or try to coerce me to do a certain
thing. I would say, "No, I will not do
it." I take every working man to be of
the same temperament and of the same
spirit. Why should any working man be
compelled by any political party to act,
or vote or pay as that party thinks fit?
Therefore, this is a Bill that safeguards
the trade unionist and practically sets
him free.
Again we have been told that this Bill
interferes with and takes away from the
working man his strongest weapon, namely,
the strike weapon which has been referred to by Senator Cunningham. I
cannot see for the life of me how any man
can say that this Bill takes away that
particular weapon. It does not interfere
in any way
with legitimate strikes.
Railwaymen, miners, bricklayers, carpenters, engineers, and transport workers
who have a disagreement with their employers are quite at liberty to test the
matter by a strike if saner methods cannot be used. I hope saner methods will
always be used, but they have a right
according to this Bill to test the matter
by a strike. But the Bill does prohibit
a general strike. Senator Dorman has
been trying to explain to us this afternoon what a general strike is, and I must
say that I am just as wise now as when
he began. He made reference to the
Israelites down in the land of Goshen. I
suppose there was a general strike on the
day they left there to go on their journey
to the Promised Land. It may have
been, but at any rate I do not understand
the definition he has given. I under-
1 stand that the Bill prohibits a general
strike, and it is very emphatic upon this
point, because it is so worded as not to
permit in any way a conspiracy against
the Government of the country. If we
look back upon past strikes that have
taken place, even apart from the general
strike, we find that sympathetic strikes in
many cases have done a great amount of
harm to the country as a whole. The
general strike in 1926 deprived the people
of their food in a great many cases.
Ships were held up; perishable food was
lost and destroyed. It deprived travellers
of transport. Instead of having buses,
trams, or trains by which to travel they
had to go on foot. It deprived the
people of light and heat, and threw
thousands of men and women who were
very much in need of money out of employment.
Then there is the political levy. There
has been a great deal said about the
political levy, and I do not suppose that
I could improve on anything that has
been said. But this I will say, that I
look upon the political levy as a sporting
Clause. The trade unionist is left a free
man, and if he has a shilling, or two
shillings, or a pound to spend he is quite
at liberty to put it on whatever political .
party he fancies. If he fancies the
Socialist or the Labour party he is quite
at liberty to put his pound on the Labour
party. If he fancies the Liberal party
he is quite at liberty to put his pound
upon the Radical, and if he fancies the
Conservative party he is quite at liberty
to put his pound upon the Tory. He is
quite at liberty to do that. There is no
person to hinder him, and so I think this
Clause about which we have heard so
much, and over which there has been such
weeping, wailing and gnashing of teeth,
is a pretty fair Clause.
A lot has been said about the civil
servants. I am not going to say any
thing about the civil servants, only that
seeing they are the servants of the State
they have no right to combine with any
political party. There is one question
that arises in my mind, and that is that
if it is right for a trade union to use
pressure upon a group of men in the
interests of the union, is it wrong for the
Government to apply pressure to prevent
an injury being done to the whole country
by a group of trades unionists? This
question has presented itself to me. If
it is right for the trades unions to bring
405 Trade Disputes Bill-

1 DECEMBER 1927
Adjournment
a
pressure upon a group of men in its own
interests it should also be right for the
Government to bring pressure upon
group of trades unionists for the benefit
and comfort of the people, and if it is
wrong for the Government it is quite
wrong for the trades unions.
Looking over the Bill and taking the
Measure as a whole I naturally come to
the conclusion that it is a fair Measure.
There is nothing in the Bill to interfere
with the independence of the trades
unions. There is nothing to prevent a
trade unionist from subscribing to any
political propaganda he wishes, and there
is nothing to debar him from voting as he
pleases. Therefore, I have great pleasure
in supporting the Second Reading of this
Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: Seldom if ever in this
House have I listened to a more interesting debate than that which is now
drawing to a close, or to more
interesting speeches than those delivered
by hon. Members of this House
who know SO well all the conditions of trade unionism and the conditions under which the workers live and
so on. I do not propose to make another
speech of any length in closing the debate. I do not wish to say anything that
would in any way hurt the feelings of the
hon. Member (Senator Dorman) opposite,
who is the Opposition in this House, but
his speech lasted for fifty minutes, and as
far as I could make out forty-five minutes
of it were devoted to dealing with matters
that had very little if anything to do
with this Bill.
Question put: "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."

Presented (pursuant to Act), and
ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have agreed to the
Workmen's Compensation Bill without
Amendment, and they have passed the
Old Age Pensions (Administration) Bill
to which they desire the concurrence of
this House.
Lisburn Electric Supply Company
(Agreement) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move the Second
Reading of this Bill, the object of which
is to confirm and give effect to an agreement made between the Ministry of
Commerce and the Lisburn Electricity
Supply Company with respect to the
transmission line to be constructed by the
company for carrying electrical energy
from Belfast to Lisburn.
in
I will explain very briefly the circumstances which have given rise to the introduction of the Bill. Some time ago a
company was registered for the purpose
of obtaining statutory powers to supply
electricity within the Urban District of
Lisburn. It proposed to buy its current
from the Belfast City Council and to
carry it by overhead transmission to
Lisburn. The overhead line which the
Company intended to erect was designed
to transmit energy at a pressure of 6,600
volts only, sufficient copper being provided
the line to furnish about 1,000
kilowatts, which is the maximum estimated requirement of the consumers in
the area covered by the company. The
Government, however, looking to the
future, and knowing that the Lagan
Valley was a very favourable field for
electricity development, thought it would
be advisable that this proposed transmission line between Lisburn and Belfast
should be made capable of a much higher
pressure than would be necessary to supply
the electrical demand from Lisburn only,
so that it might be able to provide current
for large power users on the route between
Belfast and Lisburn, and possibly in the
future form the first link of a main transmission line extending further by way of
Lurgan and Portadown.
Accordingly an agreement in the
matter was reached with the company on
Trade Disputes Bill-
Committee.
8 DECEMBER 1927
The
the lines set forth in the Schedule.
Government will advance to the company
£1,450, which will be secured by a debenture, and the company, on its part, undertakes to construct a transmission line
capable of carrying energy at a pressure
of 33,000 volts instead of 6,600 volts,
which, as I have said, would have been
sufficient for its requirements in the
Lisburn area. It will be noticed that
interest will not be payable upon the debenture until the maximum load delivered
to the company at the Belfast terminus
of the transmission line exceeds 1,000
kilowatts or until power is transmitted
over the line for the company's own purposes at a pressure exceeding 6,600 volts.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Trade Disputes and Trade Unions Bill.

Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the
said Bill." (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.

AND AVA in the Chair].

fund).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: Again I have to give an
answer to Senator Leslie on a question raised on the Second Reading.
During that debate the hon. Senator
asked for an assurance that the North of
Ireland Registrar of Friendly Societies
had been adequately equipped with the
powers of register for the purpose of the
Trade Union Acts. I am quite sure that
the hon. Senator is quite familiar with
the Government of Ireland Order, 1922.
If he will look at Article 14 of that Order
he will find that the term "society" in
the sub-section of that Order includes
trade union.
may

Mr. McDOWELL: Might I ask is it
in conformity with the rules of the House
that a Member should divide the House
on a clause or several clauses without
giving any reason for doing so.
Mr. SPEAKER: Certainly. Members are not bound to give any reason for
voting against one clause or six clauses.

CLAUSE 7 (Restraint of application
of funds of trade unions, &c., in contravention of S. 1 of Act).
**
*
*
*
Without prejudice to the right of any
person having a sufficient interest in
the relief sought to sue or apply for an
injunction to restrain any application of
the funds of a trade union in contravention of the provisions of this Act, an
injunction restraining any application
of the funds of a trade union in contravention of the provisions of section
one of this Act may be granted at the
suit or upon the application of the
Attorney-General for Northern Ireland.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
:--
Page 10, line 36, leave out from
"Act" to the end of line 40, and insert
"the Attorney-General for Northern
Ireland may sue or apply for an injunction to restrain any application of the
funds of a trade union in contravention
of the provisions of Section one of this
Act."
Mr. LESLIE: It will be remembered
that on the Second Reading, I drew at
tention to this Clause and suggested that
perhaps it might be found on inquiry that
the wording used in the corresponding
Measure passed by the Imperial Parlia
ment would not be quite suitable for the
conditions as they prevail here. Since
the Second Reading, inquiry has been
made and I am satisfied, and those whom
I referred to on the Second Reading as
competent to express an opinion on the
Measure are satisfied that there is no
occasion to ask the Committee to make
any change in the wording of the Bill
before us. Consequently, of course, I do
not move any Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Question put: "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."

CLAUSE 8 (Furnishing of returns,
etc.).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: On the Second Reading
Senator Leslie raised the question of a
reduction of the minimum penalty of £1
provided in Section 16 of the Trade
Unions (Amendment) Act, 1876, and
applied in Sub-section 4 of Clause 8 of
this Bill. I am advised that Section 8
of the Conspiracy and Protection of
Property Act, 1875, which deals with a
reduction of penalties under certain circumstances, does not apply in this case.
The returns, statements, and other information required to be furnished to the
Registrar of Friendly Societies under
Clause 8 of this Bill are most important,
and the Government are of opinion that
the penalties applied under this Clause
are not excessive.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
noble Viscount for looking into the
matter. I certainly do not want to offer
any opposition to the Clause.
Question put: "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."
The Committee divided: Contents, 14;
Not Content, 1.

Clause ordered to stand part of the
