The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
After Clause 8 insert the following new
Clause:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in any
enactment a court of summary jurisdiction, when trying a person charged
with
offence against the Trade
any
Union Acts, 1871 to 1917, the Conspiracy and Protection of Property Act,
1875, or this Act, shall be constituted
of one or more resident magistrates;
and a court of quarter sessions, when
hearing and determining an appeal
from such court of summary jurisdiction, shall be constituted of the recorder
or county court judge sitting alone.
No justice of the peace shall have any
jurisdiction to try any offence to which
this sub-section applies, except the
persons specified therein.
(2) In this Section the expression
"resident magistrate" means a magistrate appointed under the Constabulary
(Ireland) Act, 1836.
Mr. LESLIE: On the Second Reading
of this Bill I drew attention to the possible inconvenience of leaving the Trade
Unions Acts and the Conspiracy and Protection of Property Act to be dealt with
by courts of summary jurisdiction, and
also to the difficulty as regards appeals
to quarter sessions in such cases. I do not
think that there is any occasion for me to
repeat what I said then, because deliberately, with your permission, my Lord,
and with the forbearance of the House
I went into the matter rather more fully
than is usual on a Second Reading in
order that there might be plenty of time
for the consideration of the matter.
think that all I have to do now is to
formally move the Amendment as it
I
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Long, Mr. John A.
Massereene and Ferrard, Viscount
McDowell, Mr. W. J.
stands on the Paper. It will have the effect
of absolutely confining the jurisdiction of
the cases I have referred to to one or
more resident magistrates and providing
for an appeal to the recorder or the county
court judge sitting alone.
I ought, perhaps, to say that it is
possible under present conditions for a
resident magistrate in Belfast to hear
such cases alone. The question at issue
really is this. Is the Government satisfied
that it would be possible to differentiate
in the future, as it has presumably been
possible in the past, between an offence
against the one code or do they consider
the passage of this new Bill will render
the position impossible. Personally, I
cannot but think it would be safer to
insert a provision such as I have moved,
but I imagine it is not a matter which,
whatever private opinions we may entertain, will be allowed to create any profound division of opinion, because it is a
matter entirely apart from the main
structure of the Bill before us. I beg to
move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I think it should be observed in considering this new Clause
that it would involve a consequential
Amendment of the Title of the Bill, and
this may, I think, be taken as an indication that the hon. Member accepts the
view that the Amendment is really outside the scope of the Bill as it stands.
Senator Leslie is quite correct in stating
that offences under the Bill will, with
certain exceptions, be taken before a
resident magistrate ag provided in
Section 19 of the Trade Unions Act,
1871. Those exceptions are in respect of
the offences mentioned in Sub-clause. 1
of Clause 3 and Sub-clause 4 of Clause 6,
which are linked up with the Conspiracy
and Protection of Property Act, 1875.
These offences will be heard and determined by a court consisting of two or
more justices sitting in Petty Sessions as
Trade Disputes Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
provided under Section 21 of that Act.
The Amendments proposed in the Clause
are directed to an alteration of the
machinery for enforcing the general provisions of the Trade Unions Acts, 1871-
1917, and the Conspiracy and Protection
of Property Act, 1875, and they thus
reach considerably beyond the scope of
the present Bill. I regret, therefore,
that the Amendment cannot be accepted
by the Government.
Mr. LESLIE: In that case, as I have
already indicated, I shall ask leave to
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Bangor, Viscount
Barclay, Mr. William
Clark, Sir George
Coates, Sir William
withdraw the Amendment. In doing so
I may point out that as an obvious
sequel of that Amendment and the
suggested Amendment to the Second
Schedule it would unfortunately be necessary to amend the Title.

CLAUSE 1 (Provisions as to trades,
&c., permitted to be carried on in licensed
premises).
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee.
8 DECEMBER 1927
*
*
*
Provided that-
*
*
*
*
(i) this sub-section shall not apply to
any premises structurally adapted for
use, and bonu fide used, as a hotel as
herein before in this Act defined, or as
a railway refreshment room or as a
restaurant;
(ii) notwithstanding anything contained in this sub-section, any of the
following businesses, that is to say, the
sale of tobacco, table waters, and such
food as may be supplied for consumption on the premises, or the sale of
intoxicating liquor by wholesale in
pursuance of the enactments in that
behalf, may be carried on in any premises, or in any part of any premises, where an on-licence business is
carried on, but only at the times during
which such on-licence business may
lawfully be carried on;
(iii) nothing in this sub-section shall
prevent a licensed person from carrying
on at any time, in any premises, or in
any part of any premises, where his onlicence business is carried on, the business of providing temporary stabling or
similar accommodation for vehicles or
animals.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 2, line 27, after the word
"tobacco" (tobacco, table waters) to
insert the word "matches." It is, I
think, common knowledge that people
who purchase tobacco not infrequently
require to purchase matches also. As a
matter of fact, considerable discussion
took place when the Bill was in Committee in another place with a view to
getting matches inserted in the Bill.
Unfortunately, so far as I can make out,
the attempt there was made to permit the
sale of matches on licensed premises with
an off-licence, and of course the whole
question of a mixed business was raised,
and mixed business is one of the things
which I think it will be suggested we
should not touch. Consequently proposals
to insert the word "matches" in that case
I venture to
could not be accepted.
think that perhaps the circumstances are
rather different when I suggest to permit
the sale of matches in common with
tobacco in premises with an on-licence.
I have one rather special reason for asking this. A practice has grown up of late
years, usually for the purpose of assisting
charitable objects, generally in connection
with destitute children, of having little
slot machines in places of public resort
in which it is possible to insert a penny
and obtain a small packet of matches of
the kind that I hold in my hand,
whereas, of course, you would get them
in a tobacconist's for about a farthing.
That is a small form of charitable collection. Therefore I beg to move to insert
the word "matches.'
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The effect of the Amendment of the hon. Senator would be to
allow on-licence holders to sell matches.
An Amendment was proposed in another
place to enable off-licence holders to sell
tobacco and matches. That was rejected
on the ground that it was clearly laid
down in the 1923 Act what was and what
was not mixed trading. The question is
one of degree. It seems to me, and it
seems to the Government, that if publicans are permitted to sell matches really
there is no valid reason why they should
not be permitted to sell other things as
well. We must draw the line somewhere,
and the Government can see no good
reason for departing from what was clearly
the intention of the Act of 1923. I
regret the Government are not prepared
to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Well, if the Government
deliberately come between the pipe and
the matches I suppose there is nothing
more to do but to withdraw the Amendment, but I may say no very good reason
has been given for refusing it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
inn
yards
or
Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment
standing in my name has reference to the
troublesome question of opening
public-house yards
in order to allow farmers or people
attending fairs and markets to stable
their horses without breaking the law. It
is to leave out the word "temporary" in
line 42. We all remember the somewhat
unfortunate history of the 1923 Act in
this connection. I do not know how it
happened, but I think it is generally
agreed that somebody blundered. At all
events, farmers in country districts were
put to very considerable inconvenience
owing to the closing of the yards of publichouses against them and their horses and
vehicles from the early hours of the mornings, when fairs and markets were being
419 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
held. Naturally enough there was a considerable outburst of public opinion.
The Government is taking steps to mitigate the inconvenience which, I think, they
rather undesignedly created. Now that
this is so, the question arises whether we
are going to help matters very much by
introducing an indefinite term like
"temporary." I do not want any unfortunate person charged with keeping a
yard open for an unlawful purpose merely
on the ground that in the opinion of the
local policeman the stabling was rather
more than temporary. I cannot see that
the word has any particular meaning.
Therefore, I suggest we should omit it.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I am not sure that the
hon. Member fully realizes what would
be the effect of omitting the word "temporary" from this sub-section. The effect
of it would be that it would enable a
publican to have a garage in his yard.
Well, it was not the intention of the
Government to allow such a thing as
that. The only intention of the Government was to allow publicans to permit
their customers to have temporary accommodation for their vehicles and animals.
If publicans are allowed to carry on businesses which have no necessary connection with that trade and may equally well
be carried on elsewhere, such as a garage,
it would seem to be in direct contravention of the intention underlying subsection (1) of Section 6 of the Act of 1923.
I need hardly point out that to allow publicans to have garages in their yards
would bring customers to their houses.
This is surely undesirable, and directly
contrary to the Act of 1923. Therefore,
the Government cannot accept it.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might ask
the noble Viscount with reference to the
whole question whether it is intended to
prevent the man with stables in a yard
supplying fodder. It does happen when
horses are stabled or put up in a yard
they require food. We had a terrible
picture on the Second Reading of animals
left, in the early days after the 1923 Act,
without food, because the yard could not
be opened. I would really like to know
from the noble Viscount whether the man
who admits horses to stabling will not be
allowed to feed them. Upon that depends
the following Amendment, which is
bound up with the one before the Committee.
VISCOUNT
MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: Off hand I cannot answer
the hon. Member's question definitely. I
will look into the matter and find out exactly the intention before the Report
stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps the Committee
will allow me to withdraw the Amendment dealing with "temporary." I will
not move the following one and I will
bring the matter up on Report.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
Line 42, to leave out "stabling or
similar."
Amendment not moved.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
beg to move on page 2, line 43, at end
("vehicles or animals") to add "and
suitable accommodation for any person
in charge of such vehicles or animals."
I move this Amendment for the purpose
of preventing any misunderstanding that
may exist when the Act becomes law in
case those responsible for carrying out
the law might be rather strict. It has
been referred to so often, one would think
there is hardly any necessity for putting
words such as these in to make it clear,
but when we remember that after the
passing of the 1923 Act on Dry Sunday,
the first dry Sunday, horses and other
cattle stabled in licensed premises had to
go dry and without fodder owing to the
action of the police, one can readily
understand they may go so far as to say,
"We will admit animals for temporary
stabling or vehicles for housing, yet we
are not entitled to admit any person with
them.' I think it is necessary that these
words should be inserted so that the police
and those responsible would understand
that they do not expect a horse to travel
into the yard by itself, or that it is expected that a motor vehicle would be propelled into the yard without some person
taking charge of it.
""
Another thing very necessary is that
the person who has the on-licence, the inn
keeper or publican should be permitted
to provide suitable accommodation for
these persons. They cannot be admitted
to the bar or premises where drink is
Trade Disputes Bill-
Committee.
8 DECEMBER 1927
for fear they would drink any of it, but
surely it is only reasonable to expect the
publican who might be anxious to safeguard his trade or improve his trade to
provide a small room or rooms for the
accommodation of these people. When
their horses are stabled and comfortable
they also should be made comfortable
from the hour when they arrive until a
short time before the fair. I must admit
it is out of sympathy with these people I
am moving, because, as pointed out by
Senator Barclay, I know nothing about
the country. Unfortunately, I am
town-reared and town-bred so that I
cannot profess to know all about it, but I
can readily understand it must be a
terrible hardship. I remember one case,
I think it was in Ballymoney, I was passing through Ballymoney and I asked my
friend to pull up at the first refreshment
place. I said to the man behind the bar
"I suppose the 1923 Act does not affect
you."
"Does it not " he said, and he
started then to tell me how these poor
men were lined up on the other side of
the street on market day or fair day unable to get any place to put their horses
or to be accommodated themselves. There
they had to stand until ten o'clock when
the bars were opened. I can readily
understand it must be a terrible hardship on these poor fellows who were not
too well clothed or shod. I beg to move.
Mr. McDOWELL: This is what I
might call the thin edge of the wedge.
The mover of the Amendment is most
anxious that the publican or inn-keeper,
whatever you like to call him, should be put
to great inconvenience to build outhouses
for the purpose of accommodation in
stormy cold weather, because he has
already said he does not want these people
to go into the bar. I think it would
put the publican to great inconvenience
to do this. He referred also to Senator
Barclay's statement on the Second Reading. But Senator Barclay forgot to state
that when he was a young fellow going
on market day at 3, 4, or 5 o'clock in the
morning and arriving at half-past six or
seven that the public-houses were opened
at 7 o'clock. But we have men coming
to the shipyard away from Portadown,
Lurgan, Donaghadee or the coldest place
you can think of and they land here on
a wet or cold, frosty morning. I wonder
if he would ask the publicans round the
Queen's Road to make provision to give
these fellows a bit of something warm.
I can hardly support the Amendment, and
I must vote against it. I think it is the
thin end of the wedge.
There is no necessity for these men
leaving their homes at an untimely hour
to get into the market at say half-past
eight or half-past nine o'clock. They
have ample time to get in, and by the
time they have disposed of their goods if
they want any refreshment the publicans
will be open at ten o'clock. If they are
90 dry they can go to a restaurant. After
all, I hope the gentlemen farmers here
will not for a moment take it that we
consider they are most anxious to get a
drink before they go to the market so as
to put them in good bidding form. I
think it is the thin end of the wedge, and
consequently I must oppose it.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Since the Second
Reading of this Bill I have taken
occasion to speak to several people engaged in cattle dealing, and I understand
that the fairs are now started very much
later than they used to be, as indeed all
labour is, and that there is no difficulty
at all in this matter. In addition to
that one has to remember that horses are
gradually disappearing from the country,
and particularly with regard to fairs.
People who go to fairs now pay a shilling a head to go in a motor, and at any
rate fairs now do not begin until 9-30 or
10-a.m., and under these circumstances
I do not think their grievances are great,
if there are any at all. On the Second
Reading of this Bill Senator Barclay referred to his experiences at a rather
distant period when I dare say there was
a good deal of hardship, but I do not
think that exists now from all I can ascertain in discussing the matter with
people who are familiar with the cirstances.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
think this Amendment is entirely unnecessary. When a farmer goes into a fair
there is no necessity for him to spend his
time standing in a queue. If he was a
wise man he would stable his horses and
then go and transact his business. He
would not need any waiting room.
If a
farmer is to be successful he will stay at
home until it is time to leave for the fair,
and he will leave at such a time as will
enable him to arrive in the fair when it
is opened.
Mr. LONG: Senator Samuel Cunningham has just anticipated what I was
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
going to say. I have attended a good
many fairs and markets, and for thirty
years I have travelled seventeen and a
half miles twice a week to a market. A
farmer can time his arrival in a fair or
rarket to within five minutes of the opening of that fair or market. With a horse
vehicle there are no punctures to be contended with, and a farmer only arrives
in a market town in time to do his business. I am entirely opposed to this
Amendment, because I believe it would
cpen a very wide door, and it would
ender it extremely difficult for adequate
police supervision to be exercised over a
publican. My experience is that you will
find a good many unscrupulous publicans.
who would smuggle drink into these waiting rooms that are suggested in this
Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the acceptance
of this Amendment would open the door
to a lot of clandestine drinking. Publicans would be disposed to jump at an
opportunity of this kind to provide accommodation for the human animal as
well as for the beast of the field. I know
a good deal about country towns all over
Ireland, and there is already an enormous amount of clandestine drinking
going on in public houses and hotels. It
is possible to be in these premises at midnight, and for liquor to be paid for and
supplied from the bar. I have seen it.
myself and I hope the Government will
not encourage this to go on.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: So many objections have
been raised by hon. Members to this
Amendment that it is hardly necessary for
me to explain the Government's position.
What would be the real effect of this
Amendment? The effect of it would be
to allow members of the general public
to be on licensed premises during closing
hours. That would mean that the supervision of the licensed premises by the
police would be extremely difficult, even
more difficult than it is at the moment.
The Amendment is in direct conflict with
the provision of the 1923 Act, and that
provision is an important one. It places
the onus upon the publican of proving
that persons found on licensed premises
during closing hours were there for no
illegal purpose. If a publican has got to
provide suitable accommodation for persons in charge of animals and vehicles
how is he going to do it? The accommodation must have no connection with the
public house. It must have a separate
entrance, and persons would have to cone
out of the yard into the street in order to
get to the room provided. Otherwise the
publican would not be complying with
the Act of 1923. On those grounds the
Government cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: That
being so, I withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 2 (Sale and consumption
of intoxicating liquor in hotels and
restaurants).
(1) Notwithstanding the provisions
of the Act of 1923 or any other enactment it shall be lawful on Sunday or
Christmas Day to sell or consume intoxicating liquor in an hotel as defined
by sub-section (3) of section one of the
said Act, where such liquor is sold to
or consumed by a guest or traveller
who has lodged for the preceding night
or has in writing engaged a room for
the succeeding night in such hotel at
or during the hours specified for the
purpose of the sale of intoxicating
liquor in Article 1 of Part I of the
Second Schedule to that Act.
(2) Where any dinner or other entertainment is held in an hotel or restaurant which is licensed for the sale of
intoxicating liquor, it shall not be lawful for the proprietor or manager
thereof to allow the consumption of intoxicating liquor, by persons attending
such dinner or entertainment, at hours
other than those specified for the purpose of the sale of intoxicating liquor
by the Act of 1923 or any other enactment:
Provided that such consumption may
lawfully be allowed on any day, other
than Sunday or Christmas Day or a day
on which an order under section ten
of the Act of 1923 is in force, where the
proprietor or manager of the hotel or
restaurant has given previous notice to
the district-inspector (or in his absence,
to the head-constable) of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, acting for the
district in which the premises are
situate, of the intention to hold any
425 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

8 DECEMBER 1927
Committee.
dinner or entertainment, and has received a permit from such districtinspector or bead-constable authorising
the consumption of intoxicating liquor
in a specified room or rooms during the
hours mentioned in such permit; but so
that such proprietor or manager shall
not, in connection with any such dinner
or entertainment, sell or expose for sale
intoxicating liquor at any hours other
than those specified for those purposes
by the enactments aforesaid.
The following Amendments stood ou
the Order Paper:-
Page 3, line 10, leave out "Sunday or
Christmas Day."-(Mr. J. C. Glendinning).
Page 3, line 15, leave out from "hotel"
to the end of line 17 (at or during hours
specified for the purpose of the sale of
intoxicating liquor in article 1 of Part I
of the Second Schedule to that Act).
-(Mr. Joseph Cunningham and Mr. J.
C. Glendinning).
The CHAIRMAN: I understand the
hon. Senator is going to move the two
Amendments together.
Mr. GLENDINNING : I was just
going to ask your permission to take the
two Amendments together. If they were
adopted the sub-section would then
read: :-
(1) Notwithstanding the provisions
of the Act of 1923 or any other enactment it shall be lawful to sell or consume intoxicating liquor in an hotel
as defined by sub-section (3) of section
one of the said Act. where such liquor
is sold to or consumed by a guest or
traveller who has lodged for the preceding night or has in writing engaged
a room for the succeeding night in
such hotel.
The object of these Amendments is to
remove the restrictions on bona-fide
residents in hotels, and I ask hon.
Members to regard these Amendments in
the light of common sense, and to deal
with the matter as ordinary members of
the public. As I said on the Second
Reading, my reasons for moving these
Amendments are three in number.
first concerns people coming over from
Great Britain to Northern Ireland and
staying in hotels here. I am proud, and
The
I hope most of us are proud, to belong
to the United Kingdom. I have always
loved to regard our part of Northern
Ireland as if it were a county or shire on
the other side of the water where people
are of the same type and have the same
ideas. There is nothing calculated to do
more harm to our beloved Province than
the idea of a person coming over here
and finding himself in many respects in
a different country. When a traveller
from Great Britain comes over here and
takes up his residence in an hotel he goes
about his day's business, and when
he comes in again at night he goes
into the lounge of his hotel and calls for
a drink. He cannot get it. He probably
says to himself, "This is a funny place
I am in with all these petty restrictions.
I hope I shall not have to come back
here. Such a thing as that belittles
this country and does it a great deal of
harm.

The
My second reason is with regard to
residents in hotels in general. Broadly
speaking, residents in hotels may be regarded as of two distinct classes.
first class comprises the business people,
and if owners and managers of hotels
were consulted I think they would say
that the travelling residents are really
the mainstay of the hotel trade. The
business-man staying in an hotel is
already subjected to a considerable
amount of discomfort. He is living in an
uncongenial atmosphere and he loses a
good many of the home comforts he is
accustomed to, and it is rather tantalizing
to him that he should be restricted from
so many of the comforts that he enjoys
in his own home or club. A business-man
does his business during the day and after
dinner he may go out to a place of amusement. He comes back into the lounge
of the hotel and probably wants the little
refreshment that he is accustomed to have,
but he finds that he cannot get it.
As a
bona-fide resident in an hotel I think he
should be able to get a drink at 10 or 11
o'clock at night if he wants it. That
applies to a very large class of hotel
residents.
Another class of residents in hotels are
those who are there on holiday. I would
like hon. Members to consider the life in
an hotel during the summer months, from
May until August. We are then under the
Summer Time Act and as a matter of fact
at the closing hour it is only 7-30 p.m.
by ordinary time. People are out play-
Intoxicating Liquor Bill--
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Glendinning]
ing golf or tennis or walking until dusk,
which is about 10 or 11 o'clock. When
those people come back to the hotel in
which they are staying they cannot get
a drink. I think that is another very
good reason why the Government should
accept these Amendments. Even those
who are not engaged in playing golf or
other games probably dine later in the
summer time than they usually do. They
may have their dinner at 8 o'clock. After
sitting chatting they may go into the
lounge to have a liqueur with their coffee,
only to be informed by the waiter that
they cannot have it because it is after 9
o'clock. I think that is a very irritating
restriction.
That brings me to the third reason I
have for proposing this Amendment.
People who are caught like that make up
their minds that they will not be caught
again, and they take good care that on
every other night of the week they will
have something upstairs. The result is that
they will order drink to be taken up to their
bedroom, and I venture to say they will
actually drink more in their bedroom
than they would if they had been able
to have a small whisky in the ordinary
course downstairs. I think that is only
a common sense way of looking at the
matter. The adoption of these Amendments would be in the interests of true
temperance, because I believe more harm
is done to true temperance by these petty
restrictions than in any other way. One
hon. Senator, speaking on the Second
Reading of this Bill, said he would object
to these Amendments because people
would engage rooms in an hotel on a
Saturday night and they would bring in
as many people as they liked on Sunday
to drink. I would ask hon. Senators not
to allow their imagination to run away
with them. I do not think anything like
that would occur.
a
I would ask hon. Members to remember
that hotel owners and managers are
respectable body of persons. There is in
existence a very strong hotel managers'
association, and I think that if the
Government accepted these Amendments
and removed these restrictions, that
association would see that the Clause was
not abused. The Government have gone
a step in the right direction in this Bill
regarding hotels, and if they went.
little bit further and removed all these
restrictions I believe the hotel managers
association would take care that the
Act
was not infringed in any way.
And as a matter of fact could it be infringed? The really decently managed
hotel looks with considerable suspicion on
any person coming in without baggage
in the ordinary way. They want to know
why they want a room without any apparent evidence of travelling or anything
else. The probability is that unless the
man puts down a heavy deposit he would
have very great difficulty in getting a
room in the hotel at all. With regard to
the small hotel, which Senator Dickson
says might infringe the Act, my opinion is
if it were tried on in a small hotel the
police and everybody else would twig it
at once, and know that it was a bogus
business altogether. The hotel proprietor would be too much concerned about
his licence to permit it going on.
to say.
I have only one or two further things
I am not asking the Government
to abandon any principle for the sake of
public popularity, but there certainly is
a feeling that the Government in this
Bill have shown some indifference to the
general views of the community. I think
even down in another place a good many
people on the Government side would in
their hearts be in sympathy with this
Amendment. I think it is only a very
reasonable request that people living in a
hotel temporarily should be able to regard
that hotel as their home, and there should
be no restrictions in regard to liquor or
anything else. I beg to move.
Mr. BARCLAY: I do not think it
would be wise on the part of this Committee
to accept this Amendment. I think une
Government has done pretty well for the
hotels by allowing them to open their premises on Sunday and Christmas Day for
certain hours for the purpose of selling
and for the consumption of intoxicating
liquor.
Mr. DORMAN: They have not done it.
Mr. BARCLAY: Not done it?
Mr.
MCDOWELL: You are right
enough. Go on. Do not heed him.
Mr. BARCLAY: I think it would not
be wisdom to extend the time any longer
than in the Bill. To do so to my mind
would be creating a law for the rich and
the aristocrat and at the same time ignoring the poor and the working men. The
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee.
8 DECEMBER 1927
class of folk who go to these hotels which
have been spoken of, such as the railway
hotels and other hotels in the city, would
not go to a public house in a side street
which has its door shut all day on Sunday. I think if the House accepted that
Amendment it would create ill feeling,
and it would encourage members of the
Socialist party in the class warfare they
are trying to carry on.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. BARCLAY: I object to this
Amendment, and I will vote against it.
Mr. LESLIE: The first thing, I think,
that has to be pointed out is that the
Amendment really before us is to leave out
the words "on Sunday or Christmas
Day." The Paper as printed left out the
word "on."
Mr. GLENDINNING: Yes; I noticed
that.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that perhaps one
ought to warn the Committee that if this
Amendment is carried-and on general
principles I think I must support it-if
it should be carried other provisions
would be necessary, and it may be that
these provisions would not be confined
within the corners of this particular Bill.
It would mean a very serious change, but
the mere fact that it makes a change in
the Act of 1923 does not seem to me to
be any particular reason for rejecting
the Amendment. The Act of 1923, like
any other statute, is liable to revision by
Parliament from time to time, and the
Act of 1923 is not perhaps a singularly
good example of omniscience. If it were so
it would not be necessary for Parliament
to consider an amending Bill in respect of
it at all.
I
I do not quite follow the hon. Senator
from Londonderry who moved this
Amendment in his reference to Ulster as
being a county of Great Britain.
rather thought that the hon. Senator,
like myself, would have some pride in
being Irish in the sense that we are all
part of this country, and however great
our affection for Great Britain still we
would like to retain some individuality.
The difficulty which I have in maintaining the present arrangement is that if a
man goes to a properly licensed hotel,
which pays a very heavy duty, he is under
restrictions which he is not under if he
goes to a temperance hotel.
If he goes
for
to a so-called temperance hotel he can take
his whisky with him, and apparently consume it at any time provided there is no
sale or exposure for sale. That is an
arrangement to which hotel keepers very
strongly object. Also, I think there is
too much tendency to regard this matter
as if all hotels were what are commonly called public houses. An hotel
does not exist primarily for drinking
purposes. Such
as
drinking
does
take place is purely ancillary to
the general accommodation
the
traveller. I do not think that distinction
has been sufficiently maintained. I do not
know if the Government give us this
Amendment whether it would be possible
at this late period of the year to deal
with the other provisions which I referred
to as being probably necessary. I think
more regard ought to be shown to the
feeling that there is in the Province towards greater liberty as against these
restrictions on their liberty which they
never anticipated would be imposed upon
them. It is not for us in this House I
think to pay any particular regard to
extremists on one side or the other. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) We are not concerned with the shouts of the so-called
Temperance party, and we are not particularly concerned to support the drink
trade. What we are concerned with, I
think, is to see that the public gets
reasonable convenience if it wants it. Our
duty is to the public as a whole, and not.
to the temperance orator or the publican
in particular. I should give general
support to this Amendment if it is
pressed to a conclusion. But I am not
quite certain in my own mind that it
would be altogether well to accept it,
but if it does not materialize now I think
the matter ought not to be lost sight of
and ought to be brought forward on a
later Clause of the Bill when I suggest.
a certain possibility to the House. For
the present I must say I hope this Amend
ment will be received in a cordial and
more receptive spirit. This is not a trifle
or a matter of whether a man can buy u
box of matches or not. This is a much
more serious thing. I must support the
Amendment of my hon. Friend.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I do not agree
with the previous speaker that many of
the hotels in Ireland are used for the
Intoxicating Liquor Bill—
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dickson]
purpose of hotels. I quite agree that in
the cities of Belfast and Derry and perhaps in seaside resorts hotels are conducted for the purpose for which they
are named as hotels and where people
stay. But if we go round Northern Ireland in every little village you have
"hotel" marked up on the outside of a
place where no person ever stays. All
that is necessary to turn that public-house
into a hotel is to have four rooms-
Mr. LESLIE: No.
Mr. HILL DICKSON---- and to get
licensed accordingly as a hotel. These
rooms are usually occupied by the proprietor himself, because he would have
no place else to sleep in if he did not.
They are simply public-houses, and I
hold that the owners of these places are
not philanthropists. They are out to make
money, and if you allow the visitor who
comes down on Saturday and pays half-acrown for a room which he need not
necessarily occupy he can open the publichouse to the whole village next morning,
and invite the whole village. He is
within the law in doing so. It would be
worth his while, and it would not be
breaking the law. I hold it would be reintroducing the bona-fide traveller
Clause. I do not suggest for one moment
that any respectable well-managed hotels
in the places I have referred to in the
cities would permit it. But take the
little villages, where as I tell you, no
person ever sleeps. They are simply
public-houses.
Mr. LESLIE: As I have a loose copy
of the 1923 Act with me, perhaps I might
interpose for a moment to remind my hon.
Friend of what the provisions are regarding hotels:
The expression "hotel" means
premises licensed for the sale of intoxicating liquor by retail, which are
structurally adapted to be used and
are bona-fide used for the purpose of
the reception of guests and travellers-
Therefore, if the small hotels so called
in the North of Ireland, with which I
gather neither of us is likely to have any
sympathy, have guest rooms which are
being used for the purposes of the proprietor of the hotel and not bona-fide for
the reception of guests and travellers they
are breaking the law, and would not be
entitled to the protection which my hon.
Friend from Londonderry wishes to give.
There is no mistake about it. It must be
a bona-fide hotel for the reception of
guests and travellers.

Amend-
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
did not intend to support this
ment, but evidently on this Amendment
hinges the whole principle. If this matter
is decided it really does away with the
other Amendment to the other portion
which I have down to be deleted. I think
as far as I can see we are really discussing the principle of the entire removal
of the limitations on hotels at the present
time. I want to say I am certainly in
favour of removing unnecessary restrictions placed on hotels, and I am speaking
of proper bona-fide hotels, such as exist in
Belfast, Derry, Portrush, Newcastle, and
these other seaside towns. It is quite
evident in the North of Ireland we are at
a disadvantage. We depend to a considerable extent on the tourist traffic and
we are endeavouring as far as possible
to encourage that traffic. To retain the
restrictions that at present exist on those
who occupy or reside in hotels is injurious
to hotel traffic and tourist traffic.
Furthermore, hotels are considerably used
by travellers, and I think we all desire
to encourage as far as possible travellers
coming to Ireland, even to other parts of
Ireland as well as to our own Six Counties.
To inflict these restrictions still on
those who are making use of hotels is
very unfair. It is more like grandmotherly legislation. I must admit that
I do not see the necessity, even for temperance purposes, to have such restrictions
imposed on grown-up persons. It might
be necessary for young persons who have
no control over themselves and who require the protection of the law, but in the
case of grown-up people I cannot for the
life of me see any necessity for it, even
looking at the matter from the temperance
point of view. The policy of the Unionist party so far as the drink traffic is
concerned is education and regulation.
No one would attempt to defend those
who take drink to excess, and what we
aim at is to leave some freedom to those
who are able to take care of themselves
and not impose our views upon them
against their will. If this liberty is taken
advantage of it is the fault of the authorities for not looking after licensed premises properly. If the police were doing
their duty in regard to the ordinary
public-houses there would not be any
433 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

8 DECEMBER 1927
Committee.
drinking taking place on a Sunday, because the law distinctly states there must
be no licensed premises open for the sale
or consumption of drink on Sunday.
There-
There are a large number of people who
make an hotel their home, and such
persons are entitled to the same freedom
and privileges as they would have if they
were living in their own homes.
fore, I think the Government should remove these slight restrictions. A most
unfair position is created under this Bill
While the owner of a bona-fide hotel has
to pay for his licence and must satisfy
the authorities as to the structural suitability of the hotel he is restricted to a
much greater extent than the person who
may have what he calls a temperance
hotel on the other side of the street.
Drink can be carried into that temperance hotel, and can be consumed at any
hour of the day or night irrespective of
the police or anyone else. To place any
restrictions on the bona-fide hotel that is
properly licensed is very unfair, and,
therefore, I am fully in favour of the
further freedom that is asked for in this
Amendment being given to those who are
using hotels as their residence, so that
they will not be restricted to any particular hours during which they can purchase and consume drink on the hotel
premises.
"con-
It is almost ridiculous to say
sume," because everyone knows that even
if the hours of sale are restricted a person
cannot be prevented from consuming,
because if he buys drink during opening
hours he can take it to his bedroom and
consume it whenever he likes, irrespective of the police or anyone else. It would
be very hard for the police to detect
drinking under those circumstances, and
I think the best way out of the difficulty
would be to remove the restrictions and
give hotel guests absolute freedom.
VISCOUNT BANGOR: I received a
letter a day or two ago, from the Secretary of the Belfast and North of Ireland
Hotel Association, and I dare say other
Members have received a similar letter.
They point out the disabilities under
which they labour in being restricted in
these hours, and they also point out that
in towns of under 5,000 inhabitants the
hours are from 10 a.m. to 9 p.m., and
that although the permanent population
is under 5,000, in the summer time it is
a great deal more than 5,000. Even in
that case they cannot sell any excisable
refreshments after 9 p.m. As has been
pointed out by Senator Glendinning,
under the Summer Time Act 9 p.m. is
really 7-30 p.m., and in the summer time
people like to take their dinner a little
later.
They will come in and order
liquor, and at 9 o'clock it is swept off the
table and they are not allowed to have it.
The proprietors of these hotels are also
in an unfair position compared with the
owners of temperance hotels, for the
latter can take in liquor at any time they
like. This Clause is interfering with the
liberty of the subject, and therefore I
shall certainly support the Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the whole point
centres on the word "consume." If a
man has his home in an hotel, as many
individuals have, he is going to be debarred under this Clause from a right
and privilege he can exercise in his own
home or in any temperance hotel. I do
not speak at all with the object of encouraging the consumption of intoxicating
liquor, because I would desire to see everyone a total abstainer like myself, but it
seems to me that we are depriving the
citizen who may of necessity be compelled
to make his permanent or temporary
residence in an hotel of a right he can
exercise in his own home or in a temperance hotel. As a lover of freedom I shall
support the Amendment, if it goes to a
division.
Mr. McDOWELL: Senator Glendinning's first objection to this Clause was
that it would have a bad effect on tourists
visiting this country. I wonder if he has
allowed his imagination to run away with
him to the extent of believing that
tourists come to Ireland for no other purpose than to taste Bushmills and Comber
whisky. As a rule I think they come to
see the beauties of Ireland, and my information is that the tourist traffic to Ireland has increased since 1923. There is
the point about travellers. They come
here, and through force of circumstances
they go to hotels. They have from 10
a.m. to 10 p.m. to drink as much as they
like, and a traveller who cannot drink all
he wants in that time does perhaps deserve
to have two or three hours extra. I cannot see any reason why hotel hours should
be any more than public house hours.
They should all be treated on the same
basis, and if a man wants a drink he can
get it before 10 p.m. I cannot follow
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McDowell j
He
the reasoning of Senator Viscount Bangor
in what he said about summer time.
said 9 p.m. really means 7-30 p.m., but
he should not forget that the clock has
changed as well as the hour.
Mr. DORMAN: The sun has not
changed.
no
Mr. McDOWELL: It makes
difference so far as the time in an hotel
is concerned. I think this is another
attempt, even by those who believe in
temperance and have been temperance
advocates for fifty years, to open the door
wider. The licensing laws seem to have
had a curious effect. They created a
certain position to enable a man to get
drunk, and then threw a lot of temptation
in his way to get drunk again. That
seems to be at variance with the real
intention of the licensing laws. Temperance people want to try to introduce
healthy social conditions for the people.
For these reasons I shall oppose the
Amendment.
VISCOUNT
MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The Amendments proposed
by Senator Glendinning are very important, and they are Amendments
which cut clean across one of the main
objects of the Bill of 1923-clean across
it. The effect of the Amendments must
be to allow a guest or traveller in an
hotel to obtain drink at any hour of the
day or night. One of the main principles
of the Act of 1923 was the limitation of
opening hours in all licensed premises.
These Amendments would practically do
away with that principle. A similar
Amendment was moved in another place,
and after considerable debate it was rejected by the Government on the ground
that, as I said before, it cuts across the
main principle of the Act of 1923. This
Amending Bill was not brought in by the
Government to modify any of the main
principles of the Act of 1923. It is true
that it is an amending Bill, but it is not
intended to seriously modify any of the
main principles of the Act of 1923 on
which this Bill has been founded. I think
hon. Members should realize that in the
Bill which is now before the House the
Government have endeavoured to take
what I may call the media via between
the extremists on the one side and the
extremists on the other side. It is a
Bill that has been debated for many days
in another place, and it has come to us in
its present form.
I must also point out that if the Committee accepts these Amendments they
will make a very serious change in the
Bill, and so far as I know it may mean
other changes in the Bill subsequently.
This must cause considerable delay and it
might mean that we should have to hold
up the Bill until another Session. Therefore, I do appeal to the hon. Member not
to press these Amendments.
Mr. GLENDINNING: After listening
to all that has been said on these Amendments, I do not think there has been
any real objection offered to the statements I made, except by the Noble
Viscount. I had intended to divide the
Committee on these Amendments. But
I now find myself in a very difficult
position indeed. I stated myself on the
Second Reading that I regarded the Bill
as a very important Measure, and one
calculated to do a great deal of good in
many other respects. I have already admitted that even with regard to hotels
the Bill has gone a step in the right
direction by allowing guests in hotels on
Sunday and Christmas Day to have a
drink and not confining them to the time
they are actually having a meal. I should
be very sorry indeed if any step on my
part resulted in endangering the Bill or
postponing the operation of it for any
considerable time. I should like to ask
the Leader of the House where we would
be if the Committee carried the Amendment--where we would be with regard to
the fate of the Bill.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member must either move his Amendment or not move it.
Mr.
I am per-

fectly prepared to move the Amendment.
Mr. SPEAKER proceeded to put the
first Amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: With the
greatest respect I understood you were
taking the two Amendments together.
namely, to leave out "Sunday and Christmay Day" together with the next one in
line 15.
Mr. SPEAKER: I intend to put both

cessful in the first Amendment then I

leave out "Sunday and Christmas Day."
The Question is that the words proposed to
be left out stand part of the Clause.
Mr. GLENDINNING: My point is
this: If we carry the first part of the
Amendment and lose the other Amendment then the hotels will lose the advantage they are gaining under the Amendment. That is my point.
Mr. SPEAKER: That is the fault of
the hon. Member in putting down the
Amendment. I am bound to put the
Amendment as down on the Paper.
At the very
Mr. GLENDINNING:
beginning I understood you to say the
two Amendments would go together as
one. I asked permission to have the two
taken before I spoke on my Amendment.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am bound to put
one Question at a time. I cannot put
two Amendments at one and the same
time. What I pointed out to the hon.
Member originally was that the first
Amendment, as far as I could see, made
no sense apart from the other Amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: Quite, and
I read out how the Clause would run
with my Amendments from beginning to
end.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry if there
was any misunderstanding. I have to
put the Question.
Mr. GLENDINNING: In that case
I shall not divide the Committee on the
first Amendment alone, because I could
not risk taking away from the hotels the
advantage already gained in the Bill.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: On
a point of order. The Committee is
divided.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have put the

Question: "That the words proposed
to be left out stand part of the Clause,"
put, and agreed to.

must admit-

Mr. LESLIE: I might, perhaps, make
a little explanation as regards the headconstable. It may be remembered I spoke
about the head-constable in various passages in the Bill on Second Reading and
suggested an Amendment. I find on
inquiry there are cases where the words

Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Amendment of Provisions
of Section 9 of the Act of 1923 as to new
licences):
(1) In any case where (whether before or after the passing of this Act)
compensation has been awarded under
the Act of 1923 by reason of the nonrenewal in respect of any premises of
a licence for the sale of intoxicating
liquor, such premises shall not, for the
purposes of paragraph (a) of section
nine of the Act of 1923, be treated as
premises coming within the first exception in that paragraph.
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
(2) No licence shall be granted under
paragraph (u) (i), (b), (c) or (e) of
section nine of the Act of 1923 except
a licence which is identical in character
with the licence or licences previously
in force, extinguished or surrendered
(as the case may require) or a licence
to which paragraph (iii) of sub-section
(6) of this section applies.
(3) It shall not be lawful to grant,
in pursuance of the provisions of paragraph (a) (i) of section nine of the
Act of 1923, a retailer's on-licence
which does not contain any condition
as to early-closing under section two of
the Licensing Act (Ireland), 1874 (in
sub-section referred to as "a full-time
licence") for premises having an earlyclosing licence.
Any full-time licence which has been
granted since the passing of the Act of
1923, for premises having at the passing
of that Act an early-closing licence,
shall be renewable only as an earlyclosing licence; and any certificate
granted at the annual licensing sessions
held in the year nineteen hundred and
twenty-seven, and authorising the issue
of a full time licence for such premises,
shall have effect as a certificate for an
early-closing licence.
(4) the following paragraph shall be
substituted for paragraph (b) of section
nine of the Act of 1923:-
"(b) Where, by reason of the expiration of a lease, a retailer's onlicence or a retailer's off-licence in
respect of premises comprised in the
lease is extinguished or surrendered,
the licensing authority may, notwithstanding anything in this section, grant a licence for suitable
premises in the vicinity of the
premises to which the licence so extinguished or surrendered was
attached:
Provided that no such licence shall
be granted to any person unless he
has abandoned any claim which he
may have, under section five of the
Town Tenants (Ireland) Act, 1906,
or any subsequent enactment, to
compensation for the loss of goodwill
in respect of a tenancy of the premises to which the licence so extinguished or surrendered was attached."

(5) Where the business of a restaurant, being a restaurant which has an
on-licence in respect of which reduced
duty has been paid under section fortyfive of the Finance (1909-10) Act, 1910,
is proposed to be transferred to premises
other than those in respect of which the
licence is in force, then the licensing
court may, notwithstanding anything
in section 9 of the Act of 1923, grant
a certificate authorizing the issue of an
on-licence for suitable premises in the
immediate vicinity of the premises
from which the business is to be transferred in substitution for the licence
attached to the last-mentioned premises:
Provided that--
(i) no public bar for the sale of
intoxicating liquor shall be kept
under authority of such licence; and
(ii) where the premises originally
licensed are acquired compulsorily
by any Government department or
any local or public authority no
licence shall be granted under this
sub-section to any person unless he
has abandoned any claim which he
may have to compensation in respect
of so much of the value of the premises compulsorily acquired as is
attributable to their use as licensed
premises.
(6) A licence shall not be granted
under paragraph (c) of section nine of
the Act of 1923 in substitution for any
existing licences, except under the
following conditions:
(i) The effect of the substitution
must be that the sale of every kind
of intoxicating liquor ceases in at
least two separate sets of premises or,
where an off-licence, attached to the
premises in respect of which the new
licence is sought, is surrendered, in
at least one separate set of premises;
(ii) The new licence must be identical in character with the licences in
substitution for which it is sought,
except in cases to which the next following paragraph applies;
(iii) Where the application is for a
new retailer's on-licence, one of the
existing licences, in substitution for
which the new licence is sought, may
be a retailer's off-licence;
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
8 DECEMBER 1927
(iv) The required increase of population shall be proved by means of a
certificate of the Minister of Home
Affairs that he is satisfied that there
has been an increase of not less than
twenty-five per cent. since the census
ascertained next before the passing of
the Act of 1923; and such fee shall
be payable for the issue of the certificate as the said Minister may, with
the approval of the Ministry of
Finance, determine.
(7) Where a new licence is granted
under paragraph (a), (b) or (e) of
Section nine of the Act of 1923 or under
Sub-section (5) of this Section, any convictions recorded against the old licence
shall be deemed to apply, and always
to have applied, to the new licence. A
new licence shall, if granted under the
said paragraph (e), include both the
old and the new premises.
Mr. GLENDINNING: With regard to
the Amendment, standing in my name, I
would like to explain to hon. Members
who do not exactly understand this-
Mr. SPEAKER: May I just, first of
all, so that there will be no misunderstanding, explain that I must put line
23 to line 26. If I were to put the
Amendment put down by the hon. Member it would knock out Mr. Leslie's
Amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: Am I in order
in moving my Amendment now?
Mr. SPEAKER: Yes.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I beg to move
Clause 3, page 4, line 23, to leave out
lines 23 to 31 inclusive (the second paragraph of Sub-clause (3). It seems that
after the passing of the 1923 Act certain
traders to the number of 10, I believe,
and all in County Fermanagh, applied to
the county court judge for an extension
of their early-closing licences, and the
county court judge granted the extension.
These traders since then have been enjoying full-time licences. They have paid
the full-time duty and no objection has
been taken by the trade to their enjoyment of it. The Licensed Vinters'
Association of County Fermanagh fully
approved of the transaction. It is only
fair to say that the Government took
another case to the higher court, and the
licence was refused by the higher court,
although it also had been granted by the
county court judge. The first part of
Clause 3 is to prevent anything like that
happening in future. The paragraph
which my Amendment seeks to eliminate
is retrospective, and goes back to wipe out
what has been done with regard to these
ten traders, and to restore the early-closing licence which they formerly possessed. These members of the Licensed
Traders' Association have got this extension of time by the law of the land.
They have held it since then. They have
been permitted by the Government to enjoy all the benefit of it. It really only
means one extra hour from eight to nine
o'clock. Now, I am prepared to admit
that the Government are perfectly right
from their point of view in going back,
because it was never intended, I daresay,
that the Act of 1923 should result in
early-closing licences being extended to
full-time licences. Why it was done I am
sure I do not know, but I would appeal
to this House and to the Government, as
it has been done, and as these few traders
alone are affected, and as the Government
themselves have practically acquiesced in
it, and as they have held the licences for
three years the Government should not
go back and put in this Clause which deals
with these few people alone. I am not
acquainted with any of them. The hon.
Senator, Mr. Porter Porter, who lives in
their district and knows them all, is keenly
interested in this Amendment. As a
matter of fact, I understood at first he
was going to propose it himself, but he
is in London, and he asked me to take
over that duty for him.
I think, after all, as this thing has been
done and no principle of the Bill is
affected by it in any way the Government might leave out this particular
Clause, and leave these few men to retain
the benefit they have got. The Government can make friends or enemies, and
here is an opportunity of making several
very good friends in County Fermanagh
who might, otherwise, be inimical to them.
It would, I admit, be a very generous act
on the part of the Government. None the
less I would like to see these few people
remain, as their county court judge gave
them full-time licences. It would be a
generous thing on the part of the Government to leave out this retrospective
part of the Clause.
Mr. MeDOWELL: In connection with
these nine or ten Fermanagh publicans, I
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McDowell]
think the same thing applies perhaps to
the whole Six Counties. I would like to
see justice done to every man, but in
connection with these short-time houses I
will put it this way.
Two houses are up
for sale. One is a short day and the
other is a long day, or one eight hours and
the other nine hours, whichever way you
like to put it. These men bought these
houses the early-closing ones--at a certain price. Other County Fermanagh
men bought the other houses at a higher
price perhaps, to the tune of anywhere
between £6,000 and £14,000. Is it
right, even in the interests of the trade,
for these men to have the same facilities
for the small price as those who gave the
higher price. I contend that it is not.
I think you will find there are other
houses in the Six Counties in exactly the
same position. These are short licences
and others are long licences,
and they bought accordingly. They
knew what they were doing when they
were buying. Consequently, I think
they have no right to be considered. It
is only restricting them from breaking the
1923 Act deliberately.
Mr. DORMAN: I think there is a great
principle involved in this matter, and a
principle that this House, as a Second
Chamber, is set up to protect. There are
twelve individuals who are apparently interested in this particular clause. There
are the nine individuals engaged in the
sale of intoxicating liquor in a legal and
constitutional manner. There is the
learned County Court judge who has confirmed the application of these nine citizens for an extension of their licence at
a period of transition when the applicants
were passing from the control of previous
Acts under the control of a new Act that
was termed by many persons a temperance
Act. I understand that when these nine
individuals made their application it was
opposed, whether by the police or by the
temperance party in Co. Fermanagh I
do not know. If the applications were
opposed on the part of the police, I believe
they would be acting under the instructions of the Ministry responsible for the
enforcement of that licensing Act.
County Court judge, believing that the
intention of the Government in that
Act was to unify the conditions of licence
holders, extended to these nine individual
licence holders the right of opening for
one hour longer for the sale of liquor.
The
If that was considered by the then Attorney-General as being contrary to the
Act, it was his duty, in my opinion, to inform the Minister of Home Affairs that in
his opinion the decision of the County
Court judge was contrary to the Act of
Parliament, and it was his duty to advise
the responsible Minister to appeal to a
higher court against the decision of the
County Court judge. Whether or not the
then learned Attorney-General had a
doubt in his mind as to the correctness of
the decision of the County Court judge I
do not presume to say. But suppose he
had no doubt, and that the responsible
Minister of the Executive had a doubt, it
was his duty to direct the Attorney-
General to institute an appeal immediately, so as to ascertain what was the
law on the question.
After the County Court Judge, in
accordance with his interpretation of the
law, had given these individuals an extended licence, I am informed that
several of them-I do not know any of
them personally-proceeded to spend
money on their permises on the faith that
the Government would not appeal against
the decision of the County Court Judge,
and in the hope that they would be ultimately reimbursed by the proceeds of
their trade during the extra hour they
were allowed to keep open. So far as I
know there has been no complaint on the
part of the police or the Temperance
party in Fermanagh that these individual
licence holders have violated the law and
have not conducted their premises in a
proper manner.
I do not want to occupy the time of the
Committee unnecessarily, but on the
Second Reading of this Bill the noble Viscount stated that these cases were ruled
by the decision in another case concerning
a lady who made an application for an
extension of her licence for the following
year. I understand that it was against
the decision of the same County Court
Judge that the appeal was made, and in
the higher Court his decision was reversed.
I do not think it is right that we should
allow the Minister of Home Affairs to
clandestinely put into this Bill this retrospective Clause. In confirmation of my
argument may I quote from a celebrated
Dublin lawyer, a well-known gentleman
who was at one time the leader of the
Unionist party of Northern Ireland, Lord
Carson. Speaking in the House of Lords
445 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

8 DECEMBER 1927
Committee
last week he spoke very strongly of the
apparent negligence of the House of Lords
in executing its constitutional duties from
day to day. He made one observation
which is directly applicable to the
principle about which I am speaking.
He said he was utterly opposed to the
growing system of legislation which deprived his Majesty's subjects of the opportunity of going to his Majesty's Courts.
I believe it is our duty to eliminate this
particular Clause from the Bill, and to
say to the Minister of Home Affairs and
the responsible Members of the Government, "We will not assist you to deprive
his Majesty's subjects of the right of ap-
When
pealing to his Majesty's Courts.
these licence holders make an application
for the renewal of their licences, the
proper authority can take steps to prevent them getting the full licence which
they have been granted, but it is not for
the Government, in this Bill, to attempt
to deprive them of a right which has been
conferred upon them by the County Court
Judge.
briefly when he said that if we took the
first part of the paragraph out we should
be giving an undue advantage to certain
people.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The last word of the hon.
Member who has just sat down, and the
remarks of Senator Hill Dickson, are the
real justification for this Clause. What
Senator Glendinning wishes to do by this
Amendment is to regularize something
which is illegal. These nine licensees
were given a full license through a mistake of the County Court Judge. A test
case was taken to the Court of Appeal
and the decision of the County Court
Judge was held to be wrong. Because
these licensees got a full-time licence in
error, when they ought only to have had
an early-closing licence, and because they
have enjoyed this privilege for two
three years, we are now asked to continue
to allow them to have something they
ought never to have had, to the great
disadvantage of other early-closing licen-
I should also
sees in the Six Counties.
I have much pleasure in supporting the Amendment.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: The effect of
this Amendment would be that some
traders would get an advantage over
other traders in the Six Counties through
the mistaken decision of a County Court
Judge. I shall oppose the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am in a difficulty over
this proposal, I am bound to oppose
Senator Glendinning as regards his
Amendment to do away with the first
paragraph, but I hope that with his wellknown forgiving spirit he will support me
when I suggest to the Committee that we
should do away with the second part of
the paragraph. The difficulty is this.
It never was the intention of the 1923
Act that early-closing licences should ever
become full-time licences. I think that
is perfectly plain. It never was intended
under any circumstances that an earlyclosing licence should become a full-time
one. That being so, any such licence
granted since the Act of 1923 was passed
must have been granted owing to some
mistake, and, after all, nobody in the
County of Fermanagh, or outside it, has
any vested interest in the maintenance of
the law in the existing way. The other
question raised in the latter part of the
paragraph is a different one. Senator
Hill Dickson put the matter fairly and
or
point out, that assuming this sub-section
was taken out of the Bill I do not think
it would help these licensees very much
because they could then be forced to proceed by mandamus and in all probability
they would lose their licence altogether.
I really think that in the interest of the
licensees themselves, the hon. Member
would be well advised to withdraw his
Amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: If there is any
danger of the persons concerned losing
the licence that they will enjoy apart
from this Clause, I certainly will withdraw my Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 3, page 4, line 26, leave out
from "licence" to the end of line 31
(and any certificate granted at the
annual licensing sessions held in the
year nineteen hundred and twentyseven, and authorisng the issue of a fulltime licence for such premises, shall
have effect as a certificate for an earlyclosing licence).
Mr. LESLIE: Now we approach the
second half of this paragraph and it seems
to me that although I could not support
Committee
Intoxicating Liquor Bill--

THE SENATE
Mr. Leslie]
the proposal to leave out the first part,
the proposal to leave out the second part
is an entirely different matter. The only
effect of leaving out the second part of the
paragraph would apparently be that these
licence holders in Fermanagh would be
able to carry on their full-time licences
until the expiration of the current licensing year for which it was granted, and
at the end of that year they would have
to revert to their proper position as early
closing licences. The position as regards
the present licensing year seems to me to
be distinctly different. What these people say in the petition which they have
sent to me, and I presume to other Members of the Committee, is:-
It is now contended by the persons
in charge of the proposed Bill that no
legal rights are being taken from us.
This is entirely contrary to the facts.
At the last Annual Licensing Sessions,
each of us, when applying for a renewal
of our licence was objected to on the
grounds that the order made at Enniskillen Quarter Sessions on the 26th
October, 1923, was invalid. The objection was overruled by the Annual
Licensing Court, and the District
Inspector appealed to the Quarter
Sessions. The District Inspector withdrew each of the appeals having ascertained that no authority save Parliament could deprive us of the benefit of
the Judicial Decision under which we
acquired our licence. Accordingly,
the second paragraph of Section
(3), Sub-section 3, has
been put
in the Bill for the sole purpose
of depriving us nine people of our
rights. The deletion of this paragraph
from the Bill will only affect us nine
traders. It cannot have any effect on
the Early-Closing Licence in Belfast or
elsewhere.
The principle at stake is-are the
rights of us nine licensed traders to be
forfeited by an Act of Parliament. We
respectfully submit that it would be
against common justice to single us out
and forfeit our rights acquired under
the Judicial Decision of the County
Court Judge.
I must say that I think their claim is
good. They ought to be left alone until
the end of their licensing year. If the
District Inspector did not choose to carry
on his appeal or take such steps as he
might have taken by case stated it is not
the affair of these nine people in Fermanagh. I am sorry the hon. Senator
from Fermanagh (Mr. Porter-Porter)
who first brought this matter before us
has found it impossible to attend to-day,
but I hope that in his absence the limited
plea I have put forward will be supported
by Senator Glendinning, and I hope the
Committee will consent to do what I
really think is a simple act of justice,
namely, to leave these people alone until
teh expiration of their current licence and
then they must come under the ordinary
law. As it stands at present I think
they are right. Apart from the direct
interference of Parliament the licences
cannot be interfered with. It is not fair
to load the dice against them for this
short time.
Mr. GLENDINNING: It is scarcely
necessary for me to say anything on the
subject. I do heartily support the
Amendment of Senator Leslie as better
than nothing.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): The Government regard
this Clause as of some importance,
because it not only involves the number
stated here, but there are other matters
of an analogous character affected by it.
I cannot follow the hon. Senator who
claimed that grievous injustice would be
done to those people because their wholetime licences would cease to have effect
from the passing of this Act. In fact,
the Section is interwoven with the whole
principle of the Clause, and I do not see
that it can be eliminated from it. Certainly the Government would look with
grave apprehension if there was any departure from the literal terms of the
Clause, whereby the licensee is only entitled to his short-time licence from the
passing of this Act.
and any certificate granted at the
annual licensing sessions held in the
year nineteen hundred and twentyseven, and authorising the issue of a
full-time licence for such premises, shall
have effect as a certificate for an earlyclosing licence.
It was perfectly obvious that a mistake
had been made in granting the full-time
licences, because on appeal it was overturned. Having been overturned, the
licence reverts to an early-closing one.
The Government see no reason whatever
449 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

8 DECEMBER 1927
Committee
why any departure should be made from
the principle. Therefore, I hope the
Committee will support the Government
in carrying out the full effect of Subsection (3) of Clause 3.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I cannot
say that I have been convinced in this
matter. I presume I am right from what
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Finance, says, in saying that the time remaining is very short. He does not give
us dates. They say that at the last
Annual Licensing Sessions their application was objected to, and the objection
was overruled.
Mr. POLLOCK: The extension should
not have been given.
Mr. LESLIE: The objection was overruled. The district-inspector appealed.
The resources of the law were exhausted
against these people. Surely they ought
to remain until the licences expire. They
will have a grievance if it is taken away
from them now.
Mr. POLLOCK: Senator Hill Dickson
really crystallized the whole subject. It
is an injustice to others carrying on their
legitimate business in a legitimate way.
Apart altogether from the argument of
Senator Leslie, I do not see that the
licensee has any ground for complaint.
For these years they have practically been
carrying on illegally a trade they should
not have been carrying on, and because
of the summary discontinuance by the
Bill as before the House, it does not
appear to me they are suffering any
grievance of any kind. I may say there
are other cases not exactly, but almost on
parallel lines with these, and I hope
Senator Leslie will not press anything
that would be an embarrassment to the
Government.
Mr. LESLIE: I am extremely sorry, as
I said before, that the Senator from that
county is not here. I do not feel justified
in his absence in pressing anything that
would be
embarrassment
an
to the
Government.
Mr. POLLOCK: It would be.
Mr. LESLIE: I will ask to be per
mitted to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 6,

line 18 (Sub-clause (6), paragraph IV)
after "fee' ("such fee shall"') to insert
"not exceeding twenty shillings." This
matter is one of fees. I more than once
objected to fees being left so to speak
blank in a Bill to be settled by one of
two Departments. It is usually the
Minister of Home Affairs with the approval of the Minister of Finance.
have always maintained the constitutional
principle that the fees should be settled
by Parliament. It is Parliament's business to deal with a matter of this sort. It
is not the business of the two Departments. I have suggested a fee not exceeding 20 shillings because I think it
would not be likely to be greater than
that. The point at issue is not so much
the amount of the fee as the principle that
the fee ought to be in the Bill, and ought
to be the work of Parliament. You will
find in other passages of the Bill the fee
is definite. The practice is, I am afraid,
rather increasing of leaving the fees
blank. There cannot be any reason why
the House should not be told what the
fee is. The Bill has been on the stocks
two years, and I presume the Ministries
concerned are aware of the amount of the
fee they propose to charge. If not they
ought to be. I am rather glad to have
the opportunity of again asserting my
adherence to the constitutional principle
in the presence of the Minister of
Finance.
Mr. POLLOCK: I do not think you
need worry.
Mr. LESLIE: It is a question of principle, and I beg to move to insert "not
exceeding 20 shillings."
Mr. POLLOCK : I hope Senator
Leslie will not press his Amendment.
There is nothing vital underlying it. It
rather suggests that the Minister of Finance would not exercize the necessary
scrutiny of the fees paid in certain circumstances. It is not contemplated that
the fee will exceed 20 shillings. In fact,
I can give that assurance. But it is
impossible at the present moment
visualize exactly the work that will be
involved with regard to these certificates.
Therefore, there is necessity in some circumstances for elasticity in these matters
where provision has to be made for certain
services. I can give the assurance that
there will be no lack of opportunity to
criticise the fee put in in such circumstances.
to
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: May I take it that the
fee will not exceed 20 shillings.
Mr. POLLOCK: As I said, it is impossible to prophesy what amount of work
will be involved in matters that may arise
under this Clause. At the same time, so
far as can be judged now the fee is not
likely to exceed 20 shillings. It must
not be taken as a definite promise, but I
can give the assurance that it is not
likely.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I beg leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment. by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Provision as to licences
for the sale of wine and sweets), 5 (Provisions as to wholesale dealers), 6 (Provisions as to particulars required in connection with the issue of licences), 7 (Miscellaneous amendments of licensing law)
and 8 (Interpretation of expression
"licence") ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Amendment of provisions.
of the Act of 1923 as to methylated spirits.
and methylated ether).
*
