to
""
say
(6
what I said about forage. Reference to
col. 419 of No. 27 of the Senate Debates
will show, I think, that speaking of
forage I stated that it was entirely
bound up with the question of temporary
stabling. That is what I meant. The
point, of course, is this. I asked the
Government to define, if they could, the
word temporary.' I did not get much
satisfaction then, but the difficulty about
"temporary" was that it left it open to
a policeman to complain that a horse or
vehicle had been stabled for too long a
time. The word "temporary' does
not help us in the least, but if
the two suggestions I had intended
to make had been accepted, I think .
the difficulty would have disappeared.
My idea was that it was not
necessary
about
anything
"temporary" or similar" at all, but
simply leave it that a yard might be open
for the provision of accommodation for
vehicles or animals. Directly you get
over that it would at once be recognized
that a horse might stay there over-night.
If a horse stays there over-night the
farmer, who, as the right hon. Gentleman
says, does very often bring his fodder
with him, will have gone home. What
is going to happen to the horse? Surely
the Minister of Home Affairs does not
want to reproduce the horrors dilated on
by hon. Members on my left; those
horrors that took place on Dry Sunday,
the Sunday succeeding the date of the
coming into operation of the 1923 Act
when the poor animals were left unattended because it was thought that if the
yards were opened it would be alleged
that the public-houses were opened
during prohibited hours.
It appears to me that we have not got
much satisfaction out of these new words
which were put in in order to prevent a
misconstruction of the terms of the Act
of 1923. I am sure the House would be
only too pleased to give the right hon.
Gentleman the opportunity to speak
again, and then, perhaps, he would be
able to tell us what exactly he does mean
by the word "temporary." The whole
Certain
difficulty seems to me to be this.
people seem to think the world will come
to an end if a cold and famished farmer
gets intoxicating liquor to drink at too
early an hour in the morning. That is
a view I do not share. It seems to me to
be much more likely that the unfortunate
farmer will come to an end if he does
not. That is an example of opposite
points of view. I say the licensing laws.
were made for the public, not the public.
for the licensing laws, and that is really
the main point of difference between
those who do not support the whole 1923
Act and the present Bill, which will be
part of it, and those who do. None of us
want to encourage the tippler, but we do
not like the suggestion that a balance has
got to be held between the mere tippler
and the person who wants to say that nobody shall have a drink at all, or at all
events only at the time somebody else
suggests.
Passing from that to the general question of the sale of drink, the right hon.
Gentleman rather accused me of having
moved an Amendment on that subject,
although I did not do so. There was an
Amendment to leave out the two words,
Sunday and Christmas day," and to
add "or during the hours." The effect
of that Amendment, if it had been
carried, and if nothing else had been done,
would have been that the resident in a
hotel could have got his drink at any
time, just as he could in his own house.
It has just been admitted that the effect
of the 1923 Act was to cut off an hour,
because before the passing of that Act
you could get a drink up to 11 p.m., and
now you cannot get one after 10 p.m.
There was a general feeling in my part
of the country-I do not say it was a
universal feeling, but there was a general
feeling of expectation that this Bill,
when it came, would, as somebody had
said, round off the corners of the 1923
Act and would take away some of the
restraint that was regarded as having
gone beyond legitimate interference with
personal liberties. The people who
thought that will, I think, entertain distinct feelings of disappointment.
(6
However, what merit the Bill has--and
I do not for one moment say it has not
merits-I am not wishing to be unappreciative of the alteration as regards the
substantial meal, although I recognize
the difficulty of defining "substantial "
just as I recognize the difficulty of defining temporary." I think one may
consider whether in the future the proper
solution of that difficulty is not to permit
variation in the hours. In order to prevent undue tippling-and I use that
word advisedly it is probably necessary
to limit the hours of public-houses, but I
cannot see any reason why the old licensed hours, the hours at which hotels could
495 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

THE SENATE
Third Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
remain open for supplying drinks, should
vary according to the population of the
town. It seems to me that possibly a
solution may be found by permitting
variations of those hours, more or less by
the general common censent of those concerned. There remains also the question
of the temperance hotel, to which I hope
the right hon. Gentleman will address
himself the disability that a licensed
hotel is under as compared with a temperance hotel.
Leaving those matters which relate to
an Amendment which, as I say, I did not
myself move, to the question of clubs.
I still regret that clubs were not dealt
with as a separate Measure, and I cannot
but feel that the Government, in the provisions they have placed in the Bill as
regards clubs, are asking Parliament to
legislate for the exception and
and not
for the rule. There are seventy registered clubs in Northern Ireland. Those
are the figures the right hon. Gentleman
is reported to have given, and I should
like to know how many of those clubs
there are that can be properly described
as drinking hells.
Sir DAWSON BATES: You will know
after the Bill passes.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I am afraid the
right hon. Gentleman has gone a little
in advance of his knowledge. Seventy
clubs for Six Counties and two County
boroughs is not a large number. However, I did not move that Amendment,
and therefore I will not deal with the
matter further.
The only other point to which the right
hon. Gentleman alluded in relation to
myself, was the question of Article 5 in.
the Second Schedule. He has given very
much the explanation that I am sure must
be the case, because although I cannot
agree yet that Article 5 in the Second
Schedule is in any way self-explanatory,
it could not very well have any other
meaning than what it has been stated to
have. When the right hon. Gentleman
says I suggested it would stop rehearsals
altogether, he is not, if I may say so, as
strictly accurate as I am sure he would
like to be. What I did suggest was that
apparently the wording of the Article
would preclude the tenant of the premises using those premises for any purpose connected with a theatre or music
hall, such as the admission of people
wanting to arrange scenery or to have
rehearsals, and rehearsals, I understand
from those who frequent stage doors and
other places, are held at most unholy
hours. They necessarily have to be held
when the stage is not being used for a
performance. I imagined that the explanation of the Article might be found
in the terms of the licence. I cannot say
that I think the wording of the Article
is very explanatory. On the other hand,
I do not suppose it will occasion any difficulty, the more so as the right hon.
Gentleman has said the intention is not
to interfere with what you may call the
more or less private portion of the theatre.
The intention is to prevent theatres and
music halls being open for performances
except during the hours mentioned in the
licence. That is the intention, and as
long as that information is conveyed to
those interested, I do not imagine there
will be any difficulty.
I do not think there is any other point
on which I should desire to detain the
House. I certainly shall not oppose the
Third Reading, because the Bill has certain merits, and although this House
cannot claim the honour or distinction of
having originated any of those merits,
neither can this House be fairly blamed
for the maintenance in the Bill of any
original sin with which it may have
entered this Chamber I shall certainly
not oppose the Third Reading.
Mr. DORMAN: Since the last meeting
of this House I have heard the Government accused of playing up to the publicans in introducing this Measure. I am
not going to express an opinion whether
that is true or not. I rise to express my
approval of at least two portions of this
Measure. The first is the determination
of the Government not to grant any new
licence where an off or on-licence is
abolished. I believe there are too many
licensed premises in working class areas
in all big centres of population. I believe that they are a class who, suffering
through depression and other things, have
a craving for some stimulant to enable
them to feel something like a normal condition. I whole-heartedly approve of that
action on the part of the Government.
I also wish to express my approval of
the action of the Government in giving
power to district-inspectors of the R.U.C.
in regard to the question of clubs. I be-
497 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

13 DECEMBER 1927
Third Reading
lieve when the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Home Affairs, gets the district-inspectors, who are men of character
and ability and, 1 presume, impartiality,
it will probably be discovered that some
clubs are not generally run for the
economic and social benefit of the members, but for the purpose of enriching
those who started them.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I just want to
say a few words in reply to the remarks
that have fallen from hon. Senators with
regard to the question of stabling. My
hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) objects to the
term "temporary." In this Bill we want
to draw a hard and fast distinction between the ordinary livery stable and
public-house. I absolutely see no reason
why a horse should not get shelter
in a yard overnight, in the event of the
owner being unable to leave the house,
My hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) talks about
interference with the liberty of the subject, and used those words in connection
with the Section which deals with the
restriction of the sale of drink after ten
o'clock to a resident. In dealing with
the liquor laws, I do not believe in dealing
with them or any other laws in the way
of laws for the rich and laws for the poor.
1 could understand my hon. Friend being
logical on this subject, if he had advocated the opening of public-houses on
Sunday. I do not think any hon.
Senator in the House would advocate the
opening of public-houses on Sunday.
Those of us who can afford to live in a
hotel ought, I think, to suffer a certain
amount of inconvenience, having regard
to the good done to the community as a
whole by passing a restriction Act such
as this may be, even though it does interfere with the liberty of the subject.
After all, we have suffered inconvenience
by the Motor Act and other Acts.
Senator Dorman, a short time ago, spoke
about intemperance, but he himself, a
member of a trade union, has not free
liberty. Nobody has.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes.
Colonel SHARMAN
No.
CRAWFORD.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think hon.
Members will agree that it is absolutely
essential, for the good of the community,
that there should be no further extension
in regard to the sale of intoxicating
liquors. Now, with regard to the club
question, I do not know whether my hon.
Friend would like the same conditions
to prevail here in regard to club
licences as prevail in England. In England anybody can get a club licence, and
it is one of the greatest curses and
nuisances the police have in putting an
end to those clubs. They spring up like
mushrooms. In England the magistrates
cannot refuse to give a club licence. As
often as an application is granted the
police raid the house. We want to do
away with that. We want to put the
clubs on a reasonable basis. I really do
not see why a club that gets certain privileges should not submit to certain restrictions. I do not believe any decently
run club will suffer a modicum of harm
by the passing of this Bill. I do not believe that there are many drinking clubs.
I believe they exist to a small extent.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear,
Sir DAWSON BATES: We do not
want to have a lot of clubs springing up
in Ulster that are going to give trouble
to anybody. My hon. Friend knows perfectly well that there is a tendency on
the part of a certain class of clubs to sell
liquor on Sundays to the members, who
retail that liquor at about three times thé
price to individuals who are not members.
I mention that as one form of abuse.
Therefore, it is very necessary that there
should be some control. The Bill applies
to clubs, whether working-men's clubs or
other clubs. I think most of the clubs
that do exist in Northern Ireland are
working-men's clubs. The hon. Senator
referred to temperance hotels. I know
certain very undesirable practices exist,
but law, I suppose, will never make the
world pure. By this Bill we are trying,
at all events, to make the North of Ireland a little more temperate, and we are
rounding off the corners. I hope that the
House will give this Measure the Third
Reading.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read the Third time') agreed to.
Moved That the Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)*
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill without any
Amendment.

Moved That this House do now

...In Committee accordingly.
[Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM

CLAUSE 1 (Transfer of non-contributory old-age pensions service to the Ministry of Labour).
Mr. LONG: Before we bid farewell
to the Ministry of Home Affairs in regard
to the administration of old-age pensions,
I should like to quote some figures relating to the appeals from Londonderry
County during the past year. There were
321 appeals against decisions of the subcommittees in the County of Londonderry.
These appeals, the Committee will understand, were lodged by the pension officers.
The decisions of the sub-committees were
confirmed in 143 cases, and reversed in
178 cases. These figures represent about
45 per cent., but in addition there are
always a few claims ruled by the subcommittee which are not appealed against
by the pension officers. It may fairly be
assumed that the 143 claimants, whose
claims were allowed on appeal, would not
be in receipt of old-age pensions had it
not been for the work of the sub-committees.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Functions of pension.
officers, local pension courts and umpire),
3 (Appointment of umpire), 4 (Constitution of local pension courts), 5 (Administrative expenses), 6 (Adaptation of enactments and interpretation), 7 (Transitory
provisions, and 8 (Short title, citation,.
commencement and repeal), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Sehedules agreed to..

without amendment, to the House.

Adjournment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, That this House do now adjourn
until Wednesday, 21st December.
Mr. LESLIE: I would like to ask the
noble Viscount, the Leader of the House,
the question of which he has been given.
private notice. I am putting the question on behalf of my right hon. and
gallant Friend for County Down (Colonel
Sharman Crawford), who was obliged to
leave the House before the conclusion of
our proceedings. I want to know whether
the noble Viscount can give us any further
information respecting what is generally
501 Adjournment-

13 DECEMBER 1927
The Mails Question.
called the mail question, that is, of
course, the acceleration of the delivery of
letters between Great Britain and Northern Ireland. I do not know whether the
noble Viscount has had time, since he
received the notice, to collect any data
on the subject, but if, as is only too
likely, considering the shortness of
-the notice he has received, he has
not any figures or information to
give us this afternoon, I hope he will
be so kind as to look into the matter
further, with a view to making a statement at the next available opportunity.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I regret the length of the notice I received has
not given me sufficient time to get the
information required, but I shall certainly do my best to obtain it and shall
promulgate it at a suitable time in the
future.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Half after Four o'clock.

Mr.
DISHORNING OF CATTLE.
SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
desire to ask the Leader of the House
whether it is a fact that young cattle imported from the Free State are frequently
dishorned by farmers and dealers in
Northern Ireland without any anesthetic
being used, and whether the Government
will take steps to secure compliance with
the law in such cases. I do not think I
The London-Belfast Mails.
The London-Belfast Mails.
21 DECEMBER 1927
need make any explanation in asking this
question, except to say that I believe it
is only necessary to draw attention to this
matter to have the Government take it
up, and I think it will be looked after
closely. I will just formally ask the
question.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
not aware that young cattle imported
from the Free State are frequently dishorned without the use of an anesthetic
by farmers and dealers in Northern
Ireland.
The Ministry of Agriculture has, since
its inception, advocated by every means
in its power the dishorning, when a few
days old, of such calves as are eventually
to be sold for fattening purposes.
This
propaganda will be continued, and I need
hardly add that if any specific case of
a contravention of the provisions of the
Animals (Anæsthetics) Act, 1919, comes
under notice the law will be put in motion
at once
THE LONDON-BELFAST MAILS.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I have
here a reply to a question asked by
Senator Leslie the last time we met, in
which he asked me to get some information, on behalf of Senator Colonel Sharman Crawford. Here is the reply:
The reversion to the Holyhead-Kingstown route for the conveyance of mails
between London and Belfast in either
direction, has resulted in two distinct
advantages:-
(1) Letters posted in Belfast in time
for inclusion in the mails despatched.
by the mail train to Dublin are included in the first delivery in London
on the day following. No other route
would permit of this connection;
(2) Correspondence passing from
London and district to Belfast posted
too late for despatch by the Fleetwood
route now reaches Belfast at or about
9-10 a.m. with almost unfailing regularity, and is thus included in the
second delivery in Belfast commencing
at 10-45 a.m.
According to information supplied by
the Post Office, deliveries in Belfast are
now much more punctual than when the
Stranraer-Larne route was utilized for
conveyance of London-Belfast correspondence. This improvement has been reflected in an almost entire absence of
complaints regarding incoming London
mails.
In addition to the letters posted in
London early enough to make the connection, a considerable volume of letters
from the North of England destined for
Belfast is carried via Fleetwood. Unfortunately the timekeeping of the Fleetwood steamers has been unusually irregular during the past few months, with the
result that the incoming letters frequently
fail to secure inclusion in the first delivery
in Belfast starting at 7.30 a.m. The
irregular timekeeping is attributable to
the fact that one of the steamers employed on the route is incapable of
making the passage in the scheduled
time. The matter has been called to
the notice of the Postmaster-General,
who in turn has brought it to the attention of the London, Midland and Scottish
Railway Company. The Company, while
unable to effect any immediate improvement in this respect, have directed attention to the fact that new and faster
steamers will be employed on the service
as from the date when the transfer from
Fleetwood to Heysham takes place, and
have intimated that an improvement in
the timekeeping will thereby result. The
Ministry is directing its effort to securing,
if possible, arrival of the new steamers
by not later than 6-15 a.m., so as to ensure of the mails being included in the
first delivery.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am much obliged for the statement made
by the Leader of the House, but I would
like to point out just one or two things
with regard to the Holyhead route.
There is really no advantage in it, because when the mails by the Holyhead
route come in they are detained in the
Post Office in Belfast until the other
mails come in and are delivered with
them. As a matter of fact the mail has
been more irregular in its delivery in
Belfast lately. When the new 21-knot
boats are built the train will probably
leave London an hour later and arrive in
Belfast about 6 o'clock or 6-30. There
is no doubt that the Fleetwood route
always meant the earliest delivery in
Belfast. I suggest and it is only a
suggestion, because I believe the delay
King's Speech.
King's Speech.
THE SENATE
[Colonel Crawford]
is in the Post Office chiefly, here in Belfast that they will consider, when this
new route comes in, sending the outgoing
mail by the Holyhead route and the incoming mail by Fleetwood or Heysham
as the case may be, which would be delivered in Belfast two or three hours
earlier than by the Holyhead route. I
am not asking for a reply now, as I hope
to raise the question again next Session.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
PROROGATION OF PARLIAMENT.
His Grace the Governor, being seated.
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know, "It is his Grace's pleasure that they attend him in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar, with
their Speaker, his Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:
KING'S SPEECH.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I congratulate you upon the result
of your labours during the session now
concluded.
I regret that it cannot be said that
Agriculture has yet emerged from that
period of depression which has for so
long affected conditions in the primary
industry of Northern Ireland. Fortunately the spell of fine weather which
marked the late autumn was of great
benefit to the farmers in enabling the
harvest to be saved. It is to be deplored, however, that owing to the low
prices prevailing in respect of certain
commodities, farmers have not secured
proportionate remuneration for their
laborious work. It is hoped that the
steps taken by the Ministry of Agriculture, particularly in the matter of
granting a further sum of £40,000
towards the extension of the Live Stock
Improvement Schemes, ably administered by the County Committees, will
promote beneficial results.
I am very gratified to observe that the
improvement in trade continues, the
number of persons registered for employment at the Exchanges having been
on the 31st October the lowest since the
Industrial depression which followed the
Great War, and on the 5th December,
26,710, a reduction of 22,697 since the
beginning of this year. Most of the
staple industries have shared in the
better state of trade, and in the linen
industry, in particular, the percentage
of persons registered as unemployed has
fallen from 14.5 per cent. in January,
1927, to 6.8 per cent. My Government
has continued to develop trade and
foster employment through the facilities
afforded by the Loans Guarantee Acts,
loans to the extent of £2,700,000 having
been guaranteed during the year ended
30th September, while the maximum
limit under these Acts has been increased from £9,000,000 to £10,500,000.
In addition since 1st January, 1927,
133 schemes of public utility, giving
employment to a considerable number
of people, have been initiated by Local
Authorities with the assistance of
Government grants amounting
£208,205.
to
I note with satisfaction that measures
of great social value have been passed.
The Acts that have amended and consolidated the law relating to Workmen's
Compensation, that have provided
precautions against the danger of lead
poisoning, and that have safeguarded
those employed in the quarrying industry, mark important steps in the
development of my Government's policy
of improving the industrial conditions
of the people. The Trade Disputes and
Trade Unions Act, while providing
additional safeguards for the liberty of
the individual and the vital interests
of the community, preserves to employers and employed their right to
combine for the furtherance of their
own legitimate interests.
I am glad to learn that preparations
have been completed for bringing into
operation on the 2nd January next the
final portion of the Contributory Pensions Act, under which pensions will be
provided for insured persons and their
wives at the age of 65. It is estimated
that the number of persons who will at
once benefit will be over 8,000. The
passing of the Old Age Pensions Act
also will serve to co-ordinate the administration of the Contributory and Noncontributory Pension Schemes.
The steps which have been taken to
remove the difficulties and anomalies
21 DECEMBER 1927
King's Speech.

End of Session.
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
which have arisen in connection with
the interpretation and administration
of the Intoxicating Liquor Act of 1923,
will prove beneficial.
I am gratified to find that my Government's subsidy arrangements in connection with housing have produced such
satisfactory results that in so far as the
larger types of houses governed by
various statutes are concerned it is now
considered unnecessary to accord further
assistance from public funds. It has
been decided, however, to augment
grants for the purpose of encouraging
the building of the smaller types of
houses more suitable for the requirements of the industrial wage earner,
and I hope that in due time by these
means the housing difficulty will have
been completely removed.
I am also glad to note that a special
grant of £30,000 to assist in the provision of Ex-Servicemen's houses has
been made.
Steps have been taken which will, it
is hoped, tend to develop and safeguard
the supply of Tuberculin Tested Milk,
and I trust that full advantage will be
taken of this Measure and that a pure
and clean milk supply may be ensured
for Northern Ireland.
I note with satisfaction that it has
been possible to afford some relief from
taxation by the exemption from Entertainment duty of certain outdoor
sports, and in the case of other entertainments the remission of duty on the
charge for admission to the cheaper
seats.
Members of the House of Commons.
I thank you for the provision of
supplies for the public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I am gratified to learn that the conditions in Northern Ireland still continue to be peaceful, and that cases of
serious crime are now happily rare.
It is my earnest prayer that the
blessing of the Almighty may rest upon
all your past and coming labours.
Then the Proclamation Proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
was read by the Speaker as followeth :
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament.
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said
Act, that the Governor of Northern
Ireland shall in His Majesty's name
summon, prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
this present Parliament of Northern Ireland should be prorogued to the sixth day
of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-eight:
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, Governor of
Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation
do publish and declare His Majesty's
Pleasure that the said Parliament be,
as from the reading of this Proclamation
in the Parliament of Northern Ireland,
prorogued to Tuesday, the sixth day of
March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-eight.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the said
sixth day of March, One Thousand Nine
Hundred and Twenty-eight, assemble
and be holden for the dispatch of divers
urgent and important affairs, and the
Senators and Members of the House
of Commons of Northern Ireland are
hereby required to give their attendance
accordingly.
Given at Government House, Hillsborough, this twentieth day of December,
One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty-seven.
Signed by his own hand,
ABERCORN
GOD SAVE THE KING.
His Grace, the Governor, then retired.
End of the Third Session (opened 15th
March, 1927) of the Second Parliament
of Northern Ireland, in the Eighteenth
year of the Reign of His Majesty King
George the Fifth

UNIVERSITY of CALIFORNIA
AT
LOS ANGELES
LIBRARY
UC SOUTHERN REGIONAL LIBRARY FACILITY
A 000 175 239

Tuesday, 15th March, 1927.
The Third Session of the Second Parliament of Northern Ireland, appointed to
meet 15th March, 1927, in the Seventeenth
year in the reign of King George V, was
opened by the Governor (His Grace the
Duke of Abercorn, K.P.) in person.
The House met at Five minutes before
Twelve of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER
(Most hon. the Marquess of Dufferin and
Ava, D.S.O., D.L.) in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately
in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, His Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne: ---
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
It is a matter of profound gratification that such a considerable improvement has taken place in regard to
Unemployment. To-day the number
registered at the Employment Exchanges as out of work is 37,125 as
compared with 64,000 a little over a
year ago. This improvement has been
most marked in the Linen Industry,
and has been reflected in the important
shirt and collar manufacturing trade
in Londonderry. A year ago I referred to the financial assistance which
my Government was rendering to the
advertising of Ulster linen in the
United States of America and I am
glad to know that this effort is bearing
fruit.
Further contributory causes
towards the improvement have been the
stabilization of foreign exchanges and a
widespread recognition of the fact that
prices have now fallen to a firm level.
I am also glad to observe that the
Shipbuilding Industry is sharing in the
revival in trade. My Government
is continuing the Loans Guarantee
A
3King's Speech

THE SENATE
Papers Presented
facilities to foster further trade where-
Cover possible.
I
To amend and consolidate the law
relating to Workmen's Compensation.
To extend the powers of Railway Companies with regard to
the owning and running of
omnibuses.
earnestly pray that those engaged
in the various industries of Northern
Ireland will use their utmost endeavours
that the improvement in
to ensure
the volume of employment will be
maintained.
Members of the House of Commons.
Proposals will be laid before you with
the object of according to Road
Authorities further powers in connection with the improvement of the roads
generally and particularly in relation
to widening and rounding-off corners,
with the object of reducing risks of
road accidents to the lowest possible
dimensions.
As it was not found possible to
complete the necessary arrangements
to admit of legislation being introduced
during last Session in regard to the
minor difficulties which have arisen in
the interpretation and administration
of the intoxicating Liquor Act, I
trust that the Bill which is now to be
laid before you will receive your earnest
consideration.
A Bill will be submitted to you to
deal with the evil of the worrying of
sheep and cattle by dogs.
It is also proposed to bring the
existing arrangements for the provision
of funds for the erection of labourers'
cottages by Rural District Councils
more into harmony with the system
which is already in operation in
connection with the provision of capital
funds for Urban housing and Local
Government services generally.
Other Bills will be laid before you
dealing with the following matters:—
To make provision regarding Unemployment Insurance.
To protect persons engaged on
painting work
against
dangers of Lead Poisoning.
the
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
My Government has caused an expert
investigation of the potentialities of
Coastal Harbours to be undertaken
with a view to the development of the
Fishery Industry of Northern Ireland,
and has set up a Departmental Committee to inquire into the marketing of
Agricultural Produce.
I pray that your deliberations may,
under the Divine blessing, result in the
happiness and contentment of the
people of Northern Ireland.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
Papers Presented to Parliament.

appended to Council Order Northern
