Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
NEW MEMBER SWORN..
Mr. James Hamilton, sworn, and took
his seat as Mayor of Derry.
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO
HIS MAJESTY'S MOST GRACIOUS
SPEECH.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
Mr. BARBOUR (in morning dress):
I beg to move: That a humble Address
be presented to his Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
your Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament.
With that touch of human sympathy
and that deep interest in his subjects'
welfare, His Majesty opens his Speech by
calling our attention to the somewhat
brighter business outlook which at long
last seems to be opening up.
The linen industry seems to be beginning to come back to its own, and the
Government are to be congratulated on
the measure of support which it gave to
the advertising campaign which was initiated in the United States, which is the
most important market for our linens.
This, in conjunction with the increased
activities in the shipyards, goes a long
way to dispel the cloud of pessimism which
has for so long been overshadowing our
two main urban industries.
The public generally will welcome the
giving of further facilities to our road
authorities whereby the use of our roads
may be rendered less dangerous.
This may seem to some an unimportant
Measure, but one has only to read the

Press with a little care to realize what a
serious question it is. Local Authorities
up to now may possibly not have done all
that a critical public might think they
should, but while it is easy for anyone to
point out a danger, it is not always easy
to gain the consent of a reluctant owner
for the land necessary to remedy the evil.
Any Measure which will facilitate procedure, without undue hardship
private ownership, will be generally
welcomed.
on
In a piece of major legislation such as
our Intoxicating Liquor Act, it is only
reasonable that with the lapse of time
and experience gained in the working of
the Act some minor anomalies should
come to light and need rectification.
The sheep and cattle raisers of the
country will be glad to know that an
effort is to be made to cope with the
hardship to which they are subjected
in the matter of dogs molesting their
animals.
I am sure that this House will give its
cordial support to any Measure which
will provide the means of facilitating the
economical erection of cottages in rural
areas.
The other Bills mentioned in the Royal
Speech speak for themselves, with the
exception of the proposal to allow railways
to add to the existing number of omnibuses plying on our roads. Being of a
somewhat bucolic mentality I confess I
regard this proposal with some anxiety.
I confess that I had noticed no lack of this
means of transit in any part of Northern
Ireland in which I had been. In fact it
has occurred to me that some of our roads
were rather overworked in this respect,
and that the lives of His Majesty's loyal
subjects in some localities might be likely
to last longer if the number of omnibuses
could be reduced rather than increased.
But I suppose we must regard this proposal more through the eyes of the coming generations than with the dimming
vision, which is all that is left to some
of us. Undoubtedly this means of transport-whatever its handicaps may behas brought great blessings in its train.
It has enabled many people to live in
healthy surroundings in rural, or semirural districts while carrying on their
life work in our urban centres-thus
tending to relieve congestion. Again,
there is no doubt that we are vitally
15 MARCH 1927
Debate on Address
Debate on Address

-


e
e

g
me
ans-
De
rain.
en
heir
in
in
expressing the sentiments of this House
and of the whole community.
(Hon.
Senators: Hear, hear.)
interested in the maintenance of our
railways, and if this Measure can give
them a new lease of life it is well worthy
of support. Whatever the future may
have in store for us at the moment we
are not in a position to do without our
railways, and if by any reasonable
Measure of this kind we can lighten the
difficulties with which they have to contend I feel sure it will receive sympathetic
consideration.
Though late in the day, perhaps, I
feel sure that this House would like to
record its intense satisfaction with the
high honour which it has pleased His
Majesty to confer on our Prime Minister.
With His Majesty's pleasure goes all the
goodwill of the people of Northern Ire-
Iand. The task he set out to do has been
done well, despite difficulties which might
well have daunted a less intrepid leader.
Lord Craigavon has created a place for
himself in history of which he may well
be proud. His recent tour of Northern
Ireland, embracing as it did our rural
as well as urban communities, has proved
a source of intense satisfaction to those
who are not privileged to live within the
precincts of Belfast, and his statement
that he intends to give special attention
to the needs of our rural population will
revive the hopes for better things in the
hearts of our farmers.
And here may I venture to offer a suggestion that in considering all the problems
which agriculture presents for solution,
he may give special attention to the question of assisting organised self help, or,
in other words, agricultural co-operation.
Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South
Africa all compete with us in the British
markets, and in all these Dominions a
liberal measure of support is forthcoming
for the agricultural co-operative movements. I beg to move.
Mr. GLENDINNING (in morning
dress): I appreciate being called upon to
second the Motion so eloquently proposed
by the hon. Senator (Mr. Milne Barbour)
as a signal honour not only to myself but
to the city and district with which I am
by residence associated. I most heartily
join with the mover in his congratulations
to the Prime Minister on the distinguished
honour conferred upon him by His
Majesty the King; a great honour gained
by great services to his country and the
Kingdom, and in this I feel that I am
I should also like to add my tribute of
commendation to the Prime Minister on
the wise and sagacious step taken by him
and the Home Secretary in their recent
progress throughout the Province, and in
having what he appropriately termed a
heart-to-heart talk with the representatives of every responsible authority and
of every interest in the country. I think
it is a splendid thing that the heads of the
administration should tour the highways
and by-ways of Northern Ireland and
meet personally those who have grievances,
real or imaginary.
This facilitates the friendly discussion
of problems which, unexplained, leave
misapprehension, criticism, and even distrust in the minds of the people, and
which may be dissipated and removed by
a short conversation. His promise, made
at Derry, that the tour would be an
annual affair, was received with enthusiasm, and though we are well aware of
the many pressing calls made upon the
time and attention of the Prime Minister,
still I think the head of the Government
could not do a better or more solidly useful work than to engage in these conferences throughout the Northern
After all, we are a comparatively small
community, and
close and intimate
confidence between the Government and
the people established in this way is a
striking step in the right direction.
a
area.
Before referring to the Speech from the
Throne, may I congratulate the Government on the peaceful state of the country
during the interval since we last met, and
also on the happy prospect before it. Any
controversies which have manifested themselves have been confined to the columns
of the public Press. I shall only refer to
one or two of them, and very briefly.
The first is the suggestion of an all-red
route for the mails. In that connection,
while I sympathise with the idea, I sincerely hope that no definite step will be
taken which would jeopardise the existing
service via Kingstown and Holyhead, so
convenient for business people in the
southern and western areas, until
equally good alternative service for these
districts is an accomplished fact.
an
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Glendinning]
My second reference is to the dissatisfaction of Secondary School teachers with
the present provision for superannuation.
I would appeal to the Government to
grant such increase in the pension scale
as would place them more on a par with
the teachers in England, and remove what
I regard as their most reasonable claim on
the administration.
I cannot speak concerning agriculture
with the authority of Senator Barbour,
but I think the entire community will
welcome the decision of the Government
to appoint a Committee to inquire into
prices, transport, and the marketing of
agricultural produce. This is another
illustration of the anxiety of the Government to advance the interests of the leading industry of the Province.
With regard to the King's Speech, it is
certainly encouraging to note the great
diminution in the number of the unemployed. Everyone must long for the time
when our industries will have absorbed
those who are really willing to work, but
for whom there is no work. The evils of
the existing state of affairs are too well
known and too rightly deplored to need
any comment from me.
Reference has been made to the shirt
and collar manufacturing trade in
Londonderry. Our new colleague, the
Mayor of Derry, will be a better exponent
of that than I, but I myself have visited
some of the manufacturers, and they tell
me what I am sure will be pleasant information; that although many of the
advantages which Derry once possessed in
competition with other centres have disappeared, notwithstanding this the conditions during the strike, and at the
present time were and are much better
than might have been expected, and it is
hoped that with better times in Great
Britain those healthy conditions may be
prolonged if not improved upon.
The revival in the shipbuilding trade
must prove of great benefit to the City
of Belfast. Would that there were any
prospect of the Derry yard resuming, but
it is at least hopeful that the company
and plant are still in existence and ready
to start should circumstances make it
possible.
I was interested to read in the Press
of the discovery of a new material woven
from flax and artificial silk which is said
to promise well for the weaving industry
of Northern Ireland, and which may still
further diminish the number now seeking
employment.
As regards the forecast of legislation,
compared with the previous Speech from
the Throne, we are all, I believe, gratified
to learn that the programme is of a
practical kind, and designed to further
the best interests of the Province.
The
Prime Minister of what is henceforth
to be termed the Parliament and Government of the United Kingdom of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland, in defending the modest programme submitted at
the beginning of a new Session, said it
was thought well not to attempt too much,
but rather to submit a programme which
it was possible to carry into effect.
other words, it was considered better to
set before Parliament a programme with
which it could comfortably deal rather
than one so over-loaded that most of it
had in the end to be jettisoned, while that
which was attempted was because of the
rush and crush, often crude and ill
digested.
In
I am glad that the Measures outlined
in the Speech from the Throne are of such
a number and nature that Parliament
will be given sufficient matter to engage
its best energies and yet permit of mature
consideration. I beg to second.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish in the first
place to express my appreciation of the
very able manner in which the Address
to His Grace the Governor has been moved
and seconded. I am sure that the reduction in the number of unemployed is,
as the Speech from the Throne states, a
matter of profound satisfaction to us all.
I recognise that the Government of
Northern Ireland has probably had to
face a more gigantic problem in this respect than has had to be faced in any of
the States constituting the great British
Empire or the Commonwealth of Nations,
inasmuch as our industry was in pre-war
times largely for a world market, and,
therefore, an old-established Government
would have had a very great advantage
in meeting the post-war difficulties that
arose in consequence of the dislocation of
the markets of the world which were
largely the customers of this particular
area of Northern Ireland.
I think the figures are still lamentable
Debate on Address
Debate on Address

15 MARCH 1927
from the point of view of the loss that
we are sustaining in potential wealth by
the enforced unemployment of men and
women who are prepared to enter industry and produce something in return
for that which they are consuming.
a
I
think it would be
low estimate to
calculate that probably over £5,000,000
will be lost in production to the State
during next year if the present number
of unemployed are necessarily kept in enforced unemployment during that particular period. The effects upon many of
the young men who are in the early stage
of their journeymenship is certainly
disastrous. Large numbers of them who
have completed their time as skilled
artisans have been unemployed for one,
two, or three years, and while unemployed
they will be losing the adaptability they
have acquired.
We are also, I am sure, gratified that
the Government is taking steps to safeguard the public and motorists from the
dangers to which they are exposed by
corners in the various roads which obscure
vehicles at an angle in the roads on which
motorists are travelling from day to day.
Any legislation to reduce the number of
accidents due to that cause will be
welcome.
I do not like to have to strike a note
of anything like discord. I would
personally wish to be able to give my full
support to the Address, but circumstances
exist in our midst to-day of such
character that I feel I must necessarily
voice an expression of regret in the
following Amendment, which I move to
be added to the Address: -
a
but regret that in the Gracious Speech
from the Throne no mention has been
made of any intention of the Government to amend the Rent Restriction
Act so as to provide adequate protection
for the tenants of the Six Counties.
This is a very pressing question indeed
so far as the most helpless part of the
community is concerned. Since the prewar period there has been an increase of
approximately 700 per cent. of one-room
dwellers in the City of Belfast. When
first looked at these figures do not seem
to convey very much, but when one
examines them it is found that the
number of one-room dwellers, which include from three to six persons per room,
has increased to an enormous extent in
comparison with the general increase in
one-room dwellers. The number of rooms
in which are resident three, four, five,
and six persons has increased by from 700
to 1,200 per cent. compared with the prewar period, the result of that being that
owing to the congestion the bread-winners
suffer more from sickness and the children
suffer from overcrowding.
The large increase in the death rate
amongst the citizens of Belfast is due to
the ever increasing number of the workingclass population who are forced to resort
to one-room dwellings. It may surprise
some of the Members of the Senate when
I tell them that the one-room dwellers
to-day in Belfast are actually paying more
rent for their single apartments than
would have enabled them in pre-war times
to have maintained a residence with four,
five, six, seven and eight apartments. There
are numerous portions of the city in which
one-room dwellers are paying from 8s to
15s per week for a single apartment in
which to house their families from week
to week.
Everyone who has studied the vital
statistics of any community knows that
the death rate amongst one-room dwellers
is
more than double the death rate
amongst those who are more fortunately
circumstanced. I therefore wish to express the hope that the Government will
see its way to amend the Rent Restriction
Act so as to protect the tenant from the
evils that prevail to-day. If some step is
not taken to protect the tenants in this
matter the result will inevitably be that
the conditions will become more intense,
the sickness amongst these one-room
dwellers will be increased, the death
rate will be increased, and charges
will be heaped upon the local Boards that
would be obviated if proper housing
accommodation was within the reach of
the people.
Mr. LONG: I should like to briefly
express the pleasure with which I notice
that the Government has decided to legislate in three particular directions, amongst
others. The first is that proposals will be
laid before Parliament with the object of
according to road authorities further
powers in connection with the improvement of the roads, and I should like to
emphasise what Senator Barbour has said
-that local authorities may have been
remiss in this matter in the past, but
they were very often brought up against
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
reluctant owners who did not wish to part
with their land, and certainly not at a
fair price. The local authority with
which I am connected endeavoured to
secure the widening of a corner in a
country place and they were asked to pay
by the owner of the land at the rate of
£860 an acre. Any simplification of procedure for acquiring land at a fair price
by a local authority would, I feel sure,
be welcomed, and would be useful and
exceedingly desirable.
Another matter which I note the
Government are to deal with is the worrying of sheep and cattle by dogs. I have
no idea what the proposals of the Government are, but it is a matter in which
legislation is long overdue. In the
County of Londonderry the County
Council has made a Regulation that dogs
must be under control from sunset to
sunrise, but for some reason or other that
regulation seems to be ignored, and very
few dogs are under control during those
hours. The fault is not altogether that
of the Constabulary, because it is very
difficult to detect a dog in a country place
on a dark night, and also discover who
the owner is. However, I hope and trust
that the Government have found a way
to deal effectively with this pressing
matter.
The other matter on which I am pleased
to note the Government are going to bring
in legislation is in regard to the provision
of funds for the erection of labourers'
cottages.
The Rural District Councils
are in a difficulty with regard to the provision of cottages. They find it is almost
impossible to provide cottages under the
present Regulations at a rent which the
agricultural labourer can pay, and
although cottages are very much needed
in many any districts the Rural District
Councils are reluctant to put on the rates
the sum that would be necessary if these
cottages were erected.
I should like to join the mover and
seconder of the Address in expressing the
pleasure with which local authorities
received the visit of the Prime Minister
and the Minister of Home Affairs in the
course of their recent tour. It has often
been a wonder and a surprise to me that
persons can be found to discharge the
onerous and responsible duties of the
various authorities engaged in local
government work.
Without fee or reward, and often exposed to unfriendly
criticism, they do their very best for the
interests committed to their charge, and
they regard it as a great compliment that
the Prime Minister should come and consult with them, and they certainly will
consider it an incentive to persevere in
their good work. I should like in conclusion, if I may, to compliment the
mover and seconder of the Address
on the very admirable manner in which
they have discharged their important
function.
Mr. LESLIE: The whole House, I am
sure, has listened with great pleasure to
the terms in which the Reply to the
Gracious Speech was moved and seconded.
We all share in the satisfaction which is
natural on such occasions when the
speeches are so eloquent and instructive,
and, if I may say so, I feel an additional
touch of personal satisfaction in hearing
the speech of an old colleague on the
County Council. The mover of the
Address has sat for very many years on
the Antrim County Council, and on that
body it has often been my privilege to
listen to cogent arguments of his expressed with the same facility and diction
as that with which he has favoured us
this afternoon.
It is only natural that in moving the
Address in Reply the hon. Senator should
have dwelt lovingly, perhaps, on what has
long been a favourite child of his own—
the question of agriculture and its organisation on co-operative lines. That is an
important subject to which the hon.
Senator who gave us the advantage of his
views just now has devoted many years,
and it is a matter to which he has given
unremitting attention, and I believe the
result of his efforts has been not unsatisfactory to himself.
The speech of the seconder, if I may
say so, was also very pleasant to listen to.
I have not had the advantage of serving
on a public body with the hon. Senator
from Derry, but I think I may say that
all of us have a very warm feeling in our
hearts for Derry and all those connected
with it, and association with the hon.
Senator does nothing to chill the warmth
of our feelings.
Passing now to the Amendment moved
by the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman).
before he actually approached the question
of Rent Restriction he drew attention,
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
15 MARCH 1927
naturally enough, to the fall in unemployment which is disclosed in the Gracious
Speech. It is a matter indeed for profound
gratification that, although unemployment does press heavily upon us still, the
percentage of unemployed amongst us
appears to be now only three per cent.
This time last year it was almost double
that figure. I think that percentage is
approximately correct. We are all looking forward to a still further reduction,
and we hope that in time there may be
more funds available for meeting the
expenses of habitation, whether by rent
or otherwise.
I
The hon. Senator who moved the
Amendment to the Address gave us a
harrowing picture of the number of
people in Belfast who are condemned to
pass their family life in single-roomed
houses, and he suggested, if I understood
him correctly, that something could be
done to mitigate the evil by the amendment of the Rent Restriction Acts.
am afraid that I do not quite follow the
chain of reasoning by which he arrived
at that conclusion. It seems to me that
if you want to prevent whole families
living in one-roomed houses the first requirement is that there should be an
adequate supply of houses of more than
one room for them to resort to. It does
not seem to me that any alteration in the
rents of existing houses has any bearing
on the question, unless it can be shown
that there is a substantial number of unoccupied houses at present in the area
under discussion. The hon. Senator did
not give us any figures pointing to the
fact that houses in Belfast generally were
unoccupied, and I think that we should
require definite and reliable information
on that point before we start off in chase
of what I am afraid I must regard as a
will-o'-the-wisp. But I am not, of
course, to be understood as in any way
decrying the evil of insufficient housing
accommodation, nor the evil of overcrowding. It is an evil which is not
entirely of modern days. It has been
dealt with by reformers for certainly the
better part of a century.
Now, passing to the actual Gracious
Speech itself, and the Measures the production of which it forecasts, to some
extent I think it will be found that the
majority of the Measures mentioned will
be based on legislation recently introduced in England. For instance there is
the proposal to legislate in the matter of
lead poisoning. A Measure for the
protection of our workers against the illeffects of dangerous trades has occupied
the attention of Parliament across the
There are
water for many years past.
several Measures dealing with that. We
have ourselves already followed to some
extent in the footsteps of those who have
been working in that way in England,
and I am sure we will be quite ready
to adopt any well-thought-out proposal
for following on the same lines. That
matter of dangerous trades is one of
those on which it is absolutely imperative
we should keep strictly on all fours lest
we should allow Northern Ireland to become an experimenting ground for proceedings forbidden across the water and
which might be permissible here.
The
some
But
I observe that something is to be done
in the way of amending and consolidating
the law of Workmen's Compensation. I
am sure we shall welcome that Measure.
It is, if I may say so, due to us.
House, I think, will share my recollection
that it is now some three years since it
was necessary for us to pass a Measure,
with a celerity which occasioned
remark at the time-not criticism.
when some three years ago we were confronted with a Workmen's Compensation
Act, it was clearly understood that the
Bill was not to remain for any length of
time on the Statute Book, but that a consolidating Measure should be introduced
at the earliest practicable moment. That
moment it seems has now very nearly
arrived, and I am sure we shall be prepared to deal with a Measure that is
likely to be somewhat lengthy and possibly of some complexity, though not in
any way controversial. We shall be prepared to give that Measure the consideration which it will demand.
As regards dogs, I am sure that our
farmers and all those interested in
sheep raising and with sheep may be
included other forms of animal lifewill welcome the assurance that that
matter is to be dealt with. Those of
us who live in the country, and who
endeavour to keep sheep, and particularly
those of us who endeavour to breed sheep,
are aware from bitter personal experience
of the heavy losses which are occasioned
by the ravages of dogs. These dogs do
not, unhappily, limit their operations to
night time. They worry sheep just as
much sometimes in the day as at night.
I am afraid that nothing no further
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
restrictions, as regards licence duty or as
regards collars with name and address
attached will be of any use. I am afraid
that what we require is something that
will practically ensure, so far as we can
ensure, the destruction of dogs wandering
at will about the country where there are
sheep and other attractive forms of animal
life to damage, and will require that
those dogs should practically be treated
as wild beasts and be destroyed as such.
Nothing less than that, I am afraid,
would meet the evil which has grown
to enormous size. Nothing less than
that would protect our strictly
domestic animals, such as sheep from
sufferings which
it
is impossible
to contemplate with
calm mind.
One has seen sheep so worried and torn
as to be unable to move. One has seen
sheep with legs practically raw while the
sheep were alive, and it has been found
necessary to bestow on the sheep an end
to its life which might have been more
happily bestowed on the particular dog
which was the author of the evil. The
evil has grown to immense proportions,
and we are gratified to hear that it is
about to be dealt with.
a
Taking the Speech as a whole I am sure
we shall be happy-all of us, on reconsideration-to join in the Address of this
House, and that we shall feel that the
opening of our work of this Session has
been auspiciously begun.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
should like to join with the other speakers
in offering my congratulations to the
mover and seconder of the reply to the
Speech from the Throne. I am very
pleased to see reported a considerable reduction of unemployment in our counties.
There is no part of the House I think
which will be better pleased with that
state of affairs than the working class
representatives here. It must be admitted
that that reduction has been brought
about to a considerable extent by the
action of our Government. They have
done a considerable amount to reduce unemployment particularly by the use of
the Loans Guarantee Acts and other
means, and we are much indebted to the
Government for the action they have taken.
I am very pleased to see that they are
intending to further amend the Unemployment Insurance Acts. It is a complicated matter and requires attention.
I think every one of us sympathises to
a considerable extent with those called
upon to pay exorbitant rents, particularly
those in business properties. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) There are undoubtedly many cases where landlords or
owners are imposing considerably on the
tenants of property in business parts of
the city. We are also sympathetic with
those called upon to pay high rents for
dwelling houses. After all, this is really
caused by the shortage of houses, and to
complain of one-roomed dwellers having
to pay such exorbitant rents-I might
draw my hon. Friend's attention to this
fact is only an exposure of the greed
amongst our own class which causes this.
It is, unfortunately, the workman who
has a room to let who is more greedy
towards the poor creature who desires
shelter than the landlord who has other
house property to let. While I am very
sympathetic to those called upon to pay
these exorbitant prices I cannot see that
the Government can do very much in
that respect. It is impossible to pass
legislation to deal with every matter.
Surely we expect to require something like
honourable conduct from the working
people who own a house and have rooms
to let as well as from the landlords. I am
sorry I cannot see my way to vote for the
Amendment, although my sympathy goes
out to poor people called upon to pay
exorbitant rents.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would just wish to
associate myself with the previous
speakers in congratulating the mover and
seconder of the Address.
I think they
have put the different points very clearly
before us so that each one of us can
understand what is meant.
Regarding
the Speech from the Throne, I may say
with Senator Cunningham that I am
pleased to see the great reduction in un
employment. We are to-day living in a
different atmosphere from what we were
in this day twelve months. Although
there was a little bit of movement in the
right direction at that time, there was
hanging over our heads a dark cloud in
the coal industry. That cloud has burst
and passed away, and there was not so
much damage done after all. But today the sky is clearer than it was twelve
months ago, and so we are here at the
beginning of this Session with brighter
hopes regarding the future. I am sure
that if all sections of society do their
share, in twelve months from now we
shall find things a great deal better than
they are even at the present time.
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
15 MARCH 1927
We
some-
There is something like 25,000 men and
women, boys and girls, still unemployed
and, as I have said, if all sections of the
community do their part we will within
twelve months from now have these
people employed in useful employment
producing goods for the benefit of the
whole community. To bring this about
there are four things which appear to me
to be essential to put into operation. The
first is we would have to be willing to
make great sacrifices. Capital would
have to sacrifice something; Labour
would have to sacrifice something.
would have to be willing to sacrifice
something, and by sacrificing
thing, it would show that we desire
the good of others and the benefit
of our country. Then the second thing
would be unity-unity of purpose of all
sections of the community working together for one object. And the third would
be co-operation--all co-operating together
for the same object, and the fourth would
be a spirit of patriotism. That is, we
should put our own country first, our own
people first, our own industry first, ourselves first, and then if we have anything
to spare afterwards we may apply it to
our neighbour and foreigner across the
water. There is a spirit, at the present
time, in the country of condemning ourselves, condemning our industry, condemning our own Governments, both the
Imperial Government and our own Ulster
Government, and upholding others. Well,
that does not pay. It does not increase
the wealth of our country, and it does not
find employment for our working classes.
It is all right if coming for a kind of
entertainment now and again, but when
it becomes a perpetual thing, it becomes
injurious to the industry of the country,
and it is the poor who have got to suffer.
I was reading sometime ago in one of
the week-end papers where a writer there
was speaking on somewhat similar lines.
He said, "We have had enough of talk
in the past. What we want to-day is
something a-doing, something done." He
put it this way: He said we have had
enough of this highly explosive verbal gas
which suggests nothing and does nothing,
and only creates in the minds of workingclass people hatred, envy, and ill-feeling.
We
I agree with that gentleman.
want to have peace, and we want to see
more unity between Capital and Labour.
I may say that we want to get more cooperation between Capital and Labour, and
we want to realize that the interest of
one lies in the other; that the one cannot
exist without the other, and that by both
co-operating prosperity and happiness
shall come to all. If you can only get
all sections of society to lay hold upon the
cable of industry, and pull with a long pull
and a strong pull and a pull altogether,
we shall be able to bring back to our
country those markets and those industries that have been lost to us owing to
the strike and other causes, and our
country will be a great deal better off
than it is to-day.
There is another matter in the
Gracious Speech that I should like to
draw attention to, and none of the
previous speakers have mentioned it.
That is the proposal to extend the powers
of railway companies with regard to the
owning and running of omnibuses.
While I benefit to a certain extent by
buses, I should be sorry to see the railway companies suffering in any way.
They have served the community well in
the past and they will serve the community well in the future, and the reason
I am inclined to sympathise with the
railway companies is this. They supply
a tremendous amount of employment, and
if anything was to happen to the railway
companies look at the large number of all
classes of men who would be thrown on
the streets unemployed. Therefore I
think it should be the duty of every good
citizen
encourage the railway
companies, who are large employers of
labour, large ratepayers, and large taxpayers.
to
Buses are very useful. If I am
travelling to the country I can get a return ticket from Belfast to Magherafelt
or Ballymoney almost one hundred per
cent. cheaper than a railway ticket, but
by going by bus I am only employing two
men, and these men are in a great many
cases paid a very small wage. When I
travel by rail I am employing a large
number of men, and although I benefit by
travelling by bus I do not want to be selfish. I want to sacrifice something for
the benefit of my fellowmen. I have
great pleasure in congratulating the mover
and seconder of the Address upon their
speeches, and I hope that before this
Session is over some very useful legislation will be passed for the benefit of the
whole community.
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: In
rising to speak to this Motion I would
like first of all to congratulate the mover
of the Address on the very full and interesting speech he gave us, and might
I say how pleased we all were to see his
brother, the Minister of Commerce, sitting beside him on that auspicious occasion. I would also like to say how much
I appreciate the very able speech of Senator Glendinning in seconding the Address
in Reply.
or
In looking through the Gracious
Speech, there is one point I would like
to draw attention to. The Government
refer to the financial assistance they rendered to the linen trade in advertising
linen, and they rightly take credit for it.
I think we all know that if advertising is
not followed up the money spent is
not of much service. If we go to
London or to America to buy linen
goods we do not know whether
not we are getting linen. We may get
cotton goods made locally and finished
with a limen finish. This may be bought
by a lady, and when she gets it home she
may discover it is only cotton that she
could have bought as cotton. This gives
a bad name to the linen trade, and it
would be of great benefit if the Government would follow up what they have
already done by adopting some system of
inspection with the object of ensuring
that material which is labelled as linen in
London or anywhere else is really linen,
and that the buyer is getting honest value
for his or her money, and that he is getting flax when he is paying for flax.
If the Government took some such step
as I suggest we should have our tables
covered with white linen tablecloths
sooner than by any other means. I am sorry
the Minister of Commerce is not present
to tell us whether anything can be done
in this connection, but I wish to draw the
attention of the Government to the matter. In conclusion, I think the speech
from the Throne is short and to the point,
and we have every reason to hope that the
Measures forecasted in it will be of great
benefit to the community. I therefore
most heartily support the Motion.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I should like to be permitted, at the outset of my speech, to
make a brief reference to the high honour
which His Gracious Majesty the King was
pleased to bestow upon our Prime Minister
at the beginning of the year, mention of
which has already been made by the hon.
Senators who have moved and seconded
the Humble Address. I believe that
every hon. Member of this House, no
matter what his political creed and convictions may be, will join with us in
congratulations to the man who, through
stress and strain, has laboured unceasingly to bring peace and tranquillity to
Northern Ireland.
When His Majesty came in person,
almost six years ago, to open our First
Parliament, he expressed himself as
،،
confident that the important matters
entrusted to the control and guidance of
the Northern Parliament would be
managed with wisdom and with moderation, with fairness and due regard to
every faith and interest and with no
abatement of patriotic devotion to the
Empire." The signal distinction which
he has now seen fit to confer upon the
Head of our Government may be accepted,
I think, as a clear indication that that
confidence was well founded, and that to
our Prime Minister, above all, is due the
greatest praise for the sane and successful administration of the affairs of
Northern Ireland. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.)
I am only echoing the feelings of every
Member of this House when I offer my
congratulations to the mover and
seconder of the Humble Address upon
the felicitous manner in which they delivered their speeches. The hon. Senator
who moved the Address bears a name
which is known, I am sure, to every
citizen of Northern Ireland and which is
recognized by them as connoting public
service in the highest and truest sense,
love of one's fellow-men, and zeal for the
well-being of the State. As you are all
aware, Mr. Barbour has been for the past
twenty years an untiring worker in the
cause of agriculture, seeking especially
to promote amongst the farmers of this
country those methods of co-operation
which are admitted to be essential to the
success of our chief industry.
For almost an equal length of time he
has been prominently identified with the
local government of his own county, and
more recently he has added to the
obligations which the State already owes
him by assuming the Chairmanship of the
Education Committee, which is engaged
in his own part of County Antrim in
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
15 MARCH 1927
carrying out the duties imposed upon
local authorities by the Education Act of
1923. But it is unnecessary for me to
recount at length the various claims
which the hon. Senator has upon the
gratitude of Northern Ireland. We in
this House welcome him, not only for his
good works, but for his charm of personality, and we shall be forgiven, I trust,
if we express a regret that the responsibilities which he has lately undertaken in
a distant part of our Empire deprive us
oftener than we would wish of his counsel
and assistance in this Chamber.
The hon. Senator who seconded the
Motion is also very well known throughout Northern Ireland, and particularly
SO in his own city of Londonderry.
where he has always stood as the embodiment of those loyalist principles which we
in this part of Ireland are proud to
cherish so fervently. A constant worker
for the welfare of his own native city,
he has not been unmindful of the duty
which every good citizen must owe to the
State as a whole, and the Members of
this House have always listened with
pleasure and benefit to the contributions
which he makes from time to time to our
debates.
The legislative programme which is
outlined in the Gracious Speech from the
Throne is not as ambitious or as varied
in aspect as that which was presented to
us a year ago, but, nevertheless, Bills
will be introduced which are of great importance. You will be asked to consider,
amongst others, a Bill under which local
road authorities will be accorded increased powers in regard to the improvement and the widening of public roads.
Danger to the travelling public arises
from two main causes. Under the first
head come either defects in the vehicle,
defects in the driver, or carelessness and
negligence; while under the second head
may be grouped such factors as narrow
roads, blind corners, obstructions, etc.
Steps have already been taken by Parliament to remove or remedy the first class
of factors, and it is now considered, as a
natural corollary, that every possible
effort should be taken to remove any defects in our road system which might be
conducive to the danger of the person or
property.
The improvement of roads has been
well maintained during the year. Grants
have been made from the Road Fund in
aid of expenditure by local authorities
amounting to over £50,000, an amount
which is nearly four times the grant paid
at the time of the transfer of services to
Northern Ireland.
An important step in connection with
the improvement of road traffic conditions was taken by our Parliament last
year when it passed the Motor Vehicles
Act. This Act, introduced, as hon.
Senators are aware, the principle of
differentiation in regard to motoring
offences by specifying substantial penalties for those offences which are conducive
to the danger of those using the roads and
by applying a more modified scale of
penalties for simple technical offences.
of
The most important element in the Act,
however, is contained in Part II, which
establishes for the first time in Northern
Ireland-or, indeed, in any part of the
United Kingdom-the principle
central control and licensing of public
service vehicles. This portion of the Act
will probably come into full operation in
the course of a few months.
The Gracious Speech referred to a Bill
which will be submitted to you and which
deals with the evil of the worrying of
cattle and sheep by dogs. Those of you
whose homes, like mine and Senator
Leslie's, are in the country, must often
have encountered instances where substantial loss has been caused to farmers
by reason of dogs worrying their stock,
and as Senator Leslie has said, that
worrying is not necessarily done by night.
I myself, it might be interesting to say,
lost four sheep in about a quarter of an
hour one afternoon between two and
three. It has often been felt that existing legislation on the subject was to some
extent inadequate. The problem is an
extremely difficult one, but it is hoped
that a remedy may be found in the Bill
which will be laid before you during the
Session.
Hon. Senators will, I am sure, share to
the full the gratification expressed in the
Gracious Speech at the improvement
which has taken place in regard to
unemployment. Almost exactly a year
ago, when I made my first speech
leader of this House, I referred to the unhappy fact that more than 61,500 persons
were at that time unemployed.
as
That
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
figure has now been decreased by over
24,000, and we shall all pray that the
corner has at last been turned and that
an era of prosperity is beginning to dawn
for the industries of Northern Ireland.
Every practicable opportunity
of
fostering industry and creating additional
employment has been taken by the
Government, in conjunction with our
local authorities, who have continued to
bring forward relief schemes. Since the
prorogation of Parliament in November
last twenty-six schemes in different parts
of the Province have been approved, the
Government's contribution in respect of
the wages expended on unemployed men
specially engaged on these schemes being
estimated at more than £28,000.
You
will be interested to learn that the total
number of relief schemes approved since
they were first initiated in May, 1922, is
783, while the estimated grant from the
Government has been £837,000. There
are at present over 1,400 men actually at
work on schemes of this nature.
The Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Scheme is gradually coming into fuller operation, the
number of widows benefiting having increased from 3,350 about a year ago to
4,283, while the figure for the dependant
children in receipt of allowances is now
9,890, as against 8,300. 1,123 orphans
are also in receipt of weekly payments.
In July of last year there came into
operation that part of the scheme which
enables Contributory Old-Age Pensions to
be paid to persons over 70 years of age on
an insurance qualification, and 4,495 pensions of this kind are now being paid,
bringing the total number of beneficiaries
under the new Act to 19,791 persons.
I may, perhaps mention here that there
has been an increase in the number of
firms enrolled on the King's National Roll
of Honour, and that there are 637 more
disabled ex-Servicemen employed to-day
than a year ago. In other words, the
number of such men unemployed has
fallen by about one-third. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
The present position in the linen industry gives reasonable grounds for optimism as to the future. The difficulties
which were caused by the coal strike in
Great Britain last year have been overcome and a feeling of greater confidence
now
obtains. Reports from overseas
markets indicate that the demand for
linen goods is increasing, and you will be
glad to know that several local weaving
factories, which had been closed for a considerable time, have been reopened. The
unemployment figures for the industry are
at present lower than they have been at
any time during the past two years, and
while it is true that the export statistics
do not yet fully reflect the improvement
which has actually taken place, yet it may
be confidently anticipated that the values
of exports will increase during the next
few months. The linen publicity campaign which was recently launched in the
United States of America has already
proved productive of result, and sales of
damask goods are steadily increasing.
It is pleasing to be able to record that
the prospects for the shipbuilding industry
have also improved. The local firms have
secured substantial orders for both liner
and cargo vessels, which will give muchneeded employment to a sorely-tried
industry.
Recent launches from the
Belfast shipyards have included some of
the finest motor-vessels in the world, and
it is very gratifying to find that we are
in the van in respect of the most modern
methods of shipbuilding.
I referred last year to the scheme of
loans which was brought into operation in
1925 by the Ministry of Agriculture,
whereby farmers, more particularly in the
poorer districts, were enabled to secure
money at a low rate of interest for the
purpose of replacing live stock which had
been lost in previous abnormal seasons.
The Government are aware that the repayment, at the present juncture, of the
first instalment of these loans would, in
certain exceptional cases, be a matter of
difficulty and hardship and it has been
decided that, so far as special cases are
concerned, an extension shall be granted
to the end of June of this year. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). I may mention
that similar loans will be available again
this year and those farmers who
desirous of obtaining the facilities which
they offer would be well advised to make
application to the Ministry of Agriculture
as soon as possible and, in any case, not
later than the end of next month.
are
Hon. Senators may, perhaps, remember
that a few years ago the Commission on
the Natural and Industrial Resources of
Northern Ireland, of which I had the
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
15 MARCH 1927
honour to be the Chairman, made an exhaustive investigation of the possibilities
of increasing the afforested areas of this
country. Certain recommendations were
submitted to the Government and I am
glad to be able to report that important
steps have now been taken in this direction. Arrangements have been completed
for the acquisition by the State of additional lands for forestry purposes, for the
supply to farmers, at cost price, of forest
plants grown in the nurseries of the
Ministry of Agriculture, and for the
making of grants to land-owners towards
the cost of preparing ground and planting
trees. A satisfactory feature of these extended forestry operations, and one which
will commend itself to this House, is that
they will provide an opportunity for
giving work to a certain amount of unskilled labour.
The Gracious Speech makes reference to
the setting up of a Departmental Committee to inquire into the Marketing of
Agricultural Produce. The Committee
will investigate the cost of transit of farm
produce and the reasons for the difference
which exists between the prices obtained
by producers and those paid by customers,
while the methods of marketing agricultural produce generally will be closely
scrutinised. No impartial and fairminded critic of the Government can
deny, I think, that every possible effort
is being made to place the agricultural
industry in a position to hold its own
against the increasingly severe competition which is being encountered at the
present day.
The administration of services in connection with jurisdiction, law and order
presented no unusual feature during the
You will have
year.
seen from the
Judges' Addresses to the various Grand
Juries at the Assizes that the state of the
country is, on the whole, very good and
compares favourably with previous years.
The alterations in the law relating to
juries, embodied in the Jury Laws
Amendment Act (Northern Ireland),
1926, and the Supreme Court of Judicature of Northern Ireland Act, 1926,
have come into operation generally and
are proving effective in equalising the
burden of jury services in Northern
Ireland.
Before I conclude I should like if I may
to say a few words about my own department, the Ministry of Education.
The hon. Senator who seconded the
Motion to the Address referred to the
recent agitation which has arisen in the
Press with regard to the salaries and
pensions of Secondary teachers. I can
assure the hon. Senator and all the other
Members of this House that the matters
of which complaint has been made have
been most carefully investigated by myself, and I am satisfied that, in all the circumstances, the Secondary Teachers of
Northern Ireland have been treated by
the State not only with justice but also
with generosity. I shall be happy if this
House desires it to give some more detailed statement on the whole subject at
a later period.
re-
The year which has passed has been one
of normal progress, and the Education
Act is steadily being put into operation
throughout Northern Ireland. A great
deal of what may be called spade-work
has been done by the local education
authorities, the ground has been
viewed, and the task which lies before
them is now
clearly appreciated.
Although this preliminary work must
from its very nature be comparatively
unobtrusive and to the onlooker unproductive of result, it is, nevertheless of
the greatest importance, for
foundation is being laid upon which will
be built the educational system of the
future--a system which I am confident
will prove to be worthy of the high hopes
which have been lavished upon it.
a
sure
It
I now come to the question of the
Amendment to the Address which has
been moved by Senator Dorman.
is difficult for me to say what kind of
legislation could be proposed by the
Government to remedy the evil-the undoubted evil which he mentions. It
would be difficult to imagine legislation
which would not interfere unduly with
the great majority of good landlords
in its endeavour to penalise the few
bad ones. The whole solution of the
subject lies in the provision of a greater
number of houses. That has been alluded
to by two of the hon. Senators who
spoke. I think I can say that the
Government has done everything in its
power to allay the shortage. I have
some figures here, but I would prefer at the
present moment to speak merely on general
lines. I really think the Government
has done everything it can to remedy the
