Lead Paint (Protection against Poisoning) Bill [S.]
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I beg to move, That this
Bill be now read a Second time. It is on
similar lines to an Act which was passed
by the Imperial Parliament last year, and
which came into operation in Great
Britain on the 1st of January of this year.
Lead poisoning amongst persons engaged in the manufacture and use of lead
paints is due principally to the use of
white lead which, on account of its marked
durability, is the principal lead compound
used in paints. A painful and distressing
disease, known as plumbism may be incurred, the results very often being fatal
to the unfortunate victims. The incidence of this form of poisoning has
occupied attention for many years,
and, with the permission of the House, I
shall give a short summary of the
negotiations and conferences which took
place in the past and which led up to the
passing of the Lead Paint Act by the
Imperial Parliament in 1926.
In 1911 the British Government
appointed two Departmental Committees
to investigate the danger attendant on
the use of lead paints in the painting of
buildings and vehicles. These Committees
satisfied themselves that lead poisoning
occurred to a serious extent in these two
trades, and that the main channel of lead
absorption was by the inhalation of dust
when surfaces which had previously been
painted with lead paint were being
rubbed down with dry sand-paper to produce the smoothness necessary for the
application of a fresh coat of paint.
These two Committees were of opinion
that efficient and non-poisonous substitutes
for white lead were available and that
their compulsory use would not lead to
financial loss, and they, therefore, recommended that the use of lead compounds
Lead Paint Bill-
Second Reading
24 MARCH 1927
and paints should be almost completely
prohibited. It should be borne in mind,
however, when considering the Reports
of these Committees, that at the time they
made their investigations dry rubbingdown was the only practicable method
in use, as waterproof sand-paper had not
been invented, and, furthermore, it had
not been ascertained definitely whether
the non-poisonous substitutes, which were
paints with a zinc base, would be as satisfactory under exposed weather conditions
as the lead paint which it was sought to
prohibit.
During the war, white lead available
for painting was reduced to a negligible
quantity, and paint manufacturers were
compelled to use a zinc base for paints to
a much greater extent than formerly. The
experience which was thus gained showed
that the zinc substitutes, when exposed to
humidity or hard wear, were much inferior to lead, unless they were protected
by enamel or varnish, thereby adding
materially to the cost of the work.
We come now to the First International
Labour Conference which was held at
Washington in 1919. At that Conference
a recommendation was passed which led
to the Women and Young Persons
(Employment in Lead Processes) Act of
1920, whereby regulations were imposed
in regard to the employment of women
and young persons under eighteen in any
process involving the use of lead compound if the process were such that dust
were produced from the compound, or
the persons employed were liable to be
splashed by it.
Two years later, in April, 1921, the
International Labour Conference, which
was held at Geneva, went a stage further
and considered the question of the prohibition of the use of white lead in painting.
Towards the end of the year, a draft Convention was agreed to prohibiting,
amongst other things, the use of white
lead and sulphate of lead in the internal
painting of buildings. It should be
noted that
Committee of Experts,
which had been set up to advise the
Conference was in favour, not of the
prohibition of lead paints, but of the
framing of Regulations as regards their
use, and the Chairman of this Committee
has stated that on the last day of the
Conference, when matters were rather
hurried, the Committee's recommendaa
tion was rejected, and the Convention
adopted upon arguments which were not
strictly scientific. I may add that this
draft Convention has never been adopted
by any part of the British Empire.
In the meantime, the Imperial Government had set up in the same year, 1921.
a Committee known as the Norman Committee, which was asked to re-examine
the ground explored by the two Departmental Committees in 1911 in the light of
the developments which had subsequently
taken place. The Norman Committee
examined a great number of witnesses,
who agreed generally that lead poisoning
among painters was due almost entirely
to the inhalation of lead dust, and that
this danger might be obviated by the use
of waterproof sand-paper and wet rubbing. As regards the durability of zinc
paints compared with lead paints, a series
of practical tests was made which proved
conclusively that the lead paint was
greatly superior.
This Committee therefore reported that,
as there was no efficient substitute for
lead paint for outside painting and for
certain kinds of interior painting, and as
the danger arising from the inhalation
of dust could be obviated by the use of
waterproof sand-paper, it was of opinion
that the use of lead paint need not be
entirely prohibited for the painting of
buildings.
The next stage was the introduction,
in 1924, of a Bill in the Imperial Parliament, which was designed to give effect
to the Geneva Convention and to prohibit
the use of lead paint. The Measure,
however, was so strongly opposed that it
was withdrawn, and before the Government of the day was able to decide on
a new line of action a General Election
took place, which resulted in the return
of Mr. Baldwin's Ministry. In March
of last year that Government introduced
a Bill which provided for the regulation
of the use of lead paint, instead of prohibition, which the former Bill had
sought to secure. It received the Royal
Assent in December last, and came into
operation for Great Britain on the first
day of this year. During the passage
of the Bill in the Imperial Parliament
it was made quite clear that the Measure
was, to some extent, experimental, and
that if it were found that a system of
regulation was powerless to reduce the
number of workers smitten with the
Lead Paint Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
disease of plumbism, then the Government would not hesitate to bring in a
Bill for prohibition.
We
come now to the position of
Northern Ireland with regard to this
difficult question; and I do not think
the Members of this House will require
an apology from me for having detained
them so long with a recital of the events
which led up to the Imperial Act of
last year. Our Government, that is the
Government of Northern Ireland, has
been moved by two main considerationsfirst, that every reasonable and possible
precaution should be taken to safeguard
workers in our painting industry from
falling victims to this terrible disease,
and, secondly, that the industry should
not be placed under any greater restriction or disadvantage than is absolutely
necessary to secure such safeguards.
is believed that the Bill now before the
It
House achieves these two objects.
It
asks that the Ministry of Labour be given
power to make Regulations for prohibiting, in connection with the painting of
buildings, the use of any lead compound
except in the form of paste or paint
ready for use; for the prevention of
danger arising from the application of
lead paint in the form of spray; for
prohibiting dry rubbing-down and scraping; for providing for the periodical
medical inspection of workers; for securing to the workers facilities for washing;
for the use of protective clothing; and
for the distribution of instructions regarding hygienic precautions.
The
If the Bill becomes law, every person
who employs other persons in painting
buildings, whether he uses lead paints in
his work or not, will be required to send
to the Ministry of Labour a written notice
stating his name and address, and he will
be obliged to keep at his office a register,
in the prescribed form, of the persons
employed by him in painting, and the
work on which they are employed.
Bill also provides for the notification by
employers of cases of lead poisoning
occurring to their employees, and gives
factory inspectors the same powers of
entry and inspection in any case where
persons are employed in painting buildings as they already have under the
Factory and Workshop Acts, in respect
of factories and workshops.
It is estimated that the Bill will bring
about 1,200 persons, comprising painters,
paper-hangers, and decorators within the
scope of the factory code for the first
time. It is, I think, a Bill which is
unlikely to provoke any controversial
criticism, for it is designed towards the
protection of our workers. I beg to
move.
Colonel SINCLAIR: After the admirable résumé of the object of this Bill
which has been furnished by the noble
Viscount it will not be necessary for me
to say very much about it. The Bill
endeavours to deal with an important
aspect of the whole question of lead
poisoning. For many years this has
been before not only the public but the
medical profession, and suggestions have
been made that it is high time that something was done. Members of the Senate
may not be aware that the mortality
of plumbism or lead poisoning is as high
as 22 per cent., and there has been in
the United Kingdom a matter of 500
cases a year of the more severe type,
not to mention persons who suffer from
the milder type of the disease and who
attend the extern department of the
hospitals.
I understand from the noble Viscount
that this is an experimental Bill, and that
if it is not sufficient for the purpose of
protecting the workers engaged in this
particular trade it will be followed by a
prohibitive Measure at some later date.
That is an uncertain period, and in the
case of a Bill such as this which may
not be, and in my judgment is not,
thorough enough to meet the case, there
is an inclination to let things drift for
a number of years and not follow what
I deem to be the better course of prohibiting the use of lead paint.
When we scan the Bill and see the
Regulations it is proposed the Ministry
should make, they seem to be fairly
adequate, but I cannot see how the inspection is to be carried out in anything
approaching a satisfactory manner. There
are 70,000 house painters in the United
Kingdom, and the noble Viscount has
told us that there are 1,200 in this part
of the world for which we legislate. They
are employed in small groups of two,
three, four, five, seven, and so on, in
houses scattered in towns all over the
Province, and how a satisfactory system
of inspection is to be carried out under
such conditions I fail to see. A whole
army of inspectors would be necessary to
Lead Paint Bill-
24 MARCH 1927
Second Reading
see that the Regulations were observed
by house painters scattered in this fractional manner all over the Province.
I think it is undesirable to multiply
officials unduly, and therefore I cannot
get away from the feeling that the
inspection will not be very thorough.
Add to that the consideration that
workmen, even intelligent workmen, are
very careless of themselves. Propaganda
can be carried on as to the advantages
likely to accrue to them by observing
certain precautions such as the wearing
of overalls, washing, and the use of suitable moist sand-paper. These things may
be attended to for a time, but presently
they will be ignored, and how the Regulations will be enforced I cannot see.
should have preferred total prohibition
instead of these proposed Regulations.
It would probably result in a diminution
of house painting and there would perhaps
be an initial shrinkage of this work, but
by and by when a non-poisonous substitute has been discovered and provided
no doubt the amount of house painting
work would return to normal.
I
It is not my intention to divide the
Senate on a matter of this kind, because,
after all, the Bill is a step in the right
direction, and I think it would be unwise
to divide the House on it. But I cannot
allow the opportunity to pass without
expressing my firm conviction that the
Regulations will be quite inadequate and
ineffective, and that before long the
Government will have to bring in a new
Measure. I feel that, notwithstanding
these adverse criticisms, I should be entitled to vote for the Bill on the principle
that it is a step in the right direction,
but a very feeble step, and one which I
hope will ultimately be followed by a
more thorough Measure that will reduce
the incidence of this terrible disease and
the heavy mortality it entails.
The disease is one of insidious onset,
and it does not therefore attract so much
public attention as a more acute disease
with its tragic results. Nevertheless, it
is there, and in the more mild cases,
which are not so easily recognised, if
medical advice is not sought the amount
of misery and discomfort occasioned is
much greater than the statistics supplied
to the Home Office would indicate, as
these merely deal with the severe cases
and the mortality that takes place.
Mr. LESLIE: I have listened with
very great interest, both to the noble
Viscount and the hon. and gallant
Senator who represents the University
(Colonel Sinclair), and I feel that my
ignorance on the subject of lead paint
generally has been somewhat lessened by
what I have heard. But I have not
heard so far this afternoon anything to
explain why the danger from lead poisoning appears to be confined to painting
in buildings, and does not apparently
apply to the use of white lead and its
compounds on ships, where you would
imagine there would be a great deal of
painting. I know that the regulation of
ships was left out of the English Measure,
and, I presume, deliberately, and I presume it is with equal deliberation that
reference to ships is omitted from the Bill
before us. On the face of things it does
not seem quite clear why.
Taking the Bill as it is, I shall certainly most heartily support it, and I
think I may say that if the Bill does
not succeed in checking the evils to
which the hon. and gallant Member
for the University alluded, I should be
equally prepared to support a Measure
which went further and adopted absolute
prohibition. All I shall have to say at
any stage of this Bill is to press the
Senate to accept at a later stage a slight
Amendment which would ensure that the
precautions which we give to young
persons is extended to children. It might
be that children-that is, people under
fourteen years of age-might possibly be
employed, and there is just a curious
little conflict of definition in two
of the Statutes comprised in the
code of which this Bill will become
part which would make it desirable
that we should say definitely that
everybody up to the age of eighteen
is to be protected under the four corners
of the Measure which I am sure we are
about to pass.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should
like to say in answer to the last speaker,
who referred to the omission of ships
from the Bill, that shipbuilding and repairing yards are factories and workshops
subject to the provisions of the Factory
and Workshops Act, and they already
have certain precautionary regulations
applied to them with regard to the use of
lead paint. That is the reason for the
omission of ships from the present Bill.
Of course, the danger of inhalation of
Lead Paint Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
lead is greater inside a building than it
is outside.
With regard to the alleged difficulties
of inspection, I do not quite agree with
the hon. and gallant Member for the
University (Col. Sinclair). The Trade
Unions which are connected with this
trade are very well organized, and I think
they can be trusted to look after their
own members very efficiently. So far as
the employers are concerned they are
liable to certain penalties, and there is
always hanging over them the possibility
of having their trade interfered with if
they do not do everything in their power
to observe the provisions of the Act. In
this connection I should like to emphasize
that both in England and here the
Measure is largely experimental, and if
it is found that the incidence of plumbism
continues to the same extent as it did
before the Bill was passed, the Imperial
Government have said they will bring in
a prohibitive Measure, and no doubt we
should follow suit.
VISCOUNT BANGOR: Senator Leslie, in his speech, remarked that this Bill
did not apply to ships. When I looked
through the Bill for the first time I
noticed in paragraph 1, Sub-section (c)
that there is a prohibition of dry rubbingdown and scraping. I happened to be in
a ship-chandler's shop this morning and
made inquiries, and he produced some of
this special sand-paper for use in connection with water. He told me that he
was going to get in a large stock. I
asked him how he would manage when
they were going to take white enamel
paint off ships or yachts and wanted to
scrape it down, and he produced some of
this waterproof sand-paper and gave it to
me. Perhaps the Bill is not so far-reaching as it might be, but I shall certainly
support the Second Reading.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I was
very much interested in the statement
made by the noble Viscount, and more
especially in the information we received
from the hon. and gallant Member for
the University. I have had a good deal
of experience in lead paint work, and I
am very pleased to see the Government
exhibit such an interest in the welfare of
the workers as even to bring forward a
Bill of this description, although it may
not be so far-reaching as some Members
desire. One thing that has to be taken

into account is the real ignorance of the
workers with reference to the seriously
poisonous nature of this material that
they are continually called upon to work
with. I do not know that there will be
any diminution in the number of cases of
plumbism until some steps are taken to
bring very forcibly before young people
and older tradesmen the serious consequences that may arise from allowing this
poison to penetrate into their nails.
Year after year I have been working
with lead for jointing purposes on ships.
We use white lead to a considerable extent
for steam joints, and red lead is used to
a great extent also. The hands are used
for working with this material, and it is a
very serious matter indeed for workmen,
and particularly for boys, who do not
know the poisonous nature of the lead,
to have to work it with their hands and
have no means of properly cleansing their
hands and nails and getting it removed
until they return home. It must be
apparent that a considerable number of
cases of plumbism that occur could be
prevented if proper information about
the disease, and the precautions necessary
to prevent it were posted in the works
where the material is used.
I am pleased to see that the Government are introducing this Bill, and I
should be glad to know that they were
following it up by posting notices giving
information about the disease in every
place where it is used. I am satisfied it
would remove a great deal of the danger
to which workers are at present exposed,
because boys who are called upon to work
with this lead would appreciate its danger.
Mr. BARCLAY: I am very pleased
that a Bill such as this has been brought
before the House. I think it will be of
great benefit to those who are employed in
this particular trade. I am, however,
sorry to find that it does not extend to
shipbuilding. In the shipbuilding industry there is from day to day a certain
amount of carelessness, especially amongst
the workpeople themselves. We have twc
classes of painters in the shipyards. One
class is known as leaders and the other as
painters, the latter being more of the
artistic class. Amongst red leaders there
is a tremendous amount of indifference
and carelessness in painting. They are
on piece work and are paid by the square
foot or square yard, and when those men
get to work and the brushes begin to
move the paint goes on very rapidly.
Lead Paint Bill-
Second Reading
24 MARCH 1927
Sometimes there is as much on the deck
and on my clothes and the clothes of other
workmen as there is on the place where it
is wanted, owing to so many bolts and
other things being in the way of the brush.
I see there is a provision in the Bill
for a place to be provided for the clothes
of the workmen and for overalls to be
provided for them while they are working, and I think it would be a good thing
if those Regulations were applied to the
shipyards, and not alone to the man who
is using the brush but to those who are
working beside him, such as the joiner
and the mechanical engineer.
I come
home many a night and find the back of
my coat is covered with paint. If it is
injurious to the painter working in a
building it must be injurious to the man
working in the shipyard, and why should
he not be protected in the same way as
the man working in a building? However, the Bill is a step in the right direction, and perhaps later on we may have
its benefits extended to the men working
in the shipyards. I have great pleasure
in supporting the Measure.
Mr. DORMAN: I also wish to express my approval of the Bill, and I
would like to emphasise the importance
of the Government including in the Bill,
if possible, some compulsory method of
posting instructions to young people
cautioning them of the dangers of lead
paint poisoning and advising them as to
the means of safe-guarding themselves
against the possibility of contracting this
disease. I suggest that advisedly, because I have been interested in another
industry in which I know, from observation and study, that apprentices are
subject to the incipient stages of the
disease, which is not merely injurious to
themselves but is afterwards transmitted
to others and affects the physical wellbeing of future generations. I think that
if a compulsory notification was placed in
all workshops where lead paid is used it
would prevent the possibility of the
disease spreading to the extent it does
at present.
The other trade to which I refer is
plumbing. I believe that before the
Royal Commission that sat just before
the outbreak of the war-the International Commission on the subject
of the disease of syphilis-it
stated there were several firms in Great
was
Britain which had compelled their workpeople to subject themselves to a periodical medical examination because of the
fact that apprentices in using tools that
had been used by adults had contracted
the disease, which had injuriously affected their future lives. Therefore, I
trust that some steps will be taken to
have compulsory notifications, such as I
have suggested, posted in workshops. I
have noticed that it is generally young
people who are engaged in the operations
of scraping and dry sand-papering,
which, as the hon. and gallant Member
for the University has pointed out, is
one of the ways of contracting this
disease of plumbism. I heartily approve
not only this Bill but any Measure designed for the protection of the working
classes.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I,
like the other hon. Members who have
spoken, approve of this Bill, which seems
to be necessary for the preservation of the
health of our race. There is one point 1
would like to have some information on,
and it is in regard to the inspection of
buildings. If, for instance, an inspector
suspects that there is painting going on
in my house can he come in and inspect
it? If that is so, I think some protection
should be afforded to the private householder.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to ask
the noble Viscount if it is not a fact
that Section 3 of the Bill, which notifies
that certain Sections of the Factory and
Workshops Act, 1901, shall apply, does
provide that the Regulations shall be
posted up on the premises where these
trades are carried on, and thus provides
for young persons, and in fact all persons
connected with the trade,
certain
amount of information as to the dangers
to which they are exposed.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: One or two interesting matters have been raised by hon. Members
who have spoken in this debate. Senator
Joseph Cunningham raised the question of
the posting of notices. I should like to
inform the House that power to do this
is given under the Regulations. With
reference to the question brought forward
by Senator Barclay, as the Leader of the
House told us, shipbuilding comes under
the Factory and Workshops Act, and
certain precautionary measures are set
out in certain Sections of that Act which
Lead Paint Bill-
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
provide for the prevention by mechanical
processes of inhalation or dust, washing
accommodation, prevention of the taking
of meals in rooms where poisonous materials are used. Furthermore, if in any
branch of the trade these precautions are
found to be inadequate-|
Mr. MCDOWELL: If it is true what
Senator Barclay has said that these
precautions are not taken in the shipyards
-what position do we find ourselves in
in relation to this Bill?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The fact is that under the
provisions of the Factory and Workshops
Act these precautions can be taken. I
am not saying they are taken, but power
is given to take these precautions.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday.
Cookstown Electricity Special Order,
VISCOUNT
1926.
MASSEREENE
FERRARD: I beg to move-
AND
That the Cookstown Electricity Special
Order, 1926, made by the Electricity
Commissioners for Northern Ireland
under the Electricity (Supply) Acts, 1882
to 1919, and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce under the Electricity (Supply)
Act, 1919, in respect of the Urban District of Cookstown, in the County of
Tyrone, be hereby approved.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Before
formally moving the Adjournment of the
House, I desire to say that I should like
to get the Agricultural Research Station
Bill through all its stages by Tuesday
evening next, as it is rather important
to finish it before the end of the financial
year. I have the choice of suspending
Standing Orders on Tuesday to enable the
Bill through all its stages by Tuesday
stages on that day, or the House could
sit on Monday and take the Second
Reading on that day, and the remaining
stages on Tuesday. It is a small Bill
consisting of four Clauses, one of which is
a financial Clause, but I am entirely in
the hands of the House.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to know if the Bill will be
in the hands of Members before Tuesday.
Could it be sent to them?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: As the Bill is one of extreme brevity, which has been very much
before the public during the past week,
I should think we should not find any
difficulty in dealing with it on Tuesday
in all its stages.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Then
I move, That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly
at Four o'clock until
Tuesday next.
Secrecy of the Ballot.

29 MARCH 1927
THE SENATE
re
he

Tuesday, 29th March, 1927.
e
d
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the
Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Finance.
Return of Civil Servants for the year
1925-26 retained in the Service after
attaining the age of 65.
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Ministry of Finance.
Form of Declaration made by the
Ministry under Sections 7 (1) and 9 (1)
of the Teachers' Superannuation Act
(Northern Ireland), 1926.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Antrim Electricity Supply Bill.
The following report was made from
the Joint Standing Orders Committee, to
whom was referred the examiners' certificate relating to the Petition for a Bill by
the promoters of the North Ulster Electricity Supply Bill, viz.:-
That the Committee had met and had
resolved, That the Standing Orders not
complied with in respect of the said Petition ought to be dispensed with, and
leave given to introduce the Bill.
Business of the House.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I beg to move,
That Standing Order No. XXV be dispensed with for the purpose of passing
the Agricultural Research Station Bill
and the Consolidated Fund Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I do not rise to
oppose the Motion made by the noble Viscount, but for the purpose of expressing
my disapproval of the conduct of the
Government in regard to the apparently
scant courtesy they are extending to this
House by asking us to consider and pass
through all their stages Bills such as those
which are brought before us to-day. It
is true that we have no right to amend
the Consolidated Fund Bill. We have
only power to accept or reject it, but I
think that when legislation is presented
to this House for consideration which it
has only the right to accept or reject, the
Government ought to give due opportunities to the Members of the House to give
the greatest possible consideration to the
Measures. I hope the Government will
bear this in mind in their future relations
with this House,
VISCOUNT
CHARLEMONT:
I
might say that I am in sympathy with the
hon. Member who has spoken, but with
regard to the Agricultural Research
Station Bill and the Consolidated Fund
Bill it is important that these Measures
should be passed before the end of the
financial year. The former Bill is a relatively small one on which I do not think
much discussion is possible.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Questions to Minister.
SECRECY OF THE BALLOT.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Dorman :
To ask the Leader of the House
whether, in view of the practise that
has become common in Belfast at Parliamentary and Local Government
elections of allowing persons who are
not appointed by the Returning Officer,
are not officers of the Law, and are not
sworn to secrecy to enter the polling
booths with the voters, the Government
will take steps to ensure that the secrecy
of the Ballot is preserved, by excluding
from the booths during the polling
hours all unauthorized and unsworn
persons.
Secrecy of the Ballot.
Secrecy of the Ballot. 52
who saw the man attempting to enter the
ballot box with the woman, caught him
by the shoulder and pulled him away.
The point, however, is that the man was
not expelled from the booth although he
had interfered with the secrecy of the
ballot.
THE SENATE
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to draw the
attention of the Leader of the House to
the question I have put down on the
Order Paper. It has been my fortunate,
or unfortunate lot, to have contested elections in various parts of the country both
for Parliamentary and Local Government
bodies, and I have been struck by the
custom that has been allowed to grow up
at times of Parliamentary, Municipal,
and Poor Law elections in the City of
Belfast, of persons, as I have stated in
the question, other than persons appointed
by the Returning Officer, persons who are
not officers of the law, and who are not
sworn to secrecy, entering the polling
booths. On many occasions these unauthorized persons have opportunities of
ascertaining how various electors cast
their votes, thus rendering the Ballot Act
inoperative so far as these elections are
concerned. It is well known that the
Ballot Act was placed on the Statute
Book to protect the elector from being
victimized in his means of living by others
discovering through the Ballot box his
political faith.
In justification for my putting down
this question, I would like to relate an
incident that took place at the last election. I have myself witnessed such incidents as this, having been a candidate in
several elections and having participated
in other elections as an official. At one
of the booths at which I was acting as a
tally clerk for a candidate at the last
election I happened to get out of the
tally room to observe what was going on
in the booth. I followed one man, who
was accompanying a woman, for about
thirty yards along a pathway, and he was
warning the woman to be very careful.
The man had in the lapel of his coat a
Party emblem, but I had no Party emblem in my coat.
The man entered the booth with the
lady voter and I attempted to follow
them, but immediately a representative
of the opposing candidate, who was wearing the colours of that candidate in the
lapel of his coat, peremptorily ordered me
out of the booth. I understood that I
had no legal right to be in the booth and
I immediately retired into the porch and
watched what took place. The man went
to the polling clerk with the woman, and
he also had the audacity to pass from the
polling clerk to the ballot box. To his
credit, I may say, the constable on duty
I watched several incidents of that kind
taking place. I then wrote a letter to
the presiding officer telling him I knew
I had been ordered out of the booth, that
I was aware I had no legal right to enter
it, that I had seen persons who had not
been sworn to secrecy and who were
not representatives of the Returning
Officer enter the booth, and that unless
the practice was immediately discontinued
I would utilise the first opportunity I had
in this House to draw the attention of
the Government and people to this particular practice that was calculated to make
the Ballot Act inoperative so far as the
preservation of the secrecy of the ballot
was concerned. I am pleased to say that
from 12-30 until 8 p.m. on that day the
practice was not continued.
I know from personal observation that
the practice was pursued in other parts
during that election. I anticipate that
the reply of the Government will be that
the law at present on the Statute Book
is ample to protect the voters. I grant
that, but inside that law there has grown
up in Belfast a custom that does not prevail in any part of Great Britain or any
other part of Ireland, the custom of
permitting unsworn and unauthorized
persons wearing distinct colours indicating
the party to which they belong, to accompany crippled, infirm, blind, and especially illiterate voters, and to stand beside
them while the presiding officer puts to
them the question as to whom they wish
to vote for.
The purpose of the Ballot Act is thereby
rendered abortive, and such persons have
no right to be present when a voter is
telling the presiding officer which candidate he or she wishes to vote for. I hope
the result of this question will be that
the Government will draw the attention
of the various returning officers to this
objectionable and illegal practice, or,
failing that, that the returning officers
will see that the matter has been referred
to in this House, and that in the future
they will take steps to prevent a continuance of the custom.
Secrecy of the Ballot.
Secrecy of the Ballot.
29 MARCH 1927
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Before he replies to this question, I would
like to ask the Leader of the House if
this violation of the Ballot Act is in any
way confined to one particular Party?
My observation of the voting at the
different elections has proved to me that
the objectionable practice to which Senator Dorman has referred is practiced by
the hon. Member's own Party to a greater
extent than by any other Party.
Mr. DORMAN: So far as I am concerned I wish to give an unqualified contradiction to that statement. During
the course of one election three young
ladies came to my tally room and offered
their services for personation purposes,
and I was really shocked.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon. Member is not going to make a second speech.
Mr. DORMAN: I am not, but I want
to quote an instance to disprove the hon.
Member's accusation.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I can
quite understand that the hon. Member,
with all his modesty, would be easily
shocked by such an occurrence, but I have
had considerable experience of electioneering and so far as I am concerned
I have always been most anxious to see
elections conducted honestly, because I
consider it is much better for an election
to be conducted in a strictly honest manner so that if a candidate is defeated he
is defeated by honest and fair means, and
not by corrupt methods. Unfortunately
a system has grown up in Belfast under
which the Ballot Act has been abused to a
considerable extent, and that system has
been encouraged by one Party, not the
Unionist Party.
Mr. DORMAN: What Party?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
hon. Member (Mr. Dorman) has referred
to organized bands being arranged, and I
have seen a good many cases where bodies
of persons have been organized for the
purpose of assisting their candidate, to
the detriment of the Unionist candidate.
I hope it will not go out that this practice is attributable to the Unionist Party,
because I must say the Unionist Party is
whole-heartedly in favour of honest
electioneering.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I have carefully followed the remarks of the hon.
Senator who put down this question, and,
although I may be wrong, I do not think
he has given the House any evidence that
the person to whom he referred was not
a sworn official. We are all familiar
with elections and we know that persons,
who invariably have been previously
sworn, do act in the manner the hon.
Member has described, except that they
do not go up to the ballot box. These
persons, however, have authority to
act as they do and have a perfect right
to be in the booth, which the hon. Member admits he had not. We have only
the hon. Member's word that the person
to whom he referred had no authority to
be in the booth on this occasion and,
personally, I am not convinced that the
hon. Senator has made any case whatever.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:I think
the two Members who spoke first rather
wandered from the immediate question
in saying that their respective parties
were relatively guiltless of this alleged
practice. As a matter of fact the Ministry of Home Affairs has no information
that presiding officers have failed in the
performance of their duties, or that the
secrecy of the Ballot is violated, as suggested in the hon. Member's question.
The returning officer is responsible for
the conduct of elections, and for the
appointment of presiding officers to preside at each polling station. The duty
of the presiding officer so appointed is to
keep order at his station, regulate the
number of electors to be admitted at a
time, and to exclude all other persons
except the clerks, the agents of the candidates, and constables on duty. The
duties of both the returning officer and
the presiding officer are prescribed by
statute, and they are each liable for any
wilful misfeasance or wilful act or omission in contravention of those duties.
The Under Sheriff of each county acts as
returning officer for Parliamentary Elections, and in the case of local government
elections, the principal officers of local
authorities act as returning officers.
Senator Dorman admitted that the
moment he drew the attention of the
presiding officer to the practice in the
particular case he mentioned it was immediately stopped, and therefore, I think
the case of the Government for non-interference is justified, on the ground that the
existing law is sufficient to deal with the
matter. At the same time I hope the
55 Kingstown Mail Route.

THE SENATE
Research Station Bill
[Viscount Charlemont]
publicity given to this question will cause
those who may have been guilty of this
practice to reflect on the danger of it.
Holyhead-Kingstown Mail Route.
on
The following question stood
the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state whether letters
addressed to the Six Counties, and
being carried via Kingstown, will be
sorted on the train as formerly between
Dublin and Belfast; and, if so, whether
the work will be done by Irish Free
State or by English officials.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM : I ask
the question which is down in my name
with the object of ascertaining if the
twenty sorters who were formerly employed on the Great Northern Railway
mail train would still be used in connection with the resumed mail service on that
train. As far as I know the letters are
not sorted on the train between Dublin
and Belfast, and if that is so the delivery
in Belfast will be no quicker than it is
at present. The delivery will not commence until 10-45 a.m., which is the time
of the delivery of the mails that arrive
via Stranraer. The Postmaster-General,
in answer to a question asked in the
Imperial House of Commons, stated that
this matter had been dealt with as a
result of conversations between the
Postmaster-General, the Railway Company, and the Government of Northern
Ireland, and he (the Postmaster-General)
understood they were quite satisfied that
what had been done would be the best
plan.
After reading that answer, I take it
that the Government of Northern Ireland
have gone into this particular question
about which I am seeking information.
It is important to know whether there
will be twenty Free State officials travelling on the train to Belfast to sort our
letters or whether there will be twenty
British officials coming from Dublin to
perform that duty. I am very anxious
to hear what the reply of the Government
will be.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
Ministry of Commerce is still awaiting
from the Post Office, London, particulars
of the arrangements to be made in connection with the use of the Holyhead-
Kingstown route for correspondence
passing between London and Belfast. I
understand, however, that such
pondence will not be sorted between
Dublin and Belfast, but will be conveyed
in sealed bags over this part of the
journey.
corres-
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I would like
to join with the hon. Member opposite
in expressing the feeling that if the mails
are not sorted on the train it will give
very little help in the earlier delivery of
letters in Belfast. I would like to say
incidentally that I am very glad, and so
is the commercial community generally,
that it has been decided to revert to the
Holyhead-Kingstown route, even if only
as a temporary expedient, because the
delay and loss has been incalculable
during the past few months. I would
like to see some effort being made by the
Government and I am sure they will
make some effort to get what we want,
and that is an earlier delivery of letters
in the city of Belfast.
Mr. BARBOUR: On this question I
would like to say that I too am very glad
to know that the Holyhead-Kingstown
route is to be used. In all the discussion
that has taken place on this question it
is curious how little the main object of
the change has been mentioned, namely,
Mr. SPEAKER: I did not interrupt
the previous speaker, but I am afraid the
hon. Member is not in order in discussing
the general question of the mails on this
particular question which merely deals
with the sorting of the letters.
Agricultural Research Station Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, That this Bill be now read a
Second time. This is a Bill to give
effect to an agreement for the establishment of an Agricultural Research Station
for Northern Ireland, at Hillsborough,
Co. Down. A copy of the Agreement
appears in the schedule to the Bill.
In recent years both England and Scotland have increased and extended con-
57 Research Station Bill-

29 MARCH 1927
Second Reading
siderably their activities in the domain
of research, particularly in connection
with the many perplexing problems which
beset farmers in those countries. As,
however, the agricultural conditions
which prevail in our province are totally
distinct from those which obtain across
the Channel, our farmers have to contend with special problems for which there
is no analogy in either England or Scotland. Strong representations were made
to the Government by prominent agriculturists as to the need for investigating
these special problems, and when ultimately a carefully prepared scheme for
the institution of a Northern Ireland
Research Station for Agriculture was
submitted, it was supported by both the
Ministry of Agriculture and the Ministry
of Finance.
The Station, as I have mentioned, will
be situated at Hillsborough, and will have
attached to it close on 500 acres of landportion of surplus lands acquired in connection with a residence for the Governor.
The Imperial Authorities have transferred these lands to our Government free
of charge. The principal, although not
the sole, purpose for which the
Station will utilize these lands will be
investigations on the nutrition of every
class of live stock-dairy cows, pigs,
poultry, etc.
There is no need to stress the importance of such work, particularly in view
of the lean years which farmers have
unfortunately experienced recently, but I
wish to utter a word of caution. From
remarks which I have observed from time
to time in the Press, some persons seem
to think of research as a process for
mechanically turning out ready-made
solutions for agricultural troubles. This,
of course, is a complete misconception.
The word research," as I need scarcely
remind the House connotes constant,
minute, and patient investigation, extending possibly over a series of seasons
before it could be definitely asserted that
solution to the particular problem
under examination had been obtained.
a
،،
The Station will be controlled and
managed by a Board of Trustees, whom it
is proposed in the Bill to incorporate as
The Agricultural Research Institute of
Northern Ireland." The Board, which
is quite independent of any Government
Department, consists of nine members,
representing the Ministry of Agriculture,
the Agricultural Faculty of Queen's University, the Ulster Farmers' Union, and
the Ulster Agricultural Organisation
Society.
Under the agreement the Government,
in addition to handing over nearly 500
acres of land, will provide a capital sum
of £30,000 for buildings and for equipping and stocking the Station. The cost
of maintaining and running the Station,
when completely organized, is estimated
at £3,000 per annum. This income will
be available from an Endowment Fund of
£60,000, which the Government will also
provide.
All these sums will be paid
over to the Trustees by 31st March, 1932.
I beg to move.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I would like
to make a few remarks on this Bill, and
to call the attention of the House to the
letters which are being written and the
speeches which are being made by certain
people in Ulster concerning the action of
the Ministry of Agriculture. Hon. Members have probably noticed that the people
who write these letters and make these
speeches are not really agriculturists.
I
have read all the letters very carefully,
and I have tried to read the speeches, and
in no case have I found that the writer
or the speaker is a man with an agricultural training. I have not noticed that a
farmer from the district of the Ards,
which is perhaps the best farming district
in Ulster, has made any adverse criticism
of the Ministry of Agriculture. Perhaps
the Ministry have taken up the attitude
of treating the criticisms with the contempt they perhaps deserve, but I think
we, as a House, should show that we regard the work of the Ministry in a better
light than do those gentlemen who have
written and spoken on the subject.
Agriculture is now in a different state
from that which it was in fifty years ago.
It has to be carried on in a different way,
and in a more scientific way, and this
Research Station which it is proposed to
set up is, no doubt, greatly needed in
Northern Ireland. We badly require
something of that kind. Agriculture must
improve. The Ministry has been abused
a great deal for its past work, but I think
it has accomplished a great deal of good.
In fact, I think every Member of this
House will support me in saying that in
years gone by farmers sold their yearling
bulls for £10 or £12, and now they get
£100, which is entirely owing to the
Research Station Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Porter]
Ministry's foresight and wisdom, to their
action in promoting cow-testing associations, and to their policy in killing scrub
bulls. No matter what people say to the
contrary, stock in the North of Ireland
has greatly improved, and anyone who
has got an eye for a beast can walk into
a fair and see cattle of a quality he would
not have seen fifty years ago.
There have been many other steps
taken by the Ministry to improve agriculture, but I will not detain the House by
detailing them. I will only say that I
am not much of a believer in the quack
cry that the farmers are “stoney broke."
Farmers made a good deal of money
They quite forget that
during the war.
now.
It must be remembered that the
conditions of agriculture vary almost
If one year is a good year
every year.
the next one is probably a bad one, and
you may lose some of the money you made
the previous year. That has to be taken
into consideration. You must remember
that you cannot make an omelette without breaking eggs, and I think the money
which the Ministry of Agriculture propose to spend upon this Research Station
is money that will be well spent. I am
very glad to be able to support the Bill.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to say how pleased I am to
see this Bill brought in, and I am very
glad to see that a Member of the Senate
is one of the trustees on the Board that
will have charge of this Research Station.
It is a good thing to have a Member of
the Senate as a trustee, as he will be
here to answer any questions that we may
ask as to the working of this scheme.
With regard to the Ministry of Agriculture, I am of the opinion that there has
been no Department of the Government
that has done so much for the country
as that Ministry has done. In my young
days, and I am not so very old, the
farmers would grow their oats and
potatoes and hay and would not vary
them. They picked off the worst seed,
and it was like money that went to
religion. If they had bad potatoes they
were good enough for seed, and if they
had bad, fusty oats it was good enough
for seed. The Ministry of Agriculture
have shown the farmers that if they do
not put in good seed they are not making
the most of their land. I am very pleased
to see this Bill introduced, and I think
the House will agree that it is a good
thing for the country that the Ministry
of Agriculture are providing these
examples for the farmers, because we
cannot forget that agriculture is really
the mainstay of our Province.
I have
great pleasure in supporting the Bill.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I rise to support the Second Reading of this Bill, and
I have no doubt it will do a great deal
of good. We cannot divorce agriculture
from science.
Agriculture must keep
pace with scientific research as every other
industry and business must. I notice
that this Bill is for the purpose of carrying out research in animal nutrition, and
to conduct experiments and investigations
relating to animals and crops. I know
there are many stations in England,
Scotland, America, Italy and elsewhere,
and I am inclined to think the results of
many of these experiments could be got
from these stations without going to a
great deal of expense in our own little
area, and without overlapping these experiments. I freely admit that the conditions in this country are not like those
in America or Italy, or even in England
and Scotland, but the propagation of
different varieties of oats which take five
or ten years before they become marketable, is a very slow way of improving the
present agricultural depression.
I do not suggest that the money which
will be given as a grant for this purpose
should be devoted, as has been suggested
in another place, to the reduction of
rates, because it would give such a very
small amount to each individual holder
that he would never know the value of
it. While I am in favour of this Research Station at Hillsborough, I cannot
lose sight of the fact that we have one at
Stormont, another at Queen's University,
and we have agricultural colleges where
experimental work is going on
different parts of the Six Counties. My
only object in rising is to see that none of
these experiments are overlapped, and
that where any information can be gained
from research work carried on in other
countries, it should be obtained by means
of documentary evidence from those
countries without going to the expense
of repeating those experiments in Northern Ireland.
in
Mr. BARBOUR: I welcome the Bill
and I have confidence in supporting it
owing to what our Ministry of Agriculture has done in the past. People are
inclined to laugh at the result of research
Research Station Bill-
Second Reading
29 MARCH 1927
work, but those who think and have given
sufficient time to the study of the question
to warrant them in criticizing it will say
that it is a big measure of help which is
extremely valuable to the country. Here
the Ministry have succeeded in getting
all the Northern farmers to agree to
forego to-day the immediate benefit of a
reduction in the rates and sacrifice themselves to supply a certain amount of
capital which is necessary in establishing
this Research Station. Personally, as a
North of Ireland man, I think it would be
extremely regrettable if we were content
to take up the sort of parasitical position
that we would allow Germans and Italians
and Chinese to carry out all this research
work and rely on their information,
which they paid for, to benefit us.
rather like to feel that we are carrying
our share of the burden.
I
I also think that in doing our own work
for ourselves we are much more likely
to get a fuller measure of benefit out of
the work than if we relied on the Chinese
and these other self-sacrificing foreigners.
Besides, I always find that the people
who do this work would get a year ahead
of us if we relied on them. They always
get ahead of us, and this is an attempt
on the part of our Ministry of Agriculture to try and help our people to get into
the first rank. If our agriculture is ever
to be successful, if ever there is to be
decent life and comfort on the land we
will have to compete at the top of the
market and not at the bottom. Therefore, I have very great pleasure in supporting the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to express my
approval of this Measure. I think а
Government in its relation to the land,
and those who work on the land of any
country, hold an analogous position to
that of the rational father of a family.
The source of the people's life, happiness,
and prosperity is in the land, and it depends very largely on the use that is made
of the land and the development of its
potentialities what the standard of comfort and civilization of any people will be.
I do not presume to speak with anything
like authority on the question of agriculture, but I have had the advantage of
having seen land from the crudest stage
of savage inhabitants up to the highest
point of civilization. I know this country,
this little island of ours, and I have observed for thirty-five years the advantages
that have come to the backward parts of
Ireland from the establishment of the
Department of Agriculture and Technical Instruction, the setting up of which
diverted the centre of that organization
from London to our own country, and put
the responsibility on the shoulders of
Irishmen, and educated our countrymen
in this particular line, to make the best
possible use of the land of the country.
I can assure hon. Members there are
many parts of the country in which the
advantages of that higher education
which was conferred upon the young sons
of farmers, have not yet been fully
realized owing to the fact that many of
those who were of conservative ideas so
far as the land was concerned, and who
were in control of the land at the time
the Department was established, have not
yet passed off the scene and transmitted
to their better educated children the control of the land so as to better develop it
in the interests of the people. I believe
this Research Station will result in the
betterment and uplifting of the standard
of comfort in the homes of farmers and
of those who are employed as agricultural
labourers in the Province.
This is another striking instance of the
necessity of Governments recognizing the
failure of private enterprise and incentive of gain in accomplishing all that is
necessary for the needs of the people from
day to day. Hon. Members may smile,
but it is an argument in favour of the
fact that private enterprise and incentive
of gain have not produced, from the area
over which the Government has control,
the best possible results, and, therefore,
I welcome the Bill as a semi-Socialistic
Measure, and as a recognition by the
Government that they have a right to
develop the resources of the country in
the interests of the people.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT : I
should like to say how pleased I am with
the kind reception that has been given
to this Bill, semi-Socialistic as Senator
Dorman thinks it may be. The Government will see that there is no overlapping
in any way between the work carried on
at this Station and the experiments carried on at various stations in other parts
of the Six Counties, or in relation to
statistics which we get from other countries abroad.
I should have said in my original
speech on the Bill that the House should
63 Ministerial Offices Bill.

Lead Paint Bill.
[Viscount Charlemont]
be very pleased with the fact of Senator
Barbour having consented to give his
services as a trustee of this Research
Station. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
I think the Government is to be congratulated on securing as a trustee of this
Station a Member of this House who can
give such valuable information on the
more scientific aspects of agriculture.

Salaries of Ministerial Offices Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move the Second
Reading of this very short Bill, the necessity for which I think will be apparent
to hon. Members of this House. As this
House is no doubt aware, when this
Government was set up one Minister was
in charge of the two Departments of
Agriculture and Commerce, and one Parliamentary Secretary was appointed to
the Ministries of Education and Commerce. The Act of 1921, therefore, in
its Schedule, speaks of the Minister of
Agriculture and Commerce and of the
Parliamentary Secretary of the Ministries
of Education and Commerce, but owing
to the changes that have taken place
there is now a separate Minister for
Agriculture and Commerce and a Parliamentary Secretary for the Ministry of
Education alone.
The Bill before the
House merely regularizes in Clause 1,
Sub-Section (1) the position as it now is,
and in Sub-Section (2) orders that the
sum of £2,500, which was devoted as to
be divided equally amongst the Members
of the Cabinet, should now be divided
amongst those Ministers who in fact
actually receive Cabinet salaries. I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.

CLAUSE 1 (Regulations as to use of

CLAUSE 2 (Prohibition of employment of women and young persons in
painting buildings with lead paint).
Mr. LESLIE: Before we part with
Clause 2, I should like to say it is the
Clause I had particularly in my mind in
speaking on the Second Reading as regards the definition of a young person.
I may, perhaps, take the opportunity of
saying that the general Amendment, if
I might call it so, standing in my name
for a later Clause, has really particular
reference to this Clause before us, but
it has been pointed out to me that there
are certain complications, owing to the
extraordinary maze of existing definitions
which would make it desirable that we
should not deal with the matter this afternoon, but that opportunity should be
taken of raising the question, perhaps,
on this Clause, or perhaps on a later Clause
of the Bill, at a later stage in the passage
of the Measure. Therefore I shall not
move the Amendment on the Paper to
which I have referred.
Lead Paint Bill
Adjournment

CLAUSES 3 (Application of certain
provisions of the Factory and Workshop
Act, 1901), 4 (Registers, returns, &c.), 5

ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Definitions).
In this Act the expression "lead
paint" means any paint, paste, spray,
stopping, filling, or other material used
in painting which, when treated in a
manner prescribed by rules made by the
Ministry of Labour, yields to an
aqueous solution of hydro-chloric acid
a quantity of soluble lead compound
exceeding, when calculated as lead
monoxide, five per cent. of the dry
weight of the portion taken for
analysis; and the expression " building" includes fixtures.

Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie : -

Labourers' Cottages (Loans) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In rising to ask the House
to give the Bill a Second Reading I may
perhaps inform the House that this Bill
is more or less in the nature of a Money
Bill and, therefore, I do not think I need
detain the House at any great length.
The Bill makes reference to Subsection (2) of Section 1 of the
Labourers Act of 1923, under which
annual loan charges of rural district
councils in respect of Labourers Acts
schemes have hitherto been subsidised by
the Government to the extent of 36 per
cent., and it proposes to repeal that Subsection as regards future schemes from
1st April of this year up to 31st March,
1930, and to provide instead a subsidy
of 40 per cent of the annual loan charges
of rural district councils.
As hon. Members are probably aware,
the financial scheme under which the
Labourers Acts are administered was
formulated over twenty years ago when
advances could be obtained much more
cheaply than at the present day, and so
owing to the rise in the value of money
the fixed percentage grant no longer
truly represents the real assistance
which is being given by the Government
to the construction of these houses.
In future, therefore, advances for the
erection of labourers' cottages will be
made from the Government Loans Fund,
thereby eliminating the anomalies which
arose as the result of issuing stock at the
low price necessitated by the low rate of
interest payable. This entails a readjustment of the fixed percentage grant which
has hitherto been paid, and it has been
decided that a Government grant of 40
per cent. of the loan charges will have
the result of fairly distributing the
financial responsibility as between the
local authorities and the Government.
I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: I have previously assured
the House of the anxiety of local
authorities to provide cottages for agricultural labourers, and I have also dealt
with their difficulties. They are mainly
to provide a suitable cottage for
labourer at a rent which that labourer
can pay on the small wage he receives.
a
Second Reading
Labourers' Cottages Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
Local authorities have been in a difficulty
because they found in the past that, for
the reasons stated by the noble Viscount
who moved the Second Reading of this
Bill, they could not provide a cottage at
what was considered an economic rent for
agricultural labourers. They were reluctant to subsidise these agricultural
labourers out of the rates, and, if I am
correct in my view, on a cursory reading
of this Bill, that the object and the
result of the Measure will be to enable
local authorities to bridge that difficulty,
and facilitate the building of labourers'
cottages, I shall certainly welcome the
Bill. My view, after a cursory reading
of the Bill, is that it will be a step in that
direction, and will contribute to that
result. Therefore, I support the Second
Reading.
Mr. DORMAN: I also wish to welcome
the introduction of this Measure, or any
Measure which is calculated to improve
the conditions of the working classes. I
especially welcome this Bill because I
know that for many years disparaging
comparisons have been made as to the
moral status of the people living in this
particular part of Ireland as compared
with the people living in other areas.
These statistics have had a more or less
world-wide dissemination, and have, no
doubt, lead to a misconception as to the
real moral status of the people in this
particular part of the country.
I believe that that has been mainly due
to the want of proper housing facilities
being brought within reach of the
agricultural labourers in the Province
of Ulster as compared with those in
Munster and Leinster. The labourers in
the other parts of Ireland, from the time
that the Labourers Act was placed upon
the Statute Book, have forced their representatives to make every provision for
the housing of the coming generation,
the young men and women who
entering into the bonds of holy
matrimony and setting up homes. Unfortunately that progress has not been
correspondingly made in the North of
Ireland, with the result that the disparaging comparisons I have already
referred to have been made, and
justifiably made, from the Government
statistics.
are
There is another aspect of this particular Measure I would like to refer to,
and it is this. Making a comparison of
the incidental expenses charged in connection with the erection of labourers'
cottages,
I believe, speaking from
memory, that the legal and other expenses in regard to the erection of labourers
cottages in this part of the country have
been abnormally high in comparison with
the expenses in Munster and Leinster. If
my memory serves me right, I think that
in one district of Cork the average legal
expenses in connection with the erection
of each labourer's cottage are somewhat
over £7, and in some parts of County
Down they have amounted to £32. That
is an aspect of the erection of labourers'
dwellings that ought to be given due consideration by public representatives on
local bodies to whom these facilities will
be extended. I have great pleasure in
welcoming the Bill, believing that it will
do good to those whom the Government
is anxious to assist.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: This being a
Money Bill I take it that we can have
very little to say to it. I understand,
however, from the Labourers Acts that
rural district councils in Northern Ireland were receiving a grant of 36 per
cent., but under this Bill we are to
receive 40 per cent. Therefore, in connection with those schemes that are at
present under consideration but have not
been completed, it would be in the
interest of rural district councils to hold
them up until after 1st April, when
they will get 40 per cent. instead of 36
per cent. In the district I represent,
Newtownards, we have a big scheme coming on and we have done our level best
to force the Government's hands to get it
ready, but if this Bill becomes law I
should be inclined to tell the rural
council to hold back until 1st April and
get 4 per cent. additional grant.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope I need scarcely
say that on a Bill of this kind I can only
rise to support it. All of us who have
been concerned in any capacity during
the past few years in securing or promoting the erection of labourers'
cottages know what the difficulties have
been and what the necessities have been.
We must all agree with the hon. Member,
the Chairman of the Londonderry
County Council (Mr. Long) that the real
problem is to provide a cottage which
can be rented at such cost as the agricultural labourer can pay in the way of
rent. That is always our problem and
the only way we can meet it is, I think,
73 Labourers' Cottages Bill

31 MARCH 1927
Dogs Bill
by cheapening the various costs of
erection.
was
As regards those costs, Senator Dorman mentioned the heavy percentage of
the cost which is taken up by the legal
expenses of transfer. Those expenses in
the past have been very heavy. Indeed,
for reasons that do not matter I have
more than once come into contact with
them, but I think it is fair to remind the
House this afternoon that owing to the
transfer, which is now almost complete,
of the agricultural land in Northern Ireland from the landlord to the tenant
occupier those expenses will necessarily
tend to diminish. The expense
almost invariably caused by the mechanical difficulty of proving title, and it
frequently happened that where the
property had been in the hands of one
family for two
three centuries to
prove the family charges, not to mention any other charges on such property
of them, perhaps, then held
at the day on which those charges were
first imposed, led to a cost of transfer
which was absolutely exorbitant.
of the advantages of land transfer is that
that cost will to a great extent disappear,
and to that extent we shall be assisted
in our beneficent endeavour to improve
the housing of the agricultural labourer.
none

There is one other point that I should
like to mention, because I think it is
worth while that it should be mentioned,

ordinary way which entails the final disappearance or, rather, marks perhaps the
disappearance which has already taken
place of what used to be called the "Land
Act terms" in connection with the erection of some of these cottages. One hears
that expression "Land Act terms" frequently in the country districts. It has
been inappropriate for some years past,
and the objection to it of course is that
or if people think you can get money for
d labourers' cottages on the terms on which
land could be bought under the Act of
1903 they are making a grievous mistake.
fe It is sometimes thrown up both against the

money for labourers' cottages on Wynd-

wut it. Of course, the charges are entirely
en unfounded, and,
as I say, the passage of
I thi
the Bill may perhaps serve to remind the

supporting the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Royal Assent.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
authorised by the Prime Minister, on his
behalf, to inform the Senate that His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland,
in the name of, and on behalf of, His
Majesty the King, has been pleased to
give his assent to the following Bills: -
Agricultural Research Station.
Consolidated Fund.
Dogs Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move the Second Reading of this very
short Bill, which seeks to enact that it
shall be a valid defence to an action for
damages for the shooting of a dog to prove
that the dog was shot when chasing or
worrying cattle. The Bill is very simple,
but it is hoped by the Government that,
if it receives the approval of Parliament
and is placed upon the Statute Book, it
will go some way towards mitigating an
evil from which almost every sheep-keeper
in the country districts has suffered.
It is
The last legislation on this difficult
matter is contained in the Dogs Act of
1906, which provides that the owner of a
dog shall be liable in damages for injury
done to any cattle by that dog.
common knowledge, however, that the Act
has by no means proved as efficacious as
was hoped. First of all, the dog and its
owner must be identified. Hon. Members
know as well as I do that dogs that worry
sheep are generally dogs that come from
a distance. The reason for that is beyond the capacity of the Leader of the
House to give, but it is a fact, I believe.
Dogs Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
.
[Viscount Charlemont]
In the second place, when the owner of
the dog has actually been identified, and
an action brought against him by the
farmer whose sheep have been worried, it
frequently happens damages cannot be
obtained owing to the fact that the dog
is not of sufficient financial
owner
standing.
a
My right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, received a deputation
short time ago from the Ulster Farmers'
Union, who supplied details of fully
authenticated
cases of sheep-worrying
dogs. During 1926 there were 115 sheep
and lambs killed by dogs, 95 worried and
7 flocks chased; while, since the beginning of the present year, there have been
75 killed, 2 worried and 4 flocks chased.
Now, there is no doubt whatever that
these figures are not final. Indeed, I
could add to them myself from my own
personal experience, and I have no doubt
many other hon. Members of this House
could do so also.
I
There has recently been in Northern
Ireland an increase in the number of
sheep kept by our small farmers, who are
beginning to find it a profitable occupation, and this Bill is designed to help
them, so far as can be done without acting unfairly towards the dog owner.
do not think that I need say any more; I
do not anticipate that any Member of
this House will oppose the Bill, or try
to show that it is designed unfairly
against the dog owner. A. very important safeguard towards that end is inserted in the Bill, by which it is required
that the person who shot the dog shall
notify the police within 48 hours. I beg
to move.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: This Bill will be
heartily welcomed by the farming community in every part of the Province,
and I think the statistics, which the
noble Leader of the House has given,
show the absolute necessity for it. The
present remedies in the case of sheep
worried or killed by dogs are quite ineffective, and people hesitate to let themselves in for a law suit with very doubtful results. The law being ineffective it
became necessary for some steps to be
taken. I think the Government is to be
congratulated on bringing forward
Measure of this kind which, though
perhaps not remedying the evil to the
fullest extent, will nevertheless go far
to mitigate it. The difficulties of tracing
a
dogs that have worried or killed sheep aru
very great, and the art of eluding the Act
by owners has been brought to a very
fine point.
I know myself where within the last
few months eight ewes have been killed
and the rest of the sheep so driven and
tormented that the lambs they had to
produce have been abortions.
Unless
some Measure of this kind was introduced
the keeping of sheep would not only
diminish but would probably be terminated in the Province. Sheep are most
valuable animals. First of all, as we all
know, we have lambs and we have wool,
and the sheep themselves are beneficial to
the land, often bringing back land to a
state of productiveness when it has been
exhausted. They also have a very good
taste for noxious weeds, which is another
advantage. Therefore, it is essential
that the keeping of sheep should be
encouraged as much as possible, and,
consequently, I very cordially support
the Measure proposed.
Mr. LESLIE: Like the hon. Senator
for Armagh (Mr. Armstrong) whose
voice, if I may digress for a moment, we
are very pleased to hear once again in
our counsels, I welcome this Bill, and I
desire to thank the Government for having brought it in, principally, of course,
from the public standpoint, and partly,
as I must admit, from the private
standpoint, as one who endeavours
to mitigate the losses that agricultural property entails by rearing
sheep and lambs. The difficulty is one
which has already been alluded to. The
noble Viscount, who moved the Second
Reading, pointed out that if a farmer
who had been injured was able to identify
the dog and the owner, and to bring an
action, he stood to lose the fruits of his
successful action owing to the fact that
the owner of the dog was a man of straw.
There is another very serious difficulty.
The heaviest loss is really not the worry.
ing of a few incidental sheep, although
sheep may be killed, but the hunting of
ewes that are in lamb. These ewes do, as
has been said, cast their lambs. If you
can identify the dog and the owner, and
if the owner is a man of substance still
you cannot recover damages for the loss
of those lambs because it is to some extent
hypothetical and the law cannot go so
far. The lambs may have been lost for
some other reason, and you have to stand
