Workmen's Compensation
(Miscellaneous Amendments) Bill [S].
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move the Second Reading of this Bill,
the object of which is to amend the provisions of the Workmen's Compensation
Acts and to give effect in Northern Ireland to certain enactments of the Workmen's Compensation Act, 1925.
As hon. Members are aware, the law
regarding Workmen's Compensation in
Northern Ireland is contained in no
fewer than five statutes, dating from 1906
to 1923, and it is now considered
desirable that they should be consolidated,
as has been done in Great Britain by an
Act passed two years ago. But before
the introduction of such a Measure of
Consolidation, you are asked to consider
the Bill now before the House, in which
it is sought to give effect to amendments
of the law which have been made in
Great Britain since our own Parliament
last legislated on the subject that is, in
1923. In addition, an opportunity is
taken of including in the Bill provisions
for improving the machinery for determining compensation matters, and
provision for giving effect to a resolution
of the Workmen's Compensation Sub-
Committee of the Imperial Conference of
1926.
a
It will be sufficient for me, I think, to
indicate very briefly the tenor of the
various clauses in the Bill. Regarding
Clause 1, an Act of the Imperial Parliament passed in 1924, namely, the County
Courts Act, provided that in Workmen's
Compensation cases, fees should be payable by employers but not by workers.
This Clause, as hon. Members will see,
will bring the law here into line with that
in Great Britain.
The first Sub-section of Clause 2 seeks
to amend two provisions contained in the
Act of 1906, and in the Act of 1923. As
the law stands, a workman who meets
with an accident while under the age of
21 years, may apply for an increase in
the amount of his compensation, on the
basis of what he would have been earning
on the date of the review if he had remained uninjured. But the review
must take place not less than six months
after the accident, and it must also take
place before the man is 21 years old.
Hence, a youth aged 20 years and 7
months, for example, at the date of the
accident is unable to comply with the
law, and this Sub-section seeks to remove
that hardship, by permitting youths injured within six months of becoming 21,
to have their cases reviewed before or
within six months after the workman
attains the age of 21 years.
The second Sub-section of Clause 2 has
been inserted at the request of his
Honour the Recorder of Belfast, in order
to render the procedure under Section
14 of the 1923 Act more effective.
Clause 3 extends the powers of County
Court Judges and Clerks of the Crown
and Peace in proceedings under the
Workmen's Compensation Acts, by enabling money standing to the credit of a
deceased dependant to be distributed
without Probate or Letters of Administration, while
Clause 4 confers on them the power of
making orders for examination of witnesses, etc. Similar powers were conferred on County Court Judges and
Registrars in England by the County
Courts Act of 1919, and it is considered
desirable that our own Courts should be
in the same position.
Clause 5 introduces a corrective amendment by law in regard to priority payments in the winding up of a company.
In effect, it brings the existing provisions
into harmony with the Companies (Con
solidation) Act, 1908.
Clause 6 deals with compensation for
workmen suffering from silicosis. Under
the provisions of the Workmen's
Compensation (Silicosis) Act, 1918, which
133 Workmen's Compensation Rill

12 MAY 1927
Second Reading
at
S
e
of
re
ev
ak
the
e
B
applies to Northern Ireland, the Ministry
of Labour has power to set up special
schemes of compensation for workmen in
any specified industry involving exposure
to this disease. In Great Britain that
Act of 1918 was extended by an amending Act in 1924, to cover the
of workmen suffering from silicosis
accompanied by tuberculosis, and this
Clause seeks to do the same for Northern
Ireland.
case
Clause 7 seeks to empower the Ministry
of Labour to extend the provisions of the
Workmen's Compensation Acts to the
crew of any aircraft, the owner of which
resides, or has his place of business, in
Northern Ireland. Similar powers were
conferred on the Imperial Home Secretary by the Workmen's Compensation
Act of 1925.
The object of Clause 8 is to give effect
to the resolution passed by the Imperial
Conference held last year, suggesting
that
should be made
arrangements
between the different parts of the Empire
whereby any sum awarded as compensation to a beneficiary in one part of the
Empire could, in the event of that
beneficiary being or becoming resident in
another part of the Empire, be transferred to and administered by the
competent authority of that part of the
Empire in which the beneficiary has gone
to reside. The terms of the resolution
are set out in the Schedule to the Bill.
Clause 9 confers upon masters, seamen,
apprentices to the sea service, apprentices in the sea-fishing service or pilots,
a right to take proceedings for recovery
of workmen's compensation in a Northern Ireland Court, under Section 35 of
the Workmen's Compensation Act, 1925
(Imperial), in cases in which those persons now have a right to take such proceedings under the provisions of Section
7 of the Workmen's Compensation Act,
1906, and the rules of court governing
the taking of proceedings under that Act.
The Clause may be described as a revision, rather than a substantive amendment, of the law, because it does not alter
any rights, but merely enables the recent
Imperial Act of 1925 to be cited in lieu
of the Act of 1906 in cases where the
proceedings can, under the existing law,
be taken in a Northern Ireland Court.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we shall all
welcome this Bill as the first instalment
of the fulfilment of a promise which
some of us, perhaps, were beginning to
think was long overdue. There was a
distinct promise given or perhaps I
ought not to go as far as to say it was a
promise-but it was suggested when we
passed the Workmen's Compensation Act,
1923, that it would not be long before we
had an opportunity of consolidating our
law on the whole subject. The proposal
to do that, to consolidate the law, was
made again in the Gracious Speech at the
opening of this Session, and was received
with very great satisfaction. We can
only hope that when we do get our consolidating Measure we shall be a little
more successful than the Imperial Parliament has been with the Act of 1925,
for I think I am correct in stating that
that consolidating Act was amended
once last year, and I think it is in some
danger of requiring further amendment.
That
As it is I think we shall find, when we
do get our consolidating Measure, that
it will be possible to compress into one
statute all that part of the law relating
to compensation for accidents, which is
comprised in what we may call the second
cycle of legislation of that class.
is, legislation which was introduced originally in England chiefly, I think, at the
instigation of the late Mr. Joseph Chamberlain towards the end of the nineties,
when the right to compensation was really
based on the contract of service, as distinct from the earlier matters dealt with
by such Acts as the Fatal Accidents Acts
and the Employers' Liability Acts, which
are what I would venture to describe as
the earlier chapter of the volume on the
same subject.
I confess that I did not quite follow
the noble Viscount in his reference to
Clause 5 of the Bill before us, and I think
that at a later stage it might be worth
while, by formal Motion, to direct more
specific attention to Sub-clause 2 of
Clause 2; and to Clause 4, which
introduces matters that are somewhat
novel to us. I am sure that all that will
be required will be a little explanation
on those points, and that can be got
later more conveniently than to-day.
I am rather inclined to think that
Sub-clause 2 of Clause 2 must be dependent on some proposal at present before
the Imperial Parliament. It is the
section referred to as being recommended
by his Honour, the Recorder of Belfast,

and I think, perhaps, there might be something more than we were told about that.
I do not think there is anything more that
one can usefully say at this time about
the Bill except that although the Bill
does deal with a more or less intricate
subject it is remarkably easy to follow
and should not, I think, give us any
difficulty at any stage of its passage
through the House.
I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
should feel obliged if the hon. Senator
would bring forward the points he has
mentioned at a later stage, when I shall
be in a position to give him fuller information than I can at the present moment.

CLAUSE I (Provision and running of
road vehicles by railway companies).
or
(1) It shall be lawful for any railway company whose railway system is
situate wholly or partly in Northern
Ireland, to provide, own and maintain
omnibuses, coaches and other vehicles
moved or drawn by mechanical
animal power (in this Act referred to
as road vehicles") and to use and
run road vehicles upon public highways
for the conveyance of passengers and
passengers' luggage and the carriage of
merchandise; and for the purposes of
this Act any such railway company
shall have the following powers:-
،،
(a) To demand and take fares and
charges in connection with such conveyance and carriage as aforesaid;
(b) To provide and equip garages,
offices and other buildings in connection with the exercise of the powers
conferred upon such railway company
by this Act;
(c) To apply their funds in connection with the exercise of any of
their aforesaid powers; and
(d) From time to time to raise
such moneys as may be necessary
for the purpose of providing road
vehicles, garages, offices, machinery,
plant and equipment in connection
with the exercise of their aforesaid
powers, as if that purpose were a
purpose for which such railway company may, by virtue of their powers
as existing immediately before the
passing of this Act, raise money by
borrowing or by the creation and
issue of new stock as additional
capital, and subject to any limitations applicable to the exercise of
their last-mentioned powers.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 1, line 8, after the word "moved"
،،
،،
(other vehicles moved or drawn) to insert
the word "propelled." This is really a
very simple matter. The point is that
the word "moved' has always been, if
one may say so, a railway" word, but
when motor vehicles came into being it
was always customary to use the expression propelled." You find that in the
Light Locomotives Act of 1896, which is
the birth Charter of motor vehicles, and
the vehicles to which this Bill will apply
will be in the main motor vehicles. It is
rather desirable that for the sake of
making our road vehicles light locomotives
we should use the same word. When
we come to the Taxing Acts, the Finance
Acts of 1920, and so on, we find that the
duties are applied to vehicles propelled
by mechanical power, and, finally, in the
Motor Vehicles Act, which we passed last
Session, we defined our Public Service
Vehicles, and in so far as road vehicles
under this Bill are omnibuses they will be
Public Serivces Vehicles we defined a
Public Service Vehicle as a vehicle propelled by mechanical power, and therefore
I think it is desirable that for the sake of
attracting all the laws and regulations
which apply to mechanically propelled
vehicles we should insert the word "propelled in this clause. I beg to move.
"
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I am prepared to accept
the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause

We spoke on the Second Reading of the

proposing to do that it seems to me that
it is desirable that we should give direct

perhaps to some companies who may
desire actually to build in these workshops. I therefore suggest here and
in another place the insertion of the word
"workshops," and I think that perhaps
the necessity for the insertion of the word
occurs more on the second Amendment,
which, if the Committee accepts this one,
I shall move afterwards. It is more clear

CLAUSE 1 (Fees in Workmen's Compensation proceedings) ordered to stand

CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as to Weekly
Payınents).
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I should
like to give a little explanation as to the
effect of Clause 2, and also as to the words
"Clerk of the Crown and Peace'" which
appear in the clause.
Clause 2, Sub-clause 2, of the Bill has
been drafted to improve the procedure
contemplated by Section 14 of the 1923
Act, in cases where a dispute has arisen
as to whether a workman has wholly or
partially recovered from his accident, or
whether his incapacity is due in whole or
in part to the accident. The Section as
Part
it stands provides for the arbitrament of
a medical referee, but does not give power
expressly to anybody to fix in terms of
money what, according to the effect of the
medical referee's certificate, the future
weekly compensation payment of the
workman is to be. This leads to a deadlock in the event of the certificate of the
medical referee being disputed, and in such
circumstances the only remedy open to the
employer or workman, as the case may be,
is to make a fresh application for review
of the weekly payment. The Clause in
question will remove this anomaly by
giving the Clerk of the Crown and Peace
or, on appeal, the Judge the power to determine having regard to the medical referee's certificate, whether the weekly
payment shall be ended or diminished, if
diminished, the extent of the diminution,
and for dealing with any sums paid into
Court accordingly.
It will be noticed that the new Subsection mentions the Clerk of the Crown
and Peace, whereas the provision which
it amends speaks of the Registrar of the
County Court. This is a change rather of
drafting than of substance, and we are
making it now because we propose to
make it in the Consolidating Bill to which
I referred on Thursday last.
In view of what has been said in this
House, perhaps I ought to explain now
why this drafting change is proposed.
The Act of 1906, which is the principal
Act of the code, gave various functions to
an officer described as the "registrar of
the county court." This description is wide
enough in actual practice to include two
officers in the first place the Clerk of the
Crown and Peace, who holds a united
office, comprising functions of the former
Clerk of the Peace and Registrar of Civil
Bills, and in the second place the Judge's
Registrar. Under the Act of 1906 the
143 Workmen's Compensation Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Viscount Charlemont]
،،
work is done by the Clerk of the Crown
and Peace, and he is the officer designated
for that purpose by the Rules of Court
made under the Act. If, therefore, we
substitute the expression
Clerk of the
Crown and Peace for the expression
Registrar in our Consolidating
Measure, we shall not be making any
change of law but merely clarifying the
existing law.
،
دو
Mr. LESLIE: There is nothing for me
to say except to thank the noble Viscount
for the explanation he has given us of this
particular Sub-section. I think it will
be agreed that it was very desirable that
that explanation should be given, because
the Sub-clause as it stands does involve
a slight departure from the wording of
the English Act of 1925, and, therefore,
it would perhaps have been a cause of
some difficulty to us when we came to consolidate the Measure later on if we had
not that explanation on record. The
same remarks apply to the substitution of
the Clerk of the Crown and Peace, which
is now made perfectly clear. That expression will, I understand, be used
throughout the Consolidating Measure.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Distribution of compensation on the death of a dependant), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Rules as to examination
and discovery, &c.).
Mr. LESLIE: I am going to ask the
noble Viscount to tell us a little more
about the Rules of Court. The point that
occurs to one from a reading of the Clause
is, who is going to make these Rules of
Court? As a matter of fact it can be
found out, by a certain amount of research, on whom the duty is cast, but it
requires the assistance of, not a lighted
candle, but an absolute searchlight. For
the same purpose as we received an explanation on another Clause, to clear the
way for a Consolidating Measure, I think
it would be desirable if the noble Viscount
told us a little more about who is going
to make these Rules of Court. Also I
should like him to confirm my view that
the Rules of Court here referred to as
Rules of Court under the principal Act,
the Act of 1906, will apply equally to our
entire code of compensation cases. As the
Committee will remember, when we passed
the Act of 1923-what I am going to call
the intermediate Act-by means of an
extremely carefully drawn citation and
construction Clause we made the Act of
1906, or rather a reference to that Act,
apply to the entire code of five or six Acts
to which the noble Viscount (Viscount
Charlemont) alluded. But it will confer
a service on us if the Leader of the House
gives us a little more information on the
point to which I have referred.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Paragraph 2 of the Second Schedule to the
Workmen's Compensation Act, 1906,
brings workmen's compensation matters
within the jurisdiction of the judge of the
county court, according to the procedure
prescribed by the Rules of Court, while
Paragraph 12 of the said Schedule provides that the duty of a county court judge
under the Act shall, subject to Rules of
Court, be part of the duties of the county
court, that the officers of the court shall
act accordingly and that Rules of Court
may be made both for any purpose for
which the Act authorises Rules of Court
to be made, and generally for carrying
the Act into effect so far as it affects the
county court and proceedings in the county
court.
The effect of Section 79 of the County
Officers and Courts (Ireland) Act, 1877,
the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, and
Orders in Council made thereunder and a
subsequent amendment made by the
Parliament of Northern Ireland in 1925
is that county court rules are now made
by the Lord Chief Justice of Northern
Ireland with the concurrence of the chairmen of quarter sessions or any two of them
nominated by the Lord Chief Justice.
February, 1926,
By order dated 1st
the Lord Chief Justice of Northern Ireland nominated His Honour Herbert
Thompson, Recorder of Belfast, County
Court Judge and Chairman of Antrim,
and His Honour Arthur Henry Bates,
County Court Judge and Chairman of the
County of Down, to be the two chairmen
whose concurrence in the making of these
rules, under the County Officers and
Courts Act, 1877, should be necessary and
sufficient. I think the principal Act is
sufficient for the purpose indicated.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
May I ask the Leader of the House if he
can give us any information as to when
the present Session is likely to end. I
understand from inquiries made elsewhere
that very little business remains to be
done, and as some of us will be going
away it would be a convenience if we
had an idea when the House would be
likely to adjourn for the Summer Recess.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I cannot give the hon. Senator definite information. There are three Finance Bills
coming forward, but I think I can safely
say that the Session will probably conclude at the end of the present month.

VIEWING OF BODIES BY
CORONERS' JURIES.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether any steps have been, or will
be, taken to direct the attention of
those concerned to the provisions of
Section 17 of the Jury Laws Amendment Act (Northern Ireland), 1926.
comes
Mr. LESLIE: The question standing
in my name this afternoon relates to the
somewhat gruesome subject of the inspeсtion of bodies by Coroners' Juries. That
inspection is one which has always had
to be discharged by juries and
down to us from very ancient times. It
has always been singularly objectionable,
and this Parliament, with a view of relieving juries from an unpleasant duty
which was frequently unnecessary, deliberately enacted, when dealing with the
Jury Laws Amendment Bill last year, a
paragraph dealing with the matter. I
think it will save time if I read the
Section-Section 17 of the Jury Laws
Amendment Act of last year:-
Notwithstanding anything contained
in section forty-six of the Coroners
(Ireland) Act, 1846, or in any other
enactment, no juror upon an inquest
held by a coroner shall be required to
view the body at the first or any
Loans Guarantee Bill- 150

18 MAY 1927
Viewing of Bodies by Juries
рал
ал
mac
ectra
urpose
ha
t. D
ompla
OW'S
D.
T
on
este
H
10%
ta
ingra
uired)
sitting of the inquest, unless the
coroner in any particular case considers
that such view is necessary in order to
assist the jury in arriving at their
verdict; and no inquisition nor judgment to which the said section relates
shall be quashed, stayed or reversed
because, by reason only of the provisions of this section, the body was not
viewed by the jury upon an inquest.
That Section would seem to convey in
fairly unmistakeable language the intention of Parliament. The proposal was,
as a matter of fact, received with satisfaction in Parliament when the Government introduced it. Therefore, having
regard to the plain wording of the Act,
I was somewhat surprised to see in yesterday's newspapers a statement that at
an inquest in the Six Counties-I do not
think that either place or Coroner concerns Members in the least, so I will say
that at an inquest held in the Six
Counties the Coroner said
he observed that the Coroner in County
Antrim recently announced that he
would not ask juries to view dead
bodies, as it was not necessary. He was
afraid that if he did so his inquest
would be illegal. He knew the Act on
the subject applied only to England
and Scotland and did not affect
Northern Ireland, which was still under
the old law making it compulsory for
juries to view the body.
Well, the fact that that particular
Coroner might be under a misapprehension is not, perhaps, of very particular
importance and would not call for attention here, but unless he has been misreported or his remarks have been unreasonably condensed in the newspapers
it would seem that he has passed what
can only be regarded as an unwarrantable reflection on his colleague in the
County of Antrim. That is a matter to
which attention ought to be directed here,
because it is obviously undesirable that
gentlemen holding the responsible and
onerous position of Coroner should engage
in a combat with each other through the
medium of the newspapers. It is also
very undesirable that there should be any
misunderstanding in the public mind as
to the duties that are cast on those who
are summoned to attend as jurors at
inquests.
Therefore, I thought the best plan to
as
a
follow would be to put down a question
on the Paper which will afford an opportunity for anything to be done that may
not hitherto have been thought necessary
to be done. If there is any reasonable
ground for thinking there is any misunderstanding amongst Coroners
whole as to the meaning and intention
of that Section of the Northern Ireland
Act-which, of course, is entirely distinct
from the English Act, which was passed,
as a matter of fact also during the last
year-it may be possible to remove that
misunderstanding to some extent and at
the same time enlighten the public mind
as to the duties of jurors in a way that
will prevent that most undesirable possibility of coroners disagreeing with each
other and having their disagreements reported through the medium of the newspapers.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I have read the cutting
referred to, and, speaking for my own
county, I can only say how sorry I am
that the Coroner for Tyrone should have
cast any kind of reflection on the Coroner
for the County of Antrim, in which
Senator Leslie resides.
I am informed by the Minister of Home
Affairs that, although it is not actually
the duty of the Ministry to advise
Coronors in such matters, arrangements
are being made to direct the specific attention of all Coroners to the provision
of the Section referred to, under which it
is no longer essential that the Coroners'
Jury should be required to view the bodv,
except in cases where the Coroner considers that such view is necessary to
assist the jury in arriving at their verdict.

Suspension of Standing Order.
Moved: That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting in respect of any Bills received
from the House of Commons. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
disapproval of the conduct of the Government in so frequently asking for the
suspension of Standing Orders when we
are called together for the purpose of
considering legislation of a very serious
character. Probably few of the Senators
have had an opportunity of examining
the Bills that are before us to-day as they
left the other House, and I think my own
feeling with regard to my function as a
humble Senator is that we occupy so important a position in this State of ours
that more consideration should be given
to us especially with regard to financial
Measures which we have no right to
amend, and under the rules of procedure
we have only the right either to accept
to reject. Ample time should be
afforded Senators if necessary to interview interested parties in the State
whose interests may be affected by such
legislation, and I purpose dividing the
Senate upon this particular Question today as a protest against the continuous
application for the suspension of Standing Orders.

The Holyhead-Kingstown Mail Route.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham: To ask the Leader of the
House if he will state the hours of the
arrival in Belfast since Easter holidays
of the English mails received via Holyhead and Stranraer respectively, and the
hours at which the delivery of each commenced; and if he will ascertain from the
Post Office what proportion of the letters
formerly sent from Belfast by Stranraer
has been diverted to the Kingstown route
since the recent change was made.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
just wish formally to ask the question
standing in my name as I know the
Government will give the information required. It is only for the information
of this House.
a
com-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In reply to the right hon.
Member I may say I have obtained
through the Ministry of Commerce
statement showing the hours of arrival in
Belfast of the English mails received via
Holyhead and Stranraer respectively
since 19th April last, as well as the hours
at which delivery of these mails
menced on each day of that period. As,
however, the Schedule is rather long I
propose, with the right hon. Member's
consent, to hand him a copy for his subsequent perusal. In briefly summarising the
Schedule, however, I may point out that
in the case of the mails conveyed via Holyhead the earliest and latest times of
arrival were 9-15 and 9-45 a.m. respectively, while, in the case of those conveyed
via Stranraer the corresponding times
were 9.28 a.m. and 10-58 a.m. respectively. The average time of arrival taken
over the whole of the period was 9-20 a.m.
in the case of the Holyhead mails and
9-46 a.m. in the case of those conveyed
Herdman, Sir Emerson C.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Massereene and Ferrard, Viscount
Porter, Mr. John Porter
McDowell, Mr. William J.
via Stranraer. The times of commencement of the city delivery vary between
10-45 a.m. and 11 a.m., the difference
being occasioned by the varying volume
of mails received. The average time of
commencement over the entire period was
10-49 a.m.
In regard to the latter part of the hon.
Member's question, I am informed that
of the mails formerly sent from Belfast
via Stranraer 67 per cent. have been
diverted to the Holyhead-Kingstown
route. Of those diverted 75 per cent.
have gained by the alteration, an acceleration in delivery averaging three hours
having been effected by the change. In
the case of London, for example, the delivery of letters received via Holyhead
and Kingstown commences at 7 a.m. and
of those carried via Stranraer at various
times between 9-15 a.m. and 10 a.m.
according to the district.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Arising out of the answer from the Leader
of the House I would just like to ask a
question, which is a very serious question,
at the present time. The point was
made that the advantage of sending to
Dublin was to get an earlier delivery, but
by the return, which the hon. Senator
who asked the question has received, I
find much to my astonishment that the
mails whether they go by Holyhead or by
Stranraer are delivered at the same time,
so that there is really no advantage in the
mails going that way. That is the
report of the Government that the
mails via Holyhead and those via Stran.
raer are delivered about 10-45.
mails are sent out by the same delivery.
It seems to be an extraordinary thing that
the mails should go by Dublin except for
the advantage of Belfast. The fact is
they do not gain one single minute
whether they go by Stranraer or by
Dublin.
Both

question asked I would ask the noble
Viscount to circulate the information
given to the right hon. Gentleman with

Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In rising to move the
Second Reading of the Exchequer and
159 Financial Provisions Bill

THE SENATE
Finance Bill
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Financial Provisions Bill I may say, as
hon. Members are aware, that this is a
Money Measure, and I will not detain
the House at any length. It will perhaps
suffice if I give a brief indication of the
effect of the various Clauses.
Clause 1 is connected with the very
difficult and complicated question of our
financial relations with the Imperial
Government. Under the Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Act of 1923 it was
enacted that any surplus arising on the
accounts in any financial year was to be
applied, in the first instance, towards the
reduction of any capital liabilities in connection with the public debt of Northern
Ireland, and for this purpose a Reserve
Fund was set up. But at present it is
not possible in any given year to fix the
actual surplus, or it may be, deficit,
until matters of detail have been fixed by
the Joint Exchequer Board and the Imperial contribution finally determined.
Thus adjustments have to be made, and
it is proposed in this Clause that in future
they shall be made, not in the cash
accounts of following years, but within
the Reserve Fund.
as
Clause 2 is simpler and, hon.
Members will see, provides for the setting
up of a Sinking Fund for the repayment
of the capital invested in Ulster Saving
Certificates. I may, perhaps, point out
here that previous legislation has
already authorized the establishment of
a Sinking Fund for the payment of
interest on these Certificates.
Clause 3 authorizes the allocation of
£40,000 out of this year's revenue for the
purpose of assisting the development of
the agricultural industry. It will be
applied principally towards schemes connected with the improvement of live
stock, and the machinery provided by
the County Committees of Agriculture
will be utilized for the schemes.
Clause 4 is I venture to say, particularly interesting to you yourself, my
Lord, and I am sure this House will rejoice at the concession which the Government have seen fit to make in connection
with the houses erected for ex-Servicemen
by the Irish Sailors' and Soldiers' Land
Trust with which you are so closely connected. Briefly, it is provided that the
ordinary housing subsidy of £80 per
house will be payable in respect of the
houses erected by the Trust and commenced on or after 1st January, 1926.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now
Committed.-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read
the Third time.-(Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
have agreed to the said Bill without
any Amendment.
Finance Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read
First time. (Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard).
a
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move the Second
Reading of this Bill, and in spite of the
limited powers which this House has in
regard to Measures of this kind I shall
ask the permission of hon. Member to explain in a few words the effect of the
changes which the Bill proposes to make.
Clause 1 provides for the remission of
Entertainments Duty on all payments for
admission to any entertainment up to and
including sixpence. This relief will take
place as from 1st June next.
Clause 2 gives exemption, from 1st
June, 1927, to entertainments consisting
of indoor games or sports where no money
is awarded or paid to the contestants and
gives exemption also to all outdoor
games or sports other than those
involving the use of participation of
horses, dogs or other animals, or the
use of mechanically-propelled vehicles. It
will be noted that incidental music will
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
25 MAY 1927
be allowed at such games or sports and no
liability to tax will be entailed thereby.
Clause 3 deals with agricultural shows,
and as the law stands at present exemption cannot be given to any agricultural
show if it includes any attractions in the
nature of gymkhanas or other contests or
competitions of a similar character. This
Clause now proposes, as hon. Members
will see, to exempt from tax all agricultural shows promoted by a society towards which a grant is given by the
County Committee of Agriculture, with
the approval of the Ministry of Agriculture.
Sub-paragraph (b) of the Clause provides that the entertainment in respect
of which exemption is granted must consist mainly of exhibitions, displays of skill,
or performances which would ordinarily
entitle the show to exemption.
Clause 4, which in the copy of the Bill
now before the House is wrongly numbered, is quite clear and requires no explanation from me.
Clauses 5 and 6, which are numbered
in error as Clauses 4 and 5, deal with the
technical subject of Death Duties.
Clause 5 seeks to remove an anomalous
distinction between the assessment of fre
and settled property to duty, which has
existed since 1914 as the result of an oversight. At present, property settled before
1894 is not aggregated for purposes of
Estate Duty when passing on the death
of the life tenant with free property also
passing on that death. There is now no
reason for this distinction and as opportunity has been taken to remove it in
Great Britain the time is ripe for a similar
change in Northern Ireland.
Clause 6 seeks to provide relief from a
form of double taxation by which claims
to legacy duty and succession duty in two
countries, that is, either in Great Britain
and in Northern Ireland, or in Northern
Ireland and in the Irish Free State, may
arise simultaneously in respect of settled
property passing on death. The Government of Ireland Act, 1920, contains
provision-Section 28-which prevents
double taxation in respect of Estate Duty,
but this provision is not sufficiently wide
to cover this particular type of case.
reciprocal arrangement to avoid double
a
A
taxation in this respect has been entered
into with the Governments of Great
Britain and of the Irish Free State. This
Clause is intended to give legislative
sanction to this arrangement. I beg to
move.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill before us is,
as the noble Viscount reminded us, a Bill
which it would not be within our Province to amend even if we desired to do so,
but it is perhaps fortunate that when there
are mistakes in printing it is not necessary to have a formal Motion made for
their correction. I did not quite follow
the alteration the noble Viscount made in
the numbering of the Clauses, and therefore I think that, perhaps, I should say
that the copy I have in my hand is a copy
of the Bill as amended on Report. which
contains two Clauses numbered 3. As it
happens, that matter is of no consequence
as it can be adjusted hereafter.
The Bill does contain two concessions of
distinct value, and one of them will be
received with the very greatest satisfaction
throughout a large portion of the Province. That is the reduction which the
Minister of Finance, in preparing his
Budget statement, has found it possible to
make in the Entertainment Duty. For
some reason or other those who paid for
admission to entertainments at a low rate
felt very much aggrieved at the amount
of tax which was placed upon them, but
it does not seem to me to be generally
recognized that those who can afford
amusement can also afford a contribution
to the State. But very bitter complaint
was made, and there will be much satisfaction at the amelioration which has
been made.
The other concession, of course, relates
to the arrangement which will prevent
double death duties being payable when
property is assessable in Great Britain
or the Free State as well as in Northern
Ireland. That will be welcome. There is
one omission which I am sorry to notice,
and that is that in adjusting our death
duties or the scale and basis of our death
duties as between Great Britain and
Northern Ireland opportunity has not
been taken to assimilate the practice in
Northern Ireland, and that in Great
Britain in regard to protection to agricultural property which was given to comparatively small estates in the Finance
Measure which was based on Mr. Winston
F
Finance Bill
Appropriation Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Churchill's penultimate Budget. It will
be remembered that some two years ago
in Great Britain relief was given to Supertax, and by way of compensation something was added to Death Duties, and it
was generally said at the time that the
burden of the new Death Duties fell on
those who received no relief from Supertax.
Complaint was made by the agricultural
interests in Great Britain and they succeeded in getting agricultural properties
exempted from the additional scale. That
procedure has not yet been followed in
Northern Ireland, but I live in hope that
next year, when the Minister of Finance
is looking round for recipients of such
bounties as he is prepared to give, this
claim will be remembered and conceded.
The Bill as a whole affords yet another
proof of the strength of our financial position and reminds us once more of the debt
we owe to our Minister of Finance for the
care with which he has guided our Exchequer through
very troublesome
period.
a
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
cannot understand, and Senator Leslie
did not make the point clear to me,
whether under Clause 5 there will be relief
in regard to Super-tax, or whether the
successors of deceased persons will obtain
relief in respect of death duties. Is the
effect of the Clause to relieve taxation or
to increase it?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: It is a relief in one case and
an addition in the other.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
In which case is it an addition.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The addition is where property settled before 1894 is not aggregated
for purposes of Estate Duty, and the relief
is in double taxation.
Mr. LESLIE: May I just explain that
the relief to which I was referring was
relief in double taxation only. I was
not dealing with the earlier part.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

165 Workmen's Compensation Bill
25 MAY 1927

Adjournmental
Workmen's Compensation
(Miscellaneous Amendments) Bill [S.].
Moved: That the House now take into
consideration the Amendment made by
the Commons to this Bill.--(Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Commons Amendment:
Clause 8, page 6, line 8, at end add
"For the purpose of this section, His
Majesty's dominions' includes territories
under His Majesty's protection, and such,
if any, of the territories in respect of
which a mandate on behalf of the League
of Nations has been accepted by His
Majesty, as His Majesty may by Order
in Council direct to be included in His
Majesty's dominions for the purposes of
this section."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move that this
House do agree to the Amendment made
by the Commons to the Workmen's Compensation (Miscellaneous Amendments)
Bill. The addition to Clause 8 of the
Bill, as passed in the Senate, has been
inserted at the suggestion of the Imperial
Government. It extends the definition
of "His Majesty's Dominions " to Protectorates, and territories in respect of
which a mandate on behalf of the League
of Nations has been accepted. It thus
enables the benefits of the Clause to be
extended to workmen's compensation
awards, as between Northern Ireland and
those areas.
Mr. LESLIE: Of course there can be
no possible objection to the Amendment
which has been moved, because it all
comes to this, as the noble Viscount has
said, that benefit shall follow the flag, but
the suggested addition does, if I may say
so, rather lead one to fear that a new
terror has been added to Parliamentary
life, because I am afraid we shall in
future have to watch the expression "His
Majesty's Dominions" to see that it is
sufficiently amplified until the day comes
when we have a new Interpretation Act
of our own, which I have pleaded for
inore than once, as we are getting expressions which require a definition once and
for all. Then we may be able to put
something in which would save unnecessary and unpleasant repetition.
When we get also to the Consolidation
Bill, which I was pleased to hear the
noble Viscount ask the House to give a
First Reading to this afternoon, we shall
be able to consider how far it will be
necessary for us to extend this principle.
I do not imagine for one moment that it
will give us the smallest difficulty, because
of the purposes for which any alteration
of that sort is intended, that is to say,
that the right of compensation is to arise
from the fact of the accident and is quite
unaffected by accident of locality. That
is the whole tenor not only of this
Amendment, but of the Clause in the
Bill to which it relates.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move that this
House do now adjourn until 12-15 o'clock
on Tuesday next.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
would like to ask will Tuesday be the last
day of meeting. Will that be the
closing day of the sitting?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In reply to the hon. and
gallant Member, the House will meet on
that day for the purpose of the Royal
Assent, and that will be the last day of
the sitting.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
J

of the Clock until Tuesday
next, 31st May.

Colonel CRAWFORD: May I just ask
a question regarding a matter that has
caused some little inconvenience to some
Members of the House. It is with reference to the official notice calling this House
together. The Notices we got a couple of
days ago summoned us here for twelve
o'clock to-day, but when some of us
arrived here at some inconvenience, having
been engaged on committees, we found by
the Order Paper that the House was to
meet at 12-15 o'clock. I want to know
what is the correct way of knowing when
the House does meet?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In reply to the right hon.
and gallant Member, he is now referring
to the Whip I sent out that the House
would meet at noon. The correct time
for the House to meet is as stated on the
Order Paper. It was really owing to an
unfortunate oversight that the hour of
twelve o'clock was inserted on the Whip,
and I much regret the inconvenience
caused to some Members, as I understand
has been caused.
On Question, Motion agreed to
Adjourned accordingly at twenty
minutes after Twelve o'clock
until such date as Mr. Speaker
may appoint.

Quarries Bill [S.]
A Bill to consolidate, with amendments, the enactments relating to
quarries, and for purposes connected
therewith, was presented by Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard, read the First
time, and ordered to be read a Second
time on the next Sitting Day, and to
be printed. (Bill No. 5).
Workmen's Compensation Bill [S.]
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In rising to move the Second
Reading of the Workmen's Compensation
Bill I will only detain the House a few
moments. As hon. Members are probably aware, it is a Consolidation Bill and
is brought forward for the purpose of
fusing together, for the sake of clarity,
the provisions of several existing statutes.
As the Bill is not introduced for the
purpose of making new law, I need not,
I think, say any more but shall confine
myself to a formal motion that it be read
a Second time. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: We are all, I am sure,
very glad to see that this Bill, which
has been promised us for some years, has
now at last assumed definite shape, and,
if I may say so, we are also very pleased
that it was found possible to circulate
the Bill-which is necessarily a Measure
of some size and some little complexity
to us in order that we might study it
during the Recess. The Bill itself, as the
noble Viscount has just told us, is aimed at
consolidation and not at amendment, and
therefore there is no necessity to go into
any details in respect of it.
In consolidating our law we are following the precedent set in the Imperial
Parliament in 1925, and we are taking
the same opportunity as that Parliament
took of bringing up to date and clarifying the whole code of Workmen's Compensation; that is the code of Workmen's
Compensation as distinct from the earlier
Common Law, the Fatal Accidents Acts and
Employers' Liability Acts, all of which
I think are saved in, I think it is
Clause 29, of the Bill before us. It will
be important at a later stage to bear in
mind what we are doing in this Bill and
what we are leaving untouched.
173 Workmen's Compensation Bill.

11 OCTOBER 1927
Consolidation Bills.

There are two matters that I think
ought to be referred to to-day, because
they are both of great importance and
will, perhaps, present problems of some
difficulty, and in this connection they
owe their origin to accident rather than
to anything else. There are two questions. One is the position of adopted
children, and the other is the position of
a certain class of illegitimate child. Any
difficulty that may arise as regards the
adopted child is due to the fact that
the legalisation of adoption was not
sanctioned by the Imperial Parliament
until 1926. That is one year after their
Consolidating Measure was brought in.
I think we shall have to consider whether
we ought to make some prospective provision for the possibility of our following
on similar lines as regards adoption, and
1 I believe that the right hon. Gentleman,
the Minister of Home Affairs, has already
stated that it is the intention of the
Government to introduce a Measure of
that class into this Parliament at no very
distant date.
Whether it will be possible in a Consolidating Measure for us to deal with
the subject I am not at all clear, but it
is a matter that I think we ought to discuss later on with considerable care. The
English Measure, or the Imperial
Measure, gave the adopted child certain
rights against the adoptors, but gave no
rights as against third parties. That is
why we must consider it very carefully.
The second point is due to the fact that
our law as regards provision for illegitimate children differs in one important
way from the law in England. When we
were dealing with that some three years
ago we provided that the liability of a
putative father for contribution towards
the support of an illegitimate child should
not terminate on death, but if the father
did in fact leave any estate the illegitimate child was to come in.
I remember distinctly that when
speaking on that subject my right hon.
and gallant Friend (Colonel Sharman-
Crawford) reminded me that the Workmen's Compensation Acts did deal with
problems of that nature, but the difficulty
is that we have continued the liability of
the putative father after death; in England they have not. This Bill will be
found to protect the interests of that
child, who is simply illegitimate because
the parents have not taken the trouble
to get married in accordance with
ordinary law. Such a child is frequently
the offspring of a permanent and monogamous union and is perfectly well-known
by everyone as the child of the two
parents. The interests of that child are
protected by the Bill before us and by the
English Measure. But there remains the
more or less unrecognized child of
putative parent, and such a child in
Northern Ireland is protected after the
death of its father.
a
It will be for us to consider very carefully that the illegitimate child gets the
same protection as the child of a recognized parent. That also will, I think,
be found to be a matter of some technical
difficulty but in no sense a matter of controversy. Indeed, there is nothing in the
nature of controversy in the Measure, and
I have every reason to believe that when
it is passed and becomes part of our
Statute Law in its new form-because
there is no change proposed when it is
received as part of our general law I
think it will be found to assist us in the
administration of the claims which must
necessarily from time to time arise in
cases of injuries caused to workers in the
course of their employment. I have the
greatest possible pleasure in supporting
the Second Reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Consolidation Bills-Joint Committee.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move to resolve, 'That all
Consolidation Bills in the present Session
be referred to a Joint Committee of both
Houses of Parliament, consisting of eight
Members."
I may perhaps indicate to the House
the procedure which it is proposed to
adopt with regard to Bills of this nature,
a procedure which, as hon. Members are
aware, is strictly in accordance with
established practice at Westminister.
Consolidation Bills, such as the one to
which this House has just given a Second
Reading, will be introduced in the Senate
and the various stages through which they
pass in this House will be, for the most
part, of a formal character, although, of
course, it must be clearly understood that
opportunity will be offered for the movement of any Amendments which may
seem to be desirable. After a formal
175 Consolidation Bills.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Second Reading such as we have had today, the Bill will be committed to a
Joint Committee of both Houses, whose
duty it will be to examine each enactment
of the Bill in its relation to existing law
and to satisfy themselves that it reproduces the existing law correctly and without amending it. The Committee then
presents its Report and the Bill is recommitted to a Committee of the Whole
House, when the Amendments proposed
by the Select Committee are made. The
subsequent stages in this House
formal, but as I said before, further
Amendments may be made either on the
recommittal of the Bill or on the Third
Reading. The Bill is then passed and
sent through to the other House for consideration, where it goes through the
usual stages, which are, as a rule, formal.
I thought it would perhaps be as well if
I explained in these few words what the
procedure will be.
are
on
On Question, Motion agreed to.

be named of the Joint Committee
Consolidation Bills:- Mr. J. Cunningham, Mr. J. C. Glendinning, Mr. T. M.
Greer, Mr. J. G. Leslie.-(Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard).

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Of course,

should think the House will meet again

On Question, Motion agreed to.

Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation)
Act.
ENDORSEMENT OF LICENCES.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie : -
To ask the Leader of the House whether
a circular letter was issued by the Ministry
of Home Affairs, intimating that magistrates were bound to cause motor driving
licences issued under Part I of the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1926, to be endorsed
in respect of
first conviction for an
offence under sub-section (1) of section 1
of the Act, or in respect of a first or
a
179 Oral Answers.

THE SENATE
Oral Answers.
second conviction for exceeding a speed
limit; and if so, on what authority; and
whether the King's Bench has declared
such view of the law to be incorrect, and,
if so, whether any steps have been taken
to remove any false impression which the
circular may have created in the minds
of any magistrates.
Mr. LESLIE: The first of the questions standing on the Order Paper in my
name this afternoon has relation to an
occurrence which it would be impossible to
describe as other than unfortunate, but
now that the occurrence has, in fact, taken
place, we perhaps are concerned with considering the probable effects of that occurrence and the steps that will be taken to
prevent anything of the sort happening
again. The facts, so far as I understand
them, seem to be that something in the
nature of a circular, which I myself have
seen, was issued by the Ministry of Home
Affairs, a circular couched, I may say,
perhaps in rather impérative terms,
terms more imperative than those of the
communications which magistrates were
accustomed to receive in the old days,
when the Chief Secretary in Dublin Castle
informed benches of Magistrates he was
advised so-and-so. This circular was sent
to Clerks of Petty Sessions, and it intimated very candidly-using, I think, the
word "must," which is the word I was
thinking of when I said the terms were
imperative-that magistrates were bound
to cause motor driving licences issued
under Part 1 of the Motor Vehicles
(Traffic and Regulation) Act (Northern
Ireland), 1926, to be endorsed.
I should like to know on what authority that a circular was issued. Subsequently the power of compelling magistrates to act in that manner was taken to
the King's Bench and the King's Bench
gave an interpretation of the particular
Section of the Motor Vehicles Act which
did not correspond with the meaning
which it was sought to attribute to that
Section in the circular to which I have
referred. Since then, so far as I know,
and I have made inquiry from two or
three petty sessions clerks, no attempt
has been made to acquaint magistrates with the fact that the Home Office
circular has been, to to speak, overruled
by the judgment of the King's Bench.
Consequently it may happen that there
are still magistrates in various parts of
the Six Counties who are unaware that
the Ministry of Home Affairs circular is
incorrect and that, I suggest, is a state of
things that should not have been allowed
to occur.
The consequences of the mistake to individual holders of licences are likely to
be serious. It is true that for the purpose of increasing penalties, it is not the
conviction that matters, it is the offence,
but after all, if a man's licence is endorsed and it should happen that he is
again charged with a similar offence, and
if it should further happen that his
licence is legally before the court, the
court would be very likely to impose a
more severe penalty, on the ground that
the first offence recorded on the licence
had been considered of sufficient importance by the magistrates who tried it to
render
an endorsement advisable, for
magistrates have always power to endorse
a licence. The question was whether they
were compelled to do so. There was that
possible consequence. There was the
other consequence that if the man happened to be in search of an appointment
and the prospective employer asked to see
his licence, there would be an endorsement
upon it.
I think the matter was distinctly unfortunate, because it cannot do otherwise
than raise in the minds of magistrates a
distinct distrust of the communications
sent to them by the Ministry of Home
Affairs. I think that if a mistake has
been made the only thing that can be
done is to endeavour to repair it as completely as possible, and for that purpose
I suggest, if it has not already been done,
that a circular should be sent to every
Petty Sessions division which received the
original one, saying that its terms were
incorrect and must be amended at once.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
endeavour to explain the regrettable incident to which the hon. Senator has referred. Some time after the passing of
the Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Act, 1926, the Ministry of Home
Affairs issued a memorandum to Clerks
of Petty Sessions showing, in tabulated
form, the penalties which were prescribed
by the Act. I did not see the circular,
but I shall gladly accept the word of the
hon. Member that its terms were very
imperative.
The Ministry was advised that under
the terms of Section 4 of the Act, magistrates were bound to cause driving licences
181 Oral Answers.

25 OCTOBER 1927

herm

H
issued under Part 1 of the Act to be endorsed in respect of a first conviction for
an offence under Sub-section (1) of Section
1 of the Act, or, in respect of a first or
second conviction for exceeding the speed
limit, and accordingly it was so stated in
the memorandum referred to. This
memorandum, which was prepared for the
convenience of magistrates and clerks of
petty sessions, had, of course, no binding
force and merely indicated what the view
of the Ministry was as to the terms of the
Act.
The Ministry frequently pointed out, in
course of correspondence, that it was not
competent for the Ministry to give any
binding decision on such a matter, which
could only be determined by the courts.
Magistrates were in no way obliged to act
upon the view of the Ministry as expressed
in the memorandum, if they themselves
took a different view of the law.
circular which was issued was based upon
the opinions of the legal advisers of the
Ministry.
The
The matter was brought before the
court of the King's Bench on a case
stated, and that court determined that it
was not mandatory upon the magistrates
to endorse in respect of a first conviction
for an offence under Sub-section (1) of
Section 1 of the Act, or in respect of a
first or second conviction for exceeding the
speed limit.
As this decision of the King's Bench
did not support the view taken by the
Ministry's advisers, the Ministry considered that it was right to issue a circular to clerks of petty sessions pointing
out the effect of the King's Bench decision
and directing that the previous circular
of the Ministry should be amended in
accordance therewith.
This action, which was taken in July
last, should remove from the minds of
magistrates any misconception as to their
powers on this point.
Col. SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Arising out of that answer, I quite agree with
Senator Leslie, but at the same time the
magistrates at the court at which I adjudicate, Bangor, did not look upon the
terms of the circular issued by the Ministry of Home Affairs as being obligatory,
and our decisions were arrived at on the
merits of the case.
district we
In our Petty Sessions
always did endorse a licence
because it seems to me that if we did not
endorse a licence for a first offence the
offender might go all over Ireland committing a similar offence, and in each place
it might be a first offence. I think it
ought to be made compulsory for Petty
Sessions Courts to endorse licences for a
first offence.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I think the
point of Senator Leslie's question is
whether magistrates thought, in consequence of this circular, that it was mandatory for them to endorse the licence,
and, if so, what is to become of the poor
fellow who has had his licence endorsed
under those circumstances.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I should like
to know what position the House is in.
Senator Leslie has asked a question. He
made a speech before he asked it, and
then there is a small debate.
Μι
DEPUTY-SPEAKER:
Senator
Sir George Clark wants to test my abilities
as Deputy Speaker, but I am afraid I
cannot answer his question.
Col. SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am
rather surprised at an old Parliamentarian like Senator Sir George Clark
asking such a question. He has sat in
the House of Commons, but he has not sat
in the House of Lords where the procedure
is quite different to that in the Lower
House. In the Upper House Members
get much more liberty than they
do in the Commons, and I was careful
to preface my remarks with the Parliamentary expression, "Arising out of that
answer."
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I was only
asking for information.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
have given it to you.
APPEALS AGAINST DISMISSALS
BY MAGISTRATES.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie: -
To ask the Leader of the House how
many appeals against dismissals by magistrates sitting in Petty Sessions have been
brought under the provisions of Subsection (4) of Section 21 of the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Act
183 Oral Answers.

THE SENATE
Oral Answers.
(Northern Ireland), 1925, and in how
many cases the decisions of the magistrates have been upheld and in how many
reversed; and what steps are taken to
make effective the assurance given in this
House by the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Home Affairs, that the
power of appeal will only be used by the
police in the case of a gross miscarriage of
justice." (The Senate Debates, 17th
November, 1926, col. 475.)
،،
Mr. LESLIE: The second question
on the Paper in my name relates
to a matter which is even more
important, I think, than that to
which I have already drawn attention.
The House will perhaps remember that
when we were dealing with the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Bill,
about this time last year, we then had a
provision inserted to the effect that any
police constable aggrieved by the decision
in a prosecution should have a right of
appeal against the decision of the magistrates. I pointed out at that time with
all the force at my command, that a proposition of that kind was utterly unknown
to the law of England, and I might have
gone further and said it was a proposal
unknown in any civilized country. But,
at all events, the question went to the
House and the Clause was allowed to
stand.
a
to
bench
of
Consequently it is now competent for
any police officer who brings a prosecution
under this Act which
magistrates dismisses,
go to
Quarter Sessions and appeal against the
decision. In fact he is able to treat it
almost as if it was a civil case. I have,
myself, observed in the newspapers
various cases in which this power has been
made use of. It so happens that I, myself, have only been concerned on the
Bench in one case where such an appeal
has been brought. In that particular
case, if I had to sit on it again, and if I
heard the same evidence again, I should
do exactly as I did before.
However, that particular case went to
the County Court Judge, sitting alone.
The same constables gave evidence as had
given evidence in the court below. They
did not give the same evidence, and the
decision of the magistrates was overruled,
rightly or wrongly it is not for me to say.
There was that unfortunate incident in
that case, and there was yet another. It
to happens that while that particular case
was at hearing in the court below a magistrate came on the bench and announced
that as he had not heard the evidence of
the first witness he would not adjudicate,
and he did not. At the conclusion of the
case he announced that he had not adjudicated, and he had not. Most unfortunately that was misheard where the
reporters sat, and the decision of the
court was reported as a majority decision,
and I gather the County Court Judge was
so informed, the fact being that it was a
unanimous decision, or at least a decision
from which no magistrate ventured to
dissent.
What happened in that particular case
was that the smallest possible fine was in
flicted and most likely it was deserved,
but the defendant had to pay the costs of
the appeal, 40s. The other case which I
have noticed is this. A man was charged
with being drunk in charge of a motor
car. According to the newspaper report
the man and the car were found under
circumstances distinctly compromising.
He was taken to the police barracks and
the police doctor said he was drunk. The
man himself sent for another doctor, who
said he was not drunk. The magistrates
dismissed the case, but their decision was
overruled.
I cannot help thinking that those two
cases could not possibly be described as
gross miscarriages of justice, and that is
the expression which was used by the
Minister of Home Affairs when inducing
this House to accept the Clause in question. I do not think it is necesary to
labour the effect on a man who is convicted
on appeal. The House will remember
that owing to the absence of a provision
in the Act there is a very cruel consequence for conviction under this Act.
A conviction stands against a man for
ever. An endorsement may only endure
for three years, but a conviction remains
always, so that if a man is convicted twice
in 1927, and a third time in 1937, and if
nothing happens in the meantime, the
conviction in 1937 will be a third conviction. That is the effect it would have, so
it becomes of the greatest importance that
the utmost care should be taken to see
that a man is not unduly oppressed.
I think we are entitled to require that
the power of appeal will only be used by
the police in the case of a gross miscarriage of justice. We are entitled to insist
that the power of appeal shall only be

ゴ
d
so used, and I am afraid I must add to
that that so far from that being the case,
in most of the cases one has seen recently
in the newspapers, the decision of the
magistrates dismissing the case has been
upheld. I do not know what figures the
noble Leader of the House will give me,
but I think it is common knowledge that
there have been several cases of late where
the decision of the magistrates dismissing
cases has been upheld by the superior
Court. I therefore put this question on
the Paper in order that it might be indicated-in fact, that the public might be
informed that Parliament does not cease
its vigilance as regards the Measures it
passes; that is to say, that Parliament
accepts the responsibility of seeing that
the Acts it passes are carried out in
accordance with its intentions.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Appeals have been brought in 25 cases from
dismissals of prosecutions under the
Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation)
Act (Northern Ireland), 1926. Of these,
11 cases are pending and 14 have been
In 7 cases the decisions of the
heard.
magistrates have been upheld, and in 7
they have been reversed.
Shortly after the passing of the Act, instructions were issued that appeals under
this Act were only to be proceeded with
with the sanction of the chief officer of
police in each county, or in cases of doubt
or difficulty with the approval of the
Ministry. As the hon. Senator is aware,
an appeal against a decision of the
magistrates in petty sessions must be
lodged within three days and, accordingly,
where the local police are of opinion that
there should be an appeal, it is necessary
that they should give notice of appeal
forthwith, but it by no means follows
that all such notices of appeal are pursued. Instructions have been given that
appeals should only be proceeded with
where it would appear that there has been
an obvious miscarriage of justice.
The hon. Senator will doubtless realize
that in the early stages of the administration of an Act such as this, appeals are
much more likely to be necessary than
after the Act has been in force for a year
or two. Points of doubt and difficulty
arise which it is desirable should be
settled. At the same time he may rest
assured that appeals will not be brought
unless there is a substantial reason. This
has been the attitude of the Ministry
since the Act came into force, and there
is no intention of departing from this
course.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that
answer I feel bound to point out that the
expression used by the Minister of Home
Affairs was "gross miscarriage of justice,"
not " obvious mistake."
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I agree
that that was the expression used, but,
at the same time, is it not desirable that
points of difficulty should be settled even
it a miscarriage of justice has been obvious
rather than gross.
Antrim Electricity Supply Bill.
A Message was brought in from the
House of Commons to acquaint this House
"That they have ordered:—
"That the Promoters of the Antrim
Electricity Supply Bill have leave to suspend any further proceedings thereon in
order to proceed with the Bill, if they
shall think fit, in the next Session of
Parliament, provided that notice of their
intention to do so be lodged with the
Clerk of the Parliaments not later than
One o'clock on the day prior to the close
of the present Session, and that all fees
due thereon up to that period be paid.
"That such Bill be deposited in the office
of the Clerk of the Parliaments not later
than Three o'clock on or before the third
day on which the House shall sit after the
next meeting of Parliament, with a declaration annexed thereto, signed by the
agent, stating that the Bill is the same
in every respect as the Bill at the last
stage of the proceedings thereon in this
House in the present Session.
"That the proceedings on such Bill shall
be pro forma only in regard to every stage
through which the same shall have passed
in the present Session, and that no new
fees be charged in regard to such stages.
"That the Standing Orders by which the
proceedings on Bills are regulated shall
not apply to such Bill in regard to any of
the stages through which the same shall
have passed during the present Session.
"That all petitions presented in the
present Session against the Bill shall stand
referred to the Committee on such Bill in
the next Session of Parliament."
187 Adjournment-

Business of the House.
Ordered: That the said Message be
taken into consideration to-morrow.
Adjournment.

to move, that this House do now adjourn.

should like to ask what is the procedure
during the next two days, particularly in
regard to the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill, in which I am interested.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I propose to take the Second Reading of the
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill tomorrow, and also the Second Reading of
the Sale of Milk Bill. The Second
Reading of the Quarries Bill will be
taken on Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at

Antrim Electricity Supply Bill.
The House (according to Order) proceeded to take into consideration the Commons message of yesterday respecting the
said Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise for the purpose of
moving, as a formal matter, and in my
capacity as one of the Senators coming
from the County of Antrim, the following
Resoluti on :--
"That this House agrees to the Resolution of the House of Commons: That the
promoters of the Antrim Electricity
Supply Bill have leave to suspend any
further proceedings thereon in order to
proceed with the Bill, if they shall think
fit, in the next Session of Parliament,
provided that notice of their intention to
do so be lodged with the Clerk of the
Parliaments not later than One o'clock
on the day prior to the close of the present Session, and that all fees due thereon up to that period be paid.
"That such Bill be desposited in the
office of the Clerk of the Parliaments not
later than Three o'clock on or before the
third day on which the House shall sit
a
after the next meeting of Parliament,
with
declaration annexed thereto,
signed by the agent, stating that the
Bill is the same in every respect as the
Bill at the last stage of the proceedings
thereon in this House in the present
Session.
"That the proceedings on such Bill shall
be pro forma only in regard to every stage
through which the same shall have passed in the present Session, and that no
new fees be charged in regard to such
stages.
"That the Standing Orders by which
the proceedings on Bills are regulated
shall not apply to such Bill in regard
to any of the stages through which the
same shall have passed during the present
Session.
"That all Petitions presented in the
present Session against the Bill shall
stand referred to the Committee on such
Bill in the next Session of Parliament."
The House will appreciate that the
acceptance of that Resolution is nothing
but a formal step that does not bind this
House or any Member of it either to the
Bill to which it refers as a whole or to
any detail of that Bill.
If the House
assents to this Resolution and if the Bill
proceeds the Preamble will have to be
proved before the Committee in the usual
way and any private rights or interests
which will be affected will be as amply
protected when the time comes as they
would be if the Committee stage of this
Measure were taken during the present
Session.
The reason the promoters have had to
apply for leave to suspend any further proceedings is, as I think is generally known,
that the Bill which they desire to promote
is to some extent dependent on a hypothetical Bill expected to be produced by
the Government dealing with the
Bann drainage. The two questions,
as the House is aware from Blue Books
laid on the table of this House,
are closely connected and it would be difficult perhaps to deal with one without the
other. I need scarcely remind the House
that there are many precedents at Westminster for the course which I am asking
the House to take to-day. Reference to
May's Parliamentary Practice, page 741,
gives a little schedule of Bills to which
191 Expiring Laws Bill—/

this procedure has been applied. The list

suspending the said Bill.

Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
had intended to take a certain course of
action in connection with one of the Acts
which this Bill proposes to continue. If
I had not received a satisfactory assurance from the Minister of Home Affairs
I had intended to move an Amendment
at a later stage-
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER: I think
the hon. Member is out of order. The
noble Viscount (Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard) is about to move the
Second Reading of the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill.
Its
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In rising to move the
Second Reading of the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill I need only say that
this Bill is, as hon. Members are
probably aware, in exactly the same
form as the Bill which was introduced
into this House about a year ago.
provisions will no doubt be familiar to
all hon. Members. It proposes that
certain temporary Acts, which otherwise
would come to an end at the close of the
year shall be continued for another
year. The Schedule shows what these
Acts are. Part I refers to those which
are Acts of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom, but which are still applicable
to Northern Ireland, and Part II is concerned with Acts passed by our own
Parliament. I do not think it is
necessary for me to say anything more
on the Bill. I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
apologize for having been a little premature, but I want to say that there is
a certain Act which in my opinion should
not have been continued during the last two
years. I had intended moving an Amendment at a later stage of this Bill, but I
have received from the Minister of Home
Affairs what I regard as a satisfactory
assurance which renders it unnecessary
that I should proceed any further at this
stage. I still wish to retain my right to
move an Amendment later if the assurance
I have received is not carried out.
Mr. DORMAN: When a similar Bill
was before this House last year I offered
an objection to a continuation of at least
one of the Acts embraced in this Bill, and
I think I have since been justified in that
objection by the statements that have been
made in public by the Prime Minister and
various members of the Government and
of the party that supports the Government,
in both Houses of Parliament in Northern
Ireland. On various occasions the Prime
Minister has declared that there was no
State practically in the civilized world in
which peace reigned so widely and in which
law and order were so highly respected
as in the area of Northern Ireland.
When speaking on the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill last year I said:-
At the time these powers were given to
the Government a very large proportion
of the representatives of the people of
Northern Ireland had refused to participate in the business of the Parliament
of Northern Ireland, believing that they
were acting in accordance with the desires and opinions of their constituents.
Since then the representatives of that
large section of the people of Northern
Ireland have taken their places in this
Parliament, and have pledged themselves to co-operate with the Government
for the purpose of mutually carrying on
the Government of Northern Ireland.-
(Parliamentary Debates, Senate, Vol. 7,
No. 14, Col. 317, 3rd November, 1926.)
In consequence of that I expressed my
disapproval of the continuance of the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act. On that
occasion the noble Viscount, the Leader
of the House, declared that the Govern-
193 Expiring Laws Bill-

Second Reading
