in
res
ment would not like to err from excess of
zeal, but would prefer to be over-anxious
and allow the Act to stand for a short
time longer. He added that it was hoped
the Government would be able to discontinue it at no distant date. I am aware
that, owing to the provisions of this Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act, various
citizens of Northern Ireland who, I have
reason to believe, are quite as innocent of
the desire or intention to commit crime as
I or any other Member of this House is,
are unable to reside in Northern Ireland.
I know at least one such individual with
whom I have been acquainted for fifteen
or sixteen years and with whom I have
been in close and intimate contact. He was
incarcerated under this particular Act and
when he was liberated he had to depart
from Northern Ireland and set up business in another part of Ireland. I know
of another case in connection with an
organization of which I happen to be
chairman, an organization the title of
which has probably created a state of
mental panic in the minds of some of the
officials in Northern Ireland, namely, The
International Class War Prisoners Aid
Association. Some time ago a group of
us thought it would be advisable to establish a branch of that institution in Northern Ireland, and a young man whose
mother was dependent upon him undertook the honorary secretaryship of that
organization. He got into touch with
headquarters in London with a view of
establishing a branch in Northern Ireland.
How the authorities got to know the
young man was acting thus I do not know,
but his home was immediately searched
and he was arrested and incarcerated
without any charge being preferred
against him. He was thus deprived of his
right to exercise his power to labour
for the support of himself and his
mother. This is a constitutional organization that has ramifications in all the
civilised countries of the world, and
its object is not illegal and not
revolutionary. It is an organization for
the purpose of helping those who are left
a state of destitution because their
bread-winner has been suspect and
arrested, and to enable the victims of
what is commonly called class war to receive some support from their fellowworkers. The organization is quite constitutional and has not any revolutionary aims in view other than those I have
already referred to. I foresee the
in

possibility that, as Chairman of an
organization like that, my home might
be raided, and I might be arrested and
deprived of my civil liberty as a British
subject without a charge being preferred
against me in a Court of Justice.
If there is any country in the world
in which the Judiciary is looked up to
with respect and confidence by the
people it is the United Kingdom. Our
judiciary is independent of the executive of the country and of the Crown,
and on numerous occasions they have
taken upon themselves the right to stand
up against both the Crown and the
Executive for the purpose of securing
and perpetuating the civil rights of the
citizens, no matter how poor they may
be. It seems to me that the continuance
of this particular Act from year to year
is not only placing power in the hands
of the Government to be used against
individual citizens, but it is also more or
less an insult to the judiciary which has
the confidence of the people.
It seems
to
me surprising that the judiciary,
whom history points to as the final
source of protection of the citizen against
arbitrary conduct either on the part of
the Crown or the Executive of the
country, have not protested against
such a course.
When we look back upon some of the
greatest revolutions that have taken
place in the world's history, we find they
have originated in an attempt on the
part of the Crown to deprive the
citizens of their rights. Therefore, I
feel it my duty at this stage to offer
my protest against the continuance of
this Act, and at a later stage I shall
divide the House in an effort to get this
iniquitous Measure removed from the
Statute Book of Northern Ireland at the
earliest possible date.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not propose to
address the House at this stage on any
details of the Measure before us, but as
I understand from the hon. Senator who
has just sat down that the Committee stage
is likely to be enlivened with a debate and
division-I presume on an Amendment to
omit from the Schedule the Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Act as that is to
happen I will take, if I may-and I must
say I am not sure that I shall not be incurring your censure, Mr. Deputy Speaker
-this opportunity of referring to another
Measure comprised in the Schedule of
G
195 Expiring Laws Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
enactments to be continued. It has been
the practice in this House to allow something in the nature of discussion on individual Measures in the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill, especially when it is not
proposed to bring up the matter again on
the Committee stage.
I therefore take this opportunity of
putting it to the Government that they
should, between this year and next year,
when in the natural course of things a
similar Bill will be before us, consider
whether the time has not come when the
Emergency Powers Act can be left out of
the Schedule. The Emergency Powers
Act, as the House will remember, was
passed under circumstances of great stress
which were then in existence. It was
passed unanimously by the House, and
should a similar case unhappily occur
again it would undoubtedly be passed
again, and nobody would be more in
favour of such a Measure than myself.
But times have changed very much now,
and I would like to ask the Government
to consider whether this Measure, which
allows the Minister of Home Affairs to
dissolve an elected body in the shape of
county or district councils, should be continued any longer.
The Ministry has, as the House is
aware, certain powers of dealing with
Poor Law Guardians under another Statute, and in the Local Government Act of
1898, the Act under which county and
district councils were established, there is
power for an Order to be made by the Lord
Lieutenant, as it is there expressed, to
conform to the circumstances of the time,
and if an Order is made by the Lord Lieutenant, or any authority having power to
make an Order, and if that Order is not
obeyed it can be enforced, and if a High
Court mandamus is not obtained the High
Court has powers of suspension and substitution of a commissioner. If those
powers are still considered insufficient,
there would seem to be other courses which
it would be better to take in normal
times.
It would be possible to bring in a short
Measure proposing to deal with troubles
of this sort in very much the same way as
the Bill which has already been before us
this afternoon, The Ministry of Home
Affairs (Provisional Order Confirmation)
A Provisional Order might be
brought in and the matter dealt with by
Bill.
Parliament in that way, or definite power
might be taken to the Ministry to do certain things, subject to the approval of
Parliament. Either of those courses
would be better in normal times than the
present course. It is always preferable
that in any case where something in the
nature of a dispute arises between the
Government and a local body, which,
after all, is an elected body representative
of somebody, it should be settled by a
third party, and that third party is the
court.biede
I do not propose to move any Amendment in Committee, because the retention
of the Bill does nobody any material
harm, and if we suddenly decided to excise it we might leave ourselves in the
position of abolishing one Bill without
having another to take its place, and that
would not be desirable. I ask the Government to take this matter into consideration during the twelve months that will
elapse before we are again asked to consider it. Taking the Bill as a whole, including, I am afraid, the Measure to
which Senator Dorman has just referred,
I shall support it.
AND
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE
FERRARD: I do not propose, at this
stage of the Bill, to answer in detail the
speech of Senator Dorman. I take it
from his speech that he will move an
Amendment at a later stage to exclude a
certain Act from the provisions of this
Bill, and I shall then have an opportunity
of replying to him.
With regard to what the hon. Member
(Senator Leslie) has said, I am sure the
Government will take the question of the
retention of the Emergency Powers Act
very seriously into consideration during
the next twelve months. These Acts
are only retained after very careful consideration and consultation, but I am
quite sure I can give that assurance to
the hon. Senator.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time" agreed to.
Sale of Milk Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I beg to move the Second
197 Sale of Milk Bill-

26 OCTOBER 1927
Second Reading

T
d

T
axi
ت
that
F

reading of this Bill which, though short,
is very important, as hon. Members will
appreciate.
or
The Bill is purely voluntary, and is
designed to enable milk producers whose
milk is certified free from tubercular
bacilli to be granted a licence to that
effect, and, incidentally, it will also ensure that those producers whose milk is
not so certified shall no longer be allowed
to advertise it as being "certified",
"Grade A", "tuberculin tested",
under any other similar designation. In
future, before the producer is allowed to
use any of these designations it will be
necessary for him to satisfy the Ministry
of Agriculture that his herd has been
tested and found satisfactory, and that
the milk is being produced and sold under
conditions ensuring that the supply is free
from tubercular bacilli.
It will be seen that the Bill prescribes
penalties for contravention of its provisions and in this connection it may not
be out of place to give a short explanation. There will be two classes of persons
liable to prosecution. The first consists
of those who, without a licence, sell milk
under some special designation implying
that it is of the same quality as that sold
by a licensed producer. Such persons
commit an offence deliberate and naturally do not merit very great consideration
The second class will be producers who
have obtained a licence, and who for some
reason or other subsequently fall short
of the required standard, or commit a
breach of the conditions governing the
grant of the licence. In these latter
cases it is not intended to institute proceedings immediately. Instead, an investigation will be made by inspectors of
the Ministry of Agriculture, who will
endeavour to ascertain the reason for the
decline, and to suggest an adequate
remedy. Prosecution will be instituted
only in the last resort when it is found
that the producer is guilty of deliberate
and continual failure to comply with the
conditions, and even then it may be considered sufficient to revoke the licence. I
beg to move.
Mr. LONG: A pleasant feature of this
Bill is that there is no coercion or compulsion applied, although I understand
that Senator Dorman does not agree with
that attitude. In my opinion the people
of Ulster do not care for coercion or compulsion in a matter of this kind. From a
brief study of the Bill I am convinced that
the promoters will, in some degree, achieve
the object they have in view. In my
opinion there are three factors which will
go a long way towards achieving success.
The first is if the Regulations made under
the Bill are reasonable. The second is if
an expenditure is not called for that is
prohibitive. A number of men of moderate means will, no doubt, endeavour to
obtain a licence, but if an extravagant
expenditure on non-essentials is called for
men will certainly not embark upon a
scheme of this nature.
The next factor which 1 think will
make for success will be if the inspectors
appointed under the Bill are reasonable.
From the speech of the noble Leader of
the House I gather that they will be inspectors of the Ministry of Agriculture,
and I have every confidence in those of
them I have met. Another factor that
will make for the success or otherwise of
the Bill will be the attitude that the
general public take up in regard to it.
If the general public show that they wish
to have this commodity, this tuberculintested milk, they must, of course, be prepared to pay a slightly higher price for it,
and if the public show by their purchases
that there is a real demand for it, I have
no doubt the dairymen will supply that
demand. A number will, no doubt, start
the project for financial reasons, and a
smaller number will adopt the scheme
from higher and better motives.
There are a larger number of people
and a larger number of cattle suffering
from tuberculosis than is generally supposed. I have been informed that one
out of every eleven persons who die have
been suffering from some of the numerous
forms of tuberculosis. The hon. and
gallant Member (Colonel Sinclair) is a
better authority on that question than I
am, but I know for a fact that a promising
family in my neighbourhood has been entirely wiped out and the doctor in attendance attributed their diseases to infection
received from contaminated milk. Any
steps taken to secure an improvement,
however slight, should be welcomed.
With regard to the number of cows
suffering from tuberculosis, I should like
to tell the House that the Londonderry
Asylum Committee supply milk to the
institution, and for many years they have
bought the very best cows to be obtained,
199 Sale of Milk Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Long]
regardless of cost. In this matter they
have felt that the best cows were the
cheapest and most economical in the end.
In a recent test, out of 48 cows and they
are a picture to look at-eight were
found to be suffering from tuberculosis.
If one-sixth of that herd is suffering from
tuberculosis-a carefully selected and
splendid looking herd-what is the proportion in the ordinary herd? I should
think that at least 50 per cent. of the
cows in Northern Ireland are suffering
from tuberculosis.
The expense entailed in keeping a
licensed dairy will be very considerable,
and not the least part of that expense
will be represented by the scrapping of
these unsound cows that have failed to
pass the test. It would be interesting if
we asked ourselves what is to become of
those cows that fail to pass the test. Suppose that a Mr. Brown has a licensed
dairy or is trying to establish a licensed
dairy. Fifty per cent. of his cows fail to
pass the test. What is done with those
cows? A few, no doubt, are used for
food, and the public at one fell swoop
consumes that infected meat, but the
great majority of those cows go to another
dairy. They will be sold to a Mr. Robinson, who has an ordinary dairy, or to
farmer Jones.
What is achieved by moving these cows
from Brown to Jones? I suggest that if
the Ministry of Agriculture are in real
earnest in this matter their logical duty
is to require the rejected cows in Brown's
dairy to be branded as suffering from
disease, thus letting all and sundry know
that these cows are unsound. That step
might be considered drastic and severe,
but I feel sure it would secure the approbation of all interested in the welfare of
society and the health of the community.
There is another matter I would like to
bring before the House with a view to
having some information given on the
Third Reading of the Bill. It is a well
known fact at least I have been informed it is well known that a cow
which has been subjected to a tuberculin
test and failed to pass will, if again submitted to the same test in a week's time
successfully pass it. I understand that
dealers who expose cows for sale readily
submit to their cows being tested.
they fail they take the cows back that day
week and with equal readiness again sub-
If
mit them to the test, and the cows pass
on the second occasion.
I submit it is the duty of the Veterinary
Department of the Ministry of Agriculture-and they are a very competent body
-to make such provision for testing as to
secure absolute accuracy so that there shall
be no possible doubt about a cow being
free from disease when it has passed the
tuberculin test. In the ordinary course
of events a person purchasing a cow is unaware whether it is the first or second
occasion on which it has been tested, and
I submit it is the duty of the Veterinary
Department not only to devise a method
by which the object I mentioned can be
attained, but also to put into the hands
of every veterinary surgeon in Northern
Ireland the means of seeing that every
cow tested in the Province is really and
truly free from disease. On the whole I
support the Bill, but I want to reserve
the right to suggest some amendments in
Committee.
Mr. DORMAN: This Bill is a step in
the right direction. The subject with
which it deals is one in which I have been
intensely interested for the last thirtyfive years, and during the course of my
lifetime extremely wide opportunities
have been afforded me of endeavouring,
as an unskilled man from the medical
point of view, to ascertain the sources of
illness and death of probably thousands
of members of the working classes who
became victims of tuberculosis. My interest in this question has gone sufficiently
far back to justify me in saying that,
before the inspection of milk was brought
under the control of the municipalities of
the country I had taken a personal interest in this particular question, because
of my occupation in life as manager or
inspector of an Industrial Assurance
Company. In that capacity I was brought
daily into contact with fellow-workers
who have passed over the border, many of
them prematurely, through this widespread cause of infection.
It seems to me that to make a Bill of
this kind optional is leaving it open for
that dread disease to continue in
the community from this particular
source of infection. Unless it is made
compulsory the real source of the spread
of the disease will be more or less left
uncontrolled. In my opinion the sources
of the supply of all milk ought to be
under inspection to ensure that they are
201 Sale of Milk Bill-

26 OCTOBER 1927
Second Reading
ed

caz
at
allti
WAR


でき
Y
be
free from contamination. I do not wish
to occupy the time of the House by giving
numerous illustrations which have come
under my personal observation, but I
think I will utilize this opportunity of
giving at least one which will show that
it is not merely the congested areas and
the poor and needy that are subject to
contamination from this particular source
but those of the professional and upper
classes of society.
I remember the late Professor
McWeeney telling us some twenty years
ago of a medical colleague whose wife
died from this dread disease in early life,
and she left one child. After the death
of his wife he took upon himself the responsibility of personally examining every
particle of animal food before it was
handed to the cook to be cooked for the
use of the family. At the age of twentyone his only daughter showed indications
of having been infected with this disease.
For two years he was troubled in mind to
ascertain the source of the contamination.
He had a residence in the country to
which he used to go during the summer,
and it suddenly struck him that the contamination might have taken place there.
He consulted a maid, who attended his
child while she was resident in the
country, and inquired into her habits.
She incidentally told this doctor that the
child was very fond of going to an adjoining farm and asking the farmer for
drink of hot milk as it came from the
cow. The Professor's friend immediately
despatched the maid to this country
residence and instructed her to bring back
to him a bottle of the hot milk as it came
from the cow. He passed it on to Professor McWeeney for bacteriological examination, and it was discovered to be
teeming with tubercle bacilli.
a
One illustration like that ought to be
sufficient to rouse us as to the importance
of this question and the dangers of leaving a Measure such as this optional. All
the sources of the supply of milk should
be under strict inspection in the interests
of the health and well-being of the community as a whole. The amount of unnecessary suffering and early death and
misery amongst the working classes in
Belfast is increasing according to the
report of the doctor in charge of that
particular department of our municipal
services. While I give whole-hearted
support to this first step I hope the day
is not far distant when the Government
a
will place on the Statute Book of Northern Ireland
Bill to enable local
authorities to inspect every source of milk
supply in order to preserve the health of
the public.
Mr. HILL DICKSON : I desire to give
my support to the Bill and I congratulate
the Leader of the House and the Ministry
of Agriculture on having introduced to
us another Measure for the grading of
foodstuffs in Northern Ireland. Other
similar Measures that have been introduced by the Ministry of Agriculture include the grading of eggs, which has
proved beneficial not only to the sellers
but to the entire community. We have
also passed Acts dealing with the grading
of potatoes, apples, and so on.
This Bill,
dealing with the grading of milk, which
is the food of infants and is the only food
complete in itself, is one of the greatest
importance. I would like to see it go
further, but if we are to believe Senator
Long, that 50 per cent. of the cows that
come into the market are suffering from
tuberculosis, I am afraid our milk supply
would be cut down by half and there
would be a great shortage of milk.
This is a Bill to provide for the granting of licences for the sale of milk under
special designations. I do not know if
the House is aware of the fact that there
are only three herds in Northern Ireland
that supply Grade A milk. There are
several herds supplying tuberculin-tested
milk, but Grade A milk is a very costly
article to provide. Tuberculin-tested
milk, of course, means the milk of cows
that have been tested and found to be free
from tuberculosis.
I notice in the Bill the words, "or any
other designation." We have milk supplied that is pasteurised or brought to a
certain heat and we are told that when
it is brought to that heat any germs there
may be in it are destroyed, and that it
makes it a very indigestible food for infants. I submit that that milk should be
labelled as such. I saw an interesting
article on this subject in the evening paper
last night by Sir Bernard Spilsbury. Referring to infectious foods he said:-
Although in one group the microbes
were killed by heating, the poison was
still conveyed through the dead organism. Recent experiments had shown
that boiling the organisms not only did
not destroy their poisonous action but
Sale of Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Hill Dickson]
rendered them more intense in their
action.
Sir Bernard Spilsbury is one of the
greatest authorities in the world on this
subject. If the fact of bringing milk up
to a certain temperature does not destroy
the germs it should not be sold as tuberculin-free milk unless the cows have been
tested and proved to be free from tuberculosis. I desire to support the Bill, and
I think it goes just as far as it is possible
to go at present.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I am inclined to think
I am approaching this Bill from
slightly different angle perhaps than the
hon. Member who has just sat down.
Indeed, to be perfectly candid, when I
first read the Bill I was not quite sure
that it was a Measure to which this House
ought to give a Second reading. However,
the Measure comes from elsewhere, and
there is no doubt it is a Measure with
excellent intentions, and I think it will
be agreed that whatever one may think
about certain details of the Bill, it would
be a pity to summarily reject it even
though there might not be much chance at
this period of the session of making the
Bill more suitable than it is at present.
The Bill, as I understand it, is first of
all directed towards the prevention of
fraud. To sell milk of some kind, say
tuberculin-tested, which is not tuberculintested is a fraud, and usually a very
heartless fraud, because a person who will
buy and presumably pay a higher price for
milk which he believes to be more pure
than the ordinary milk is usually anxious
to obtain the milk for a child or sick
person. But you can scarcely have your
Minister of Home Affairs setting out to
pass special Bills for the prevention of
every description of fraud. There was
a doggerel, a most immoral doggerel, attributed to the Tichborne claimant, who
was reported to have said:-
as
Some have money, no brains, some
have brains and no money; them
has money, no brains meant for them as
has brains, no money.
That highly objectionable doggerel has,
I am afraid, been acted upon by certain
offenders, and one cannot complain that
the Minister of Home Affairs is proposing
to deal with them. There is undoubtedly
precedent for dealing with frauds. There
1S the Misdescription of Fabrics Act,
which referred to flannelette, and another
one dealing with rag flock, but in
those cases the evils are tackled by
means of Regulations and not by
issuing licences. There does not seem
to be any great reason why a man
should take out a licence to follow a lawful
occupation, but one does not want to split
hairs about that. Neither is it necessary to consider very carefully the possibility of the Minister being engaged in
chasing down the scale of paragraph A of
the first Clause. You can imagine your
semi-fraudulent dealer driven from Grade
A to Grade B, and before the Minister
has had time to deal with that he has
started on Grade C. There are possibilities there, but let us get to the more
serious points.
I think it is agreed that one, at all
events, of the prolific sources of tuberculosis is tubercular milk, and I think it
is further agreed that one of the prolific
sources of tubercular milk is the tubercular cow. But it is by no means I think
accepted that tuberculosis always comes
from milk or that a tubercular cow always
gives tubercular milk. I saw it stated in
the newspapers the other day by a doctor,
whose name I did not recognize, that the
most prolific source of tuberculosis was
tubercular meat. I have also heard-and
I cannot help wondering whether it was
not from the hon. and gallant Senator
opposite (Colonel Sinclair) that I heard
this some twenty years ago, when he and
I were engaged in doing something in support of an anti-tuberculosis campaign initiated by a noble and large-hearted lady,
who has, perhaps, received rather less
credit for her work than she deservedthat the tubercle bacillus, the little rod
with which we are chastised, was always
present in the air when you breathe, and
that the ordinary person cannot go
through the day without imbibing a certain number of these bacilli, and he has
to rely on his own state of health to
throw them out.
However, that may be, I think we have
to consider whether this Bill will really
help us to obtain a supply, a plentiful
supply, of cheap and good milk, because
however much there may be danger from
milk which is not entirely free from tuberculosis it is a very grave question whether
you would not do more harm by unduly
restricting the supply of milk than by

ix
letting it go. It is perhaps of greater
importance that large quantities of milk
should be available, and if anything done
in the way of improving the supply does
in fact diminish the quantity either by
diminution or by reason
of the price, so that the poor
not obtain it, it will work out badly. On
the other hand, if we can get better milk
let us by all means do it.
actual
can-
Let us look at the Bill from this point
of view. What inducement does this Bill
offer to anybody to produce or sell milk
of the class indicated? The Bill, apparently, is to be operated, so I understand,
from the speech of the noble Leader of
the House, by the Ministry of Agriculture. There is no reference to that
Ministry in the Bill. It is the Ministry
of Home Affairs throughout. Milk, I
presume, is intended to be the milk of
cows, although there is no reference to
cows in the Bill at all.
Milk is also
obtained from mares, goats, and asses,
usually under certain conditions, but I
presume the Bill before us is limited to
the ordinary milk, which is the milk of
the cow. What inducements are offered
to anybody to incur the very serious expense which has been referred to by the
hon. Senator, the Chairman of the
Londonderry County Council (Senator
Long)? He spoke of the difficulty of producing tuberculin tested milk, and he also
spoke of the expense of erecting the byres
according to the latest and most approved
methods.
I would imagine the intention is that
both these expenses should be incurred
before a man is in a position to get a
licence. I do not know when it is expected
he will do these things, because no date
of commencement is given in the Bill, but
a man builds his byre, and then gets his
cows, if he can. He then applies for a
licence, but there is nothing in the Bill
to show us what conditions are likely to be
imposed in that licence. A man is invited
to put his head very much into a noose.
Before he gets a licence he has to pay
fees. We are not told what the fees will
amount to. Apparently the fees are to be
settled by the Ministry of Home Affairs
in consultation with the Ministry of Finance. That is an unconstitutional procedure to which I object very strongly. The
fees ought to be prescribed definitely by
Parliament. It is not a matter that
should be left in the hands of one or two
Ministries.
When this Bill made its appearance in
another place the position was rather
worse, because there was nothing in it
which would require that Regulations and
so on should be laid before Parliament.
That, however, has been corrected, although I may mention that the corresponding English Measure, the Milk and
Dairies Act which was passed in 1922,
but which did not come into operation
until 1925, contains a requirement not
only that Regulations should be laid before
Parliament but that they should lie on
the Table for the Statutory period. Passing from the fees, as to which we are told
nothing, we are told that a licence so
granted may be at any time revoked by
the Ministry of Home Affairs for noncompliance or for any other reason the
Is it
Ministry may deem sufficient.
gravely thought a man is going to buy a
special herd of cows, build a special byre,
and run the risk of his licence being taken
away without any power of appeal? It is
all very well to say this is a voluntary
Measure, but the fly is not bound to walk
into the Ministerial parlour, and if we
walk in we want to have some assurance
that we shall be allowed to stay.
might provide that the revocation of a
licence should only be incurred by an
offence against the provisions of the Act
or against some provision of the ordinary
Food and Drugs Act.
We
After that we are introduced to the
minimum fine. I have mentioned the
minimum fine in this House before, and I
regret it may be necessary for me to
mention it again at a later stage. It
will be seen that the minimum fine can be
applied to two classes of offences. The
Noble Viscount, the Leader of the House,
has carefully distinguished them, and I
would ask hon. Senators to consider those
two offences again, and see how they
square with the minimum fine. Passing
from that, we get power given to an
authorised officer to take and submit for
expert examination samples, and so on.
It is necessary that samples should be
taken, but what is meant by expert examination, and who is to carry it out we
are not told. There is no provision whatever here of the kind to which we are
accustomed in the Food and Drugs Act.
We are all familiar with the procedure
where someone gets authority to take
samples of milk or butter, and he is bound
to divide it into three parts, one he sends
for analysis, one he delivers to the seller
207 Sale of Milk Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
who may, if he likes, get his own analysis,
and the third is preserved for reference.
There is no provision of that kind here.
The proposal is that the Inspector or person having authority to act is the inspector for the purposes of the Food and
Drugs Act. That inspector has a colloquial existence rather than a statutory
one. There are various inspectors who
have power to take samples, but at all
events these people, by whatever name
they may be called, are apparently to be
empowered to take a sample and send it
away for private examination by itself.
That is not fair, and I think that might
be corrected.
In England the Act is apparently
worked more through the local authorities
than the Ministry of Health, and so far as
I have been able to follow the provisions
of the Act, a sample taken must be divided
into three parts in the same way as a
sample taken under the Food and Drugs
Act. If you are going to take a sample
in this unusual manner what inducement
are you offering to a man to build a byre,
buy a herd of cows, and take out a licence?
Most of those matters can perhaps be
dealt with at a later stage, and I believe
myself that one of the best things we can
do for the Bill besides making some alterations of the kind indicated, would be to
insert towards the end-where we may
have
deal with the matter
of appeal from
decision of the
magistrates in case of conviction-a
provision that the Act should remain in
force for. let us say, two years and no
longer unless Parliament otherwise declared. If we do that there will be an
opportunity of seeing during the intervening years whether the Bill did, in
fact, result in inducing people to come
forward and supply the good, pure milk
we desire to see, or whether the terms and
conditions are so rigorous that people
would not touch it.
to
a
I do not propose to oppose the Bill because, although I have criticized it according to my lights as the Bill appears to me,
I certainly would wish it all possible success, for if the end apparently desired can be attained, it is certainly worth
our while to try it. But I do hope we
will not take the Committee stage unduly
early, and that in the meantime we shall
very carefully consider the provisions of
the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: 1
think Senator Leslie has wandered very
far from the Bill before the House. If
he would read the title he would see that
the object of the Bill is--
to provide for the granting of licences
for the sale of milk under special designations.
It is a voluntary Measure, and if the
grades of milk specified are provided it
will be open to the public to buy this milk
at a slightly higher cost than the ordinary
milk, but those who do not want the milk
need not buy it. As regards the lurid
picture that has been painted of the ravages of tuberculosis, it must be remembered
that milk is not the only source of infection. There are many other causes which
it is just as important to deal with.
The character of the test to be carried
out is not made clear in the Bill-whether
it is to be a single or multiple test. If it
is only to be a single test, I do not think
it will be of the slightest use.
Of recent
years the highest price paid for cattle has
been for bulls going to the Argentine.
A first test the primary test is made,
but there must also be a second test, and
I believe that even when the animals
arrive in the Argentine they have to go
through a third test. I heartily support
the Bill, in the hope that it will be a step
towards securing a supply of pure milk
for the community.
Colonel SINCLAIR I intervene in
this discussion owing to one or two kindly
references that were made to myself, particularly by Senator Leslie, with whom I
was associated in Lady Aberdeen's antituberculosis crusade. It is with some relief that I learn from Senator Leslie
that he is not going to condemn
this Bill outright, and that he
not move its total rejection.
It seems to me that on the principle of
half a loaf being better than no bread,
the House should pass this Bill and perhaps, either in Committee or at some
future date, amend it to meet some of the
objections, many of them sound, that
Senator Leslie has mentioned.
will
I think this is a useful Bill. It may be
weak in some respects, but it is a step in
the right direction. I would like to
keep before me the ideal outlined by
Senator Dorman; that is to have herds
i absolutely free from tuberculosis through-
Second Reading
Sale of Milk Bill-
26 OCTOBER 1927


TR
ther
hea
h
ot
Of
THE
attk
esti

out the Imperial Province, so that there
will be a general guarantee
that the milk
produced in the Province would be free
from tubercular bacilli. I should like to
support Senator Leslie and Senator Sharman Crawford in pointing out that, although milk is, no doubt, a source of
tuberculosis, there are other sources of infection that must not be lost sight of and
that this Bill does not deal with and is not
intended to take notice of. Amongst
these are unsanitary dwellings occupied
by consumptive patients, the breath of
whom, laden with inspissated sputa, may
be inhaled or otherwise disseminated by
the atmosphere. Therefore this Bill only
touches one part of the source of tuberculosis, but in so far as it goes I support
the Measure.
a
May I refer for a moment to Sir
Bernard Spilsbury. I know Sir Bernard
Spilsbury very well. He is a man of the
highest scientific attainment, he is
trained jurist, and every word he utters
is uttered with a full sense of responsibility. The extract which has been read
of his recently expressed views is very interesting, but I am sure he would be the
last to suggest that a generalization of
that kind should be made of universal
application. If it were so, and the statement attributed to him of the indestructibility of microbes and their poisons were
accepted, then there would be no use in
the surgical profession making the elaborate sterilizations they have had to make
to secure the surgical triumphs that have
been so conspicuous during the last
twenty-five or thirty years. A generalization of that kind may be useful amongst
scientists and laboratory workers, but
when you come to deal with Measures
applied to the food of the people we may
have to be content with something short
of the ideal.
Scientists know that certain forms of
microbes are not capable of entire de-
Take, for example,
struction by heat.
anthrax or splenic fever in cattle. There,
while the bacilli themselves can be destroyed, it seems almost impossible to
destroy their spores, the seeds by which
they propagate themselves. That, however, is no reason why efforts should not be
made in many directions to at least secure
attenuation of the virus ensuring that
even a residual potency in the toxins and
germs may be happily overcome, so that
the resisting power of the human body
might be able to successfully cope with
such mitigated toxins.
I would like to associate myself wholeheartedly with the very sound and sensible
observations of Senator Long. With regard to the statistics which he quoted, in
so far as they related to human beings I
think they are correct. I am not capable
from memory of checking his veterinary
statistics, but I should hope that the herds
throughout the Imperial Province are not
so diseased as he represented when he said
that 50 per cent. of the cattle are suffering from tuberculosis. At the same time
there must be a large number of cattle so
affected, and it would be a very desirable
thing to have absolutely tuberculin-free
herds. But that would entail a great
deal of spade work in the first instance.
We must have clean byres, sound cattle,
and properly tested cattle; and here
I think the right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford) sounded
a very useful note when he said that the
tuberculin test should not be decided on
one examination, but that two or even
three tests should be carried out where
that is possible. A cow that showed
tuberculin on one test and not on a subsequent test should still remain suspect
and should be set aside until an adequate
test had absolutely proved the soundness
of the animal.
Nothing is more difficult than the taking of samples and putting them through
the usual bacteriological tests. The possibilities of error are numerous, and it
would be better in a matter of such importance that the test were not single but
multiple. May I just allude to one other
point. We know now that an animal
with tuberculosis in the udder is particularly dangerous because the germs are
located so near the source or the origin of
the milk, and such animals should be
not only removed from the herd but
One must remember that
slaughtered.
in some way those tuberculin germs have
got into the udder. How do they get
into the udder? They must have been
either swallowed or inhaled by the animal,
and the only channel by which they can
reach the udder would be through the circulation of the blood which in turn may
deposit tubercular germs in the flesh and
other parts of the animal, showing how
other tissues than the milk of the animal
may disseminate tuberculosis.
If I gather correctly the sense of the
remarks of the previous speakers, the Bill
might be improved in some respects later
211 Sale of Milk Bill.

THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
on, whether in Committee or by reenactment in a year or two is not of very
great moment now. But I hope the
public will appreciate the effort that is
being made by the Government to give to
the community generally a supply of pure
milk. Although it does not concern this
Bill there are other matters that will
have to be attended to before this object
can be fully achieved. One is the scandalous way in which milk is mixed and exchanged in the open streets. Another
concerns people borrowing and lending
on stormy days, when germ-laden dust is
blowing about and carrying microbes.
There is a great deal to be done before we
can attain our ideal of furnishing an
absolutely wholesome food to the community.
Senator Leslie has set out a few of the
defects in the Bill, and I hope we shall
have an opportunity of welcoming not
only Measures that will tend to provide
milk which is not tainted with tubercle,
but Measures to prevent it being contaminated in transit between the producer
and the consumer and so to some extent
rendering the provisions of this Bill less
necessary.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: In view
of the manner in which this Bill has been
received I do not propose to say very much
in reply to the debate at this particular
stage. Of course, this is only a very
short step towards giving a pure supply
of milk to all classes of the community in
Northern Ireland. While it would be
very desirable to promote a Bill to secure
that every cow in Ulster should be
tuberculin tested it would be a gigantic
project. It would cost a great deal of
money and would take a good deal of
time, especially as it is the technical view
that every cow should be subjected not
to a single but a multiple test. With regard to the various technical points that
have been raised, as they are outside my
own province I should like to defer dealing with them until the Committee stage
of the Bill is reached, for which I will
give as full facilities as I can.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Workmen's Compensation Bill (S.).
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Consolidation
Bills:-
(i) The Committee have examined the
Workmen's Compensation Bill and have
made therein the Amendments specified
in the Appendix to this Report.
(ii) The Committee are satisfied that
the Bill, as amended, reproduces the existing law without alteration and recommend that the Bill be proceeded with.
(iii) The Committee wish to draw
attention to a question which the trend
of their discussions leads them to fear
might conceivably be raised in relation to
Clause 35 of the Bill. Clause 35, which
provides for the application of the Bill,
and of the Workmen's Compensation Act,
1925, to persons employed in ships, is
based on the Act of 1906, as extended
by later legislation and reproduces
Section 9 of the Workmen's Compensation (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1927. When that
Section was before the House of Commons
of Northern Ireland as a Clause the limitations of the powers of the Parliament
of Northern Ireland as regards making
laws in respect of navigation, which were
enacted by the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, were considered, and the
Attorney-General stated to the House:-
"The law as it stood, or rather as it
stands at present, is that under Section 7
of the Workmen's Compensation Act of
1906 these very proceedings can
be
brought by a workman in the County
Court in Northern Ireland.
I conceive that this legislation is within
the powers of this Parliament." (Parliamentary Debates, House of Commons,
Vol 8, No. 27, col. 1765, 20th May,
1927).
The Clause was then accepted and the
Bill containing it received the Royal
Assent on 31st May, 1927.
Nevertheless, a possibility, however remote, may be thought to remain, that a
plea might be set up in a County Court
in Northern Ireland, as against a person
employed in a ship who had suffered injury,
213 Workmen's Compensation Bill.

26 OCTOBER 1927
Adjournment.
S
thes
tok
or against some other person claiming in
respect of such injury, that Clause 35
(assuming that Clause to have been enacted) would be void as a contravention
of the Act of 1920.
The Committee feel that litigation as to
that, whatever the ultimate result, would
impose a hardship on the claimant from
which, or from the risk of which, both the
Parliament of the United Kingdom and
the Parliament of Northern Ireland would
desire to protect him.
The Committee, accordingly, submit
that it ought to be considered whether,
as a matter of precaution, His Majesty's
Government should be approached in
order that declaratory legislation may be
initiated in the Parliament of the United
Kingdom.
Ordered: That the said Report do lie
upon the Table.
Ordered: That the said Bill be recommended to a Committee of the whole
House.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now
adjourn.-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at twenty
minutes before Five o'clock.A

JasnumojbA
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Orders Confirmation Bill.
✓ Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With the leave of the House I
beg to move the Motion standing in my
name on the Order Paper, in an amended
form, substituting the name of Mr.
William Barclay for the name of Mr. W.
J. McDowell. I, accordingly, beg to

Mr. J. G. Leslie.
Mr. W. Barclay.
On Question, Motion agreed to
Quarries Bill (S.).
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): In moving the Second
Reading of this Bill perhaps I may explain the circumstances which have led to
its introduction. There are in Northern
Ireland some hundreds of quarries, in
many of which explosives and machinery
are used. The system of inspection and
regulation set up by the Quarries Act,
1894, is limited to quarries exceeding 20
feet in depth. Hon. Senators will
realise the extent of this industry when
I mention that there are 361 quarries of
this description on record, and of these no
less than 227 were worked during the year
1926.
All quarries, without reference to depth,
also
are either "workshops" or, where machinery is used, "non-textile factories"
within the meaning of the Factory and
Workshops Acts. Quarries are
subject to other Statutes of general
application, including the Notice of
Accidents Act, 1906, the Check Weighing
in Various Industries Act, 1919, the Employment of Women, Young Persons and
Children Act, 1920, and the Workmen's
Compensation Act (Northern Ireland),
1923.
So far as quarries exceeding 20 feet in
depth are concerned, the administration in
Northern Ireland, both of the Quarries
Act and of the Factory and Workshop
Acts, is mainly in the hands of the
Inspector of Metalliferous Mines and
Quarries, who holds appointment both as
an Inspector of Quarries and as an
Inspector of Factories, and is a part-time
officer of the Ministry of Commerce.
Quarries not exceeding 20 feet in depth
have not been subjected to regular inspection in Northern Ireland by the Factory
Inspectors, but in the event of a fatal
accident occurring it has been their
practice to visit the quarry and attend the
inquest.
I understand that the limitations of
regular inspection to quarries exceeding
20 feet in depth was originally intended
to be of temporary duration, and was
applied in order that the work to be done
should not exceed the capacity of the new
body of Inspectors set up in the United
Kingdom in the year 1894. Many
quarries not exceeding 20 feet in depth
use explosives and machinery and employ
a number of men, and experience has
shown that the depth of a quarry cannot
be held to be a true measure of the necessity and importance of inspection and
regulation. The existing difference in
status and treatment between quarries
above and below 20 feet in depth would
appear, accordingly, to have little to
commend it, and it would seem desirable to
grant to the workers of all quarries equal
protection under the law, so far as such
an end can be attained, by bringing all
quarries under a single jurisdiction and
system of inspection.
This Bill has, accordingly, been prepared in order to consolidate, and amend
where necessary, the law relating to
quarries so that the new Measure will form
Quarries Bill:
Second Reading
27 OCTOBER 1927


of t
bod
art
e.
ni

a simple code suitable to the requirements
of the industry and readily available for
the information of employers and workers
alike.
The principal Amendments incorporated
in the Bill are as follows:-
(a) The definition of
quarries' to
which the code is made applicable is
altered so as to include all quarries;
(b) A definition of the term "occupier' is introduced so as to attach responsibility to persons working the
quarries who hitherto could not
definitely be held accountable;
(c) Provisions are introduced for
further safeguarding the use of steam
boilers and machinery;
The
(d) Rules of general application are
introduced approximating closely to the
Special Rules which heretofore were
applied only in individual cases.
justification for this course is that the
ordinary precautions and rule of conduct necessary to ensure the safety of
the workers thus imposed will be placed
on record and brought to the notice of
all concerned in the operation of a
quarry.
The Bill does not impinge upon the
Workmen's Compensation Act (Northern
Ireland), 1923, except in the subject
matter embodied in Sections 25 and 26,
the general provisions whereof have been
re-enacted in Clause 6 in a form simpler
and more in consonance with quarry
conditions, or upon the Check Weighing
in Various Industries Act, 1919, or upon
the Employment of Women, Young Persons and Children Act, 1920.
It is not anticipated that the additional
work of inspection and administration
proposed by this Bill will call for the appointment of any additional staff. I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite sure the
administrative details of the Bill before us
are not likely to occasion this House any
trouble. The only point that occurs to me
as likely to be one which will require careful consideration is the point to which the
noble Viscount alluded at the very commencement of his speech, that is the
alteration in the definition of the term
"quarry." The word "quarry" as distinguished from mine is always taken to
mean a place where minerals are got at
from the surface rather than a place where
they are got at by a process of underground tunnelling, and if one thinks for
a moment of a common stone quarry and
compares it with a coal mine the difference is at once apparent.
But the Bill proposes to definitely alter
the limitation of a quarry which was put
in the Act of 1894 as regards depth of
feet, and the point this House will have
to watch with great care is that we are not
introducing what I might call a "farm"
quarry, the little stone quarry which is to
be found on many farms throughout the
Province, or the little gravel pit-that we
are not introducing there a code of Rules
and Regulations which would be in no
way applicable. There is a very wide difference between the twenty feet in the Act
of 1894 and these small quarries or pits.
I do not know how it will be thought well
to deal with that, whether by inserting as
a qualification a quarry in which not more
than four to five feet is worked below the
surface.
At any rate we shall have to exercise
great care, because in the ordinary farmyard occupation of ploughing you not only
come upon small stones but you often disclose the presence of boulders of some size.
In that case do the workers on that farm
resort to that place for the purpose of
getting stone, which the owner of the farm
would probably afterwards use to mend
roads? They do resort to that place and
get stone and we must see that we do not
inadvertently turn that field into a quarry
or the yard where the stone breaking is
subsequently done into a place adjacent to
a quarry. No doubt, the Government will
look into that matter and tell us later how
it is proposed to guard against these possibilities.
There is just one other point I should
like to mention, because the noble Viscount alluded to it. If we look at the
Second Schedule we find a reference to
Sections 25 and 26 of the Workmen's Compensation Act (Northern Ireland), 1926.
A consolidating Bill dealing with Workmen's Compensation is about to engage
our attention, and I thought it might save
time if I pointed out that these two particular sections referred to in the Schedule
of this Bill are exempt from the general
Quarries Bill
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
repeal which will be found in the Schedule
of the other Bill. Taking the Bill as a
whole, there is nothing to occasion objection, but I hope the points I have raised
will not be lost sight of.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Senator Leslie has asked part of the question I was going to put to the Leader of
the House. It refers to the meaning of
the word "quarry." I thought the Bill
might be prejudicial to County Councils,
and especially those that employ direct
labour and are the renters either by right
of law or by agreement with the tenant of
certain stone quarries. The County of
Down, which many people say ought to
be called the County of Up and Down,
has many of these stone quarries but,
except in the case of one or two of the best
known quarries, most of the stone we
raise is not got from below the surface at
all. 75 per cent. of the stone we get is
obtained from eruptions of rock or stone
above the ground, and I should like to
know if it is the intention that this kind
of quarry should come within the provisions of this Bill. I do not think they
should be interfered with, and if they are
it will require a large number of inspectors
to supervise them. We have no really
deep quarries in County Down.
The only other point I should like to
mention is this. This is the age of officialdom, and I hope the noble Leader of the
House will tell us how many inspectors it
is proposed to appoint under this Bill, or
that he will at any rate give the House
an assurance that the number to be appointed will be limited.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to welcome this
Bill because it is bringing under the protection of the law a number of workmen
who hitherto have not been protected by
Act of Parliament. I happen to be a
representative of a party that is continuously accused of preaching what is
commonly termed "class war," and there is
always held up to us the importance of
individual initiative and incentive for
gain and competition, a trinity of ideals
we are accused of being out to destroy.
Since I have taken my seat in this House
it has struck me that of the legislation
which the Government, as the representatives of the public and the safeguarders
of their health and well-being, have
found it absolutely necessary to bring before us, nine-tenths has been found
necessary because of this particular trinity
that is always being held up for our admiration and as being essential for the
welfare of society.
This Measure is nothing more or less
than an attempt on the part of the legislature to mitigate the suffering of a class
that has hitherto not been protected and
that is now going to have extended to it
the protection of the law. What for? To
protect it from greed and desire and a deliberate attempt to obtain gold and other
money at the risk of the life, limb, and
health of those who from day to day are
engaged in attending the necessary machinery to carry on the business.
I am surprised that a number of the
Members of the Government of Northern
Ireland and their supporters are from
platforms continuously endeavouring to
induce the people of Northern Ireland to
believe that this small party, the
Socialist party, is out to disseminate the
doctrine of class war. We are not out for
anything of the kind, but we are out for
the specific purpose of seeing to it that the
unfortunate working class are not overlooked and neglected. Not only the
legislature of Northern Ireland but the
legislatures of every civilized country in
the world are from day to day engaged
in introducing legislation to mitigate the
sufferings that necessarily accrue from
that particular warfare that we are out to
abolish if possible. I welcome this Bili
simply because its purpose is to protect
the most helpless army engaged in that
class war from accidents due to the negligence of those who have charge of this
machinery.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to ask the noble Leader of the
House if the provisions of this Bill will
apply to well-sinking. In order to sink
wells we often have to cut down through
stone, and I hope the Bill will not interfere with farmers who sink wells to get
spring water.
I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
would prefer to postpone until a later
stage of the Bill the answers to some of
the points that have been raised during
the course of the debate. It would be extremely difficult for me to define the dif
ference between a quarry from which
stones bigger than one could lift are obtained and a quarry from which stones
used by County Councils are procured. I
will consider that, as well as the question

Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation)
Act.
ENDORSEMENT OF LICENCES.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Hill
Dickson:-
To ask the Leader of the House if he
will state whether any convictions took
place as a result of the misleading circular
which was issued by the Ministry of Home
Affairs in respect of the Motor Vehicles
(Traffic and Regulation) Act, 1926; and,
if so, whether any steps are being taken
to remedy any injustice so done
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In putting the
question which appears in my name on the
Paper I desire to say that the word "conviction" should read "endorsement." It
was not the question of convictions I was
so much interested in. I do not mind a
man being convicted for committing an
ffence, but when a circular is issued to
magistrates by the Ministry of Home
Affairs which makes it appear to magisrates that when they have decided to conict they must endorse the person's licence,
and when the terms of that circular are
a
fterwards upset by the King's Bench
Division on appeal and the circular is
subsequently withdrawn by the Ministry
of Home Affairs, I think it is very unfair
to the persons who may have had their
licences endorsed in consequence of that
circular. If the magistrates had not received this circular and had not been
under the impression that they were bound
to endorse an offender's licence it might
not have been endorsed. When people
who have had their licences so endorsed
go to seek employment elsewhere the first
see
thing the prospective employer asks is to
their licence, and when he sees a
licence has an endorsement on it, it militates against the man's chance of employ.
ment.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
And quite right, too.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
hon. Member having made it clear that
the word "conviction' was wrongly used
in the question, and that he refers to endorsements, I will omit the first part of
the answer to the question, which was
based on the assumption that "conviction" was the word intended to be used.
From returns furnished to the Ministry it appears that between the dates on
which the two circulars were issued, 208
licences were endorsed. It is impossible
to say how many of these were endorsed
by the magistrates, by reason of the view
expressed by the Ministry that endorsement was compulsory. It may be admitted that in all probability a considerable proportion were so endorsed.
The question appears to suggest that
an injustice has been done, but this suggestion would seem to result from a misapprehension as to the effect of an endorsement of a licence under the Act.
Many people, no doubt, think that an
endorsement under this Act is analogous
to an endorsement under the Intoxicating
Liquor Acts, but this is quite fallacious.
Under the Intoxication Liquor Act it is
only convictions which are actually endorsed on the licence that entail consequences if the holder is subsequently
convicted. In other words if
licence-holder upon whose licence
two convictions have been endorsed is convicted a third time, his licence is forfeited. An endorsement under the
Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulations)
Act has no such effect.
a
The increased penalties for second or
subsequent offences depend on previous
convictions whether they are endorsed or
whether they are not. If the previous
conviction is not endorsed it is the duty
of the police to inform the court thereof
at the proper time, and to offer proof of
it. Endorsement on the licence is merely
a convenient form of record, which enables the previous conviction to be easily
proved, but it does not in any way preclude proof of the previous conviction
which has not been endorsedTGCTG
223 Motor Vehicles Act!
Endorsement of Licences 224
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
The convictions in these 208 cases did
not in any way depend upon the Ministry's
circular, and whether they were endorsed
or were not endorsed, the police, in the
case of subsequent conviction would be
bound to bring them to the notice of the
magistrates and to prove them in the
ordinary way.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of what has
just been said, I may perhaps remark
that I am very glad to find that a considerable interest is taken in this House
in the matter to which the question of
my hon. Friend refers. It has just been
admitted that in no less than 208 cases
something may have been done which
would not have been done but for a mistake made in the Ministry of Home
Affairs. We were told the day before
yesterday that the circular was issued on
the advice of the Ministry's legal advisers,
and I can only hope that those legal advisers, whoever they may be, will feel
something in the nature of repentance
when they learn of the consequences of
their mistaken action.
To suggest that there is a popular idea,
a popular mistake, of the effect of an
endorsement under the Motor Acts, and
that the situation is in any way comparable with that under the Intoxicating
Liquor Acts seems to me to be somewhat
absurd. The confusion, if any, does not
seem to be in the mind of the general
public. I am afraid I must say that I
think some confusion as regards this
matter has been in the minds of the Parliament of the United Kingdom and the
Parliament of Northern Ireland. I venture to say, when speaking on the Second
Reading of the Bill of 1926, that I had
never been able to clearly appreciate with
what object licences under the 1903 Act
were endorsed, because however many
endorsements a man had on his licence
under that Act, it did not in the least
prevent his getting another driving
licence. County councils had no power
to refuse to issue another licence merely
because his previous licence was plastered
with endorsements.
But there is this effect. If a man is
seeking employment and his licence is
endorsed he is far less likely to get that
employment; in fact the endorsement of
a licence is, as I think the Lord Chief
Justice said, a penal provision.
magistrates do become aware of the endorsement of a licence, a fact of which they
If
should be and are kept in the most complete ignorance until the question of conviction or acquittal is decided, they are
likely to consider, and I venture to say
I think they should consider, whether the
previous offence has been of so serious a
charcter that the bench who tried it felt
it right to endorse the licence, and we
should consider whether that does not lead
them to consider the imposition of a more
severe penalty than the minimum penalty
which happens to be provided in the Act
Certainly I, myself, would give effect to
that consideration.
The whole question of the production
of these licences in court seems to me to
be one of great difficulty. It is universally admitted that until a conviction has
been decided upon a licence cannot be
produced. It is equally universally held
that if a licence is produced to the court
it is the best possible evidence for satisfying the requirements of the Prevention of
Crimes Act, as being an abstract of the
proceedings of another court, and it
usually settles the question of identity.
But our 1926 Act does not provide any
satisfactory method of getting the licence
before the court.
There is a provision
that the holder of a licence must produce the licence in court if required, and
the requirement can only be by the court
itself. I think, reading our Act of 1926
with its predecessor of 1903, we are driven
to the conclusion that the only purpose
for which a court can possibly call for the
production of the licence is so that it may
be endorsed, in which case the licence in
most cases would never get to the bench
at all, but would simply be handed to the
clerk, who would make the necessary endorsement.
If a court were to require a man whom
they had just convicted to produce his
licence, to see whether or not it was endorsed, it seems to me they would be requiring the man to give evidence against
himself. As an old writer said-and I
am sure this will be familiar to the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Sharman Crawford)-
Magistrates must be very careful as
regards the common law lest it break
in upon them and level their proceedings.
I think that is one of the classics with
which magistrates ought to invest them.
selves. On the other hand it is obviously
27 OCTOBER 1927
Motor Vehicles Act

Workmen's Compensation Bill [S.]
Order for the House to be put into

most convenient that the licence should
be produced and if, as I have heard, a
police officer asks a man in court for his
licence, although I do not think the man
is bound to give it to him, if he does in
fact give it to him, the court cannot interfere to prevent the police officer from using it. Therefore I hope those points
Cd will attract the attention of the House or
the legislature, but in the meantime I
think we are satisfied that due care will
be taken in future to render, as far as
possible, absolutely impossible the recurrence of any mistake of this sort, which
has such far reaching consequences for so
many comparatively innocent people.

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
had not intended to intervene in this debate, but Senator Leslie always seems to
have the faculty of provoking me to get
up to say something. I cannot agree
with his views on this question at all.
The number of endorsements on licences
that has been quoted by the leader of the
House cannot be wholly ascribed to the
circular issued by the Ministry of Home
Affairs Speaking for the bench
which I sit, I can say that 90 per cent.
of the endorsements put on licences are
put on solely by the desire of the bench,
and not in consequence of the circular
issued by the Ministry. I think it is
most desirable that in every case of a first
offence the licence should be endorsed.
In the end it is the quickest and easiest
way, because otherwise the police might
have to spend some considerable time
searching the records to ascertain whether
a man had been before the court before
or not.
mo
also think it is in the interests of the
Lor driver himself, because if his
licence is not endorsed for a first offence
hemight go and commit similar offences
in Other parts of the country, where they
would be regarded as first offences.
hope all benches of magistrates will follow the example of my bench, and endorse
licences for all first offences.
I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think
I can give the assurance Senator Leslie
asks for that as far as the Home Office
can preclude the possibility of anything
of the same nature happening again they
will do so.

Bill.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

Mr. LESLIE: Before I move the first
of the Amendments which stand in my
name, as the one who had the honour to
act as Chairman of the Joint Committee,
there are two things I should like to say.
First I should like to remind the Committee that when speaking on the Second
Reading of this Bill I drew attention to
two matters, the cases of adopted children
and a special class of illegitimate children,
particularly the illegitimate girl between
the ages of 15 and 16. Those matters, I
ought to tell the House, were carefully
considered by the Joint Committee, and
it was decided that the Bill before us
made no alteration in the existing law,
and that those two questions were in no
way affected by the Bill before us. That
really appears in the second paragraph
of the Report laid on the Table yesterday,
with special reference to the cases at issue.
The other matter on which I should like
to speak on behalf of the Committee is to
express our great obligation to Sir Arthur
Queckett, partly for the careful marginal
notes with which he furnished us and
which shortened our task very much, and
partly for the evidence he gave before us
and the memorandum he submitted.
Without that assistance our task, as indeed I think you, Mr. Deputy-Chairman,
will agree as you were a member of the
Committee, would have been very much
more onerous, and I am not sure that we
H
227 Workmen's Compensation Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
would have discharged it so well as we
hope we have done.
Passing to the first Amendment, I
move that in Clause 3, page 4, line 10,
to leave out the word "Force" (Royal
Ulster Constabulary Force). It is merely
because the expression Royal Ulster Constabulary is the proper expression, being
the expression used in the Statute establishing the Force. I beg to move.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I should like
to take this opportunity of very heartily
concurring with the remarks of Senator
Leslie as regards the great assistance Sir
Arthur Queckett gave the Joint Committee. The fact of the matter is that
the matters which came before the Committee were largely of a legal character,
and the time of the Committee was largely
saved owing to the fact that we had in
the chair a lawyer of the ability of
Senator Leslie, and another able legal
representative in the person of Mr. J. C.
Davison, K.C., M.P. Both these Members had taken the trouble to go carefully
through all the previous Bills dealing with
this subject, and they had compared them
very thoroughly with the Bill now before
the House. That action saved the Committee a great deal of time.
The
memorandum and notes which Sir Arthur
Queckett placed before us were also of the
greatest assistance, and I readily concur
with Senator Leslie in paying that tribute to Sir Arthur Queckett.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

compensation), 5 (Meaning of "employer"), 6 Liability in case of Workmen

(Rules for determining earnings), 11 (Reviews of weekly payments), 12 (Limitation of power of employer to end or
diminish weekly payments) and 13 (Redemption of weekly payments), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.

notice of accident and claim for compensation).
*

Provided that-
(a) the want of or any defect or inaccuracy in such notice shall not be
a bar to the maintenance of such
proceedings if the employer is proved
to have had knowledge of the accident from any other source at or
about the time of the accident, or if
it is found in the proceedings for
settling the claim that the employer
is not, or would not, if a notice or
an amended notice were then given
and the hearing postponed, be prejudiced in his defence by the want,
defect or inaccuracy or that such want,
defect or inaccuracy was occasioned
by mistake, absence from the United
Kingdom, or other reasonable cause;
and
(b) the failure to make a claim within
the period above specified shall not
be a bar to the maintenance of such
proceedings if it is found that the
failure was occasioned by mistake,
absence from the United Kingdom,
or other reasonable cause.

we see in large type Second Schedule."
