Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie: -
Clause 1, line 13, at end of Sub-section
(2), insert the following new Sub-section.
"(3) The fees prescribed by regulations made under this Act shall in no
case exceed the respective maximum
amounts set out in the Schedule to this
Act."
Mr. LESLIE: We now approach
another matter dealing with a point which
attracted some attention during the
Second Reading, the question of fees to be
charged those people who are accepting the
encouragement held out by the Ministry
of Home Affairs and the Ministry of Agriculture to engage in the supply of superexcellent milk. It was pointed out on the
Second Reading that before a man applied
for a licence he was not unlikely to want
to know what fee he was going to have to
pay for his licence. I think it was thought
to be not unreasonable that a man wishing
to get a licence under an Act of Parliament would want information as to the fee
he was to pay and that he would refer to
the Act of Parliament for information on
the subject.
If there is a man outside, shall I say the
circle of the favoured five, who wishes to
supply this milk under these conditions
and he wants to know what fee he has to
pay, how is he to find out? It is the
ordinary rule when anything in the nature
of taxation is proposed to put the rate of
taxation definitely into the Measure in
order that Parliament may get the control
which it ought to have, and the same
practice is followed usually as regards fees.
It is usual to insert the limitation as to
fees. In the case before us there can be
no difficulty in setting out in the schedule
of the Bill the fees which are going to be
charged.
Hon. Members who examine the Bill
will see that as it stands at the moment
no date is named for the commencement of
the Bill.
It therefore follows that the
moment it receives the Royal Assent anybody supplying T.T. milk without a licence
-and he will not have a licence-will be
committing an offence. The only protection that man will have will be the protection in Sub-clause 5, which states that a
prosecution shall not be instituted except
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
with the consent of the Ministry of Home
Affairs. But the fact that the Bill comes
before us in that condition seems to me to
indicate fairly plainly that the department
must know to-day what fee they propose to
charge and for what services they propose
to charge it. Therefore I cannot see any
objection to inserting in the Bill
schedule showing what the fees are and
what for, in order that Parliament may
have an opportunity of examining those
fees and saying whether they are too much
or too little.
a
The point was made by Senators other
than myself on the Second Reading, and
no doubt we shall have the advantage of
hearing their present views on the subject.
For the moment I can see no suggestion
that could be usefully made other than
the proposal to enumerate the fees in the
schedule and to attach them to the Bill.
The suggestion I have made is that the
fees should be inserted in maximum, in
order, of course, that the Ministry should
not be deprived of the possibility of revising their fees on an upward or downward
scale. Provision as regards the assessment
of these fees is, as you will see from
Sub-section 7, left to the Ministry of
Home Affairs, subject to the approval of
the Ministry of Finance
Although I believe the Ministry of
Home Affairs, probably with the great
assistance of the Ministry of Agriculture,
will do everything in their power to keep
these fees down to the irreducible limit, as
the Ministry of Finance is to be brought
into it and is entitled to a voice in the
settlement, it has occurred to me whether
that Ministry will not be disposed to see
that special steps are taken to ensure that
the business under this Bill is self-supporting, and it may very well be that the
Ministry of Finance may take a far more
severe view in the matter of fees than
either the Ministry of Home Affairs or the
Ministry of Agriculture. I cannot help
thinking that it is for Parliament to interpose and exercise its authority and to
say definitely how far those fees shall
go and at what point they are to stop.
Accordingly I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I cannot say anything more about
this question than point to the fact that
it was fully debated in another place,
when a specific pledge was given by the
Prime Minister that in prescribing the
fees the utmost care would be taken to
ensure that they would be calculated on
a basis having relation only to the cost of
the service of inspection, and that under
no circumstances would any profit be
made out of the fees. The Members in
another place accepted the Government's
assurance on that point. Perhaps in my
answer to this Amendment I may be able
to tack on a little about the infallibility
of Government Departments, and also
about the Act being administered harshly
in connection with this and the preceding
Amendment. If the Bill was administered harshly or the cost of the fees was
too high it would defeat the very object
of the Measure.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount
having appealed to those who have
authority, and those who wield that power
having incurred such responsibilities as
there may be in passing the proposal, it
would not be for us to continue the discussion.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
1, to leave out from the word "fine,"
in line 16, on page 2, to the word
،،
not" in line 17 (of two pounds or
any greater sum). This is another problem-the problem of the minimum fine.
The problem of the minimum fine
has engaged the attention of this House,
either sitting as a House or as a Committee, on more than one occasion of late.
Since the Parliament of Northern Ireland
was established it has engaged the attention of both Chambers on more than one
occasion. Strong remarks are to be found
in the Reports of the proceedings in another place emanating from the Government Bench, and pointing out the impropriety of interfering with the discretion of magistrates by requiring minimum
fines to be inflicted. A statement made
by the noble Leader in this House is on
record that the Government are not
wedded to the principle of the minimum
fine, yet here we find in a Measure dealing with a perfectly new subject, a
matter which magistrates have had no
opportunity of dealing with, a proposal
for a minimum fine.
In speaking on this subject on a former
occasion I pointed out that one of the
difficulties of dealing with the matter was
that whenever a proposal of a minimum
fine was made you had to examine
it not alone on its own particular
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1927

who
hat
biz
Drops
ue th
drav
cannot fail
to
merits. There is also the general
question of interfering with the discretion of magistrates, an interference
which anybody who moves about
the country or reads the newspapers
notice is greatly
resented by magistrates themselves. Expressions are to be found reported again
and again that magistrates feel they are
required to sit there, as I think one gentleman said, as the dummies of the Ministry
of Home Affairs. If that feeling grows
what will the effect be? It will be that
the class of man we most desire to have
as a magistrate will decline the honour,
or, if he accepts it, he will not go on the
Bench. If once you interfere with the
exercise of the discretion of a man in any
office you will find that the best men will
not take the office. I do not say you will
not have plenty of applicants for what is
very erroneously called "Appointment
to Justice of the Peace," but I do say
you are gravely risking the chance of
getting the best men.
mean.
Another thing I said when speaking
on this subject was that there were cases
to be found in the Statute Book where
minimum fines were justified, even in my
opinion, and it so happens that with care
and good management the case to which
I am now referring might have come
within that category. The standing case
is the case of what they call the Fishery
Act. I think it is under a different Act,
but everybody knows what I
Certain offences in connection with the
taking of fish are subject to a minimum
fine of £4. That Act has been on the
Statute Book for some time and can be
defended, and I have always myself been
prepared to defend it on the ground that
that offence against the law, besides being
an offence against the Crown in the
ordinary way, was to some extent an
offence against private rights. That is,
there is a certain wrong done by the use
of illegal instruments in fishing to those
who have the legal right to take fish in a
legal way.
Similarly in this Measure if a man who
has not got a licence to sell T.T. milk
does purport to sell it he is not only offending against the Statute, but he is doing
something in the nature of wrong to the
man who is lawfully supplying T.T. milk.
If the minimum fine had been limited to
cases of that sort I should not have had
anything to say against it. Indeed, being
anxious to encourage the supply of this
super-excellent milk in every way that
would induce men to make a success of it
I think I would support it. Unfortunately
the provision does not relate only to that.
It will catch the person who contravenes
in any way the conditions of the licence.
That sort of contravention is purely a
matter for the discretion of the Court
which hears the evidence and tries the
case.
If
I am sorry that we have not in this
House-a House which we endeavour to
model on the principles and practice of
another Second Chamber across the
water; I hope on the principles of that
Chamber as it was last century rather
than to-day-we have not copied that
Chamber by the introduction of what I
understand is called a Protest Book.
there was such a book I would long ago
have recorded my protest against the insertion of anything which impaired the
discretion of a legally constituted court.
Again and again I have suggested that
if you cannot trust your magistrates to
administer the laws faithfully and properly, or "indifferently" as in an ancient
expression which I think I noted the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel
Sharman Crawford) using, if you cannot
trust them change them and make some
other arrangement, but as long as you
have a court to administer the King's
law do not impair its discretion, but leave
it to do the work for which it was appointed. Therefore, I am obliged to
oppose this proposal, although had it
been limited in the manner I have indicated I should certainly not have troubled
the Committee with this Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I think that if ever
there was a Bill brought before the Committee that justified the Government in
establishing a minimum fine for transgressions of that Bill when it became law
it is this Bill. I should have expected
that Senator Leslie would have been disposed to propose an Amendment to increase the minimum fine in this particular case rather than to eliminate it
from the Bill. I have no desire to cast
any reflection upon magistrates, but we
all know that magistrates of Petty
Sessions in every part of Ireland, in adjudicating in cases of offences against
such Acts as this, often come to a decision by a majority vote. I am perfectly
satisfied that occasionally cases do occur
of a majority being artificially manu-
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
factured in favour of some individual who
may be brought before a bench to answer
for an offence in an area where he may be
able to exercise some influence. I do appeal
to the Government not to agree to the
abolition of the minimum fine from this
Measure, because I think that instead of
being taken out of the Bill it should be
increased to £5.
Mr. LONG: I have a certain amount
of sympathy with the Amendment proposed by Senator Leslie, but I regret to
say I cannot go all the way with him.
It seems to me there are two classes of
offences possible under this Bill, namely,
fraudulent contravention of the conditions of a licence and violation of the provisions of the Statute which might be due
to pure carelessness or negligence. It
seems to me that some differentiation
should be made in those two classes of
offences, and therefore I suggest that if
the hon. Senator altered his Amendment
to confine the operation of the minimum
fine to fraudulent contraventions of the
Act, and eliminate it as regards other
offences it might meet with the approval
of the Government.
VISCOUNT
CHARLEMONT:
I
would prefer not to enter into an argument with Senator Leslie on the question
of the minimum fine itself. As regards
the introduction of a Protest Book, I do
not think that is at all necessary because
immediately a minimum fine is mentioned
Senator Leslie jumps up, so I do not
think it is necessary for him to inscribe
his protest with pen and ink.
As regards this particular Amendment,
I am prepared to meet both Senator
Leslie and Senator Long in differentiating between the two different classes of
offenders, the actually fraudulent person
and the merely forgetful one. I would
therefore suggest that Senator Leslie's
Amendment should stand, and at the end
of Sub-section (3) on page 2 of the
Clause there should be added:-
Provided that where a person not
having a licence granted by the Ministry
of Home Affairs under this Act is convicted of selling milk, or exposing or
offering milk for sale in contravention
of the foregoing provisions of this
Section, he shall be liable for the first
offence to a fine of not less than two
pounds, and for a second or subsequent
offence to a fine of not less than five
pounds.
That would differentiate between the
fraudulent and the negligent man, and if
Senator Leslie will agree to that I will
accept his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: If it were physically
possible I would almost leap the Table
to receive the suggestion of the noble
Viscount. It seems to me to be most admirably adapted to meet the case, and it
corresponds exactly with the differentiation I myself desired. Therefore, I will
certainly accept it. I take it the position
now is that the noble Viscount will accept
on behalf of the Government my Amendment on the Paper and that which follows,
and that when we reach line 22 he will
move his own Amendment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT indicated assent.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I formally move the
consequential Amendment, in line 18,
page 2, to leave out from the word "fine"
to the word "not" in line 19 (of five
pounds or any greater sum).
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, in Clause 1, page 2, line 22, at
the end of Sub-section (3) to add :-
Provided that where a person not
having a licence granted by the Ministry
of Home Affairs under this Act is convicted of selling milk, or exposing or
offering milk for sale in contravention
of the foregoing provisions of this
Section, he shall be liable for the first
offence to a fine of not less than two
pounds, and for a second or subsequent offence to a fine of not less than
five pounds.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:-
Line 23, leave out from the beginning
of the line to "such" in line 29, and
insert: "For the purposes of this Act
it shall be lawful for any authorized
officer to take and submit for analysis
or for bacteriological or other examination samples of any milk offered or exposed for sale, and for such purposes
to enter any premises upon which he has
reason to believe that
Sale of Milk Biil -
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1927
T

I

d

a
Mr. LESLIE: Since I put this Amendment on the Paper I have had the advantage of
consultation with those
officers of the Ministry of Home Affairs
and another Ministry who are likely to be
concerned in the administration of this
Measure, and I learn from them that the
purpose for which I put down my original
Amendment as it stands on the Paper
would not really be of any great assistance. That is to say, they do not contemplate exercising the power which I
proposed to give them. Accordingly all
that it is now necessary for us to do is to
leave out of line 24 on page 2 the word
"expert" (for expert examination), and
to insert "analysis or for bacteriological
or other." That means that a sample
may be taken and treated in exactly the
same way as is provided for in the case of
samples across the water. I venture to
think that that will be rather better than
using the expression "expert examination" which is not defined and is perhaps
open to difficulty. I beg to move accordingly.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: Would the hon. Senator
read out the lines as they will then
stand?
Mr. LESLIE: Certainly.
Any authorized officer shall have
power to take and submit for analysis
or for bacteriological or other examination samples of any milk offered or
exposed for sale
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
prepared to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:
Clause 1, at end of Sub-section (4) insert the following new Sub-section :-
"(4) (a) The result of an analysis or
bacteriological or other examination of
a sample of milk taken under this Act
shall not be admissible as evidence in
proceedings under this Act, or in proceedings under the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts, 1875 to 1907, unless the
provisions of the last-mentioned Acts
which relate to the division of samples
into parts are complied with, but if
those provisions have been complied
with, the result of the analysis shall be
available for proceedings under the
said Acts (as if it had been procured
in accordance with those Acts), as well
as for proceedings under this Act."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it will
be necessary to trouble the Committee at
any length with this Amendment. I
will formally move it for the sake of keeping myself in order, but if the noble
Leader of the House can assure us that
this T.T. milk will only be sold in sealed
bottles, and transported in sealed bottles,
I think that would dispose of the difficulty
one feels as regards the abandonment of
the ordinary provisions of the Sale of
Food and Drugs Acts. In that case I
should ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Milk
will only be sold and transported in sealed
bottles.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: Now I pass to the
following Amendment :---
In Clause 1, at the end of line 39, after
Sub-section (4) insert the following new
Sub-section :-
"At the hearing of the information
in any proceedings under this Act the
production of a certificate purporting
to be signed by the person to whom any
sample of milk taken under this Act
has been submitted for analysis or
bacteriological or other examination,
shall be sufficient evidence of the facts
stated therein, unless the defendant
shall require that such person shall be
called as a witness."
I think this proposal, which is, of
course, to bring in a practice which is
common under the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts is one which should go into
this Bill. It will save expense. If a
man is summoned for a contravention of
the provisions of the Act, and if it is
necessary to produce a certificate, I think
we should provide that the production of
a certificate purporting to be signed by
the person who made the examination,
shall be accepted as evidence, unless the
defendant requires that the person who
made the examination shall appear as a
witness. I think it is desirable that this
should be inserted in the Bill, and I move
accordingly.
Sale of Milk Bill--
THE SENATE
Committee
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
After Sub-section (4) of Clause 1, insert
the following new Sub-section :
"A warranty or invoice shall be
available as a defence in a prosecution
for an offence against this section in any
case where a warranty or invoice would
be so available if the prosecution had
been taken under the Sale of Food and
Drugs Acts in respect of the sale of an
article of food, and section twenty-five
of the Sale of Food and Drugs Act,
1875, and section twenty of the Sale of
Food and Drugs Act, 1899, shall apply
accordingly."
That is a simple protection for the
honest retailer, and I think it ought to go
into the Bill. The provisions of the two
sections it is proposed to apply are well
known. The principle is that the seller
who proposes to rely upon the defence of
selling stuff in the same condition in which
he received it must give notice to the prosecution. It is a provision that has been
found of use on the sade of Food and
Drugs Acts, and it is required in the interests of the retailer. It must be remembered that under this Bill not only
the man who is responsible for the production of the milk, the man who has
got the byre and the cows, but the retailer in Belfast or elsewhere to whom he
sells the milk in sealed bottles will also
need a licence.

It is readily comprehensible that that
man might be held responsible for an
offence he did not commit unless he had
this protection. Therefore, I suggest
we should insert this Sub-clause, which is
not in its wording original. I cannot
claim credit for its invention, but I suggest we should insert it in the Bill.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie: -
Line 40, leave out from "prosecution"
to "under" in line 41.

Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title).
This Act may be cited as the Sale of
Milk Act (Northern Ireland), 1927.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 2, to insert the following
new Sub-section :-
"(2) This Act shall come into operation on the second day of January,
Nineteen hundred and Twenty-eight."
In this Amendment I propose that this
Act shall come into operation on 2nd
January, 1928. I think it is necessary
that we should insert a date for the commencement of the Act, and I need scarcely
remind the Committee that the fact that
it does not commence until the date suggested will not deter the Ministry and
others from taking the necessary preliminary steps to bring the Act into
operation. I imagine that the Department are prepared to accept 2nd January,
1928, as giving sufficient time, and therefore I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
Government will accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I now beg to move to
insert at the end of Clause 2 the words,
"and shall continue in force for three
years and no longer, unless Parliament
otherwise determines."
We have already spoken more than once
about this proposal, and I am hoping we
shall be assisted with an expression of
the views of hon. Senators upon it. The
idea is, of course, that we should give
the Bill a limited duration in order that
we may see whether it is a success or not;
whether it is possible to satisfy the public
demand for T.T. and other milk, or
whether it is found in fact that when this
Measure has become an Act the public demand is still unsatisfied. We can then
consider whether perhaps that is due to
the more or less stringent provisions we
have inserted in the Bill.
Sale of Milk Bill-
Committee
2 NOVEMBER 1927


a
The other point of view, which I expect
some other hon. Member will refer to, is
that it might be said that if we limit the
operation of the Bill to three years we
should be doing something that might
discourage a man from taking out
licence. He might say he would not build
his byre and buy his cows if the protection is only to last for three years. I am
extremely doubtful which way the chief
difficulty lies, but I still do not like a
certain provision which I mentioned before. On the other hand, the objection
to that may, as often happens, disappear
with practical experience, and for the
moment I will put before the Committee
the desirability of inserting the words,
which will have the effect of limiting the
operation of the Measure to three years,
unless, of course, Parliament otherwise
determines.

It is very likely that before the three
years are out Parliament may be dealing
not only with the question of T.T. milk,
but the whole question of the milk supply,
and if that did happen it is not unreasonable to suppose that this Bill would
be repealed and incorporated in the new
Measure. As a matter of fact, the Committee is probably aware that the corresponding English enactment to this Bill
is part of the Milk and Dairies Amendment Act, and it may very well be that
in the future we shall be following on the
same lines. I, therefore, beg to move
this Amendment, and I do not think its
existence in the Bill would create
great many difficulties.
a
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I shall have to resist this Amendment. If the Bill proves to be successful
the operations under it will develop. If
on the other hand producers find it impossible for them to comply with the prescribed conditions, they will simply cease
to apply for licences, and the Department
will find that their Bill has failed in its
object. If it became generally known
that the Bill was to have a life of only
three years very few people would go to
the trouble of building up herds which
immune from tuberculosis. For
those reasons I must oppose the Amendwere
ment.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount has
taken the other view, and I do not
desire to press the matter against the
advice he has given.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put, That Clause 2 as amended
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to draw
attention to the Amendment which
stands in my name at the end of the
Paper. I should like to take the opportunity of explaining that both that
Amendment and an earlier one which I
did not move were put down on the Paper
originally as matters of precaution in case
the acceptance of other alterations in the
Bill might have rendered it necessary to
make those Amendments. They were
not put down on the presumption that
they were originally left out of the Bill.
It was a possibly necessary precaution.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: If I am in
order I would like to give the Committee
some facts as to what has already been
done in County Down in connection with
the supply of tubercular-free milk. This
Bill will, undoubtedly, make a great improvement in the milk supply, but I
would not like it to go out that nothing
has already been done to secure cows free
from tuberculosis. The following are the
amounts paid for the year ending 30th
September last for animals slaughtered in
County Down:-
£
S.
d.
166 animals slaughtered 1,213 26
Veterinary Surgeons'
Fees paid
384 17 0
Total £1,597 19 6
That is for cows and other animals that
have shown signs of tuberculosis in
County Down alone. I take it that other
counties have expended sums in respect of
the matter. My only reason for rising
was to show that we have been doing something in the past in this matter.

Preamble and Title agreed to.

CLAUSE 1 (General provisions as to
safety).
*
For the prevention of dangerous
accidents, and for ensuring the safety
of persons employed, in every quarry,
the following provisions shall have
effect:-
(a) The working of the sides and of the
overburden or tops of the quarry
shall be carried on so as to prevent
dangerous falls of material;
(b) The top of the quarry, if dangerous, and the top of every shaft
shall be kept securely fenced;
(c) If quarrying work is carried on
after dark, the quarry shall be
under the supervision of a competent person appointed by the
owner, occupier or agent for the
،،
purpose;

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 1, line 11, to leave out the
words " or tops." The point is that the
expression or tops is perfectly well
understood in the Rules made under the
Metalliferous Mines Act, but I think they
are a little inappropriate here. They are
apparently used as an alternative to the
word overburden," which is defined for
us in the ordinary dictionary manner in
Clause 30 of the Bill, and does not relate
only to anything that overhangs
quarry after the manner of a snow cornice in an Alpine mountain The word
"overburden" is perfectly clear by itself,
and I think the introduction of the two
words, " or tops' would be rather superfluous. I beg to move.
a
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
prepared to accept that Amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
may be out of order, but I understand
that all the Amendments on the Order
Paper are accepted by the Government,
and if the hon. Senator (Senator Leslie)
would confine himself to formally moving
the Amendments it would mean a great
saving of time.

That is the first part of the Amendment,

And any opening or shaft made for

Those are the words I want inserted. The

Also it is desirable to get in the two
words, "if dangerous." I beg to move.

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie : 一
Clause 4, page 5, line 14, leave out from
"a" to "may" in line 15 (resident magistrate appointed under the Constabulary
(Ireland) Act, 1836), and insert instead
thereof the word "justice."
Mr. LESLIE: This is a point of somewhat greater importance
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
But it is accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: Even so, perhaps I
ought to explain why I have put down
the Amendment. I am sorry if I shall
not be able to interest the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman
Crawford).
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
Nor the Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: But I cannot help feeling that some explanation ought to be
offered to the Committee of these Amendments.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member is quite justified in giving an
explanation of his Amendments; otherwise how can the Committee know the
reason for the Amendments.
Mr. LESLIE: The point here is that
this Sub-section, as we shall see in reading it, is only to come into operation if
something is going on in a quarry which
in the opinion of the inspector involves
imminent danger to life. Anything of
that sort must be dealt with immediately,
and it is therefore necessary, I think, that
the appropriate remedy should be found
at the earliest possible moment. Quarries,
as a rule, are situated in the country,
very often quite a long way from a town,
and Courts of Summary Jurisdiction in
the country usually sit only once a month.
It is therefore not very much use waiting
to apply to a Court of Summary Jurisdiction when you are dealing with a case
of imminent danger to life.
Resident Magistrates appointed under
the Act of 1836 are not usually to be
found in a spot immediately adjacent to
a
quarry. Therefore, I substitute for
"Resident Magistrate" the word
"Justice" in order that any Justice of
the Peace having jurisdiction in the
a
county concerned may, if necessary, make
this Order. He cannot do much harm.
The most he can do is to give an Interim
Order suspending operations until
Court of Summary Jurisdiction does sit,
and he has this advantage over a Resident
Magistrate, who may be residing in
Belfast or some other large town, that he
can himself go and see the quarry before
he makes the Order. I am advised that
the conferring of this power on ordinary
magistrates is more in accordance with the
Factories and Workshops Act than limiting it to Resident Magistrates. I think
the point in suggesting Resident Magistrates was to prevent any possibility of
local influence, but I do not think that
ought to weigh against the urgency of
imminent danger to life.
Mr. MCDOWELL: I do not think the
argument of the hon. Senator is in conformity with the speech he delivered the
other day. We were then led to believe
that it very frequently occurred that
benches of magistrates were packed for a
specific purpose. If that is so I do not
see why the hon. Senator wants to do
away with Resident Magistrates. For
this reason I shall vote against the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot charge my
memory with the speech the hon. Senator
refers to, but I do not think I made any
suggestion against magistrates. My remarks have always been rather on the
other side, I ought perhaps to explain
that this is not a case for a bench of magistrates at all. This application will be
made ex parte to the selected magistrate
very much in the same way as an application for a writ of Habeas Corpus may
be made to a judge. It may be made
to a magistrate in his bedroom or diningroom. The only point is that it shall be
made as speedily as possible.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: Has not the
hon Member (Senator Leslie) made a mistake in drafting the Amendment to leave
out from "a to may." Should it
not be to leave out from "or" to "a."
،،
Mr. LESLIE: The effect of the alteration will be that the Sub-clause will run
like this, if the Committee accepts the
Amendment: -
Where a complaint has been made
under this Section, the Court, or a
justice may, on application ex parte...
and so on.

Sir GEORGE CLARK: I see.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am

VISCOUNT
CHARLEMONT:
I
should like to refer at this point to the

present in existence.

will carry out the provisions of this Bill.
I

should have said that at the present time

CLAUSE 16 (Power of inspector to
conduct proceedings before justices).
An inspector, if so authorized in
writing by the Ministry of Commerce,
may, although he is not a barrister-atlaw or solicitor, prosecute, conduct or
defend before a court of summary jurisdiction or resident magistrate any information, complaint or other proceeding arising under this Act or in the
discharge of his duty as inspector.

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
"(4) Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, nothing done by
virtue of any power under section one
hundred and sixty-two of the Grand
Jury (Ireland) Act, 1836 (as adopted
by the Local Government Adaptation
of Irish Enactments Order, 1899), as
extended by section twelve of the Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1898, shall

of this Act as against the owner,

provision would have so applied had

power.")
Mr. LESLIE: I am suggesting here as
a new Sub-clause the words on the Paper.
The point is, that, as most hon. Members
are aware, there are compulsory powers
now under the Local Government Act,
1898, inherited, so to speak, from the old
Grand Jury Act, under which a person
may enter upon the lands of another
without his consent, chiefly in connection
with the mending of roads. It seems
only fair that a man who has no choice in
the matter should be protected by such
words as I suggest against such a possibility as the place entered being deemed
to be a quarry for the purposes of this
Act and the man himself becoming liable
to the provisions of the Act, which might
perhaps involve expense. It is a perfectly ordinary protective Clause which I
think the Committee might desire to
insert.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
The hon. Senator has not given us the
full information he generally gives us on
most Amendments.
Mr. LESLIE: Why? (Laughter.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
I do not know what the effect of the
Amendment will be. County councils
experience considerable difficulty from
persons who object to the officials going
on to their lands for stones for road mending, and I should like to know whether
this Amendment will take away from
county councils the power they have to
go into quarries on the side of the road
for the purpose of procuring stones. A
Bill will be introduced in another place
later on to give county councils additional powers in this respect, and I do
not know whether this Bill will in any
way clash with the provisions of the Bill
to be introduced at a later date. I hope
Senator Leslie will be able to assure me
K

that the Bill before the Committee does
not interfere with the present powers of
county councils.
Mr. LESLIE: The proposed Subsection will not interfere in any way with
the existing powers of county councils.
If it has any effect at all as regard county
councils it will be to render the exercise
of their powers more easy by removing
possible causes of objection on the part
of the owner.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: I have to move the
Amendment standing in my name which
relates to a really purely formal matter.
I should explain to the House that the
Amendment has been put down in response to representations which were
received from the authorities in England
after the Bill had passed through the
hands of the Joint Committee, and after
that committee had reported to this
House. I may say that I have had an
opportunity of consulting the greater
number of my colleagues on that committee and that we are all in agreement that
the Amendment should be made, out of
deference to the English authorities, with
whom, as the matter is a reserved service,
we are naturally anxious to agree. Had
those partcular words been suggested to
us when sitting as a committee we would
have accepted them at that time. I now
ask the House to make the necessary
correction,

Housing Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In rising to move the
Second Reading of this Bill, I should like,
first of all, to inform the House that the
object of the Bill is firstly, to alter and
in some cases increase the Government
grants and otherwise continue the operation of the Housing Acts for another year,
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE

[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
and secondly, to extend the powers of
local authorities in connection with the
210sale of houses.
1S
Hon. Members are aware that, as the
law now stands, the existing Housing Acts
come to an end in March of next year.
Under these Acts grants are made in respect of houses coming within a minimum
of 500 superficial feet and a maximum of
950, and a free grant of £80 is given by
the Government where the house
erected within six months from the date
of commencement, and £60 where a longer
period is taken. Further, local authorities are empowered either to give a loan of
£100 free of interest for twenty-five years
and to remit the rates up to a period of
ten years, or to give a free grant of £40
and to remit the rates for a like period.
The Government now feel that the need
for the larger type of house has been met
to a great extent, and that subsidies in
respect of this type shall cease. A great
deal, however, still remains to be done to
provide houses of the type which is
within the means of the working man and,
accordingly, it is proposed in this Measure
to subsidize houses of not more than 650
superficial feet. In addition, the subsidy
will be increased from £80 to £100 in the
case of those houses completed within six
months from the date of commencement,
and from £60 to £80 where the house
takes longer to build. This is contained
in Clause 1 of the Bill.
It will be noted that the grants will be
payable in respect of houses commenced
on or after 1st August, 1927, and completed before 1st April, 1929, and that
local authorities will retain the power to
make supplemental grants or loans free
of interest, and to exempt houses from
payment of rates for a period not exceeding ten years. The Bill also expressly
provides that the arrangements made
therein are not to affect in any way the
grants to the Sailors' and Soldiers' Land
Trust for the erection of ex-Servicemen's
houses.
I come now to the second Section of
the Bill, which is rather technical and
somewhat difficult to follow. Hon.
Members will, I trust, bear with me if
I appear somewhat tedious in endeavouring to explain the purpose of its provisions in simple language. As I have said
before, local authorities may subsidize
the building of a house in alternative
ways; that is, they may give a loan of
£100 free of interest and repayable in
25 years, or they may give a free grant
of £40. The relationship which these
figures bear to each other will be apparent
when I explain that it costs the local
authority the same amount of money to
give the loan free of interest as it does
to give the free grant. It will also be
helpful if hon. Members will bear in
mind that the proportion of 100 to 40 is
as 5 is to 2.
of a
Under Section 1 of the Housing Act of
1924 it was provided that local authorities
might sell houses erected by them at
the price which they actually cost to
build, less the amount of the Government
grant and the local authority grant, if
the house happened to be in an area
where the local authority had agreed to
give a subsidy. For example, if the cost
house was £370, the Government
subsidy being £100 and the local
authority subsidy £40, the purchaser
would pay £370, less £140; that is £230.
The usual method by which this £230
was provided, the purchasers generally
being people without much capital, was
for the local authority to require the
payment in cash of a small sum, say £10,
the balance being advanced to the
purchaser, to be repaid by instalments
with interest over a period of 35 years.
sum
Under this system, as hon. Members
will appreciate, the repayments made in
the early years of the period consisted
chiefly of the interest due, and the capital
outstanding was reduced very
slowly. Thus a considerable number of
years had to elapse before the purchaser
could feel that he had paid off a substantial portion of his debt, and that his
payments for the rest of the period of
35 years were rapidly reducing his capital
liability.
The object of the Clause in the present
Bill is to enable local authorities, if they
so desire, to finance these purchasers by a
somewhat different method which, without
placing any additional burden on the
rates, will be more advantageous to the
purchaser and will have the further merits
of clearing off the mortgage in a shorter
period and of enabling the annual payments by the purchaser to be put in reduction of the capital outstanding, rather
than in repayment of interest!
ding


Second Reading
Housing Bill-
10 NOVEMBER 1927

give
dre
pwh
costs
tof
rest a
of 100

ually

rity p

that'
hth

The
I mentioned earlier in my speech that
£40 in cash was equivalent to a free loan
of £100. The proportion of 5-2 being
observed, it follows that £140 in cash is
equivalent to a free loan of £350.
local authority, with its Government
grant of £100 and its own subsidy of £40,
will be allowed under this Clause to give
the purchaser of a subsidy house a free
loan of £350, instead of, as in the past,
paying over a sum of £140 towards the
reduction of the gross price of the house.
I will, if I may, take a concrete example to show the effect of the new
proposal, as compared with the former
state of affairs.
If the gross cost of a
house was £370, under the Housing Act
of 1924 the authority would deduct the
grant of £140 and would sell the house at
£230. The purchaser would pay £10 in
cash and would borrow the balance,
£220, under the Small Dwellings Acquisition Act, making himself liable for the
repayment of £15 3s. 7d. per year
for a period of 35 years. Under
the new scheme, the £140 grant is
converted into an interest free loan of
£350. The price of the house, as before,
is £370 and the purchaser pays £10 cash,
being still liable for £360. But he is
given an interest free loan of £350, which
he pays back in twenty-five years at the
rate of £14 per year. He has also to
pay back the remaining £10, with interest, which involves him in a payment
of 15s 8d per year, making his total
annual repayments £14 15s 8d. Thus he
discharges his liability at a less cost, and
in twenty-five years instead of thirty-five
as formerly, and furthermore, he is able,
at any date during the twenty-five years,
to estimate his position with regard to repayments, instead of finding himself inextricably entangled in the difficulties of
Sinking Fund finance, as was the case
under the 1924 Act. I trust I have said
sufficient to indicate the general purport
of the Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I want, in the first
place, to draw attention to the fact that
the Bill, which we are now considering,
only came into the possession of some
hon. Senators late last night, and I want
once again to protest against being called
here for the purpose of considering a Bill
that I have not had sufficient time to
read and digest. Those of us who have
to follow our daily work and return
home after a hard day's work the night
before this House sits are not in a position to give proper and careful consideration to even short Measures, such as this.
In the first place, we are not provided
with the Acts of Parliament that may
be referred to in the new Measures
brought before us, and we are debarred
of the right of taking advantage of the
facilities afforded us in the Parliamentary
Library. Therefore, I again desire to
appeal that more consideration be given
to those of us who have not had the advantage of a legal training, and, therefore, require longer time to study the
Bills that are brought before us.
With regard to this Bill, I welcome it,
and I welcome the fact that the Government have realised the necessity of increasing the subsidy and encouraging the
building of houses that are within the
reach of the working classes. I noticed that
the Parliamentary Secretary of the Ministry of Home Affairs, referring to the model
house placed in the smoke-room for the
inspection of Members, made the statement that the model represented a house
of 500 superficial feet. I find that the
first Clause of the Bill is to enable
authorities in rural districts to erect
houses of 500 superficial feet, and houses
erected in other districts are required to
have an area of 550 superficial feet.
would like the noble Leader of the House
to tell us whether that model house will
be adopted in rural areas in Northern
Ireland in contravention of the first
Clause of this Bill. In the Bill it is distinctly laid down that houses erected in
Belfast must have an area of at least
550 superficial feet.
I
There is one matter to which no reference is made in the Bill, and about
which I would like to ask the noble Viscount a question. That is what will be
the average ground rent of these houses.
After all, I do not think it is possible for
the Government of Northern Ireland, or
any other Government to devise satisfactory housing schemes for the workingclasses to comply with the justifiable demand that the house rent ought to be in
proportion to a man's earnings if the
ground rent is not reasonable. If my
memory serves me right, before the War
the Belfast Corporation was about to
launch out on a housing scheme by means
of direct labour. I think they called
together the auctioneers and estate agents
and a special committee was set up and an
investigation made as to what was the
minimum cost at which they could secure
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
land for the erection of working class
houses. It was discovered that the
land inside the city boundary was £5,800
per acre.
to
After the War the Coalition Government set up a special Commission for the
purpose of considering and reporting
to Parliament what was the aggregate
number of working-class houses that
should be erected per acre. The
object of this was
lessen the
possibility of a recurrence of what happened during the War, when the medical
branch of the Army returned one-third of
the men who offered to enlist as unfit for
service. In other words they were Class
C3 men. This Commission came to the
conclusion that if the nation was to raise
healthy and physically perfect men in the
future, the erection of houses should be
restricted to a maximum of twelve houses
per acre. Assuming that the Belfast Corporation doubled the maximum recommended by that Commission and erected
twenty houses per acre at a cost of £5,000
per acre for the land, the ground rent
alone of each working man's house would
amount to 5s 6d per week.
In other words, the ground rent itself
would constitute the total amount that an
unskilled working-man ought to be asked
to pay for a house, because no workingman ought to be asked to pay more than
20 per cent. of his aggregate earnings for
house accommodation.
If he is compelled to pay a larger proportion than
that for house accommodation, it is
utterly impossible for any working man
to get sufficient of the necessaries of life
to maintain the standard of comfort that
prevails in his class of life. I hope the
noble Viscount will be able to give the
House an answer to my question, because
the figures he has given us are very
attractive. I can see that the scheme
can be taken advantage of by unskilled
workers, because there
agencies in the community that will place
facilities at the disposal of the working
classes to enable them to take advantage
of these increased subsidies and other
benefits that local authorities will be
entitled to give to prospective purchasers
of houses under this Bill, but much will
depend upon the ground rent per house.
are numerous
I regret that the space allotted to
houses to be erected under this Bill is not
greater. I tried last night to examine
the figures given by the Parliamentary
Secretary to the Ministry of Home
Affairs, and I discovered that the
smallest room in the model house that was
exhibited in the smokeroom should never
be occupied by more than one person at
a time. It contains the minimum air
space that Dr. Newman, who is an
authority on public health, states is
essential for sleeping accommodation.
That minimum should be at least 300
cubic feet, and according to Dr. Newman's work on public health the air in
the smallest room in that model house
would require to be renewed no less than
eight times an hour, or sixty-four times
during the night.
I can speak with authority on this particular subject, because I have had
peculiar opportunities of observing the
effect of insufficient housing accommodation upon the health of the working
classes. I know there are a large number
of people who think it is of very little
importance what cubic space there is in
the houses of the working classes.
As
I have said before in this House,
I happen to have been engaged for a
period of over forty years in an occupation
which gave me wide opportunities of observing the effects of overcrowding, and
insufficient space, upon the health of the
working classes.
I have known of a working man living
in a small house with a large family, and
I have watched the progress of that family
for 15, 20, 25, and sometimes over 30
years, and I state here without the possibility of any Member justifiably denying it, that the evil consequence of overcrowding and sufficient air space is not
manifested during the early years of the
life of a working class family. It generally manifests itself after the children of
that family have finished their time in a
skilled occupation, and when they are exposed to the effects of the stress and strain
of their work. They more easily fall victims to any diseases that are prevalent if
they have been reared in circumstances
where they have had insufficient accommodation. Consequently, much unneccessary suffering is inflicted upon the working classes.
Notwithstanding the fact that I regret
the insufficiency of the space allotted to
these houses, I do say that this Bill is
a step in the right direction. I hope local
Housing Bill-
10 NOVEMBER 1927

more

another speech will have to be made by
the noble Viscount.
authorities in Northern Ireland will take
advantage of the Bill and although I
know this will not meet with the approval
of many Members of this House will
launch schemes for the construction of
working class houses by means of direct
pelabour, because, in every instance where
local authorities in Great Britain have
erected working class dwellings by direct
labour, they have built those dwellings at a cheaper rate than they could
have been erected by contract, and competent authorities have stated that the
material and workmanship were far
superior to those in houses erected by private enterprise. While I support the
Bill I regret the cubic space in the houses
to be erected is not what it ought to be
to enable the working classes to rear

VISCOUNT
MASSEREENE MASSEREENE
AND
FERRARD: I regret that the hon. Member did not get his copy of the Bill until
late last night. If the hon. Member, at
the close of our sitting on Tuesday, had
gone to the library he would have obtained a copy of the Bill without any
trouble.
Mr. DORMAN: I took the trouble to
come here yesterday to try to get a copy
of this and the other Bill that is to come
before us to-day and I could not obtain
a copy.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I got a copy from the library
two days ago and I took it there were
other copies available for hon. Mem-
The hon. Member referred to the
bers.
model house as containing 500 superficial
feet. I understand that that house is somewhat over 500 superficial feet, and I think
that if the hon. Senator inspects it he will
find that what I say is correct. As regards
the question of ground rents, that is a
matter that is not referred to in this Bill
and therefore we cannot deal with it here.
This Bill is to enable houses to be built
and it has nothing to do with the question
of rents. I would like to say one word as
regards direct labour. My experience
when I had the privilege of being a nember of the London County Council a good
many years ago was directly contrary to
that of Senator Dorman.
Mr. LESLIE: I was anxious--
Mr. SPEAKER: It would be much
Mr. LESLIE: I was about to explain
that I did not rise before the noble Viscount because what I was going to say
would not require a reply.
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not think that
makes any difference. I think all the
speeches should be made before the
Government replies.
Mr. LESLIE: What I was about to
say was that I hoped that this advance,
or continued advance, along the lines of
the housing policy of the Government
would be noted outside the two Chambers
of Parliament as an indication of the fact
that this Parliament was engaged in
really big work in the interests of the
Province. I was somewhat afraid, as I
have no doubt other hon. Senators are
perhaps sometimes afraid, lest criticisms
which are directed in the newspapers,
and even for that matter in both Houses
of Parliament, against the Government
on more or less points of detail, details
sometimes in the Measures presented to
us, and sometimes in the administration
of those Measures, should attract an un
due amount of attention, and really big
matters, such as the housing policy of
the Government, should be lost sight of.
Persons have mentioned this Bill to
me, basing their remarks on what they
had seen in the newspapers during its
progress in another place, and I have
heard the opinion expressed uniformly
that the Bill might be of use in the rural
areas as providing for the possibility of
the further erection of houses of the type
that are most required. I have not
heard any criticizm of the superficial
area of the houses. On the contrary, I
have heard it said that the smaller the
house a man can do with the better, because there is more chance of the rent
being within his means. For that reason,
I welcome the Bill.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill
be now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Rent Restrictions Bill—
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Rent and Mortgage Interest Restrictions
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move the Second Reading of this Bill,
which is designed to cope with two
abuses which have arisen under the Rent
Restriction Acts. The Bill need not detain us very long, I think, and I shall
confine myself to a brief indication of the
remedies which it proposes to effect.
As this House is aware, a furnished
house is exempted in some respects from
the provisions of the Rent Restrictions
Act, a fact which has led to some landlords adopting a practice of putting in a
nominal amount of furniture and letting
their houses as furnished houses.
This
practice is clearly against the spirit of the
Acts and, accordingly, it is proposed, in
Clause 1 of the Bill now before you, that
a dwelling-house, the rateable value of
which is greater than £8 and which is subject to control, shall not be deemed to be
bona-fude let as a furnished house unless
it contains all such furniture as is reasonably required, having regard to the
character of the house. The same Clause
also provides that in future no house of
a valuation of £8 or under can, in any
circumstances, be let as a furnished house,
the reason being that tenants of these
small houses seldom need a furnished
house, and the landlord's action in letting
them as furnished is simply a pretext for
evading the restrictions of the Acts.
I come now to Clause 2. Hon. Members are aware that it is possible for a
landlord under the former Rent Restriction Acts, to obtain possession of a house
when it is required for residence by himself or by certain specified relatives. It
is provided in the Act of 1920, as amended
by the Act of 1925, that where a landlord
has so obtained possession of a house and
it transpires subsequently that the order
was made on misrepresentation or concealment of facts, the court may order the
landlord to pay the tenant adequate compensation for loss or damage incurred by
him as the result of the order.
Now this remedy only applies to cases
where the tenant has compelled the landlord to take him to court, and no provision
is made for the tenant who has received a
notice to quit, and who has come to the
conclusion that it is useless for him to
fight the matter, but who may, in fact,
have been induced to give up the house
on fraudulent representations. It is not
right that the tenant who has acted thus
should have no remedy, while the tenant
who forced his landlord into court should
be protected, and, accordingly, it is proposed in Clause 2 that the tenant shall
have his remedy, whether there have been
proceedings for possession or not.
It is also provided that the landlord,
when giving notice in writing to the tenant, shall state that he requires possession
of the house for himself or some specified
relation. It is hoped by this provision to
make it easier to obtain proof of misrepresentation on the part of the landlord,
whenever such has occurred.
I may say that the drafting of Subclause 2 of the second Clause does not
appear at present to be perfect, and I
hope, when the Committee stage is
reached, to move a slight amendment
which will make it clear that its provisions are not retrospective. I beg to
move.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to welcome this
Measure, because I think the conditions
that have been prevailing in Belfast
with regard to houses of the class dealt
with by this Bill have not been realised
as they should be. I, myself, know of
houses that in pre-war days were let at
3s 9d and 4s a week. The landlord has
placed in those houses furniture of the
value of 15s or £1 or 30s, and has then
charged the incoming tenant a rent of
from 15s to £1 a week on the pretence
that he was letting the house furnished.
The housing conditions in Belfast at
present are really very bad. I think
Belfast is probably the only large city
in Great Britain and Northern Ireland in
which one-room dwellers have increased
to the extent they have. For every oneroom dweller resident in Belfast before
the war there are to-day seven. That is
a shocking state of affairs to exist in any
progressive community, and it points to
the necessity of the Government and local
authorities concentrating their attention
on this problem.
Anyone who knows the congested
working-class areas cannot but be convinced that if the laws at present on the
309 Rent Restrictions Bill-

the
he c
been
ell
Statute Book were scientifically and
rigidly administered the conditions could
be improved. If an inspection of the
conditions of working-class dwellings in
Belfast was undertaken and a report was
published, it would shock the more fortunate section of the community to know
that so many of their fellow creatures
were living under such conditions. This
Measure is calculated to mitigate, in some
degree, those conditions inasmuch as it
will curtail the propensity of certain
landlords to take advantage of the housing shortage to profiteer at the expense
of those who have no alternative but to
pay the exorbitant rents demanded for socalled furnished houses.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
desire to say how pleased I am that the
Government have introduced this Bill.
It is a Bill intended for the protection
of the working classes and, what is most
regrettable, it is to protect them against
unscrupulous members of their own class.
I noticed that Senator Dorman was not
able to refer to the bloated Capitalists in
this connection.
Mr. DORMAN: I never used the term.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I said
he was not able in this connection to refer to the bloated capitalists. It is quite
true that in regard to the abuses which
this Bill seeks to remove a state of affairs
has existed in Belfast that is deplorable.
Some members of the working class have
imposed on those in much worse circumstances than themselves and they have
compelled them to pay large rents for
small houses in which they have placed
some furniture. There is one point I
would like to bring before the notice of
the Government. I understand there are
a good many instances in the city where
persons have taken large buildings and
sub-let them into smaller tenancies. They
have provided a certain amount of furniture for the different tenants and they
have charged them exorbitant rents.
would like to know if the provisions of
this Bill will apply to such cases to protect these poor people who are suffering
at, in many cases, the hands of workingclass landlords.
I
It is quite apparent that it is
the shortage of houses that is responsible
for all this crowding into houses and other
buildings. If there were sufficient houses
people would not be driven to accept the
conditions under which these small tenements are offered. This is a case of the
Government having to protect some of the
poorer members of the working class from
some of their own class who are imposing
upon them, and I would like to know if
this protection will extend to those persons
who occupy apartments that have been let
to them by persons who own large buildings which they have divided and sub-let
as furnished apartments.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to ask
how this Bill is going to affect those people
who have taken these furnished houses in
the past. Hon. Senators have referred
to the fact that houses with a few articles
of furniture in them have been letas
furnished houses, and I would like to
know whether the people who took those
houses and occupy them at present will
have to continue to pay the rent they are
now paying, or whether the Bill will enable them to obtain a reduction in their
rent. Take the case of a house which
was occupied by a tenant from before the
War up to last year.
The house then
became vacant and the landlord put into
it a sofa, a set of curtains, a table, and
perhaps a few pots and pans. Previous
to the War the rent of that house was
something like 5s a week, and with the
rise in the rates and the increase authorized by law, that rent was increased to
7s 6d or 8s a week. When the house
becomes vacant and the landlord puts
those few articles of furniture into it he
lets it at 25s a week. I would like to see
tenants who have taken houses under
those circumstances protected as well as
those tenants who may take such houses
in the future.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask the
noble Viscount if, when he is considering
the amendment of Sub-clause 2 of Clause
2, he will at the same time consider
whether we ought not to do something in
the way of defining the word "subsequently" used in the first Sub-clause of
Clause 2. As it stands it seems to me
that the suggestion of misrepresentation
and so on can be made by an outgoing
tenant at any time, however long, after
he had given up possession of the house.
That is scarcely fair to the man from
whom he took the house. If a charge of
misrepresentation and so on and concealment of material facts is to be made, it
is not unusual to require that it
should be made within some specific
time, and not of great duration.
I do
311 Rent Restrictions Bill-

Adjournment
[Mr. Leslie]
not know whether there is anything in
any of the earlier Acts which would
limit the time within which a claim
can be made, but I would ask the noble
Leader of the House to look into it and,
if it is necessary that any Amendment
should be made, perhaps he will do it
when he is dealing with the other
Amendment he has already referred to.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: With
regard to the questions that have been
asked about sub-letting and the position
of existing tenants of furnished houses, I
should like to have an opportunity of
looking into those points before we consider the Bill further. I think the Bill
would cover the cases of sub-letting referred to by Senator Joseph Cunningham, provided the house is under control, but I am not perfectly certain.
I
will consider the desirability of adopting
Senator Leslie's suggestion on the Committee stage of the Bill.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill
be now read a Second time) agreed to.

and 16 Geo. 5, c. 12, as to houses let with

VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: When
this Bill was last before the House two
questions were asked by Senator Joseph
Cunningham and Senator Barclay respectively. Senator Cunningham asked what
would be the position of persons who have
taken large buildings and let them into
smaller tenancies, and if they would come
within the provisions of this Bill. The
answer to that is in the affirmative.
Senator Barclay asked if existing tenancies would come under the operations of
the Bill. The answer to that is also in
the affirmative in the case of those tenancies which, if the letting had been unfurnished, would have been under control.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill

Rent Restrictions Bill
The Price of Pork
THE SENATE
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move the following Amendment :---
Clause 2, page 3, line 10, at the end of
paragraph (b) (iii) add :
Provided that in the application of
sub-section (1) of section five of the
principal Act to a case where the landlord has given to the tenant a notice to
quit before the passing of this Act such
landlord shall give to the tenant within
fourteen days after the passing of this
Act the notice in writing required by
paragraph (d) and sub-division (iii) of
paragraph (e) of the said sub-section.
I should like to point out that there is
a misprint in the Amendment as it appears
on the Order Paper. In the last line but
one, after paragraph (d) the word "of"
should read "and". It is a printer's
mistake. As the Members of the Senate
will recollect, I stated in moving the
Second reading of this Bill that the drafting of Sub-clause 2 of Clause 2 was not
entirely free from objection. The object
of that clause is to compel a landlord to
give to a tenant a written statement showing the purpose for which he requires the
house, and the Clause provided that this
notice shall be given at the time that the
notice to quit is being served. I think it
will be agreed that under ordinary circumstances, that is the proper and fairest
time for giving the notice. It was, however, pointed out at a later stage of the
proceedings in another place, that the
Clause so drafted would render all notices
to quit served before the passing of this
Act and now running invalid, in that it
would be impossible for the landlord te
satisfy the requirements of the Clause,
and to show that he had served the notice
at the same time as the notice to quit.
The
The Government are of the opinion
that the best course is to give an interval
after the passing of the Act, within which
the necessary notice may be served in
cases where the notice to quit was served
before the passing of the Act.
Amendment accordingly provides that in
such cases notice shall be served within
fourteen days of the passing of the Act.
It may possibly occur to hon. Members
that this period is somewhat short, but in
the first place I would point out that by
this time the provisions of the Bill are
widely known, and all persons concerned
in the administration of property will be
aware of its requirements. In the second
place the first Quarter Sessions after the
passing of this Bill will be held in January
next, one month after the Bill is likely
to become law, and it is desirable that the
landlord should have as long as possible
within which to serve his notice, and that
the tenant should have a reasonable
opportunity of considering his position
and determining whether he will resist
the proceedings in the light of the information given in the notice. Accordingly, it seems fair to divide the available interval equally between the two
parties. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to welcome the
introduction of this Amendment, because
at the present time there are a large number of landlords who are exercising the
right of serving notices for the purpose
of obtaining part of the arrears of rent
due under the tenancy. This particular
Amendment will make it compulsory upon
landlords who have been resorting to that
method of getting part of the arrears of
rent to serve, at the time they serve the
notice to quit, a notice specifying the
purpose for which they require the house.
I welcome the Amendment, as it will protect many of those persons who are receiving these notices from day to day.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, order to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Short title), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported,
with Amendment, to the House.

Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendment.
The Price of Pork.
QUESTION TO MINISTER.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay:-
To ask the Leader of the House if he
can state the reason for the great dis-
The Price of Pork
The Price of Pork
15 NOVEMBER 1927


그만!
اب
Π
ET

crepancy in the price of pork as sold
by the producer and bought by the
consumer; and whether the Government will take steps to stop profiteering
in this commodity.
Mr. BARCLAY: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House the question which
stands in my name on the Order Paper.
I do not intend to say very much, because
it is a subject that offers an opportunity
to every Member of the House to say
something if they so wish. It is a question that severely touches the pockets of
two sections of the community. The producer does not get sufficient for the
trouble and labour he expends to put the
article on the market, and the consumer
has to pay far too much for the article
when he comes to buy it. The reason
that prompted me to ask this question
was that I saw in the newspaper a few
nights ago a report of a farmers' meeting
that was held in the County of Fermanagh I think it was in the town of
Enniskillen--at which a number of farmers gave expression to their feelings as
to the injustice inflicted upon them,
owing to those engaged in the pork trade
not receiving the price they were entitled
to.
One farmer said that pork could be
bought in the market at Enniskillen at
from 48s to 52s a cwt. It is brought to
Belfast and sold there at a price 100 per
cent. higher. A lady from whom I made
inquiry to-day told me that of late she
had not bought so much ham or bacon as
she did some time ago, and the reason
she gave was that it was too dear and she
could not afford to pay the high price
that was charged for it. The result was
that she and her family had to deny themselves this very important article of food.
She informed me that a few weeks ago she
was paying 2s 3d per lb. for ham. Then
there was a reduction to Is 10d per lb.,
and last week she paid 1s 8d a lb. for
ham. Therefore, you have the fact that
pork can be bought in the town of Enniskillen for 48s a cwt., and in the city of
Belfast it is retailed over the counter at
186s 8d a cwt.
I would like to know where the
difference between £28s Od and £9 6s 8d
goes to. I think the Government should
interest itself in this question, because in
the past the Government has done a great
deal and has spent a considerable amount
of money to encourage the agricultural
industry in Northern Ireland. The
Minister of Agriculture nas not spared
himself. He has spent both time and
energy in visiting the different parts of
Ulster encouraging the farmers to experiment in different ways. Some of those
experiments have not proved very profitable to the farmer, who has been put to
a. loss in connection with them. I think
it was and is the intention of the Government that the farmers should have the
benefit of the money that was provided
for this purpose, but we find that instead
of the producer and the consumer benefiting, both are losing. The farmer is
not getting a fair price for his produce,
and the consumer has to pay too much for
the necessaries of life the farmer produces. Therefore I think the Government should inquire whether some step
cannot be taken to remedy this evil, so
that justice may be done to both producer
and consumer.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: 1 would like
to support the remarks of Senator Barclay on this subject. 1 am sure all the
Members of this House are aware that
this question has been the subject of considerable agitation in the country for
some time, and it is a very vital question with the people in the country, and
particularly the poorer people. There is
an old saying that a pig pays a tenant's
rent, and that is one of the reasons why
the Government should do something to
stabilise the price of pork. Two or three
counties have sent deputations either to
the Government or the Ministry of Agriculture on this subject. If pork can at
one period be sold in the market at 106s
or 120s a cwt, as it was eight or ten
months ago, and then drop down to what
Senator Barclay states is the present
price, namely, 48s a cwt, there must be
something radically wrong.
Senator Barclay mentioned as the price
of ham in the shops in Belfast, a sum
which I thought was rather high, but I
dare say he is correct. We do not find
the price of pork fluctuating in the shops
in the same way that it does in the country
markets. If the Government could help
the country in any way in this matter
it would be one of the most beneficial acts
they could perform. When the poor
farmer has to pay, as he is now paying,
20s a bag for yellow meal, and has to buy
his young sucker pigs for £2 or £3 and
ام
The Price of Pork
The Price of Pork
THE SENATE
[Mr. Porter Porter]
keep them for six months, and then sell
them at 48s a cwt.-and no pork is being
bought over 2 cwt. in weight-he will not
make much money.
Mr. DORMAN: This is another instance of the folly of producing things for
profit instead of for use, Farmers are not
producing pork for the purpose of feeding
the people of Northern Ireland. They
are producing pork for the purpose of
making a profit, and the larger the profit
the farmers get, the better they are satisfied with the result of their labour. The
man who buys from the farmer does not
buy to provide for the needs of the people,
but that he also may make a profit. The
man who cures the pork when it comes
to his factory does not cure it, and will
not cure it unless he knows there is a
certainty of his making a profit, and the
higher the profit he makes the more satisfied he is. I am very pleased to see the
hon. Senators behind me coming over to
the philosophy of the
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is
rather wandering from the question before
the House. He appears to be giving an
essay on a subject that may be very interesting, but it has nothing to do with the
question.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to express my
approval of the question which appears
on the Order Paper. It is calculated to
draw the attention of the public to the
wrongs that are inflicted upon consumers
owing to the vast profits that are made
between the time food leaves the producer
and reaches the consumer.
This
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
question of the discrepancy between the
price of pork received by the producer
and that paid by the consumer, is not, of
course, purely a local question. It was
considered inter alia in its wider range by
the Departmental Committee established
under the Chairmanship of the Marquess
of Linlithgow in 1923, I think, by the
Ministry of Agriculture and Fisheries, to
inquire into the distribution and prices
of agricultural produce. If the hon.
Member will refer to the report of that
committee he will find that the position
regarding the spread between consumers'
and producers' prices has been examined
at some length. While it cannot be
satisfactorily dealt with in a reply to a
Parliamentary question, I would draw

“
attention to the fact that the Linlithgow
Committee, after stating that " a marked
difference beween the position of primary
producers and that of distributors is a
common feature of every industrial depression in this and other countries," go
on to say that apart from the special
circumstances arising out of the economic
conditions created by the war, a stage of
national development has been reached,
of such complexity and interdependence,
that the fundamental problem of the
cheap and efficient distribution of foodstuffs is one of the most pressing and important with which modern society is
faced. Primary producers, whose produce
is sold in the wholesale markets in competition with supplies from abroad, are
restricted in their returns by the prices
realized for the imported article.
prices they receive are largely fixed by
forces outside their individual or even
collective control."
The
So far as the local aspect of this matter
is concerned, the immediate question
turns, perhaps, on the stabilisation
of prices. It is admitted that fluctuations in the price of pork have been
acute from time to time, and occasionally
unexpected. At conferences summoned
by the Ministry of Agriculture the whole
question of stabilisation has been gone
into by representatives of the Ulster
Farmers' Union, the Ulster Agricultural
Organization Society and the bacon curing interest. While the Ministry would
welcome an arrangement whereby an advance indication of probable prices would
be available for the farming community,
it is clear that there would be serious
difficulties in putting such an arrangement into force as a voluntary measure.
Official machinery for the fixing of prices
is clearly out of the question.
I understand that in several districts
in Northern Ireland there has been a tendency to sell fat pigs by live weight, and
that in some quarters serious consideration has been given to the question of
catering for the British pork market in
Great Britain. These two factors will,
no doubt, tend towards the stabilisation
of prices.
I propose to circulate, in connection
with this matter, a lengthy tabular
statement showing the prices of Northern
Ireland pork and Irish bacon and hams
from the beginning of July to the end of
October of this year.

Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that
reply, may I ask the noble Viscount if the
report of Lord Linlithgow's Committee is
available in the Library, and if it is not,
whether the Government can arrange for
some copies to be supplied ?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I do
not know at the moment whether or not
copies are available in the Library, but
I will make inquiries,
Sir GEORGE CLARK: Arising out of
that answer, would it not be better if
people stopped buying pork, because I
understand the medical profession do not
recommend pork as a food. It seems to
me, therefore, that consumers have the
matter in their own hands, because there
is no necessity for them to buy pork if it
is too dear. Let them do without it and
they will be much healthier.

I should like to give notice that tomorrow intend to move the suspension
of Standing Orders, in order to get the
Housing and Rent and Mortgage Interest Restrictions (Amendment) Bills
through their remaining stages with the
utmost expedition.
