Wednesday, 16th November, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
MASSEREENE
VISCOUNT
AND
FERRARD: In the absence of my noble
Friend, the Leader of the House, I beg
to move, That Standing Order No. XXV
be suspended for this day's Sitting in respect of the Rent and Mortgage Interest
Restrictions (Amendment) Bill.

Rent and Mortgage Interest Restrictions
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for Receiving Report of Amendment read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move, That this
Report be now received.
On the Second Reading of the Bill,
Senator Leslie suggested that consideration should be given to the word "Subsequently" in line 25 of Clause 2, Subclause (1). I think the idea the hon.
Member had in his mind was that the
word "subsequently" was too wide, as it
would enable the tenant to bring an
action many years after his cause of
action had arisen.
It may be pointed
out, however, that this Sub-clause merely
extends to persons who have not resisted
the landlord in Court the remedy of
false representation which tenants who
had resisted legal proceedings already
have got. The wording of the Section is
precisely the same in this respect as is the
original Section of the principal Act of
1920, which is still in operation in respect
of what might be termed Court cases. No
hardship seems to have resulted from the
use of the word in the original Section,
although this Section has been in force
number of years, and actual
experience does not show any necessity
for altering this form of words.
for
a
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to thank
the noble Viscount for looking into the
question, and I am glad to have elicited
the information he has just given to the
House. I quite agree that in the
circumstances it would be a great mistake
phrase which has been
sanctioned by custom and found efficacious
in the past. I think there is no doubt
that the resources will be sufficient to deal
with the possible difficulty I anticipated
if it should unfortunately ever arise.

THE SENATE
THE SENΑΤΕ.

Tuesday, 29th November, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
The Registrar-General's 5th Annual
Report containing general abstracts of
Marriages, Births and Deaths registered
in Northern Ireland during the year 1926.
Report of the Ministry of Finance for
Northern Ireland on the Administration
of Charities in Northern Ireland for the
year ended 31st December, 1926.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Dangerous Drugs (Approved institutions) (Northern Ireland) Order (1927).
Presented (pursuant to Act), and
ordered to lie on the Table.
Ministry of Agriculture:
Report of the Departmental Committee
on the Transit, Prices, and Marketing of
Agricultural Produce.
Presented (by Command), and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Moved: That Standing Order No.
XXV. be suspended for this day's sitting,
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Intoxicating Liquor and
Licensing Bill.-(Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
PARLIAMENT HOUSE AT
STORMONT.
Mr. LONG: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether he can state what
progress is being made with regard to the
building of the new Parliament House at
Stormont, and whether he is in a position to say when the building is likely to
be completed; and whether it is a fact
that the expense of renting the present
temporary Parliament House is borne by
the Imperial Exchequer.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I have to inform the hon.
Gentleman that the basement work in
connection with the new Parliament
Buildings at Stormont has now practi
cally been completed, and that, as explained in answer to a similar question
in another place on the 22nd instant, a
contract for the superstructure of the
building has now been advertised. The
Imperial Office of Works, who are the responsible authorities, are prepared to
prosecute, with all possible expedition,
the work yet to be completed, but in an
undertaking of this magnitude it would
be unwise to indulge in any forecast of
definite or even approximate dates. The
Government has lost no opportunity of
stressing the importance of the new Parliament House being available at the
earliest possible moment. I suggest to
the hon. Member that he should avail himself of the kind invitation of the contractors to see, to-morrow morning, the progress that is being made with the work.
The reply to the latter part of the
question is in the affirmative.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Arising out of the answer of the noble
Viscount, I should like to ask him
whether, in any further contracts that
are given in connection with the building
of the Parliament House, the Government
will take steps to safeguard the County
Council of Down against liability
they might incur on account of the cutting up of the road leading to Stormont.
During the progress of the work that has
already been done there, the road which
was used for the cartage of stones was
very badly cut up. The Down County
Council was involved in a law case over
the matter and owing to something being
omitted from the contract the County
Council lost the case and had to bear the
cost of keeping the road in repair.
hope that in any contract that may be
I

will be safeguarded in this respect.

steps have so far been taken, but I will

Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time. - Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In the regrettable absence,
through illness, of the noble Viscount,
the Leader of the House, I beg to move
the Second Reading of this Bill.
Its
I object, as hon. Members all know, is to
carry more fully into effect the general
intention of the Intoxicating Liquor Act
of 1923, and to remedy certain technical
defects of that Act which have been disclosed by actual practice and experience.
This House will recall that on the
Second Reading of the Intoxicating
Liquor Bill in 1923 we were favoured
with a visit from the Prime Minister,
who spoke eloquently in its support and
expressed high hopes that it would do
much to promote the cause of true temperance throughout Northern Ireland.
That those hopes have been realized will
be admitted by all here to-day, and there
must be very few people, I think, who
will deny that the Act of 1923 has justified its place on the Statute Book.
The Bill before us is an Amending
Bill and it will, therefore, be necessary,
in explaining its general effect, to keep
hefore us the main provisions of the
parent Act, which is being amended. The
title of that Act reads as follows:
"An Act to make provision for the
closing on Sunday and Christmas Day
of certain premises licensed for the sale
of intoxicating liquor, the abolition of
certain off-licences, and the settlement
of claims for compensation arising
thereout; to impose restrictions on the
sale of methylated spirits and to amend
the law relating to the sale and supply of
intoxicating liquor by retail, and for
purposes connected therewith."
Clause 1 of the present Bill must
be read in conjunction with Section 2 of
the 1923 Act. It will be remembered
that that Section states that it shall not
be lawful for any person to carry on, in
any part of any premises licensed for the
sale of intoxicating liquor by retail for
consumption off the premises, any trade
other than that trade or the sale of table
waters. One of the effects of that Section has been that the legality of selling
liquor by wholesale, in off-licensed premises, has been rendered doubtful, and
as such a prohibition was never intended,
it is made clear in Clause 1, Sub-clause
(1), that henceforth it shall be lawful for
the holder of a retail off-licence to carry
on in the same premises the sale of liquor
by wholesale.
Sub-clause (2) of the first Clause, besides providing a more satisfactory wording for Section 6 (1) of the 1923 Act, has
also the following effect:
(1) It permits the inclusion in hotels
of legitimate subsidiary businesses as
hairdressers' shops, stock rooms, etc.;
(2) It definitely permits publicans to
carry on the sale of intoxicating liquor
by wholesale in the same premises as
are used for their on-licence business;
(3) It prohibits the sale of nonintoxicants in a public house during
closing hours. The necessity for such
a provision will, of course, be quite
clear;
(4) It permits publicans to provide
temporary stabling accommodation, not
only during opening but also during
closing hours. This concession, which
was not allowed by the 1923 Act, has
been made to convenience farmers who
arrive in country towns at the early
hours of the morning, especially on
Fair days;
331 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
DA Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
(5) It prescribes a specific penalty
for mixed trading. This is necessary,
as it was doubtful whether any penalty
could be imposed under the 1923 Act,
for a breach of the statutory prohibition regarding mixed business.
Now I come to Clause 2. Clause 2 relates to the hours during which liquor
may be consumed in hotels. Sub-clause
(1) provides that the hours during which
it is legal to sell or consume drink in a
hotel on Sunday or Christmas Day are
ordinary week-day hours, that is, 10 a.m.
to 9 p.m. or 10 p.m., as the case may be,
and it will no longer be necessary for a
bona-fide resident in the hotel to have a
substantial meal in order to obtain a
drink. Sub-clause (2) makes it unlawful
for the proprietor of an hotel to allow the
consumption of liquor after closing hours,
unless a dinner or other entertainment is
being held and he has secured a permit
from the police authorities authorising
such consumption.
Clause 3 is a lengthy Clause, and will
require some detailed explanation. As
regards the first Sub-clause, under Section 9 of the 1923 Act, new licences were
allowed to be issued for premises which
were licensed between 1920 and 1923, and
there was nothing in the law to prevent a
man from obtaining compensation and
then applying for a new licence in respect
of the same premises. It is now proposed
definitely to put an end to the possibility
of a compensated licensee obtaining a new
licence for the same premises on the
ground that he was licensed in 1920.
Sub-clause (2) provides that where a
new licence is substituted for a licence
previously in force, the new licence shall
be of the same quality as the old; that
is, if the old licence was an off-licence,
the new one shall also be an off-licence,
and so on. Sub-clause (3) provides that no
licence which was an early-closing licence
at the date of the passing of the 1923
Act can be extended to a full licence
under Section 9 (a) of the 1923 Act, and
every licence so extended shall revert to
an early-closing licence.
I will pass over Sub-clauses (4) and (5),
which are not very important, and I will
come to Sub-clause (6) Under Section
9 (c) of the Act of 1923, the issue of a
new licence is permitted in a ward or
electoral division where the population
has increased by 25 per cent. since the
last census and the applicant must surrender two licences of the same quality
in substitution for the new one. Under
the Sub-clause before usanew onlicence is permitted for the surrender of
one on-licence and one off-licence, but the
surrender of these two licences must involve the cessation of the sale of intoxicating liquor in two different sets of premises. It is also made clear that the
phrase "the last census" means the census
of 1911, that is, the last census before the
passing of the 1923 Act.
Clause 4 deals with the provisions as
to licences for the sale of wine and sweets,
and I may, perhaps, here remark that
the word "sweets" means, in this connection, fermented liquor made from
fruit and sugar. Under Sub-clause (1)
the future issue of a wine retailer's onlicence or a sweets retailer's on-licence is
absolutely prohibited, and the future
issue of sweets retailer's off-licences is restricted to premises where spirits or beer
off-licences are in force. These licences
have hitherto escaped the provisions of
the 1923 Act, and in view of the, what I
might call pernicious, concoctions which
are at present being sold, it has been
thought advisable to restrict their issue
in future.
The chief effect of Sub-clause (2) is to
provide that no future wine off-licences
shall be issued, except in respect of premises continuously licensed since before
the passing of the 1923 Act. Sub-clause
(3) provides that no person shall be entitled to take out or hold a wine retailer's
off-licence for any premises in respect of
which compensation was awarded under
the former Act, while Sub-clause (4) confers upon wine off-licensees the same
benefit as is conferred upon publicans and
beer or spirits off-licensees by Clause 3,
Sub-clause (4), of the present Bill. The
last Sub-clause of Clause 4 exempts from
all the foregoing provisions of the Clause
those wine licences which are issued to
chemists and which authorize the supply
of wine only on presentation of a doctor's
prescription.
Clause 5 allows the issue of a spirit
dealer's licence to wholesalers, which will
authorize the sale of quantities of not less
than 2 gallons consisting of different
denominations, such as whisky, brandy,
rum, etc., to holders of retailer's on- or
| off-licences. Perhaps I might explain to
333 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Second Reading daal 334
29 NOVEMBER 1927
cent.
licant

pr
of t

ause
ne of

sinet
shall
man
the House that under the former Act a
could sell two gallons of whisky
or two gallons of brandy, not
less. Under this Clause, so long as the
total amount he sells is two gallons or
over, he can distribute the two gallons
among the different denominations of
spirits. Hitherto the wholesaler has
been prohibited from selling in these
smaller quantities and inconvenience has
been caused to certain retailers which it
seemed unfair to impose upon them. It
will be observed that a somewhat similar
concession is also allowed to wholesale
cider dealers.
Clause 6 is administrative and will ensure that the description of the licensed
premises in the Excise licence and in the
magistrates' renewal certificate is always
the same, and does not vary from year
to year, except in accordance with a definite order of the court.
Clause 7 is fairly simple and calls
for little explanation, except, perhaps,
with regard to sub-Clause (4).
Hon.
Members are aware, of course, that under
the 1923 Act if a publican gets three enhis licence that licence
dorsements on
automatically becomes null and void, but
where two or more persons consecutively
possess licensed premises, four, and not
three, endorsements are necessary to cancel the licence and they must all occur
within five years. It will be appreciated
that these provisions gave scope for
evasion and the present sub-Clause is designed to put an end to it. It provides
that in all cases three endorsements shall
annul the licence with, however, the further provision that where there has been
a period of five years with, so to speak, a
clean sheet, the licence shall be considered
to be free of all endorsements.
Clause 8 provides a definition for
the term "licence" as used in the 1923
Act, the chief practical result being that
by bringing wine licences within the provisions of Sections 2 and 3 of the former
Act the payment of compensation to
holders of extinguished wine licences is
legalised. It also brings wine licences,
under Section 8 of the 1923 Act and renders them endorsable.
I come now to Clause 9, which deals
with methylated spirits and methylated
ether. It has been found necessary to
tighten up the restrictions imposed by the
1923 Act upon the sale of these products
and sub-Clause (1) will, it is hoped, go
far towards affecting that end. It gives
the Ministry of Home Affairs power to
make regulations prescribing the kinds of
accounts and records that are to be kept
by the retailers, and it will enable the
Ministry to provide for a record of all
purchases being kept, thus devising a
check on the retailer. It also provides that these regulations shall apply
to all retail sales and there will no longer
be a hiatus between the sales up to one
gallon and the sales over four-gallons.
-Hitherto, as the House is probably aware,
no record of sale was required for
quantities between these two figures and
the result, as one might expect, has been
abuse. The remaining sub-Clauses are
simple and do not require any explanation from me.
Clause 10 also is simple and is concerned with restrictions to be placed
upon the sale of alcohol other than ethyl
alcohol, which is, of course, the alcohol
of which wine, beer, whisky, etc., are
composed. The Clause has been inserted
in view of the increasing sale by
publicans of iso-propyl alcohol to fortify
cheap wines. This type of alcohol is
practically duty free and can be purchased very cheaply. It is a by-product
of petroleum and in its effect is much more
toxic than whisky.
Clause 11 amends the law with regard
to the registration of clubs. With regard
to the first Sub-clause, I may explain
that at present there are certain specified
grounds of objection to the grant or renewal of a club certificate, and if no objection is lodged, the certificate is granted
automatically. The Sub-clause now before us is designed to make it very difficult for any club, other than a bona-fide
club, to obtain a certificate, by placing
the clubs themselves the onus
proving affirmatively certain specified
matters which, as hon. Members will see,
are set forth under the heading (a) of
the first Sub-clause. I may mention
that the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister for Home Affairs, speaking on this
Clause in another place, recited some
figures with regard to clubs which were
certainly very striking and which will,
perhaps, bear repetition.
on
of
He said that registered clubs in Northern Ireland had increased, since the 1923
Act, by seventeen, their membership had
335 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading

[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
increased by less than 4,000, but their
expenditure on the purchase of liquor had
mounted from £38,426 to £76,202-that
is, by considerably more than £37,000.
That is sufficiently clear evidence, I
think, that certain of these clubs are not
genuine, and the Government are justified
in asking for additional powers which will
do much towards weeding out the malafide clubs. Incidentally, I may point
out that Sub-clause (3) gives power to a
district-inspector to enter a registered
club at any time, and it will no longer be
necessary for him to obtain a warrant in
open court.
as a
Clause 12 seeks to clarify the law relating to the issue of liquor licences to
theatres and music halls. It provides
that no building shall be used
theatre or music hall without a licence
for that purpose having first been obtained
from the licensing court, and in this connection the word "licence" refers to a
licence for the theatre as a theatre, and
has nothing to do with a liquor licence.
The conditions governing its issue are
contained in the Second Schedule to the
Bill, and the police and the sanitary
authority are given power to object if
the proprietor refuses to comply with the
sanitary authority's regulations regarding proper accommodation, fire escapes,
etc. When this 'theatre" or "entertainment" licence has been obtained,
application may then be made for a liquor
licence, and objections may be urged by
the police to such an application just as
if it were an application for a publican's
licence.
I must apologize to the House for
taking up a good deal of time in an exposition of the various aspects of the Bill.
I trust, however, that I have made myself sufficiently clear, and I shall be happy
at a later stage to go more fully into
any points that may require elucidation.
I beg to move.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I do not intend
to say very much at the present time with
regard to this Bill. My attitude towards
strong drink in general is very neatly expressed by a modern writer who said he
regarded alcohol in the same way that
he regarded a dictionary. He did not
often have recourse to it, but it was very
useful to have when it was required. I
sympathize very much with the Government in their effort to steer a fair and
even course between the two extremes of
| opinion in this country, and I certainly
welcome this Bill as very valuable legislation. There is very little in it that I
take serious objection to. The new
Clause with regard to the sale of methylated spirits and ether, and the provision
to prevent the doping of wine and so on
should prove very useful. I followed
closely the passage of the Bill in another
place. I listened to some of the speeches
and I read a good deal of others, and
some of the arguments put forward by
the Opposition had a good deal of my
sympathy from a common sense point of
view.
as
I only intend, at this stage, to refer to
two points in the Bill. I do think the
Government should have agreed to admit
an amendment to remove all restrictions
on persons resident in hotels. There has
been a lot said about the tourist traffic
but I do not want to refer to tourists in
general. It has always caused me a
certain amount of detestation to think
that a person coming here from England
or Wales on business, or for any other
purpose, should find himself up against
something which makes him think that he
is in a different country. We should try to
preserve the same social conditions
exist in England, and it is not pleasant
to think that a person coming over here
and staying in an hotel has reason to
believe that he is in a less pleasant and
sociable country than the one he is accustomed to live in. A person resident
in an hotel, if he knows he cannot get
drink at a certain hour of the day, will
have that drink sent to his bedroom, and
I think that is a practice that would be
objected to by those who have very strong
temperance ideas. It leads to the
pernicious habit of secret drinking.
think that a person living in an hotel
should have all the comforts he would
have if he were residing in his own house.
In the course of their business commercial
travellers and other people are, unfortunately, obliged to live in hotels and I do
not see why they should be under any
restrictions whatever.
I
The only other part of the Bill I want
to refer to is Sub-clause 3 of Clause 3.
I understand that this affects about ten
traders, all in the County of Fermanagh,
and that no other traders in the entire
Six Counties are affected by it. It seems
that this is special legislation dealing
with these particular traders. They had
early-closing licences prior to the passing

of the 1923 Act, and on the passing of
that Act they applied to the County
Court Judge for a full-time licence, and
it was granted. Since then they have
been carrying on their business as fulltime licensees, and no objection has been
taken to that position of affairs.
this special legislation will make them revert to their former position as earlyclosing licensees, and will deprive them of
one hour's trading, that between 8 and 9
o'clock at night, which is the most important hour of their day's business.
I do not think it is fair that the
Government should pass special legislation relating solely to these very few
traders, and the Government would be
doing a very fair, reasonable, and kind
act to these particular traders by leaving
them alone and letting them enjoy the
class of trade they have had since the
passing of the 1923 Act. With regard
to the remainder of the Bill, as I have
said, I do not take any objection to it. I
think it is a very good Measure, and I am
prepared to support the Second Reading.
Mr. DORMAN: I do not profess to
have any knowledge of the licensed trade.
I settled this question for myself more
than fifty years ago, having for that
period been a total abstainer. It appears
me there are certain provisions
the
to
in this Bill which deprive
citizens of common rights that even those
who are total abstainers and may be designated Pussyfoots regard as being unfair,
because they recognize that the rights
and privileges they claim for themselves
should be accorded to others. I especially want to draw attention to the Clause
which has been referred to by Senator
Glendinning, the Clause which deprives
the man resident, under ordinary circumstances, in an hotel of the right of
extending to his guests in licensed premises an opportunity of participating in
social intercourse and having liquor
during that social intercourse.
are a very large number of individuals in
Ireland, and in Northern Ireland also,
who make licensed hotels their ordinary
home, and it seems to me that this Clause
will deprive those men of a right they
could exercise in a temperance hotel in
the same locality.
There
Assuming that I was living in licensed
premises and had some friends calling on
me on a visit, I would be deprived of the
liberty of giving them hospitality in the
form of intoxicating liquor, whereas if
I was resident in a temperance hotel, in
the same town or even in the same street,
I might bring in whatever intoxicating
refreshments I might choose for the purpose of offering hospitality to friends in
my apartments.
Sub-clause (1) of Clause 2 states:-
Where such liquor is sold to or
consumed by a guest or traveller who
has lodged for the preceding night or
has in writing engaged a room for the
succeeding night in such hotel at or during the hours specified for the purpose
of the sale of intoxicating liquor in
article 1 of Part I of the Second Schedule to that Act.
Sub-clause (2) goes on to say:-
Where any dinner or other entertainment is held in an hotel or restaurant which is licensed for the sale of
intoxicating liquor, it shall not be lawful for the proprietor or manager
thereof to allow the consumption of imtoxicating liquor, by persons åttending
such dinner or entertainment, at hours
other than those specified for the purpose of the sale of intoxicating liquor
by the Act of 1923 or any other enactment:
I want to know if all such residents in
hotels will be deprived of the right of
giving this hospitality to their friends
after licensed hours. There is another
question I would like to refer to, and it
also has been touched upon by Senator
Glendinning. That is in regard to Subclause (3) of Clause 3. It seems to me to
be foolish for the Government to extend
a right, such as has been extended to a
small number of licence holders in the
County of Fermanagh, and then shortly
afterwards to deprive them of that very
same right. I understand that the hour
that was added to their licensed hours
was the most important hour of the day
from the point of view of their business.
It is certainly the hour of the day when
those who have been engaged in a hard
day's toil, during the summer season,
would be inclined to resort to licensed
premises for the purpose of obtaining a
stimulant.
I understand that on the faith of the
Government continuing this right to this

ciate the efforts of the Government to

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM : When
the Act of 1923 was passed I considered
there was a very unfair restriction imposed on grown-up people throughout the
Six Counties, and I am sorry to say the
Government has not been guilty of any
rash action in any Amendments they have
put forward to remove that restriction.
Their Amendments are of a very minor
character and of very little consequence.
They have evidently attempted
take
middle
I know
that the views of the Government
this particular question are very
strong, and perhaps that would account
for the very weak action they have taken.
It is unfortunate that when a Bill dealing
with intoxicating liquor is introduced we
have wiseacres on both sides telling us,
on one side, of the benefits to be derived
from the consumption of alcohol, how a
little of it makes the tired workman
vigorous and energetic, and that if the
consumption is reduced the Chancellor of
the Exchequer will be stranded financially.
on
On the other hand we have people
telling us that it is the ruination of the
homes of the people and of their health,
and we are actually told it is the cause of
the present unemployment. It is very
difficult for one to decide which of the
two sides to take notice of. From my
own experience I have come to the conclusion that it is a matter of agreeing
that it is only the excess consumption of
alcohol which makes it injurious. I have
mixed with the workers very closely on a
Friday night when they are paid, and on
a Saturday night, and I must admit that

if alcohol is taken in moderation there is
certainly some benefit to be derived from
it. I do know that if one feels mentally
tired and unable to sleep, and one is
lucky enough to have spirits in the house
they act as a great soother and put one
to sleep. I have experienced it, and I
know it is also very good for indigestion.
،،
I consider that in this House we have
a calm atmosphere, with reasonable common-sense minds, that should be able to
view this Bill from an impartial standpoint. In my opinion, further Amendments in this Bill are necessary. I remember very well that on the first dry
Sunday after the 1923 Act came into
operation even horses, cows, and donkeys
that happened to be stabled on licensed
premises had to go dry," because the
police in some districts would not allow
the men to attend them. I see that in
this Bill the Government has made some
little improvement in that respect, and
they have now given permission for temporary stabling and for accommodation
to be given for vehicles and animals on
licensed premises. I look upon that concession as a great boon, particularly to
those in the country districts. There
are men there who set out early in the
morning, probably on a stormy and wet
morning, when it may also be snowing,
to go to a fair or market, and, unfortunately, when they come to the first inn
it is closed. They cannot even put their
horses up for a rest or to give them a feed.
This Bill has removed that injustice
to some extent, and now the poor woman
trudging along the road with a donkey
will be able to have the donkey stabled.
But the Government has not given any
consideration to the woman in charge of
the donkey, or to the man in charge of
the horse or motor vehicle, and I think
the Government should still further
amend the Bill by enabling accommodation to be provided for the persons in
charge of these horses and carts and other
vehicles. Surely the Government cannot expect that a person in charge of an
animal is going to rest in the stable until
such time as the fair or market may be
opened. A short time ago I read a book
-I think it was "The Cloister and the
Hearth"-in which travellers were advised to lie close to the cow, where they
would get some little heat. Probably it
is intended that these poor people should
lie close to the animal to keep warm instead of being allowed in a room of the
inn to warm themselves at the fire.
341 Intoxicating Liquor Bill- 29 NOVEMBER 1927

Second Reading
t
Although there has been considerable
clamour against clubs, the Government
have not interfered with the hours of
clubs, but they have decided to require
the district-inspector to exercise supervision over club premises. I quite agree
that that is necessary, because since Sunday closing was established a considerable
number of clubs have sprung up for purposes for which I do not think clubs
were originally intended. I was responsible for the formation of one club, probably the largest in existence in Northern
Ireland. A club is intended to prevent
excessive drinking. It is intended to provide recreation and other amusements for
the members of the club, so that they
will not be inclined to drink to excess,
and from my experience I must admit
that clubs have had that result. I have
known men who were excessive drinkers,
and when they have become good billiard
players they were quite willing to spend
their time playing games, and they forgot
about drink. From my own observations
I know that to be a fact.
Therefore, I say that clubs were not
intended for the purpose for which some
of those that have recently been established are being used at the present time,
and I am very pleased that the Government, while they have not interfered with
the hours, have decided to exercise supervision over clubs and to see that they are
used for the purpose for which they were
intended. I believe that will result in
the abolition of the bogus clubs that have
been set up since the passing of the Act
of 1923.
With regard to hotels, I think the
Government have done wrong in not
giving a full license to hotels and entire
freedom to the person who makes an hotel
his home. Surely if the Government
were satisfied to grant unlimited hours
for drink in the clubs, in workmen's
clubs as well as in the better class clubs,
they are, in fairness, bound to give the
same liberty to those residents and other
travellers who are continuously making
use of hotels. I remember the great fight
there was in England before workmen's
clubs had the same liberty as they
enjoy to-day. At that time the argument was used that all men are equal in
the sight of the law, and that workmen
were just as much entitled to a club and
its privileges as employers and other
better-class persons. The people who use
an hotel as their home or as a temporary
residence are just as much entitled to this
freedom as are the working-men members
of a club in either the city or the country,
and I am sorry the Government have not,
so far, seen their way to amend the 1923
Act in that direction.
It must be injurious to the North of
Ireland when people come from England
and other places where they have been
accustomed to the liberties and privileges
enjoyed by clubs and licensed houses, to
find that they are thus restricted in the
North of Ireland. Personally, I cannot
see how the Government are going to
carry out this restriction. If a person
cannot obtain drink during ordinary
hours, I do not see how they are to be
prevented having drink in their bedroom
in the hotel. It is merely leaving the
gate open for persons to violate the law.
If an Amendment is put down on the
Committee stage to delete Sub-section 1
of Clause 2 I shall vote in favour of it.
If the latter portion of that Sub-clause
was deleted it would leave hotels with the
full liberty that, in my opinion, they are
entitled to. On the whole, I have pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of
the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
With regard to the restrictions on hotels,
I made inquiries in official quarters, for
the benefit of tourists and strangers and
people who are bona-fide occupants of
hotels, and I was assured--and I think a
study of the Bill will confirm it that the
serious restrictions which were formerly
imposed on people living in hotels have
been taken away by this Amending Bill.
Under the Act of 1923 people could not
obtain liquid refreshment in an hotel on
Sunday unless they had a meal, but under
this Bill such persons will be able to get
liquor in an hotel at any time of the day,
whether it is a week-day or Sunday.
That takes the sting out of the special
point that has been made with reference
to the restrictions on hotels. I was, myself, going to move an Amendment on
the Committee stage dealing with that
matter, but I am so satisfied that that
restriction has been removed under this
Bill that I do not now intend to do so.
I would like to point out what is almost
a comical thing in connection with liquid
refreshment that is served in an hotel.
I might ask Senator Porter Porter to
join me at dinner in an hotel, and
343 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
AD
[Colonel Crawford]
although I might have a bottle of champagne on the table and be able to drink
it, I would not be allowed to give Senator
Porter Porter a drink. I think that is
an anomaly that should be removed. I
am told that one of the difficulties in the
way of allowing persons to entertain
guests in hotels, especially on a Sunday,
is that there are people who, knowing
the restriction, would send one of their
friends to take a room in an hotel on the
Saturday night, and if that restriction
was not in the Bill a man might entertain a party of forty or fifty people who
would all pay for the drink consumed,
although one man would be regarded as
the host. With the exception of such
small points as that, I think the Bill
meets with the approval of both sides the
Government were anxious to meet, and I
heartily support the Second Reading.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
There is one matter to which I would like
to draw the attention of the Government,
and it is a matter that has not, so far,
been alluded to. The practice I am
about to refer to is the exception rather
than the rule. There are public-houses
where the drainings of tumblers and other
utensils
returned and served out
are
If
again, and I am strongly of opinion that
when granting licenses, the authorities
should see, as far as possible, that on
licensed premises there is a jaw-box and
drain running right into the sewer.
there is a case of foot and mouth disease
in the country the whole district is closed
to prevent the spread of the disease, but
a person suffering from syphilis or cancer
may come into a public-house for a drink,
and if he has a cut in his mouth or a bad
tooth he is liable to spread the disease.
I think there should be some system of
inspection, and if a licensee is found to
allow drainings of glasses or cups to be
used again, his licence should be endorsed.
I look upon such a thing as being worse
than many of the offences for which
licences are endorsed, and I hope the
Government will take some steps in the
direction I have indicated. I believe
they would have the support of the bulk
of the publicans, because from what I
know I am happy to say that the majority
of licensees would not tolerate such a
practice. If the Temperance party would
expend some of their energies in this
direction they would do a great deal of
I think this Bill is a fair and
good.
honest attempt on the part of the Government to clear away some of the difficulties that have arisen in the Act of 1923,
and I have pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: As a rule I
am afraid that I do not devote to Bills
that come before this House the attention that I ought to, but this Bill is of
such vital importance to the people of
Northern Ireland, regulating, as it does,
what they drink, how they drink, and
where they drink, that I have studied it
closely. I have also taken the trouble to
see publicans and other people in Enniskillen, and by other people I mean
those who, like Senator Dorman, have not
taken drink for forty years.
I have
talked to them on this Bill to find out
what they think about it, and many of
them agree with the remarks that have
been made by Senator Glendinning with
regard to Sub-clause 3 of Clause 3.
They think it is very unfair to those
licence holders in County Fermanagh who
had their hours increased by one hour,
and who are now told, by this Bill, that
they must revert to their eight-hour
licence. The County Court Judge in
Fermanagh, who I believe is a very able
judge, granted these nine people in Fermanagh this licence extending their hours
by one hour, from 8 to 9 o'clock, and
although I have heard there
appeal and that the licensees lost their
case, I am informed that it did not apply
to these nine men in Fermanagh.
was an
We have heard a good deal to-day about
hotels, and I must say I was shocked by
what Senator Samuel Cunningham said.
I never heard of the practice of the dregs
of glasses and cups being given to people
to drink again, and I am afraid the hon.
Senator must have been in very bad company to be in a position to bring such a
matter before this House. It is a thing
that would take me a long time to believe,
and I know that in Fermanagh such a
thing would never happen. I support
the remarks of Senator Glendinning.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that now
that we have got this Amending Bill of
the Intoxicating Liquor Act of 1923a Bill which was originally promised us,
if I am not mistaken, in the Gracious
Speech at the opening of Parliament in
1926, two years ago-perhaps the
first thing and the most important thing
for us to bear in mind is that we are

Second Reading

ass
d
I

a
a
sitting here as a Second Chamber. Most
of us, no doubt, have our private opinions,
and possibly some of us hold those opinions
more strongly than others, and some hold
them in one direction and others hold them
in another direction. But I think we ought
to remember that it is not a case where
we ought to insist unduly on our private
opinions, but what it behoves us to do is
to exercise our powers of revision rather,
perhaps, by excision than by insertion.
Particularly I think we should be very
careful before we insert any provision in
this Bill on the same lines as some matters
which have been dealt with elsewhere and
definitely pronounced against. But that
I only venture to put forward as a general
observation and I do not mean to imply
that we are to be deaf to such a petition
as that voiced so eloquently from both
sides of the House in respect of those nine
people in Fermanagh, nor do I think we
should altogether disregard what has been
said by Senator Joseph Cunningham.
But I do think we should be very careful
in what we do.
Complaints will no doubt be made-in
fact they have been made-as to the
maintenance of complete Sunday closing
and as to the preservation of such an
antiquated absurdity as that which differentiated between the visitor to Portrush
and the visitor to Bangor. It is certainly
based on a somewhat out-of-date consideration relative to population. The distinction, as the House will remember, is
that a town with a population under
5,000 is to have its public houses closed
an hour earlier than a town that has a
population exceeding that figure.
The
reason for that distinction and it is a
distinction that has endured for some
time; it was not the product of the Act
of 1923-the reason for the distinction has
been variously given. One suggestion is
that a complaint was made on behalf of
the publicans of the smaller towns that if
their hours were lengthened their duties
of supplying liquor were also lengthened.
That is that if they were licensed to be
open for so many hours, for that number
of hours were they bound to be open.
Owing, as they are reported to have said,
to the small size of their population that
lengthened opening was not necessary.
Another explanation is that in the
smaller towns the police force is so small
that the public houses could not be
effectively supervised. I hope that that
is not the true explanation, because it
seems to suggest that the publicans exist
for the purpose of being supervised by
someone else rather than for the purpose
of meeting a public want. But in either
case it has to be considered that the
situation has wholly changed. In the
first place, the population is very much
less than it was in the early days I am
speaking of, and the number of public
houses is very much less also, but still
that distinction does remain and I am
not sure that it would be for this House,
for the reasons I gave just now, to alter
it. I think one may probably apply
what was said by the Minister of Home
Affairs in another place, and I propose
to read the quotation:-
An Act which does not commend itself
to the main body of good-living citizens
is bound, sooner or later, to become a
dead letter, and the non-observance of
a particular law can only have a disastrous effect upon the observance of
the law in general. - (Official Report,
Commons, Vol. 8, No. 39, Col. 2451,
1st November, 1927.)
I think we must leave the particular
matter of which I have spoken to be dealt
with at some future date. Again complaint on a very different line will be
made to the absence of codification of our
Licensing law. Whenever a law relating
to the supply of intoxicating liquor is
brought before Parliament someone or
other invariably is struck with the idea
that it is time the law was codified. I
entirely share that view and I should
welcome a real codification of the law relating to intoxicating liquor, but we have
to remember the great difficulties that are
in the way. There are, I believe, not less
than
score of different kinds of
licences; there are not less than
twice that number of statutes still
in operation on the Statute Book, and
some of the statutes, as I think another
speaker has said, are more than a century
old. The difficulties are very great, but
I do not think we ought to add to those
difficulties and I shall presently talk
about one point in this Bill in which I
think we have quite unnecessarily added
to the confusion which already exists, or
rather we have sought to add to it.
a
Passing more directly to the Measure
itself, I agree entirely with the proposal
in the First Clause of the Bill as regards
Intoxicating Liquor Bill—
- Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
wholesalers. That was one of the unconsidered trifles of the Act of 1923 and we
rejoice that the matter has now been
attended to. It is really the principal
reason for welcoming this Bill that a good
many of the odds and ends are to be
cleared up and the matter to some extent
clarified. I welcome such a concession as
is contained in Clause 2 as regards hotels.
If the provisions of that Clause are intelligently worked and I have no reason to
suppose that they will not be I have
every reason to believe that a good deal
of the objection which we made to the
1923 Measure so far as that particular
part of it is concerned will disappear. I
think that is so, but at the same time I
would not shut the door or seek to shut
the door against any proposed alteration,
because such a proposal has some public
support behind it and ought to be considered on its merits.
The actual detail is of some interest
because it is here that for the first time
in the Bill we find "head-constable" introduced as an alternative to "districtinspector." It occurs throughout the Bill
and therefore I mention it to-day.
I
understand that when this Bill was under
discussion in another place it was suggested it ought to be made possible for permission to be given by a sergeant of
police, it being pointed out that in many
of the smaller districts and Portrush is
one-the highest policeman in the town
was a sergeant. The right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Home Affairs,
objected, and with his view I imagine this
House will be in accord, but I do think
it is rather a question whether throughout the Bill we should admit "headconstable" as an alternative, not because
there is any objection to a head-constable, but because it seems to cast a
rather unfair duty on an applicant, because in every case it is suggested that in
the absence of a district-inspector a
head-constable may act. I do not think
an applicant should trouble whether a
district-inspector is absent from his post,
because he cannot be in every part of his
division, and we should accept the position
that notice to
district-inspector
should be good enough whether he is in
barracks or not.
a
Coming to the next Clause, I am
afraid I have to admit that I do not fully
understand the purport of some of the
Sub-clauses. This is the Clause about
which complaint is made about Fermanagh. As I understand it, it seems to me
that the first part of sub-Clause 3 relates
to existing early closing licences which are,
as I understand it, to be carried on as
early closing licences, and to that I think
there can be no objection. At the end
there is something which I do think
might perhaps be an injustice. That is
that apparently several full-time licences
have already been issued and it is
suggested that those during their cur.
rency were declared to be illegal. I think
that is a matter more for the Committee
stage than for to-day.
Coming to Clause 9 of the Bill, it deals
with methylated ether. I am so much
in sympathy, as I am sure we all are,
with anything that can be done to prevent the use of methylated ether as a
drink that I am wondering whether we
have not gone a little too far in the restrictions. I am not, I regret to say, in
any way fully equipped with knowledge
as regards methylated ether, and I was
hoping I should get enlightenment before
the debate was over. I understand it is
possible to use methylated ether for the
purpose of anasthetics. If that is so we
shall have to watch that we have not gone
a shade too far. I am not sure whether
this same methylated ether is not used
in the manufacture of a certain class of
scientific instruments. I am thinking
particularly of thermometric and barometric instruments. I do not know
whether the point of the use of methylated ether in that connection has been
considered.
I imagine than in such cases, as also in
the case of the sale of methylated ether
for use in laboratories, regulations will be
applied, but I should like the noble Viscount to look into this matter and tell us
at a later stage whether any words ought
to be inserted to deal with that point.
Of course, as regards the use of what has
been referred to as "Red Biddy," there is
nothing to be said. Everyone is entirely
behind the Minister in his proposals to
stop its use if he can. Indeed, it will be
remembered that some years ago Sir
Harry Quilter tried to introduce a
Measure in the British Parliament to
secure pure beer, and many people, including temperance people, have often
expressed regret that his proposals were
not carried.

I now come to the question of clubs.
The whole question of these clubs-and
ร่
349 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

29 NOVEMBER 1927
Second Reading
!

i


I
clubs are referred to in the Title of the
Bill-is dealt with in Clause 11, and I
must say I am rather surprised, when I
begin to try to study the Bill, at the conclusion which I ultimately came to.
suppose if there was one thing more than
another in which everybody was agreedthat is to say, everybody who is agreed
that the laws relating to intoxicating
liquor in Northern Ireland require
Amending-it was that it was necessary
to tighten up the regulations relating to
clubs. I think it was a stock phrase.
But I am bound to say that the noble
Viscount, in moving the Second Reading
of the Bill, and quoting the words of the
Minister of Home Affairs, did not at all
make out any case for interference with
clubs. He told us, I think, that whereas there were 53 clubs in Northern Ireland in 1923, in 1927, four years later,
there are 17 more.
ed
Seventeen clubs have come into existence
in four years. There are eight
counties in Northern Ireland, including
the county boroughs, and that means two
apiece and one over. As a matter of fact
I believe I am correct in stating that the
rise in the number of registered clubs in
Belfast during that period is limited to
two. Unless the other fifteen clubs not
in Belfast are concentrated in some particular area in which case one would
like to know what area it was it would
not seem that there is any very great case
for new interference.

There is another difficulty. The Clause
dealing with this matter contains certain
provisions to which I myself should be
very much inclined to object. It contains numerous provisions which have, I
think, to be considered very carefully in
relation to the effect they would have on
clubs in country districts. There are
clubs in country districts and apparently
there are fifteen new clubs outside Belfast and I do not think the provisions of
this particular Clause are altogether suitable. Nor do I think they are very
necessary, but I do object very stronglyand I was rather surprised that Senator
Joseph Cunningham did not object in the
course of his very interesting allusion to
clubs I do object very much to the
wholly unnecessary legislation by reference introduced by this Clause.
The whole law relating to the registration of clubs in Ireland is contained

either in the Act bearing that name of
1904-which has never been seriously
touched by the Imperial Parliament,
although it was touched once in the following year-but the whole law on the
Registration of clubs in Ireland is to be
found on the Statute Book in this Act of
1904 and one of the Schedules to our own
Act of 1923. I can see no reason whatever for including clubs in the Bill before
us and I am sure it would facilitate the
passage of the Measure if this Clause were
left out entirely and if the whole question
of clubs was dealt with in one simple comprehensive Measure. It is perfectly easy
because there are only two sets of statute
laws to be considered.
I
Passing to theatres, I should like to ask
the noble Viscount how many theatres or
music halls there are in Northern Ireland, and where they are. I believe I
am correct in stating that it was necessary,
owing I think to a question which arose
in the city of Londonderry, for Parliament to interfere as regards the licensing
of theatres as such, because I believe there
was very considerable doubt as to the
effect on various theatres of the authority
which had power to grant licences.
should like to know how many theatres
and music halls there are and whether I
correctly understood the noble Viscount
to say that in the event of a theatre
obtaining a licence as a theatre it would
be open to such theatre to apply for a
licence for the sale of intoxicating liquor
therein. I have to concede that it may
be desirable to have a licence for the sale
of intoxicating liquor because there are
people who go to the bar of a theatre between the acts.
Again it seems to me that we are
creating somewhat of a dangerous precedent by opening up ground which more
particularly belongs to the local authority
and we use words which I do not understand. The Public Health Acts Amendment Act which is mentioned in the Bill
did not entirely apply to Ireland and if
certain sections of it have been applied
to Ireland it can only, I think, be by
virtue of one of those detestable adaptations of Enactment Orders against which
one is always prejudiced. There was a
power in our Local Government Act
under section 104 or thereabouts, of
enacting various matters, and it may be
that there is something done there which
deals with the question before us.
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
But as it stands I think Sub-clause 7
of Clause 12 appears to be rather out of
place in its present company. I say that
without prejudice to the merits of the
provisions it contains. I imagine there
will be some few minor Amendments
moved, but those do not concern us today. I do not think there is anything in
the Bill as a whole which should prevent
us giving it a Second Reading, whatever
we may do with some of its provisions at
a later date.
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I add a few
words of commendation in relation to
Clauses 9 and 10, which provide for restrictions of the sale of methylated spirits
and methylated ether. This is not the
time or place to deliver a physiological
lecture on alcohol generally or as to its
necessity or advantage. But I would like
to observe in passing that I think the
members of the profession to which I have
the honour to belong are extremely careful as a body in ordering alcoholic tonics,
and that these are ordered with a full
sense of responsibility. We are as a body
very careful not to order these things
without some limitation to their duration,
and it would always be well to refer to the
particular member of the profession to
ascertain whether a licence for the continuation of the supply of these things
should be debited to him.
It is more in reference to that part of
the Bill dealing with methylated spirits
and ether that I desire to speak. I welcome very heartily the new provisions in
the Bill to control the sale of these materials. I do not think I fully endorse what
Senator Leslie has said, but no doubt
there are plenty of opportunities for the
supply of these methylated spirits in connection with the manufacture of scientific
instruments. I am more concerned with
their use as stimulants or fortifiers in connection with ordinary drinks as retailed
in public houses or in druggists. Perhaps
one reason why this drink came to be particularly favoured was because on market
days people could become riotously or
gloriously drunk four times in one day for
2d. or 3d.
It commended itself to a certain class
on that account, but the deleterious effects
of the use of this methylated spirit and
methylated ether are so pronounced and
so obvious to the members of the medical
profession that they are of the opinion
that the sale of these spirits cannot be too
strictly limited or supervised.
I hope
Senator Leslie's fear that in country dis-.
tricts the small number of inspectors
available will lead to an evasion of the
Act will not prove to be true, but surely
without multiplying the army of inspectors some provision should be made for
supervising the sale of methylated spirits.
With regard to Clause 10, I think one
might say that if alcohol is to be provided
it is of the utmost importance that pure
ethyl alcohol should be used. Much of
the evil effects of alcoholic beverages is
due to the adulteration of drinks by
petrid alcohol or wood spirit, and, apart
altogether from the use of these things by
themselves, when they are improperly employed either as stimulants or fortifiers a
great deal of harm is done. Therefore,
I hope that these provisions, which I welcome with great cordiality, will not only
be on the Statute Book but will be enforced, and that we may be freed from the
reproach which certain parts of the Six
Counties have been credited with of using
methylated spirits and methylated ether
to an extraordinary and undue extent.
a
With reference to Senator Leslie's remark about the use of these materials for
anasthetics, pure ether is used occasionally in methylated form, but he is quite
right in pointing out that in
local
anasthetic involving the use of spirits
methylated ether is perfectly harmless
and is quite a good anasthetic on account
of its cheapness. As regards the abuse of
it in this connection, I do not anticipate
any improper use of it in such a direction
because in this instance these spirits are
used by qualified people with a full knowledge of their potency and they are not
scattered broadcast to be used by everybody and anybody.
Like other speakers, I think some
Amendments are necessary in this Bill,
but they are not of a very vital character;
they are more in the nature of Amendments that might add to the efficiency of
the Measure.
In so far as the general
public and the vendors of these spirits
come to realize that the provisions of the
Bill are really useful and efficacious I
think there will be greater care in carrying out the details of the Bill with the
least temptation to conduct an evasive
sale of these spirits.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to say
that this Bill has my sympathy and sup-
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Second Reading
29 NOVEMBER 1927
8.

of
eva
rue, b
port. I think it will prove more workable in certain respects than the Bill of
1923. That Bill was hard to understand
and it was also unworkable in some respects, but I think the Bill now before
the House will render the provisions of
its predecessor more workable. I would
dien like to congratulate the Government on
the step they have taken to amend the
1923 Act as regards the provision of
10,11 stabling accommodation for animals beto be longing to farmers in the country. Senator
Joseph Cunnngham, who referred to this
matter, has not had the practical experience of a farmer or a farmer's son travellance
sed. I
ic beve
of d
ing ten or twelve or more miles with two
nihorses and a load of flax or pork, leaving
the
home at 3-30 or 4 o'clock in a morning,
in and arriving in a market town to find
that the yards of public houses were
closed against him. I have had the
experience of getting up early and travelling in the dark on a November or
December morning and arriving in Cookstown. I have not had the experience of
having the public house yards closed
against me because this has only been
done since the 1923 Act was passed.
fra
arts
eth
ndi
For
em
That Clause in the Act of 1923 was a
great injustice to the farmers; in fact it
was a tyrannical provision and I am very
glad to see that that injustice is removed
in this Bill. But I think the Government
should have gone further. Senator
Joseph Cunningham advocated that the
public houses should be open an hour or
so earlier for the sale and consumption of
intoxicating liquor, but I could not go so
far as that considering that I am a temperance man and that I have been an advocate of temperance all my life. I would,
however, go so far as to suggest that the
Government might allow the public house
or hotel kitchen to be opened so that
breakfast could be provided for the
farmers if there was a little room convenient to the kitchen where it could be
served and where the farmer could warm
himself both inside and outside.
I agree that when the Government has
done something for the comfort of the
horse or donkey they should do something
for the comfort of the old man or old
woman. The question of providing
breakfast in the public house was raised
some time ago and the suggestion was
made that farmers could go to a restuarant in the same town. It struck me that
the person who made that suggestion had
no idea what a country town was like. I
know good country market towns where
there is not a restaurant and the only place
where one can get anything to eat is the
eating house. That is generally a very
small building and it is not a very
encouraging place for anybody to go to.
I would rather go and sit in a public
house than go to what are called temperance hotels.
In this eating house you can get a beef
steak but the story in the country is that
when you get this beef steak you have got
to sit on the floor lest you strike your head
against the slates. Men have been known
to choke and die because this meat is so
tough. It always comes from the flesh of
an aged cow which has served its day and
generation producing stock and milk and
butter, and it ends its career on the table
of the country eating house. I would not
recommend any man to go to these places,
but I would suggest that the Government
should allow the kitchens of public houses
to be opened earlier for the convenience
of farmers arriving in a market or fair
town early in a morning.
Clause 10 prohibits the mixture of
alcoholic liquors. We are all aware that
it has been the custom on such special
occasions as 12th July, 17th March, and
on hiring or fair days for this mixture to
be made up. It is known as Fighting
Whisky and a great many men often went
home without their coat and vest and hat.
I am glad to see there will be some supervision over this traffic and that those who
buy whisky will get genuine whisky.
Taking the Bill as a whole, I am satisfied
it is a great improvement on the Act of
1923, and if the Government see their
way clear to bring in any Amendments on
those points I have mentioned they will
have my sympathy and support.
AND
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE
FERRARD: I do not propose to answer
in detail at this stage the various points
that have been raised but I will deal with
them at a later stage of the Bill. I would
like to say one word on the question of the
men who had a full-time licence and who
under this Bill will have to revert to an
early-closing licence. I understand that
certain people who had early-closing
licences were given full-time licences by
the County Court Judge, but there was a
test case and on appeal it was held that
the decision of the County Court Judge
was not justified under the Act of 1923.
M
355 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
Adjournment
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Therefore, these people have really been
trading under a licence they were not
entitled to. All that this Bill does is to
say that they must give up what they
were never entitled to.
Senator Leslie seems to think that the
legislation regarding clubs is not justified
by my statement in moving the Second
Reading, but I think he rather missed my
point, or perhaps I did not make it clear.
Although it is true there have only been
17 new clubs registered since the Act of
1923 was passed, and although the membership has increased by less than 5,000,
the point is that the consumption of
liquor had practically doubled. In view
of that fact can it be said that those clubs
were bona-fide clubs?
Senators Joseph Cunningham and
Barclay raised the question of providing
accommodation for farmers in the early
hours of the morning. I think the hon.
Members will appreciate this fact, that
however desirable it may be and I do
not say it is not desirable--that these
people should have shelter for themselves
as well as stabling for their horses, the
dfficulty would be to secure adequate
supervision to see that intoxicating liquor
was not sold to these people during those
early hours. I do not see what provision
the Government could bring in to meet
that difficulty. On another stage of the
Bill I will reply in detail to the various
points raised during the debate.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Adjournment.
AND
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE
FERRARD: I beg to move, That this
House do now adjourn until Thursday.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Could the noble Viscount tell the House
when the Committee stage of the Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Bill will be
reached so that those who wish to move
any Amendments can have them tabled?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I understand that the
Trade Union and Trade Disputes Bill will
be finished in another place to-morrow
and I thought it would be convenient to
hon. Members if this House sat on Thursday for the First and Second Readings of
that Bill. Then we could take the Committee stage of both Bills on Thursday
week.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Surely it is not the intention to take the
Committee stage of two such important
Bills on the same day.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: It depends entirely on the
Amendments that are put down. Ivannot say what Amendments there will be.
Mr. LESLIE: I thought the proposal of the noble Viscount as regards
the other Bill he mentioned would commend itself to us all. It is an unusual
course, but it would probably be more
suitable, and I do not imagine that the
Committee stages of the two Bills are
likely to involve a very protracted sitting.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five o'clock until Thursday next.
