Business of the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move, That Standing Order No. XXV. be suspended for
this day's sitting for the purpose of taking
the Second Reading of the Trade Disputes
and Trade Unions Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: On several occasions I
have risen in my place here to protest
against the frequent suspension of the
Standing Orders. When speaking to the
noble Viscount, the Deputy Leader of
the House, yesterday, I was told that I
would get a copy of this Bill. It may,
no doubt, be said on behalf of the Government that the Members of this House
have had an opportunity of getting possession of this Bill when it was placed
upon the Table in another place.
generally look forward to any Bill that is
placed upon the Table in another place
We
as
having of necessity to pass through
the storm and stress of political and partisan discussion, and I think we are
justified in anticipating that it will
emerge from another place in another
form to that which it assumed when it was
introduced. Hon. Members of this House
are surely not expected to devote their
time to a study of all the Measures
they are placed upon the Table in another
place when it is to be anticipated that
Amendments will make changes, and,
possibly, substantial changes, in the
Bills. If Members want to have an intelligent grasp of the provisions of these
Measures they must wait until they come
from another place in their complete form.
That is the time we ought to be allowed
opportunities of concentrating
thoughts upon them.
our
The Parliament of Northern Ireland
has no reason to complain of this House.
There is probably not another part of
the vast Commonwealth of Nations constituting the British Empire, of which, I
hope, we are all proud to be a part, that
has had such faithful, economic, efficient
and expeditious service given to all the
Measures submitted for its consideration,
as the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland has rendered to the Government of Northern Ireland in its consideration of all the Measures that have
been submitted to it from time to time.
I think the Members of the Senate of
Northern Ireland ought to feel proud of
the fact that they really can claim to be
Members of the most economical Legislative Chamber in the British Empire.
I am certain of that fact, because I
have children living under six of the
Governments which are members of the
British Empire. They take an interest
in the various Governments under which
they have to live, and I am speaking from
personal knowledge when I say that this
is the most economic and probably the
most efficient, as it is certainly the most
expeditious, Chamber, and that has been
proved by the fact that from time to time
it has suspended its Standing Orders so as
to expedite the business of the State.
Therefore, I propose to exercise my right
as a humble Member to divide the House
on this particular question.
Mr. LESLIE: Speaking generally, I
am fully as prone as any Member of this
Senate to ask for time for the consideration
359 Suspension of Standing Orders
Suspension of Standing Orders 360
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
of Measures placed before us, but in this
particular instance it is, I think, only
right that I should express the opinion
that the proposal which has just been
made by the noble Viscount was made
after consultation with the Members of
the Senate generally. The view taken,
as I understand, was that as it was necessary owing to the late season of the year,
that time should be economized, the point
we had to direct our attention to was, how
could we best utilize such time as was
available. The general opinion, I understood and the noble Viscount will, 1
think, agree was that the better plan
as regards the Bill to which this particular Motion relates, as well as the Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Bill,
was to keep plenty of time between
the Second Reading and the Committee stage, rather than to spend the
time between the First and Second Reading stages.
I remember that two or three years ago
I myself had the advantage of a discussion on this question with the noble
Marquess, the first Leader of this House
(The Marquess of Londonderry), and he
expressed the view that I venture to repeat now, namely, that it is between the
Second Reading and the Committee stage
that time is most required. In the case
of Bills that have attained such public
notoriety as the Trade Disputes and Trade
Unions Bill, and the other Bill which
came before this House last week, it would
be idle to pretend that the Members of
the Senate have no knowledge of the
contents of those Measures before they
are actually laid on the Table here. We
could not bind ourselves to any opinion
as to the details of those Measures but,
on broad principles, I think all of us must
have been acquainted with all it was
necessary to know for the Second Reading for some weeks before. I shall,
therefore support the Motion of the noble
Viscount.
Mr. LONG: I should like to support
the suggestion of Senator Dorman,
that the noble Viscount should kindly
refrain from pressing his Motion. I only
got a copy of the Bill three minutes ago,
and it seems more or less a farce to expect Members to discuss intelligently the
Bill, or even to listen intelligently to
the noble Viscount while he explains the
provisions of the Measure. Therefore I
should be very glad if the noble Viscount
would graciously accede to the request
made by Senator Dorman. I have no
knowledge of the consultation to which
Senator Leslie has referred. I was not a
party to it, and I never heard of it before.
Apart from this particular Bill I think it
is a vicious principle to give a Bill a First
and Second Reading on the same day.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
only intervene to point out one fact. I
am heartily in sympathy with the remarks
that have been made as to the way the
Members of the Senate are treated in regard to some of the Bills that come before the House, but I would like to point
out that one of the causes of the delay in
receiving this Bill was the fact that the
members of Senator Dorman's party in
another place unnecessarily wasted the
time of the Lower House in discussing
this Measure. We must also take into
account the fact that the business must
be carried out by a Parliamentary diary,
and if this Motion before the House was
not carried it would delay and probably
upset the whole Parliamentary programme. I am sure Senator Dorman is
the last person who would desire that that
should happen.
I think it might be a good thing if
the noble Viscount would convey to the
Government the feeling of the Senate that
if a similar proceeding happens again,
the Members of this House will very
strongly object to it. To-day I shall vote
for the Motion for the last time, and I
hope it will not be necessary to bring
forward a similar proposal in the future.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
think that on this particular occasion the
opposition to the Motion before the House
should be waived. While there may not
have been a direct agreement come to
between the noble Vscount and the Members of the Senate, there was an understanding that the First and Second
Readings of this Bill would be taken
to-day, as was done in the case of the
Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Bill
last week. I certainly was under that impression, and as Senator Leslie has
pointed out, we are all more or less acquainted with the principles of this Bill
although we may not be fully acquainted
with the details. I do not see why we
should not proceed with the Second Reading to-day.
to
Na
361 Suspension of Standing Orders 1 DECEMBER 1927
At the same time, I think some effort
should be made to avoid, in future, the
possibility of Bills having to be unnecessarily rushed through this House. There
have been many occasions when the time
available has not been so limited as it
is now, when Bills have been rushed
through their various stages in this
House, and when it was not necessary
that those Bills should have been pressed
as they were. That happened in the case
of the Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing
Bill last week, and it has occurred again
in connection with the Bill now before
the House. On the present occasion,
however, I must support the Motion of
the noble Viscount.
Mr. MCDOWELL: To a certain extent I am sorry I have to rise in support
of Senator Dorman. We cannot get away
from the fact that this Bill is of vital
importance, so far as the industrial
workers of Northern Ireland are concerned. It took another place a considerable time to discuss this measure; in fact,
they have spent nights at it.
Whether
that was a waste of time or not is not
for us to say, but I consider that a Bill of
the importance of this Measure should
have our serious consideration, and that
it should not be given a First and Second
Reading on the same day. A similar
thing happened in the case of the Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Bill last
week, and I understand it is proposed to
take the Committee stages of that Bill
and the one now before the House on
Thursday next.
I contend that we have ample time between now and Thursday to give this Bill
a Second Reading, and to put down any
Amendments we may think necessary. I
think it would be in the interests not
only of this House but of the community
generally that this Bill should be considered in the way it is entitled to be
considered, and if the suggestion of Senator Dorman was adopted we should prodeal with the Bill
bably
expeditiously than we shall do otherwise.
My opinion is that it is time the Members of the Senate were considered a little
bit in these matters, because it is the desire of every Member that each Bill
coming before the House should be fully
and thoroughly considered.
more
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I hope no hon. Member in
Suspension of Standing Orders 362
this House will for one moment imagine
that it was with anything but the greatest
reluctance that this Motion was put before the House. As hon. Members are
aware, the business of this House towards
the close of a Session is verly largely determined by what takes place in another
place, and we are to a certain extent tied
down, as the right hon. and gallant
gentleman (Colonel Sharman-Crawford)
has said, to a diary. It is quite true that
this Bill, in the form in which it left
another place, was only received here very
recently, and it is also true, as Senator
Leslie has pointed out, that the general
principles of this Bill have been known to
all of us for some time past. I fully
admit that this is a very important Bill,
a Bill which, as has been stated, affects
the industrial community of this province.
on
I am entirely in the hands of the House
in regard to this Motion. I have no desire to press it, if it is the wish of the
House that the Second Reading of this
Bill should not be taken this afternoon.
At the same time, I would point out that
the Adjournment on Tuesday I
mentioned that the First and Second
Readings of this Bill would be taken today, and if I recollect aright there was
no objection raised to that course, unless
it was by the hon. Member opposite (Mr.
Dorman.) If the Second Reading is not
taken to-day, it must entail this House
sitting not only in Christmas week, but
possibly afterwards. If hon. Members
wish to do that it is for them to decide,
but it will unquestionably mean the
House sitting probably right up to the
New Year. However, I leave the matter
in the hands of the House to decide. NG
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: If
I may speak again, with the permission of
the House, I should like to say that I see
no reason why we should not sit on Tuesday and take the Second Reading of this
Bill then. We could then take the Committee Stage on Thursday, and this
would not interfere with the Parliamentary diary.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I sympathise
with the Motion more or less. I have
followed the arguments against it, and I
must say I have not been impressed that
there is any reason why we should not
take the Second Reading to-day. I came
here to-day because I understood the
Second Reading was to come on. I have
363 Suspension of Standing Orders
Suspension of Standing Orders 364
THE SENATE
[Mr. Glendinning]
a
travelled
very long distance, and at
this season of the year I cannot very conveniently come from Londonderry to this
House, and I would protest against
having to come here again quite unnecessarily. On the general question I
might agree with Senator Dorman, but I
think it is true to say there is not a Member of this House who is not acquainted
with every Clause of this Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: Question.
Mr. GLENDINNING: If they are
not, they have not been following affairs
in England or Northern Ireland. This
Bill has gone through another place without any Amendment, and I think we are
all familiar with every Clause of it.
Therefore, I think we are prepared to
listen-in spite of what Senator McDowell
says-intelligently to an explanation of
the provisions of the Bill. I am altogether in favour of the Standing Order
being suspended for the purpose of proceeding with the Second Reading.
Mr. MCDOWELL: I should like to say
that in my opinion a Bill is not the property of this House until such time as
it is laid on the Table. It does not matter how long it has been in another place
or before the country. It does not become our property until it is laid on the
Table of this House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The suggestion of Senator
Colonel Sharman-Crawford that the
Second reading be postponed until Tuesday next would certainly meet with my
approval, provided that the House would
agree to take the Committee stage on
Thursday. That would not alter the
Government programme, and it would
give hon. Members who feel very strongly
on this point time to consider the Bill,
although it might inconvenience the hon.
Member for Derry (Mr. Glendinning),
who came specially from Derry to-day in
anticipation of the Bill being read a
Second time.
Mr. LESLIE: I am bound to say that
I am in the same position as Senator
Glendinning. I came here to-day for
the express purpose of taking part in the
Second Reading debate, and I am afraid
I could not personally attend on Tuesday
of next week. I think that after what
was said on the Adjournment on Tuesday
there really is no reason why we should
not take the Second Reading to-day. It
will put several of us to inconvenience if
we alter the proposal, because after all
we shall in effect have been brought here
to-day for no purpose whatever.
Mr. HILL DICKSON : I think it
would be a great pity if the Second Reading of this Bill was postponed after what
was indicated on the Adjournment on
Tuesday. If we heard an explanation of
the Bill from the noble Viscount to-day
we would be in a much better position to
consider what Amendments, if any, might
be necessary before the Committee stage
on Thursday. It would give us a week
instead of only two days to consider that
matter.
Mr. DORMAN: If I may be permitted
to speak again, I should like to say that
the Deputy-Leader of the House consulted me the other day on this question.
I am not disposed to interfere unnecessarily with the transaction of the business of this House, but on numerous
occasions I have protested against Bills
being rushed through this Chamber in
the way that it is proposed to rush this
Bill. If my memory serves me right, I
think the first time I rose in this House
was for the purpose of protesting against
the suspension of this Standing Order. At
that time I did not know anything about
Parliamentary procedure, and I did not
realize what the suspension of that
Standing Order would
I rose and protested against the
proposal, because I thought it would
mean that I would be expected to get up
and speak on a Measure, the provisions
of which I had not had an opportunity
of reading. I was under the impression
that the Deputy-Leader of the House
would furnish me with a copy of the Bill
yesterday, but I understand it did not
emerge from another place until two
o'clock the previous morning. The Bill
has only now come into my possession,
and I am expected to participate in a
discussion of the merits or demerits of its
provisions. I must reluctantly divide the
House on the Motion.
mean.

Mr. SPEAKER: Does the noble Viscount propose to agree to take the Second
Reading next Tuesday? If so, there
will be no necessity for me to put the
Motion.

| Long, Mr. J. A.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed The Lisburn
Electricity Supply Company (Agreement)
Bill, and the Trade Disputes and Trade
Unions Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Lisburn Electric Supply Company
(Agreement) Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time," (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Trade Disputes and Trade Unions Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time,'"--(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move the Second
Reading of the Trade Disputes and
Trade Unions Bill, and in doing so I do
not anticipate that this House will expect from me a lengthy defence of the
policy of the Government in introducing
the Bill to this country. I shall, therefore, content myself with saying that the
Bill is on the Statute Book of the Imperial Parliament, and our Government,
after mature consideration of the provisions which it contains, have come to the
conclusion that it is only proper that it
should be made law in Northern Ireland,
both from the point of view of uniformity
and also because its provisions appear to
be just and reasonable.
Massereene and Ferrard, Viscount
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
| McDowell, Mr. W. J.
The purport of the Bill is clearly
shown in the Title on the first page.
seeks
It
to declare and amend the law relating to trade disputes and trade
unions, to regulate the position of civil
servants and persons employed by public
authorities in respect of membership of
trade unions and similar organizations,
to extend Section 5 of the Conspiracy
and Protection of Property Act, 1875,
and for other purposes connected with
the purposes aforesaid."
I shall take the Clauses of the Bill one
by one, and endeavour to explain, clearly
and concisely, the effect of each.
The first Clause is, of course, the vital
Clause, Sub-clause (1) is framed as being
declaratory and not as an amendment of
the existing law. It declares that a
strike or lock-out is illegal if it satisfies
two conditions:-
1. If it has any object other than or
in addition to the furtherance of a
trade dispute within the industry in
which the strikers or employers lockingout are engaged, and
2. If it is a strike or lock-out designed or calculated to coerce the Government either directly or by inflicting
hardship upon the community.
I should like to emphasise the fact that
the "sympathetic" strike, so-called, is not
prohibited under the clause. Such a
strike might well come under the provisions of the first condition laid down,
that is, its object would be "other than
the furtherance of a trade dispute," but
if it were purely "sympathetic," and if
there were no other ulterior motive it
would not be calculated to coerce the Gov-
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Thus the second condition
ernment.
would not be satisfied and it is the fulfilling of both, and not one, of these
conditions that will render a strike illegal.
The Sub-clause then proceeds to define
"a trade dispute within an industry' and
in this connection I may point out that
it does not alter the existing definition
of "a trade dispute;" it merely copies the
definition from the Trade Disputes Act of
1906 in order to define the larger expression "a trade dispute within a trade or
industry." The general meaning is perfectly clear and does not call for any great
elaboration by me.
Sub-clause (2) lays down the penalties
for engaging in illegal strikes and lockouts, and provides a safeguard for the
ordinary workman who merely ceases to
work and takes no active part in the
furthering of an illegal strike.
Sub-clause (3) provides that where any
person is charged before any court with
an offence under Clause 1, no further proceedings shall be taken against him without the consent of the Attorney-General,
and Sub-clause (4) provides that any person taking part in an illegal strike or
lock-out will be denied the benefit of the
Trade Disputes Act, 1906, which gave
relief from actions for damages for conspiracy, allowed peaceful persuasion, and
relieved officials of a trade union from
any liability for any tort committed on
its behalf.
I come now to Clause 2.
The first
Sub-clause provides that no person refusing to take part in an illegal strike or
lock-out shall on that account be subject
to expulsion from any trade union or
society, or to any fine or penalty, or to
deprivation of any right or benefit, or be
liable to be placed at any disadvantage as
compared with his fellow-members. The
second Sub-clause deals with the same
matter, and gives to a person who has
been penalized by a trade union for refusing to take part in an illegal strike or
lock-out the right to apply to the Court
for redress. Sub-clause (3) is clear. It
provides that the enactment which prevents penalization for refusal to take part
in any illegal strike or lock-out shall be
retrospective to 1st May, 1926.
Clause 3 deals with intimidation, and
makes picketing unlawful if the pickets
attend at or near a house or place where
a person resides or works in such a manner
as to be calculated to intimidate any
person in that house or place. It lays
down the second main principle of the
Bill, which is that no person shall be compelled by threats to cease from work
against his will.
an
Sub-clause (2) alters the existing law
by giving a new meaning to the word
"intimidation." Under Section 7 of the
Conspiracy and Protection of Property
Act, 1895, intimidation is made
offence, but there have been decisions
given that in order to come within the
meaning of that Act the intimidation
practised must include the threat of
personal violence. The new definition
given in this sub-clause is quite clear, and
I may incidentally point out that its
terms are in accordance with the recommendation made by the Royal Commission in 1905, whose chairman was
Lord Dunedin.
Sub-clause (4) amends the picketing
section of the Trade Disputes Act, 1906,
and is designed to prevent the recurrence
of such cases of coercion and undue interference with the liberty of the subject
as have happened in the past. Under
the new provision it will not be lawful for
one or more persons to watch or beset a
house where another person resides for
the purpose of inducing him to work, or
to abstain from working.
Clause 4 deals with provisions as to
political funds. In considering this
Clause it must be borne in mind that
prior to 1913 it was unlawful for a trade
union to have other than what are called
"statutory" objects, that is, objects consisting of the regulation of the relations
between workmen and masters, or the
imposing of restrictive conditions on the
conduct of any trade or business, or the
provision of benefits for members. Trade
unions, therefore, could not apply their
funds to political purposes.
The Trade Union Act of 1913 altered
the position and made it legal for a trade
union to raise political funds subject to
the approval by a secret ballot of the
members concerned, and subject to the
rights of any member to claim exemption
from contributing. There is evidence to
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
1 DECEMBER 1927
EIR

of h

E

TE


1show that the requirements of the Act
have been infringed in several instances,
and undue pressure has been brought to
bear upon workmen by exposing lists of
those claiming exemption, and by levying
political contributions with other contributions, so that a member has no fair
opportunity of objecting to contribute.
With this explanation the meaning of
Sub-clause (1) becomes simple. Briefly
put, it substitutes the method of "contracting in' for 'contracting out" which
was provided by the 1913 Act. Subclause (2) also becomes clear. It provides that all contributions to the political
fund must be levied and made separately
from any contributions to the other funds
of a trade union, and that the assets of
a political fund shall be kept strictly
separate from the assets of other funds
which are not to be applied in furtherance of a political object.
Sub-clause (3) is consequential and
follows upon the preceding Sub-clauses.
The remaining Sub-clauses are simple and
do not require any explanation at this
stage of the Bill's progress.
Clause 5 deals with the position of
established Civil Servants in the Civil
Service of Northern Ireland. In short,
it provides for the making of regulations
prohibiting established Civil Servants
from being members of an organzation of
the nature of a trade union, unless that
organization is confined to persons employed by or under the Crown, and is not
associated with any political party or
organization. I do not think there is any
hon. Member of this House who can
reasonably object to the provisions of this
Clause.
Clause 6 prohibits any local or other
public authority from making it a condition of employment or continuance in
employment, that a person shall or shall
not be a member of a trade union, or to
impose any condition upon employees,
whereby those who are or are not trade
unionists are liable to be placed under
any disability or any disadvantage as
compared with other employees. It also
provides that an employee of a local
authority who wilfully breaks his contract
of employment, knowing that the probable consequence of his action will be injury or grave inconvenience to the community, shall be liable, on summary conviction, to a fine not exceeding £10 or to
imprisonment for a term not exceeding
three months. The Clause is designed
to maintain essential services upon which
the public depends, and which clearly
must be maintained irrespective of trade
disputes.
Clause 7 gives power to the Attorney-
General to apply for an injunction to restrain the illegal application of trade
union funds in contravention of the provisions of Clause 1, and it is then a matter for the court to decide as to the
legality of the strike or lock-out, and as
to whether an injunction should issue to
restrain an application of the funds of
the union to an illegal purpose. It will
be observed that the Clause does not take
away the right of any person, who has
a sufficient interest, to apply for an injunction to restrain an illegal application
of trade union funds.
Clause 8 deals with the furnishing of
returns by trade unions. At present a
trade union registered in Northern Ireland files all its Rules and Returns with
the Register of Friendly Societies for
Northern Ireland, and complete information about these unions is available at
the Registrar's office on payment of the
usual fee. A union registered in Great
Britain and operating in Northern Irland
is, however, required only to lodge its
Rules with the Registrar in Northern Ireland, and it is not obliged by law to
furnish a financial return to the Registrar in Northern Ireland.
The first Sub-clause of the Clause before us proposes to equalise the position
as between Northern Ireland unions and
British unions, and it provides that certified copies of the returns made to the
Registrar in Great Britain shall be forwarded to the Northern Ireland Registrar
with an additional statement giving such
separate Northern Ireland figures as the
Registrar may require.
Sub-clause (2) provides that unions
operating in Northern Ireland from headquarters outside the United Kingdom
shall furnish to the Registrar a return
on the same lines as the returns made by
a Northern Ireland union. Sub-clause (3)
provides that every trade union which is
not registered in Northern Ireland, but
carries on business here shall furnish to
the Registrar the name and address of
some one resident in Northern Ireland
and authorised to accept on behalf of
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
the union service of process, and any
notice. This provision is adapted from
Section 274 of the Companies (Consolidation) Act, 1908, which, as it applies in
Northern Ireland, puts a similar obligation on ordinary trading companies which
are formed outside Northern Ireland but
carry on business inside our area.
Sub-clause (4) is incidental upon Subclause (3) and requires no explanation except that Section 15 of the 1876 Act provides that a trade union which fails to
give any notice or to send any document,
which under the Act it is required to
give or send is liable to a penalty of not
less than one pound and not more than
five pounds. I beg to move.
a
Mr. DORMAN: I appreciate the
lucid explanation the noble Viscount has
given us of this Measure. There never
has been
law promulgated in any
country, from the oldest law of which we
have a record, which was promulgated
four thousand years ago, of which it has
not been said by those who promoted
it that it was in the interests of the community, and for the welfare of the community as a whole. It does not require a
very extensive legal knowledge for any man
who examines the Statute Book of those
days, and concentrates thought upon the
consequences of the various Acts of Parliament which at that time were placed
upon the Statute Book to realize that at
the time they were placed upon the Statute Book they were declared by their
promoters to be in the interests of the
community as a whole, and for the establishment of just relations between the
various members of the community. But
many of these Measures were afterwards
discovered to be of an intensely biassed
character in their application to the community as a whole. Whether the law
was promulgated by an absolute monarch
claiming the Divine right of ruling in
his own person, or by the aristocracy, or
by representatives of Government,
this claim has always been made for legislation that has been placed upon the
Statute Book.
I have closely followed the debate on
this Bill in another place, and I followed,
to some extent, the debates in the Imperial Parliament. I do not see that
many of the speakers-probably because
they might have been ruled out of order,
as I may be examined the circumstances
leading up to the necessity of a Bill such
as this being placed on the Statute Book
either of Great Britain or Northern Ire
land. We passed through a great war,
and the victors, glorying in the victory,
imposed upon the defeated enemy certain
indemnities and reparations. Individuals
pointed out that the consequences of these
reparations would be fatal to the interests
of various economic and commercial undertakings in the victorious countries.
One of the particular industries for which
reparation had to be made was the great
basic industry of coal.
It was pointed out even by the
economic advisers of the British Government that to pursue the policy they were
then pursuing would ultimately mean
chaos, disintegration, and the absolute
destruction of this great basic industry
of our great British Empire. These
prophets have unfortunately proved
correct, and the position of that particular
industry was due to the evil effects of these
reparations. It was in a state of absolute
chaos, and its world-markets were lost,
simply because those markets had been
captured by our late enemies owing to the
coal reparations. The position became
so serious that the Imperial Government
found it necessary to appoint a Royal
Commission to inquire into the position
of the industry. That Commission made
certain recommendations, which were
ignored by the Government. Two Departmental Committees were set up, and
they also made recommendations to the
Government, but their recommendations
were likewise ignored. A second Royal
Commission, known as the Samuels Commission, was set up to inquire into the
chaotic condition of this particular industry. The only remedy that
apparently present in the minds of the
coal owners was the remedy of reducing
the wages or extending the hours of
miners so as to overcome the difficulties
that confronted this basic industry in the
world's markets.
was
I have here the recommendations of
that Royal Commission. It particularly
refers to that claim made by the proprietors of the collieries of Great Britain,
and the Commission unanimously decided
that to adopt that recommendation would
mean chaos. I have no desire to occupy
the time of the House unnecessarily in
making quotations from that Commission's Report. Suffice it to say, that
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
1 DECEMBER 1927
F

the

they picked out this particular suggestion
to reduce wages or prolong hours as an
absolutely futile policy to be pursued if
ever this great basic industry—
Mr. GLENDINNING: On a point of
Order. Unless it is an accepted fact
agreed to by the Members of this House,
that this Bill was solely the outcome of
the coal strike, I do not think, with the
greatest respect, that the hon. Member is
in order.
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps the hon.
Member would allow me to carry out my
duties.
Mr. DORMAN: If hon. Members will
bear with me, they will ultimately see
the connection between what I am saying
and this Bill. I think it will be
generally admitted that the necessity for
bringing in legislation such as this, one
hundred and two years after the repeal of
the Combination Laws, is solely due to
the position to which this great industry
was brought, and in consequence of the
appeal made by those engaged in this
particular industry to their organized
fellow workers. I have evidence to prove
that.
We have to remember, in considering
this argument I am putting forward, that
it was not until this appeal was made
that there was any idea that the organized
workers of Great Britain should come out
on strike. It would have been illegal,
under our Constitution, for the mineowners to have put up notices at the pitheads to the miners, to the effect that
they would have to work an extra hour
underground, unless the Government had,
in the first place, repealed the Act already
on the Statute Book and given them Constitutional power to apply such new conditions on the resumption of work in the
coal mines. I would like to make one
quotation from the Report of the Royal
Commission, which proves what I have
stated.
The increase of working hours now,
so far from being a self-evident partsolution for the coal trouble, is full of
the difficulties which usually attend
reversals of policy.
Further down it is stated:
If Britain lengthened her hours these
other countries
that is, the countries which had captured
our world markets
would certainly consider whether they
should follow suit. In this way any
gain in power of competing with
foreign coal might be swept away. All
that would have been achieved would
be a general lowering of the standard
of leisure in all mining countries.
In the last paragraph of their Report,
the Commission state:-
Extension of working hours at this
time of depression in not a natural, but
an unnatural, way of reducing costs
and meeting the immediate difficulties.
On the other hand, looking away from
the immediate difficulty to the time
when the various measures which are
proposed for reorganization of the industry shall have had their effect, we
see no reason why the standards of
living and leisure already won should
not be maintained.
That was the standard of leisure and comfort the coal miners had attained before
this lock-out. The Royal Commission in
its report saw no reason why that standard should not be maintained while the
industry recovered its prominent position
in the markets of the world.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have purposely
given the hon. Member a good deal of
latitude, but I must now ask him to deal
with the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I regret that I am
not able to continue to pursue that line of
thought, because I think I would have
been able to show hon. Members its connection with the Bill. As I am debarred
from following that line of argument, I
would crave permission to read the appeal
made by the men engaged in this industry in June, 1926.
Mr. SPEAKER: Only if it has some
relation to the Bill before the House. If
not, it would not be in order.
Mr. DORMAN: I think it has, considering that this Bill is based on what
was assumed to be a sympathetic or
general strike, which was the outcome of
this appeal. This is the appeal made in
June last year by the men engaged in the
coal-mining industry of Great Britain :---
Having thus defined the issues, we
ask your Executive to give us the fullest support in continuing our resist
376-
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATË
[Mr. Dorman]
ance to demands which, if submitted
to, will be extended to the whole working class.
The mineowners and the Government
are now merely waiting until the sufferings of our people become unendurable and they are forced by sheer starvation to submit.
We are convinced that the organised
Labour movement will not allow us
to go under, and is fully prepared to
give us the necessary support.
Now Mr. Speaker, you will see the connection.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid I do not.
Mr. DORMAN: The statement proceeds:-
Such support will naturally take two
forms, financial aid and sympathetic
action.
We are asking every trade union to
assist us, both centrally and through
its local organization and membership,
with financial aid.
This Bill is calculated to prevent sympathetic action being taken, because if
men in one trade come out in sympathy
with men in any other trade or industry
they are liable to be fined or imprisoned.
Hon. MEMBERS: No.
Mr. DORMAN: Very well. The appeal concludes:
We also ask all organizations concerned with transport to take definite
sympathetic action by authorising their
members to refuse to handle coal while
the lock-out continues.
We submit these requests for your
earnest consideration, and shall be glad
to have your decision at the earliest
possible moment.
Yours fraternally,
On behalf of the Miners' Federation
of Great Britain.
(Signed),
Herbert Smith, President.
Thomas Richards, Vice-president.
W. P. Richardson, Treasurer.
A. J. Cook, Secretary.
a
con-
The result of that lock-out and that
appeal was that a large number of the
organized trades unionists of Great
Britain, in sympathy with their fellow
workers in the coal industry, withdrew
their labour from the market in which
they usually sold it, in order to manifest
their feeling against what they
ceived to be the oppression imposed on
their fellow workers in another branch
of industry. There has always been a
state of fear and dread in the minds of
a certain class with regard to the actions
of the working classes when an industrial
conflict has taken place. I was struck
in reading in this connection from an
authority that I do not think any Member
of this House will object to have quoted.
Mr. Benjamin Disraeli wrote
book
called "Cybil, or The Two Nations,"
which I would commend to the Members
of this House. He was speaking of a
time when the country was passing
through a crisis something like the crisis
of last year when the general strike took
place. The governing class, and certainly
the upper classes whose opinions the
writer imaginatively tries to describe
were in a state of panic, and he directs
these words to them at that particular
time. We who are members of the great
Labour Party, the coming party in Great
Britain, and I hope in Northern Ireland,
were accused of creating class hatred and
ill-will, and of stirring up strife in the
community of which we are a part, but
under almost similar circumstances Benjamin Disraeli wrote the following words:
"Two nations between whom there is
no intercourse, and no sympathy, who
are as ignorant of each other's habits,
thoughts and feelings as if they were
dwellers in different zones, or inhabitants of different planets, who
formed by a different breeding, are fed
by different food, are ordered by different manners, and are not governed
by the same laws, the Rich and the
Poor."
are
Then he goes on to say in another part
of his book and this is almost prophetic
of the conditions that prevail in Great
Britain, if not in Northern Ireland, today:-
"Predominant opinions are generally
the opinions of the generation that is
vanishing. Let an accident, which
speculation could not foresee, the
balanced state at this moment of Par-
377-

Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
1 DECEMBER 1927


liamentary parties, cease, and in a few
years, more or less, cease it must, and
you will witness a development of the
mind of England, which will make up
by its rapid progress for its retarded
I live among these men; I
action.
know their inmost souls; I watch their
instincts and their impulses; I know
the principles which they have imbibed,
and I know, however hindered by circumstances for the moment, those
principles must bear their fruit. It
will be a produce hostile to the oligarchical system. The future principles of
English politics will not be a levelling
principle; not a principle adverse to
privileges, but favourable to their extinction. It will seek to ensure equality,
not by levelling the few, but by elevating the many.
All that the people want is justice;
that labour should be as much respected
by law and society as property."
That is what I believe this Bill is calculated to retard. This Bill is distinctly
calculated to retard the combination of
Labour, not in the industrial field, but in
the political field. So long as the workers
were willing, as I have known they
were willing during the last quarter of
last century, through the Parliamentary
Committee of the Trade Union Congress,
to set a certain number of their members
apart and pay them for remaining in the
vicinity of the Parliament of Great
Britain and Ireland in London, for the
purpose of lobbying the Members of the
two great political parties in the State,
the Tories and the Liberals, no objection
was raised. There would be no objection
at all to their continuing to spend money
in that way, but there is objection to their
spending money for the purpose of getting
direct representation in Parliament, and
in my opinion one of the objects for which
this Bill is going to be placed upon the
Statute Book is to prevent the workers
obtaining that direct representation to
which they are entitled.
If we look back at the constitution of
Parliament in Great Britain and Ireland
as it existed 100 years ago, we find that
267 individuals had the right of nominating 487 Members of the Imperial Parliament out of a membership of 658.
Some people each had the right of nominating half a dozen Members. I hope I
will not be out of order when I state that
a nobleman of County Down had the right
of nominating three Members of the Irish
Parliament before it was abolished, and
he received considerable fees for relinquishing his privileged position of nominating those Members. I have gone into
the figures, and I have calculated that if
his ancestor had invested that money at
5 per cent., it would to-day amount to a
sum of £23,000,000. It was stated then
that 280 persons had the right of nominating 487 Members of Parliament, and
that there was an electorate of only
500,000 persons in Great Britain and
Ireland.
In other words, the Parliament of
100 years ago was elected by 100,000
odd less electors than we have on the Parliamentary franchise of this little State
of Northern Ireland to-day. The fran.
chise has been widened, and widened on
foundations that constitute the aggregate
of sovereign power, and instead of one out
of twenty-eight having the right to exercise the Parliamentary franchise, one in
two has the right of exercising the Parliamentary franchise to-day. It is only
to be expected that when the vast majority
of people have the right of exercising the
Parliamentary franchise, they will seek
to obtain direct representation in Parliament.
Mr. SPEAKER: I find it very difficult
to connect what the hon. Member is saying with the Bill before the House. I
wish he would find it possible to stick to
the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: It is perhaps my
ignorance of Parliamentary procedure
that causes me to get off the beaten
track when speaking on an important Bill
such as this. I believe this Bill is going
to create new crimes. It is going to
bring chaos into many branches of municipal undertakings in Great Britain and
Northern Ireland, which thirty-five or
forty years ago were in a state of chaos
through men leaving their work because
of the injustice of long hours and so on.
I believe that if the heads of the most
efficient departments of the municipalities of Great Britain and Northern Ireland were consulted to-day they would
place it on record that the introduction of
organizations containing 100 per cent. of
the workers of the various municipal
branches over which they have control
has brought peace, efficiency, and economy into those municipal undertakings.
I believe that if our gas manager in Bel-
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
fast, one of the most eminent gas managers to be found in any part of the world,
was consulted on this particular question
he would say it was his desire that 100
per cent. of the men under his control
should always be in one organization, because that would enable him to satisfactorily deal with the men through the
officials of that organization.
What will working men have to decide
under this Bill? The ramifications of
trades unions extend over a large number
of industries and trades and if I am requested by the Committee of my trade
union to go and interview a member of
a trade union, who has come out on strike
it may be that immediately I have interviewed that individual that strike may be
declared to be illegal. There are various
trades whose ramifications extend to all
industries. The engineering trade, for
instance, is probably represented in every
large undertaking in Belfast. If the
engineers in any of the shipyards strike
against what they conceive to be an injustice so far as their remuneration is
concerned, would the engineers, say, in
some of the big linen mills in Belfast,
who are members of the same trade union
as the men in the shipyard, be justified
in coming out on strike also, and if they
did come out on strike would they be
liable to the penalties imposed under this
Bill? How is an engineer to decide
whether or not he is participating in a
strike confined to a particular industry
under circumstances such as these?
is impossible for him to decide that
question.
It
He will have to ask himself what is the
object of the strike, and whether the
object is to intimidate the Government.
Eight years ago eight and a half million
workers in Great Britain and Northern
Ireland were members of trades unions.
Assuming that each member represented
a family of four, it meant that the part of
the community which constituted trades
unions was composed of thirty-two
million people out of a total of forty-five
million people. The man who is interviewing a person who is on strike has also
to consider whether the individual he is
interviewing has
reasonable apprehension of suffering an injury. It is wellknown to everybody that traitors are
cowards and that it is part and parcel
of their cowardly nature that they entertain unreasonable apprehensions. That
a
is the case whether the traitor is engaged
on the battlefield or in the navy, and I
know something about one branch of the
service, having served in it for many
years.
A coward is always pre-eminetly distinguished by entertaining unreasonable
anticipations, and his cowardice is
mainly composed of the entertainment of
these unreasonable anticipations. Why
do not the Government interfere with
other organizations that are inflicting
injury on the community? There is a
large trade union of newsagents and
book-sellers in Belfast. A friend of mine
recently invested a large portion of his
savings in starting a bookshop and he
made application for membership of this
organization. But he is debarred from
Membership because he has not established his business outside a quarter mile
radius from the business of any other
member of that organization. That is
tyrannical, and why should the Government not legislate to prevent such a thing
as that?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: And
that is a trade union.
it.
Mr. DORMAN: You are a member of
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
not.
Mr. DORMAN: I am sorry. This is
the first reactionary principle introduced
in this country for 125 years, since the
first Apprentices Act was placed upon the
Statute Book. Hon. Members who will
take the trouble to see how the workers
were treated under the old Combination
laws will realize that they were not treated
as badly as they will be under this Bill
when its provisions come to be applied by
the courts when conflicts take place between capital and labour. Under the
old Combination laws fourteen men were
engaged in the winter in a certain business undertaking, and their employer
proceeded to deduct 105d. from their
wages for light during the dark hours of
those winter days. When the fourteen
men withdrew their labour as a protest
against the employer's action they were
brought before a court of summary jurisdiction and sent to prison for three
months. A celebrated economist who is
called the father of British political
economy, Adam Smith, points out in the
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.

not 5
A
"Wealth of Nations" that under these
old Combination laws the employers
combined to reduce wages, prolonged
hours, and worsen conditions, and that
they frequently sat on the Bench and administered the law against their
employees who had gone on strike.
own
This Bill has produced a lot of talk
about a general strike. There never has
been a general strike under what we call
free labour. There have been three great
strikes but no general strike under free
labour in any country. There was the
great strike of the Israelites in Egypt
when they went out of Egypt. Probably
had they remained behind they would
have found that the law was too powerful
for them, but they had to get out to get
free. There was the great strike of the
slaves in one of the states of Greece, and
there was also the great strike of slaves
in one of the West Indian Islands. There
have been other strikes in Western China
during recent years when all those engaged in that particular area ceased work.
What is a general strike? May I
presume to give a definition? I believe
a general strike may be said to have taken
place when those in any town, county,
province or country who have no means
of livelihood but the sale of their labour
power from day to day withdraw that
labour power from the control of those
to whom they are accustomed to sell it
in order to obtain their living. As
Mr. Ramsay Macdonald has said, there
never has been a general strike under free
labour. He stated that it was the last
resort of a lunatic, and I think my colleague, Mr. William McMullen, M.Р.,
improved upon that when he said it
would be the first resort of a lunatic and
the last resort of sensible men. A general
strike would mean the withdrawal of the
labour of the cook, the butler, the housemaid, the nurse, the keepers in
asylums, the warders in our prisons, and
by all those who have no means of living
except by the sale of their labour.
S
our
A general strike of that kind would
bring us back to a state of barbarism and
avagery. Under a civilization such as
☑ ☑urs there cannot be a general strike.
eople talk vaguely of a general strike
ithout taking time to visualise what it
Jould mean to a civilized community as
whole. A general strike would not be a
It would be a revelation;
revolution.
t would reveal to those who conol industry and command the credit
of the country that, after all, we are all
inter-dependent, and that the many are
not under a greater obligation to them
than they are to the many by reason of
the service the many give to them from
day to day.
As I have quoted an eminent Tory
statesman may I be permitted to also
quote an eminent Liberal statesman.
The words are taken from an essay by the
eminent Duke of Argyle on the subject of
Law and Politics. He said:-
،،
What combination does for the
protection of labour, it does better,
and with better consequences, than
positive institutions. Men are driven
to excessive labour, because if they don't
work excessively, others will. But it
is the effect of combination that others
won't
*Under positive institutions they are not allowed. Under
combination they are determined not."
He goes on to say:-
"It tends to subordinate the present
to the future, and the temporary interests of self to the permanent welfare of the brotherhood of men. And
this it tends to do in classes otherwise
prone to follow only the impulses of
the moment.
I believe the Governments of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland are largely
following the impulses of the moment in
trying to place legislation such as this on
the Statute Books of both countries.
SO
"and to consider only the apparent interests of the individual. These considerations should disabuse our minds
of the unjust and unreasonable prejudice against the principle of combination which still betrays itself
strongly in the language of many
politicians. When the working classes
combine for the protection of their
labour against the effects of unrestricted
competition, they are simply taking
that course which is recommended alike
by reason and by experience. It is the
course which Parliament has indicated
as the right course both by what it has
itself done, and by what it has declined
to do."
Parliament had indicated the course
because other interests had combined to
obtain representation in Parliament to
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
protect themselves, and why should
Labour not do likewise? There is not one
working man out of probably 500,000 who
could aspire in his individual capacity to
be returned to Parliament. How many
working men are in a position to write a
cheque for the nomination fee of £150
to entitle them to become a candidate for
a seat either in the Imperial Parliament
or Northern Parliament? Even if they
had sufficient money to put down the
necessary nomination fee how many
members of the working class could face
the expense of £1,000 or £2,000 that
would be entailed in fighting a Parliamentary election They could not do so,
and therefore they have found it necessary to do what was stated by the Duke
of Argyle in his book-to combine
together for a specific purpose and under
more just and reasonable conditions.
I was reading in the newspapers yesterday about two of the great armament
firms, one of which has written down its
capital by eleven million pounds and the
other by twelve millions. That had been
done as a result of a majority vote of the
shareholders under the Limited Liability
Act. Have the minority of those shareholders the right to vote themselves out
of that scheme? No. I myself have a
few shares in a company the capital of
which has been reduced to one-eighth of
its original value.
Mr. JOSEPH
Capitalism.
CUNNINGHAM:
Mr. DORMAN: Does the law give me
the right as a minority to vote myself out
of that Limited Liability Company and
secure my savings? It has meant a reduction of all the savings I have on earth
to one-eighth of their value a year ago.
In my opinion this Bill has been conceived in fear, it has been born in a panic,
and it is calculated in a very short time
to come to an untimely end. The only
hope for the working classes of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland is that the
promoters of this Bill, those who have
fathered this reactionary Measure, may
come down from the seats of the powerful
before that time arrives. We are not
asking anything unreasonable. We are
asking that a fair opportunity should be
given to the workers to combine in the
industrial field and in the Parliamentary
field, just as the Limited Liability Companies and Legal Associations and other
such organizations have a right to decide
what they will do by means of a majority
vote of their members. We claim that
the Government should continue to allow
us to use that majority vote in the old
way, extending the right to everyone of
our members, if they wish to exercise it,
of contracting out of the obligation to pay
this Parliamentary levy.
Where is the justification for this Bill?
Where are the petitions from the trade
unions? Where are the court records
that any particular member of a trade
union in Great Britain or Northern
Ireland has been persecuted or penalized
or has been assaulted or injured because
he has exercised his right to contract out
when the majority of the members of his
trade union have decided to contract in
and pay a reasonable contribution to the
upkeep of the party that is out to try to
establish more just, more reasonable, and
a more merciful relationship between
citizens themselves and between employers
and employees. I feel that in the further
stages of this Bill it would be utterly futile
to exercise my right to waste the time of
the Senate by moving amendments, but I
propose to exercise my right to divide the
House on every Clause of the Bill in order
to at least hand down to future generations a record of the fact that there was
one Member of the Senate who saw the
possible evil consequences of legislation
of this particular kind which, as I have
already said, was conceived in fear and
born in panic.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
am sure we are all somewhat puzzled to
know whether Senator Dorman was
speaking in favour of or against the
Second Reading of this Bill. Some of the
arguments he put were satisfactory from
my point of view, particularly his reference to the Newsagents' Association,
which was formed some time ago, a body
which is so tyrannical that its members
object to another newsagent starting a
business within a certain radius of the
businesses of the members of that association. It is another instance of the
tyranny that can be exercised by a body
of persons. Senator Dorman almost led
the House to believe that this Bill was
the result of the general strike brought
about in a hurried manner owing to the
coal strike, but surely he forgets that the
trade unions and the Labour Socialist
party threatened the country for years
and years that they were going to have

a general strike. Therefore, it did not
require the coal owners' lock-out or
strike to bring about that general strike.
And let me tell Senator Dorman more,
that they had actually set up a body and
had asked the trade unions throughout
the country to vote for or against giving
this particular council power to control

*Therefore, the general strike was not an

or
I am very pleased to see this Bill introduced, and I am glad that our
Government have followed the policy of
Great Britain step by step. Never was
there a greater necessity for a Bill of this
character than there is at the present
time. Trade unionists such as myself
and others in Northern Ireland
are very closely associated with trade
unions in England. Our headquarters
are nearly all situated in London and
some are in Manchester and Newcastle,
so that to have legislation controlling the
affairs of trade unions for England alone
without the Northern Paliament adopting the same legislation would be injurious to trade unionists as a whole.
It has been said that those who are likely
to support the Second Reading of this
Bill are inspired by motives of hostility
to trade unions. I do not think there is
any person who would dare to say that I
am prompted by a feeling of class or
partisan hostility to trade unions. No
man, I do not care who he is, has a greater
or higher respect for trade unions than
I have.
There was no necessity for Senator
Dorman to put forward arguments in
favour of trade unions, because I am
satisfied that they are a great benefit to
the country. They prevent a great deal
of friction and trouble between employers
and workmen, and I am satisfied that in
Belfast and the North of Ireland we have
very sensible and moderate-minded trade
union officials, who, I believe, help considerably to settle differences that may
crop up between employers and their
workers, For the past twenty years I
have given the whole of my spare time
to the trade union of which I am a member, that is the Engineers' Society, and I
feel very proud when I think how the
members of that trade union appreciate
my efforts as shown by the fact that they
have elected me to some of the highest
positions they could place me in in that
society. It is something for one to look
back upon with pride, and I can honestly
and openly say that I have given of my
best to the interests of the members of
that society, and I believe with very good
results.
During the time I occupied those
positions improved conditions were obtained for that trade without any strike
or any trouble. At the time I am speaking of the trade unions were conducted in
a fair and proper manner. We had men
of all shades of political and religious
views associated in one trade union for industrial purposes, and the members were
most particular that no offence was given
to those who were in the minority. Had
a man suggested anything of a political
or religious nature he was severely
penalized. Those were the days when
trade unions had no politics. They knew
no politics, and they had to pay
political levy. I welcome this Bill because I believe it will improve the trade
unions and will restore to them the
freedom and liberty that has been
filched from them. During the past few
years a small body of extremists has
gradually obtained control of the
machinery of trade unions. The great
majority of trade unions have become
disgusted with these extremists, and they
have left the administration of those
trade unions to this small minority to
carry on as they wish.
Mr. DORMAN: Shame.
no
It
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
is a shame. The ordinary member is
absent from the meetings of his trade
union while these extremists monopolize
the official positions and carry on the
affairs of the union as they think fit. One
thing every person must admit, and that
is that this Bill has been one of the
biggest surprises that any Opposition.
particularly the Labour-Socialist party,
could have had. They hoped to engineer
great opposition to this Measure, and they
expected the workers to rise in arms and
create a revolution because the British
Government in the first instance and the
Northern Government afterwards introduced this Measure. But the fact of the
matter is that the workers throughout
the country were glad that this Bill was
introduced. There is no doubt about
that. It has saved them from the
N
387 Trade Disputes Bill-

THE SENATECT
Second Reading.
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
tyranny of those people who are trying
to dominate the positions and use the
honest trade union members for political
reasons. I believe that all workers
throughout the country with the exception of a small number of extremists are
very pleased to find this Bill introduced
for their safety and welfare.
It must be admitted that trade unions
were formed and set up by decent, honest
workmen, who bound themselves together
for industrial purposes, to improve their
conditions and to protect themselves from
unscrupulous employers. While that
policy was carried on it was very satisfactory. Then we find that a number
of years ago the Labour-Socialist party
introduced the political levy and persuaded the workers to submit to this levy.
They said, "You cannot be represented
in Parliament by bloated capitalists or
employers. It is necessary for workmen to
go to Parliament themselves; it is necessary for one who knows the exact conditions under which the workers live and
work to be able to go to Parliament to
properly express the feelings of the
workers." They persuaded the workers
to submit to this levy, but what do we
find. to-day? We do not find the Labour-
Socialist party saying that it is only the
worker who is entitled to represent the
workers. Only recently we find Mr.
Mosley, a nobleman who, I believe, had
Royalty attending his marriage, standing
as a Labour candidate, and when he was
seeking election, instead of referring to
his wife in the manner in which she was
entitled to be referred to, he called her
"Mrs." to make her more popular
amongst the mill workers and miners.
Such piffle and bluff! One is surprised
that men could be gulled by such a proceeding. It is not only the young men
who are gulled, because you even see greyhaired men like myself taken in by such
piffle and nonsense. Not only have they
wealthy men in the Labour party, but
they have men of title, and what in the
name of heavens they know about the
conditions of the workers, or how they
live or work, is more than I can understand. As for lawyers, at one time the
Labour-Socialist
party looked upon
lawyers as the greatest enemies they
could have, and now there are any number
of K.C.'s in the party looking for posts.
It is ridiculous to think of the way the
poor workers have been gulled by these
extremists. They have been successful in
exploiting the workers and obtaining this
political levy from them, and they have
got such an amount of money that the
late Mr. Keir Hardie was able to have a
trip to Egypt and India, and Mr. Ramsay
MacDonald has been able to have a similar
trip at the expense of workers like myself.
They have been successful in getting
that money, and they have attempted to
control the trade union movement, but I
am afraid they have made a terrible
blunder, and there is no doubt they have
acted in a brutal way so far as the controlling of the trade union movement is
concerned. In the first place, they engineered the general strike undoubtedly
for political purposes. They thought
they would be able to drive the British
Government out of office, and step into
office themselves. The Government were
well aware that the workers wanted some
change in the law relating to trade
unions. They were continually appealing
change because trade
unions were being carried on for purposes
for which they were never intended. They
came along with the general strike which
they forced on the people, but, unfortunately for them, they had not the workers
behind them.
for such

a
mistake

have pointed out what
their action was, but the Labour party

The Government were forced to take
action which I think was necessary, and
if the general strike did nothing else
except to force the British Government
to protect the trade unionists of the
country from these tyrannical individuals
who had obtained control of the unions it
has done
great deal. It has
saved him from very many humiliating positions. The question is
asked repeatedly why this Bill has been

said in the earlier part of my speech, how

are striking. The food supply is cut off.
Trains cease running, and there is no
necessary it was that legislation should

that carried in Great Britain. There is
one thing sure, and that is this. I believe
means there is no place throughout the United

than in the North of Ireland.
I know
they were much disgusted and dissatisfied
with the conditions as they were with regard to trade unions, and there was considerable agitation and friction to have
some change made. There was no one did
more than myself to draw the attention
of the Government to the injury which
the decent trade unionist was suffering.
an
Since the introduction of this Bill they
have howled at the street corners and at
other meetings about the injustice inflicted on trade unionists, but the most
remarkable thing is that these people,
who are howling most about trade
unionism, are men with no record of
trade union work, and who simply know
nothing about trade unions. For instance, we find insurance agents championing the cause of trade unionsinsurance agents who have been
infliction on trade unions, as I know.
They are the men who lapse policies on
their poor unfortunate victims, and when
death comes the poor people get no
insurance benefit. Some of these are the
people who are championing the cause of
decent trade unionists in the country,
telling the Government it is an injustice
they are inflicting on trade unionism by
passing this Bill. It can be said at the
same time that there is no injustice inflicted on trade unionists. Trade unions
have still the right to strike, just the
same as they ever had. It is altogether
ridiculous
that the trade
say
unionist is hampered in any of his legitimate rights. If any disagreement or
trouble occurs between employer and employee and the society representing the
employees, it is quite within their rights
to withdraw their labour at any time.
The only restriction placed by this Bill
on the workers is that they shall not strike
against the Government or against the
community. And, good gracious, anyone
will admit that you are only protecting
the worker against himselfto
Col. SHARMAN CRAWFORD : Hear,
hear.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: -by
insisting on such restrictions. In
a
of getting proper food to the
proper localities. The result is that the
greatest sufferers are the wives and children of the strikers. It is a case of the
Government protecting the workers from
themselves.
I remember speaking to someone who
had been very busy during the general
strike on the North-East Coast. They had
been sending fish and flesh from a big
centre to the smaller districts. It seems
that the people, the miners particularly,
in these smaller districts are the most
difficult to please. They insisted on
having fresh fish when it was impossible
to get it, and fresh meat when it was
difficult to send it. That is the difficulty
they found themselves in. Actually, the
strike that did take place was simply an
endeavour to coerce the Government, but
at the same time it was causing tremendous grievance and hardship to the
workers themselves and their families
during the coal strike. I do not think
there is anyone in a decently conducted
trade union movement who would support a general strike. Then we have
picketing. Certainly some restrictions
have been introduced with reference to
picketing. I did not hear even Senator
Dorman say one word against the Bill as
far as the restriction on picketing is concerned. I have always been very willing
to urge my fellow workmen to become
trade unionists, and to stand shoulder to
shoulder with one another, but I never
did see that it was proper or humane to
inflict injury or to force those who were
not so willing as others at the time to
strike. The cases we read of during the time
of the miners' strike were deplorable.
There is not a decent trade unionist would
defend such conduct. I say it is well the
Government have introduced legislation to
prevent such coercion during these periods.
As far as the levy is concerned, which
Senator Dorman made reference to, I
think this is the Clause which gives tha
greatest satisfaction to the greatest number of trade unionists throughout Northern
Ireland to-day. In the North of Ireland,
unfortunately, we had to suffer a double
dose of this political levy. Not only were
the workers required to pay a levy for the
Imperial elections, but they were also
called upon to pay a levy for the Northern
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
penalty imposed on the workers. If anyone would ask the reason why this Bill
should be applied to Northern Ireland I
think that is complete justification more
than anything else. There is more reason
why Northern Ireland should have this
Bill than why the British people should
have it, because they are only penalized
to the extent of one shilling a quarter
against Northern Ireland having to pay
a double levy.
Another thing must be admitted to be
very unfair, and I am sure any decent
honourable trade unionist will admit it.
It was very unfair to force a young man
to join a trade union for industrial purposes-it was looked upon as something
necessary for a boy finishing his apprenticeship to become immediately a member
of a trade union which was kept in existence for the protection of the interests
of the trade, and then for him to find, although he may have been a Nationalist,
a Liberal, or a Conservative, he was forced
to pay subscriptions towards a party which
he probably detested and looked upon
with disgust. That he should be asked
to pay a levy towards the upkeep of these
people was more than anyone could accept
or tolerate, especially in a place like Northern Ireland where the people had been
so willing for years and years even to fight
to maintain that civil and religious liberty
with which they had been so much impressed. I think it was almost impossible
to expect that they would allow the levy
to go on for any length of time without
trying to force the Government to take
some proceedings.
As far as the Civil Servant is concerned
I must admit that I think it is only proper
and right that a Civil Servant should be
responsible only to his employer and to
the Government. I think it is very unfair that they could belong to a trade
union, or be associated with those revolutionary. people who may be inclined to
bring them out on strike and create
trouble and disturbance in Government
circles. As far as the Civil Servants are
concerned I very much approve of the
Bill. If it was not for the Labour Socialist Party's deceptive methods
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:-by
which they have taken control of the trade
unions one might not have had reason to
complain of trade union law at the present
time. When the trade unions were being
conducted by the old trade unionists
they gave their services liberally and
freely. It was at that time a labour of
love. You very often heard of a trade
unionist who gave his services for two or
four hours in the evening without any
return or any pay. That is all changed.
The administration of the different
societies now since the Socialist party got
control has increased tremendously. Instead of a labour of love these Socialists
in office insist on getting their full pound
of flesh. They insist on being paid for
everything they do.
I think they have
adopted the Yorkshireman's advice, "If
thou dost ought for nought do it for
thysel." They do nothing for any other
person. If the Socialists had acted towards the trade unions as the old trade
unionists acted there would have been no
need for this restrictive legislation.
am very pleased indeed to give my support to the Second Reading of the
Measure.

Mr. LESLIE : A Measure corresponding
in its terms to the Bill which is before us
this afternoon has, during the present
year,
been discussed ad infinitum
across the Channel, and the actual
Bill which we have here to-day has
been discussed in another place perhaps
one might say ad nauseam. I think that
what emerges from all the discussions is
a recognition on the part of all people
who are reasonably sane that the Bill contains nothing but certain prophylactic
provisions which are necessary as a safeguard to the public as a whole. When I
say the public as a whole I mean deliberately to include the employee just as
much as the employer. The Bill, in fact,
is really nothing but the common law as
defined in a recent judgment commonly
known as the Astbury judgment-nothing
but the common law supported by such
additional provisions as modern developments have shown to be necessary.
protection given in the Bill is really under
four heads. The public as a whole is to
be protected from coercion. The wouldbe worker is to be protected from intimidation, and the extension given to the
definition of intimidation is really nothing
more than we have been accustomed to
for some years past when it has been necessary to deal with that matter.
The
worker is also to be protected from having
his money spent against his will on subjects of which he does not approve, and
