And now after the able exposition of
the aims of true labour and real labour
which have been given, not for the first
time, by the hon. Senator on my left, I
do not think there is any necessity to
detain the House longer on the subject,
to the general principles of which I have
already alluded, but I want to say a few
words now as regards the methods of
administration which may perhaps unbecome
fortunately
all hope that this Bill when it becomes
an Act will remain quiescent. We should
like it to be a strictly dormant statute,
but it may be necessary to enforce the
provisions of this Bill, and, if so, the
enforcement will have to be the work of
the courts. One of the main principles
after all of the Bill is, that no association
whatever is above the law, and the law
is above any association. If we have to
go to the courts to enforce this Measure
it is possible that one of three courts may
be resorted to. First of all, the court of
summary jurisdiction, and as to that court
there is apparently a somewhat complicated tangle which I am afraid it will
be necessary to ask the House in Committee to unravel.
was
The Bill before us, as hon. Senators
will see, is to be construed and cited as
one with the Trade Union Acts, 1871 onwards. Now, the Trade Union Act of
1871, the first of the series, most clearly
provides for the hearing of cases by a
court of summary jurisdiction, which,
in Ireland, outside Dublin, was to consist of one resident magistrate. The expression used
the rather loose
expression "resident magistrate" and of
that I am rather glad. It further provided for appeal to the ordinary court of
quarter sessions. It, therefore, would
seem to follow that any summary jurisdiction proceedings under this Bill, when
it becomes an Act, will be taken before
a court consisting of a resident magistrate.
There is another Act referred to many
times in this Bill and closely connected
with it, and when this Bill is passed the
two Acts will be inextricably interwoven.
I am referring to the Conspiracy and Protection of Property Act, 1875, to one of
the sections of which we are adding a
provision. Now, under the Conspiracy and
Protection of Property Act, provision is
made for the constitution of a court of
summary jurisdiction in Ireland, and it
is provided that that court of summary
jurisdiction shall consist of two magistrates sitting in petty sessions, and again
appeal is given to the ordinary court of
quarter sessions. It seems, therefore, that
if we pass this Bill as it is under one set
of contigencies which might conceivably
arise-I think it is in Clause 2-that
proceedings would have to be taken before one court and in another set of contingencies they would have to be taken
before another court.
What would happen as regards section 5
of the Conspiracy Act I really do not
know.
It would appear that the one
part would be tried by the ordinary
magistrates and the other part by
another court. Of course, it is I think
obvious, unless there is some explanation
that that state of affairs cannot be
allowed. Therefore, I am proposing
to ask the House when in Committee to
accept an amendment definitely confining
the jurisdiction under the Trades Disputes
Act, 1871, the Conspiracy and Protection
of Property Act, 1875, and this Act, to
the resident magistrates, with appeal to
the county court judge sitting alone. I am
not proposing to include the new Emergency Powers Act which we passed last
year and which is also referred to in this
Bill, because, if I understand the matter
correctly, the powers conferred by that
Act on His Grace to make regulations
are sufficently wide to enable His Grace
to provide for that court.
a
Leaving the court of summary jurisdiction we come to the county court. The
county court may, perhaps, be called into
operation under an early Clause of the
Bill, and, as a matter of fact, I think I
am correct in saying the county court in
England has already been put in operation in Liverpool in connection with
case arising there. There is nothing to
be said as to the county court for the
county court is under our own control.
There is also the possibility of the intervention of the high court being sought,
and the possibility of the high court coming into the matter appears, for the first
time, in the same Clause as the county
court. What we are doing there seems
to me to be nothing but providing what
I should describe as an alternative atonement to be made by the wrong-doer, but
the position seems to me to become very
different when we get to Clause 7. That
is one of the most essential Clauses of
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the Bill-I think the noble Viscount drew
special attention to it-the Clause which
enables the Attorney-General to take certain action. In that Clause we have
followed, word for word, the English Act,
and, so far as England is concerned, I see
no objection to the wording of that
Clause. But there is a point, I think,
we must consider here, and although I
shall put an Amendment on the Paper
it is quite possible that discussion may
show there is no reason for alteration. The
difficulty is, we say an injunction may be
granted. The power of granting an injunction is inherent in the high court. It was
specially preserved for our own high court
in 1920. It is one of the foundations of
the equitable jurisdiction. The power to
grant an injunction is one which this Parliament did not give, and I do not think
this Parliament can give. It certainly
cannot take it away, and I think we ought
not to attempt to define it. It seems to me
that it will be necessary to make some
slight alteration, so to speak shedding the
shadow and supporting and securing the
substance. May be by some words we should
definitely give to our Attorney-General the
power which at present we imply, but we
should refrain from telling the high court
by what means they are to do their work.
That, as I say, is a matter that really calls
for most careful consideration on the part
of those competent to express an opinion.
I am sure it would be most repugnant to
this House to do anything in any way
trenching on a reserved subject, such as
the prerogative of the high court.
There is another officer to whom I want
to refer, that is, the officer appointed to
exercise in Northern Ireland the functions
of the registrar of friendly societies.
It
will be seen that that officer has very
important functions. I am, therefore,
going to ask the noble Viscount to be so
kind as to tell us at a later stage whether
he is quite certain, to put it bluntly, that
the provisions we have made respecting
that officer are watertight. The difficulty,
if there is one, seems to arise in this way.
The work of the friendly societies was
committed to the Government of Northern Ireland in 1920. This Parliament, if
I remember correctly, put the matter
under the supervision of the Ministry of
Commerce. The Minister of Commerce
appointed an officer to discharge those
duties and that officer, as I think we all
know, is the permanent secretary of the
Ministry of Commerce. But the question
that does occasion some little anxietythough I am quite prepared to believe it
is all right-is whether by a chain of
adaptation orders or otherwise, we have
sufficiently thrown into the hands of our
registrar all the work of societies registered under the Trade Union Act of 1913.
Hitherto, all the work in Northern Ireland under the Trade Union Acts, which
required the registrar, was done by the
assistant registrar. I am, therefore,
asking the noble Viscount to satisfy us
at a later stage that everything in that
way is as it should be.
I have two questions to ask the noble
Viscount which he can reply to at a later
stage. I do not wish to press for a reply
now. I want to draw attention for a
moment to Sub-clause 3 of Clause 2. I
want to ask whether I am correct in
assuming that that Sub-clause is introduced only for the sake of conformity
with the English Measure, and, so to
speak, for the prevention of accidents?
Am I correct in assuming it has no special
meaning so far as the North of Ireland
is concerned? The second question is in
relation to the minimum fine which the
noble Viscount spoke of, and which is
carefully provided for in Sub-clause 4 of
Clause 1. I think I am correct in saying
that that minimum fine, which is the
small one of £1, is reducible to 5s under
a provision of the Conspiracy of Protec
tion of Property Act. I would like the
noble Viscount to confirm that, lest it
be thought to be a big minimum fine. I
have really nothing more to weary the
House with except perhaps that I should
say I support the principles of the Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am only intervening for a few minutes in
the debate. I am sorry that the hon.
Senator (Mr. Dorman) has left, as the few
remarks I wish to make were in reply to
what he put forward. Time has been
pretty well taken up by previous speakers
on matters which could largely have been
dealt with on the Committee stage.
Senator Dorman referred to the injury
sustained by the coal industry through
the loss of foreign markets. I am afraid
that it was the fault of the leaders of
the trade union movement who recommended the general strike that the foreign
coal markets were lost. Owing to various
reasons it is now very difficult to get them
back. We have heard different opinions
expressed here to-day by Labour Members
of the House as to the necessity for the
Second Reading.
Trade Disputes Bill-

gi
C
ان
ther
introduction of this Bill. Having heard
those different expressions of opinion how
is the ordinary man with no knowledge of
trade unions to judge of the matter? I
am glad that the hon. Senator (Mr.
Dorman) has come back again. I wish to
impress on him that everyone in this
House knows how much good trade unions
have done. What are the reasons for the
introduction of this Bill? It is not the
fault of the trade unions. It is the fault
of the men whom they appointed as their
leaders, or we might call them agitators,
who make a livelihood out of the business.
Trade unionists as a whole are in favour
of this Bill. It is really the trade union
leaders who are to blame for the
necessity and introduction of this
Bill. I would, in conclusion, make
a personal appeal to Senator Dorman.
There is no Member whose views
and ideas-though we do not agree
with his views politically-are
respected in this House. I was astonished
when at the end of his speech he said that
simply for the purpose of obstruction, not
because the Bill was right or wrong, he
was going to oppose all the Clauses of the
Measure. Now that he has made his
strong protest I hope that when he thinks
more seriously over the matter he will
see his way not to take up time in obstructing the passage of this useful Bill
at a later stage.
more
Mr. McDOWELL: As a trade unionist of 35 years' standing and as one who
has occupied all the important positions
in connection with the local trade union
movement I think I have a right to speak
with some knowledge on the Bill before
the House. I am strongly inclined to
think that the old principle of trade
unionism as we used to understand it has
ceased to function. We are in favour of
the Bill before the House, but the
Socialist element are trying to capture
and govern our trade unions. As I have
said, I have occupied the principal offices
in my trade union for practically thirtyfive years, and I never had a dispute
except one. Yet I was able to bring that
trade union into a position second to
none, both as regards wages and conditions of labour, in the North of Ireland,
nay, perhaps in Great Britain. The trade
union rules at that particular time provided that neither religion nor politics
should be allowed under any circumstances. Leaders of the trade unions of
to-day, because I hold a certain political
view, say that I am opposed to the
interests of the working men. They cannot dispute the fact, however, that it was
men like myself who brought trade unionism to the perfection it was in a few years
The men then combined only to
ago.
improve conditions so far as wages and
labour are concerned.
What do we find? We find that the
trade unions are really becoming the
great political organizations, and that is
why they have ceased to function as
trade unions. There is scarcely a trade
union which fulûls the promises of years
gone by, but we have tried to work in
conformity with the rules of our trade
unions. No matter what a man's political
views might be, and no matter what his
creed might be he had the same right to
occupy the highest position as any other
man had. Now these men say because I
believe in Conservative or Unionist
principles I am an enemy of the working
man. Let me point out that 75 per
cent. of the social reforms brought about
for the welfare of the working man and
working woman have been passed by
Unionist or Conservative Governments,
and yet the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman)
would try to make us believe, as he always
does, on every conceivable occasion, that
as a class we are opposed to one another.
I do not think he is right. In connection
with the general strike, Senator Dorman
says that the majority has the right to
rule.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. McDOWELL: We dispute this.
We say we are in a majority when a
general strike takes place. Take one
shop. The men leave off work at halfpast five o'clock in the evening and go
out. The next morning there is a picket
standing at the gate and they prohibit
the men from going into work. They
had no knowledge the previous night that
anything was wrong. They are told the
next morning that they cannot start work.
I wonder where the majority comes in
there. In the old days there had to be
a two-thirds majority before a strike,
let alone a general strike, was called.
The Labour leaders have tried to
manipulate the position with a conference of five or so and they are able to
declare a general strike, and we, the poor
humble tools, must fall in with their
wishes whether we desire it or not. We
are in favour of this Bill because it is
against the principle of a general strike.
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McDowell].
Let me now touch on the sympathetic
strike. I remember at one time there was
a trades council here. I was a member of
that council, and we carried on business
successfully as far as the interests of the
working men were concerned. Then it
commenced to take a political turn, and
a great many of the unions fell out of it.
After that a federation was started; it
was called the Shipbuilding and Federated
Trades Council. What did that stand
for? At that particular time one trade
was infringing on the work of the
other. Shipwrights were against the
joiners and joiners were against the
shipwrights; plumbers were against
engineers and engineers were against
plumbers, and this association was formed
for the purpose of settling the differences
between the different trades.
What is the upshot of it now? This
body has become a political organization.
It is composed of quite a number of
trades, the members of which are skilled,
semi-skilled and otherwise. If a man
does not belong to the same trade as I do
and he has no conception of what work I
have to perform, how can he be in
sympathy with me when he is receiving
10/- a week less than I am receiving?
He cannot be. It is unnatural to expect
him to do so when the question of a
sympathetic strike comes in. So far as
this Bill is concerned it is essential that
it should be passed without Amendment.
In connection with the political levy, I
think it is ridiculous that a young man
joining a trade union should be practically
compelled to pay to this political levy.
Mr. DORMAN No, no.
Mr. McDOWELL: The hon. Senator
says "No, no." I quite admit so far as
the law is concerned that is right, but so
many obstacles are placed in the way that
trade unionists cannot possibly comply
with the law. Consequently they have to
pay or fall into arrear, and if they do not
pay they are looked on as something
beneath the others. We do not wish to
do them an injury. Under the Bill if
they want to pay to the political levy they
are quite at liberty to do so, but we who
object will not pay any more. This Bili
gives us the right not to be intimidated
as far as this political levy is concerned.
I welcome the Bill, and I think it ought
to be accepted and passed without Amendment. Je
a
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support
the Second Reading of this Bill. I do not
intend to say very much, as a good deal
has been said this afternoon in this
chamber, but I would like to say I believe
it is necessary that a Bill such as this
should be brought in. I am sorry that
the Governments of to-day have found it
necessary to bring in such
Measure.
One would naturally think, seeing we are
living in an age of education, enlightenment and advancement, that we would
have left behind us those barbarous tactics
which Senator Dorman made reference to
a short time ago. One would think that
we would have left them behind, and that
we would have had a more sane way of
settling these disputes. Strikes and lockouts to my mind
interfere with the livelihood and the
They
comfort and the happiness of the people,
especially those who are weak. What I
mean by the weak are the aged and the
children of the men and women who are
brought out on strike or who, perhaps,
find the door of the workshop or factory
in which they earn their living closed
against them. To my mind strikes and
lock-outs are inhuman and ought to be
done away with altogether.
are inhuman.
We have been told on several occasions
there was not the slightest necessity for
either the Imperial Government or the
Government of Northern Ireland bringing
in such a Measure. If we let our minds
go back over the past few years since the
war-since 1918--what do we find? We
find up to the present year nothing but
industrial wars and rumours of industrial
wars. I was going to say, vapour, and
fire and smoke, but I am very glad to be
in a position to say it is not necessary to
say that. We find wars and rumours of
wars, and we also find threats of revolutions of different kinds, and we find an
upset of our social conditions in these
islands. I would like to draw the attention of the House to some of the strikes
which took place during that period. I
would mention first the general strike,
which has been emphasized here
much this afternoon.
This general
strike cost the country something like
£30,000,000——
SO
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: More.
Mr. BARCLAY---and, remember, it
was of short duration. It also cost the
ratepayers over £3,500,000 in extra
401 Trade Disputes Bill-

1 DECEMBER 1927
Second Reading.
t
L
relief. It added over 500,000 to the
number of unemployed, and it cost the
workers of the country £20,000,000 in
wages lost. That was the general strike.
I would also refer to the coal strike of
1926. It cost the country between
£250,000,000 and £300,000,000, and
every day the miners stopped out of the
mines added £2,000,000. It cost the
workers, apart from the miners, at that
time something like £40,000,000 in wages
lost. It cost the trade unions £5,000,000
of the savings they had set aside for superannuation and other purposes. It added
600,000 to the unemployment list, not
counting the miners. I would also draw
the attention of the House to the railway
strike of September, 1919. This strike
cost the country directly something like
£10,000,000, and it put out of work
about 375,000 men. Then the coal strike
of October and November, 1920, cost
the country directly something like
£26,500,000. The miners lost in wages
£15,000,000, and the other workers who
were thrown out of work numbered about
350,000. There was also the coal strike
of 1921.
Mr. DORMAN: The lock-out.
unem-
Mr. BARCLAY: Was it a lock-out?
This cost the country something like
£33,000,000. It increased the
ployment payments to £13,000,000 and
it lost markets abroad which some hon.
Member made reference to this afternoon. From April to July the country
lost something like £50,000,000.
The
House will see from those figures that it
was necessary for the Government to take
some drastic steps to save the industrial
and social welfare of the country. That
is one of the reasons why they have
brought in this Bill. Then, again, we
have been told that there was no demand
for this Bill, that the country had given
no mandate to the Government to bring
it in. I do not think it is necessary for
the Government to go to the country for a
mandate to bring in such a Bill as this.
A democratic Government is elected by
the votes of the people for the purpose of
catering for and looking after the welfare
of the people. When a small section of
the community rises up in a tyrannical
way against the great majority it is the
duty and the business of any democratic
Government to save the majority from
the power of the minority. Therefore,
we find the Government has brought in
this Bill for that particular purpose.
When the Imperial Government introduced this Bill into the Imperial House
of Commons there was a terrible lot of
talk and there was a terrible lot of threats.
Some hon. Senator has mentioned here
to-day some of the threats. There were
going to be great demonstrations in the
city of London. There were going to be
great processions in the streets, so great
that the traffic would be held up, and the
parks and other places in and about
London were to be crowded with trade
unionists protesting against this Bill.
one
What happened? I never heard tell
of a procession. I never heard tell of any
of the big demonstrations in the parks.
The only protest that was made was made
at the Trade Union Congress, and as
Senator McDowell has said, the men there
were elected by a certain section of the
community who were favourable towards
them, and they only spoke for
section of trade unionists. I would like
to draw the attention of hon. Members to
the Trades Congress in Edinburgh, and if
this is not a mandate from the electorate
I do not know what can be a mandate.
At the Trades Congress in Edinburgh
Mr. J. H. Thomas, who was Colonial
Secretary during the Labour party's term
of office, speaking on Tuesday, September
6, 1927, said
there were three by-elections at which
the Trade Disputes Bill was made the
Labour issue. Their own people constituted 90 per cent. of the electorate,
but their candidates were turned down
by the votes of their own people.
If that is not a mandate I do not know
what you would call one. Here are three
by-elections fought during the time this
Bill was before the Imperial House of
Commons. The Labour party at those
three elections made the Trade Disputes
Bill their main issue, or, as we would say
in Belfast, the principal plank in their
platform. We must not forget that Mr.
Thomas said that 90 per cent. of the
electors of those three constituencies were
working class people, trade unionists.
He says "their own people," and, therefore,
we find that 90 per cent of 'their own
people' turned down three candidates
and returned to the Imperial House of
Commons three men in favour of this
Bill.
I said I was not going to say very much,
but I am afraid I am going further than
Trade Disputes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
I intended. (Hon. Members: No, no.)
Looking at the Bill I naturally come to
the conclusion that it is a very harmless
document. I do not see anything that
any working man may be afraid of. In
the first place, it does not interfere in
any way with the rights of trade unionists
to organize themselves in trade unions
for the purpose of collective bargaining
or for the purpose of improving their
working conditions. All the Bill does is
simply to safeguard trade unionists from
any political party. It sets a man practically free. I would not like the Unionist
party to have any kind of a grip upon me.
I have an independent spirit. Although
I might not be independent financially,
still I have something within me that is
independent. I would not like any
Unionist, even you, Mr. Speaker, to
come to me and tell me to do a certain
thing or try to coerce me to do a certain
thing. I would say, "No, I will not do
it." I take every working man to be of
the same temperament and of the same
spirit. Why should any working man be
compelled by any political party to act,
or vote or pay as that party thinks fit?
Therefore, this is a Bill that safeguards
the trade unionist and practically sets
him free.
Again we have been told that this Bill
interferes with and takes away from the
working man his strongest weapon, namely,
the strike weapon which has been referred to by Senator Cunningham. I
cannot see for the life of me how any man
can say that this Bill takes away that
particular weapon. It does not interfere
in any way with legitimate strikes.
Railwaymen, miners, bricklayers, carpenters, engineers, and transport workers
who have a disagreement with their employers are quite at liberty to test the
matter by a strike if saner methods cannot be used. I hope saner methods will
always be used, but they have a right
according to this Bill to test the matter
by a strike. But the Bill does prohibit
a general strike. Senator Dorman has
been trying to explain to us this afternoon what a general strike is, and I must
say that I am just as wise now as when
he began. He made reference to the
Israelites down in the land of Goshen. I
suppose there was a general strike on the
day they left there to go on their journey
to the Promised Land. It may have
been, but at any rate I do not understand
the definition he has given. I understand that the Bill prohibits a general
strike, and it is very emphatic upon this
point, because it is so worded as not to
permit in any way a conspiracy against
the Government of the country. If we
look back upon past strikes that have
taken place, even apart from the general
strike, we find that sympathetic strikes in
many cases have done a great amount of
harm to the country as a whole. The
general strike in 1926 deprived the people
of their food in a great many cases.
Ships were held up; perishable food was
lost and destroyed. It deprived travellers
of transport. Instead of having buses,
*trams, or trains by which to travel they
had to go on foot. It deprived the
people of light and heat, and threw
thousands of men and women who were
very much in need of money out of employment.
Then there is the political levy. There
has been a great deal said about the
political levy, and I do not suppose that
I could improve on anything that has
been said. But this I will say, that I
look upon the political levy as a sporting
Clause. The trade unionist is left a free
man, and if he has a shilling, or two
shillings, or a pound to spend he is quite
at liberty to put it on whatever political
party he fancies. If he fancies the
Socialist or the Labour party he is quite
at liberty to put his pound on the Labour
party. If he fancies the Liberal party
he is quite at liberty to put his pound
upon the Radical, and if he fancies the
Conservative party he is quite at liberty
to put his pound upon the Tory. He is
quite at liberty to do that. There is no
person to hinder him, and so I think this
Clause about which we have heard SO
much, and over which there has been such
weeping, wailing and gnashing of teeth,
is a pretty fair Clause.
A lot has been said about the civil
servants. I am not going to say anything about the civil servants, only that
seeing they are the servants of the State
they have no right to combine with any
political party. There is one question
that arises in my mind, and that is that
if it is right for a trade union to use
pressure upon a group of men in the
interests of the union, is it wrong for the
Government to apply pressure to prevent
an injury being done to the whole country
by a group of trades unionists? This
question has presented itself to me.
it is right for the trades unions to bring

a
pressure upon a group of men in its own
interests it should also be right for the
Government to bring pressure upon
group of trades unionists for the benefit
and comfort of the people, and if it is
wrong for the Government it is quite
wrong for the trades unions.
Looking over the Bill and taking the
Measure as a whole I naturally come to
the conclusion that it is a fair Measure.
There is nothing in the Bill to interfere
with the independence of the trades
unions. There is nothing to prevent a
trade unionist from subscribing to any
political propaganda he wishes, and there
is nothing to debar him from voting as he
pleases. Therefore, I have great pleasure
in supporting the Second Reading of this
Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD:
Seldom if ever in this
House have I listened to a more interestto
a
to
more
ing debate than that which is now
drawing
close, or
interesting speeches than those delivered
by hon. Members of this House
who know SO well all the conditions of trade unionism and the conditions under which the workers live and
so on. I do not propose to make another
speech of any length in closing the debate. I do not wish to say anything that
would in any way hurt the feelings of the
hon. Member (Senator Dorman) opposite,
who is the Opposition in this House, but
his speech lasted for fifty minutes, and as
far as I could make out forty-five minutes
of it were devoted to dealing with matters
that had very little if anything to do
with this Bill.
Question put: "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."

Lisburn Electric Supply Company
(Agreement) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move the Second
Reading of this Bill, the object of which
is to confirm and give effect to an agreement made between the Ministry of
Commerce and the Lisburn Electricity
Supply Company with respect to the
transmission line to be constructed by the
company for carrying electrical energy
from Belfast to Lisburn.
I will explain very briefly the circumstances which have given rise to the introduction of the Bill. Some time ago a
company was registered for the purpose
of obtaining statutory powers to supply
electricity within the Urban District of
Lisburn. It proposed to buy its current
from the Belfast City Council and to
carry it by overhead transmission to
Lisburn. The overhead line which the
Company intended to erect was designed
to transmit energy at a pressure of 6,600
volts only, sufficient copper being provided
in the line to furnish about 1,000
kilowatts, which is the maximum estimated requirement of the consumers in
the area covered by the comp company.
Government, however, looking to the
future, and knowing that the Lagan
Valley was a very favourable field for
electricity development, thought it would
be advisable that this proposed transmission line between Lisburn and Belfast
should be made capable of a much higher
pressure than would be necessary to supply
the electrical demand from Lisburn only,
so that it might be able to provide current
for large power users on the route between
Belfast and Lisburn, and possibly in the
future form the first link of a main transmission line extending further by way of
Lurgan and Portadown.
The
Accordingly an agreement in the
matter was reached with the company on
410-
Trade Disputes Bill-
Committee:
8 DECEMBER 1927
-
efr
rise
me
for
ΤΣ
Com

!
a
The
the lines set forth in the Schedule.
Government will advance to the company
£1,450, which will be secured by a debenture, and the company, on its part, undertakes to construct
transmission line
capable of carrying energy at a pressure
of 33,000 volts instead of 6,600 volts,
which, as I have said, would have been
sufficient for its requirements in the
Lisburn area. It will be noticed that
interest will not be payable upon the debenture until the maximum load delivered
to the company at the Belfast terminus
of the transmission line exceeds 1,000
kilowatts or until power is transmitted
over the line for the company's own purposes at a pressure exceeding 6,600 volts.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: Again I have to give an
answer to Senator Leslie on
question raised on the Second Reading.
During that debate the hon. Senator
asked for an assurance that the North of
Ireland Registrar of Friendly Societies
had been adequately equipped with the
powers of register for the purpose of the
Trade Union Acts. I am quite sure that
the hon. Senator is quite familiar with
the Government of Ireland Order, 1922.
If he will look at Article 14 of that Order
he will find that the term "society" in
the sub-section of that Order includes
trade union.

stand part of the Bill."

Mr. SPEAKER: Certainly. Members are not bound to give any reason for
voting against one clause or six clauses.

of funds of trade unions, &c., in contravention of s. 1 of Act).

Without prejudice to the right of any
person having a sufficient interest in
the relief sought to sue or apply for an
injunction to restrain any application of
the funds of a trade union in contravention of the provisions of this Act, an
injunction restraining any application
of the funds of a trade union in contravention of the provisions of section
one of this Act may be granted at the
suit or upon the application of the
Attorney-General for Northern Ireland.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie: -
Page 10, line 36, leave out from
"Act" to the end of line 40, and insert
"the Attorney-General for Northern
Ireland may sue or apply for an injunction to restrain any application of the
funds of a trade union in contravention
of the provisions of Section one of this
Act."
Mr. LESLIE: It will be remembered
that on the Second Reading, I drew attention to this Clause and suggested that
perhaps it might be found on inquiry that
the wording used in the corresponding
Measure passed by the Imperial Parliament would not be quite suitable for the
conditions as they prevail here. Since
the Second Reading, inquiry has been
made and I am satisfied, and those whom
I referred to on the Second Reading as
competent to express an opinion on the
Measure are satisfied that there is 110
occasion to ask the Committee to make
any change in the wording of the Bill
before us. Consequently, of course, I do
not move any Amendment.
Amendment not moved.

part of the Bill."

CLAUSE 8 (Furnishing of returns,

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: On the Second Reading
Senator Leslie raised the question of a
reduction of the minimum penalty of £1
provided in Section 16 of the Trade
Unions (Amendment) Act, 1876, and
applied in Sub-section 4 of Clause 8 of
this Bill. I am advised that Section 8
of the Conspiracy and Protection of
Property Act, 1875, which deals with a
reduction of penalties under certain circumstances, does not apply in this case.
The returns, statements, and other information required to be furnished to the
Registrar of Friendly Societies under
Clause 8 of this Bill are most important,
and the Government are of opinion that
the penalties applied under this Clause
are not excessive.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
noble Viscount for looking into the
matter. I certainly do not want to offer
any opposition to the Clause.
Question put: "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie : 2T
After Clause 8 insert the following new
Clause:-
(1) Notwithstanding anything in any
enactment a court of summary jurisdiction, when trying a person charged
with any offence against the Trade
Union Acts, 1871 to 1917, the Conspiracy and Protection of Property Act,
1875, or this Act, shall be constituted
of one or more resident magistrates;
and a court of quarter sessions, when
hearing and determining an appeal
from such court of summary jurisdiction, shall be constituted of the recorder
or county court judge sitting alone.
No justice of the peace shall have any
jurisdiction to try any offence to which
this sub-section applies, except the
persons specified therein.
(2) In this Section the expression
"resident magistrate' means a magistrate appointed under the Constabulary
(Ireland) Act, 1836.
Mr. LESLIE: On the Second Reading
of this Bill I drew attention to the possible inconvenience of leaving the Trade
Unions Acts and the Conspiracy and Protection of Property Act to be dealt with
by courts of summary jurisdiction, and
also to the difficulty as regards appeals
to quarter sessions in such cases. I do not
think that there is any occasion for me to
repeat what I said then, because deliberately, with your permission, my Lord,
and with the forbearance of the House
I went into the matter rather more fully
than is usual on a Second Reading in
order that there might be plenty of time
for the consideration of the matter. I
think that all I have to do now is to
formally move the Amendment as
it
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Long, Mr. John A.
Massereene and Ferrard, Viscount
McDowell, Mr. W. J.
stands on the Paper. It will have the effect
of absolutely confining the jurisdiction of
the cases I have referred to to one or
more resident magistrates and providing
for an appeal to the recorder or the county
court judge sitting alone.
I ought, perhaps, to say that it is
possible under present conditions for a
resident magistrate in Belfast to hear
such cases alone. The question at issue
really is this. Is the Government satisfied
that it would be possible to differentiate
in the future, as it has presumably been
possible in the past, between an offence
against the one code or do they consider
the passage of this new Bill will render
the position impossible. Personally, I
cannot but think it would be safer to
insert a provision such as I have moved,
but I imagine it is not a matter which,
whatever private opinions we may entertain, will be allowed to create any profound division of opinion, because it is a
matter entirely apart from the main
structure of the Bill before us. I beg to
move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I think it should be observed in considering this new Clause
that it would involve a consequential
Amendment of the Title of the Bill, and
this may, I think, be taken as an indication that the hon. Member accepts the
view that the Amendment is really out.
side the scope of the Bill as it stands.
Senator Leslie is quite correct in stating
that offences under the Bill will, with
certain exceptions, be taken before
resident magistrate
provided in
Section 19 of the Trade Unions Act,
1871. Those exceptions are in respect of
the offences mentioned in Sub-clause 1
of Clause 3 and Sub-clause 4 of Clause 6,
which are linked up with the Conspiracy
and Protection of Property Act, 1875.
These offences will be heard and determined by a court consisting of two or
more justices sitting in Petty Sessions as
as

CLAUSE I (Provisions as to trades,
&c., permitted to be carried on in licensed
premises).
Intoxicating Liquor Bill—
Committee.
8 DECEMBER 1927
(
*
*
*
Provided that-
*
*
*
*
*
(i) this sub-section shall not apply to
any premises structurally adapted for
use, and bona fide used, as a hotel as
hereinbefore in this Act defined, or as
a railway refreshment room or as a
restaurant;
(ii) notwithstanding anything contained in this sub-section, any of the
following businesses, that is to say, the
sale of tobacco, table waters, and such
food as may be supplied for consumption on the premises, or the sale of
intoxicating liquor by wholesale in
pursuance of the enactments in that
behalf, may be carried on in any premises, or in any part of any premises, where an on-licence business is
carried on, but only at the times during
which such on-licence business may
lawfully be carried on;
(iii) nothing in this sub-section shall
prevent a licensed person from carrying
on at any time, in any premises, or in
any part of any premises, where his onlicence business is carried on, the business of providing temporary stabling or
similar accommodation for vehicles or
animals.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 2, line 27, after the word
"tobacco" (tobacco, table waters) to
insert the word "matches." It is, I
think, common knowledge that people
who purchase tobacco not infrequently
require to purchase matches also. As a
matter of fact, considerable discussion
took place when the Bill was in Committee in another place with a view to
getting matches inserted in the Bill.
Unfortunately, so far as I can make out,
the attempt there was made to permit the
sale of matches on licensed premises with
an off-licence, and of course the whole
question of a mixed business was raised,
and mixed business is one of the things
which I think it will be suggested we
should not touch. Consequently proposals
to insert the word "matches" in that case
could not be accepted. I venture to
think that perhaps the circumstances are
rather different when I suggest to permit
the sale of matches in common with
tobacco in premises with an on-licence.
I have one rather special reason for asking this. A practice has grown up of late
years, usually for the purpose of assisting
charitable objects, generally in connection
with destitute children, of having little
slot machines in places of public resort
in which it is possible to insert a penny
and obtain a small packet of matches of
the kind that I hold in my hand,
whereas, of course, you would get them
in a tobacconist's for about a farthing.
That is a small form of charitable collection. Therefore I beg to move to insert
the word "matches."
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The effect of the Amendment of the hon. Senator would be to
allow on-licence holders to sell matches.
An Amendment was proposed in another
place to enable off-licence holders to sell
tobacco and matches. That was rejected
on the ground that it was clearly laid
down in the 1923 Act what was and what
was not mixed trading. The question is
one of degree. It seems to me, and it
seems to the Government, that if publicans are permitted to sell matches really
there is no valid reason why they should
not be permitted to sell other things as
well. We must draw the line somewhere,
and the Government can see no good
reason for departing from what was clearly
the intention of the Act of 1923.
regret the Government are not prepared
to accept the Amendment.
I
Mr. LESLIE: Well, if the Government
deliberately come between the pipe and
the matches I suppose there is nothing
more to do but to withdraw the Amendment, but I may say no very good reason
has been given for refusing it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment
standing in my name has reference to the
troublesome question of opening
inn yards or public-house yards
in order to allow farmers or people
attending fairs and markets to stable
their horses without breaking the law. It
is to leave out the word "temporary" in
line 42. We all remember the somewhat
unfortunate history of the 1923 Act in
this connection. I do not know how it
happened, but I think it is generally
agreed that somebody blundered. At all
events, farmers in country districts were
put to very considerable inconvenience
owing to the closing of the yards of publichouses against them and their horses and
vehicles from the early hours of the mornings, when fairs and markets were being
419 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
held. Naturally enough there was a considerable outburst of public opinion.
The Government is taking steps to mitigate the inconvenience which, I think, they
rather undesignedly created. Now that
this is so, the question arises whether we
are going to help matters very much by
introducing an indefinite term like
"temporary." I do not want any unfortunate person charged with keeping a
yard open for an unlawful purpose merely
on the ground that in the opinion of the
local policeman the stabling was rather
more than temporary. I cannot see that
the word has any particular meaning.
Therefore, I suggest we should omit it.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I am not sure that the
hon. Member fully realizes what would
be the effect of omitting the word "temporary" from this sub-section. The effect
of it would be that it would enable a
publican to have a garage in his yard.
Well, it was not the intention of the
Government to allow such a thing as
that. The only intention of the Government was to allow publicans to permit
their customers to have temporary accommodation for their vehicles and animals.
If publicans are allowed to carry on businesses which have no necessary connection with that trade and may equally well
be carried on elsewhere, such as a garage,
it would seem to be in direct contravention of the intention underlying subsection (1) of Section 6 of the Act of 1923.
I need hardly point out that to allow publicans to have garages in their yards
would bring customers to their houses.
This is surely undesirable, and directly
contrary to the Act of 1923. Therefore,
the Government cannot accept it.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might ask
the noble Viscount with reference to the
whole question whether it is intended to
prevent the man with stables in a yard
supplying fodder. It does happen when
horses are stabled or put up in a yard
they require food. We had a terrible
picture on the Second Reading of animals
left, in the early days after the 1923 Act,
without food, because the yard could not
be opened. I would really like to know
from the noble Viscount whether the man
who admits horses to stabling will not be
allowed to feed them. Upon that depends
the following Amendment, which is
bound up with the one before the Committee.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: Off hand I cannot answer
the hon. Member's question definitely. I
will look into the matter and find out exactly the intention before the Report
stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps the Committee
will allow me to withdraw the Amendment dealing with "temporary." I will
not move the following one and I will
bring the matter up on Report.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie: -
Line 42, to leave out "stabling or
similar."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
beg to move on page 2, line 43, at end
("vehicles or animals'') to add "and
suitable accommodation for any person
in charge of such vehicles or animals.”
I move this Amendment for the purpose
of preventing any misunderstanding that
may exist when the Act becomes law in
case those responsible for carrying out
the law might be rather strict. It has
been referred to so often, one would think
there is hardly any necessity for putting
words such as these in to make it clear,
but when we remember that after the
passing of the 1923 Act on Dry Sunday,
the first dry Sunday, horses and other
cattle stabled in licensed premises had to
go dry and without fodder owing to the
action of the police, one can readily
understand they may go so far as to say,
"We will admit animals for temporary
stabling or vehicles for housing, yet we
are not entitled to admit any person with
them." I think it is necessary that these
words should be inserted so that the police
and those responsible would understand
that they do not expect a horse to travel
into the yard by itself, or that it is expected that a motor vehicle would be propelled into the yard without some person
taking charge of it.
Another thing very necessary is that
the person who has the on-licence, the inn
keeper or publican should be permitted
to provide suitable accommodation for
these persons. They cannot be admitted
to the bar or premises where drink is
421 Trade Disputes Bill-

8 DECEMBER 1927
Committee.
な

am
for fear they would drink any of it, but
surely it is only reasonable to expect the
publican who might be anxious to safeguard his trade or improve his trade tu
provide a small room or rooms for the
accommodation of these people. When
their horses are stabled and comfortable
they also should be made comfortable
from the hour when they arrive until a
short time before the fair. I must admit
it is out of sympathy with these people I
am moving, because, as pointed out by
Senator Barclay, I know nothing about
the country. Unfortunately,
I
town-reared and town-bred SO that I
cannot profess to know all about it, but I
can readily understand it must be a
terrible hardship. I remember one case,
I think it was in Ballymoney, I was passing through Ballymoney and I asked my
friend to pull up at the first refreshment
place. I said to the man behind the bar
"I suppose the 1923 Act does not affect
you." "Does it not"? he said, and he
started then to tell me how these poor
men were lined up on the other side of
the street on market day or fair day unable to get any place to put their horses
or to be accommodated themselves. There
they had to stand until ten o'clock when
the bars were opened. I can readily
understand it must be a terrible hardship on these poor fellows who were not
too well clothed or shod. I beg to move.
Mr. McDOWELL: This is what I
might call the thin edge of the wedge.
The mover of the Amendment is most
anxious that the publican or inn-keeper,
whatever you like to call him, should be put
to great inconvenience to build outhouses
for the purpose of accommodation in
stormy cold weather, because he has
already said he does not want these people
to go into the bar. I think it would
put the publican to great inconvenience
to do this. He referred also to Senator
Barclay's statement on the Second Reading. But Senator Barclay forgot to state
that when he was a young fellow going
on market day at 3, 4, or 5 o'clock in the
morning and arriving at half-past six or
seven that the public-houses were opened
at 7 o'clock. But we have men coming
to the shipyard away from Portadown,
Lurgan, Donaghadee or the coldest place
you can think of and they land here on
a wet or cold, frosty morning. I wonder
if he would ask the publicans round the
Queen's Road to make provision to give
these fellows a bit of something warm.
I can hardly support the Amendment, and
I must vote against it. I think it is the
thin end of the wedge.
There is no necessity for these men
leaving their homes at an untimely hour
to get into the market at say half-past
eight or half-past nine o'clock. They
have ample time to get in, and by the
time they have disposed of their goods if
they want any refreshment the publicans
will be open at ten o'clock. If they are
so dry they can go to a restaurant. After
all, I hope the gentlemen farmers here
will not for a moment take it that we
consider they are most anxious to get a
drink before they go to the market so as
to put them in good bidding form. I
think it is the thin end of the wedge, and
consequently-I must oppose it.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Since the Second
Reading of this Bill I have taken
occasion to speak to several people engaged in cattle dealing, and I understand
that the fairs are now started very much
later than they used to be, as indeed all
labour is, and that there is no difficulty
at all in this matter. In addition to
that one has to remember that horses are
gradually disappearing from the country,
and particularly with regard to fairs.
People who go to fairs now pay a shilling a head to go in a motor, and at any
rate fairs now do not begin until 9-30 or
10-a.m., and under these circumstances
I do not think their grievances are great,
if there are any at all. On the Second
Reading of this Bill Senator Barclay referred to his experiences at a rather
distant period when I dare say there was
a good deal of hardship, but I do not
think that exists now from all I can ascertain in discussing the matter with
people who are familiar with the cirstances.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
think this Amendment is entirely unnecessary. When a farmer goes into a fair
there is no necessity for him to spend his
time standing in a queue. If he was a
wise man he would stable his horses and
then go and transact his business. He
would not need any waiting room. If a
farmer is to be successful he will stay at
home until it is time to leave for the fair,
and he will leave at such a time as will
enable him to arrive in the fair when it
is opened.
Mr. LONG: Senator Samuel Cunningham has just anticipated what I was
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
going to say. I have attended a good
many fairs and markets, and for thirty
years I have travelled seventeen and a
half miles twice a week to a market. A
farmer can time his arrival in a fair or
rmarket to within five minutes of the opening of that fair or market. With a horse
vehicle there are no punctures to be contended with, and a farmer only arrives
in a market town in time to do his business. I am entirely opposed to this
Amendment, because I believe it wouid
cpen a very wide door, and it would
ender it extremely difficult for adequate
police supervision to be exercised over a
publican. My experience is that you will
find a good many unscrupulous publicans
who would smuggle drink into these waiting rooms that are suggested in this
Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the acceptance
of this Amendment would open the door
to a lot of clandestine drinking. Publicans would be disposed to jump at an
opportunity of this kind to provide accommodation for the human animal as
well as for the beast of the field. I know
a good deal about country towns all over
Ireland, and there is already an enoramount of clandestine drinking
going on in public houses and hotels. It
is possible to be in these premises at midnight, and for liquor to be paid for and
supplied from the bar. I have seen it
myself and I hope the Government will
not encourage this to go on.
mous
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: So many objections have
been raised by hon. Members to this
Amendment that it is hardly necessary for
me to explain the Government's position.
What would be the real effect of this
Amendment? The effect of it wond be
to allow members of the general public
to be on licensed premises during closing
hours. That would mean that the supervision of the licensed premises by the
police would be extremely difficult, even
more difficult than it is at the moment.
The Amendment is in direct conflict with
the provision of the 1923 Act, and that
provision is an important one. It places
the onus upon the publican of proving
that persons found on licensed premises
during closing hours were there for no
illegal purpose. If a publican has got to
provide suitable accommodation for persons in charge of animals and vehicles
how is he going to do it? The accommodation must have no connection with the
public house. It must have a separate
entrance, and persons would have to conse
out of the yard into the street in order to
get to the room provided. Otherwise the
publican would not be complying with
the Act of 1923. On those grounds the
Government cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: That
being so, I withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 2 (Sale and consumption
of intoxicating liquor in hotels and
restaurants).
(1) Notwithstanding the provisions
of the Act of 1923 or any other enactment it shall be lawful on Sunday or
Christmas Day to sell or consume intoxicating liquor in an hotel as defined
by sub-section (3) of section one of the
said Act, where such liquor is sold to
or consumed by a guest or traveller
who has lodged for the preceding night
or has in writing engaged a room for
the succeeding night in such hotel at
or during the hours specified for the
purpose of the sale of intoxicating
liquor in Article 1 of Part I of the
Second Schedule to that Act.
(2) Where any dinner or other entertainment is held in an hotel or restaurant which is licensed for the sale of
intoxicating liquor, it shall not be lawful for the proprietor or manager
thereof to allow the consumption of intoxicating liquor, by persons attending
such dinner or entertainment, at hours
other than those specified for the purpose of the sale of intoxicating liquor
by the Act of 1923 or any other enactment:
Provided that such consumption may
lawfully be allowed on any day, other
than Sunday or Christmas Day or a day
on which an order under section ten
of the Act of 1923 is in force, where the
proprietor or manager of the hotel or
restaurant has given previous notice to
the district-inspector (or in his absence,
to the head-constable) of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, acting for the
district in which the premises are
situate, of the intention to hold any

dinner or entertainment, and has received a permit from such districtinspector or head-constable authorising
the consumption of intoxicating liquor
in a specified room or rooms during the
hours mentioned in such permit; but so
that such proprietor or manager shall
not, in connection with any such dinner
or entertainment, sell or expose for sale
intoxicating liquor at any hours other
than those specified for those purposes
by the enactments aforesaid.
The following Amendments stood on
the Order Paper:-
Page 3, line 10, leave out "Sunday or
Christmas Day." (Mr. J. C. Glendinning).
Page 3, line 15, leave out from 'hotel'
to the end of line 17 (at or during hours
specified for the purpose of the sale of
intoxicating liquor in article 1 of Part I
of the Second Schedule to that Act).
-(Mr. Joseph Cunningham and Mr. J.
C. Glendinning).
The CHAIRMAN: I understand the
hon. Senator is going to move the two
Amendments together.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I was just
going to ask your permission to take the
two Amendments together. If they were
adopted the sub-section would then
read: -
(1) Notwithstanding the provisions
of the Act of 1923 or any other enactment it shall be lawful to sell or consume intoxicating liquor in an hotel
as defined by sub-section (3) of section
one of the said Act. where such liquor
is sold to or consumed by a guest or
traveller who has lodged for the preceding night or has in writing engaged
a room for the succeeding night in
such hotel.
The object of these Amendments is to
remove the restrictions on bona-fide
residents in hotels, and I ask hon.
Members to regard these Amendments in
the light of common sense, and to deal
with the matter as ordinary members of
the public. As I said on the Second
Reading, my reasons for moving these
Amendments are three in number. The
first concerns people coming over from
Great Britain to Northern Ireland and
staying in hotels here. I am proud, and
I hope most of us are proud, to belong
to the United Kingdom. I have always
loved to regard our part of Northern
Ireland as if it were a county or shire on
the other side of the water where people
are of the same type and have the same
ideas. There is nothing calculated to do
more harm to our beloved Province than
the idea of a person coming over here
and finding himself in many respects in
a different country. When a traveller
from Great Britain comes over here and
takes up his residence in an hotel he goes
about his day's business, and when
he comes in again at night he goes
into the lounge of his hotel and calls for
a drink. He cannot get it. He probably
says to himself, "This is a funny place
I am in with all these petty restrictions.
I hope I shall not have to come back
here." Such a thing as that belittles
this country and does it a great deal of
harm.
a
My second reason is with regard to
residents in hotels in general. Broadly
speaking, residents in hotels may be regarded as of two distinct classes. The
first class comprises the business people,
and if owners and managers of hotels
were consulted I think they would say
that the travelling residents are really
the mainstay of the hotel trade. The
business-man staying in an hotel is
already subjected to
considerable
amount of discomfort. He is living in an
uncongenial atmosphere and he loses a
good many of the home comforts he is
accustomed to, and it is rather tantalizing
to him that he should be restricted from
so many of the comforts that he enjoys
in his own home or club. A business-man
does his business during the day and after
dinner he may go out to a place of amusement. He comes back into the lounge
of the hotel and probably wants the little
refreshment that he is accustomed to have,
but he finds that he cannot get it. As a
bona-fide resident in an hotel I think he
should be able to get a drink at 10 or 11
o'clock at night if he wants it. That
applies to a very large class of hotel
residents.
Another class of residents in hotels are
those who are there on holiday. I would
like hon. Members to consider the life in
an hotel during the summer months, from
May until August. We are then under the
Summer Time Act and as a matter of fact
at the closing hour it is only 7-30 p.m.
by ordinary time. People are out play-
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
T
THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Glendinning]
ing golf or tennis or walking until dusk,
which is about 10 or 11 o'clock. When
those people come back to the hotel in
which they are staying they cannot get
a drink. I think that is another very
good reason why the Government should
accept these Amendments. Even those
who are not engaged in playing golf or
other games probably dine later in the
summer time than they usually do. They
may have their dinner at 8 o'clock. After
sitting chatting they may go into the
lounge to have a liqueur with their coffee,
only to be informed by the waiter that
they cannot have it because it is after 9
o'clock. I think that is a very irritating
restriction.
That brings me to the third reason I
have for proposing this Amendment.
People who are caught like that make up
their minds that they will not be caught
again, and they take good care that on
every other night of the week they will
have something upstairs. The result is that
they will order drink to be taken up to their
bedroom, and I venture to say they will
actually drink more in their bedroom
than they would if they had been able
to have a small whisky in the ordinary
course downstairs. I think that is only
a common sense way of looking at the
matter. The adoption of these Amendments would be in the interests of true
temperance, because I believe more harm
is done to true temperance by these petty
restrictions than in any other way. One
hon. Senator, speaking on the Second
Reading of this Bill, said he would object
to these Amendments because people
would engage rooms in an hotel on a
Saturday night and they would bring in
as many people as they liked on Sunday
to drink. I would ask hon. Senators not
to allow their imagination to run away
with them. I do not think anything like
that would occur.
I would ask hon. Members to remember
that hotel owners and managers are a
respectable body of persons. There is in
existence a very strong hotel managers'
association, and I think that if the
Government accepted these Amendments
and removed these restrictions, that
association would see that the Clause was
not abused. The Government have gone
a step in the right direction in this Bill
regarding hotels, and if they went a
little bit further and removed all these
restrictions I believe the hotel managers
association would take care that the
Act was not infringed in any way.
And as a matter of fact could it be infringed? The really decently managed
hotel looks with considerable suspicion on
any person coming in without baggage
in the ordinary way. They want to know
why they want a room without any apparent evidence of travelling or anything
else. The probability is that unless the
man puts down a heavy deposit he would
have very great difficulty in getting a
room in the hotel at all. With regard to
the small hotel, which Senator Dickson
says might infringe the Act, my opinion is
if it were tried on in a small hotel the
police and everybody else would twig it
at once, and know that it was a bogus
business altogether. The hotel proprietor would be too much concerned about
his licence to permit it going on.
I have only one or two further things
to say. I am not asking the Government
to abandon any principle for the sake of
public popularity, but there certainly is
a feeling that the Government in this
Bill have shown some indifference to the
general views of the community. I think
even down in another place a good many
people on the Government side would in
their hearts be in sympathy with this
Amendment. I think it is only a very
reasonable request that people living in a
hotel temporarily should be able to regard
that hotel as their home, and there should
be no restrictions in regard to liquor or
anything else. I beg to move.
Mr. BARCLAY: I do not think it
would be wise on the part of this Committee
to accept this Amendment. I think the
Government has done pretty well for the
hotels by allowing them to open their premises on Sunday and Christmas Day for
certain hours for the purpose of selling
and for the consumption of intoxicating
liquor.
Mr. DORMAN: They have not done it.
Mr. BARCLAY: Not done it?
Mr. McDOWELL: You are right
enough. Go on. Do not heed him...
Mr. BARCLAY: I think it would not
be wisdom to extend the time any longer
than in the Bill. To do so to my mind
would be creating a law for the rich and
the aristocrat and at the same time ignoring the poor and the working men.

class of folk who go to these hotels which
have been spoken of, such as the railway
hotels and other hotels in the city, would
not go to a public house in a side street
which has its door shut all day on Sunday. I think if the House accepted that
Amendment it would create ill feeling,
and it would encourage members of the
Socialist party in the class warfare they
are trying to carry on.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. BARCLAY: I object to this
Amendment, and I will vote against it.
Mr. LESLIE: The first thing, I think,
that has to be pointed out is that the
Amendment really before us is to leave out
the words "on Sunday or Christmas
Day." The Paper as printed left out the
word "on."
Mr. GLENDINNING: Yes; I noticed
that.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that perhaps one
ought to warn the Committee that if this
Amendment is carried and on general
principles I think I must support it-if
it should be carried other provisions
would be necessary, and it may be that
these provisions would not be confined
within the corners of this particular Bill.
It would mean a very serious change, but
the mere fact that it makes a change in
the Act of 1923 does not seem to me to
be any particular reason for rejecting
the Amendment. The Act of 1923, like
any other statute, is liable to revision by
Parliament from time to time, and the
Act of 1923 is not perhaps a singularly
good example of omniscience. If it were so
it would not be necessary for Parliament
to consider an amending Bill in respect of
it at all.
I
I do not quite follow the hon. Senator
from Londonderry who moved this
Amendment in his reference to Ulster as
being a county of Great Britain.
rather thought that the hon. Senator,
like myself, would have some pride in
being Irish in the sense that we are all
part of this country, and however great
our affection for Great Britain still we
would like to retain some individuality.
The difficulty which I have in maintaining the present arrangement is that if a
to
man goes to a properly licensed hotel,
which pays a very heavy duty, he is under
restrictions which he is not under if he
goes to a temperance hotel. If he goes
to a so-called temperance hotel he can take
his whisky with him, and apparently consume it at any time provided there is no
sale or exposure for sale. That is an
arrangement to which hotel keepers very
strongly object. Also, I think there is
too much tendency to regard this matter
as if all hotels were what are commonly called public houses. An hotel
does not exist primarily for drinking
purposes. Such drinking as does
take place is purely ancillary
the general accommodation for the
traveller. I do not think that distinction
has been sufficiently maintained. I do not
know if the Government give us this
Amendment whether it would be possible
at this late period of the year to deal
with the other provisions which I referred
to as being probably necessary. I think
more regard ought to be shown to the
feeling that there is in the Province towards greater liberty as against these
restrictions on their liberty which they
never anticipated would be imposed upon
them. It is not for us in this House I
think to pay any particular regard to
extremists on one side or the other. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) We are not concerned with the shouts of the so-called
Temperance party, and we are not particularly concerned to support the drink
trade. What we are concerned with, I
think, is to see that the public gets
reasonable convenience if it wants it. Our
duty is to the public as a whole, and not
to the temperance orator or the publican
in particular. I should give general
support to this Amendment if it is
pressed to a conclusion. But I am not
quite certain in my own mind that it
would be altogether well to accept it,
but if it does not materialize now I think
the matter ought not to be lost sight of
and ought to be brought forward on a
later Clause of the Bill when I suggest
a certain possibility to the House. For
the present I must say I hope this Amend
ment will be received in a cordial and
more receptive spirit. This is not a trifle
or a matter of whether a man can buy a
box of matches or not. This is a much
more serious thing. I must support the
Amendment of my hon. Friend.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I do not agree
with the previous speaker that many of
the hotels in Ireland are used for the
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dickson]
purpose of hotels. I quite agree that in
the cities of Belfast and Derry and perhaps in seaside resorts hotels are conducted for the purpose for which they
are named as hotels and where people
stay. But if we go round Northern Ireland in every little village you have
'hotel" marked up on the outside of a
place where no person ever stays. All
that is necessary to turn that public-house
into a hotel is to have four rooms--
Mr. LESLIE: No.
Mr. HILL DICKSON---and to get
licensed accordingly as a hotel. These
rooms are usually occupied by the proprietor himself, because he would have
no place else to sleep in if he did not.
They are simply public-houses, and I
hold that the owners of these places are
not philanthropists. They are out to make
money, and if you allow the visitor who
comes down on Saturday and pays half-acrown for a room which he need not
necessarily occupy he can open the publichouse to the whole village next morning,
and invite the whole village. He is
within the law in doing so. It would be
worth his while, and it would not be
breaking the law. I hold it would be reintroducing the bona-fide traveller
Clause. I do not suggest for one moment
that any respectable well-managed hotels
in the places I have referred to in the
cities would permit it. But take the
little villages, where as I tell you, no
person ever sleeps. They are simply
public-houses.
Mr. LESLIE: As I have a loose copy
of the 1923 Act with me, perhaps I might
interpose for a moment to remind my hon.
Friend of what the provisions are regarding hotels:
are
The expression "hotel" means
premises licensed for the sale of intoxicating liquor by retail, which
structurally adapted to be used and
are bona-fide used for the purpose of
the reception of guests and travellers-
Therefore, if the small hotels so called
in the North of Ireland, with which I
gather neither of us is likely to have any
sympathy, have guest rooms which are
being used for the purposes of the proprietor of the hotel and not bona-fide for
the reception of guests and travellers they
are breaking the law, and would not be
entitled to the protection which my hon.
Friend from Londonderry wishes to give.
There is no mistake about it. It must be
a bona-fide hotel for the reception of
guests and travellers
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
did not intend to support this Amendment, but evidently on this Amendment
hinges the whole principle. If this matter
is decided it really does away with the
other Amendment to the other portion
which I have down to be deleted. I think
as far as I can see we are really discussing the principle of the entire removal
of the limitations on hotels at the present
time. I want to say I am certainly in
favour of removing unnecessary restrictions placed on hotels, and I am speaking
of proper bona-fide hotels, such as exist in
Belfast, Derry, Portrush, Newcastle, and
these other seaside towns. It is quite
evident in the North of Ireland we are at
a disadvantage. We depend to a considerable extent on the tourist traffic and
we are endeavouring as far as possible
to encourage that traffic. To retain the
restrictions that at present exist on those
who occupy or reside in hotels is injurious
to hotel traffic and tourist traffic.
Furthermore, hotels are considerably used
by travellers, and I think we all desire
to encourage as far as possible travellers
coming to Ireland, even to other parts of
Ireland as well as to our own Six Counties.
To inflict these restrictions still on
those who are making use of hotels is
very unfair. It is more like grandmotherly legislation. I must admit that
I do not see the necessity, even for temperance purposes, to have such restrictions
imposed on grown-up persons. It might
be necessary for young persons who have
no control over themselves and who re
quire the protection of the law, but in the
case of grown-up people I cannot for the
life of me see any necessity for it, even
looking at the matter from the temperance
point of view. The policy of the Unionist party so far as the drink traffic is
concerned is education and regulation.
No one would attempt to defend those
who take drink to excess, and what we
aim at is to leave some freedom to those
who are able to take care of themselves
and not impose our views upon them
against their will. If this liberty is taken
advantage of it is the fault of the authorities for not looking after licensed premises properly. If the police were doing
their duty in regard to the ordinary
public-houses there would not be any
