F
=?
drinking taking place on a Sunday, because the law distinctly states there must
be no licensed premises open for the sale
or consumption of drink on Sunday.
a
There are a large number of people who
make an hotel their home, and such
persons are entitled to the same freedom
and privileges as they would have if they
were living in their own homes. Therefore, I think the Government should remove these slight restrictions. A most
unfair position is created under this Bill
While the owner of a bona-fide hotel has
to pay for his licence and must satisfy
the authorities as to the structural suitability of the hotel he is restricted to a
much greater extent than the person who
may have what he calls
temperance
hotel on the other side of the street.
Drink can be carried into that temperance hotel, and can be consumed at any
hour of the day or night irrespective of
the police or anyone else. To place any
restrictions on the bona-fide hotel that is
properly licensed is very unfair, and,
therefore, I am fully in favour of the
further freedom that is asked for in this
Amendment being given to those who are
using hotels as their residence, so that
they will not be restricted to any particular hours during which they can purchase and consume drink on the hotel
premises.
It is almost ridiculous to say "consume," because everyone knows that even
if the hours of sale are restricted a person
cannot be prevented from consuming,
because if he buys drink during opening
hours he can take it to his bedroom and
consume it whenever he likes, irrespective of the police or anyone else. It would
be very hard for the police to detect
drinking under those circumstances, and
I think the best way out of the difficulty
would be to remove the restrictions and
give hotel guests absolute freedom.
VISCOUNT BANGOR: I received a
letter a day or two ago, from the Secretary of the Belfast and North of Ireland
Hotel Association, and I dare say other
Members have received a similar letter.
They point out the disabilities under
which they labour in being restricted in
these hours, and they also point out that
in towns of under 5,000 inhabitants the
hours are from 10 a.m. to 9 p.m., and
that although the permanent population
is under 5,000, in the summer time it is
a great deal more than 5,000. Even in
that case they cannot sell any excisable
refreshments after 9 p.m. As has been
pointed out by Senator Glendinning,
under the Summer Time Act 9 p.m. is
really 7-30 p.m., and in the summer time
people like to take their dinner a little
later. They will come in and order
liquor, and at 9 o'clock it is swept off the
table and they are not allowed to have it.
The proprietors of these hotels are also
in an unfair position compared with the
of temperance hotels, for the
latter can take in liquor at any time they
like. This Clause is interfering with the
liberty of the subject, and therefore I
shall certainly support the Amendment.
owners
Mr. DORMAN: I think the whole point
centres on the word "consume." If a
man has his home in an hotel, as many
individuals have, he is going to be debarred under this Clause from a right
and privilege he can exercise in his own
home or in any temperance hotel. I do
not speak at all with the object of encouraging the consumption of intoxicating
liquor, because I would desire to see everyone a total abstainer like myself, but it
seems to me that we are depriving the
citizen who may of necessity be compelled
to make his permanent or temporary
residence in an hotel of a right he can
exercise in his own home or in a temperance hotel. As a lover of freedom I shall
support the Amendment, if it goes to a
division.
Mr. McDOWELL: Senator Glendınning's first objection to this Clause was
that it would have a bad effect on tourists
visiting this country. I wonder if he has
allowed his imagination to run away with
him to the extent of believing that
tourists come to Ireland for no other purpose than to taste Bushmills and Comber
whisky. As a rule I think they come to
see the beauties of Ireland, and my information is that the tourist traffic to Ireland has increased since 1923. There is
the point about travellers. They come
here, and through force of circumstances
they go to hotels. They have from 10
a.m. to 10 p.m. to drink as much as they
like, and a traveller who cannot drink all
he wants in that time does perhaps deserve
to have two or three hours extra. I cannot see any reason why hotel hours should
be any more than public house hours:
They should all be treated on the same
basis, and if a man wants a drink he can
get it before 10 p.m. I cannot follow
İntoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McDowell]
the reasoning of Senator Viscount Bangor
in what he said about summer time.
said 9 p.m. really means 7-30 p.m., but
he should not forget that the clock has
changed as well as the hour.
He
I must also point out that if the Committee accepts these Amendments they
will make a very serious change in the
Bill, and so far as I know it may mean
other changes in the Bill subsequently.
This must cause considerable delay and it
might mean that we should have to hold
up the Bill until another Session. Therefore, I do appeal to the hon. Member not
to press these Amendments.
Mr. DORMAN: The sun has not
changed.
no
Mr. McDOWELL: It makes
difference so far as the time in an hotel
is concerned. I think this is another
attempt, even by those who believe in
temperance and have been temperance
advocates for fifty years, to open the door
wider. The licensing laws seem to have
had a curious effect. They created a
certain position to enable a man to get
drunk, and then threw a lot of temptation
in his way to get drunk again. That
seems to be at variance with the real
intention of the licensing laws. Temperance people want to try to introduce
healthy social conditions for the people.
For these reasons I shall oppose the
Amendment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The Amendments proposed
by Senator Glendinning are very important, and they are Amendments
which cut clean across one of the main
objects of the Bill of 1923-clean across
it. The effect of the Amendments must
be to allow a guest or traveller in an
hotel to obtain drink at any hour of the
day or night. One of the main principles
of the Act of 1923 was the limitation of
opening hours in all licensed premises.
These Amendments would practically do
away with that principle. A similar
Amendment was moved in another place,
and after considerable debate it was rejected by the Government on the ground
that, as I said before, it cuts across the
main principle of the Act of 1923. This
Amending Bill was not brought in by the
Government to modify any of the main
principles of the Act of 1923. It is true
that it is an amending Bill, but it is not
intended to seriously modify any of the
main principles of the Act of 1923 on
which this Bill has been founded. I think
hon. Members should realize that in the
Bill which is now before the House the
Government have endeavoured to take
what I may call the media via between
the extremists on the one side and the
extremists on the other side. It is a
Bill that has been debated for many days
in another place, and it has come to us in
its present form.
But
Mr. GLENDINNING: After listening
to all that has been said on these Amend.
ments, I do not think there has been

I now find myself in a very difficult
position indeed. I stated myself on the
Second Reading that I regarded the Bill
as a very important Measure, and one
calculated to do a great deal of good in
many other respects. I have already admitted that even with regard to hotels
the Bill has gone a step in the right
direction by allowing guests in hotels on
Sunday and Christmas Day to have a
drink and not confining them to the time
they are actually having a meal. I should
be very sorry indeed if any step on my

postponing the operation of it for any
considerable time. I should like to ask
the Leader of the House where we would
be if the Committee carried the Amendment-where we would be with regard to
the fate of the Bill.

CLAUSE 3 (Amendment of Provisions
of Section 9 of the Act of 1923 as to new
licences):
(1) In any case where (whether before or after the passing of this Act)
compensation has been awarded under
the Act of 1923 by reason of the nonrenewal in respect of any premises of
a licence for the sale of intoxicating
liquor, such premises shall not, for the
purposes of paragraph (a) of section
nine of the Act of 1923, be treated as
premises coming within the first exception in that paragraph..
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
(2) No licence shall be granted under
paragraph (a) (i), (b), (c) or (e) of
section nine of the Act of 1923 except
a licence which is identical in character
with the licence or licences previously
in force, extinguished or surrendered
(as the case may require) or a licence
to which paragraph (iii) of sub-section
(6) of this section applies.
(3) It shall not be lawful to grant,
in pursuance of the provisions of paragraph (a) (i) of section nine of the
Act of 1923, a retailer's on-licence
which does not contain any condition
as to early-closing under section two of
the Licensing Act (Ireland), 1874 (in
sub-section referred to as "a full-time
licence') for premises having an earlyclosing licence.
Any full-time licence which has been
granted since the passing of the Act of
1923, for premises having at the passing
of that Act an early-closing licence,
shall be renewable only as an earlyclosing licence; and any certificate
granted at the annual licensing sessions
held in the year nineteen hundred and
twenty-seven, and authorising the issue
of a full time licence for such premises,
shall have effect as a certificate for an
early-closing licence.
(4) the following paragraph shall be
substituted for paragraph (b) of section
nine of the Act of 1923:-
"(b) Where, by reason of the expiration of a lease, a retailer's onlicence or a retailer's off-licence in
respect of premises comprised in the
lease is extinguished or surrendered,
the licensing authority may, notwithstanding anything in this section, grant a licence for suitable
premises in the vicinity of the
premises to which the licence so extinguished or surrendered was
attached:
Provided that no such licence shall
be granted to any person unless he
has abandoned any claim which he
may have, under section five of the
Town Tenants (Ireland) Act, 1906,
any subsequent enactment, to
compensation for the loss of goodwill
in respect of a tenancy of the premises to which the licence so extinguished or surrendered was attached."
or

a
re-
(5) Where the business of
staurant, being a restaurant which hasan
on-licence in respect of which reduced
duty has been paid under section fortyfive of the Finance (1909-10) Act, 1910,
is proposed to be transferred to premises
other than those in respect of which the
licence is in force, then the licensing
court may, notwithstanding anything
in section 9 of the Act of 1923, grant
a certificate authorizing the issue of an
on-licence for suitable premises in the
immediate vicinity of the premises
from which the business is to be transferred in substitution for the licence
attached to the last-mentioned premises:
Provided that--
(i) no public bar for the sale of
intoxicating liquor shall be kept
under authority of such licence; and
(ii) where the premises originally
licensed are acquired compulsorily
by any Government department or
any local or public authority no
licence shall be granted under this
sub-section to any person unless he
has abandoned any claim which he
may have to compensation in respect
of so much of the value of the premises compulsorily acquired as is
attributable to their use as licensed
premises.
(6) A licence shall not be granted
under paragraph (c) of section nine of
the Act of 1923 in substitution for any
existing licences, except under the
following conditions:-
(i) The effect of the substitution
must be that the sale of every kind
of intoxicating liquor ceases in at
least two separate sets of premises or,
where an off-licence, attached to the
premises in respect of which the new
licence is sought, is surrendered, in
at least one separate set of premises;
(ii) The new licence must be identical in character with the licences in
substitution for which it is sought,
except in cases to which the next following paragraph applies;
(iii) Where the application is for a
new retailer's on-licence, one of the
existing licences, in substitution for
which the new licence is sought, may
be a retailer's off-licence; ethn

Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

(iv) The required increase of population shall be proved by means of a
certificate of the Minister of Home
Affairs that he is satisfied that there
has been an increase of not less than
twenty-five per cent. since the census
ascertained next before the passing of
the Act of 1923; and such fee shall
be payable for the issue of the certificate as the said Minister may, with
the approval of the Ministry of
Finance, determine.
(7) Where a new licence is granted
under paragraph (a), (b) or (e) of
Section nine of the Act of 1923 or under
Sub-section (5) of this Section, any convictions recorded against the old licence
shall be deemed to apply, and always
to have applied, to the new licence. A
new licence shall, if granted under the
said paragraph (e), include both the
old and the new premises.
Mr. GLENDINNING: With regard to
the Amendment, standing in my name, I
would like to explain to hon. Members
who do not exactly understand this
Mr. SPEAKER: May I just, first of
all, so that there will be no misunderstanding, explain that I must put line
23 to line 26. If I were to put the
Amendment put down by the hon. Memberit would knock out Mr. Leslie's
Amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: Am I in order
in moving my Amendment now?
Mr. SPEAKER: Yes.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I beg to move
Clause 3, page 4, line 23, to leave out
lines 23 to 31 inclusive (the second paragraph of Sub-clause (3). It seems that
after the passing of the 1923 Act certain
traders to the number of 10, I believe,
and all in County Fermanagh, applied to
the county court judge for an extension
of their early-closing licences, and the
county court judge granted the extension.
These traders since then have been enjoying full-time licences. They have paid
the full-time duty and no objection has
been taken by the trade to their enjoyment of it. The Licensed Vinters'
Association of County Fermanagh fully
approved of the transaction. It is only
fair to say that the Government took
another case to the higher court, and the
licence was refused by the higher court,
although it also had been granted by the
county court judge. The first part of
Clause 3 is to prevent anything like that
happening in future. The paragraph
which my Amendment seeks to eliminate
is retrospective, and goes back to wipe out
what has been done with regard to these
ten traders, and to restore the early-closing licence which they formerly possessed!! These members of the Licensed
Traders' Association have got this extension of time by the law of the land.
They have held it since then. They have
been permitted by the Government to enjoy all the benefit of it. It really only
means one extra hour from eight to nine
o'clock. Now, I am prepared to admit
that the Government are perfectly right
from their point of view in going back,
because it was never intended, I daresay,
that the Act of 1923 should result in
early-closing licences being extended to
full-time licences. Why it was done I am
sure I do not know, but I would appeal
to this House and to the Government, as
it has been done, and as these few traders
alone are affected, and as the Government
themselves have practically acquiesced in
it, and as they have held the licences for
three years the Government should not
go back and put in this Clause which deals
with these few people alone. I am not
acquainted with any of them. The hon.
Senator, Mr. Porter Porter, who lives in
their district and knows them all, is keenly
interested in this Amendment. As
matter of fact, I understood at first he
was going to propose it himself, but he
is in London, and he asked me to take
over that duty for him.
a
I think, after all, as this thing has been
done and no principle of the Bill is
affected by it in any way the Government might leave out this particular
Clause, and leave these few men to retain
the benefit they have got. The Government can make friends or enemies, and
here is an opportunity of making several
very good friends in County Fermanagh
who might, otherwise, be inimical to them.
It would, I admit, be a very generous act
on the part of the Government. None the
less I would like to see these few people
remain, as their county court judge gave
them full-time licences. It would be a
generous thing on the part of the Government to leave out this retrospective
part of the Clause.
Mr. McDOWELL: In connection with
these nine or ten Fermanagh publicans, I
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McDowell]
think the same thing applies perhaps to
the whole Six Counties. I would like to
see justice done to every man, but in
connection with these short-time houses I
will put it this way. Two houses are up
for sale. One is a short day and the
other is a long day, or one eight hours and
the other nine hours, whichever way you
like to put it. These men bought these
houses the early-closing ones-at a certain price. Other County Fermanagh
men bought the other houses at a higher
price perhaps, to the tune of anywhere
between £6,000 and £14,000. Is it
right, even in the interests of the trade,
for these men to have the same facilities
for the small price as those who gave the
higher price. I contend that it is not.
I think you will find there are other
houses in the Six Counties in exactly the
same position. These are short licences
and
others are long licences,
and they bought accordingly. They
knew what they were doing when they
were buying. Consequently, I think
they have no right to be considered. It
is only restricting them from breaking the
1923 Act deliberately.
Mr. DORMAN: I think there is a great
principle involved in this matter, and a
principle that this House, as a Second
Chamber, is set up to protect. There are
twelve individuals who are apparently interested in this particular clause. There
are the nine individuals engaged in the
sale of intoxicating liquor in a legal and
constitutional manner. There is the
learned County Court judge who has confirmed the application of these nine citizens for an extension of their licence at
a period of transition when the applicants
were passing from the control of previous
Acts under the control of a new Act that
was termed by many persons a temperance
Act. I understand that when these nine
individuals made their application it was
opposed, whether by the police or by the
temperance party in Co. Fermanagh I
do not know. If the applications were
opposed on the part of the police, I believe
they would be acting under the instructions of the Ministry responsible for the
enforcement of that licensing Act. The
County Court judge, believing that the
intention of the Government in that
Act was to unify the conditions of licence
holders, extended to these nine individual
licence holders the right of opening for
one hour longer for the sale of liquor.
If that was considered by the then Attorney-General as being contrary to the
Act, it was his duty, in my opinion, to inform the Minister of Home Affairs that in
his opinion the decision of the County
Court judge was contrary to the Act of
Parliament, and it was his duty to advise
the responsible Minister to appeal to a
higher court against the decision of the
County Court judge. Whether or not the
then learned Attorney-General had a
doubt in his mind as to the correctness of
the decision of the County Court judge I
do not presume to say. But suppose he
had no doubt, and that the responsible
Minister of the Executive had a doubt, it
was his duty to direct the Attorney-
General to institute an appeal immediately, so as to ascertain what was the
law on the question.
After the County Court Judge, in
accordance with his interpretation of the
law, had given these individuals an extended licence, I am informed that
several of them-I do not know any of
them personally--proceeded to spend
money on their permises on the faith that
the Government would not appeal against
the decision of the County Court Judge,
and in the hope that they would be ultimately reimbursed by the proceeds of
their trade during the extra hour they
were allowed to keep open. So far as I
know there has been no complaint on the
part of the police or the Temperance
party in Fermanagh that these individual
licence holders have violated the law and
have not conducted their premises in a
proper manner.
I do not want to occupy the time of the
Committee unnecessarily, but on the
Second Reading of this Bill the noble Viscount stated that these cases were ruled
by the decision in another case concerning
a lady who made an application for an
extension of her licence for the following
year. I understand that it was against
the decision of the same County Court
Judge that the appeal was made, and in
the higher Court his decision was reversed.
I do not think it is right that we should
allow the Minister of Home Affairs to
clandestinely put into this Bill this retrospective Clause. In confirmation of my
argument may I quote from a celebrated
Dublin lawyer, a well-known gentleman
who was at one time the leader of the
Unionist party of Northern Ireland, Lord
Carson. Speaking in the House of Lords
445 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

8 DECEMBER 1927
Committee

:
last week he spoke very strongly of the
apparent negligence of the House of Lords
in executing its constitutional duties from
day to day. He made one observation
which is directly applicable to the
principle about which I am speaking.
He said he was utterly opposed to the
growing system of legislation which deprived his Majesty's subjects of the opportunity of going to his Majesty's Courts.
I believe it is our duty to eliminate this
particular Clause from the Bill, and to
say to the Minister of Home Affairs and
the responsible Members of the Government, 'We will not assist you to deprive
his Majesty's subjects of the right of appealing to his Majesty's Courts." When
these licence holders make an application
for the renewal of their licences, the
proper authority can take steps to prevent them getting the full licence which
they have been granted, but it is not for
the Government, in this Bill, to attempt
to deprive them of a right which has been
conferred upon them by the County Court
Judge.
porting the Amendment.
briefly when he said that if we took the
first part of the paragraph out we should
be giving an undue advantage to certain
people.

Amendment is to regularize something
These nine licensees

were given a full license through a mistake of the County Court Judge. A test

and the decision of the County Court
Judge was held to be wrong. Because

three years, we are now asked to continue
to allow them to have something they
ought never to have had, to the great
disadvantage of other early-closing licen-

point out, that assuming this sub-section

because they could then be forced to proceed by mandamus and in all probability
they would lose their licence altogether.
I really think that in the interest of the

Amendment.
sees in the Six Counties. I have much pleasure in sup-
Mr. HILL DICKSON: The effect of
this Amendment would be that some
traders would get an advantage over
other traders in the Six Counties through
the mistaken decision of a County Court
Judge. I shall oppose the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am in a difficulty over
this proposal, I am bound to oppose
Senator Glendinning as regards his
Amendment to do away with the first
paragraph, but I hope that with his wellknown forgiving spirit he will support me
when I suggest to the Committee that we
should do away with the second part of
the paragraph. The difficulty is this.
It never was the intention of the 1923
Act that early-closing licences should ever
become full-time licences. I think that
is perfectly plain. It never was intended
under any circumstances that an earlyclosing licence should become a full-time
one. That being so, any such licence
granted since the Act of 1923 was passed
must have been granted owing to some
mistake, and, after all, nobody in the
County of Fermanagh, or outside it, has
any vested interest in the maintenance of
the law in the existing way. The other
question raised in the latter part of the
paragraph is a different one. Senator
Hill Dickson put the matter fairly and
or
Mr. GLENDINNING: If there is any
danger of the persons concerned losing
the licence that they will enjoy apart
from this Clause, I certainly will withdraw my Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 3, page 4, line 26, leave out
from "licence" to the end of line 31
(and any certificate granted at the
annual licensing sessions held in the
year nineteen hundred and twentyseven, and authorisng the issue of a fulltime licence for such premises, shall
have effect as a certificate for an earlyclosing licence).
Mr. LESLIE: Now we approach the
second half of this paragraph and it seems
to me that although I could not support
447 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee

[Mr. Leslie]
the proposal to leave out the first part,
the proposal to leave out the second part
is an entirely different matter. The only
effect of leaving out the second part of the
paragraph would apparently be that these
licence holders in Fermanagh would be
able to carry on their full-time licences
until the expiration of the current licensing year for which it was granted, and
at the end of that year they would have
to revert to their proper position as early
closing licences. The position as regards
the present licensing year seems to me to
be distinctly different. What these people say in the petition which they have
sent to me, and I presume to other Members of the Committee, is:-
It is now contended by the persons
in charge of the proposed Bill that no
legal rights are being taken from us.
This is entirely contrary to the facts.
At the last Annual Licensing Sessions,
each of us, when applying for a renewal
of our licence was objected to on the
grounds that the order made at Enniskillen Quarter Sessions on the 26th
October, 1923, was invalid. The obection was overruled by the Annual
Licensing Court, and the District
Inspector appealed to the Quarter
Sessions. The District Inspector withdrew each of the appeals having ascertained that no authority save Parliament could deprive us of the benefit of
the Judicial Decision under which we
acquired our licence. Accordingly,
the second paragraph of Section
(3), Sub-section 3, has been put
in the Bill for the sole purpose
of depriving us nine people of our
rights. The deletion of this paragraph
from the Bill will only affect us nine
traders. It cannot have any effect on
the Early-Closing Licence in Belfast or
elsewhere.
The principle at stake is are the
rights of us nine licensed traders to be
forfeited by an Act of Parliament. We
respectfully submit that it would be
against common justice to single us out
and forfeit our rights acquired under
the Judicial Decision of the County
Court Judge.
I must say that I think their claim is
good. They ought to be left alone until
the end of their licensing year. If the
District Inspector did not choose to carry
on his appeal or take such steps as he
might have taken by case stated it is not
the affair of these nine people in Fermanagh. I am sorry the hon. Senator
from Fermanagh (Mr. Porter-Porter)
who first brought this matter before us
has found it impossible to attend to-day,
but I hope that in his absence the limited
plea I have put forward will be supported
by Senator Glendinning, and I hope the
Committee will consent to do what I
really think is a simple act of justice,
namely, to leave these people alone until
teh expiration of their current licence and
then they must come under the ordinary
law. As it stands at present I think
they are right. Apart from the direct
interference of Parliament the licences
cannot be interfered with. It is not fair
to load the dice against them for this
short time.
Mr. GLENDINNING: It is scarcely
necessary for me to say anything on the
subject. I do heartily support the
Amendment of Senator Leslie as better
than nothing.
of
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): The Government regard
this Clause as
some importance,
because it not only involves the number
stated here, but there are other matters
of an analogous character affected by it.
I cannot follow the hon. Senator who
claimed that grievous injustice would be
done to those people because their wholetime licences would cease to have effect
from the passing of this Act. In fact,
the Section is interwoven with the whole
principle of the Clause, and I do not see
that it can be eliminated from it. Certainly the Government would look with
grave apprehension if there was any departure from the literal terms of the
Clause, whereby the licensee is only entitled to his short-time licence from the
passing of this Act.
and any certificate granted at the
annual licensing sessions held in the
year nineteen hundred and twentyseven, and authorising the issue of a
full-time licence for such premises, shall
have effect as a certificate for an earlyclosing licence.
It was perfectly obvious that a mistake
had been made in granting the full-time
licences, because on appeal it was overturned.. Having been overturned, the
licence reverts to an early-closing one.
The Government see no reason whatever
Intoxicating Liquor Bill—
Committee
8 DECEMBER 1927
why any departure should be made from
the principle. Therefore, I hope the
Committee will support the Government
in carrying out the full effect of Subsection (3) of Clause 3.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I cannot
say that I have been convinced in this
matter. I presume I am right from what
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Finance, says, in saying that the time remaining is very short. He does not give
us dates. They say that at the last
Annual Licensing Sessions their application was objected to, and the objection
was overruled.
Mr. POLLOCK: The extension should
not have been given.
Mr. LESLIE: The objection was overruled. The district-inspector appealed.
The resources of the law were exhausted
against these people. Surely they ought
to remain until the licences expire. They
will have a grievance if it is taken away
from them now.
Mr. POLLOCK: Senator Hill Dickson
really crystallized the whole subject. It
is an injustice to others carrying on their
legitimate business in a legitimate way.
Apart altogether from the argument of
Senator Leslie, I do not see that the
licensee has any ground for complaint.
For these years they have practically been
carrying on illegally a trade they should
not have been carrying on, and because
of the summary discontinuance by the
Bill as before the House, it does not
appear to me they are suffering any
grievance of any kind. I may say there
are other cases not exactly, but almost on
parallel lines with these, and I hope
Senator Leslie will not press anything
that would be an embarrassment to the
Government.
Mr. LESLIE: I am extremely sorry, as
I said before, that the Senator from that
county is not here. I do not feel justified
in his absence in pressing anything that
would be an embarrassment to the
Government.
Mr. POLLOCK: It would be.
Mr. LESLIE: I will ask to be permitted to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 6,
This
I
line 18 (Sub-clause (6), paragraph IV)
after 'fee' ("such fee shall") to insert
"not exceeding twenty shillings."
matter is one of fees. I more than once
objected to fees being left so to speak
blank in a Bill to be settled by one of
two Departments. It is usually the
Minister of Home Affairs with the approval of the Minister of Finance.
have always maintained the constitutional
principle that the fees should be settled
by Parliament. It is Parliament's business to deal with a matter of this sort. It
is not the business of the two Departments. I have suggested a fee not exceeding 20 shillings because I think it
would not be likely to be greater than
that. The point at issue is not so much
the amount of the fee as the principle that
the fee ought to be in the Bill, and ought
to be the work of Parliament. You will
find in other passages of the Bill the fee
is definite. The practice is, I am afraid,
rather increasing of leaving the fees
blank. There cannot be any reason why
the House should not be told what the
fee is. The Bill has been on the stocks
two years, and I presume the Ministries
concerned are aware of the amount of the
fee they propose to charge. If not they
ought to be. I am rather glad to have
the opportunity of again asserting my
adherence to the constitutional principle
in the presence of the Minister of
Finance.
Mr. POLLOCK: I do not think you
need worry.
Mr. LESLIE: It is a question of principle, and I beg to move to insert "not
exceeding 20 shillings."
It
Mr. POLLOCK: I hope Senator
Leslie will not press his Amendment.

rather suggests that the Minister of Finance would not exercize the necessary

(2) It shall not be lawful for any person to supply, sell or offer for sale
methylated ether, except in the following cases:-
(a) Where the ether is supplied,
sold or offered to a person registered
under the Medical Acts or the Dentists' Acts, 1878 to 1927, for the purposes of his profession; or
(b) Where the ether is supplied,
or sold on the order of a person registered as aforesaid, for use in hospital, infirmary, dispensary, or other
similar institutions; or
(c) Where the ether is supplied,
sold or offered to a wholesale dealer
for the purpose of export from
Northern Ireland: or
(d) Where the ether is supplied,
sold, or offered for sale, by wholesale,
to a retail dealer in the ordinary
course of wholesale dealing;
(e) Where the transaction is for
such purpose, or takes place under
such conditions, as may be authorised
by regulations of the Ministry of
Home Affairs;
but nothing in this provision shall be in
derogation of section two of the Poisons (Ireland) Act, 1870 (as amended
by subsequent enactments) so far as it
applies to any sale of methylated ether.
Any person who acts in contravention of the provisions of this subsection, or who has methylated ether
in his possession otherwise than for a
purpose for which such ether may be
lawfully purchased, sold or supplied
under this sub-section, shall be guilty
of an offence under the Act of 1923.
(3) If a justice of the peace is satisfied, by information on oath, that there
is reasonable ground for suspecting
that any methylated spirits are exposed
or kept for sale, at any place within his
jurisdiction (whether a building or not)
in contravention of the provisions of the
Act of 1923 or this Act, that justice
may. in his discretion, grant a search
warrant authorizing any constable
named in the warrant, at any time or
times within one month from the date
of the warrant to enter (if need be by
force) the place named in the warrant
and every part thereof, and to examine
the same and search for methylated
spirits therein and seize and remove
any methylated spirits found therein,
which there is reasonable ground to
suspect are in such place for the purpose of sale at that or any other place
in contravention of the said provisions,
and the vessels containing such spirits.
In the event of the owner or occupier
of such premises being convicted of exposing or keeping for sale any methylated spirits in contravention of the
said provisions, the spirits so seized,
and the vessels containing the same,
shall be forfeited.
The provisions of this sub-section
shall apply to methylated ether in like
manner as they apply to methylated
spirits.
(4) Every wholesale dealer in methylated ether shall keep, in such form as
may be prescribed by the Ministry of
Home Affairs, particulars of all his
sales of such ether, and a wholesale

dealer failing to keep such particulars,
or to produce them to any person
authorized by the said Ministry when

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
13, line 27, after "a" ("in a hospital")
to insert "nursing-home."
Clause 9 is
rather difficult Clause dealing with
methylated ether. It will be rememhered that we all agreed that the use of
methylated ether for potation purposes
has got to be suppressed. I ventured to
point out at the time some little difficulty
in making the powers perhaps too rigid.
The fact that methylated ether is capable
of abuse does not take away the proper
use of it. It still has a proper use, and
it becomes very important in the Amending Bill to see that we are not again making mistakes. After all, we should not
have an Amending Bill if we did not make
mistakes before. After what was stated
by the Senator representing the University about the use of methylated ether for
anæsthetical purposes, it seems to me
necessary to insert nursing-home as one
of the places to which ether may be supplied on the order of a person registered
under the Medical Acts or the Dentist
Acts.
seems necessary to insert
nursing-home specially, because a nursinghome is not a hospital, an infirmary, or
a dispensary. A hospital, strictly speaking, is not a medical foundation at all.
Christ Church Hospital, commonly called
the Blue Coat School, is not necessarily
connected with healing at all. These
things implied by the words "infirmary,
dispensary, or other similar institution" are usually gratuitous and
sometimes eleemosynary as well.
They
are entirely different from a nursinghome, which is usually a private place
where paying patients are received.
is different from this paragraph, where
laboratories are spoken of and scientific
institutions requiring methylated ether.
Speaking for myself, I am not disposed to
leave nursing-homes quite in that position.
I know there are certain difficulties in the
way. Nursing-homes are not registered
here.
It
It
At the same time it seems to me
that the possible risk, in the way of
suffering, and life, is not one that ought
to be incurred. Therefore, I felt that
the Committee would support my suggestion that these words should be inserted.
The responsibility of leaving them out is
a responsibility I would desire to avoid.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
think this Amendment is unnecessary.
He speaks about a hospital. I always

"nursing-home" you will have to define

was my house a nursing-home? (Laughter.) I know there was plenty of nursing.
I think this Amendment is unnecessary,

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Article 9 of the Second Schedule to leave
out "two" and insert "one." The point
is to ask the Government whether they
really do want two resident magistrates
to transfer or renew a license. There is
always trouble in country districts in getting two resident magistrates together,
and it is very unfortunate that we cannot
get a club registration renewed without
collecting two resident magistrates. It is
a very expensive and troublesome business, and it occurs to me that here is a
case where one magistrate would do.
may be that all the theatres and music
It
halls in Northern Ireland are concentrated in Belfast and Derry where two
resident magistrates can easily be got,
but it seems to me that one resident
magistrate ought to be sufficient.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I can quite confirm what Senator Leslie has said as to
the difficulty of getting two resident
magistrates in country districts, and the
inconvenience that is caused. If the Government could see their way to meet this
difficulty I think it would be advisable
to do so.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: There are only five SO
called theatres in Northern Ireland, four
in Belfast and one in Derry. That will
answer the question asked by Senator
Leslie on the Second Reading. The
reason why the Government consider it
necessary that there should be two
rather than one resident magistrate is
that theatres are in a somewhat different
position to public-houses. The higher
value of a theatre as compared with
public-house necessitates greater care in
the transfer or renewal of a licence. The
Committee will appreciate that it was
mainly for the sake of economy that provision was made for the renewal or transfer of a publican's licence to be granted
by one resident magistrate, but as four
of the five theatres in Northern Ireland
are in Belfast and the other in Derry no
great expense would be incurred by requiring the attendance of two resident
magistrates.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Schedule agreed to.

Old Age Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
9 DECEMBER 1927
VEI
