THE SENATE.

Friday, 9th December, 1927.
The Senate met at Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Old Age Pensions (Administration) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In rising to move the
Second Reading of the Old Age Pensions
(Administration) Bill, I should like to
say that my right hon. Friend, the Minister of Labour, has come here to-day, in
order to answer any technical points that
may be raised during the course of the
debate, and to which I myself might find
some difficulty in replying.
As hon.
Members know, this is not the first time
the right hon. Gentleman has been in this
House. He was here twice in 1923 and
again in 1925, and I am sure we are all
pleased to see him here once again. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
The purpose of this Bill is to transfer
to the Ministry of Labour the responsibility for the administration in Northern
Ireland of the public service in connection with non-contributory old-age pensions, and to make certain changes in the
method of determining claims and questions under the Old Age Pensions Acts.
It is, I think, desirable that I should, at
the outset, give to the House the reasons
which have led to the introduction of this
Bill. Non-contributory old-age pensions
transferred Service under the
are
a
Government of Ireland Act, and responsi-
☑ility for their administration at the
☑oment rests with two Departments of
he Government, the Ministry of Finance
nd the Ministry of Home Affairs, and,
s respects certain important functions,
With Local Pensions Committees.
An essential feature of the effective administration of the Service consists in a
preliminary investigation of claims, and
as the Ministry of Finance has not a staff
of its own officials at local centres in
Northern Ireland outside Belfast, that
Department in 1922 made an agreement
with an Imperial Department, the Commissioners of Customs and Excise, under
which the Customs and Excise officers,
who, as hon. Members know, have many
local duties to perform, would carry out
the necessary preliminary investigations
on claims to pensions. The Imperial
Department was, of course, recouped by
this Government the cost of the time of
Customs officers engaged on this work.
Hon. Members will remember that
when the Contributory Pensions Act of
1925 was passed, the Ministry of Labour
was made the Department responsible for
1ts administration. That Act made provision for pensions at 70 years of age.
without restriction as to means, for persons insured under the National Health
Insurance Acts, who satisfied certain insurance tests, and it further provided that
trom 2nd January, 1928, insured persons
will become entitled to pensions at 65
years of age.
It is obvious that a simpler and more
economical service could be secured by the
transfer to one Department of the administration of all the old-age pension
services, both contributory and non-contributory, certain of the tests, for instance,
the age test, being the same in each case.
Trouble would be saved to the claimant
who would only have one Government
Department to deal with instead of
several, as is the case at the moment.
Moreover, the Ministry of Labour has
already a network of local offices throughout Northern Ireland, and a staff experienced in investigation of similar questions arising under the Contributory
Pensions Act. For these reasons it has
been decided to propose the transfer of
the service to the Ministry of Labour.
Before alluding to the suggested
changes of machinery, I should like to
inform the House briefly what are the
existing arrangements. A person wishing to claim a pension obtains a form of
application from the post office, completes
it and sends it to the Pensions Officer whe
is, as I have mentioned, an officer of the
Customs and Excise Department.
The
Pension Officer makes investigations into
the circumstances of the claim, and, when
he has done so, forwards a Report with
his recommendation to a Local Pension
Committee. These Committees, as hon.
Sir
463 Old Age Pensions Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Members are probably aware, were established under the Old Age Pensions Act of
1908, and are appointed by County and
County Borough Councils and, in certain
circumstances, by Urban District Councils. Where necessary, Local Pension
Committees set up Sub-Committees to
act for districts within the Committees'
areas. The Local Pension Committee
considers the claim in the light of the
Report from the Pension Officer and gives
its decision.
From this decision the claimant or the
Pension Officer may appeal to the Ministry of Home Affairs, which is the final
authority. It will be noted that there are
three different authorities concerned with
the determination of a claim, viz., the
Pension Officer, who is nominally an
Officer of the Ministry of Finance, but
actually an officer of an Imperial Department; second, the Local Pension Committee, a body appointed by a local
authority, and third, the Ministry of
Home Affairs. In addition to these
authorities dealing with non-contributory
pensions there is the Ministry of Labour
which administers or will administer all
pensions payable to insured persons.
these circumstances a certain amount of
confusion must inevitably arise in the
minds of applicants as to what authority
ought to be approached in order that they
may secure any benefits which they may
be entitled to claim.
In
I now come to the machinery proposed
by the Bill. As at present, a person
wishing to claim a pension will obtain a
form from the nearest Post Office: after
completing it he will send it to the nearest
Local Office of the Ministry of Labour.
An officer from the Ministry of Labour
will investigate the claim and will forward it with his report to the headquarters
of his Department. The claim will there
be considered by a Pension Officer, to be
appointed by my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Labour. If the Pension
Officer, after an examination of all the
facts, considers that a pension should be
paid, he authorises the claim at once.
Hon. Members will see, therefore, that
in the case of what I might call straightforward claims there will be no delay in
authorisation. If, however, a Pension
Officer considers that a claim should not
be admitted, he informs the claimant accordingly. The claimant will then have
the right of appeal to a Local Pension

Court, which will be a body composed
ordinarily of three members, two of whom
will be drawn from panels appointed by
the Minister of Labour, and the third a
Chairman appointed by my right hon.
Friend.
The panels from which the personnel
of the Local Pension Court will be drawn
will be set up in various areas throughout
the country. The Local Pension Court
will consider the appeal and will make
a recommendation to the Pension Officer.
The Pension Officer is required to
give effect to that recommendation
unless he disagrees with it, in which
event he must refer the matter to
the Umpire for a final determination.
The Umpire will be an independent person appointed by His Grace the Governor.
Again, should the Local Pension Court
recommend to the Pension Officer that a
claim for pension should be disallowed,
the claimant may appeal to the Umpire
with the leave of the Court, and a form
of words has been introduced in the Bill
in Sub-Cection 5 of Clause 2 with the
object of securing that a claimant should
be given the right of appeal to the Umpire
in any case where it seems to the Court
reasonable that he should have it.
Hon. Members will appreciate that an
unrestricted right of appeal would inevitably lead to frivolous appeals going forward, thus adding unnecessarily to the
cost of administration. It is anticipated
that the proposals in this Bill will result
not alone in the facilitating of applicants
for pensions but also in economies in the
cost of the administration of the Old-Age
Pensions Acts.
Before concluding, I should like to pay
a tribute on behalf of the Government to
the able and efficient service which has
been given to us by the officers of the
Customs and Excise Department, who
have assisted in the administration of this
Act since our Government took over responsibility for it, but I think a special
tribute is due to the members of the
Local Pensions Committees in every area
in Northern Ireland who have given loyal
and dutiful service without reward or
remuneraion, and often at considerable
personal inconvenience. My right hon.
Friend hopes to be able to avail himself
of the services of these ladies and gentlemen to assist in the administration of
the Act under the new procedure proposed in the Bill. I beg to move:
Old Age Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
9 DECEMBER 1927
い
Mr. LONG: I should like to thank the
noble Viscount for the very lucid explanation he has given of this Bill. I regard
the Old Age Pension Act as one of the
two most beneficent Acts the Imperial
Parliament ever passed. It has conferred
inestimable blessings on a very considerable number of people in Northern Ireland. I have been concerned in the administration of the Old Age Pensions
Act since it was first passed in 1908.
I
am the Chairman of the Pensions Committee in the County of Londonderry, and
for the first sixteen years after the passing
of the Act I was Chairman of a Local
Pensions Committee, so that I am familiar
with the procedure that was adopted. I
must say that I consider that the concluding sentence of the noble Viscount's
speech is in conflict with the intention of
the Bill. He pays a very lurid compliment to the members of these Local Committees, and yet they are being treated
with an undeserved slight in being done
away with. I can vouch for this fact,
that the Pensions Committee which constitutes or nominates these Local Committees is most careful in the selection
of the personnel of committees. They are
distributed over a wide area, so that they
may have local knowledge, and they are
selected for fair-mindedness. I must say
the Local Committee with which I was
associated very fairly considered every
claim that came before it. While it was
so that one of our members never could
see anything wrong with a claim of a
person from his neighbourhood, on the
whole I think the Members discharged
their duties very creditably.
Her
I should also acknowledge the sympathetic manner in which the Home
Ministry have dealt with appeals coming
from County Londonderry. I have
worried them perhaps more than any
other person in Northern Ireland on behalf of old-age claimants. I have great
interest in the Bill and its administration. Recently I called at the Ministry of
Home Affairs regarding a poor old woman
who made a claim for a pension.
pension was allowed by the Local Pension
Committee, but an appeal was lodged by
the pensions officer. On calling with the
Home Ministry regarding the matter, I
discovered that if the age she gave on her
marriage certificate was correct she would
still want 166 years of being the age for
a pension. That is one of the peculiar
things we are up against. However, in
another case about which I called recently, I found the valuation of the local
pension officer was a ridiculous thing.
Very many of the pension officers with
whom I have come into contact had no
conception of common sense in dealing
with the people. For instance, if a
poor woman reared ten turkeys, and sold
them for 12s each, the pension officerat least one pension officer-returned her
income as £6. He regarded that as net
income out of her ten turkeys. Last
week I had occasion to call at the Ministry
of Home Affairs regarding an old-age
pensioner.
The pension officer in that
case had found that the man was entitled
to free turbary-the right to cut turf--
and estimated forty loads of turf at 5s a
load, and a total income of £10 from that
right of free turbary. Everyone who is
familiar with the cutting of turf knowsthese were, I should say, for his own
use they would cost at least as much
as they would be worth. The man could
buy coal quite as reasonably. I must say
the Home Ministry agreed with me that
that was an unreasonable thing to charge
that man with.
With regard to the reported saving of
£10,000 which is supposed to accrue after
the passing of this Act I fail to see how
it is arrived at. I should like very much
if the right hon. Gentleman the Minister
of Labour would give us details of that
saving. I know there are eighty clerks
of local pensions committees operating at
say an average of £50 each, and that would
be about £4,000 that might be said to be
saved. On the other hand the chairmen
of these local committees will be paid in
the future. I think that that would
almost if not quite wipe out the saving
that would be effected by the discontinuance of these clerks. Clerks of some sort
and from some place will have to be procured and I think they will have to be
paid. I could recite numerous cases in
which the local knowledge of these local
pensions committees has been of service in
preventing fraudulent claims being
granted. I should like the Minister of
Labour to very kindly furnish details
of that £10,000 that we are supposed to
save. My fear is that the trend of the
Government, as disclosed in this Bill, is to
do away with unpaid assistance in the
shape of these local committees, and to
inaugurate an era of officialdom. That I
think would be very regrettable, and I
trust that it is not the intention of the
Government. I should like the information I ask for before I support the Second
Reading.
Old Age Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to join with the noble Viscount in saying how pleased we are to
have the Minister of Labour attending in
our House this afternoon. It is one of the
advantages of our constitution that Members of the Government are privileged
to sit in this important House along with
such important people. (Laughter.)
As far as the Old Age Pensions (Administration) Bill is concerned I have
really no particular objection to raise to
it, because if the intention is economy
and the simplifying of the system which
has been in existence I certainly think it
will serve a good purpose. The system
that has been in operation for some time
was not simple. It was very confusing.
I have many old-age applicants with me
from time to time wondering at the
delay. It had to pass through different
hands. So that if it is intended to simplify I believe it will have some good
effect. There is only one objection that
I have heard raised to it, and I have some
little feeling in this respect myself, and
that is the abolition of the committees
who were acting voluntarily. I would
like the Minister of Labour to say whether
he is perfectly satisfied that the Government officials will deal as sympathetically
with these poor applicants as the voluntary committees did. We know perfectly
well that among the applicants for old-age
pensions there are a number of very poor
individuals. Indeed, I am sure a considerable number are illiterate, and require a large amount of assistance. Now
it is not usual to find Government officials
very considerate for these dull people who
may apply to them for assistance.
always think those who are closely associated with them know them best and are
more likely to give the sympathy and
assistance they require. That is one of
the principal objections that the electors
have to this Bill.
I
With reference to the courts of referees
I do hope when these referee courts are
set up and certainly when they are carried out properly they are quite satisfactory-I do hope that in connection with
the old-age pensions they will be a little
more satisfactory than the referee courts
which we know of in dealing with unemployment benefit. There was a time
when those courts were very satisfactory.
They could not have been better than
when they had on the bench a workman's
representative, an employers' representa-
| tive, and a chairman. But seldom do we
find the three persons acting on it. It is
generally left to the Chairman to decide.
I feel that the applicants do not get decisions which the cases require. I do hope
care will be taken so far as the old-age
pensioners are concerned, because the
applicants for pensions are likely to be
even more dull and nervous-probably I
might go so far as to say stupid-than
even those applying for unemployment
benefit, so they would deserve a little
more sympathy.
There is the other matter too. I know
that the change of method may displace
some very old and valued persons that
have been giving service in the old-age
scheme for a long time. Those who have
been acting as clerks on old-age pension
committees have given very valuable service, and have become acquainted with the
system and grown up with it, and more
or less attached to it, and are very sorry
to part with it. It is not altogether sentiment; it is remuneration I am just at the
moment thinking of. There are many of
those people who are dependent to a considerable extent on the little remuneration
received from this source, and to have it
cut off just immediately without any consideration is rather unfair, especially
when we listened to the latter part of
the noble Viscount's speech where he
applauds the service they have given so
satisfactorily. If they are to be thrown
over without any consideration, then the
remarks made by the noble Viscount will
not be taken as altogether genuine.
I am sure, at any rate, we will be
pleased to hear the Minister giving
further information on those queries I
have referred to, and while I have no
doubt that the best possible administration of this scheme will be carried out-
I know the Minister of Labour himself
may be very sympathetic to every person,
but he has got to depend on officials carrying out the work. I intend to support
the Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: With a Minister
so wise and sympathetic as the right hon.
Gentleman, I feel sure that many of the
doubts and fears that have been expressed
this afternoon will be removed. Of the
old system I had some experience, like my
hon. Friend beside me. I served about
12 years as chairman of an old-age pensions committee. In spite of complication and the difficulties from the three
469 Old Age Pensions Bill-

9 DECEMBER 1927
Second Reading
L
Si
Departments of State concerned in it, it
was marvellous how well it worked on the
whole. Of course, at first our work was
very heavy, much heavier than it can be
now, because we had accumulations of
people who had passed the age of 70 and
were waiting for the old-age pension, and
for some years the work was undoubtedly
difficult, but after that it became practically routine, and presented very little
difficulty. The members who formed that
committee were, generally speaking, farmers and came from different districts,
and the way in which they performed
their duties could not be excelled by any
body that I have been acquainted with.
They were impartial and fair in all the
decisions they gave. We are singularly
fortunate in having an excellent secretary who has since become the Secretary
of our Education Committee, and we had
no difficulty at that time in dealing with
the Local Government Board, which was
the authority then. Very often that
Board supported the committee in cases
where an appeal had been presented by
the pension officer. There were complaints in some parts of the country, but
I must say that in our part we worked
perfectly harmoniously with the pension
officer and the Local Government Board.
In the beginning it was required that a
man should work according to his ability,
opportunity, and need. I think that by
an amending Act that has been changed.
There was an interesting case before that
amending Act, where for the first time I
think it was recognized by the Local
Government Board that begging came
within the definition of opportunity and
need, and a man who openly declared that
for fourteen years he had done nothing
but beg was given his pension. No
wonder that Clause was withdrawn in the
amending Act. The new system will, of
course, entail some hardship on clerks to
Old Age Pension Committees, but I am
sure the Ministry will soften this as much
as possible. All changes involve hardship
to someone. It is very difficult to avoid
it. With regard to economies which it
has been said will result from this Bill,
I shall listen with great interest, as I am
sure every Member of the House will, to
the explanation the right hon. Gentleman
will give as to the saving that will result.
It is not obvious on the face of it, I must
say, but I am sure that with his powers
of explanation and persuasion the right
hon. Gentleman might be able to change
our minds.
Mr. DORMAN: I am very pleased to
see the Minister of Labour here to-day,
as he will be the Minister under whose
Department this Act will be administered.
I presume this Bill has been introduced
for the purpose of economy and uniformity
of administration, and it contains nothing
of a controversial character. I happen to
have for many years represented the
organized workers on the Ward Committee
for the ward in which I live. It has
been a recognized fact in the large industrial centres of Northern Ireland that
the organized trade unions have been
asked to nominate a representative for
the Old Age Pension Committees, and I
believe the Department was considerably
assisted by the knowledge possessed by
these representatives. I wish to express
the hope that the right hon. Gentleman
may see his way in setting up the new
committees to give the organized workers
some direct representation on these committees, especially as they are so well able
to acquire the knowledge necessary for
this work.
sent
a
There is another aspect of this question
that I want to specially stress in the
presence of the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Labour. There are at prenumber of individuals who
members of old-age pensions committees
think have exceeded the qualifying age,
but who are not able to obtain what is
considered by the Department the necessary evidence to enable them to satisfy
the Department as to the legality of their
claim. It is a well-known fact that even
in contracts for large sums of moneyand I speak from personal experiencewhere one of the two parties has entered
into a deliberate contract for a proposal
form of insurance for, say £1,000 payable
at a certain age or at death, the contract
contains a Clause making it necessary for
proof of age to be obtained at the time
the endowment becomes due or at the
time of the decease of the person insured.
I know from practical experience that all
offices recognizes the impossibility under
certain circumstances of the assured
person being able to produce documentary
evidence of age, and the practice amongst
all respectable offices in Great Britain and
America has been that where two competent individuals - persons who have
attained an age to satisfy a magistrate
they are truthful-are produced and make
a sworn declaration before a magistrate
that the person making the claim has
attained the age given on the original
Old Age Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
proposal the claim is admitted. I know
from personal knowledge that many individuals have had their pensions delayed
sometimes for three and sometimes for
six months, and I understand the Department will date back a pension only 28
days. I would, therefore, make an appeal
to the right hon. Gentleman to see that
this particular aspect of the question has
his serious consideration, especially in
view of the large number of persons who
in Northern Ireland to-day cannot
produce documentary evidence of their age
owing to the unfortunate destruction of
the necessary documents in Dublin. I
would appeal to the Minister of Labour to
bear this fact in mind.
Mr. LESLIE: Like the speakers who
have preceded me, I desire to welcome the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Labour. What I am about to say is said
without any lack of appreciation of the
services which the noble Viscount, the
Parliamentary Secretary to the Department of the Prime Minister, renders to
this House, for those are services we all
greatly appreciate; and, indeed, we are
frequently moved to wonder at the
miracles he performs in connection with
Bills which are apparently cast at him
usually at the shortest possible notice.
We entertain the very highest regard for
the noble Viscount-(Hon. Members:
Hear, hear)-but at the same time we
do like to see from time to time the
Ministers in charge of the Bills that are
brought before us. We have seen the
right hon. Gentleman here before and we
hope to see him again. Indeed, the noble
Viscount told us that this is his fourth
visit in six years. We venture to hope
that in the course of a long and successful innings he will be able to improve his
average.
Passing to the Bill itself, one can only
welcome the unification of administration
and control which this Bill will introduce.
Of the departments of the Government
which we have in Northern Ireland undoubtedly the department most appropriate to this particular social service is
the Ministry of Labour, which is already
responsible for other services which are
closely connected--almost more than connected since the introduction of the Contributory Pensions Act with this service,
and it would be a great advantage to have
the control in the hands of one Minister
who is responsible to Parliament for all
of them. As regards the pension officer,
I am bound to say that my own experience
as a member of the Old Age Pensions
Committee in a rural district was that
the Excise officer was both efficient and
considerate, and I cannot recollect that
on those occasions when there was a difficulty in proving age and that difficulty
was always before us, because even before
the destruction of records in Dublin the
fact still remains that the registration of
births in Ireland only began in 1861. a
date which obviously was important to
many of us I do not recollect a claim
dating back only 28 days. Difficulties of
age were often got over by the production
of a Statutory declaration, but it was the
duty of the committee to see that it was
a bona-fide declaration. However, when
it was got over I do not recollect a claim
dating back only 28 days. My impression
was that the petitioner was put in the
position he would have been in if he had
been able to put in a certificate of birth
at the date of his original claim.
to
The future constitution of these
committees will not, I fancy, be
found in practice
differ very
materially from the present constitution. I think it was Senator Dorman
who made a point of the necessity for
bringing people on these committees who
were in direct sympathy with the persons
applying for pensions. That point seems
to me to be answered in the Bill, and I
expect the Minister of Labour will refer
us to the paragraph which says that so far
as possible at least half the court shall
consist of representatives of the persons
most likely to be affected, or words to that
effect. Speaking on the question of those
committees there does seem to me to be
a possible danger as regards the chairman.
The chairman is to be appointed by the
Ministry of Labour, but I presume that
the intention is by Regulation to make
some provision for the appointment of a
deputy chairman. Of course, a deputy
chairman appointed by the Ministry while
actually engaged would be the chairman,
but one has always found on these local
committees that now and again some particular individual cannot attend. It may
be that he is ill or otherwise engaged, and
those temporary disabilities are just as
likely to affect an appointed chairman
as anybody else. It will, therefore, be
necessary, I think, that steps should be
taken to meet that possible contingency,
but I imagine that the power of Regulation is sufficiently wide to cover that,
and that the right hon. Gentleman will
not require any further power.
473 Old Age Pensions Bill-

9 DECEMBER 1927
Second Reading

Then there remains the question of
clerks. My experience is that the clerk
to an Old Age Pension Committee was
usually a pluralist of perhaps rather a
pronounced kind. Speaking generally,
I do not think there is going to be any
great hardship there. From the point of
view of the applicant it seems to me that
if anything he has been rather more
favoured than he was entitled to expect.
He has most ample provision for appeal. A
wonderful opportunity for appeal is provided in what is after all not a matter of
litigation. The only other difficulty that
occurs to me is one that can perhaps be
cleared up. We were told that the present practice is for a person to go to the
post office and get a form of application.
That is to remain. Then, apparently,
the form of application has to be sent,
not, as I expected, direct to the pension
officer, but to a representative of the
nearest Labour Exchange, and by him
transmitted to the pension officer. That
is introducing a new link in the chain.
Then the pension officer gets the form and
goes on with the business, and he should
be able to deal more promptly with the
matter than the Pension Committee
hitherto have been able to do.
I think the right hon. Gentleman will
be able to tell us a little more about that
difficulty of the claim not going direct
from the applicant to the pension officer.
There is a possibility of a breakdown
there. Taking the Bill as a whole it is
with very great pleasure that I support
it, because I believe it will be found to
be an advantage. Hitherto, the Ministry
of Labour has been singularly successful
in dealing with pensions on a contributory basis, and with certain other
matters, and I have every reason to
believe it will be equally successful in
dealing with ordinary old-age pensions
when this service is transferred to it.
we
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): I would like in the first
instance to thank the noble Viscount
and those other hon. Members who
have in such a kindly way welcomed
me here to-day. Although it may
not be generally known-because
who are in public life very often try
to hide our feelings-I am a shy man, and
therefore I have hesitated to intrude
myself upon this honourable Hquse more
frequently than necessary in the past.
But I can assure Members that my hesitation will be less pronounced in the
future owing to the kind way in which I
have been received to-day. May I also,
in passing, congratulate the noble
Viscount on the very able speech which
he delivered in introducing this very important administrative Measure. I think
it is well up to the standard of this
honourable House and the Parliamentary
institutions of Northern Ireland. I would
also like to say that I appreciate very
much the sympathetic way in which this
Measure has been approached to-day by
every Member who has spoken, and such
criticism as has been made I recognize has
been put forward with a view to obtain
information and to be helpful. And it is,
therefore, a very particular pleasure to me
to endeavour to answer the various points
which have been made in such a sympathetic manner.
The hon. Senator (Mr. Long) who sits
upon my right paid a very deserved
tribute to
the pensions committees
throughout Northern Ireland. I would
like to associate myself with that tribute.
It has occurred to me that these committees have in the past performed many
very useful duties, and one of these has
evidently not been generally known up to
recently, that they have acted as educational establishments for a great number
of public men in Northern Ireland, because many of the hon. Members who
spoke in the Commons and I think every
hon. Senator who has spoken to-day has
referred to the fact that he has acted
upon these committees. With all
modesty may I add for six years I was
chairman of one of these committees
myself. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Well, it is natural, therefore, I should
desire to retain the services of these committees, and the Bill provides that
regulations shall be made placing these
public-spirited ladies and gentlemen who
have acted on these committees in the
past upon the panel which shall form
the basis from which committees will in
the future be formed. And in addition
to that, in passing, I may say I have
undertaken in the Commons to see that at
least one of those serving on these committees shall belong to the class from
which claimants are drawn. I think that
will ensure that the claims shall get every
sympathy.
I would like to add that I very fully
appreciate the very sympathetic way in
which the Ministry of Home Affairs have
dealt with our old-age people. I recognize
that it is the wish of every right thinking
Old Age Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Andrews]
man and woman in Northern Ireland that
our old people should receive every
courtesy when claiming their pensions,
and should obtain every assistance in
order to establsh their claims, and should
be in no way embarrassed or agitated,
but on the contrary they should realize
that they are going to obtain something
that the law entitles them to.
I have been asked as regards the question of economy. Well, of course, I can
only give estimates, because in establishing a new departure of this kind it is
obvious to everyone you cannot lay it
down and say the saving is definitely
going to be £10,000 or any other figure.
But my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Finance, did say in the House of
Commons that it was his opinion that the
saving would approximate to the sum
mentioned. I have been asked definitely
to say where the principal saving would
occur. The service which was an agency
service carried out by the Customs officials
of the Imperial Ministry, although a
very efficient service was an expensive
service, because these officials were generally very highly paid. We propose to
utilize our Labour Exchange managers or
officers for the purpose of making inquiries which were previously made by
the Customs officials, and we see no reason
why that service should cost anything
additional to what it is costing at the
present time. In the country districts at
any rate managers would be quite able to
take on this additional work, especially
as unemployment has fallen so considerably. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
I may say that the reason why claims
have in the first instance to be sent to the
Labour Exchanges is because it will be the
duty of the Labour Exchange managers to
make all investigations which they think
necessary in the first instance before transferring the claim to the head office. It
will then be open to the pensions officer at
headquarters, who will be appointed by
myself, to pass the claim provided he feels
that the evidence is sufficient. If, on
the other hand, he is doubtful, he can
refer the claim himself to the local pensions committee, when, no doubt, further
investigation will be made. Or, if he rejects the claim, it will then be open to
the claimant to appeal against that
decision, and be heard in person as in the
past with a view to establishing the claim
if possible. It will only be in case of
appeals from the local body that the services of the umpire will be utilized, and
I think it is right and proper that the
final court to investigate claims should be
one apart from any Government, no
matter which party happens to be in
power-|
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. ANDREWS-apart from any
Government department, but by a gentleman appointed by the Governor himself,
and absolutely free from any party influence of any kind or description. I
think that that will recommend itself to
every hon. Member in this House. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
The difficulty in deciding these claims
in the past, I admit, has been one of age,
and I have no hesitation in saying the
Department will recognize the difficulty
which some claimants have in establishing
their age. When they cannot produce birth
certificates any other evidence which is
sufficient to satisfy any reasonable man
will be accepted in lieu of birth certificates. And, may I add in passing,
this difficulty of age will in a very few
years disappear, because about 62 or 63
years ago there was compulsory registration, and so a great deal of this difficulty
of age will be got over and the work of
administration will be very much simplified. The only other test that has got
to be applied, of course, is one of means,
and there, I think, reasonableness has got
to be shown again, because many applicants for these old-age pensions in future
will be of the farming and shopkeeping
community, because, as hon. Members
know, in future almost all our industrial
classes will be entitled to contributory
pensions.
A great difficulty at the present time I
recognize is to establish the value of a
small holding. Well, amongst other
things I happen to farm a pretty large
holding, and I therefore have no exaggerated views (laughter) of the value of
ither large holdings or small holdings at
the present time. Therefore, if I have
any influence in the matter I do not think
the interests of the claimants will be overlooked.
I think I have covered most of the
points that were raised. Reference was
made by the hon. Senator opposite (Mr.
Leslie) to the question of chairmen and
477 Old Age Pensions Bill
Lisburn Electric Supply Bill 478
9 DECEMBER 1927
deputy chairmen, and I am very glad
that he raised the matter, because it is
one of some importance. One of the
reasons for the payment of the chairman
is that he should be a man upon whom the
Department can call at any time, and
when he is asked to act in the capacity
of chairman he will do so on the basis
that unless he attends he will get no fee.
Therefore, I think he is very unlikely
to be absent unless he finds he is unable
to attend his work, and then as he will no
doubt want to keep on good terms with
the Department, so that other opportunities of a similar character may be granted
to him. He will notify the Department
that he is unable to attend, and the
Department will see that someone else
takes his place. So that I do not think it
will be necessary to appoint deputy chairmen.
Mr. LESLIE: He will not be a permanent chairman?
Mr. ANDREWS: No. The chairmen
who will be appointed over the whole of
Northern Ireland will be men who to
some extent will be mobile. If one is
unable to go another will be asked to
take his place. I think that I have really
answered all the points that were raised
by hon. Members with the exception
of one, and that was about the retrospective character of the claims rejected
in the first instance. If hon. Members
will refer to Clause 2, Sub-section (7),
they will see that when a claim has been
reconsidered it will be retrospective to the
date of the original claim.
a
I would like the House to appreciate
that really the only reason that this
administrative change has been made
is because, obviously, there had to
be a good deal of overlapping since our
contributory old-age pensions came into
existence. The same claim was sometimes being dealt with by three Departments which obviously was
very
uneconomical method. And it is felt
now that making one Department the
responsible authority will again cheapen
administration, and not only do that, but
what I think is almost equally important,
expedite procedure, so that those who are
entitled to have their claims granted shall
obtain their pensions without any unnecessary delay. There are many other
reasons why I think this Bill, which is
purely an administrative one, should become the law of the land, but I will
conclude by thanking all the Members of
this House for the sympathetic way in
which they have approached the matter.
Mr. DORMAN: I would like to make
my position clear regarding what I
meant when I said that the Pensions
Committee would not date pensions back
for more than 28 days. In some cases
individuals for six months after they have
attained the qualifying age for pension
have made efforts to get documentary
evidence of their age before going to the
post office to make their application. In
such cases the committee will recommend
that the pension should be dated back
only 28 days.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think it would be
desirable that one should let claimants
all over Northern Ireland know and I
will take an opportunity of doing sothat Labour Exchange officials will act as
the friends of claimants. It will be the
duty of Labour Exchange officials to
endeavour to substantiate the claims of
applicants, always, of course, provided
that they are proper claims, and to assist
claimants in getting any evidence which
may be necessary.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.

Landlords and Prospective Tenants.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I beg to move, That this
House do now adjourn until Tuesday
next. In doing so I should like to remind the House that on Tuesday I shall
move the suspension of the Standing
Order for the purpose of passing the Old
Age Pensions (Administration) Bill,
through its remaining stages.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to draw the
attention of the Government to a practice
which is growing up at present in order
to evade the provisions of an Act which
this Parliament has placed upon the
Statute Book for the protection of
tenants of houses at a valuation of £8
and under. The Government have found
it necessary to place this Rent and Mortgage Interest Restrictions Act on the
Statute Book to prevent profiteering on
the part of landlords by the latter putting
into a house a few articles of furniture,
letting the house as a furnished house,
and charging the tenant an exorbitant
rent. What is taking place now is even
of a more heartless nature. The agents
of landlords are asking for large sums,
varying from £5 to £16, from applicants
for vacant houses in the working class
districts of the city.
There came under my notice this morning the case of one woman who made
application for a vacant house in the
centre of a working class district in the
city. The controlled rent of that house
is 5s 9d a week, and she was informed
that she could not get the house unless
she put down £16. She made an offer to
the agent to pay two months rent in
advance, and to obtain for him what he
might consider satisfactory guarantees for
the payment of the rent. She was told
that under no circumstances would she
or any other applicant get possession of
that house unless they were prepared to
put down £16, and the agent was heartless enough to suggest to this poor working woman that she should go to a moneylender to borrow the £16 to enable her
to get possession of the house.
At the present time the city is in a
deplorable condition so far as the housing
of the working classes is concerned. As
I have mentioned before, Belfast is the
only large industrial centre in Great
Britain or Ireland that has increased its
one-room dwellers between the last two
census, and the physique of future
generations is going to be seriously
affected if this condition of affairs is
allowed to continue. I make a wholehearted appeal to the Government to see
if something cannot be done to stop this
method of profiteering at the expense of
the working classes. Words fail me in
attempting to give my opinion of the
character of any man who could prosecute
a system of this kind for the purpose of
exploiting the most poor and helpless part
of the community.
VISCOUNT MASSEERENE AND
FERRARD: The reprehensible practice
of which the hon. Member has given the
House such a glaring example is one which
I personally cannot too strongly condemn.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). I cannot
carry in my mind the provisions of the
various Rent Restrictions Acts that have
been passed during the last four or five
years by this Parliament. My own
opinion is that what is taking place is
illegal, but I will at once bring the matter
to the notice of the proper authorities,
with the object of ensuring, if possible,
that this practice is not continued.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five Minutes
after Four o'clock,
until Tuesday next.

13 DECEMBER 1927
Third Reading.
+

THE SENΑΤΕ.

Tuesday, 13th December, 1927.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. JOSEPH
CUNNINGHAM (Deputy Speaker) in the
Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Education :
Regulations for the Technical School
Examinations.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and
ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Moved: "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting,
in respect of the Old Age Pensions Bill."
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question Motion agreed to.
Trade Disputes and Trade Unions Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
Mr. DORMAN: I refrained from
exercising my right to propose any
Amendments to this Bill on the Committee stage, but I felt it my duty to
review this Measure in order to show the
disabilities that would be suffered by the
working classes who refused to sell their
labour power so as to resist any injustice
that may be attempted to be inflicted
upon them by their employers. I do not
now desire to occupy the time of the
Senate at any undue length, but I want
to refer to the difficulties that will confront the working classes if this Measure
becomes the law of Northern Ireland. I
notice that under the First Clause of the
Bill any working
ing men
body of workcalculated to coerce the Government, or
who participate in a strike
man or
to inflict hardship upon the community
will come within the provisions of the
Bill and will be liable to the penalties set
forth in the Act. If I were a magistrate
in Northern Ireland and had taken an
oath or made an affirmation to administer
this Bill when it becomes an Act of
Parliament justly as between the Crown
and the subject, I would feel compelled,
while these words remain in the Bill, to
convict any man or body of men brought
before me for participating in a strike of
any kind.
a
I cannot conceive of any strike that is
not calculated to inflict injury upon the
community. Suppose I was presiding
over a bench of magistrates, and the
learned Attorney-General brought
number of individuals before that court
under this particular Clause. I do not see
how I could do otherwise than declare
them to be guilty. Suppose a baker was
brought before the court I would feel
compelled to address my fellow magistrates and say: "I cannot help believing
that this man is inflicting a hardship
upon the community. He is engaged in
an operation the continuance of which is
absolutely necessary for the life of the
community as a whole." Therefore, as
a magistrate I would be forced to come to
the conclusion that that man was guilty
and was subject to the penalties prescribed in the Bill. Suppose a tramwayman, a motorman or a conductor, was
brought before me for a similar offence.
Tramway-men cannot withdraw their
labour without in some measure inflicting
hardship upon the community inasmuch
as they would be depriving them of the
facilities for travelling from point to
point inside the city boundary. Therefore, magistrates would have no alternative but to find those men guilty. I
have no desire to go on giving a catalogue
of strikes that would come within the
purview of this particular Clause of the
Bill.
Suffice it to say that I cannot
conceive of any body of men, in our
intricate and inter-dependent social
conditions coming out on strike without
inflicting hardship on the community.
There is another point I wish to direct
attention to. It is with reference to the
words in lines 13, 14, and 15 on page 2
of the Bill, where it makes it illegal for
any person to apply sums of money in
furtherance of any such illegal strike or
lock-out. The circumstance which led to
the necessity for this Bill being intro-
Q
Trade Disputes Bill-
Third Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
duced in the Imperial Parliament was the
great lock-out of last year, when the
miners were forced out by a threatened
prolongation of their hours and the
worsening of their conditions. By direct
observation and inquiry made during last
week-end, I found out that the conditions of the men engaged in that industry
are disastrous in the extreme. They are
exactly the conditions that the Royal
Commission predicted they would be in
if the hours were prolonged and conditions worsened.
There is another part of the Bill--
Clause 2-to which I would like to direct
the attention of the House. Under this
Clause the working classes are going to be
deprived of the right of combining for the
purpose of sustaining their standard of
comfort. I was reading in the Press a
short time ago that one of the great municipalities in the Midlands of England had
a vacancy for the position of Medical
Officer of Health for which they offered
a salary of £1,500 a year. The trade
union of the medical profession refused
even to insert an advertisement in their
trade union organ to the effect that this
vacancy was open at a salary of £1,500
a year, because, I presume, the British
Medical Association had decided by a
majority vote that £1,600 a vear should
be the minimum salary attached to this
important office. I understand that the
same thing prevails in the legal profession.
The legal profession. probably by rules
drawn up by a majority vote, prevents
its members from accepting fees below the
standard they have set up. There are
numerous other instances I might quote
where organizations are left to decide this
question, while the working classes in
their organized capacity are going to be
deprived of this right and privilege.
Clause 3 says that the expression 'to
intimidate" means to cause in the mind of
a person a reasonable apprehension of
injury to him or any member of his family
or to any of his dependants. I cannot
understand what is meant by that. Supposing the trade union of which I am a
member asks me to go and interview what
we would term a traitor, a scab, or a
blackleg, a man who was not prepared to
stand by the majority vote of his union.
I approach that man with the obiect of
peacefully trying to persuade him that, in
the interests of himself and the members
of his family, he should remain loyal to
his fellow-workers. It may seem to hon.
Senators a foolish illustration, but suppose
that when I am speaking to that man
some small insect ran across my throat
and I lifted my index finger to wipe
it off. A traitor and a coward might
entertain the apprehension that I was
giving a dumb indication that I proposed to cut his throat. It may seem
foolish, but what is to prevent a man
entertaining the fear that I was using
such a threat. Suppose I unconsciously
clench my hand and raise it up my side,
what is to prevent such a man apprehending that I am about to strike him
with my clenched fist. The law is a
peculiar thing.
Hon. Members may laugh, but in this
connection, I should like to give an
illustration of what might happen. When
the old Combination Laws were in force
men who had products to sell in the
market combined to inflate prices irrespective of injury they might inflict on
the community. In the year 1812 a poor
woman who assisted to seize a man's
potatoes in the market in Manchester to
compel him to sell them at a lower price
than that he had asked, was hanged by
the neck until she was dead simply because she attempted to prevent profiteering at the expense of her own class.
I do not wish to prolong this discussion, but I cannot conceive of any
strike which could not but be argued as
being an injury to the community. I
think that this Bill is directly against a
combination of labour in the political
field. When the workers were satisfied to
approach the two great political parties
in the State by deputation and otherwise
they were all right, but since they have
determined to demand for themselves the
sovereign power in our constitution,
equivalent representation for their
members in the Parliament of the community, certain classes have been determined to clip their wings and prevent
them making that combination a success.
From the political point of view Labour
is marching on.
can hear their
footsteps-
We
Mr. DEPUTY SPEAKER: I am
afraid the hon. Senator is departing from
the Third Reading.
Mr. DORMAN: All I wish to do is to
offer my protest against this Bill as a
485 Trade Disputes Bill-

13 DECEMBER 1927
Third Reading


Member of this Honourable House, whose
function and duty is to review the
legislation passed in another place. I
believe this Bill when it becomes law will
be disastrous to the power of the working
classes and will inflict upon them an injustice that is not attempted to be
inflicted on any other class of the community which claim and do exercize the
very things that Labour is restrained
from doing under this Bill. I shall,
therefore, have to divide the House on
the Third Reading.
Mr. McDOWELL: I cannot conceive
for a moment how an intelligent Senator
such as Senator Dorman can introduce
the argument that this Bill will have a
detrimental effect on the legitimate
rights of trade unions. The other
evening a meeting was held in the city
of Belfast under the auspices of what used
to be one of the greatest trade unions in
the United Kingdom. The government
of that trade union was practically one
to be envied by every Government and
every body of working men in the world.
It was democratic in form and principle.
The society could order no strike except
by a two-thirds majority of the members,
who had perfect control over its financial
affairs. What do we find to-day? This
same trade union is controlled by those
who are not only opposed to the
principles of trade unionism but also to
the working man, and further to the
Government of the country. They have
got the members, by what method I do
not know, to sell their rights of democracy. They have no right to say
whether they will strike or not. They
have given over the control to an execиtive of ten, twelve or twenty---
Mr. DORMAN: By vote.
Mr. McDOWELL: I might say to
Senator Dorman it is known that in
some of these great societies the votes
have been faked. The members have no
control over the finances, and they have
sold, shall I say, their birthright of democracy into the hands of a few who wish
to destroy both industry and the welfare
of the working men. I am almost afraid
to launch into this because I would not
like to be called to order. It would take
one to go over a vast amount of ground
to arrive at how this thing has been got
at. We are all conversant with this important fact that for the last number of
years, since the Russian revolution,
number of men and women have been
a
going about this country trying to ruin
it. The way they are doing it is to get
into the trades unions, and by their
methods they have practically got control
of these bodies. Senator Dorman may say
"Why do they allow them to do that"?
Mr. DORMAN:
have the vote.
Hear, hear. They
Mr. McDOWELL: No doubt the
working men have been apathetic in allowing these men, who speak from soap
tubs or onion boxes, to get control of the
unions and to dictate how the working
men should act. These speakers get up
at street corners and say "We have
right to a minimum wage of £4 a week."
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
a
Mr. MCDOWELL: It is very easy to
say 'Hear, hear," to that which you can.
not get yourself.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
Hear, hear.
Mr. McDOWELL: We know that
these leaders of thought to-day are paid
four or five times the wage we used to
get. Worse than that the dupes who
have to pay get nothing for paying.
Senator Dorman says our rights are going
to be curtailed.
Mr. DORMAN: Wait and see.
Mr. MCDOWELL: I believe this Bill
is a charter of liberty. It lies in the
hands of trade unionism to make it what
it should be and what it used to be. In
the old days we could sell our labour in
the dearest market. As to all the talk
about strikes, every society will be able to
strike, but I think there will be no syn
nathetic strike. I think it is only a farce.
I could give you two illustrations. Take
the shipwrights and joiners in the shinvards. One is watching the other like
hawks all day long in case they infringe
one another's work. What sympathy is
there between those two bodies of men in
case of a strike? The same thing occurs
in regard to the engineers and plumbers
What sympathy is there between those
two trades? These people may call them
out in sympathy, but there is no sym.
pathy between them. They have no
common platform to work from, because
these men cannot possibly have sympathy
with one another, seeing they are trving
to get the bread out of each other's
mouths day after day.
487 Trade Disputes Bill.

THE SENATE
Third Reading
Mr DORMAN: Shame.
Mr. McDOWELL: I do not wish to
take up the time of the Senate. The old
trade unionists believe this Bill to be a
charter of liberty, and it is up to us to
try to rout these Red Handers-I was
going to say something worse-- out of the
country altogether. It will enable us to
get back to the old principles, and with
peace in industry we will get back into
our former thriving position. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I will only detain the
House a very few moments. Senator
Dorman, who is shortly going to divide
the House is, I cannot help thinking,
somewhat mistaken about the purport of
this Bill. If he will study Clause I he
will see, as I explained on the Second
Reading, that a strike is not illegal except
under certain conditions. As regards
Section 2, to which the Senator referred,
I fail to see the connection of this union
that would not allow its members to have
a salary of £1,500 with the Section which
is to secure and protect persons who refuse to take part in illegal strikes or
lock-outs. The hon. Senator seemed to
consider that the question of a reasonable apprehension of injury was absurd
and ridiculous. We all know that in the
old days injury meant physical violence.
We are all perfectly well aware that there
are and have been occasions when no
actual personal violence has been inflicted, and yet it is obvious to the
ordinary individual that occasions might
arise when there might be reasonable apprehension of injury. There was such a
thing as intimidating a man's family
without intimidating the man himself.
There are other questions which I need
not go into. Senator McDowell in his
able and striking speech put what I may
call the object of this Bill in one phrase.
He called it a charter of liberty. I agree
with him. I think Senator Dorman is
mistaken in the view he has taken. He
tells us to wait and see.
Mr. DORMAN: Certainly.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: I am perfectly prepared to
wait and see.
Question put : "That the Bill be now
read the Third time."
